The request highlights efforts by foreign allies to shore up a country still shaken by sporadic violence 15 months after the overthrow of President Bashar al-Assad, with the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran now rattling the wider region.
Those allies see Sharaa as crucial to preventing a relapse into sectarian fighting or civil war, after ÔÇő14 years of civil conflict drove millions of refugees abroad and allowed Islamic State to control swathes of Syria.
The militants last month stepped up attacks on military and ÔÇősecurity personnel across Syria and declared Sharaa, a former rebel, their "number one foe".
It was unclear what specifically TurkeyÔÇÖs National Intelligence Organization, or MIT, had Ôüáasked of MI6, or what new role, if any, MI6 had taken up.
The Turkish presidency said in a statement issued by its communications directorate that MIT maintains effective cooperation in the ÔÇőfight against terrorism with the international intelligence community and security units in Syria, but that this story did not reflect the truth.
"Contrary to what is claimed in the report in question, ÔÇőit is not true that MIT has made any request to MI6 regarding the protection of the Syrian President or sought to assume such a role," it said.
Turkey, Britain and the U.S. last year threw their backing behind Sharaa to try to reunite and rebuild his country of 26 million. London and Washington have scrapped most sanctions on Syria and on Hayat Tahrir al-Sham (HTS), the Islamist group he ÔÇőonce led.
The sources who spoke to Reuters requested anonymity owing to the sensitivity of the matter.
MIT, the Turkish foreign ministry, Britain's foreign office and Syria's defence and interior ministries did ÔÇőnot comment when contacted ahead of publication.
The sources, including Syrian and foreign officials, all cited rising anxiety over a series of reported Islamic State plots to kill Sharaa.
A Turkish source said that MIT, which has played ÔÇîa key Ôüárole in helping the new government to establish itself, appealed to MI6 for more support after one such incident last month. A senior Syrian security source said the request came after a ÔÇťhigh-risk assassination plotÔÇŁ, adding that MIT, MI6 and Syrian authorities were constantly sharing intelligence.
Details of the plot were unclear.
A separate Western intelligence source briefed on the matter believed Turkey wanted to introduce a Western presence in Damascus to provide something of a buffer between the agencies of Turkey and Israel, currently at loggerheads.
Last year, Sharaa and two senior cabinet ministers ÔÇőwere targeted by Islamic State in five ÔÇőfoiled assassination attempts, according to the U.N. ÔüáOffice of Counter-Terrorism. In November, Reuters reported that Syrian authorities had foiled two of the attempts .
Describing Sharaa as a "watchdog" of the global anti-Islamic State coalition, the group mounted six attacks on Syrian authorities last month in what it called a ÔÇťnew phase".
On Thursday, Damascus openly acknowledged for the first ÔÇőtime that it coordinates with MIT, saying they had cooperated to foil an Islamic State attack in the capital.
Turkish security sources said ÔÇőMIT had identified a team Ôüáof three preparing remote bomb attacks, enabling Syrian counterparts to prevent an "imminent assault".
A U.S. diplomat briefed on the matter said MIT's request to MI6 had been prompted by the Islamic State resurgence.
The Western intelligence source said the two agencies could intensify joint planning and technical operations, but that no decision had been made on whether to send British personnel to Damascus.
A Syrian security source said a Ôüáphysical British ÔÇőpresence would be "highly risky". They said MI6 had been discussed at a meeting in Damascus on February 26 between ÔÇőa delegation headed by Britain's special envoy for Syria, Ann Snow, and Syria's deputy interior minister, Major General Abdulqader Tahan.
Sharaa was a commander of Al Qaeda's Nusra Front in Syria before cutting ties with the group in 2016, then led ÔÇőa coalition of Islamist rebel factions in late 2024 to topple Assad.

item.title: Hungary's $82 million bank transit swoop deepens spat with Ukraine
item.description:
BUDAPEST/KYIV, March 6 (Reuters) - Hungary ÔÇősaid on Friday it had detained seven Ukrainians carrying around $82 million in cash and gold on suspicion of money laundering, ÔÇîas Kyiv accused Budapest of taking bank employees hostage amid a dispute over oil shipments.
The Hungarian Tax Authority's decision to have counter-terrorism forces swoop on two Ukrainian armoured vehicles transporting cash to Ukraine marks a dramatic escalation of tensions that have already resulted in Budapest blocking tens of billions of euros in European Union aid for Kyiv.
Ukrainian Foreign Minister ÔÇőAndrii Sybiha said on X that the people detained were employees of Oschadbank and accused Hungary of "taking hostages and stealing money".
"This is ÔÇőstate terrorism and racketeering," he added, demanding their immediate release and asking the EU to "provide a clear qualification of HungaryÔÇÖs Ôüáunlawful actions".
Video posted on the Hungarian government's Facebook account showed armed counter-terrorism officers jumping out of a van as the Ukrainian vehicles pulled into ÔÇőa service station, pointing their guns at the windscreen before handcuffing the people inside and forcing them to lie face down on the ground.
"The National Tax ÔÇőand Customs Administration (NAV) is pursuing criminal proceedings on suspicion of money laundering," the authority said in a statement.
"On March 5, 2026, it detained seven Ukrainian citizens, including a former Ukrainian intelligence service general, and two armoured cash-in-transit vehicles, which were transporting a total of $40 million, 35 million euros and nine kilograms of gold from Austria to Ukraine."
The tax ÔÇőauthority said it was working together with counter-terrorism forces.
Government spokesperson Zoltan Kovacs said the seven detainees would be expelled from Hungary. It was not ÔÇőimmediately clear what would happen to the money they were transporting.
Ukraine's ambassador to Hungary, Sandor Fegyir, went to the Budapest headquarters of the Counter-Terrorism Centre to try and ÔÇîmeet with Ôüáthe detained Ukrainians, two Reuters witnesses said.
The detainees' lawyer, Lorant Horvath, said he had been unable to contact them. Kyiv's ambassador to Budapest, Sandor Fegyir, was also unable to meet the detainees.
"This is an outrageous procedure... (T)he embassy, and indeed the consular service, must in all cases be allowed access to the accused," Horvath told Reuters. "Defence lawyers must also be allowed (access)."
Ukraine advised its citizens against travel to Hungary, saying it could not guarantee their safety amid what it called ÔÇőthe "arbitrary actions" of Hungarian authorities.
Hungary and ÔÇőSlovakia accuse Ukraine of deliberately delaying Ôüáthe resumption of oil flows via the damaged Druzhba pipeline for political reasons. Kyiv denies the charge, saying it needs time to repair the damage caused to energy infrastructure by a Russian drone strike on January 27.
Hungarian Prime ÔÇőMinister Viktor Orban, who faces a serious challenge to his 16-year rule in an election on April 12, has made ÔÇőthe war in Ukraine Ôüáa central plank of his campaign, saying the opposition would drag Hungary into the conflict.
He has vetoed new EU sanctions on Moscow as well as a huge loan for Ukraine over the oil dispute.
Speaking on state radio, Orban again accused Kyiv of blackmail and said Hungary would use all means at its disposal until oil flows resume. ÔüáHe did ÔÇőnot mention the detention of the Ukrainians.
Ukraine's Oschadbank said its employees had been engaged in a routine operation.
"Since the ÔÇőstart of the full-scale invasion, foreign currency and bank metals have been transported exclusively by land," it said in a statement. "Similar trips are carried out by Oschadbank's cash collection vehicles on ÔÇőa weekly basis."

item.title: Anglican conservative bloc calls for boycott of Canterbury leadership
item.description:
ABUJA, March 6 (Reuters) - A powerful ÔÇőgroup of conservative Anglicans on Friday urged its members to boycott meetings convened by the ÔÇîArchbishop of Canterbury and halt any financial contributions to the current leadership, escalating a longÔÇĹrunning rift within the church.
The declaration by the Global Anglican Future Conference (GAFCON) - a grouping of conservative churches mainly from Africa and Asia that claims to represent a majority ÔÇőof the world's Anglicans - follows a decision to establish a new council rivalling the current leadership.
Three days ÔÇőof meetings of GAFCON members in Nigeria have underscored the deep rifts within the ÔüáAnglican Communion over theological and social issues, including the ordination of women and the inclusion of LGBTQ+ members.
They ÔÇőcome just weeks before the Communion is due to enthrone Sarah Mullally as its Archbishop of Canterbury, ÔÇőuntil now spiritual leader of the world's 85 million Anglicans spread across 165 countries.
Laurent Mbanda, installed on Thursday as chairman of the new rival council, read a statement saying the Global Anglican Communion required "a principled disengagement" from structures associated with the Church ÔÇőof England.
"Leaders who hold office in the Global Anglican Communion must not attend future Primates' meetings called by ÔÇőthe Archbishop of Canterbury, nor attend the Lambeth Conference, nor attend ACC meetings or participate in Commissions of the ACC," the ÔÇîstatement Ôüásaid.
It added that leaders "should not personally approve financial contributions to the ACC. It is also expected that they will not receive financial assistance from compromised sources."
It was not immediately clear how much money was at stake.
The Church of England did not immediately comment.
Founded some 500 years ago when the Church of England broke ÔÇőfrom Rome, the Anglican Communion ÔÇőhas spread to many Ôüáparts of the world, particularly former British colonies.
In recent decades, there have been some liberal shifts within part of the Church. GAFCON was set up in 2008, drawing ÔÇőon the resistance to these changes, especially in Africa and Asia, where the ÔÇőchurch is expanding Ôüáfastest.
On Thursday, a spokesperson for the Anglican Communion Office in London said GAFCON was ignoring years of dialogue aimed at reforming the church.
On whether the church had now split, Diarmaid MacCulloch, emeritus professor of church history at the ÔüáUniversity of ÔÇőOxford, told Reuters: "Of course it's a schism."
But MacCulloch said the ÔÇőrupture need not necessarily be permanent.
"Schisms do eventually get healed, when both sides see that the issues that caused the schism don't seem ÔÇőthat important any longer," he said.

item.title: Germany's Merz warns against spread of war in Middle East
item.description:
MUNICH, March 6 (Reuters) - German Chancellor Friedrich Merz said on Friday Berlin was working with ÔÇîpartners to find an approach to end the fighting with Iran as he warned against allowing the country to fall into chaos as the war widens across the Middle East.
Merz said Germany shared the aims of the United States and ÔÇőIsrael to stop Tehran's nuclear and missile programme , its threats to Israel and its support for ÔÇőproxy groups and he said the Iranian people had the right to decide Ôüátheir own future.
"However, as the fighting continues and spreads, we also see growing risks," he said in ÔÇőa statement. "An endless war is not in our interest."
He said Germany was working with partners to formulate ÔÇőa common perspective on ending the conflict. "These considerations are ongoing," he said, without giving details.
Merz's comments reflect the unease felt in Europe at the war unleashed by the U.S. and Israeli operation that killed Iran's Supreme Leader Ali Khamenei a ÔÇőweek ago and which has since spread into Lebanon with no clear sign of an end in ÔÇősight.
As American and Israeli jets have continued to pound targets across Iran and Iranian missiles and drones have hit ÔÇîcities Ôüáin the Gulf and Israel, the economic risks have risen with global air transport in chaos and the Strait of Hormuz, one of the world's key oil shipping lanes, shut down.
Iran has not yet named a successor to Khamenei, who held power for 37 years, and Merz said the collapse of Iranian statehood ÔÇőor proxy conflicts fought ÔÇőon Iranian soil would Ôüánot be in anyone's interest.
"Such scenarios could have far-reaching consequences for Europe," he said, including uncontrolled migration by Iranians fleeing across the border if the country ÔÇőfell into chaos.
"The Iranian state must remain functional, and public order and ÔÇőbasic services must Ôüábe maintained. The Iranian economy must not collapse," Merz said.
Merz said a political process of dialogue and trust building in the region would have to begin once fighting ends. Iran's nuclear and ballistic missile programmes Ôüáwould have ÔÇőto end and be subject to verification. Iran would have ÔÇőto stop supporting proxy groups and all nuclear material would have to be secured.
If that happened sanctions might be lifted and aid ÔÇőcould be provided.

item.title: White House says US well on way toward controlling Iran airspace
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 6 (Reuters) - The United States is well on its way toward controlling Iranian airspace, ÔÇőWhite House press secretary Karoline Leavitt said on Friday, adding that ÔÇîWashington expects the achievable U.S. objectives to be completed in four to six weeks.
Speaking to reporters at the White House, Leavitt also said ÔÇőWashington was looking at potential candidates to lead Iran, ÔÇőa day after President Donald Trump told Reuters in Ôüáan interview that the United States must be involved ÔÇőin choosing the next leader of Iran.
"I know there's a number ÔÇőof people that our intelligence agencies and the United States government are looking at, but I won't get any further on that," Leavitt said.
ÔÇőIn the interview on Thursday, Trump said that he thinks ÔÇőthe next leader of Iran is unlikely to be the late Supreme ÔÇîLeader ÔüáAyatollah Ali Khamenei's son, who has emerged as a frontrunner to succeed his father, who was killed in a military strike at the start of the war.
Earlier on Friday, Trump ÔÇősaid there would ÔÇőbe no Ôüádeal struck with Iran except "unconditional surrender."
"What the President means is that when he, as Commander in Chief ÔÇőof the US Armed Forces, determines that Iran ÔÇőno Ôüálonger poses a threat to the United States of America, and the goals of Operation Epic Fury has been fully realized, Ôüáthen Iran ÔÇőwill essentially be in a place ÔÇőof unconditional surrender, whether they say it themselves or not," Leavitt said.

item.title: Pentagon taps former DOGE official to lead its AI efforts
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 6 (Reuters) - The Pentagon on Friday named as Chief Data Officer ÔÇőa computer scientist who aided billionaire Elon Musk's efforts to ÔÇîoverhaul the government last year and who has boosted white supremacists and misogynists online.
In a social media post, the Pentagon said Gavin Kliger's new role "places him at the center of the DepartmentÔÇÖs most ÔÇőambitious AI efforts," focusing on "day-to-day alignment and execution of the DepartmentÔÇÖs ÔÇőAI projects, working directly with America's frontier AI labs to Ôüásupport the warfighter."
The Pentagon did not immediately respond to a Reuters request for ÔÇőmore comment. Kliger did not immediately return a message.
Kliger, in social media posts between ÔÇőOctober 2024 and January 2025, has voiced controversial views and reposted content from white supremacist Nick Fuentes and self-described misogynist Andrew Tate.
The Pentagon's use of AI has taken center ÔÇőstage after a heated weeks-long dispute with Anthropic over guardrails on how the ÔÇőmilitary can use its AI tools led to last week's decision by the Trump administration to ÔÇîshun Ôüáthe company and replace it with OpenAI.
On Thursday, the Pentagon slapped a formal supply-chain risk designation on Anthropic - an extraordinary rebuke by the United States against an American tech company that was earlier than its rivals to work ÔÇőwith the Pentagon.
Anthropic was ÔÇőthe most aggressive Ôüáof its rivals in courting U.S. national-security officials. But the company and the Pentagon have been at odds for ÔÇőmonths over how the military can use its technology on ÔÇőthe battlefield. ÔüáThis conflict erupted into public view earlier this year.
Anthropic has refused to back down on bans for its Claude AI to power autonomous weapons and mass ÔüáU.S. surveillance. ÔÇőThe Pentagon has pushed back, saying it ÔÇőshould be able to use this technology as needed, so long as it complies with U.S. law.

item.title: Iranian attacks on civilians won't go unanswered, CENTCOM commander says
item.description:
March 6 (Reuters) - The United States ÔÇîwill respond to Iranian attacks on civilians ÔÇőthroughout the Middle ÔÇőEast, the leader of the ÔüáU.S. Central Command, ÔÇőBrad Cooper, said on ÔÇőFriday.
Cooper said that Iran has attacked 12 different countries ÔÇősince the U.S. ÔÇőand Israel launched strikes on ÔÇîthe ÔüáIslamic Republic last week, including firing seven attack drones at ÔÇőcivilian, residential ÔÇőneighborhoods Ôüáin Bahrain on Thursday night.
"This ÔÇőis unacceptable and ÔÇőwill Ôüánot go unanswered," Cooper said in a Ôüástatement.

item.title: Oil derivatives signal traders see Middle East shock as short-lived
item.description:
March 6 (Reuters) - Oil options and futures are signalling that the latest Middle East conflict may be shortÔÇĹlived, as traders pile into structures that profit from a retreat in prices after the initial spike.
Options and futures markets often provide the earliest signal of whether traders see ÔÇőa supply shock as fleeting or structural, creating opportunities to profit from sharp swings in prices.
The IsraelÔÇĹU.S. attack on Iran has ÔÇîsent shockwaves through energy markets as warÔÇĹrisk insurance costs surge, freight rates hit record levels and disruptions at the Strait of Hormuz snarl oil flows and strand hundreds of vessels. Oil prices , on Friday were at multi-year highs.
In a sign traders see the price shock as temporary, 30-day at-the-money Brent implied volatility jumped 17.5 points to 68% over the past ÔÇőweek through Tuesday, while 60- and 90-day tenors rose only 5.9 and 2.8 percentage points, LSEG data shows.
"What we're watching in real time is ÔÇőthe difference between a logistics crisis and a structural one," Brian E. Kinsella, former Goldman Sachs energy specialist, told Ôüáthe Reuters Global Markets Forum. , opens new tab
"The market is betting it's logistical and I think that is the right read," he added.
The Brent futures curve is sending a ÔÇősimilar signal.
The spread between the front-month Brent contract and the six-month contract widened to about $10, the steepest backwardation since the Russia-Ukraine war in 2022, pointing to ÔÇőtight near-term supply while suggesting short-term disruption.
Meanwhile, the put-to-call ratio on West Texas Intermediate options roughly halved to 0.35 on Monday from Friday's close, CME data showed, pointing to heavy bullish call buying before rebounding to 0.56 on Tuesday as demand returned for downside protection.
A call option gives the holder the right, but not the obligation, to buy a ÔÇőcrude futures contract at a set price, while a put option gives the right to sell one.
The ratio, meanwhile, compares bearish put options, which profit ÔÇőfrom declines, with bullish call options that benefit from rising prices to gauge market sentiment.
"Dealers are already short a meaningful amount of these deep out-of-the-money calls, creating a more ÔÇînegative gamma Ôüáprofile in crude," said Rebecca Babin, senior energy trader at CIBC Private Wealth US. That contrasts with the more typical environment where dealers are long gamma and sell into rallies.
Gamma shows how much an option's sensitivity to the futures price (delta) will change if the market moves.
Much of the 2027 Brent strip still trades below $70 a barrel, a sign markets are not yet pricing a structural shift in long-term supply, Babin said.
Producers have also used the rally to hedge ÔÇőforward output, creating natural selling pressure on ÔÇőlonger-dated volatility, she added.
Risk premiums Ôüáremain concentrated at the front of the futures curve, reinforcing the view that traders still see the disruption as temporary, said Darrell E. Fletcher, managing director of commodities at trading firm Bannockburn Capital Markets.
Brent options open interest plunged in late ÔÇőFebruary before rebounding in early March, suggesting traders unwound positions before rebuilding hedges.
Front-month open interest fell from around ÔÇő388,000 contracts on Feb. Ôüá18 to roughly 73,000 by Feb. 27, before surging to more than 700,000 contracts on March 2 as fresh positions were established.
"The open interest data seems like a pretty clear tell that it was a sharp unwind in positioning and not a structural repricing. The front end is clearly closing a trade while the Ôüáback end is ÔÇőwhat to pay attention to,ÔÇŁ Kinsella said.
Futures positioning shows a similar pattern, with more than ÔÇő40% of open interest concentrated in April through July expiries and thinner positioning further out the curve, according to CME data.
(Join GMF, a chat room hosted on LSEG Messenger for live interviews: https://lseg.group/3KFHrhe , opens new tab )

item.title: US drillers add oil, gas rigs for first time in four weeks, Baker Hughes says
item.description:
NEW YORK, March 6 (Reuters) - U.S. energy firms ÔÇîthis week added oil and natural gas rigs for the first time in four weeks, energy services firm Baker Hughes (BKR.O) , opens new tab said in its closely followed report on Friday.
The oil and gas rig count, an early indicator of future output, rose by one ÔÇőto 551 in the week to March 6. , ,
Despite this week's rig increase, Baker Hughes said the ÔÇőtotal count was still down 41 rigs, or 7% below this time last year.
Baker ÔüáHughes said oil rigs rose by four to 411 this week, their highest since early February, while gas ÔÇőrigs fell by two to 132, their lowest since early February.
In the Haynesville Shale in Louisiana and Texas, ÔÇőthe rig count rose by one this week to 53, the highest since May 2023.
In the Marcellus Shale in Pennsylvania, Ohio and West Virginia, the rig count rose by one to 27, the highest since May 2024.
In the Williston Shale in North ÔÇőDakota and Montana, the rig count fell by one to 27, the lowest since January 2022.
In North Dakota, ÔÇőthe rig count fell by one to 25, the lowest since November 2021.
The oil and gas rig count declined by about ÔÇî7% Ôüáin 2025, 5% in 2024, and 20% in 2023 as lower U.S. oil prices prompted energy firms to focus more on boosting shareholder returns and paying down debt rather than increasing output.
Financial services firm TD Cowen said 18 of the 21 exploration and production (E&P) companies it tracks planned to spend about 1% less in capital ÔÇőexpenditures in 2026 than in ÔÇő2025.
That compares with a Ôüádecline of around 4% in 2025, roughly flat year-on-year spending in 2024, and increases of 27% in 2023, 40% in 2022, and 4% in 2021.
With U.S. spot crude ÔÇőprices expected to fall for the fourth year in a row in 2026, ÔÇőthe U.S. Energy ÔüáInformation Administration (EIA) projected crude output would hold at 13.6 million barrels per day (bpd) in 2026, matching 2025's record high.
The EIA, however, made that oil price projection before the U.S.-Iran war. Since then, oil prices have spiked to their highest Ôüásince 2023.
While ÔÇőon the gas side, EIA projected output would rise from a ÔÇőrecord 107.6 billion cubic feet per day (bcfd) in 2025 to 110.0 bcfd in 2026 with spot prices at the Henry Hub benchmark in ÔÇőLouisiana forecast to jump by about 22% in 2026.

item.title: South Bow says US, Canada policy shift boosts prospects for Keystone XL revival
item.description:
CALGARY, March 6 (Reuters) - The company ÔÇőbehind a plan to revive the Keystone XL pipeline system to increase Canadian oil exports to ÔÇîthe United States is hopeful a North American policy shift in favour of energy development will shelter its project from the political and regulatory hurdles that led to its cancellation in 2021.
CEO Bevin Wirzba of Canadian pipeline operator South Bow said Friday the world ÔÇőhas changed since U.S. President Joe Biden canceled the permit for Keystone XL after years of Indigenous ÔÇőand environmental opposition.
"The policy environment in North America has been far more constructive," Wirzba said. ÔüáHe said the current war in Iran as well as the ongoing conflict in Ukraine emphasize the need ÔÇőfor energy security.
"Those realities are a great backdrop for us to provide maybe a solution," Wirzba said on a ÔÇőconference call.
South Bow, which was set up by former Keystone XL proponent TC Energy in 2024 to take over its oil pipeline business, is considering reviving some of the line that was already built in Alberta and already has all necessary Canadian permits.
The proposal is ÔÇőnot identical to Keystone XL, as the U.S. segment of the pipeline would be built not by South Bow, ÔÇőbut by U.S. partners. The full route and details of how the various segments would come together remain unknown, but the ÔÇîgoal is Ôüáto ultimately transport up to 55,000 bpd of Canadian oil to the U.S. Gulf Coast.
The company's plan comes as U.S. President Donald Trump has sought to speed energy project permitting times and boost domestic oil and gas production. A presidential permit would be required for the proposed new pipeline to ÔÇőcross the Canada-U.S. border.
In Canada, ÔÇőPrime Minister Mark Carney Ôüáis depending on the energy sector to help boost the country's economy, and has pledged to speed permitting times in addition to dropping some climate legislation that the oil industry said ÔÇőwas impeding its growth.
Carney's moves thus far have materially moved the needle in favour ÔÇőof considering a Ôüápipeline development, Wirzba said. He said South Bow has launched a formal open season process aimed at gauging commercial interest in new pipeline space, and more broadly, gauging the Canadian oil industry's confidence in its ability to grow production in the Ôüácoming ÔÇőyears.
Wirzba declined to say what its proposed project ÔÇö which South Bow is ÔÇőcalling the "Prairie Connector" pipeline ÔÇö would cost or when construction might start.
But he said many of Canada's oil sands companies have indicated they plan ÔÇőto increase output materially over the next three to five years.

item.title: Brazil's Petrobras raises possibility of extra dividends as oil prices surge
item.description:
RIO DE JANEIRO, March 6 (Reuters) - Brazil's state-run oil company Petrobras (PETR3.SA) , opens new tab could distribute extra dividends this year if ÔÇőcash flow is high, as oil prices surge while its operations ÔÇîhave so far been unaffected by the Middle-East U.S.-Iran conflict, executives told analysts on Friday.
Petrobras will need to be ready for variations in oil prices, CEO Magda Chambriard said, adding ÔÇőthat the company will not pass the sudden swings onto Brazilian consumers after attacks ÔÇőby the U.S. and Israel in Iran disrupted global energy flows earlier this week.
"We're living in Ôüáa time of high geopolitical instability, where our concern is to leave the ÔÇőcompany prepared for any scenario that may occur regarding the price of oil," ÔÇőChambriard said.
In 2025, Petrobras did not distribute extraordinary dividends, as lower Brent oil prices made it difficult for the firm to generate surplus cash flow. But Petrobras could do so this ÔÇőyear, if the sharp upturn in prices remains, said CFO Fernando Melgarejo.
"I would ÔÇőlove to submit an extraordinary dividend offering, provided we are certain that it will not impact ÔÇîthe Ôüáfinancing of our projects," said Melgarejo, adding that Petrobras will remain cautious in its decision-making due to the unstable scenario.
In the short-term Petrobras has benefited from the conflict, said Petrobras' head of logistics, commercialization and markets, Claudio Schlosser, as it ÔÇőships mainly to China, ÔÇőIndia and Europe, Ôüáregions far from the conflict zone.
"We've seen better margins," he said. "In a way, there is an increase in value and ÔÇőan interesting positioning for the company."
Oil prices have hit highs ÔÇőin the Ôüáwake of the conflict, with Brent hitting $90 per barrel for the first time since April 2024 and on track for its strongest weekly gain since the volatility seen Ôüáduring ÔÇőthe COVID-19 pandemic in the spring of 2020.
On Thursday ÔÇőPetrobras posted results for 2025 with record exports offsetting a drop in Brent oil prices for the year.

item.title: Hezbollah saw new war with Israel as inevitable and rearmed for months, sources say
item.description:
BEIRUT, March 5 (Reuters) - Lebanese armed group Hezbollah spent months restocking its arsenal of rockets and drones, using support from Iran and its own weapons factories to prepare for a new war with Israel, six sources familiar with the group's preparations said.
Down but not ÔÇőout after its devastating 2024 conflict with Israel, Hezbollah had concluded that another round of fighting was inevitable - and that this time, it could face an existential threat, according to the sources.
Reuters spoke ÔÇîto three Lebanese sources briefed on Hezbollah's activities, two foreign officials in Lebanon and an Israeli military official, who all spoke on condition of anonymity as they were not authorised to speak to the press.
The details of Hezbollah's recent efforts to rearm have not been previously reported.
The head of Hezbollah's media office, Youssef al-Zein, told Reuters that Hezbollah would not comment on its military operations, though he said the group had decided to "fight to the last breath."
Founded by Iran's Revolutionary Guards in 1982, Hezbollah launched rockets and drones at Israel on Monday to ÔÇőavenge the death of Iran's Supreme Leader Ali Khamenei, pulling Lebanon into the war raging across the Middle East.
Although the decision caught some of its own officials off guard , Hezbollah had been readying its military stockpiles and its command-and-control ÔÇőstructure for an eventual rematch with Israel, the six sources said.
To do so, it had drawn on a monthly budget of $50 million, most of it from Iran and earmarked Ôüáfor fighters' salaries, according to one of the Lebanese sources, who has been briefed on the group's finances and military activities. One of the foreign officials confirmed the $50 million budget.
It was not immediately clear how long the group had been ÔÇőrelying on that monthly budget and how it compared to its previous financial resources.
The group has said funds from Iran helped finance rents for people displaced by the 2024 war. Around 60,000 Lebanese, most of them from the Shi'ite Muslim community from which ÔÇőHezbollah draws its popular support, remained displaced over the last year, with their homes still in ruins.
Hezbollah had also worked to replenish its drone and rocket stashes through local manufacturing, the first Lebanese source, the foreign officials and the Israeli military official said. The Israeli military official said Hezbollah had used Iranian funding both to smuggle arms and make its own weapons, but added that its manufacturing capability had been diminished.
The second foreign official said the group had stationed new rockets and Iranian-made logistical materials in southern Lebanon before the latest war began.
Hezbollah's ÔÇőmedia office did not immediately respond to questions on its rearmament and Iranian support for it.
Israeli military spokesperson Lieutenant Colonel Nadav Shoshani told Reuters that Hezbollah "had a lot of arms left" and was also seeking to rearm. "They were trying to ÔÇősmuggle and we were preventing that," Shoshani said.
In 2024, a punishing two-month war with Israel ended with a ceasefire brokered by the United States. Hezbollah halted its attacks on Israel, which continued strikes on what it said were Hezbollah's efforts ÔÇîto rebuild military Ôüácapabilities.
Israel also kept troops in five hilltop positions in southern Lebanon.
Last year, Lebanon also began confiscating Hezbollah weapons in the country's south - but Israel said the group was rearming faster than it was being disarmed.
Speaking to Reuters weeks before Hezbollah entered the regional war, the first Lebanese source confirmed that the group had been rebuilding its military capabilities "in parallel" with Israel's campaign to destroy them.
The pace of Hezbollah's attacks this week provides clues about its weapons stocks.
The group launched 60 drones and rockets on March 2, the first day it attacked Israel, and a similar number the following day, said the second foreign official, who tracks Hezbollah's activities closely.
But on March 4, Hezbollah launched more than double that number of projectiles, a sign it had been ÔÇőable to draw from its larger caches, the official said.
ALMA, ÔÇőan Israeli think tank that monitors security on Israel's Ôüánorthern border, said it assessed that Hezbollah's arsenal on the eve of its attack included approximately 25,000 rockets and missiles, most of them short- and medium-range.
A video published by Hezbollah on March 4 showed a fighter setting up a drone in a wooded area. Riad Kahwaji, a Dubai-based defence analyst and founder of the Institute for Near East and Gulf Military ÔÇőAnalysis, identified the drone in the video as a Shahed-101, which he told Reuters could be produced locally.
Hezbollah has also dispatched fighters from its ÔÇőelite Radwan force back to Ôüásouthern Lebanon, Reuters reported this week . They had been withdrawn from the area after the 2024 conflict.
Israeli strikes after the 2024 ceasefire included the targeting of what Israel said were Hezbollah training camps. In late February, the Israeli military said it struck eight military compounds used by the Radwan force to store weapons and prepare for a confrontation.
The Israeli official and the first foreign official said Hezbollah had been struggling to recruit new operatives as a result.
The group lost 5,000 fighters in the 2024 war, an unprecedented blow Ôüáto its fighting ÔÇőforce, though the second Lebanese source said it still had some 95,000 fighters left.
In the lead-up to its entry into the current regional war, ÔÇőHezbollah had become convinced Israel would carry out a major strike on the group that would seek to "disable its ability to retaliate," the first Lebanese source said.
A third foreign official familiar with Hezbollah's thinking said that assessment had driven the group's decision to launch the first salvo, fearing Israel would eventually ÔÇőturn its attention from Iran to Hezbollah.
"They knew they were next on the list," the official said.

item.title: US files complaints for $15.3 million in proceeds allegedly linked to Iranian oil network
item.description:
March 6 (Reuters) - The United States on Friday filed two civil ÔÇőforfeiture complaints for more ÔÇîthan $15.3 million used to allegedly fund an illicit Iranian oil ÔÇődistribution network, the Justice ÔÇőDepartment said in a statement.
The Ôüácomplaints by the Justice ÔÇőDepartment allege that the funds ÔÇőwere being used by Mohammad Hossein Shamkhani to "operate multiple distribution companies" ÔÇőengaged in selling and ÔÇőshipping Iranian oil and other commodities in ÔÇîviolation Ôüáof U.S. sanctions.
Hossein Shamkhani is the son of Ali Shamkhani, it said, a ÔÇőtop adviser ÔÇőto Ôüáthe former Supreme Leader of Iran Ayatollah ÔÇőAli Khamenei. Both Ali ÔÇőShamkhani Ôüáand Ali Khamenei were killed in the U.S. and ÔüáIsraeli strikes ÔÇőagainst Iran ÔÇőlast week.

item.title: Swedish coast guard boards suspected stateless ship in the Baltic Sea
item.description:
March 6 (Reuters) - The Swedish coast guard said it boarded and took control of a suspected stateless vessel in Swedish ÔÇőwaters of the Baltic Sea on Friday, and it ÔÇîwas carrying out investigations on the ship.
"At 3:50 p.m. today, the Coast Guard took control of a vessel with unclear flag ÔÇőstatus and thus suspected of being a stateless vessel," ÔÇőit said in a statement, adding that the Ôüáboarding had taken place off the coast of the ÔÇősouthern Swedish town Trelleborg.
The ship Caffa was sailing under a ÔÇőGuinean flag, but the coast guard said it regarded the vessel to be stateless "based on national and international legislation". Swedish police said the ÔÇőship was suspected of sailing under a false flag.
According ÔÇőto ship-tracking service Marine Traffic, Caffa is a 96-meter general cargo ÔÇîship.
Sweden's Ôüáminister for civil defence, Carl-Oskar Bohlin, said in a post on X that the ship was a freighter on Ukraine's sanctions list.
"The ownership structure is unclear and there are ÔÇősuspicions that the ÔÇővessel is Ôüánot insured. As recently as this summer, the ship is said to have changed from ÔÇőa Russian to a Guinean flag," Bohlin ÔÇőwrote.
The coast Ôüáguard said it had launched a preliminary investigation into suspected violation of maritime law regarding lack of seaworthiness.
"The Coast Guard Ôüáhas personnel ÔÇőon board to gather information ÔÇőabout the condition of the vessel and crew, conduct searches, and conduct interviews," ÔÇőit added.

item.title: Ghana peacekeeping battalion hit by missile attacks in Lebanon, Ghana army says
item.description:
ACCRA, March 6 (Reuters) - The headquarters of Ghana's United ÔÇőNations peacekeeping battalion in Lebanon ÔÇîwas hit by missile attacks on Friday, leaving two soldiers critically ÔÇőinjured, Ghana's armed forces ÔÇősaid in a statement.
Lebanon was pulled Ôüáinto the war in the ÔÇőMiddle East on Monday, when Hezbollah ÔÇőfired rockets at Israel that ignited a new Israeli offensive against the group.
Ghanaian ÔÇősoldiers are deployed as part ÔÇőof the UNIFIL mission in Lebanon.
The statement ÔÇîfrom ÔüáGhana's armed forces did not say who fired the missiles, but it said Ghana's foreign ÔÇőministry ÔÇőhad protested Ôüáthe incident at U.N. headquarters in New York.
In ÔÇőaddition to the two ÔÇőcritically Ôüáinjured soldiers, the statement said that another soldier "has been traumatised" Ôüáwhile ÔÇőthe officers' mess ÔÇőfacility was hit and burned down.

item.title: Germany pulls further troops out of Middle East
item.description:
BERLIN, March 6 (Reuters) - Germany has pulled additional Bundeswehr troops out of the Middle East, ÔÇőa military spokesman told Reuters on Friday, ÔÇîthe seventh day of a U.S.ÔÇôIsraeli war on Iran that has convulsed the region.
Soldiers deployed ÔÇőwith the UNIFIL mission in Lebanon ÔÇőare being withdrawn due to the security Ôüásituation, the spokesperson said after the ÔÇőBundeswehr already sharply reduced its presence in Erbil ÔÇőin northern Iraq.
The RND newspaper network, which first reported the news, said that German troops stationed ÔÇőin Bahrain had already returned home, and ÔÇőpreparations for withdrawals from Kuwait were underway. The spokesperson ÔÇîdeclined Ôüáto comment on this.
The RND report also said that soldiers and staff from the German embassy in Baghdad were being ÔÇőrelocated to Jordan. ÔÇőThe Ôüáforeign ministry did not respond to a request for comment from ÔÇőReuters on the move.
Roughly 500 German ÔÇősoldiers Ôüáare deployed across the region, mainly in Iraq and Jordan. Officials have recently moved Ôüápersonnel ÔÇőout of camps and ÔÇőreduced some contingents amid heightened security risks.

item.title: Barclays says Brent could test $120/bbl if Middle East tensions persist
item.description:
March 6 (Reuters) - Barclays said on Friday that Brent crude could potentially test $120 a barrel if the Middle ÔÇőEast conflict persists for another couple of weeks.
"These numbers ÔÇîmight seem too high, especially given widespread pessimism about the oil market outlook heading into this year, but we reiterate that fundamentals are stronger ÔÇőand risks are bigger than the Russia-Ukraine conflict , when we ÔÇősaw these levels materialize," Barclays added.
Oil prices have jumped Ôüásharply as the widening U.S.-Israeli conflict with Iran has effectively closed ÔÇőthe Strait of Hormuz, constraining Middle East supplies.
Shipping through the Strait ÔÇőof Hormuz, which carries about a fifth of global oil and oil and liquefied natural gas, has been disrupted after Iran threatened to fire on ÔÇőpassing vessels.
Brent crude futures were trading around $93.60 per barrel and ÔÇőWest Texas Intermediate were at $91.62 as of 1857 GMT.
Barclays said oil volumes stranded ÔÇîon Ôüátankers in the Middle East Gulf have risen by about 85 million barrels since the conflict began, adding that risks to oil prices remain skewed to the upside.
U.S. President Donald Trump demanded ÔÇőIran's "unconditional surrender" on ÔÇőFriday, a dramatic Ôüáescalation of his demands a week into the war he launched alongside Israel, which could make ÔÇőit more difficult to negotiate a swift end ÔÇőto hostilities.
"Production shut-ins Ôüáin Iraq and Kuwait are already happening and might spread to UAE and even Saudi Arabia over time," Barclays said.
Barclays said Ôüáthe ÔÇőfarÔÇĹend 10% scenario now implies Brent could ÔÇőhit $150 a barrel before the end of the month.

item.title: Trump to forge ahead with immigration crackdown driven by top aide Stephen Miller
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 6 (Reuters) - U.S. President Donald Trump is expected to forge ahead with his aggressive immigration crackdown driven by top aide Stephen Miller and a new homeland secretary nominee who shares Trump's ÔÇőhardline view, current and former U.S. officials and lawmakers said.
The Republican president fired Homeland Security Secretary Kristi Noem on Thursday after months of controversy over heavy-handed immigration enforcement tactics ÔÇîand lawmaker questions about government contracts and turmoil within her department.
But Miller - the White House deputy chief of staff and the architect of TrumpÔÇÖs immigration agenda - retains control of the issue, three U.S. officials said. And TrumpÔÇÖs pick to become homeland secretary, U.S. Senator Markwayne Mullin of Oklahoma, is viewed as closely aligned with TrumpÔÇÖs approach.
ÔÇťStephen is a survivor,ÔÇŁ one of the officials said, requesting anonymity to discuss internal dynamics. The official said NoemÔÇÖs firing was not a referendum on Trump's restrictive ÔÇőagenda, but rather on the execution of it.
In response to a request for comment, a White House official said Miller helps coordinate a wide range of issues - from immigration to counter-cartel ÔÇőoperations - and is "working to ensure the President's policy agenda is implemented."
The Department of Homeland Security and Mullin's office did not respond to requests for comment.
Trump won Ôüáback the White House in 2024 with a campaign that heavily focused on stopping illegal immigration and ramping up deportations. Immigration was one of Trump's best-polling issues after returning to office - and a key ÔÇőfocus for the Republican Party broadly. But amid backlash over his aggressive enforcement in U.S. cities, support for his immigration approach declined in recent months , Reuters/Ipsos polls show.
Republicans currently hold narrow majorities in both chambers of ÔÇőthe U.S. Congress but are heading into midterm elections in November that threaten their control on Washington.
White House spokesperson Karoline Leavitt said on Thursday in an X post related to NoemÔÇÖs dismissal that TrumpÔÇÖs immigration goals remained unchanged.
ÔÇťPresident TrumpÔÇÖs immigration agenda is keeping our border secure and deporting illegal alien criminals from our country, and it will continue without interruption,ÔÇŁ she said.
Under NoemÔÇÖs leadership, DHS sent thousands of federal immigration agents to Los Angeles, Chicago, Minneapolis and other Democratic-led cities to seek ÔÇőout immigration offenders, sweeping through residential neighborhoods and chasing day laborers in Home Depot parking lots.
But after federal agents shot and killed two U.S. citizens in Minneapolis - Renee Good and Alex Pretti - the administration said it ÔÇőwould move to a less public-facing ÔÇťtargetedÔÇŁ approach to immigration arrests.
Current and former federal immigration officials said there had been no immediate policy shift since NoemÔÇÖs departure was announced, but several said they expected the administration to continue to avoid ÔÇîhigh-profile surges into ÔüáU.S. cities following the fallout in Minneapolis.
Mullin, 48, became a senator in 2023 after a decade in the U.S. House of Representatives. Like Noem, he is a rancher and owns a cattle ranch in Oklahoma that also serves as a wedding venue, among other businesses.
Even as a freshman, Mullin has stood out from the crowd of 100 senators, at times presiding over the Senate wearing a large, light-colored cowboy hat.
He had a short-lived career as a mixed martial arts fighter and garnered widespread media attention in 2023 when he rose from his chair during a Senate hearing to challenge Teamsters President ÔÇőSean O'Brien to "stand your butt up" and fight.
Mullin voted ÔÇőfor a Republican-backed funding package last year Ôüáthat devoted a historic $170 billion to immigration enforcement through September 2029 and backs core elements of TrumpÔÇÖs immigration platform.
Republican lawmakers praised Mullin after the news that Trump would nominate him for DHS secretary, a position that requires majority confirmation in the Senate.
ÔÇťHe's strong on the border and that's what we need,ÔÇŁ Republican Senator Eric ÔÇőSchmitt of Missouri told reporters on Thursday. ÔÇťThe deportations will continue."
Noem was sharply criticized by both Democrats and Republicans for swiftly saying Good and Pretti were ÔÇőengaged in ÔÇťdomestic terrorismÔÇŁ after they Ôüáwere killed and before a full investigation.
Mullin similarly portrayed Pretti as a threat despite video evidence that undercut that claim. He said Pretti was "a deranged individual" who had a loaded pistol and intended "to cause max damage," during an interview with Fox News on January 24, the day Pretti was killed.
Democrats on Capitol Hill welcomed NoemÔÇÖs removal but said the Trump administrationÔÇÖs approach to immigration enforcement still needed to change. Democrats have blocked funding for DHS Ôüásince mid-February in ÔÇőa push to force the White House to moderate its tactics.
Senator Chris Murphy, a Democrat from Connecticut, said funding talks are ÔÇőbeing run out of the White House and that he does not expect NoemÔÇÖs firing to break the stalemate.
ÔÇťI think weÔÇÖre better off without her, but she wasnÔÇÖt running the department,ÔÇŁ Murphy said on Thursday. ÔÇťStephen Miller runs that department and will continue to ÔÇőrun the department so I donÔÇÖt really think much will change.ÔÇŁ

item.title: Israeli military says unintentionally struck UN agency truck in Gaza
item.description:
TEL AVIV, March 6 (Reuters) - Israel's military said on Friday that a "firing component" launched by its navy unintentionally struck a fuel ÔÇőtruck belonging to a United Nations agency in Gaza ÔÇîthe previous day, an incident that prompted the agency to publicly call for a full investigation.
The United Nations Office for Project Services, ÔÇőwhich oversees fuel distribution in Gaza, said that the ÔÇőempty fuel truck was struck on Thursday around Ôüá5 a.m. from the direction of the sea, causing damage ÔÇőto the vehicle. There were no injuries.
"Our teams are taking ÔÇőextraordinary risks every day to keep humanitarian operations and life-sustaining services running," UNOPS Executive Director Jorge Moreira da Silva said in a statement, ÔÇőcalling for an investigation into the incident.
"They should not ÔÇőhave to do that under fire," he said.
In response to Reuters questions, ÔÇîthe ÔüáIsraeli military said that the incident occurred during defensive naval activity, and that a firing component deviated from its intended trajectory.
The fuel truck sustained "minor damage", the military said in a ÔÇőstatement. The military ÔÇődid not Ôüásay what type of munitions had been fired, or what had been the navy's intended ÔÇőtarget.
"The incident was reviewed, and lessons were learned ÔÇőaccordingly," Ôüáit said, without providing further details.
The fuel truck had been on its way to the Kerem Shalom crossing when it was Ôüástruck, ÔÇőand the truck's movements had been ÔÇőcoordinated with Israeli authorities in advance, UNOPS said.

item.title: Private credit woes come for Larry FinkÔÇÖs legacy
item.description:
NEW YORK, March 6 (Reuters Breakingviews) - Rising disquiet in private credit is uncomfortable for a lot of Wall Street titans, but especially for Larry Fink. The BlackRock (BLK.N) , opens new tab boss moved aggressively to transform his firm, the kingpin of public-markets investing for ÔÇőthe masses, into a private-markets rival to the likes of Blackstone (BX.N) , opens new tab or Apollo Global Management (APO.N) , opens new tab . The timing now looks awkward, as worries ÔÇîabout overheated lending mount and a key retail fund caps investor withdrawals. FinkÔÇÖs legacy project , and his companyÔÇÖs strengths, will be tested.
As part of a $28 billion string of deals to move into private markets, BlackRock agreed to acquire non-bank lender HPS Investment Partners in 2024. Included in that $12 billion acquisition was HPS Corporate Lending Partners, or HLEND, a nontraded business development ÔÇőcompany (BDC). Such funds target affluent individual investors, allowing them to withdraw their cash up to a set cap, typically of 5% of shares per ÔÇőquarter. According to a shareholder letter , opens new tab , HLEND received requests totaling 9.3%, but will only offer redemptions up to its limit, Ôüáas it was designed to do.
This is far from the first hiccup in private credit. A Blue Owl (OWL.N) , opens new tab fund barred regular withdrawals, instead selling assets to raise ÔÇőcash and return a huge chunk at once. To satisfy elevated redemptions at BCRED, the largest nontraded BDC around, Blackstone employees and the firm itself put in ÔÇőa combined $400 million to ensure all requests were covered.
HLEND, though, is the first BDC of its kind to actually prorate redemptions, though others are expected to end up following suit, according to Kevin Gannon of investment bank RA Stanger. Turning theoretical fine-print restrictions into reality may well annoy, and possibly frighten, customers.
When BlackRock bought HPS, private credit was running red-hot. ÔÇőAssets under management in direct lending funds alone had climbed to $889 billion, according to Preqin data. Apollo and its ilk saw an addressable market in ÔÇőthe tens of trillions of dollars by expanding into IOUs secured by specific assets. These strategies are increasingly pitched as a fit for the kind of mom-and-pop savers that made ÔÇîBlackRock a Ôüácolossus, with $14 trillion of assets under management.
The company got there in part by inking perhaps the most successful deal in modern asset management history, snapping up BarclaysÔÇÖ BGI in 2009 for $13.5 billion and riding then-explosive growth in exchange traded funds. If anyone has a track record of cracking new markets and reaching the retail masses through M&A, itÔÇÖs Larry Fink.
His public-markets business is still immensely successful, and HLEND is only one small piece of a larger plan. Still, rivals ÔÇőlike Blackstone are increasingly trying to eat ÔÇőBlackRock's lunch by selling retail products. ÔüáMeanwhile, from Apollo boss Marc Rowan to JPMorgan CEO Jamie Dimon, warnings of a coming private credit , opens new tab shakeout are rising. As HLENDÔÇÖs investor letter put it, disruptive periods breed new opportunities. BlackRockÔÇÖs challenge is to prove it can capitalize on ÔÇőthem.
Follow Jonathan Guilford on X , opens new tab and LinkedIn , opens new tab .

item.title: Iran's UN envoy says 1,332 Iranian civilians killed in war
item.description:
March 6 (Reuters) - Iran's U.N. ambassador ÔÇőAmir Saeid Iravani ÔÇîon Friday said at ÔÇőleast 1,332 ÔÇőIranian civilians had Ôüálost their ÔÇőlives so ÔÇőfar in the conflict with Israel ÔÇőand the ÔÇőUnited States and ÔÇîthousands Ôüámore had been injured.
He made his comments ÔÇőwhile ÔÇőspeaking Ôüáto reporters at ÔÇőthe United ÔÇőNations Ôüáin New York.

item.title: EU countries joining US, Israel attacks would become Iran's "legitimate target", Deputy Minister says
item.description:
PARIS, March 6 (Reuters) - European Union member states that join the U.S. and ÔÇőIsrael attacks on Iran would ÔÇîbecome "legitimate targets" for Iran, Deputy Foreign Minister Majid Takht-Ravanchi said in an interview ÔÇőwith TV station France 24.
"Any ÔÇőcountry that joins in the Ôüáaggression against Iran, joins America and ÔÇőIsrael in the aggression against ÔÇőIran, definitely, they will be also legitimate targets for Iran retaliation," Takht-Ravanchi said.

item.title: UK's Starmer discusses military, intelligence support with Saudi crown prince
item.description:
March 6 (Reuters) - British Prime Minister Keir Starmer spoke ÔÇőto Saudi Arabia Crown ÔÇîPrince Mohammed bin Salman after Iran's strikes ÔÇőon the kingdom ÔÇőamid a sprawling conflict in the Ôüáregion, Downing Street ÔÇősaid in a statement , opens new tab on ÔÇőFriday.
"Additional British fighter jets, helicopters and a destroyer were ÔÇőbeing sent to ÔÇőthe region, and the UK ÔÇîstood Ôüáready to support the defence of the Kingdom of Saudi ÔÇőArabia should ÔÇőit Ôüábe needed," Starmer told bin ÔÇőSalman.
The two leaders ÔÇőalso Ôüádiscussed greater intelligence cooperation in support of Ôüádefensive ÔÇőoperations and civilians.

item.title: US, Ecuador bomb drug trafficker camp near Colombia border, militaries say
item.description:
WASHINGTON/QUITO, March 6 (Reuters) - The U.S. and Ecuador carried out a ÔÇőjoint operation targeting drug trafficking operations in the South American ÔÇîcountry, authorities in both countries said on Friday, with the U.S. calling the move "lethal kinetic operations."
Neither the U.S. Southern Command, a branch of its military that oversees forces ÔÇőin Latin America, nor Ecuador's defense ministry, said if anyone ÔÇőwas killed or captured in the strike, which Ecuador dubbed Ôüáoperation "Total Extermination."
The operations used helicopters, aircraft, river boats and drones to ÔÇőlocate and bomb a drug traffickers' training camp in north-east Ecuador near ÔÇőthe Colombian border, Ecuador's defense ministry said in a statement.
The camp belonged to the Comandos de la Frontera (CDF), a Colombian crime group made up of FARC dissidents, and ÔÇőhad a capacity for 50 people, it added.
Ecuadorean President Daniel Noboa ÔÇőhas made a military crackdown on organised crime a cornerstone of his administration, and ÔÇîhis Ôüágovernment imposed tariffs on its larger neighbor Colombia, accusing it of not doing enough to fight drug trafficking.
He is set to travel to Miami this weekend to take part in the Trump administration's "Shield of the Americas" ÔÇősummit, which brings ÔÇőtogether many right-wing Ôüáleaders across the region with a focus on regional security and organized crime .
"The United States is a key ally ÔÇőin this fight," the defense ministry said.
"At the ÔÇőrequest of ÔüáEcuador, the Department of War executed targeted action to advance our shared objective of dismantling narco-terrorist networks," Pentagon spokesman Sean Parnell wrote on X.
The Ôüáoperation followed ÔÇőa similar U.S-Ecuadorean operation announced by the ÔÇőU.S. Southern Command earlier this week .

item.title: US skips congressional review to approve munitions sale to Israel
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 6 (Reuters) - The U.S. State Department ÔÇősaid on Friday ÔÇîit has made a determination approving a ÔÇőpossible foreign military ÔÇősale to Israel of Ôüámunitions and munitions ÔÇősupport for $151.8 million, without congressional ÔÇőreview.
Israel has requested to buy 12,000 BLU-110A/B general ÔÇőpurpose, 1,000-pound bomb ÔÇőbodies, the State Department said ÔÇîin Ôüáa statement.
Secretary of State Marco Rubio determined that an ÔÇőemergency existed ÔÇőthat Ôüárequired the immediate sale to ÔÇőIsrael. The announcement ÔÇőcomes Ôüáduring the U.S. and Israel's war Ôüáagainst ÔÇőIran.

item.title: US judge dismisses case by deported student who declined flight back
item.description:
BOSTON, March 6 (Reuters) - A U.S. judge who had ordered President Donald Trump 's administration ÔÇőto facilitate the return of a college student it deported to Honduras said on Friday the "sad truth" is ÔÇîthat her decision not to board a plane it arranged back to the United States meant her case must be dismissed.
Any Lucia Lopez Belloza, a 20-year-old freshman at Babson College in Massachusetts, declined on February 27 to board a flight arranged by U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement to bring her back ÔÇőto the United States after the administration said it would try to deport her again if she returned.
Lopez Belloza, ÔÇőwho came to the United States from Honduras when she was 8, has said she was unaware Ôüáshe was subject to a final order of removal that was entered when she was 11.
Her lawyers had urged Boston-based U.S. ÔÇőDistrict Judge Richard Stearns to let Lopez Belloza continue the lawsuit she filed after being detained by immigration authorities at Boston's ÔÇőLogan International Airport in November while traveling to spend the Thanksgiving holiday with her family in Texas.
But Stearns reaffirmed his earlier conclusion that he did not have jurisdiction to hear the case concerning her detention because by the time it was filed on November 21, she had been flown by ÔÇőimmigration authorities to Texas.
His sole remaining basis for jurisdiction would have been to enforce an order issued by another judge just ÔÇőminutes after Lopez Belloza's case was filed barring her from being deported or transferred out of Massachusetts for 72 hours. Lopez Belloza was flown ÔÇîfrom Texas Ôüáto Honduras the next day despite that judicial order.
A U.S. government lawyer later apologized to Stearns for a "mistake" made by a ICE officer who failed to properly alert others in the agency about the existence of the judicial order.
Stearns on February 13 ordered the administration to rectify the error by facilitating her return to the United States. The administration last week said it would do so ÔÇőby having Lopez Belloza board the ÔÇőICE flight from Honduras to ÔüáTexas.
But the administration also said ICE planned , opens new tab to move to deport her again upon arrival and had the authority to detain her. Lopez Belloza said the "nightmare" situation caused her to decline to board ÔÇőthe flight and remain in Honduras.
"The sad truth is that when Any declined the flight she ÔÇőalso waived this Ôüácourt's only remaining basis for jurisdiction," Stearns wrote.
Stearns said had she boarded the plane, the judicial order barring her swift deportation would have remained in effect, giving her "ample opportunity" to file a new case in Texas to challenge her detention.
Todd Pomerleau, Lopez Belloza's lawyer, said Ôüáhe will ÔÇőappeal.

item.title: Iran war enters second week as Trump demands 'unconditional surrender'
item.description:
BEIRUT/WASHINGTON/JERUSALEM, March 7 (Reuters) - The expanding war in Iran entered its second week on Saturday amid renewed uncertainty about how or when hostilities will end, as U.S. President Donald Trump declared he would only accept Tehran's "unconditional surrender" and Israel traded fresh attacks with Iran and Lebanon.
Trump's comments ÔÇőon social media on Friday came hours after Iran's president announced that unnamed countries had begun mediation efforts, briefly raising the possibility, however faint, of a diplomatic resolution a week after ÔÇîthe U.S. and Israel launched strikes on Iran.
"There will be no deal with Iran except UNCONDITIONAL SURRENDER!" Trump wrote. "After that, and the selection of a GREAT & ACCEPTABLE Leader(s), we, and many of our wonderful and very brave allies and partners, will work tirelessly to bring Iran back from the brink of destruction, making it economically bigger, better, and stronger than ever before."
Trump has offered shifting explanations of his war aims, raising the possibility of an extended regional conflict that has already spilled well beyond Iran's borders, shaken global financial markets ÔÇőand sent oil prices soaring.
In response to the attack, Iran has targeted Israel as well as multiple Gulf states that host U.S. military installations.
Inside Israel on Friday, explosions could be heard as Israeli defenses ÔÇőactivated to shoot down incoming Iranian fire. The UAE, Kuwait, Qatar, Bahrain and Saudi Arabia all reported fresh drone and missile attacks.
Meanwhile, Israel pursued a major expansion of the Ôüáwar in Lebanon, pounding the capital Beirut on Friday after ordering an unprecedented evacuation of the city's entire southern suburbs.
Israel also launched a new wave of attacks on Iran, saying 50 of its warplanes had hit a bunker ÔÇőstill being used by Iran's leadership beneath slain Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei's destroyed Tehran compound.
Early on Saturday, Iran's semi-official Tasnim news agency reported that Mehrabad Airport in Tehran had been struck.
There was no immediate comment from Iran's Revolutionary ÔÇőGuard or Hezbollah.
Israel has extended its bombing to Lebanon to root out Hezbollah , the Shi'ite militia allied to Iran that has been a dominant faction in Lebanese politics since the 1980s. Hezbollah fired on Israel this week to avenge the death of Khamenei.
"We're sleeping here in the streets - some in cars, some on the street, some on the beach," said Jamal Seifeddin, 43, who fled Beirut's southern suburbs and spent the night on the streets in the downtown district.
About 300,000 people have been displaced in Lebanon in the past four days, ÔÇőaccording to the Norwegian Refugee Council.
The Israeli military says it has destroyed 80% of Iran's air-defense systems in the first week of the campaign and disabled more than 60% of its missile launchers.
Trump's demand for Iran's surrender, ÔÇőand the likelihood that it would complicate any quick path to ending the conflict, sent European and U.S. stock indexes tumbling on Friday. Oil prices hit their highest prices in years with the critical shipping lane of the Strait of Hormuz effectively ÔÇîclosed down.
Trump Ôüátold Reuters in a telephone interview on Thursday that he must have a say in selecting Iran's new supreme leader to replace Khamenei, killed on the war's first day , a demand he repeated on Friday in a remarkable assertion of power over the country of more than 90 million people.
Iran's U.N. ambassador, Amir Saeid Iravani, told reporters that new leadership would be chosen "in accordance with our constitutional procedures and solely by the will of the Iranian people - without any foreign interference."
Israel has said openly that it aims to overthrow Iran's ruling system. It has been bombing parts of western Iran to support Iranian Kurdish militias who hope to exploit the war to seize towns near the frontier, according to three sources ÔÇőfamiliar with Israel's talks with the factions.
Iran has cast the ÔÇőwar as an unprovoked attack and describes the Ôüákilling of Khamenei as an assassination.
Earlier on Friday, Iran President Masoud Pezeshkian posted on social media: "Some countries have begun mediation efforts." He did not identify the countries or provide further details.
Russia is providing Iran with locations of U.S. warships and aircraft in the Middle East after Iran's ability to locate U.S. forces was degraded, the Washington ÔÇőPost reported, citing three officials familiar with the intelligence.
Russian missions in the U.S. did not immediately respond to requests for comment on the report.
Trump met with executives from ÔÇőseven defense contractors on Friday, who Ôüáhe later said had agreed to accelerate weapons production. The administration has been pressuring contractors as Iran and other recent operations have drawn down supplies.
Karoline Leavitt, a White House spokeswoman, said the U.S. has enough weapons stockpiles to meet the needs of its Iran operations, which she said would take about four to six weeks to complete.
At least 1,332 people have been killed in Iran since the U.S. and Israel launched strikes on February 28, Iravani said, citing the Iranian Red Crescent ÔüáSociety.
The Lebanese health ÔÇőministry has reported 123 people killed and 683 wounded as a result of Israeli attacks. Iranian attacks have killed 11 people in ÔÇőIsrael since the war started, and at least six U.S. service members have been killed.
Two U.S. officials told Reuters that military investigators believed it was likely that U.S. forces were responsible for an apparent strike on an Iranian girls' school that killed scores of children on the first day ÔÇőof the war. The investigators have not yet reached a final conclusion.

item.title: Pakistani convicted of plotting to kill Trump over death of Iran commander
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 6 (Reuters) - A Pakistani man was convicted on Friday of planning to kill President Donald Trump and other prominent U.S. politicians two years ago at the behest ÔÇőof Iran , the Department of Justice said.
Asif Merchant was accused of trying to recruit ÔÇîpeople in the U.S. in a plan targeting Trump and others in retaliation for Washington's killing of Iranian military commander Qassem Soleimani in 2020, when Trump was in his first term.
Targets in the ÔÇő2024 plot also included then-President Joe Biden and Nikki Haley, who ran ÔÇőagainst Trump that year for the Republican presidential nomination, federal prosecutors said.
Merchant Ôüáwas convicted of "murder for hire and attempting to commit an act of terrorism transcending ÔÇőnational boundaries," directed by the Iranian authorities, the DOJ said , opens new tab in a statement.
The trial in ÔÇőthe New York City borough of Brooklyn started last week, days before Trump ordered an assault on Iran, carried out with Israel, that has expanded into the region's biggest war in years.
Merchant admitted to ÔÇőjoining the plot with Iran's elite Islamic Revolutionary Guards Corps but testified he did ÔÇőso unwillingly, to protect his family in Tehran.
Merchant said he was never ordered to kill a ÔÇîspecific person Ôüábut that his Iranian handler named three people in the course of conversations in the Iranian capital.
Law enforcement thwarted the plan before any attack occurred. A person Merchant contacted in April 2024 to help with the plot reported his activities and became a ÔÇőconfidential informant, the DOJ ÔÇősaid. Merchant was arrested Ôüáand pleaded not guilty that year.
The Revolutionary Guards have a central role in Iran, with a combination of military and economic power and ÔÇőan intelligence network. Tehran has denied accusations that it targeted Trump ÔÇőor other ÔüáU.S. officials.
U.S. and Israeli attacks since Saturday have killed at least 1,332 Iranian civilians and wounded thousands, according to Iran's U.N. ambassador. Many top Iranian leaders, including Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali ÔüáKhamenei, ÔÇőhave been killed.
The U.S. military has said six of its service ÔÇőmembers were killed in a strike on a facility in Kuwait, while Israeli tallies show at least 10 ÔÇőcivilians have been killed across Israel.

item.title: Tornadoes kill four, injure more than a dozen in rural┬áMichigan
item.description:
NEW YORK, March 6 (Reuters) - Tornadoes and strong storms killed at least four people and injured more than a dozen in southern Michigan on ÔÇőFriday, destroying multiple homes and leaving hundreds of residents without power, ÔÇîlocal authorities said.
Soon after the National Weather Service issued tornado warnings for the area on Friday, videos on social media claiming to be from the towns of Three ÔÇőRivers and Union City showed massive whirlwinds tearing roofs off buildings ÔÇőand lifting debris into the air.
Local media footage showed smashed buildings Ôüáand cars, and poles, trees and road signs that had been blown ÔÇőover.
Three fatalities and a dozen injuries were reported in Branch County, where Union ÔÇőCity is located, the county's sheriff's office said.
One person was killed and several injured in Cass County, according to local authorities there.
"Multiple large structures - including homes and pole barns - ÔÇősustained damage ranging from major structural impacts to complete destruction," according to ÔÇőa statement on a website for Cass County.
Power outages had affected hundreds of people, local ÔÇîofficials Ôüásaid.
"We have multiple utility poles down, transformers damaged, and long stretches of power lines on the ground. The north side of Union Lake sustained some of the most severe damage, with roughly two miles of line brought ÔÇődown in that area ÔÇőalone," according to Ôüáa posting on Union City's official Facebook page that said restoration may take several days.
Michigan Governor Gretchen Whitmer said ÔÇőshe activated a State Emergency Operations Center for monitoring ÔÇőthe situation.
"The Ôüástate is ready to fulfill any resource requests that may arise. We've yet to receive any just yet. It's sounding like the local response is and Ôüáhas ÔÇőbeen able to address what's been a very ÔÇődevastating afternoon and evening in southwest Michigan," Clayton Cummins, a spokesperson for Michigan emergency services and ÔÇőpolice, told Reuters.

item.title: Iran conflict boosts U.S. Gulf oil prices to highest since 2020
item.description:
NEW YORK, March 6 (Reuters) - U.S. Gulf Coast heavy grades continued to surge on Friday as the Iran conflict spurred several Middle Eastern producers of heavy crude to curtail ÔÇőproduction and drove buyers to scoop up U.S. barrels.
Prices of Mars ÔÇîsour crude, the flagship crude produced in the U.S. Gulf of Mexico and favored by refiners globally, traded at a $11 premium to U.S. benchmark West Texas Intermediate (WTI) crude on Friday, ÔÇőbrokers said. That was the highest since April 2020, and up $4 from ÔÇőThursday.
Just a week ago it traded at a premium of $1.50.
Other heavy grades Ôüásuch as the Heavy Louisiana Sweet and the West Texas Sour also ÔÇőrose.
Benchmark crude oil prices have surged since the initial attacks last week, with Brent ÔÇőcrude settling at $92.69 a barrel, its highest level since October 2023 on Friday.
The effective closure of the Strait of Hormuz has forced several countries, including Iraq, to curb output. The strait is a ÔÇőkey route for medium and heavy sour crude from the Persian Gulf, and ÔÇőthose flows are now largely cut off. Additional production cuts announced in Kuwait on Friday ÔÇîalso Ôüáhelped lift Mars prices, a trader said.
ÔÇťRefiners that rely on these grades will need to find similar, or roughly similar, alternatives to replace the lost barrels, so Mars and other U.S. Gulf sour heavies and mediums are natural substitutes and are ÔÇőgetting bid up ÔÇőaggressively,ÔÇŁ Kpler lead ÔüáAmericas oil analyst Matt Smith said, adding that buyers, especially in Asia, are scrambling for more of these medium and ÔÇőheavy crude barrels.
"This time of year also marks the shift ÔÇőfrom winter Ôüáinto driving season, when demand typically rises across all crude grades," said Tim Snyder, chief economist at Matador Economics, adding that ultimately the supply disruption caused by war Ôüáwas ÔÇődriving prices.
"In the short term we will continue ÔÇőto see these grades rise until we see the Strait of Hormuz open up," Snyder said.

item.title: US pump prices surge as Iran war upends global energy supply
item.description:
MARIETTA/NEW YORK, March 6 (Reuters) - U.S. retail gasoline and diesel prices are soaring as the U.S.-Israel war with Iran constrains oil and fuel exports, which could be a political test for President Donald Trump's Republican Party ahead ÔÇőof midterm elections in November.
Fuel prices jumped more than 10% this week as oil rose above $90 a barrel, its highest in years, adding pain at the ÔÇîpump for consumers already strained by inflation. Trump on Thursday shrugged off higher gasoline prices in an interview with Reuters , opens new tab , saying "if they rise, they rise."
The president had vowed to lower energy prices and unleash U.S. oil and gas drilling during his second term, but much of his tenure has been marked by volatility and uncertainty amid shifts in policies like tariffs and geopolitical turmoil. The U.S. is the world's largest oil producer. It is a major exporter ÔÇőbut also imports millions of barrels a day since it is the world's largest oil consumer.
As of Friday, the national average prices for regular gasoline stood at $3.32 a ÔÇőgallon, up 11% from a week ago and the highest since September 2024, according to data from the motorists association AAA. Diesel was at $4.33, Ôüáup 15% from a week ago, surging to the highest since November 2023.
U.S. motorists in parts of the Midwest and the South, including states that supported Trump, have ÔÇőseen some of the steepest increases in fuel costs since the conflict in Iran started.
In Georgia, a swing state, average retail gasoline prices rose 40.1 cents a gallon over the past week, ÔÇőaccording to fuel tracking site GasBuddy.
Andrenna McDaniel, a healthcare insurance worker in South Fulton, Georgia, said she was surprised to see prices skyrocket overnight.
"They jumped up so quickly," she said on Friday, adding that she does not agree with the war at all.
McDaniel, a Democrat, said that for now she is only driving for the most important things, and feels lucky that she works from home so she does not have to drive as ÔÇőmuch as other people do.
Georgia voted for Donald Trump in the 2024 election.
Trump voter Richard Soule, 69, a U.S. Air Force veteran and a retired firefighter, said a little pain at the pump ÔÇőis worth Trump's efforts to protect America.
"When President Trump went in there and bombed out their nuclear, and they just thumbed their nose at it, I believe he did the right thing at the right ÔÇîtime," Soule said Ôüáon Friday as he filled up his Ford F-150 truck in Marietta, Georgia.
Other states, including Indiana and West Virginia have seen prices rise by 44.3 cents and 43.9 cents, respectively.
More pain may be on the way, analysts said, as oil prices continue to trend upward. On Friday, U.S. oil futures settled at $90.90 a barrel, up nearly $10 and the biggest single-day rise since April 2020.
"Given current market conditions, the national average price of gasoline could climb toward $3.50 to $3.70 per gallon in the coming days if oil continues rising and supply disruptions persist," GasBuddy analyst Patrick De ÔÇőHaan said.
The disruptions in the Middle East and ÔÇőthe Strait of Hormuz, a key trade Ôüáconduit, have boosted demand for U.S. oil abroad, which in turn has driven up prices for domestic refiners too.
"The U.S. has weaned itself off of its dependence on Middle Eastern crude, but obviously Asian refineries, and to a lesser extent, European refineries have not," Denton Cinquegrana, chief oil ÔÇőanalyst with OPIS. "That's what you're seeing happen in the spot market, because the demand for U.S. exports rise, and so the price rise."
Seasonal ÔÇőfactors could add further Ôüápressure. Gasoline prices typically go up in the spring and peak in the summer due to higher gasoline demand and production of summer-blend gasoline, which is more costly to produce.
Diesel fuel saw an even more aggressive jump since Iran began retaliating against U.S. and Israeli strikes, significantly disrupting shipping in the Strait of Hormuz.
Global diesel inventories have remained in tight supply due to heavy demand for heating and power generation Ôüáduring a ÔÇőprolonged winter in the U.S. and other parts of the world and a structural tightness of refining capacity.
Sticker prices ÔÇőof everything from food to furniture go up when the cost of diesel goes up, as the fuel is mainly used in freight transportation, manufacturing, agriculture, and global shipping, analysts said.
"In a world where buzzword seems to be 'affordability', that is ÔÇőcertainly not going to help," Cinquegrana said.

item.title: World's largest urban coffee plantation welcomes new trees in Brazil
item.description:
SAO PAULO, March 6 (Reuters) - The world's largest urban coffee ÔÇőplantation, located in Brazil's sprawling city of Sao Paulo, this week welcomed some ÔÇî1,500 new coffee plants as researchers prepare to study their capacity to resist climate change and pests.
Sao Paulo's Biological Institute was established in 1927 with the mission of tackling a crisis caused by pests ÔÇőlike the coffee berry-borer beetle which devour the beans hidden inside coffee cherries.
Brazil ÔÇőis the world's top producer of arabica coffee, as well as the Ôüásecond-biggest producer of canephora coffees, which include varieties like robusta and conilon.
The plantation in ÔÇőSao Paulo's Vila Mariana neighborhood, which already boasted more than 2,000 coffee plants, welcomed arabica ÔÇővarieties this week billed as resistant to pests and coffee rust, a type of fungus, as well as other plants that are more tolerant of drought-like conditions.
"The Biological Institute was created to control the ÔÇőcoffee berry-borer (which) was controlled using parasitoids, a biological control method," institute researcher and agricultural engineer ÔÇőHarumi Hojo told Reuters on Thursday.
In her hand, Hojo held two coffee cherries, showing the difference between ÔÇîa Ôüáhealthy fruit with smooth white coffee beans and another rotten on the inside after being devoured by a beetle.
Over the years, the institute began to investigate other factors affecting coffee plants, like soil and climate, and now its range of varieties grown side by side ÔÇőunder the same conditions ÔÇőshows how different Ôüáplants handle pests, disease and climate pressures, Hojo said.
Arabica coffee plants are also sensitive to hotter and drier weather caused by climate ÔÇőchange, and 300 of the new plants at the institute's plantation ÔÇőare tolerant to Ôüáwater deficits.
Now, research has produced coffee varieties that are resistant to droughts, Hojo said, adding that it would be valuable in the future to have coffee plants that can hold out Ôüáfor ÔÇőirrigation with captured rain, rather than groundwater sources that ÔÇőmay be scarce.
"We know that climate change and water availability are going to be problems for our future," Hojo ÔÇősaid.

item.title: One week into Iran war, the dangers for the US and Trump multiply
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 7 (Reuters) - One week into the U.S.-Israeli war against Iran that has plunged the Middle East into turmoil, President Donald Trump faces a growing list of risks and challenges that raise questions about whether he will be able to translate military successes into a clear geopolitical win.
Even after the killing of Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei and devastating blows against Iranian forces on land, at sea and in the air, the crisis has ÔÇőquickly widened into a regional conflict that threatens a more prolonged U.S. military engagement with fallout beyond TrumpÔÇÖs control.
That is a scenario that Trump had avoided in his two terms in the White House, preferring swift, limited operations like the January 3 lightning raid in Venezuela ÔÇîand JuneÔÇÖs one-off strike on IranÔÇÖs nuclear sites.
ÔÇťIran is a messy and potentially protracted military campaign,ÔÇŁ said Laura Blumenfeld of the Johns Hopkins School for Advanced International Studies in Washington.
