ÔÇťTrump is risking the global economy, regional stability and his own Republican Party's performance in the U.S. midterm elections.ÔÇŁ
Trump, who came to office promising to keep the U.S. out of "stupidÔÇŁ military interventions, is now pursuing what many experts see as an open-ended war of choice unprompted by any imminent threat to the U.S. from Iran, despite claims to the contrary by the president and his aides.
In doing so, analysts say he has struggled to articulate a detailed set of objectives or a clear endgame for Operation Epic Fury, the biggest U.S. military operation since the 2003 Iraq invasion, offering shifting rationales ÔÇőfor the war and definitions of what would constitute victory.
White House spokesperson Anna Kelly rejected that assessment, saying Trump has clearly outlined his goals to "destroy IranÔÇÖs ballistic missiles and production capacity, demolish their navy, end their ability to arm proxies, and prevent them from ever obtaining a nuclear weapon.ÔÇŁ
However, if the ÔÇőwar drags on, American casualties mount and the economic costs of interrupted Gulf oil flows multiply, TrumpÔÇÖs biggest foreign policy gamble could also hurt his Republican Party politically.
Despite criticism from some Trump supporters opposed to military Ôüáinterventions, members of his Make America Great Again movement have largely backed him on Iran so far.
But any softening of their support could imperil RepublicansÔÇÖ control of Congress in the November midterm elections, given opinion polls showing opposition to the war among the broader electorate, including a crucial bloc of independent voters.
ÔÇťThe American people are ÔÇőnot interested in repeating the mistakes of Iraq and Afghanistan,ÔÇŁ said Brian Darling, a Republican strategist. ÔÇťThe MAGA base is split between those who relied on no-new-war promises and ones who are loyal to TrumpÔÇÖs judgment.ÔÇŁ
High on the list of analystsÔÇÖ concerns is the mixed messaging from Trump and his aides on whether he is seeking ÔÇťregime changeÔÇŁ in ÔÇőTehran.
At the outset of the conflict, he suggested that overthrowing IranÔÇÖs rulers was a goal, at least by fomenting internal rebellion. Two days later, he stopped short of mentioning that as a priority.
But then on Thursday, Trump told Reuters he would play a role in picking IranÔÇÖs next leader and encouraged Iranian Kurdish rebels to launch attacks. That was followed by his demand in a social media post on Friday for IranÔÇÖs "unconditional surrender."
Across the region, the dangers have escalated with Iran's retaliatory strikes on Israel and other neighbors as it seeks to sow chaos and raise the costs for Israel, the U.S. and its allies.
Showing that Iran may still be able to activate proxy groups, LebanonÔÇÖs Hezbollah militia has renewed hostilities with Israel, expanding the war to another ÔÇőcountry.
American casualties have been low so far, with six service members killed, and Trump has largely shrugged off the prospects for more to come while declining to completely rule out deployment of U.S. ground troops.
Asked whether Americans should worry about Iran-inspired attacks at home, Trump said in a Time magazine interview published on Friday: ÔÇťI guess ÔÇŽ ÔÇőLike I said, some people will die.ÔÇŁ
But Jonathan Panikoff, a former deputy U.S. national intelligence officer for the Middle East, said: ÔÇťNothing is likely to hasten an early end to the war more than American casualties ÔÇŽ ThatÔÇÖs what Iran is counting on.ÔÇŁ
VENEZUELA MISCALCULATION?
Many analysts believe Trump, who has shown an increasing appetite for military action in his second term, miscalculated that the ÔÇîIran campaign would unfold Ôüálike the Venezuela operation earlier this year.
U.S. special forces captured Venezuelan President Nicolas Maduro, opening the way for Trump to coerce more compliant former loyalists into giving him considerable sway over the countryÔÇÖs vast oil reserves ÔÇô without any extended U.S. military action needed.
By contrast, Iran has proved a much tougher, better-armed foe with an entrenched clerical and security establishment.
Even the joint U.S.-Israeli "decapitation" strike that killed Khamenei and some other senior leaders has failed so far to prevent Iran from mounting a military response and has raised questions whether they could be replaced by even more hardline figures.
Looming over the conflict, however, is whether Iran could slide into chaos and break apart if its current rulers fall, further destabilizing the Middle East.
Mark Dubowitz, CEO of the Foundation for the Defense of Democracies, a nonprofit research institute considered hawkish on Iran, praised TrumpÔÇÖs overall war strategy but said the president needs to make clear publicly that he does not want to see the country disintegrate.
For now, however, one of the most pressing ÔÇőconcerns is IranÔÇÖs threat to the Strait of Hormuz, the narrow chokepoint through ÔÇőwhich a fifth of the world's oil passes. Tanker traffic has halted, Ôüáwhich could have grave economic consequences if it lasts.
Though Trump has publicly dismissed any concern about already-rising U.S. gas prices, he and his aides have scrambled for ways to mitigate the warÔÇÖs impact on energy supplies as voters tell pollsters that the cost of living is their top concern.
ÔÇťIt's an economic pain point on the U.S. economy that it seems was not fully anticipated," said Josh Lipsky at the Atlantic Council think tank in Washington.
One former U.S. military official close to the U.S. administration said ÔÇőthe widening of the war's economic impact had caught TrumpÔÇÖs team by surprise in part because those with knowledge of oil markets were not consulted ahead of the attack on Iran.
The White House's Kelly said, ÔÇťThe Iranian regime is being ÔÇőabsolutely crushed" but did not specifically address concerns Ôüáabout preparations for a war.
Trump made his decision to press ahead with the strikes
despite warnings from some senior aides that the escalation could be difficult to contain, according to two White House officials and a Republican close to the administration.
Some traditional U.S. allies were caught off guard. "It's a decision-making circle of one," said one Western diplomat.
The warÔÇÖs duration is a major unknown likely to determine the extent of its repercussions. With the price tag of the Iran campaign mounting by the day, Trump has said that the operation could last four or five weeks or ÔÇťwhatever it takesÔÇŁ but has offered little explanation of what he envisions will follow.
Retired U.S. Army Lieutenant General Ben Hodges, who served in Iraq and Afghanistan Ôüáand formerly commanded the ÔÇőU.S. Army in Europe, commended the U.S. military for its tactics in Iran. But he told Reuters: "From a political, strategic and diplomatic standpoint, it seems not to have been thought all the way ÔÇőthrough.ÔÇŁ
Trump also has a lot riding on helping oil-producing Gulf Arab states weather the Iran crisis given they have long hosted U.S. bases and have made pledges of massive new U.S. investments to him.
While Gulf allies appear to have fallen in line to support the campaign, especially after Tehran targeted them with missile and drone strikes, not everyone in the region is onboard with TrumpÔÇÖs war.
In an open ÔÇőletter to Trump published on Thursday, UAE billionaire Khalaf Al Habtor, a frequent visitor to Trump's Mar-a-Lago resort in Florida, asked: "Who gave you the right to turn our region into a battlefield?"

item.title: As war spreads, airline pilots contend with drones, missiles - and stress
item.description:
LONDON/COPENHAGEN/BEIRUT, March 7 (Reuters) - Airline pilots have faced escalating risks in recent years, from drone incursions to flight paths squeezed by conflict. Now U.S.-Israeli attacks on Iran are making the skies even more perilous and ratcheting up the pressure on those flying through them.
The outbreak of war in the Middle East has put hundreds of ballistic ÔÇőmissiles and attack drones into the skies above some of the world's busiest airports. Tehran's retaliation against the U.S. and its allies has included hitting airports, grounding scores of flights from Dubai to Abu Dhabi. A trickle ÔÇîof rescue flights has made it through for thousands of stranded passengers.
Reuters spoke to eight pilots and more than a half-dozen aviation and security insiders who said the accumulation of conflicts - from Ukraine to Afghanistan and Israel - has increased the burden on pilots, forcing them to manage shrinking airspace and the wider use of military drones far from active war zones. That's increased the strain on the mental health of pilots desperate to keep themselves and their passengers safe.
"We are not military pilots. We are not trained to deal with these kinds of threats in the air," Tanja Harter, a pilot with Middle ÔÇőEast experience and president of the European Cockpit Association, told Reuters.
The current crisis is the latest in a string of security threats the sector has faced over the years, she said, which could cause "fear and anxiety" for pilots. Airlines ÔÇőnow often have peer programs to help, she said, adding as a pilot she would not want to "share airspace with missiles."
Airspace safety has worsened in the last two-and-a-half years as conflicts have grown, Ôüáindustry experts said, through a combination of GPS spoofing - maliciously tricking planes about their position - and increased numbers of missiles and drones .
An Air France (AIRF.PA) , opens new tab flight to bring stranded French nationals home from the United Arab Emirates turned back due to missile fire on Thursday. A ÔÇőLufthansa (LHAG.DE) , opens new tab pilot on Friday diverted from Riyadh to Cairo over regional security fears.
Middle East-trained pilots have become wearily used to emergencies, the head of Lebanon's civil aviation body said. The escalating conflict put those skills to the test quickly. Video footage ÔÇőon March 5 showed planes taking off from Beirut airport as smoke billowed over buildings in the Lebanese capital.
"Middle East pilots have always faced crises, so from the start we trained how to deal with contingencies, emergencies and everything else," said Captain Mohammed Aziz, director general of Lebanon's civil aviation authority.
"No one can give you a guarantee that they won't bomb the airport or will bomb the airport."
One Middle East Airlines' pilot with a decade of experience said routes to Beirut have become more complex. In the past, shoulder-fired anti-aircraft missiles in Lebanon usually had a range of 15,000 feet, so ÔÇőpilots would increase altitude to stay out of range, he said, while planes often carried extra fuel in case they were forced to divert.
Still, most missile strikes are far enough away not to be a risk and pilots are often too busy to ÔÇőworry about them.
"You're actually busy enough on the plane trying to make sure you have clearance to land, that everything is in order, so you don't have time to process your emotions over what's happening outside the plane," he said.
The risks are not ÔÇîconfined to the ÔüáMiddle East. Since Russia's 2022 invasion of Ukraine, drones have become a key weapon on both sides. Airports in European cities from Stockholm to Munich have faced drone disruptions - suspected though not confirmed to be linked to the conflict.
Airline Captain Christian von D'Ahe, a commercial aircraft pilot for 15 years and head of the Danish Air Line Pilots Association, is alarmed by the emerging threat.
"Drones are not easily detected," Von D'Ahe said. "We can see them in the air, and they're very small. So sooner or later, something will happen."
Drones striking an aircraft's engines could cause total power loss, while damage to wings could compromise a jet's ability to manoeuvre.
Most registered planes emit a signal via a transponder, a device that identifies aircraft to radar, but drones do not, leaving pilots in the dark. Regular radars used ÔÇőby airports struggle to catch drones. Specialised radar exists, but is ÔÇőtypically operated by law enforcement or the military.
Dedrone, a Ôüácompany that produces counterdrone technology, said there were over 1.2 million drone violations in the U.S. in 2025, with more expected in coming years.
Airports can use radar, frequency sensors and jamming tools to counter drones, while some systems can "spoof" them off course. But safety concerns mean airports cannot shoot drones down.
Tim Friebe, an air traffic controller in Germany and a ÔÇővice president at the Air Traffic Controllers European Unions Coordination (ATCEUC), said drones were a "threat that is growing," while airports often had limited options.
"For now we have reports, pilot reports, or ÔÇősometimes controllers spot drones. The problem Ôüáis there's not much you can do except shutting down the airport," he said.
Drones shut down , opens new tab some of the world's biggest airports from Munich to London's Gatwick last year, driving operators to beef up their foreign object and drone detection systems, according to half a dozen industry officials.
Moritz Burger, a commercial pilot based in Germany, recalled spotting an object that looked like a balloon with a structure underneath as he was about to land at a European airport.
"I was looking out of the window and suddenly there appeared an object that passed by just Ôüábelow our aircraft. ÔÇőWe could see it for maybe one, maximum two seconds," he said, adding it startled him and left him no time to take any evasive ÔÇőaction.
"When you encounter such a near-miss or some passing object, there is not enough time to react. So it is unrealistic to expect that pilots could fly around such an object. There's pretty much nothing we can do."

item.title: Russia hits Ukraine with drones, missiles, kills at least seven in Kharkiv
item.description:
KYIV, March 7 (Reuters) - Russia launched a barrage of drones and missiles ÔÇőat Ukraine overnight on Saturday, killing at least ÔÇîseven people, including two children, in the northeast city of Kharkiv, Ukrainian officials said.
Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskiy said that ÔÇőRussia launched 480 drones and 29 missiles ÔÇőtargeting the energy sector and railway infrastructure across Ôüáthe country.
"There should be a response from partners ÔÇőto these savage strikes against life," Zelenskiy said on ÔÇőthe Telegram app.
"Russia has not abandoned its attempts to destroy Ukraine's residential and critical infrastructure, and therefore support should continue," ÔÇőZelenskiy said, urging partners to continue air defence ÔÇőand weapons supplies.
Oleh Syniehubov, Kharkiv regional governor, said that seven ÔÇîpeople, Ôüáincluding two children, were killed after a Russian ballistic missile slammed into a five-storey residential building.
Rescue workers continue to work on the site, clearing the rubble, ÔÇőhe said.
In ÔÇőKharkiv, seven Ôüáresidential apartment houses, commercial and administrative buildings, electricity distribution lines, and cars were ÔÇődamaged by the Russian attacks, Syniehubov said.
Ukrainian ÔÇőofficials Ôüásaid that Russia attacked four railway stations and other railway infrastructure in central Ukraine and port infrastructure in Ôüáthe ÔÇősouthern Odesa region, setting on ÔÇőfire containers with vegetable oil and damaging a grain warehouse.

item.title: Refugees, migrants in Lebanon find rare sanctuary from Israeli strikes in Beirut church
item.description:
BEIRUT, March 7 (Reuters) - When Israeli strikes began pummelling BeirutÔÇÖs southern suburbs early on Monday, Sudanese refugee ÔÇőRidina Muhammad and her family had no choice but to flee home on foot, eventually reaching the only shelter ÔÇîthat would accept them: a church.
Eight months pregnant, Muhammad, 32, walked with her husband and three children for hours in the dark streets until they found a car to take them to the St. Joseph Tabaris Parish, which has opened its doors to refugees and migrants.
They are among 300,000 people displaced across Lebanon this week by heavy ÔÇőIsraeli strikes, launched in response to a rocket and drone attack into Israel by the Lebanese armed group Hezbollah.
Just 100,000 of the ÔÇődisplaced are in government shelters. Others are staying with relatives or sleeping in the streets. But migrants and Ôüárefugees say government shelters were never an option for them, saying they were turned away during the last war between Hezbollah and Israel.
MuhammadÔÇÖs oldest ÔÇődaughter, now seven, stopped speaking after the 2024 war.
This time, they are even more vulnerable: their home was destroyed in this weekÔÇÖs strikes and ÔÇőMuhammad is due to give birth at the end of the month.
ÔÇťI don't know if there's a doctor or not, but I'm really scared about it because I haven't prepared any clothes for the baby, nor arranged a hospital, and I don't know where to go,ÔÇŁ she told Reuters as her younger daughter leaned ÔÇőagainst her pregnant belly.
Muhammad said she was registered with the United NationsÔÇÖ refugee agency (UNHCR) but had not received support.
ÔÇťUs, as refugees, ÔÇőwhy did we register with the U.N., if they are not helping us in the most difficult times?ÔÇŁ she said.
Dalal Harb, a spokesperson for UNHCR Lebanon, ÔÇîsaid the Ôüáagency had mobilized but reaching everyone immediately was extremely challenging given the scale and speed of displacement. The UNHCR operation in Lebanon is currently only around 14% funded, she said.
The Jesuit Refugee Service (JRS), which helped the church host displaced in 2024, is doing so again.
Michael Petro, JRSÔÇÖ Emergency Shelter Director, said the church was full within the first day of strikes, with 140 people from South Sudan, Ethiopia, Bangladesh, and other countries sheltering ÔÇőthere.
ÔÇťThere are many, many more people ÔÇőcoming than there were in 2024, Ôüáand we have fewer and fewer places to put them,ÔÇŁ he said.
Petro said he was told weeks ago that government shelters would be open to migrants if war erupted.
But when the strikes began and even ÔÇőLebanese struggled to find shelter, the policy seemed to change, he said.
ÔÇťWe're hearing from hotlines up to ÔÇőgovernment officials and Ôüáministries that migrants are not welcome,ÔÇŁ Petro said.
LebanonÔÇÖs Minister for Social Affairs Haneen Sayyed did not respond to a request for comment. On Thursday, Sayyed said Beirut shelters were full.
When Israeli strikes began, Othman Yahyeh Dawood, a 41-year-old Sudanese man, put his two young sons on his motorcycle.
They drove 75 Ôüákilometres (46 miles) ÔÇőfrom the southern Lebanese town of Nabatieh to St. JosephÔÇÖs, where they had sheltered ÔÇőin 2024.
ÔÇťI know the area is safe and there are people who will welcome us,ÔÇŁ he said.
ÔÇťWe don't know where to go; there's war there (in the south), war here (in ÔÇőBeirut), war in Sudan, and nowhere else to go,ÔÇŁ he said.

item.title: In Perpignan, French far right tests tough-on-crime blueprint
item.description:
PERPIGNAN, France, March 7 (Reuters) - In the streets of Perpignan, a faded Mediterranean city near France's border with Spain, the incumbent far-right mayor Louis Aliot is pitching a simple message as he asks voters for another term: more police, more cameras and more order.
The National Rally (RN) mayor ÔÇőhas made security the centrepiece of his administration, and his party is holding up this city of 122,000, the biggest it controls, as a blueprint for governance that it hopes to ÔÇîreplicate elsewhere ahead of nationwide municipal elections this month.
Aliot, who leads in polls despite an embezzlement conviction that could bar him from office if his appeal fails, said Perpignan has been a laboratory for RN governance since he won city hall in 2020 and a showcase for its national ambitions when the French vote for a new president in 2027.
"When the National Rally is in office, well, we govern, we run cities and we run them well," he said in an interview.
Wins in other cities would give Marine ÔÇőLe Pen's party a springboard going into next year's presidential election. Polls show the RN performing well in Toulon, Nice and Marseille, although a two-round system makes final outcomes hard to forecast.
The ÔÇőRN's law-and-order message resonates with many voters in Perpignan, where just streets from the Catalan-influenced, historic centre lie run-down neighbourhoods that are among France's poorest.
Two weeks before the ÔüáMarch 15 and 23 elections, RN leader Jordan Bardella and hard-left rival Jean-Luc M├ęlenchon - both presidential hopefuls - held duelling weekend rallies, turning the city into a symbolic battleground.
"Faced with the violence engulfing our country year after year, we ÔÇőintend to make public order an absolute priority," Bardella told supporters.
Aliot promised 50 more police officers and 200 surveillance cameras, particularly in outer suburbs to which the middle class has fled from poverty-stricken inner neighbourhoods that are home ÔÇőto the city's sizeable North African and Roma populations.
The city has already expanded its municipal force to 199 officers from 161 in 2020, and now deploys 1.6 municipal police per 1,000 residents - proportionally the highest among French cities of more than 100,000 and nearly three times Paris's 0.6, according to Reuters' analysis of Interior Ministry data.
More aggressive policing has seen drug-trafficking cases climb sharply, with Perpignan now reporting the seventh most among over 50 large French cities, up from 18th in 2020. Overall trafficking cases ÔÇőopened by police have more than doubled, while fixed-penalty fines for minor possession have quadrupled.
The security focus comes at a cost. Municipal debt stands at 1,600 euros per inhabitant, well above the 1,200 average in similar-sized ÔÇőcities, according to Finance Ministry data.
Property tax rates are higher than in 71% of comparable cities, and 98% of peers have lower local business taxes.
Even so, business weekly Challenges ranked Perpignan the third best-run city in France last week, a ÔÇîtalking point for ÔüáAliot's allies as they try to convert security-minded voters beyond the RN's base.
Aliot leads with 44% in the first round according to an early December Ifop poll, benefiting from a fractured opposition split among five rivals.
A far-right political veteran, Aliot has been central to Le Pen's campaign to soften the image of the anti-immigration party and make it more palatable to the mainstream voter. Opinion polls project either Le Pen or Bardella, her prot├ęg├ę, winning the first round of next year's presidential vote and facing a tight run-off contest.
On the ground, reactions were mixed. At a city-centre market, retiree Marie Nivet said she welcomed cleaner streets and visible policing, though she wanted more action on ÔÇődrugs.
She was undecided on whether to support Aliot because ÔÇőof the looming appeal court verdict over misuse Ôüáof EU funds that also threatens Le Pen's political survival .
Aliot said he would appeal any unfavourable outcome to France's highest court, but that others in his team could take his place if needed.
The legal uncertainty didn't bother Laure Gu├ęrin, a retired private-school administrator, who said she would back Aliot because of the RN's law-and-order stance.
Others were more ÔÇőwary. Christian Pyguillem, a retired property manager, credited Aliot with cleaner streets and better security, but said entrenched poverty in some neighbourhoods requires deeper urban renewal ÔÇőthan any mayor has attempted.
"We'll Ôüágive him another six years, but it's a vote motivated by the local situation, I don't agree with their other ideas," he said.
Rivals said the security push has failed to deliver and that city finances have deteriorated. Centre-right challenger Bruno Nougayr├Ęde accused Aliot of neglecting local duties for national politics and putting publicity before results.
"These five years have been marked by complete inaction for the people of Perpignan," Nougayr├Ęde told Reuters. "A lot of resources went into PR, but the Ôüácity itself ÔÇőhas not changed."
Hard-left candidate Micka├źl Idrac called Aliot's record "catastrophic," blaming "frenzied" investment in arming police and surveillance cameras. He also criticised what he ÔÇődescribed as divisive rhetoric.
Aliot countered that voters would judge by how they feel in their streets. He casts the RN as the party willing to enforce order after decades of drift by rivals on left and right.
"If the left had defended the poor, we wouldn't be ÔÇőhere today. If the right had defended order, we wouldn't be here today," Aliot said.

item.title: India let Iran warship dock the day US sank another off Sri Lanka, officials say
item.description:
NEW DELHI, March 6 (Reuters) - India has allowed an Iranian warship to dock as a humanitarian gesture, Foreign Minister Subrahmanyam Jaishankar said on Saturday, after the U.S. ÔÇősank another Iranian navy vessel off neighbouring Sri Lanka.
The Lavan docked ÔÇîat India's southern port of Kochi on Wednesday, the same day the U.S. submarine struck Iranian navy frigate Dena, after an urgent request from Tehran, an Indian government source told ÔÇőReuters.
U.S. President Donald Trump has said destroying the Iranian navy is one ÔÇőaim of the war he and Israel launched against the Islamic ÔüáRepublic a week ago.
The Lavan - an amphibious landing vessel, according to the U.S. ÔÇőNaval Institute's online news site - and two other ships "were coming in for a ÔÇőfleet review and then they got, in a way, caught on the wrong side of the events," Jaishankar told the annual Raisina Dialogue event.
"I think we really approached it from ÔÇőthe point of view of humanity, of other than whatever the legal ÔÇőissues were," he said. "I think we did the right thing."
At least 87 people were killed in ÔÇîthe U.S. Ôüáattack on the Dena in Sri Lanka's exclusive economic zone 19 nautical miles off the coast, outside its maritime boundaries.
India received the docking request for the Lavan on February 28, the day the Iran war started, the source ÔÇősaid late on Friday, ÔÇőadding that the Ôüárequest "was urgent as the vessel had developed technical issues".
Its 183 crew members have been accommodated at naval facilities in ÔÇőKochi, said the source, who asked not to be identified ÔÇőciting confidentiality.
The ÔüáDena was on its way back from a naval exercise organised by India, according to the drill's website and Sri Lankan officials.
Sri Lankan authorities said on Friday Ôüáthat ÔÇőthey were escorting the Iranian naval ship Booshehr ÔÇőto a harbour on the eastern coast and moving most of its crew to a navy camp ÔÇőnear Colombo.

item.title: Iran to suspend strikes on neighbours unless attacks come from them
item.description:
March 7 (Reuters) - Iran's President Masoud Pezeshkian ÔÇősaid on ÔÇîSaturday that its temporary leadership ÔÇőcouncil had ÔÇőapproved the suspension Ôüáof attacks ÔÇőagainst neighboring countries ÔÇőunless an attack on Iran came ÔÇőfrom those ÔÇőcountries.
The announcement came as ÔÇîIran Ôüácontinued to launch attacks in the ÔÇőregion ÔÇőin Ôüáresponse to U.S.-Israeli strikes ÔÇőon the ÔÇőIslamic ÔüáRepublic.

item.title: Azerbaijan says it foils Iranian plots including plan to attack major pipeline
item.description:
BAKU, March 7 (Reuters) - Azerbaijan said it had prevented several acts of "terrorist" sabotage planned by Iran's Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC), including ÔÇőa plot to attack a major oil pipeline running ÔÇîthrough the South Caucasus to Turkey.
The targets included the Baku-Tbilisi-Ceyhan (BTC) oil pipeline, the Israeli embassy in Azerbaijan, an Ashkenazi synagogue, and a leader ÔÇőan ancient Jewish community in Azerbaijan called the Mountain ÔÇőJews, according to a State Security Service statement cited Ôüáby the Azertag state news agency late on Friday.
Iran ÔÇőhas not commented on the statement.
The BTC pipeline travels via Georgia ÔÇőand Turkey and sends oil to Europe, and also accounts for roughly a third of Israeli oil imports. Any damage to its infrastructure ÔÇőcould drive global energy prices even higher as the war in ÔÇőthe Middle East enters its second week.
The Azerbaijani statement came just a ÔÇîday Ôüáafter Baku vowed to retaliate for what it said was an incursion of four Iranian drones into its Nakhchivan exclave, which injured four people and damaged airport infrastructure. Iran flatly denied it ÔÇősent the drones ÔÇőinto Azerbaijan.
Baku said Ôüáan investigation found two Iranian citizens and an Azerbaijani national had colluded to bring over ÔÇőseven kilograms of the C-4 explosive into Azerbaijan ÔÇőon Ôüáthe instructions of the IRGC. Authorities have issued international arrest warrants for four people.
On Friday Azerbaijan ordered the evacuation of its Ôüádiplomats ÔÇőfrom Iran, citing safety concerns, amid ÔÇőalready tense relations between the two countries over Baku's ties to Israel and ÔÇőTurkey.

item.title: Putin calls for immediate halt to Iran conflict
item.description:
MOSCOW, March 7 (Reuters) - Russian President Vladimir Putin expressed his condolences to Iran's president over the ÔÇőnumerous civilian casualties resulting from "the armed Israeli-American ÔÇîaggression against Iran" and called for an immediate halt to hostilities, the Kremlin said.
In a phone ÔÇőcall late on Friday with Iranian President ÔÇőMasoud Pezeshkian, Putin expressed his deep condolences Ôüáover the killing of Iranian Supreme ÔÇőLeader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei, members of Khamenei's family, ÔÇőIranian political and military leaders and "numerous civilians".
"Vladimir Putin reaffirmed Russia's principled stance in favour of an immediate ÔÇőcessation of hostilities, the rejection of force ÔÇőas a method to solve any issues surrounding Iran or ÔÇîarising Ôüáin the Middle East, and a swift return to the path of diplomatic resolution," the Kremlin said.
Putin said he was in ÔÇőconstant contact with ÔÇőthe leaders Ôüáof Gulf Cooperation Council member states.
"Masoud Pezeshkian expressed gratitude for Russia's ÔÇősolidarity with the Iranian people as ÔÇőthey Ôüádefend their sovereignty and the independence of their country. He also provided a detailed update Ôüáon ÔÇőthe developments during the ÔÇőlatest active phase of the conflict," the Kremlin said.

item.title: Inside the Nepal campaign that could vault Balendra Shah to power
item.description:
KATHMANDU, March 7 (Reuters) - The campaign that swept aside Nepal's established political heavyweights and brought 35-year-old rapper-turned-politician Balendra Shah to the cusp of power emerged out of the top floors of a six-story building in west Kathmandu.
Shah is the candidate for prime minister from the ÔÇőRastriya Swatantra Party, which is leading the vote count two days after Nepal's first election since youth-led protests in September forced the previous elected government out of power.
The final results ÔÇîfor 165 seats in direct, first-past-the-post tallies and 110 seats through proportional representation are expected by next week, according to the election commission.
And if current trends hold, Shah - or Balen, as he is better known - will have successfully harnessed a wave of support unleashed by the September uprising with one of the most sophisticated campaigns ever seen in the small Himalayan nation.
Reuters interviewed six RSP officials to gain insight into Shah's campaign, much of it coordinated out of the ÔÇőtop three floors of the party's headquarters in the capital city's Balaju neighbourhood and heavily bankrolled by the Nepali diaspora.
"We are overwhelmed by the support and love we received from ÔÇőpeople on the ground," said Bigyan Gautam, a member of RSP's national campaign team.
Shah's stunning performance stands in contrast with Bangladesh's election last month, where Ôüáa youth-driven party fizzled at the polls after emerging out of the "Gen-Z" protests that unseated the country's long-serving premier in 2024.
The heart of RSP's political machine is the Research, Strategy and ÔÇőDocumentation Department, run by an 11-member board overseeing 300 party workers divided into three national groups that supplement smaller teams led by individual candidates.
These national-level teams plan election tactics, organise rallies, create and manage online ÔÇőcontent, and track ground-level campaigning and feedback from across Nepal, three officials said.
In the run-up to Thursday's vote, the party also followed a carefully calibrated media strategy, with Shah delivering a major speech every eight days, allowing enough time for each rally to be amplified by a 660-strong social media team.
The RSP also held road shows in five to seven districts each day, backed by daily brief appearances in one of Nepal's seven provinces where Shah would ÔÇőmeet and connect with the voters.
"If you keep giving speeches, people get confused," a party official said, asking not to be named. "We let opposition parties raise some issues, and then respond once. This way, ÔÇőour message stays clear."
The centralised campaign system and large events were funded directly by the party, which has received large donations from Nepalis living abroad, particularly from those in the United States, according to two officials.
But individual ÔÇîcandidates were Ôüáresponsible for organising and financing their own campaign events, said RSP's Treasurer Lima Adhikari.
Before he joined RSP in December ahead of the polls, Shah served as the mayor of Kathmandu, a position he had won in 2022 by leveraging his popularity as one of Nepal's biggest rap stars, with millions of followers on social media.
On January 19, Shah stood with RSP founder and TV host-turned-politician Rabi Lamichhane at a rally in the capital city of Nepal's Madhesh province and told the thousands gathered: "A Madhesi boy is going to become prime minister."
Although Madhesh and the surrounding Terai plains are the most populous ÔÇőregions of Nepal, the country has long been ÔÇődominated by political elites from Kathmandu and its Ôüámountainous belt.
Shah's January speech went viral on social media, underlining the early success of the RSP's bet that he could become the first leader from the plains to secure the premiership, three party officials said.
"It was very clear to us that the nation was fed up with the old corrupt leaders ÔÇőand they were seeing hope in young leaders like Balen Shah and Rabi Lamichhane," said Birendra Kumar Mehta, a member of RSP's central committee.
"The ÔÇőparty saw this as an Ôüáopportunity."
Shah himself is contesting the election from Jhapa-5, a constituency in the plains that has long been part of the home turf of K.P. Sharma Oli, who quit as prime minister in the wake of the September protests.
In this rural pocket of Nepal, Shah has stuck with his unorthodox campaign playbook, shunning sit-down media interviews, making impromptu stops to talk with voters, and forming a group of youth volunteers across the constituency.
Part of Ôüáhis team, reinforced ÔÇőby the RSP campaign in Kathmandu, has also been collecting voter feedback and complaints, including on development projects and general ÔÇőgovernance.
"People from every part of Nepal will feel that the government is theirs and most importantly, that it is there for them," said D.P. Aryal, RSP's vice chairperson, adding that Shah's administration will look to bring in outside experts to bolster ÔÇőits capacity.
"Youth engagement and the maximum integration of technology will also be among our top priorities."

item.title: Israel carries out rare airborne raid in Lebanon; 16 killed in strikes
item.description:
BEIRUT, March 7 (Reuters) - Israel's military carried out an airborne operation that ÔÇődropped troops into a town in eastern Lebanon overnight, residents and Lebanese state media said on ÔÇîSaturday, as heavy Israeli strikes on the area left more than a dozen people dead.
Lebanon was plunged deeper into the conflict engulfing the Middle East on Monday when Iran-backed Hezbollah launched rockets and drones into Israel, which has responded with heavy bombardment across Lebanon's south, east and ÔÇőnear the capital Beirut.
Overnight, Israeli helicopters dropped troops near the town of Nabi Chit in Lebanon's eastern ÔÇőBekaa Valley, Lebanese state media and residents said. Residents and Hezbollah fighters fired on the Ôüátroops as they moved on foot, the reports said.
The Israeli troops withdrew and left by helicopter as Israeli strikes ÔÇőpounded Nabi Chit and nearby towns, state media and residents said. Israel's military did not immediately respond to Reuters questions ÔÇőon the incident.
Lebanon's health ministry said 16 people had been killed in Israeli attacks in the last 24 hours in Nabi Chit. A spokesperson told Reuters the toll included people killed in strikes throughout Friday as well as during intense overnight bombardment, and that it was ÔÇőexpected to rise further.
Two security sources told Reuters that three army troops were among the dead.
Israeli strikes have killed ÔÇőmore than 200 people across Lebanon, and orders to evacuate have displaced around 300,000 people, only a third of whom are now ÔÇîliving in Ôüágovernment shelters.
Shawki al-Masri, who lives in a town adjacent to Nabi Chit, described the overnight bombing as "a night of hell".
"We heard the helicopters over our house all night - they were so low we thought they would land on us," he told Reuters.
"People in the town woke up and started shooting at them, then the warplanes started bombing. ÔÇőIt was a very violent ÔÇőnight and only calmed Ôüádown when the sun came up," he said.
In a statement overnight, Hezbollah said it had fired on Israeli troops who had been dropped near Nabi Chit by four Israeli helicopters. ÔÇőThe group said the Israeli troops withdrew.
While Israeli forces are carrying out ground operations ÔÇőin southern Lebanon, Ôüápushing deeper into a strip of border villages, airborne operations are rare. In a 2024 war between Israel and Hezbollah, Israeli naval forces kidnapped a man from a Lebanese coastal town.
The United Nations on Saturday warned that the conflict was set to get "even Ôüáworse," ÔÇőand that talks between Israel and Lebanon "must be pursued with urgency" to ÔÇőend hostilities.
Its Special Coordinator for Lebanon, Jeanine Hennis-Plasschaert, said in a statement that it was "clear that ongoing military actions will not deliver a lasting win ÔÇőto anyone".
"Rather, they will only deepen instability and inflict further suffering," she said.

item.title: India raises cooking gas prices as Iran war hits supply
item.description:
NEW DELHI, March 7 (Reuters) - Indian companies have raised the prices of liquefied petroleum ÔÇőgas, mostly used as a cooking ÔÇîfuel, for the first time in about a year, as global prices surge with the U.S.-Israel war ÔÇőon Iran disrupting supplies from the ÔÇőMiddle East.
Indian Oil Corp, the country's top Ôüárefiner and LPG seller, has increased ÔÇőthe prices of a 14.2-kg LPG cylinder in ÔÇőDelhi by 7% to 913 rupees ($9.93), according to its website.
State refiners IOC, Bharat Petroleum Corp (BPCL.NS) , opens new tab and Hindustan ÔÇőPetroleum Corp (HPCL.NS) , opens new tab raised prices in tandem.
India, the ÔÇőworld's second-biggest importer of LPG, last year consumed 33.15 ÔÇîmillion Ôüámetric tons of cooking gas, a mixture of propane and butane, with imports accounting for about two-thirds of LPG consumption. Middle ÔÇőEastern LPG accounts ÔÇőfor Ôüá85% to 90% of those imports.
India on Friday asked refiners to boost ÔÇőLPG production to avoid any shortage ÔÇőof Ôüácooking gas in the country.
Indian companies have also raised the prices of 19-kg commercial LPG Ôüácylinders, ÔÇőmainly used by hotels and ÔÇőrestaurants, to 1,883 rupees from 1,768.50 rupees.
($1 = 91.9280 Indian ÔÇőrupees)

item.title: White House seeks bolder action on energy prices amid Iran conflict
item.description:
March 6 (Reuters) - The White House is asking federal agencies to step up efforts ÔÇőto address soaring energy prices from the Iran conflict , signaling a concern that steps taken so far may not be enough, ÔÇîaccording to two people familiar with the matter.
Senior officials have asked the departments of Energy, Transportation and Treasury and the Environmental Protection Agency to provide more policy options, with an emphasis on measures that President Donald Trump could implement without congressional approval, one of the sources told Reuters.
The requests indicate that the White ÔÇőHouse is preparing for the possibility that bolder action may be needed if oil and gas prices continue to climb. ÔÇőPolitical analysts say higher gasoline prices could hurt Trump and his Republican Party in November's midterm elections, Ôüáwhen control of Congress is at stake.
ÔÇťObviously the White House is coordinating with the interagency on this important issue, if we were ÔÇőnot, it would be a problem. President Trump and his entire energy team have had a strong game plan to keep oil prices ÔÇőstable well before Operation Epic Fury began, and they will continue to review all credible options and execute on them when appropriate," White House spokeswoman Taylor Rogers said in a statement.
U.S. and global crude oil futures climbed above $90 a barrel on Friday, with U.S. prices rising more than 12% as Middle Eastern ÔÇősupply remained constrained by the effective closure of the Strait of Hormuz amid the expanding U.S.-Israeli war against Iran.
Gasoline prices in the U.S. have ÔÇősurged in recent weeks, reaching levels not seen since late 2024. The national average for regular unleaded has climbed above $3.30 per gallon, while diesel has ÔÇîrisen to $4.26 Ôüáper gallon.
The White House has taken a cautious approach to intervening in energy markets, wary that an overly aggressive strategy could backfire. Officials say any broad measures must be carefully calibrated, noting that heavy-handed steps that fail to lower gasoline or crude prices could unsettle markets, erode confidence and trigger political backlash. Analysts, meanwhile, have expressed skepticism about how much the White House can do to rein in prices.
Officials ÔÇőhave discussed a wide array ÔÇőof options, including a federal Ôüágasoline tax holiday and looser environmental regulations around summer gasoline that will allow higher blends of ethanol, Reuters previously reported .
The Treasury Department is weighing a plan that involves using the oil futures market, Reuters reported , but ÔÇőthere is no immediate plan to announce the move.
Trump ordered on Tuesday that the U.S. International ÔÇőDevelopment Finance Corporation provide Ôüáinsurance against losses stemming from political instability or conflict for maritime trade in the Gulf. The move came after oil and liquefied natural gas tanker transit ground to a halt in the Strait of Hormuz , a chokepoint for about 20% of the world's daily oil supply.
Markets met the Ôüámove with ÔÇősome skepticism. Analysts question whether financial guarantees alone can offset the operational and security ÔÇőrisks posed by the growing tensions in the region.
On Friday, the administration announced it would provide reinsurance for losses up to $20 billion in the Gulf region to bolster confidence ÔÇőfor oil and gas shippers during the war with Iran.

item.title: Shipper MSC to introduce emergency fuel surcharge
item.description:
March 7 (Reuters) - Shipping company MSC said on Saturday it would implement an ÔÇőemergency fuel surcharge to all ÔÇîcargo from the Mediterranean (including West Mediterranean, Adriatic, East Mediterranean, Greece and Turkey) ÔÇőand Black Sea to the ÔÇőIndian sub-continent, Red Sea and East ÔüáAfrica, effective March 16.
It said ÔÇőthe surcharge would be $30 per ÔÇőtwenty-foot equivalent unit (TEU) from the Mediterranean and Black Sea to the Red Sea for ÔÇődry containers, and $50 per TEU ÔÇőfor refrigerated containers.
Dry containers from the Mediterranean ÔÇîand ÔüáBlack Sea to East Africa will be charged $60 per TEU, while refrigerated containers will be charged $90 ÔÇőper TEU, ÔÇőthe world's Ôüálargest carrier of ocean container cargo said.
MSC will ÔÇőalso impose a surcharge of $40 ÔÇőper ÔüáTEU from the Mediterranean and Black Sea to the Indian sub-continent Ôüáfor ÔÇődry containers, and $60 ÔÇőper TEU for refrigerated containers.

item.title: South Africa central bank to redraft risk scenarios as Iran war boosts oil price
item.description:
LONDON, March 6 (Reuters) - South Africa's central bank will redraft its risk scenarios for its next rate-setting ÔÇîpolicy meeting as the widening Middle East conflict pushes up oil prices, the country's central bank governor told Reuters.
The central bank is scheduled to decide on March 26 on interest rates after keeping its main lending rate unchanged at 6.75% in a split decision in late January, citing at ÔÇőthe time that policy makers wanted to see inflation expectations fall further.
"We had our baseline (in the January meeting) and ÔÇőwe had an optimistic scenario and an adverse scenario," said South African Reserve Bank Governor Lesetja ÔüáKganyago. He said the adverse scenario had assumed the average oil price for the year would reach $75 per barrel and ÔÇőthe rand would weaken to 18.50 to the dollar.
Now the previous adverse scenario, he said, "is gone - it was in the past ... ÔÇőwe will come up with a completely new one."
The Middle East crisis set off by Israel and Washington's bombing of Iran lifted Brent crude futures to more than $94 per barrel this week, while the rand weakened to 16.82 to the dollar.
Kganyago said a 10% move in the exchange ÔÇőrate would have a much stronger impact on inflation in South Africa than a similar jump in oil prices.
"Yes, it's the ÔÇőadverse scenario, but it is not playing out as we had feared," Kganyago said, adding that policy makers would only get concerned once ÔÇîthey saw Ôüáthe impact of the exchange rate filter through to prices.
"The call that as a policy maker you must make is - is this transitory, or is it persistent? And you only respond to the persistent, not to the transitory - and that is not an easy call to make."
The knock-on effects from global tensions that have kept markets on edge in recent months - and the ÔÇőmost recent crisis - would be ÔÇődominating discussions at the next Ôüámeeting, Kganyago added, speaking on the sidelines of an investor conference in London.
"We will discuss what is the impact of this geopolitics on the markets, on oil, emerging markets, and on ÔÇőexchange rates."
Asked whether South Africa would continue to buy dollars - as it has since the ÔÇőstart of the Ôüáyear - after the rand weakened nearly 4% this week against a strong greenback, the governor said the policy had not changed.
"If we think that there are cheap dollars available in the market, we will pick them up," he said, adding that overall reserve levels Ôüáhad been ÔÇőbolstered not only by dollar purchases, but by the rising value of gold in ÔÇőthe central bank's war chest and the proceeds of borrowing by the Treasury.
Central bank data released earlier on Friday showed that South Africa's net foreign reserves rose ÔÇőto $75.84 billion at the end of February from $74.88 billion in January.

item.title: Flash floods in Nairobi kill 10, disrupt flights at major airport
item.description:
NAIROBI, March 7 (Reuters) - Aid workers ÔÇîpulled bodies from floodwaters across Nairobi on Saturday after overnight flash floods killed at least 10 people, swept away dozens of cars and ÔÇődisrupted flights at East Africa's biggest airport, authorities said.
In ÔÇőthe industrial neighbourhood of Grogan, security guard John Lomayan, Ôüá34, looked at the body of an elderly man ÔÇőhe recognised - a roadside egg seller - trapped beneath a car that ÔÇőhad been washed away when the Nairobi River burst its banks.
"I saw him being carried by the water from up there," he said, gesturing ÔÇőup the road. "We didn't know where he had gone. ÔÇőIt is only now that we see him under the car".
A Reuters reporter ÔÇîsaw Ôüáthree bodies pulled from underneath cars. Police said 10 people had been confirmed dead so far.
Scientists say global warming is worsening floods and droughts across East Africa by concentrating rainfall ÔÇőinto shorter, more ÔÇőintense bursts. ÔüáA 2024 World Weather Attribution study found climate change had made devastating rains in the region ÔÇőtwice as likely as before.
Kenya Airways said ÔÇőthe rains Ôüáhad disrupted flights to Nairobi and forced some to divert to the coastal city of Mombasa.
"So many cars, so much stuff, ÔüáI ÔÇődon't know. Everything was just (washed away). ÔÇőAll of the water (came) ... from that river," shocked resident Cedric Mwanza said, referring ÔÇőto the Nairobi River.

item.title: Days after Iran strikes, Trump hosts Latin American leaders with China in focus
item.description:
March 7 (Reuters) - President Donald Trump will host Latin American leaders in Florida on Saturday as his administration seeks to counter China's ÔÇőgrowing influence in the region, a diplomatic show just days after U.S. strikes on Iran opened a new front in the Middle ÔÇîEast.
The summit comes as Trump prepares for talks with Chinese President Xi Jinping in Beijing at the end of March. The Trump administration hopes to pull Latin America closer to Washington after years of growing Chinese trade, lending and infrastructure investment in the region.
Saturday's gathering, dubbed the "Shield of the Americas," also gives Trump a chance to project strength closer to home even ÔÇőas the conflict in the Middle East leads to consequences he may not fully control, such as rising prices for oil and gas.
Kristi Noem ÔÇőwill be special envoy for the "Shield of the Americas", Trump posted Thursday. Noem was Homeland Security secretary until Trump removed her Ôüáfrom that post this week after mounting criticism of her from Congress.
The summit brings together conservative leaders aligned with Trump on security, migration and economics, reflecting a ÔÇőbroader rightward shift in parts of Latin America.
Among those expected to attend are Argentine President Javier Milei, Chile's president-elect Jose Antonio Kast and Salvadoran President Nayib Bukele, ÔÇőwhose gang crackdown, criticized by human rights groups, has become a model for parts of Latin America's right.
Politicians from across the region have toured Bukele's sprawling "mega-prison," where the United States last year deported more than 200 Venezuelans without trial.
Also expected are Honduran President Nasry Asfura, who narrowly won a disputed election with Trump's backing, and Ecuador's President Daniel Noboa, who has ÔÇőechoed parts of Trump's economic agenda and recently announced joint operations with the U.S. in a military crackdown on drug trafficking.
Many of the leaders share Trump's ÔÇőhardline view of crime and migration, favoring crackdowns over deeper social fixes and private business over the state. Their rise reflects a broader rightward turn in parts of Latin America ÔÇîat a Ôüátime when the region is being pulled between Washington and Beijing.
Ryan Berg, who heads the Americas Program at the Center for Strategic and International Studies, wrote this week that the summit is the first time in Trump's second term that Washington has brought together Latin American leaders in this way.
He said the talks are likely to focus on security, drug trafficking, money laundering and China's role in ports, telecommunications and other infrastructure.
Berg said China's trade with the region hit ÔÇőa record $518 billion in 2024, with ÔÇőBeijing loaning more than $120 billion to Ôüágovernments across the Western Hemisphere.
China's increased involvement in Latin America - from satellite tracking stations in Argentina and a port in Peru to economic support for Venezuela - has been an irritant for successive U.S. administrations.
China has expanded its reach through trade, loans ÔÇőand infrastructure, while the Trump administration has pushed governments in the region to curb Beijing's role in ports, energy ÔÇőprojects and other strategic Ôüáassets.
That pressure was on display recently when Panamanian authorities moved against a Hong Kong-based firm tied to operations in the Panama Canal, a key global freight channel.
Washington has also taken more direct steps in the region.
The United States captured Venezuelan President Nicolas Maduro on January 3 and moved to seize control of the country's oil exports, and Ôüáhas tightened enforcement ÔÇőof the decades-long U.S. embargo on Cuba.
Several Trump administration officials have told Reuters that Trump's ÔÇőmove against Maduro was intended in part to counter China's ambitions, and that Beijing's days of leveraging debt to get cheap oil from Venezuela were "over."

item.title: Nigerian army kills 45 in Katsina clash
item.description:
March 7 (Reuters) - At least 45 "bandits" were killed in a clash ÔÇőwith Nigerian troops in ÔÇîthe Danmusa area of Katsina state in northern Nigeria, according ÔÇőto a statement from ÔÇőthe Katsina State Government on ÔüáSaturday.
The violence followed an attack ÔÇőby armed gangs of ÔÇőmen from neighboring Zamfara state who rode into Alhazawa village in Musawa ÔÇőon March 5 and ÔÇőattempted to steal cattle, the statement ÔÇîsaid.
The Ôüáattackers returned the following day and encountered troops stationed at the army's Forward Operating ÔÇőBase ÔÇőin ÔüáDan Ali near Maidabino ward, leading to a ÔÇőclash in which ÔÇőthe Ôüáarmy killed 45 of the attackers, the statement said.
A captain Ôüáand ÔÇőtwo soldiers ÔÇődied in the encounter, the statement added.

item.title: Iranian hardline clerics seek swift naming of new supreme leader
item.description:
DUBAI, March 7 (Reuters) - Two influential and hardline Iranian clerics have called for the swift selection of a new supreme leader to help guide ÔÇőthe nation amid a new wave of U.S. and Israeli ÔÇîstrikes, Iranian media reported on Saturday.
The calls by the clerics suggest that at least some in the clerical establishment are uncomfortable with leaving a three-man council in ÔÇőcharge, even temporarily under constitutional rules, after the killing of ÔÇőSupreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei.
U.S. President Donald Trump has said Ôüáthe U.S. should have a role in choosing the new leader, ÔÇőa demand Iran has rejected.
Naser Makarem Shirazi, a grand ayatollah, which means ÔÇőhe commands a broad following for his religious rulings, said an appointment was needed swiftly to "help better organise the countryÔÇÖs affairs", state media reported.
Last week, two senior ShiÔÇÖite ÔÇőreligious authorities also issued fatwas, or religious decrees, calling on Muslims ÔÇőaround the world to avenge the killing of Khamenei. Makarem Shirazi said it was ÔÇîa Ôüáreligious duty for Muslims "until the evil of these criminals is eradicated from the world."
Grand Ayatollah Hossein Nouri Hamedani also urged members of the Assembly of Experts, a clerical body charged with choosing the new leader, ÔÇőto accelerate the ÔÇőprocess of picking ÔüáKhamenei's successor, state media reported.
Following rules laid out in Iran's constitution, a three-man council comprising the president, a ÔÇősenior cleric and the head of the judiciary, ÔÇőhas taken Ôüáon the supreme leader's role until the Assembly of Experts decides.
The constitution states a supreme leader should be chosen within three months, although with Ôüáwar raging, ÔÇőit is not immediately clear how quickly ÔÇőthe 88-member Assembly of Experts can convene. Sources have said some clerics have held some ÔÇőconsultations online.

item.title: Ukraine's interceptor drone makers look at exports to the Gulf as Iran war flares
item.description:
March 7 (Reuters) - Ukrainian manufacturers of cheap interceptor drones designed to knock out enemy unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs) say they have the capacity to export in large volumes, amid enquiries from the United ÔÇőStates and Middle East prompted by the Iran war .
Hundreds of drones based on Iran's Shahed model and now made in Russia fill Ukraine's skies during frequent attacks, ÔÇîand many are downed by air defences including Western missiles, fighter jets, truck-mounted guns and interceptor drones.
Now, as Iran launches drones at U.S. allies across the Gulf and beyond in response to intense U.S. and Israeli strikes, Ukraine is hoping the Middle East crisis will increase its leverage with allies by showing them the expertise it has built during four years of Russia's full-scale invasion could be invaluable to partners' own long-term security.
With missiles sometimes costing millions of dollars each, ÔÇőand in short supply as Western armies scramble to bolster their own defences, interceptors are seen as an efficient and cost-effective way to counter attacks by relatively cheap ÔÇődrones.
The United States and its allies in the Middle East have swiftly sought Ukraine's help with supplying such interceptors.
President Volodymyr Zelenskiy said on Thursday ÔüáUkraine would provide assistance in response to a U.S. request for help and said earlier that Middle Eastern nations had approached Kyiv.
Zelenskiy did not specify what this would entail, but a source familiar with ÔÇőthe matter told Reuters that the United States and Qatar were in talks to purchase Ukrainian interceptor drones.
SkyFall, a major manufacturer of drones including interceptors, said its manufacturing capacity ÔÇőhad outgrown Ukraine's ability to purchase its systems and the company was ready to export.
"We have had interest and inquiries from our (allies) and countries in the Middle East," said Ares, a representative of SkyFall who wore a balaclava as he spoke to hide his identity.
Ares spoke on condition that only his call-sign be used.
SkyFall says its P1-SUN interceptor has downed more than 1,500 Shaheds and 1,000 other drones since it entered operations four months ago.
"The ÔÇőcompany is ready to provide any necessary assistance if we get the green light from our government," he said, adding that it would only do so if it did not affect Ukraine's ÔÇőability to defend itself.
Ihor Fedirko, CEO of UCDI, Ukraine's state-backed arms manufacturers' association, estimated that manufacturers of interceptor UAVs and other counter-drone systems were making about twice as many as the Ukrainian military needed to ÔÇîdeal with Russia's frequent Ôüábarrages .
SkyFall asked that the location of its factory not be disclosed. During a recent visit, Reuters reporters saw long rows of 3D printers churning out plastic drone components and halls of workers soldering and assembling drones.
The company estimated it could manufacture up to 50,000 interceptor drones a month and export 5,000 to 10,000 without impinging on Ukraine's needs.
Although Ukraine does not shoot down every drone fired by Russia, this is not always down to munition availability and can instead be a question of co-ordination.
Kyiv is striving to better layer its air defences in order to allow fewer targets ÔÇőthrough, and sees boosting interceptor drone use as ÔÇőa key piece of the puzzle.
In February, Ôüáthey were responsible for 70% of the drones downed in and around Kyiv, according to Ukraine's military.
Most Ukrainian interceptor drones cost a few thousand dollars or less. In comparison, PAC-3 missiles used in the coveted U.S. Patriot air defence system, can cost $4 million each and ÔÇőtend to be used to bring down enemy missiles.
Shahed-136 drones, designed by Iran and now mass-manufactured by Russia, are estimated to cost between $50,000 ÔÇőand $100,000 each.
SkyFall markets the ÔüáP1-SUN as a highly cost-effective option. Depending on the model's specifications, it charges Ukraine's military about $1,000 per drone.
"If we are talking about export and ... assistance to partners, then the price will most likely be higher," Ares said, although he believed it would still represent the cheapest option.
Industry representatives said one of the biggest obstacles to using Ukrainian interceptor drones in the Gulf was pilots rather than drones. Ukraine is Ôüáthe only ÔÇőcountry with drone crews who know how to use these systems in combat.
SkyFall has its own academy that provides ÔÇőa three-week course to new pilots, and the company said it would be ready to send instructors abroad in the event that Ukraine's government allowed it to sell drones to other countries.
It also said it has developed capabilities to pilot ÔÇőits drones remotely, meaning drones could potentially be flown in the Gulf from a screen in Ukraine.

item.title: China's Xi says the military must be politically loyal, root out corruption
item.description:
SHANGHAI, March 7 (Reuters) - Chinese President Xi Jinping said on ÔÇőSaturday the military must ÔÇîbe loyal to the Communist Party and root out ÔÇőcorruption, according to a ÔÇőreport by the official Xinhua Ôüánews agency.
"There is ÔÇőno place for corruption to ÔÇőhide," said Xi.
"The fight against corruption must move forward."
The remarks ÔÇőcome amid the annual ÔÇőmeetings of the nation's top political ÔÇîbodies, Ôüáwhere officials unveiled this year's economic targets.
Two senior Chinese officials were absent ÔÇőon ÔÇőWednesday from Ôüáthe ranks of China's highest tier ÔÇőof power at ÔÇőthe Ôüáopening of the annual meetings, with top Communist Party Ôüácadres ÔÇőensnared in ÔÇőa wave of purges .

item.title: Lebanon's Hezbollah warns residents in Israel's north to evacuate
item.description:
TEL AVIV, March 7 (Reuters) - Lebanese Shi'ite militant group Hezbollah on Saturday warned ÔÇőresidents of a northern Israeli city near ÔÇîthe border with Lebanon to evacuate and head south.
Hezbollah did not specify what action, if any, it ÔÇőplanned to take against the city, ÔÇőwhich lies only a few kilometres from Ôüáthe border.
"Warning. All residents of Kiryat Shmona ÔÇőare asked to evacuate immediately. Head south," it ÔÇősaid in a statement.
Lebanon was pulled into the widening U.S.-Israel war with Iran on Monday after Hezbollah, an ÔÇőIranian-aligned group, fired rockets and drones into ÔÇőIsrael. Israel responded with heavy strikes across Lebanon's south, ÔÇîeast Ôüáand near Beirut.
Tens of thousands of Israelis living in the north evacuated their homes after war broke out in Gaza between Israel and ÔÇőPalestinian group ÔÇőHamas in Ôüá2023, as Hezbollah began firing rockets and other projectiles at northern ÔÇőcommunities.
Residents returned after a November 2024 ÔÇőceasefire Ôüábetween Israel and Hezbollah. Israeli Defence Minister Israel Katz has this week repeatedly urged Israelis Ôüáin ÔÇőthe north to remain in ÔÇőtheir homes, saying the military will protect them.

item.title: Trump says Iran will be 'hit very hard' on Saturday
item.description:
March 7 (Reuters) - U.S. President ÔÇőDonald Trump said Iran ÔÇîwould be "hit very hard" on Saturday and that he ÔÇőwas considering widening the ÔÇőareas and groups of people Ôüábeing, targeted, without providing ÔÇődetails.
"Today Iran will be ÔÇőhit very hard! Under serious consideration for complete destruction and certain ÔÇődeath, because of Iran's ÔÇőbad behavior, are areas and groups ÔÇîof Ôüápeople that were not considered for targeting up until this moment in time" ÔÇőTrump ÔÇősaid Ôüáin a post on Truth Social.
He also ÔÇőnoted that Iran had ÔÇőapologised Ôüáto its neighbours for its strikes against them, Ôüáwhich ÔÇőhe cast as ÔÇőa surrender.

item.title: Pope Leo names former envoy to Duterte's Philippines as ambassador to US
item.description:
VATICAN CITY, March 7 (Reuters) - Pope Leo on Saturday named a ÔÇősenior Church diplomat who represented the Vatican in the Philippines during former President Rodrigo ÔÇîDuterte 's war on drugs as the Vatican's U.S. ambassador and envoy to the Trump administration.
Italian Archbishop Gabriele Caccia, 68, served for two years in Manila before becoming the Vatican's U.N. ambassador in 2020.
His appointment follows Vatican ÔÇőcriticism of U.S.-Israeli strikes on Iran , with the Vatican's top diplomat, Cardinal Pietro Parolin, saying ÔÇőon Wednesday that the military campaign had undermined international law .
Leo, the first U.S. Ôüápope, has also openly criticised some of Trump's right-wing policies. He has described Trump's immigration crackdown, ÔÇőwhich has convulsed cities across the United States, as " inhuman ."
Caccia, widely seen as one of the more ÔÇőexperienced diplomats in the Vatican's foreign service, will arrive in Washington as some U.S. bishops have ramped up criticism of Trump.
He will replace Cardinal Christophe Pierre, 80, who is retiring and had been the Vatican's U.S. ÔÇőambassador since 2016.
Three top U.S. cardinals in January decried the direction of American foreign policy, saying ÔÇőthe country's "moral role in confronting evil around the world" was in question.
In ÔÇîManila, ÔüáCaccia was known for tamping down tensions between local Catholic bishops and Duterte, who frequently attacked Church officials in speeches.
Duterte, who left office in 2022, is being held in The Hague over murders committed during his war on drugs .
The Vatican, which maintains about 110 embassies worldwide, considers Washington one ÔÇőof its most important ÔÇődiplomatic postings.
The ambassador Ôüámust represent the Church's priorities at the White House, and envoys over the decades have contested policies of various U.S. administrations.
The Vatican's U.S. ambassador ÔÇőalso plays a sweeping role inside the national Church. He is ÔÇőresponsible for recommending Ôüáto the pope U.S. priests who could serve as bishops, shaping the lives of the country's 72 million Catholics.
Caccia, originally from Milan, has previously served as the Vatican's ambassador to Lebanon and Ôüáspent seven ÔÇőyears as the number-three official in the Vatican's powerful ÔÇőSecretariat of State.
The Vatican and the U.S. formally established diplomatic relations in 1984 under President Ronald Reagan. None of the ÔÇőprevious six Vatican ambassadors in Washington had served first at the United Nations.

item.title: Trump cannot end protections for 350,000 Haitians, US appeals court rules
item.description:
March 7 (Reuters) - A divided U.S. appeals court has refused to let the Trump administration revoke legal protections that allow more than 350,000 Haitians to live and ÔÇőwork in the U.S. and avoid being returned to their gang-violence -stricken country.
A 2-1 ÔÇîpanel of the U.S. Court of Appeals for the District of Columbia Circuit late on Friday rejected , opens new tab the administration's bid to pause a February 2 ruling that blocked the U.S. Department of Homeland ÔÇőSecurity from ending Haiti's Temporary Protected Status.
TPS is a humanitarian program that shields ÔÇőeligible migrants from deportation and allows them to work.
Under outgoing Homeland ÔüáSecurity Secretary Kristi Noem, the department has moved to end TPS for a dozen ÔÇőcountries as part of President Donald Trump's immigration crackdown, arguing the program was never ÔÇőintended to serve as a "de facto amnesty."
The administration had asked the D.C. Circuit to stay U.S. District Judge Ana Reyes' February order while it appeals. Her decision came in a class-action lawsuit brought by ÔÇőHaitians seeking to prevent DHS from exposing them to deportation.
Reyes found that Noem's November ÔÇőmove to end the Haitians' legal protections likely violated TPS termination procedures and the U.S. Constitution's ÔÇîFifth ÔüáAmendment guarantee of equal protection under the law.
The administration on appeal noted that the U.S. Supreme Court had twice allowed it to end TPS for Venezuelans.
But U.S. Circuit Judges Florence Pan and Brad Garcia, both appointed by Democratic President Joe Biden, distinguished the cases ÔÇőand said Haitians sent ÔÇőhome would "be vulnerable Ôüáto violence amid a 'collapsing rule of law' and lack access to life-sustaining medical care."
U.S. Circuit Judge Justin Walker, a Trump appointee, ÔÇődissented, saying the case and the earlier Supreme Court litigation involving ÔÇőVenezuelans were "the Ôüálegal equivalent of fraternal, if not identical, twins."
DHS did not respond to a request for comment.
Haitians were first granted TPS in 2010 after a devastating earthquake. The U.S. has Ôüárepeatedly renewed ÔÇőthe designation, most recently under the Biden administration ÔÇőin July 2024.
At that time, DHS cited Haiti's "simultaneous economic, security, political, and health crises", driven by gangs and ÔÇőthe absence of a functioning government.

item.title: Turkey considering deployment of F-16s to Cyprus, ministry source says
item.description:
ANKARA, March 7 (Reuters) - Turkey is considering ÔÇőthe deployment of ÔÇîF-16 aircraft to Cyprus, a Turkish ÔÇődefence ministry ÔÇősource said on ÔüáSaturday.
The person said ÔÇősuch a ÔÇőmove was one of the steps being ÔÇőconsidered amid ÔÇőthe phased planning underway ÔÇîto Ôüáensure the security of the breakaway Turkish Cypriot ÔÇőstate ÔÇőin Ôüáthe north of the ÔÇőisland, as ÔÇőconflict Ôüáspreads in the region.

item.title: EU looks to soften energy bill pressures for industry, document shows
item.description:
BRUSSELS, March 7 (Reuters) - The European Union is examining energy ÔÇîtaxes, network charges and carbon costs as possible areas for short-term measures to ease pressure on industries hit by high energy prices, a document seen by Reuters showed.
Brussels is looking ÔÇőfor quick fixes after companies warned they cannot compete with rivals in ÔÇőChina and the U.S. - even before this week's surge in oil Ôüáand gas prices sparked by the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran.
European Commission President ÔÇőUrsula von der Leyen has pledged to present options for EU leaders to consider at ÔÇőa summit on 19 March.
A Commission paper prepared for a meeting of EU Commissioners on Friday showed the bloc is exploring short-term measures to help the hardest-hit regions and sectors, without ÔÇőundermining longer-term climate laws meant to shift Europe to a cheaper, low-carbon energy ÔÇősystem.
"Any proposal for legislative change will not deliver immediately and a bridge solution may be ÔÇîneeded Ôüáto reduce energy prices in the next 2-5 years until the clean transition eases pressure on power prices as already seen in some regions," said the document, seen by Reuters.
The paper said the Commission would look at network charges - which make ÔÇőup about 18% ÔÇőof industrial power Ôüábills - and national taxes and levies, as well as carbon costs, which account for around 11% of bills.
It noted that ÔÇőgovernments are underusing existing tools to cut companies' energy bills, ÔÇőincluding state Ôüáaid to offset carbon costs and contracts for difference that guarantee industrial consumers a stable power price.
The document said that if energy supplies are disrupted further, Brussels must Ôüábe ÔÇőready to introduce measures to encourage consumers to ÔÇőuse less energy, as it did in 2022 when Russia slashed gas deliveries.
A Commission spokesperson did not ÔÇőimmediately respond to a request for comment.

item.title: Kuwait cuts oil production as precaution amid Iran tensions, KPC says
item.description:
March 7 (Reuters) - Kuwait said it had implemented a ÔÇőprecautionary reduction in crude ÔÇîoil production and refining throughput following the ÔÇőongoing attacks by ÔÇőIran against Kuwait and "Iranian threats Ôüáto safe passage ÔÇőof ships through the ÔÇőStrait of Hormuz," Kuwait Petroleum Corporation (KPC) said in a ÔÇőstatement on Saturday.
The ÔÇőstate oil company said the ÔÇîmove Ôüáwas part of its "risk management and business continuity strategy."
It said ÔÇőthe adjustment ÔÇőwas Ôüástrictly precautionary and would be ÔÇőreviewed as the ÔÇősituation Ôüádevelops, and it remained ready to restore Ôüáproduction ÔÇőlevels once conditions ÔÇőallow.

item.title: Trump announces new military coalition to 'eradicate cartels' in Western Hemisphere
item.description: March 7 (Reuters) - U.S. President ÔÇőDonald ÔÇîTrump on Saturday ÔÇőannounced ÔÇőa new Ôüámilitary ÔÇőcoalition ÔÇőto "eradicate cartels" in the ÔÇőWestern ÔÇőHemisphere and ÔÇîsaid Ôüá17 nations had formally ÔÇőentered ÔÇőthe Ôüáalliance.

item.title: Trump says Cuba negotiating deal with him and Rubio
item.description:
March 7 (Reuters) - U.S. ÔÇőPresident Donald Trump said ÔÇîon Saturday that Cuba wants to make a ÔÇődeal and is ÔÇőnegotiating with him and ÔüáSecretary of State Marco ÔÇőRubio.
Speaking at the "Shield ÔÇőof the Americas" gathering of Latin American leaders in Miami, ÔÇőFlorida, Trump said ÔÇőCuba was "at the end of ÔÇîthe Ôüáline."
"They want to negotiate, and they are negotiating with Marco (Rubio) and ÔÇőmyself ÔÇőand Ôüásome others, and I would think ÔÇőa deal would ÔÇőbe Ôüámade very easily with Cuba," he said.

item.title: Sweden says ship┬áseized in Baltic Sea had false flag, one crew member notified of crime
item.description:
STOCKHOLM, March 7 (Reuters) - A ship seized by Sweden in its Baltic Sea waters sailed ÔÇőunder a false flag and was suspected ÔÇîof violating maritime law and the national ship safety act because of its lack of ÔÇőseaworthiness, the Swedish police said on ÔÇőSaturday.
One crew member was notified about the Ôüásuspected breach of the national and ÔÇőinternational laws, Johan Andersson, deputy head of police's ÔÇőNational Operations Department, told a press conference.
Police and coast guard on Friday took control of Guinea-flagged Caffa ÔÇőoff southern Sweden, saying it had unclear ÔÇőflag status and was thus suspected of being stateless, ÔÇîand Ôüáthat they had launched a probe into suspected violation of maritime law.
"Our investigation so far strengthens our suspicions and our ÔÇőopinion that this ÔÇőship Ôüáhas extensive sea safety deficiencies," Andersson said on Saturday.
"We also have ÔÇőinformation that the vessel would be ÔÇőon Ôüáthe Ukraine sanctions list," he said.
Most of the 11 crew members on Caffa, which Ôüáaccording ÔÇőto ship-tracking service MarineTraffic ÔÇőis a 96-meter general cargo ship, were Russian, Andersson said.

item.title: Flavio Bolsonaro draws even with Lula in Brazil election matchup, Datafolha shows
item.description:
SAO PAULO, March 7 (Reuters) - A second-round runoff between Brazilian President Luiz Inacio Lula da Silva and Senator Flavio Bolsonaro would ÔÇőresult in a technical tie, according to a Datafolha ÔÇîpoll released on Saturday.
The poll shows that if the runoff were held today, Lula would receive 46% of the vote, compared with ÔÇő43% for Flavio, the eldest son of former President ÔÇőJair Bolsonaro.
If Lula were to face off against Tarcisio Ôüáde Freitas, governor of the state of Sao Paulo, ÔÇő45% of respondents said they would vote for Lula in ÔÇőthe second round, while 42% would vote for de Freitas, also a technical tie.
Brazil's general election is scheduled for October 4. If no presidential ÔÇőcandidate receives a majority of the vote, the two ÔÇőcandidates with the most votes will face off in a runoff on ÔÇîOctober Ôüá25.
According to the survey, Lula, 80, who will be vying for his fourth presidential term, leads in all firstÔÇĹround scenarios. However, FlavioÔÇÖs candidacy has gained strength since his name was put ÔÇőforward in December ÔÇőby Jair ÔüáBolsonaro, who is in jail.
Lula and Flavio, 44, also have the highest rejection rates in the ÔÇőpoll. Some 46% of respondents said they ÔÇőwould not Ôüávote for Lula under any circumstances, while 45% said the same about Flavio - another technical tie.
Datafolha interviewed 2,004 voters from ÔüáTuesday to ÔÇőThursday in 137 municipalities. The survey ÔÇőhas a margin of error of two percentage points in either direction.

item.title: UAE president says country is well and is no easy prey in first public comments since Iran strikes
item.description:
DUBAI, March 7 (Reuters) - The president of the United Arab Emirates said ÔÇőhis nation was in a ÔÇîtime of war but was well and told his enemies it was ÔÇőno easy prey, in his ÔÇőfirst public comments since Iran Ôüálaunched missiles at its Gulf ÔÇőneighbour amid U.S.-Israeli strikes.
"The UAE has ÔÇőthick skin and bitter flesh - we are no easy prey," Mohammed bin ÔÇőZayed Al Nahyan, who is ÔÇőalso ruler of Abu Dhabi, said in ÔÇîcomments, Ôüámade on Friday when visiting those injured in strikes, aired on Abu Dhabi TV on Saturday.
"We ÔÇőwill carry ÔÇőout Ôüáour duty towards our country, our people, and ÔÇőour residents who are also ÔÇőpart Ôüáof our family," he said.
The UAE, which consists of seven Ôüáemirates ÔÇőincluding Dubai, would protect ÔÇőeveryone in the country, he said.

item.title: Associate of liquidated Brazilian lender Banco Master's owner dies, lawyers say
item.description:
SAO PAULO, March 7 (Reuters) - Luiz Phillipi Mourao, an associate of lender Banco Master's owner Daniel Vorcaro, died ÔÇőon Friday night, according to a statement ÔÇîreleased by his attorneys on Saturday.
Mourao was declared brain dead after a suicide attempt, police said on Wednesday, when he ÔÇőwas among those arrested in the latest ÔÇőphase of the investigation involving the liquidated ÔüáMaster.
Master, which held under 1% of Brazil's banking ÔÇőassets, was liquidated in November due to what the ÔÇőcentral bank called a severe liquidity crisis, sharp financial deterioration and serious rule violations.
The investigation into the lender's unraveling ÔÇőhas exposed a network of influence and conflicts ÔÇőof interest shaking trust in some of Brazil's most powerful ÔÇîinstitutions.
Vorcaro Ôüáwas arrested on Wednesday, accused of bribing two central bank officials and also plotting to attack and intimidate people he perceived as acting against his interests ÔÇőwith Mourao, ÔÇőwhom he Ôüácalled "Sicario" after the hit men employed by Mexican cartels.
Vorcaro's lawyers said they denied ÔÇőthe allegations, and they expected to ÔÇődemonstrate "the Ôüáregularity of his conduct."
Minas Gerais state civil police said on Saturday that MouraoÔÇÖs body was sent to Ôüáforensic ÔÇőscientists for examination and would ÔÇőbe released to his family after tests are completed.

item.title: Israeli settler fatally shoots Palestinian man in West Bank, health ministry says
item.description:
RAMALLAH, West Bank March 7 (Reuters) - An Israeli settler shot dead a 27-year-old ÔÇőPalestinian man in the occupied ÔÇîWest Bank on Saturday, the Palestinian Health Ministry said.
The man was killed in ÔÇőthe village area of Masafer ÔÇőYatta, near the Palestinian city of ÔüáHebron, according to the Health ÔÇőMinistry and the official Palestinian news ÔÇőagency WAFA.
The Israeli military did not immediately respond to a request for comment on ÔÇőthe incident.
Masafer Yatta is among ÔÇőthe sites where Palestinians have been increasingly displaced ÔÇîby Ôüásettler violence that has risen sharply over the past two years, according to the United Nations.
Two Palestinian brothers ÔÇőwere shot ÔÇődead Ôüáby an Israeli on Monday near the city of ÔÇőNablus in the northern West ÔÇőBank, Ôüáthe Palestinian Health Ministry said.
Israeli newspaper Haaretz said the military police Ôüáwere ÔÇőinvestigating a reserve soldier ÔÇőfor Monday's shooting.

item.title: Indian Oil Corp books some oil cargoes from Red Sea, source says
item.description:
March 7 (Reuters) - Top Indian refiner, Indian Oil Corp (IOC.NS) , opens new tab , has booked some oil cargoes for loading ÔÇőfrom the Red Sea port of Yanbu, ÔÇîa company source said on Saturday.
The U.S.-Israel war with Iran has disrupted oil and natural gas ÔÇőexports from the Middle East, while ÔÇőtop oil exporter Saudi Arabia is increasing shipments Ôüáfrom the Red Sea as an alternative.

item.title: Earthquake of magnitude 5.5 strikes Indonesia's southern Sumatra region, GFZ says
item.description:
March 7 (Reuters) - An earthquake ÔÇőof magnitude ÔÇî5.5 struck ÔÇőIndonesia's ÔÇősouthern Sumatra region Ôüáon ÔÇőSunday, the ÔÇőGerman Research Center ÔÇőfor ÔÇőGeosciences (GFZ) said.
The quake ÔÇîwas Ôüáat a depth of ÔÇő101 ÔÇőkm (62.76 Ôüámiles), ÔÇőGFZ said.
