item.title: Over 150 Iranian nationals leave Lebanon including diplomats, Lebanese security source says
item.description:
March 7 (Reuters) - More than 150 Iranian nationals, including ÔÇődiplomats and their families, ÔÇîleft Lebanon on Saturday, a senior Lebanese security ÔÇősource told Reuters, ÔÇőafter an Israeli military Ôüáspokesperson threatened "representatives" of Iran in ÔÇőLebanon.
The security source ÔÇősaid they were being flown to Russia on a Russian ÔÇőplane, and that ÔÇőanother 20 Iranians had left ÔÇîon ÔüáFriday following the start of a new war between Lebanese armed ÔÇőgroup ÔÇőHezbollah Ôüáand Israel. An Israeli military spokesperson ÔÇőon Tuesday told ÔÇőrepresentatives Ôüáof the Iranian government "still in Lebanon to Ôüáleave ÔÇőimmediately before ÔÇőthey are targeted."

item.title: Venezuela's Rodriguez commits to dialogue in message to Trump
item.description:
March 7 (Reuters) - Venezuela's acting president, Delcy Rodriguez, said on Saturday that "diplomatic dialogue" ÔÇőwith the U.S. was the way ÔÇîto resolve the two countries' differences, after both governments agreed on diplomatic and consular relations.
"We reiterate our willingness ÔÇőto build long-term relations based on ÔÇőmutual respect, equality, and international law, with Ôüáa view to promoting a work agenda ÔÇőthat strengthens cooperation for the benefit of both ÔÇőcountries," Rodriguez said in a post on X addressed to U.S. President Donald Trump.
The U.S. State Department announced ÔÇőon Thursday that the two countries would formally ÔÇőre-establish diplomatic ties.
Caracas severed diplomatic relations with Washington in 2019 ÔÇîafter Ôüáthe first Trump administration refused to recognize Venezuela's then-President Nicolas Maduro as the country's legitimate leader, following a disputed election, and instead recognized ÔÇőan opposition ÔÇőlawmaker as Ôüáthe country's president.
U.S. forces captured Maduro in January after months of ÔÇőheightened tensions between the two countries, ÔÇősetting Ôüáoff a chain of changes in Venezuela, including the swearing-in of interim President Delcy Rodriguez.
The two Ôüáhave ÔÇősince gradually resumed bilateral relations, ÔÇőafter Rodriguez's interim government expressed interest in rebuilding ties with ÔÇőWashington.

item.title: ADNOC says it is managing offshore output, onshore operations continue
item.description:
March 7 (Reuters) - Abu Dhabi National Oil Company said on Saturday it is actively managing offshore output levels to ÔÇőaddress storage requirements amid the U.S.-Isaeli war on Iran, ÔÇîwhile its onshore operations are continuing.
"This approach preserves operational flexibility and will enable the company to resume normal operations without prolonged delay," ADNOC said in ÔÇőa statement.
The war, now in its eighth day, has ÔÇőblocked shipments through the Strait of Hormuz, the crucial waterway Ôüáresponsible for roughly 20% of global oil and LNG supply.
Analysts ÔÇőhave predicted the UAE and Saudi Arabia would soon have to cut ÔÇőoutput as their oil storage fills up.
ADNOC said its operations are continuing, and that it is using export capacity that bypasses the strait as well ÔÇőas international storage facilities to ensure supply continuity to global markets.
The ÔÇőUAE can bypass the strait using the Abu Dhabi Crude Oil Pipeline, also ÔÇîknown Ôüáas the Habshan-Fujairah Pipeline.
The pipeline, which has a capacity of 1.5 million to 1.8 million barrels per day, transports oil from Abu Dhabi's fields directly to the port of Fujairah on the ÔÇőGulf of Oman, ÔÇőfeeding its storage Ôüáterminals and refining facilities.
"Business units are assessing the situation on a product-by-product and transaction-by-transaction basis, considering ÔÇőthe ongoing disruption that is affecting shipping through the ÔÇőStrait Ôüáof Hormuz," it said.
ADNOC has activated well-established protocols and is working closely with authorities to protect its people, assets and operations, it added.
Kuwait ÔüáPetroleum ÔÇőCorporation began cutting oil output on Saturday ÔÇőand declared force majeure, adding to earlier oil and gas reductions from Iraq and ÔÇőQatar.

item.title: Earthquake of magnitude 5.2 strikes Afghanistan's Hindu Kush region, GFZ says
item.description: March 7 (Reuters) - An earthquake ÔÇőof magnitude ÔÇî5.2 struck ÔÇőAfghanistan's ÔÇőHindu Kush region Ôüáon ÔÇőSaturday, the ÔÇőGerman Research Center for ÔÇőGeosciences (GFZ) ÔÇősaid. The quake ÔÇîwas Ôüáat a depth ÔÇőof ÔÇő206 Ôüákm (128 miles), ÔÇőGFZ ÔÇősaid.

item.title: Cracks emerge in Iran's leadership
item.description:
DUBAI, March 7 (Reuters) - Iran's hierarchy is showing signs of fracturing over a war its leaders see as existential, with angry divisions between hardliners and more pragmatic factions laid bare by a row over President Masoud Pezeshkian's promise not to strike Gulf states.
Fissures within Iran's ruling elite were ÔÇőlong suppressed under the iron rule of Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei, but his killing a week ago has allowed them to spill out into the open as ÔÇîU.S. and Israeli strikes pile pressure on Tehran.
The unrelenting bombardment mortally imperils the Islamic Republic and has prompted its fiercest acolytes, the Revolutionary Guards , to seize a bigger role in strategy despite a decapitation campaign that has killed many top commanders.
Sources close to Iran's leadership, speaking from inside the country, told Reuters the strains were starting to show among leading figures still alive after a series of killings in the U.S.-Israeli strikes. They spoke anonymously due to the ÔÇősensitivity of the matter.
In a sign of the growing stresses to the system, clerics are accelerating the appointment of a new supreme leader with a decision possible on Sunday - though ÔÇőit is far from clear if Khamenei's successor will wield enough authority to stamp out factional disputes.
While his son, Mojtaba Khamenei , is seen as a frontrunner Ôüábacked by the Guards and his father's powerful office, he is untested, junior to most of Iran's senior ayatollahs, and has alienated moderates within the system.
Other potential candidates could struggle to uphold ÔÇőthe unquestioning obedience of the Guards required to maintain discipline within the system.
"Wartime tends to clarify power structures, and in this case the decisive voice is not that of the civilian leadership but of ÔÇőthe IRGC," said Alex Vatanka, a senior fellow at the Middle East Institute, using an abbreviation of the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps.
Pezeshkian's apology to Gulf states for a week-long blitz of their territory - and his pledge to rein in such attacks - quickly prompted pushback from hardliners in the Revolutionary Guards and clerical elite, forcing him into a partial climbdown.
In one of the most open criticisms of Pezeshkian - and a sign of ÔÇőinternal division, hardline cleric and lawmaker Hamid Rasai addressed the president on social media, saying: "your stance was unprofessional, weak and unacceptable."
When the president later repeated his earlier statement on social media, he ÔÇőleft out the apology that had so angered the Guards and other hardliners - an embarrassing retreat.
To be sure, all senior figures within the hierarchy are steadfast in their commitment to defending the Islamic Republic and its revolutionary ÔÇîtheocracy from ÔüáU.S. and Israeli attacks, but there are clear splits over their strategic approach.
Iran's leadership has sometimes played up differences between hardliners and moderates as a tactic in negotiations with the West, but the dispute over Pezeshkian's statement on Saturday revealed genuine divisions, two senior sources said.
A hardliner close to Khamenei's office, which remains a central node in the hierarchy, told Reuters that Pezeshkian's comments had angered many senior commanders in the Guards.
Another senior Iranian source, a moderate former official, said nobody would be able to fill Khamenei's shoes, describing the late leader as a formidable strategist who had led Iran through ÔÇőmany difficult periods.
With anxiety increasing in Iran's top ÔÇőranks, senior ayatollahs began to publicly urge that Ôüáthe clerical body responsible for appointing a supreme leader accelerate its work.
"It should expedite the process so that it leads to the disappointment of the enemy and the preservation of the unity and solidarity of the nation," Ayatollah Nouri Hamedani said in a statement carried by the semi-official Fars ÔÇőNews Agency.
In Iran's unusual system, an elected president, government and parliament are subservient to a clerically appointed ayatollah ÔÇőwho wields ultimate authority as Ôüásupreme leader and personally oversees the Revolutionary Guards and other powerful bodies of state.
As leader for 36 years, Khamenei often played hardline and moderate factions within the ruling system against each other while retaining the ultimate say, allowing them to voice disagreements so long as they bowed to his writ.
When he died, leadership formally passed to a constitutionally mandated interim council that included Pezeshkian, the clerical head of the judiciary Ôüáand another cleric ÔÇőfrom a hardline body called the Guardian Council.
In Khamenei's absence, strains are showing even inside that tight body, with ÔÇőthe judiciary chief, noted hardliner Ayatollah Gholamhossein Mohseni-Ejei, saying some regional states had allowed their territory to be used for attacks.
"Heavy strikes on those targets will continue," he said, contradicting Pezeshkian's more conciliatory statement.
Still, even though Khamenei did sometimes allow moderate ÔÇőor reformist voices to carry the day in disputes with hardliners, they were usually overruled when the system seemed to come under threat.

item.title: As Iran's retaliation began, US officials scrambled to arrange evacuations
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 7 (Reuters) - As the first explosions from Iran's retaliatory attack sounded across the United Arab Emirates last Saturday, the State Department was still scrambling ÔÇőto finalize a key bureaucratic task - securing approvals for at least three U.S. embassies in the region to evacuate non-essential personnel.
Memos asking State Department leadership to approve evacuations for U.S. missions in Bahrain, ÔÇîKuwait and Qatar, all of which were already coming under fire from Tehran on Saturday, were not sent up for clearance and approved until hours after the United States and Israel launched their war on Iran , and in several cases not until the following day, according to two sources familiar with the matter and half a dozen internal State Department cables seen by Reuters.
The release of public announcements that the U.S. was starting to pull out non-essential staff from Gulf Arab countries began Monday, three days into the war. For the U.S. ÔÇőembassy in Riyadh, the approval for authorized departure came on Tuesday, four days into the war and on the same day that it was struck by Iranian drones that led to a fire that ÔÇődamaged the mission's facilities.
The delay was unusual. Typically, the United States starts evacuations for a planned military action well before the event itself.
When the United States invaded Iraq in Ôüá2003, staff and U.S. citizens in the region had weeks to prepare, and at least two evacuations began more than a week before the actual operation started. Ahead of last week's strikes, Israel and Lebanon were the only regional embassies with orders for ÔÇőnon-essential personnel to depart.
The attack on Iran - the biggest U.S. military operation in the Middle East since 2003 - has put enormous strain on officials from the U.S. and other countries with citizens in the region. But lawmakers, former diplomats ÔÇőand sources involved in the process said the United States was unusually slow in activating contingency plans both for its own personnel and for thousands of stranded Americans.
The State Department's principal deputy spokesperson, Tommy Pigott, said hundreds of people were involved in the effort to help Americans return.
"We are working 24/7 and have contingency plans ready to go and implement when needed, including the ability to immediately activate the task force, which was done here," Pigott said in a statement to Reuters.
One factor underlying the uneven ÔÇőapproach, said sources familiar with the matter, was that ahead of the war's start, Trump officials kept contingency planning to a small group of officials.
In one case, officials involved in helping Americans get home had found out from a social ÔÇőmedia post from a senior Trump official that Washington was now offering charter flights to U.S. citizens, according to two people familiar with the situation.
"No directive came from anywhere," one of the people said.
Pigott said announcements on the task force that the ÔÇîDepartment created specifically Ôüáto tackle the fallout from the crisis and charter flights were coordinated with relevant officials.
In another instance, the alert for Americans to leave the region came not through normal State Department channels but again via a social media post.
The top State Department official for consular affairs, Mora Namdar, posted on X on Monday urging Americans across 14 countries in the Middle East to leave and said the U.S. was working to facilitate charter flights for U.S. citizens.
But because that message was drafted outside normal channels, State Department staff were surprised and had to update the department's formal travel advisory system that American companies and others rely on for guidance for their overseas personnel, according to two people familiar with ÔÇőthe situation.
As of Saturday, the U.S. State Department said ÔÇőit has completed "over a dozen charter flights and has Ôüásafely evacuated thousands of Americans" from the Middle East. It did not say from which locations exactly the charter flights arrived.
One flight that departed Dubai bound for Washington on Friday carrying 182 embassy personnel and their families, and 51 private U.S. citizens, was only the second charter to depart from that country, according to a March 6 cable reviewed by ÔÇőReuters. Since then, additional flights were set to take place.
When asked to confirm if specific plans were finalized ahead of the war to help evacuate American citizens from ÔÇőthe 14 countries, a State Department Ôüáofficial briefing reporters earlier this week gave a broad response.
"I wouldn't say that specifically. What I would say is, we always have contingency plans, and we're always ready to assist Americans. That's what I would say to that question," the official, speaking on the condition of anonymity, said.
The Department on Friday said it had assisted 13,000 Americans who reached out seeking help to depart.
As the Department rushed to implement plans to help Americans, novelist and filmmaker Mohana Rajakumar in Doha dialed the hotline that top ÔüáU.S. officials said ÔÇőstranded U.S. citizens should call for help. The recorded audio told her not to rely on government help to depart even as the ÔÇőU.S. government repeatedly says the safety and security of U.S. citizens around the world is its top priority.
"I can tell you every WhatsApp group that I'm in with Americans, nobody feels that way," Rajakumar said, speaking to Reuters from Doha. "Everyone is asking why didn't they tell us to leave ÔÇőgiven they knew they were going to do this? Why didn't we have the option to leave?"
Officials said that recorded message was later updated.

item.title: Over 30 hurt in Peru after nightclub explosion causes chaos
item.description:
March 7 (Reuters) - An early morning explosion at a nightclub in northwestern Peru injured at ÔÇőleast 31 people, authorities said on ÔÇîSaturday.
The government of Peru's La Libertad region said hospitals were treating individuals after the blast ÔÇőat the nightclub in Trujillo, ÔÇőwhich authorities are investigating.
Four people are in Ôüágrave condition, the newspaper La Republica ÔÇősaid, citing the regional health authority, which ÔÇősaid minors were among those injured.
It is unclear what caused the incident. Peru has experienced a ÔÇősurge in violence related to organized ÔÇőcrime, and Saturday's incident follows smaller attacks on ÔÇînightclubs Ôüáin Trujillo, according to local media reports.
The explosion occurred during a show by a Peruvian musical group and sent revelers ÔÇőrunning for ÔÇősafety.
Members of Ôüáthe Amor Rebelde group were unhurt after what they said ÔÇőwere "unfortunate events" that occurred at ÔÇőthe Ôüánightclub where they performed, according to a statement from the group on Facebook.
Video shared Ôüáon ÔÇőa Peruvian news program ÔÇőshowed scenes of chaos after a loud boom was ÔÇőheard.

item.title: Exclusive: Saudi has told Iran not to attack it, warns of possible retaliation, sources say
item.description:
March 7 (Reuters) - Saudi Arabia has told Tehran that while it favours a diplomatic settlement to Iran's conflict with the United States, continued attacks on the kingdom and ÔÇőits energy sector could push Riyadh to respond in kind, four sources familiar with the matter told Reuters.
The message was conveyed before a speech on ÔÇîSaturday in which Iranian President Masoud Pezeshkian apologised to neighbouring Gulf states for TehranÔÇÖs actions -- an apparent attempt to defuse regional anger over Iranian strikes that hit civilian targets.
Two days earlier, Saudi Foreign Minister Prince Faisal bin Farhan spoke to Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Araqchi and set out RiyadhÔÇÖs position with clarity, the sources said.
Saudi Arabia is open to any form of mediation aimed at deÔÇĹescalation and a negotiated settlement, the sources quoted ÔÇőthe minister as saying, underlining that neither Riyadh nor other Gulf states had let the U.S. use their airspace or territory to launch airstrikes on Iran.
But Prince ÔÇőFaisal was also quoted by the sources as saying that if Iranian attacks persisted against Saudi territory or energy infrastructure, Saudi Arabia would Ôüábe forced to permit U.S. forces to use their bases there for military operations. Riyadh would retaliate if attacks on the kingdom's critical energy facilities continued, he said.
The sources said ÔÇőthe kingdom had remained in regular contact with Tehran through its ambassador since the U.S. and Israeli military campaign against Iran began on February 28 following the collapse of talks on ÔÇőIran's nuclear programme.
The Saudi and Iranian foreign ministries did not respond to requests for comment.
The United Arab Emirates, Kuwait, Qatar, Bahrain and Saudi Arabia have all come under heavy drone and missile fire from Iran over the past week.
Iran's Supreme Leader, Ayatollah Ali Khamenei , was killed on the first day of the war. Tehran responded by hitting Israel and Gulf Arab states hosting U.S. military installations, ÔÇőand Israel has attacked Lebanon's Iran-backed Hezbollah armed group.
Araqchi said in an interview on Saturday that he remained in constant contact with his Saudi counterpart and other Saudi officials, adding ÔÇőthat Riyadh had assured Tehran it was fully committed to not allowing its territory, waters or airspace to be used for attacks against Iran.
Pezeshkian said Iran's temporary leadership council had approved suspending attacks on ÔÇînearby countries - unless Ôüáan attack on Iran came from those nations.
"I personally apologise to neighbouring countries that were affected by Iran's actions," he said.
To what extent Pezeshkian's remarks signal a change is unclear. There were further reports of strikes directed at Gulf states on Saturday.
Also, in a sign of possible divisions within IranÔÇÖs leadership, Khatam al-Anbiya Central Headquarters - the unified combatant command of the Iranian armed forces - said in a statement afterwards that U.S. and Israeli bases and interests across the region would remain targets.
The command said Iran's armed forces respected the sovereignty and interests of ÔÇőneighbouring states and had not taken action against ÔÇőthem so far. But it said U.S. Ôüáand Israeli military bases and assets on land, at sea and in the air across the region would be treated as primary targets and face "powerful and heavy" strikes by IranÔÇÖs forces.
U.S. President Donald Trump said in a social media post that Iran had "apologized and surrendered to its Middle ÔÇőEast neighbours, and promised that it will not shoot at them anymore. This promise was only made because of the relentless U.S. ÔÇőand Israeli attack."
Two Iranian Ôüásources confirmed that a call had taken place in which Riyadh warned Tehran to halt attacks on Saudi Arabia and neighbouring Gulf states. Iran, they said, reiterated its position that the strikes were not aimed at Gulf countries themselves but at U.S. interests and military bases hosted on their territory.
One Iranian source said that Tehran had in response demanded that U.S. bases in the region be Ôüáclosed and ÔÇősome Gulf states stop sharing intelligence with Washington that Iran believes is being used to carry out attacks against it.
Another ÔÇőIranian source said some military commanders were pressing to continue the strikes, accusing the U.S. of using bases in Gulf states and these countries' airspace to conduct operations against Iran.
Iran had in recent years mended fences with its ÔÇőGulf neighbours, including former regional arch-rival Saudi Arabia. The diplomatic campaign imploded in the blitz of drones and missiles launched by Iran's Revolutionary Guards in the past week.

item.title: Katyusha rockets target US embassy in Baghdad, security sources say
item.description:
March 7 (Reuters) - Katyusha rockets targeted the U.S. embassy in Baghdad on Saturday ÔÇőand sirens sounded, security sources and witnesses said, though ÔÇîit was not immediately clear whether the rockets caused any damage or casualties.
The attack was the first time the embassy had been fired ÔÇőon in more than two years. The last ÔÇőrecorded strike was in late 2023, when around seven Ôüámortar rounds landed in the compound amid a wave ÔÇőof attacks by IranÔÇĹaligned militias on U.S. assets in Iraq ÔÇőand Syria over WashingtonÔÇÖs support for Israel in its war against Hamas in Gaza.
Explosions were heard near the embassy in central Baghdad around ÔÇő9 p.m. (1800 GMT), and sirens warning people to take cover ÔÇősounded, witnesses said.
A senior Iraqi security official told Reuters the CÔÇĹRAM defence ÔÇîsystem Ôüábrought down one of the rockets and that none landed inside the embassy. The official said there were no American casualties.
The attack indicates that IranÔÇĹaligned Iraqi militias ÔÇö who have ÔÇővowed to retaliate ÔÇőfor the Ôüákilling of IranÔÇÖs supreme leader ÔÇö have widened their targets beyond U.S. military bases in Iraqi ÔÇőKurdistan and U.S. energy interests to now include ÔÇőthe ÔüáU.S. Embassy.
Iraqi Prime Minister Mohammed Shia alÔÇĹSudani ordered security forces to track down those who launched the projectiles, calling them ÔÇťrogue groups Ôüáoperating ÔÇőoutside the law that do not ÔÇőrepresent the will of the Iraqi people,ÔÇŁ a statement from his office said.

item.title: Ukraine's Zelenskiy says he spoke to Saudi crown prince
item.description:
March 7 (Reuters) - Ukrainian President Volodymyr ÔÇőZelenskiy said on Saturday he ÔÇîhad spoken to Saudi Crown Prince Mohammed bin Salman about the situation in ÔÇőIran and the Middle East ÔÇőand restated Kyiv's offer to help Ôüádeal with Iranian drones.
"Ukraine has ÔÇőbeen fighting against (Iranian-designed) 'Shaheds' for years, and ÔÇőeveryone acknowledges that no other country in the world has such experience," Zelenskiy said ÔÇőon Telegram of his conversation.
"We ÔÇőare ready to help and expect that ÔÇîour Ôüápeople will also receive the necessary support."
Zelenskiy said earlier this week he had spoken to the leaders ÔÇőof Bahrain, ÔÇőJordan, ÔüáKuwait, Qatar and the United Arab Emirates.
Zelenskiy has also ÔÇősaid Ukraine, which has faced ÔÇőregular Ôüáattacks by Shaheds in the four-year-old war with Russia, would help Ôüáthe ÔÇőUnited States by providing ÔÇőspecialists to counter the weapons.

item.title: Trump tells Britain he does not need its help to win Iran war
item.description:
March 7 (Reuters) - President Donald Trump said on Saturday that Britain is giving "serious thought" to ÔÇősending two aircraft carriers to the Middle East, but added ÔÇîthat the U.S. does not need them to win the war with Iran, in the latest clash between the military allies.
Trump has repeatedly criticised British Prime Minister Keir ÔÇőStarmer suggesting this week that he helped "ruin" the countries' historically close ÔÇőrelationship after London blocked the U.S.'s initial use of British Ôüábases to attack Iran.
In a post on Truth Social, Trump said ÔÇőhe "will remember" the lack of British support during the conflict with Iran.
"The United ÔÇőKingdom, our once Great Ally, maybe the Greatest of them all, is finally giving serious thought to sending two aircraft carriers to the Middle East," Trump said.
"ThatÔÇÖs OK, Prime ÔÇőMinister Starmer, we donÔÇÖt need them any longer ÔÇö But we will ÔÇőremember. We donÔÇÖt need people that join Wars after weÔÇÖve already won!"
The social media post ÔÇîcomes Ôüáafter the British Ministry of Defence said on Saturday it was preparing the Prince of Wales aircraft carrier for possible deployment.
But no final decision has been taken about whether to deploy the aircraft carrier to the Middle ÔÇőEast, a British ÔÇőofficial said.
Starmer has Ôüádefended his decision not to allow U.S. forces to use British bases to support initial strikes on Iran, ÔÇősaying he needed to be satisfied that any military action ÔÇőwas Ôüálegal and well planned.
He later granted U.S. forces permission to use British bases for what he called defensive strikes against Iranian missiles in storage depots or Ôüálaunchers.
Starmer ÔÇőearlier this year criticised Trump's desire to buy ÔÇőGreenland and said his comments that European troops avoided frontline combat in the war in ÔÇőAfghanistan were "frankly appalling."

item.title: White House halts security bulletin warning of Iran-related threats
item.description:
NEW YORK, March 7 (Reuters) - The White House has halted, at least for now, a federal security bulletin warning of ÔÇőa heightened threat to the U.S. in light of the conflict ÔÇîin Iran, a Trump administration official told Reuters.
The bulletin from the FBI, Department of Homeland Security, and National Counterterrorism Center is meant for state and local law enforcement agencies. ÔÇőThe Trump administration asked an agency to hold the immediate release ÔÇőof the bulletin for review to ensure its accuracy, said the Ôüáofficial, who spoke on condition of anonymity to discuss internal government matters.
The ÔÇőwarning, which was drafted by DHSÔÇÖ Office of Intelligence and Analysis, did not ÔÇőoffer sufficient insight and was not well written, the official told Reuters.
The Daily Mail reported on Friday the White House blocked the release of the bulletin that contains specific details ÔÇőon how Iranian proxies may carry out attacks across the United States.
The ÔÇőFBI and the National Counterterrorism Center did not immediately respond to a request for comment.
A ÔÇîU.S. ÔüáDepartment of Homeland Security spokesperson told Reuters it is normal procedure for the agency to share intelligence bulletins with the White House for review before they are sent out.
"The White House is coordinating closely with all government agencies to ÔÇőensure information being disseminated ÔÇőis accurate, up Ôüáto date, and has been properly vetted ÔÇö even if that means taking additional time to review to ensure nothing ÔÇőis done in a vacuum,ÔÇŁ the White House said in ÔÇőa Ôüástatement.
The attack on Iran is the biggest U.S. military operation in the Middle East since the 2003 invasion of Iraq.
Since last week, the U.S. completed over a Ôüádozen charter ÔÇőflights and evacuated thousands of Americans from the ÔÇőMiddle East, the State Department said on Saturday.

item.title: Four killed in Beirut hotel strike, Israel says it targeted Iranian commanders
item.description:
BEIRUT, March 8 (Reuters) - At least four people were ÔÇőkilled when an Israeli strike hit an apartment ÔÇîin the Ramada hotel building in central Beirut early on Sunday, with Israel saying it targeted Iranian commanders operating in the Lebanese capital.
The ÔÇőattack marked the first Israeli strike to hit ÔÇőthe heart of Beirut since Israel-Hezbollah hostilities resumed last Ôüáweek.
Ten people were also injured in the attack on ÔÇőBeirut's Raouche area, the Lebanese health ministry said in a ÔÇőstatement.
Israel said it targeted key commanders of Iran's elite Quds Force of the Revolutionary Guards but did not name them.
"The commanders of the ÔÇőQuds Force's Lebanon Corps operated to advance terror attacks ÔÇőagainst the state of Israel and its civilians, while operating simultaneously for ÔÇîthe ÔüáIRGC in Iran," the Israeli military said in a statement.
The hotel was housing displaced people fleeing the war in southern Lebanon and Beirut's southern suburbs, and some were seen leaving ÔÇőthe building ÔÇőfor fear of Ôüáfurther air strikes.
Last week, Israel said it had killed the commander of Iran's Quds Force ÔÇőin Lebanon, Daoud Ali Zadeh, in a ÔÇőstrike in ÔüáTehran.
Lebanon was pulled into the widening U.S.-Israel war with Iran on Monday after the Iran-backed militant group Hezbollah fired rockets Ôüáand ÔÇődrones into Israel. Israel responded with ÔÇőheavy strikes across southern and eastern Lebanon and near Beirut.

item.title: Explosion hits US embassy in Oslo, causing minor damage, police say
item.description:
OSLO, March 8 (Reuters) - The U.S. embassy in Oslo ÔÇőwas hit by an explosion early ÔÇîon Sunday, causing minor damage but no injuries, Norwegian police said.
A loud blast was heard near ÔÇőthe embassy compound in western Oslo ÔÇőat around 1 a.m. (0000 GMT), and smoke Ôüáwas seen rising from the compound, eyewitnesses ÔÇőtold local media.
"We've determined that an explosion ÔÇőhit the American embassy," police spokesperson Mikael Dellemyr told public broadcaster NRK, adding that the blast occurred ÔÇőat the entry to the consular section.
It ÔÇőwas not immediately clear what caused the blast or ÔÇîwho Ôüáwas involved, the Oslo police department said in a separate statement.
"The police are in a dialogue with the embassy and there ÔÇőare no ÔÇőreports of Ôüáany injured persons," the statement said.
The embassy did not immediately respond ÔÇőto a request for comment outside ÔÇőof Ôüáregular business hours.
The U.S. State Department did not immediately respond to a request for Ôüácomment.

item.title: Trump rejects settling Iran war, raises prospect of killing all its potential leaders
item.description:
BEIRUT/MIAMI/TEL AVIV/DUBAI, March 8 (Reuters) - U.S. President Donald Trump said he is not interested in negotiating with Iran and ÔÇőraised the possibility that the Iran war would only end once Tehran no longer has a functioning military or any remaining leadership in power.
Speaking to reporters aboard Air Force One on Saturday, Trump said ÔÇîthe air campaign could make negotiations a moot point if all potential leaders of Iran are killed and the Iranian military is destroyed.
"At some point, I don't think there will be anybody left maybe to say 'We surrender,'" Trump said.
Israel and Iran traded numerous attacks on Saturday as the U.S.-Israeli war against Iran entered a second week. Iran's president apologized to neighboring states for its attacks on U.S. facilities in those countries, in an attempt to cool anger across the Gulf, but stirred criticism from hardliners at home.
"I personally apologize to ÔÇőneighboring countries that were affected by Iran's actions," Iranian President Masoud Pezeshkian said, urging them not to join U.S.-Israeli attacks on Iran.
He dismissed Trump's demand for the Islamic Republic's unconditional surrender as "a dream," but said its temporary ÔÇőleadership council had agreed to suspend attacks on nearby states unless strikes on Iran originated from their territory.
Pezeshkian's comments caused a political stir in Iran, prompting his office to reiterate ÔüáIran's military would respond firmly to attacks from U.S. bases. Ali Larijani, Iran's secretary of the Supreme National Security Council, said on state television there was no rift among Iranian officials over its handling of the war.
The U.S. State Department did ÔÇőnot immediately respond to a request for comment.
Saudi Arabia has told Tehran that continued Iranian attacks on the kingdom and its energy sector could push Riyadh to respond in kind, four people familiar with the matter told Reuters.
The governments of Saudi ÔÇőArabia, Kuwait and the United Arab Emirates reported Iranian drone attacks in their countries on Saturday and early Sunday with varying degrees of damage but no reported deaths. Iran's Revolutionary Guards also targeted U.S. forces at a base in Bahrain, Iranian state media said.
In the United States, the White House halted for now a federal security bulletin that would have warned of a heightened threat to the U.S. in light of the Iran conflict, a Trump administration official told Reuters. But a recent U.S. intelligence assessment had warned that Iran and its proxies "probably" ÔÇőpose a threat of targeted attacks on the United States.
In Oslo, the U.S. embassy was hit by an explosion early on Sunday, causing minor damage but no injuries, Norwegian police said. Smoke was seen rising from the area around the embassy ÔÇőcompound, eyewitnesses told Norwegian daily Verdens Gang. It was not immediately clear what caused the blast or who was involved.
Huge explosions were heard in parts of Tehran, state media reported, while Israel said it had struck Iranian missile sites and command centers.
The U.S.-Israeli ÔÇîattacks have killed at Ôüáleast 1,332 Iranian civilians and wounded thousands, according to Iran's U.N. ambassador, Amir Saeid Iravani.
U.S. forces were likely responsible for an apparent strike on an Iranian girls' school that killed scores of children, U.S. officials have told Reuters. But Trump, without citing evidence, told reporters on Saturday that Iran was responsible.
"We think it was done by Iran because they are very inaccurate as you know with their munitions. They have no accuracy whatsoever. It was done by Iran," said Trump. Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth, standing behind Trump aboard Air Force One, said the matter was still under investigation.
Iranian attacks have killed 10 people in Israel. At least six U.S. service members have been killed. Their remains arrived on Saturday at an Air Force ÔÇőbase in Delaware.
In ÔÇőIran, local news agencies, citing an Iranian Oil Ministry Ôüásource, said its fuel depots were hit by strikes in three areas, including Karaj, west of Tehran.
Tehran has responded to the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran by hitting Israel and Gulf Arab states hosting U.S. military installations. Israel has launched fresh attacks in Lebanon after the Iran-aligned militia Hezbollah fired across the border.
With the conflict spreading, Israel warned Lebanon of a "very heavy price" ÔÇőif it did not rein in Iran-allied Hezbollah militants, as it pounded the group's strongholds with airstrikes and mounted a deadly airborne raid in the east.
On Saturday morning, ÔÇőmore buildings in Beirut's Hezbollah-controlled southern Ôüásuburbs had been reduced to rubble, dust and tangled wires, Reuters video showed.
The death toll from Israel's attacks on Lebanon since Monday rose to around 300, after at least four people were killed when an Israeli strike hit an apartment in the Ramada hotel building in central Beirut, Lebanon's health ministry said. It was the first strike to hit the heart of the capital since Israel-Hezbollah hostilities resumed last week.
Iran's apparent strategy of maximum chaos has driven up the costs of the conflict by raising energy Ôüáprices and hurting ÔÇőglobal business and logistics links.
Kuwait's national oil company began cutting output on Saturday, adding to earlier oil and gas cuts from Iraq and Qatar.
The ÔÇőwar has roiled global markets and oil prices have hit multi-year highs with the Strait of Hormuz effectively shut.
Hardline clerics have called for the swift selection of a new supreme leader, Iranian media reported on Saturday, with meetings occurring as soon as Sunday.

item.title: Australia weighing requests for assistance from countries attacked by Iran
item.description:
SYDNEY, March 8 (Reuters) - Australia's government said on Sunday it was considering requests to help protect countries attacked by Iran in ÔÇőthe widening Middle East conflict, but reiterated it would ÔÇînot take part in any military operations in Iran.
"We've had many countries, which are non participants, (that) have been attacked by Iran through this. ÔÇőYou would anticipate as a consequence that we have ÔÇőbeen asked for assistance, and we will work Ôüáthrough that carefully," Foreign Minister Penny Wong said in televised ÔÇőremarks.
Australia, a close U.S. ally, has said it will not deploy ÔÇőtroops to the Middle East if the conflict escalates.
Asked if Australia could help protect nations from Iranian drone and missile attacks, Wong said "correct".
"We will ÔÇőwork through that in accordance with the position that ÔÇőI have outlined, which is we are not participating in offensive action ÔÇîagainst ÔüáIran and we have made clear we will not participate in any ground troop deployment," Wong added.
Prime Minister Anthony Albanese said on Friday that three Australian defence personnel were on board ÔÇőa U.S. ÔÇősubmarine that sank Ôüáan Iranian warship with a torpedo in the Indian Ocean, but stressed that they did not ÔÇőtake part in the attack.
The conflict in the ÔÇőMiddle ÔüáEast has escalated this week, with U.S. and Israeli jets striking areas across Iran, and many Gulf cities - including in the ÔüáUAE ÔÇőand Qatar - coming under Iranian bombardment.
Australia ÔÇőis currently working with airlines to help thousands of Australians stranded in the ÔÇőMiddle East.

item.title: Families of flight MH370 passengers urge Malaysia to extend search
item.description:
KUALA LUMPUR, March 8 (Reuters) - Families of those aboard Malaysia Airlines flight MH370 on Sunday urged the Malaysian government to extend a contract it signed with deep-sea exploration firm ÔÇőOcean Infinity to continue a search for the aircraft that disappeared 12 ÔÇîyears ago.
The Boeing 777 was carrying 227 passengers and 12 crew when it vanished en route from Kuala Lumpur to Beijing on March 8, 2014, becoming one of the worldÔÇÖs enduring ÔÇőaviation mysteries.
Multiple search operations for the plane have been conducted in the ÔÇősouthern Indian Ocean since then but all have proved fruitless.
Malaysia in ÔüáMarch last year agreed to allow Ocean Infinity to resume the hunt under a ÔÇťno ÔÇőfind, no feeÔÇŁ principle, with the firm to be paid $70 million only if the ÔÇőwreckage was successfully located.
MalaysiaÔÇÖs Air Accident Investigation Bureau said on Sunday, however, that operations had not yielded any findings so far, after two search phases covering 28 days and around 7,571 square ÔÇőkilometres (2,923 square miles) of seabed.
Operations were periodically disrupted by weather and sea conditions, ÔÇőwith the second phase ending on January 23, the AAIB said.
ÔÇťThe government remains committed to keeping the ÔÇîfamilies Ôüáinformed and will continue to provide updates as appropriate,ÔÇŁ it said.
Voice370, a group representing families of those onboard, said it was unlikely for Ocean Infinity to resume the search before its contract ends in June, due to the coming winter months ÔÇőin the southern hemisphere ÔÇőand deteriorating sea Ôüáconditions.
It urged the government to grant any request for Ocean Infinity to extend its agreement, as well as expand the ÔÇősame terms to other interested exploration firms.
ÔÇťA simple addendum extending ÔÇőthe contract Ôüáperiod without altering the core terms of the agreement would allow the search to continue without delay,ÔÇŁ it said.
Ocean Infinity had conducted prior searches for the plane but failed Ôüáto ÔÇőfind substantive wreckage.
Malaysian investigators in a 2018 report drew ÔÇőno conclusion about what happened aboard the flight, but did not rule out the possibility that the ÔÇőaircraft had been deliberately taken off course.

item.title: Majority consensus reached on Iran's next supreme leader, Mehr news reports
item.description:
DUBAI, March 8 (Reuters) - A majority consensus over a ÔÇősuccessor to Iran's slain ÔÇîSupreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei has more ÔÇőor less been ÔÇőreached, Assembly of Experts Ôüámember Ayatollah Mohammadmehdi Mirbaqeri ÔÇősaid on Sunday, ÔÇőaccording to Mehr news agency.
He said, though, that "some obstacles" ÔÇőneed to be ÔÇőresolved regarding the process, according ÔÇîto Ôüáthe report.
Iranian media said the body tasked with appointing Iran's supreme ÔÇőleader ÔÇőhad Ôüáa minor disagreement over whether their ÔÇőfinal decision must ÔÇőfollow Ôüáan in-person meeting or instead be issued Ôüáwithout ÔÇőadhering to ÔÇőthis formality.

item.title: BHP Australia boss in running to lead Woodside, Bloomberg reports
item.description:
PERTH/MELBOURNE, March 8 (Reuters) - Global miner BHPÔÇÖs (BHP.AX) , opens new tab Australia President Geraldine Slattery is among the ÔÇőcontenders for the top job at ÔÇîWoodside Energy (WDS.AX) , opens new tab , Bloomberg reported on Friday, citing people familiar with the situation.
Woodside is expected ÔÇőto name a replacement for ÔÇőMeg OÔÇÖNeill soon, after she unexpectedly left Ôüáthe company in December to lead ÔÇőBP (BP.L) , opens new tab , a role she formally begins in April .
ÔÇťAs ÔÇőhas been disclosed, WoodsideÔÇÖs Board intends to announce a permanent CEO appointment in the first ÔÇőquarter of 2026. We do ÔÇőnot comment on market speculation,ÔÇŁ a Woodside spokeswoman ÔÇîsaid, when Ôüáasked whether Slattery had been formally interviewed for the role.
Acting CEO Liz Westcott and two other internal candidates are ÔÇőalso in ÔÇőthe Ôüárunning for the CEO job, said MST Marquee analyst Saul ÔÇőKavonic.
BHP declined to comment and ÔÇőSlattery Ôüádid not respond to a request for comment at the time of writing.
Slattery Ôüáheaded ÔÇőBHP's petroleum division before ÔÇőit was acquired by Woodside.

item.title: Two Kuwait fire officers killed 'while performing duties,' Fire Force says
item.description:
CAIRO, March 8 (Reuters) - Kuwait's Fire Force ÔÇősaid on ÔÇîSunday that two of its ÔÇőofficers were ÔÇőkilled "while performing duties," according Ôüáto a ÔÇőpost on ÔÇőX.
It did not provide details on ÔÇőthe circumstances ÔÇőof their deaths, but ÔÇîKuwait Ôüáhas been intercepting drones and missiles launched ÔÇőfrom ÔÇőIran Ôüáfollowing U.S. and Israeli ÔÇőattacks ÔÇőon Ôüáthe Islamic Republic.

item.title: Trump praises Italy PM Meloni's willingness to help in U.S.-Israel war with Iran
item.description:
ROME, March 8 (Reuters) - U.S. President Donald Trump praised Italian Prime Minister Giorgia Meloni ÔÇőand said she was willing to ÔÇîhelp the United States and Israel in their war with Iran, ItalyÔÇÖs Corriere della ÔÇőSera reported on Sunday.
Speaking to ÔÇőthe newspaper in a telephone call Ôüáon Saturday, Trump described Meloni as ÔÇťa ÔÇőgreat leaderÔÇŁ and said Italy was ÔÇődoing what it could to assist.
* "I love Italy, I think she is a great leader," ÔÇőTrump was quoted as saying of ÔÇőMeloni.
* "She always tries to help, she is an ÔÇîexcellent Ôüáleader and she is a friend of mine," Trump said, according to Corriere della Sera.
* Italy is planning to ÔÇősend air-defence ÔÇőaid to ÔüáGulf countries in the face of Iranian air strikes, ÔÇőMeloni said on Thursday.
* An Italian ÔÇőnavy Ôüávessel is preparing to sail to Cyprus, a navy spokesperson said on Friday, Ôüáas ÔÇőpart of a joint ÔÇőEuropean mission to protect the island after it ÔÇőcame under Iranian fire.

item.title: Israeli military says it will pursue every successor of Iran's Khamenei
item.description:
March 8 (Reuters) - The Israeli ÔÇőmilitary warned it would ÔÇîcontinue pursuing every successor of Iran's slain Supreme Leader Ayatollah ÔÇőAli Khamenei, who was ÔÇőkilled in U.S. and ÔüáIsraeli strikes on Iran.
In ÔÇőa post on ÔÇőX in Farsi, the Israeli military also warned it would pursue every ÔÇőperson who seeks ÔÇőto appoint a successor for ÔÇîKhamenei, Ôüáreferring to the clerical body charged with choosing the Islamic Republic's ÔÇősupreme ÔÇőleader.
The Ôüáwarning comes as the clerical body ÔÇőis set to ÔÇőmeet Ôüáto choose Iran's next supreme leader.

item.title: Explainer: Who might succeed in Iran's theocratic system of power?
item.description:
March 8 - Iranian clerics involved in choosing a successor to Ayatollah Ali Khamenei after his killing a week ago in U.S.-Israeli strikes say they are close to naming the Islamic Republic's new supreme leader.
Iran's revolutionary theocracy has never been in greater jeopardy , and with the clerical body tasked with naming a new leader ready for an announcement as soon as Sunday, it is hard to predict what might happen next.
Israel and the United States ÔÇőhave vowed no let up in their war, promising to kill whoever replaces Khamenei and even those involved in selecting the new leader - a group that may include the clerics who formally make the choice ÔÇîand the Revolutionary Guardsmen and political insiders who influence them.
The following explains how power is meant to operate in the Islamic Republic, how a new supreme leader can be chosen, some of the main candidates, and how the U.S. and Israeli attacks have changed the equation:
Iran's theocratic system dates to the 1979 revolution that ousted the Shah. Ayatollah Ruhollah Khomeini, the leader of the revolution, introduced a new system of rule: vilayat-e faqih, or guardianship of the Islamic jurist.
The theory holds that until the return of the Shi'ite Muslim 12th Imam, who disappeared in the ninth century, power on earth ÔÇőshould be wielded by a venerable cleric.
It means whoever takes over as supreme leader, empowered by the constitution as the ultimate authority guiding the elected president and parliament, will have to be a senior cleric.
Under Khomeini, who died ÔÇőin 1989, and Khamenei, who has ruled since then, the supreme leader has had the last say in all matters of state. But any new leader will have to assert his authority at Ôüáa moment of enormous rupture - and may struggle to do so.
The constitution says a new leader must be chosen within three months. Until then President Masoud Pezeshkian, Guardians Council member Ayatollah Alireza Arafi and Judiciary chief Ayatollah Gholamhossein ÔÇőMohseni-Ejei have taken charge as a temporary leadership council.
Choosing a new leader is meant to be the responsibility of the Assembly of Experts, a body of around 90 senior clerics who are elected every eight years, many of them very elderly.
With strikes continuing, they have been ÔÇőconducting their consultations online, Iranian officials have told Reuters, and appear close to naming a new leader.
Khamenei never publicly tapped a preferred successor and in practice the decision probably rests with the most senior figures in the Islamic Republic who have wielded power under Khamenei for many years. It is their preferred candidate who is most likely to be approved by the assembly.
Among the most important of those senior figures is Khamenei's veteran adviser Ali Larijani , often seen as Iran's foremost powerbroker. The Guards will also have a critical backroom voice in the process.
Khamenei's son, Mojtaba Khamenei , is widely seen as the most ÔÇőlikely candidate after surviving the first salvo of strikes, which killed his wife. Although Iran's ruling ideology frowns on the principle of hereditary succession, he has a powerful following within the Guards and his dead father's still-influential office.
The grandson of the revolutionary founder Ruhollah ÔÇőKhomeini is another possible choice. Hassan Khomeini is closely associated with the reformist faction that has for decades tried to moderate the Islamic Republic's stance and may be seen as better able to assuage Western enmity and calm the fury of an embittered population.
Arafi and Mohseni-Ejei are less ÔÇîprominent possibilities who Ôüáwould likely continue Khamenei's hardline stance. Mohseni-Ejei was responsible for stamping out internal protests following a disputed election outcome in 2009 when he was intelligence minister.
Assembly of Experts members Ahmad Alamolhoda and Mohsen Araki are also hardline senior clerics with a close involvement in Iranian politics who might be considered. Former president Hassan Rouhani is a senior cleric but he was distrusted by some of the most powerful hardliners who would have great sway over the choice.
The assembly could theoretically pick an even lesser known ayatollah as leader. But the ruling system has been so fractured by the strikes that it would be much harder to buttress the position of a newcomer.
The Islamic Revolutionary Guards Corps had long been expected to play a central role behind the ÔÇőscenes in determining Khamenei's successor. Unlike the ordinary military, which ÔÇőcomes under the elected president, the guards answer only to Ôüáthe supreme leader.
But its top echelons have been hollowed out by U.S. and Israeli strikes over recent years and it is far from clear how far it will still be able to influence the decision.
The most important Guards leader of recent times was Qassem Soleimani, head of the elite Quds Force unit that spearheaded Iran's regional strategy of exporting the revolution through affiliated Shi'ite militias ÔÇőin Arab countries. He was killed by a U.S. strike in 2020.
During the brief summer war last year Israeli strikes killed other top Guards commanders. And the latest strikes killed its ÔÇőlatest top commander Mohammed Pakpour, three Ôüásources familiar with the matter said.
The Basij militia, a part-time paramilitary force under Guards control, is often used to quell protests inside Iran, giving the corps a formidable role in internal control.
Since the early 2000s, the Guards' economic power has also grown as its contracting company Khatam al-Anbiya won projects worth billions of dollars in Iran's oil and gas sector. Protecting that empire may contribute to the Guards' decision on backing a new leader.
Iranians elect a president and a parliament to four-year terms. The president appoints a government Ôüáthat handles daily policy ÔÇőwithin parameters permitted by the supreme leader.
During the early years of the Islamic Republic the votes drew mass participation. But far fewer Iranians retain faith ÔÇőin electoral politics.
While President Pezeshkian, a reputed moderate, is on the three-member interim leadership committee, it is far from clear he has had any say over how events pan out and he was forced into an embarrassing climbdown on Saturday over the conduct of the war.
And while the Assembly of Experts is elected, all its ÔÇőcandidates - like those running in all Iranian national elections - must be vetted by the clerical Guardians Council, meaning only those already aligned with the authorities can take part.

item.title: Lebanese man flees hometown, months after repairing home damaged in last war
item.description:
HAZMIEH, Lebanon, March 8 (Reuters) - Just days ago, Hussain Khrais was proudly showing off his newly restored home in south ÔÇőLebanon, fixed up after being badly damaged in 2024 clashes between Israel and Hezbollah. But a new war has since erupted ÔÇîand his home is in the line of fire again.
Khrais fled his hometown of Khiyam, about five km (three miles) from the border with Israel, as Israel pounded Lebanon with heavy airstrikes last week in retaliation for Iran-backed group Hezbollah's rocket and drone fire into Israel.
"Is the house I worked so hard to build, or the business I started, ÔÇőstill there? Or is it all gone?" Khrais told Reuters from a relative's home near the capital Beirut where he and his ÔÇőfamily are now staying.
"The feeling is very, very upsetting, because we still don't know if we'll go back Ôüáor not."
It wasn't Khrais' first time - or even his second. The 66-year-old has been displaced at least four times in ÔÇőthe last four decades by Israeli incursions and airstrikes, each time returning to a town in ruins and rebuilding patiently.
Last year, he spent months and ÔÇőaround $25,000 repairing the damage from the last war between Hezbollah and Israel, which ended 15 months ago. Hezbollah started firing at Israel after the United States and Israel launched airstrikes against Iran on February 28.
"It really bothers me to think this is the life I've lived," Khrais told Reuters. "Once again, displacement, return, rebuilding, restoration - then again displacement, return, ÔÇőrebuilding. What kind of life is that?"
With no support from the Lebanese state and little coming from Hezbollah's social welfare programme, most Lebanese whose ÔÇőhomes were damaged or destroyed in the 2024 war have used their own private funds to rebuild.
Reconstruction has placed a huge burden on affected Lebanese families, still struggling ÔÇîto access Ôüátheir savings in commercial banks after a financial collapse in 2019.
Two weeks ago, Khrais had told Reuters he was scared that a new war would start. "I'm at an age where I can't start all over again. That's it," he said.
The new war has dealt Lebanese another blow. About 300,000 people have been displaced over the last week by Israel's strikes and by the Israeli military's evacuation orders, which encompass around 8% of ÔÇőLebanese territory.
Khrais is staying with around ÔÇő20 other displaced relatives, some displaced Ôüáfrom Khiyam and others from Beirut's southern suburbs, which have been hit hard by Israeli strikes.
He is glued to the television, where news bulletins have reported on Israeli troops and tanks pushing deeper into his hometown.
"I've been ÔÇőin Beirut for four days now, and these four days feel like 400 years," Khrais said.
He misses ÔÇőhis house dearly.
"Maybe the Ôüáthing I'm most attached to, is when I open the door to my children's bedrooms and see the pictures of their children hanging on the walls," he said.
"That sight is worth the world's treasures - to see my grandchildren's pictures in Khiyam."
Khrais has no news on the state of his home. He said Ôüáhe remains ÔÇőhopeful but that if it has been destroyed, he'll still do what he's always done.
"The ÔÇőbig shock would be if I came back and didn't find it. But my feeling says no, God willing, it will remain. And like I said, even if we don't find ÔÇőthe house, we'll go back and rebuild," he said.

item.title: Iran war threatens prolonged hit to global energy markets
item.description:
March 7 (Reuters) - The U.S.-Israeli war with Iran could leave consumers and businesses worldwide facing weeks or months of higher fuel prices even if the week-old conflict ends quickly, as suppliers grapple with damaged facilities, disrupted logistics, and elevated risks to shipping.
The outlook poses a global economic threat and a political vulnerability for ÔÇőU.S. President Donald Trump leading into the midterm elections, with voters sensitive to energy bills and unfavorable to foreign entanglements .
"The market is shifting from pricing pure geopolitical risk to grappling with tangible operational disruption, as refinery shutdowns and export constraints ÔÇîbegin to impair crude processing and regional supply flows," JP Morgan analysts said in a research note on Friday.
The conflict has already led to the suspension of around a fifth of global crude and natural gas supply, as Tehran targets ships in the vital Strait of Hormuz between its shores and Oman, and attacks energy infrastructure across the region.
Global oil prices have surged more than 25% since the start of the war, driving up fuel prices for consumers worldwide.
A nearly complete shutdown of the Strait means the region's giant oil producers - Saudi Arabia, the United Arab Emirates, Iraq and Kuwait - have had to suspend shipments of as much as 140 million barrels of oil - ÔÇőequal to about 1.4 days of global demand - to global refiners.
As a result, oil and gas storage at facilities in the Middle East Gulf are rapidly filling, forcing oil fields in Iraq and Kuwait to cut oil production , with the United Arab Emirates likely ÔÇőto cut next , analysts, traders and sources said.
"At some point soon, everyone will also shut in if vessels do not come," said a source with a state oil company in the region, who asked not Ôüáto be named.
Oilfields forced to shut in across the Middle East as a result of the shipping disruptions could take a while to return to normal, said Amir Zaman, head of the Americas commercial team at Rystad Energy.
"The conflict could be ended, but it could take days ÔÇőor weeks or months, depending on the types of fields, age of the field, the type of shut-in that they've had to do before you can get production back up to what it once was," he said.
Iranian forces, meanwhile, are targeting regional energy infrastructure - including refineries and terminals - ÔÇőforcing them to shut down too, with some of those operations badly damaged by attacks and in need of repairs.
Qatar declared force majeure on its huge volumes of gas exports on Wednesday after Iranian drone attacks and it may take at least a month to return to normal production levels, sources told Reuters. Qatar supplies 20% of global LNG.
Saudi AramcoÔÇÖs mammoth Ras Tanura refinery and crude export terminal, meanwhile, has also closed due to attacks, with no details on damage.
The White House has justified the attack on Iran, saying the country posed an imminent threat to the United States , although it has not provided details. Trump has also said he was concerned about ÔÇőIran's efforts to obtain a nuclear weapon.
A quick end to the war would soothe markets. But a return to pre-war supply and pricing could take weeks or months, depending on the extent of the damage to infrastructure and shipping.
"Considering physical damage due to Iranian ÔÇőstrikes, so far we have not seen anything that would be considered structural, although the risk remains as long as the war continues," said Joel Hancock, energy analyst, Natixis CIB.
The biggest question for energy supplies is how and when the Strait of Hormuz will become safe for shipping again. Trump has offered ÔÇînaval escorts to Ôüáoil tankers and promised U.S. insurance support to vessels in the region.
But safety in the waterway may be elusive, as Iran has the capacity to sustain drone attacks on shipping for months , intelligence and military sources have said.
The conflict could also encourage countries to top up their strategic petroleum reserves in the weeks and months after the conflict ends, by exposing the dangers of thin inventories. That would increase demand for oil and support prices.
In the meantime, the disruption in energy shipments is reverberating through supply chains and economies in import-reliant Asia, which sources 60% of its crude oil from the Middle East.
In India, state-run Mangalore Refinery and Petrochemicals (MRPL.NS) , opens new tab declared force majeure on gasoline export cargoes, sources said this week, joining a growing number of refineries in the region unable to fulfill sales contracts due to lack of supply.
At least two refineries in China have ÔÇőcut runs. China, a big supplier to the region, has asked ÔÇőrefineries to suspend fuel exports. Thailand has also suspended fuel Ôüáexports, while Vietnam has suspended crude shipments.
Disruption has given Russia a boost. Prices for Russian crude cargoes have risen as the U.S. has given Indian refiners a 30-day waiver to buy Russian crude to substitute for lost Middle East supply. Washington had pressured India to cut Russian oil imports under the threat of tariffs.
In Japan, the No. 2 global LNG importer, baseload power futures for Tokyo for the fiscal year starting in April ÔÇőjumped more than a third this week on the EEX in anticipation of higher fuel prices. And in Seoul, drivers queued up at petrol stations in anticipation of rising pump prices.
For European consumers, the ÔÇőcrisis in gas supplies and the higher Ôüáprices are a double whammy. The region was hit the hardest by the disruption to gas supplies due to sanctions on Russian energy imports after Russia invaded Ukraine in 2022.
Europe turned to LNG imports to substitute for Russian pipeline gas. And Europe now needs to buy 180 more LNG cargoes than it did last year to fill gas storage to the levels needed before next winter.
The supply risks to the United States are fewer, as the country has grown in recent years into the worldÔÇÖs largest oil and gas producer. But U.S. crude and fuel prices rise in tandem with international crude Ôüámarkets, so pump prices ÔÇőfor gasoline and diesel are affected even if domestic supply is plentiful.
U.S. average retail gasoline, for example, hit $3.32 a gallon nationally on Friday, up 34 cents over last ÔÇőweek, according to AAA. Diesel prices, meanwhile, hit $4.33 a gallon, up from $3.76 a gallon a week ago.
Higher prices at the pump mark a major risk for Trump and his fellow Republicans as they head into midterm elections in November.
"Gasoline prices are psychologically powerful," said Mark Malek, chief investment officer at Siebert Financial. "They are the inflation number that consumers ÔÇősee every single day."

item.title: Slovakia PM Fico to meet EU's von der Leyen to push for restart of Druzhba oil flows
item.description:
March 8 (Reuters) - Slovak Prime Minister Robert Fico said on Sunday he ÔÇîwould meet with EU chief Ursula von der Leyen on Tuesday, as Slovakia and Hungary ramp up pressure on Ukraine to restart Russian oil flows, warning Slovakia could block a major EU ÔÇőloan to Kyiv.
Russian oil flows through the Druzhba pipeline to Hungary and Slovakia ÔÇővia Ukraine have been suspended since late January, after Kyiv said a ÔüáRussian attack caused a fire that seriously damaged the line.
The outage has sparked one ÔÇőof the most heated disputes between the neighbours since Russia's 2022 full-scale invasion of ÔÇőUkraine.
Ukraine says the pipeline cannot be repaired quickly .
Hungary and Slovakia, European Union members which still import Russian oil and maintain ties with Moscow, have accused Kyiv of deliberately delaying a restart for political ÔÇőreasons.
Hungary has vetoed new EU sanctions ÔÇőon Russia and a 90-billion-euro loan by the bloc for Ukraine. Fico reiterated his threat to ÔÇîblock the Ôüáloan in a Facebook video on Sunday.
He said he would meet von der Leyen in Paris and push for experts to visit the Druzhba pipeline.
"But the most important message will be that Slovakia is ready to take over the baton ÔÇőfrom Hungary, if necessary," ÔÇőFico said in Ôüáreference to the loan.
"Blocking this huge military gift to Ukraine is a legitimate tool to achieve the restoration of oil ÔÇősupplies."
The EU Commission said Friday it was looking into ways to ÔÇőrestore Druzhba Ôüáoil flows, including potential financial assistance.
Slovakia has already halted emergency power supplies to Ukraine amid the dispute.
The dispute escalates as Hungary heads into a tight election next month, with Prime ÔüáMinister Viktor ÔÇőOrban, in power for 16 years, making the ÔÇőwar in Ukraine a central campaign theme.

item.title: Italy weighs fuel-tax cuts as firms warn of energy price surge from Middle East war
item.description:
ROME, March 8 (Reuters) - Italy is considering cutting fuel excise duties using higher-than-expected value-added tax revenues triggered by rising ÔÇőpump prices, the prime minister said, as businesses ÔÇîwarned the conflict in the Middle East could sharply raise energy costs.
Excise duties account for a large share of the pump ÔÇőprice in Italy and are levied per litre, ÔÇőnot as a percentage of the total price Ôüálike VAT.
($1 = ÔÇő0.8607 euros)

item.title: Tokyo told national oil reserve site to prepare for release amid Iran crisis, Japan lawmaker says
item.description:
TOKYO, March 8 (Reuters) - A senior Japanese parliament member said on Sunday that the government instructed a national oil reserve storage site to prepare for a possible release ÔÇőof crude, as the Iran crisis has cut supply from the Middle ÔÇîEast.
Japan relies on the Middle East for around 95% of its crude supplies, with roughly 70% shipped through the Strait of Hormuz, which has been effectively closed following U.S. and Israeli ÔÇőattacks on Iran.
Akira Nagatsuma, a member of the Centrist Reform Alliance opposition ÔÇőparty, told Reuters that an official from the Japan Organization for ÔüáMetals and Energy Security (JOGMEC) at the Shibushi national oil storage base said it ÔÇőhad received the directive from the Agency for Natural Resources and Energy (ANRE) on Friday.
Details ÔÇősuch as the timing of the release remain unclear, Nagatsuma said.
It was not immediately clear if any other storage bases received the same instruction.
An official at ANRE, under the Ministry of ÔÇőEconomy, Trade and Industry (METI), declined to comment. The Shibushi base was not immediately ÔÇőavailable for comment, while JOGMEC could not be reached outside office hours.
The Shibushi site in southern ÔÇîJapan Ôüáis one of the locations where the country's strategic oil stocks are stored.
Japan holds emergency oil reserves equivalent to 254 days of domestic consumption - among the world's largest - including government-owned stockpiles, private-sector inventories, and jointly held reserves with oil-producing countries.
Tokyo last ÔÇődrew down oil from ÔÇőits reserves in Ôüá2022 as part of an International Energy Agency (IEA)-led coordinated release following Russia's invasion of Ukraine.
Industry Minister Ryosei Akazawa said early last ÔÇőweek that there were no specific plans to release oil reserves, ÔÇőbut the Ôüáministry would continue to monitor the crude oil supply situation closely, working in coordination with the IEA.
Kyodo news agency reported on Friday that the Japanese government is considering using Ôüápart ÔÇőof its national oil reserves amid the ongoing Iran ÔÇőcrisis that has disrupted global energy supplies and may do so in coordination with other countries or ÔÇőeven on its own.

item.title: Iran attacks breach international law, Swiss Defence Minister says
item.description:
ZURICH/FRANKFURT, March 8 (Reuters) - The United States and Israel have broken international law with their attacks on Iran , Swiss Defence Minister Martin Pfister said in an ÔÇőinterview published on Sunday, the latest European leader to raise ÔÇîconcerns about the conflict.
Legal experts have said many countries will consider the attacks unjustified under the United Nations Charter, under which member countries must refrain from using force ÔÇőor the threat of force without U.N authorisation or unless acting ÔÇőin self-defence.
"The Federal Council is of the opinion that the Ôüáattack on Iran constitutes a violation of international law," Pfister told ÔÇőSonntagsZeitung, referring to the Swiss cabinet.
"In our view it constitutes a violation ÔÇőon the prohibition of violence," he added, calling on all parties involved to halt the fighting to protect the civilian population.
Pfister said he was referring ÔÇőto all the countries not complying with the prohibition on violence, ÔÇőincluding the United States and Israel.
"The Americans and Israel have attacked Iran from the ÔÇîair. ÔüáIn doing so, they, like Iran, violated international law," Pfister said.
The comments chime with those of German Vice Chancellor and Finance Minister Lars Klingbeil, who told the RND newspaper network that he had "serious doubts that this ÔÇőwar is legitimate under ÔÇőinternational law."
Klingbeil Ôüáalso came out strongly against the idea of any potential German participation in the war: "I say clearly: this ÔÇőis not our war. We will not participate in ÔÇőthis war."
He Ôüásaid there was a "great danger that we are sliding ever deeper into a world where there are no longer any rules. We do not want Ôüáto ÔÇőlive in a world where only the ÔÇőlaw of the strongest applies."
Spain has also denounced the U.S. and Israeli bombings of Iran as reckless ÔÇőand illegal.

item.title: Airstrikes, not occupation: Where Trump voters draw red lines on Iran
item.description:
March 8 (Reuters) - A week into a war with Iran that is already unpopular with much of the American public, President Donald Trump has offered various explanations for the bombing campaign, estimated the strikes could last weeks , cautioned there will likely be more U.S. casualties , and dismissed concerns about surging oil and gas prices .
While that has troubled many Americans, recent interviews with several who voted for Trump show they are largely standing by the ÔÇőpresident and his war ÔÇô at least for now. Even his most ardent supporters, however, warned that a large deployment of U.S. ground troops in Iran would alarm them.
In the days since the U.S. and Israel attacked Iran, Reuters has spoken ÔÇîto eight Americans who voted for Trump in 2024, part of a group of 20 whom Reuters has interviewed monthly since February , to hear their thoughts on the rapidly escalating conflict.
All eight opposed the idea of the Trump administration sending substantial U.S. ground forces to Iran or getting involved in a protracted effort to install new leadership. But five said they fully supported the air and sea attacks as the only way to prevent Iran from stockpiling long-range and nuclear missiles. Three were less clear about why the administration started the conflict, saying they worried it was unduly damaging the U.S. economy and endangering U.S. citizens.
Their reactions to the war so far roughly reflect the results of a Reuters-Ipsos poll conducted last weekend ÔÇőthat surveyed 1,282 U.S. adults. Nearly two-thirds of respondents who voted for Trump in 2024 said they approved of the strikes, while 9% said they disapproved and 27% said they were not sure. Overall, just one in four respondents expressed support for the U.S. attack ÔÇőon Iran.
If energy prices keep surging and Trump's tactics against Iran start to alienate his own followers , the conflict could erode support for Republicans as the U.S. heads into all-important midterm elections in November that will determine Ôüáwhether Congress stays in the party's control.
Although most of the eight voters Reuters interviewed reported that gasoline in their area had shot up between 20 and 50 cents per gallon, those who supported the strikes said they expected the higher prices to be short-lived.
Jon Webber, 45, a Walmart ÔÇőretail worker in Indiana, pointed to the struggle his parents had with volatile oil prices following the 1979 Iranian Revolution. ÔÇťYeah, it's gonna suck for a little bit, but it'll go back,ÔÇŁ he said.
After watching U.S. presidents invoke the threat posed by Iran for most of his life, Webber said it ÔÇőfelt good to see Trump cripple the regime: ÔÇťIt should have been done a long time ago and we wouldn't have had to deal with it for this long.ÔÇŁ
Near Houston, Texas, Loretta Torres, 38, said she trusted the president had acted judiciously. ÔÇťTrump was trying to get ahead of the game and trying to be proactive with the threats,ÔÇŁ she said.
But Torres, a mother of three, also said she feared the war could spin ÔÇťout of controlÔÇŁ or inspire terrorist attacks on major metropolitan areas like hers. Like all the voters Reuters interviewed, she dreaded the prospect of the U.S. becoming enmeshed in the region for years if Trump sends in ground troops.
The voters ÔÇőwho supported the strikes were confident Trump had authorized them because they were necessary to thwart an imminent attack on the United States. Democrats and even prominent conservative commentators have expressed skepticism about this, citing the administration's varying explanations for the war.
Chad Hill, 50, a supervisor at a nuclear power ÔÇőplant near his home in northwestern Ohio, said he had been expecting some type of U.S. military action, despite U.S.-Iran negotiations that had been underway over Iran's nuclear program just days before the strikes: ÔÇťUnfortunately, it seems like this was probably the only way because in the end they don't trust us and we don't trust ÔÇîthem.ÔÇŁ
Trump might need Ôüáto send a limited military detachment into the country to fully destroy IranÔÇÖs missile capabilities, Hill said, but any larger ground deployment would raise red flags for him. ÔÇťNo nation-building, that doesnÔÇÖt work,ÔÇŁ he said.
The idea of U.S. boots on Iranian ground also made Gerald Dunn, 67, uneasy. ÔÇťOnly if theyÔÇÖre invitedÔÇŁ by a new Iranian government should Trump deploy ground troops, he said, and even then, ÔÇťthe scale should be limited.ÔÇŁ
Like Hill, Dunn, a martial arts instructor in New YorkÔÇÖs Hudson Valley, applauded Trump for taking action where prior administrations had simply ÔÇťkicked the can down the road.ÔÇŁ
Near Savannah, Georgia, insurance firm employee Amanda Taylor, 52, said that while ÔÇťthereÔÇÖs so much we donÔÇÖt really know,ÔÇŁ she would back any military action that made the U.S. safer.
ÔÇťOur intelligence is usually right in these things so I'm going to hope and trust that [Trump acted] because of that, and not just because of his own gut feeling,ÔÇŁ ÔÇőshe said. At the same time, Taylor added, ÔÇťnobody wants a drawn-out, true ÔÇőwar ÔÇô I would hate to see that happen.ÔÇŁ
CONFUSION OVER RATIONALE
The shifting Ôüáreasons Trump administration officials gave for the strikes puzzled some voters.
On Monday, Herman Sims heard Secretary of State Marco Rubio say the U.S. learned Israel was planning to attack Iran and struck first to prevent retaliation ÔÇô but on Tuesday, he heard Trump claim responsibility for leading the charge based on the president's hunch that Iran would attack if the U.S. didnÔÇÖt.
Sims, 66, a night operations manager for a trucking company in Dallas, Texas, said ÔÇőthe conflicting reports ÔÇťdidnÔÇÖt make any sense,ÔÇŁ but added that he supported the strikes if they were indeed necessary to protect U.S. lives. Still, he was alarmed by the spiking gas prices and by a report that ÔÇőan ex-Marine ÔÇÖs arm was broken while he Ôüáprotested that the U.S. should not ÔÇťfight for IsraelÔÇŁ during a Senate hearing.
ÔÇťI agree 100%. We should not be fighting a war for someone else,ÔÇŁ Sims said.
In Madison, Wisconsin, college student Will Brown, 20, said he was frustrated by the administration's "wishy-washy" explanations for why the U.S. attacked when it did.
"Bombing them to the extent that we have is fine, but Trump's talked about boots on the ground and troops dying, and that I simply can't approve of," he said. Trump told the New York Post on Monday that he has not ruled out sending U.S. ground troops into Iran.
Though he was glad to see Iran's leader dead Ôüáand the country's nuclear ÔÇőcapacity reduced, Brown said he could "not fathom the amount of destruction and death" a ground invasion would bring.
Don Jernigan, 75, a retiree in Virginia Beach, said Trump had not ÔÇőjustified putting U.S. troops in harmÔÇÖs way.
Assuming the Iranian threat was ÔÇťso imminent that our cities are in danger of being destroyed from a long distance, from the mainland of Iran, then perhaps we should be destroying people from a long distance, like the mainland of America,ÔÇŁ Jernigan said.
Though he couldnÔÇÖt know what kind of threat Trump saw, Jernigan added that ÔÇőthe U.S. strikes had raised the odds of terrorist attacks against Americans.
ÔÇťIf we kill their brothers and their dads and their uncles over there, they're going to come over here and try to kill ours,ÔÇŁ he said.

item.title: Pope Leo urges end to bombing, calls for dialogue amid Iran, Middle East violence
item.description:
VATICAN CITY, March 8 (Reuters) - Pope Leo said on Sunday that ÔÇődeeply troubling news continued ÔÇîto arrive from Iran and across the Middle East, urging ÔÇőan end to the ÔÇőviolence and renewed efforts to Ôüáopen space for dialogue.
Speaking ÔÇőat the Angelus prayer ÔÇőin St. PeterÔÇÖs Square, the pope said the conflict was fuelling ÔÇőfear and hatred and ÔÇőraised concerns that it could spread ÔÇîfurther, Ôüádragging other countries, including "dear Lebanon".
"Let us raise our humble prayer to the Lord that ÔÇőthe ÔÇőroar Ôüáof bombs may cease, that weapons may ÔÇőfall silent, and that ÔÇőspace Ôüámay be opened for dialogue in which the voices Ôüáof ÔÇőpeoples can be ÔÇőheard", Pope Leo said.

item.title: From 1776 to 2026: Adam Smith's lessons for the global economy
item.description:
LONDON, March 8 (Reuters) - Tax the rich. Trash the tariffs. End monopolies.
Such are the rallying calls of many of today's most heated economic debates. They could also come straight from the pen of revered economist Adam Smith, hailed by some as the "father of capitalism" and others as an early progressive.
Smith knew nothing of Donald Trump ÔÇőor tech billionaires when he railed against trade protectionism and extreme affluence in "The Wealth of Nations", the best-read economics book in history, which celebrates its 250th birthday on Monday.
"It is the maxim of ÔÇîevery prudent master of a family never to attempt to make at home what it will cost him more to make than to buy," the Scot wrote in the seminal work, for example.
"Would it be a reasonable law to prohibit the importation of all foreign wines, merely to encourage the making of claret and burgundy in Scotland?" he adds.
The foundational text of classical economics was published on March 9, 1776, the same year as the U.S. Declaration of Independence, 13 years before the French Revolution, and amid the early convulsions of the Industrial Age.
Yet ÔÇőit's not hard to find parallels between the doctrines the book denounces - such as rival mercantilist empires seeking to minimise imports and maximise exports - and President Trump's trade tactics and "America First" credo today.
And while Smith is ÔÇőmost often cited for his free markets and free trade zeal, when it comes to distribution of wealth, he could almost be channelling Bernie Sanders or Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez, leftist ÔüáU.S. lawmakers.
"It is not very unreasonable that the rich should contribute to the public expense, not only in proportion to their revenue, but something more than in that proportion," Smith writes in the 1,000-page-plus tome, which draws on everything from ÔÇőgrape-growing to pin factories.
"No society can surely be flourishing and happy, of which the far greater part of the members are poor and miserable," he says in one of his best-known quotes .
Many scholars of the book - full ÔÇőname "An Inquiry into the Nature and Causes of the Wealth of Nations" - say it remains uncannily relevant to the economic issues of our time, though debates continue to rage about what Smith was actually saying.
Advocates of free-market economics have long claimed him as their spiritual father, while some more recent readings even peg him as a moderate progressive - something akin to a left-leaning, modern-day European social democrat.
"You can find a 'Smith' to support anything you want to say," King's College London research associate Leo Steeds said of the Scottish Enlightenment thinker.
Smith also accepted ÔÇőthat there were certain circumstances when tariffs were called for, either because the terms of trade were unfair or for reasons of security - arguments increasingly heard in the United States, Europe and other trading blocs.
