"Smith did understand those arguments," said ÔÇőEamonn Butler, director of the free market policy think tank the Adam Smith Institute in London. "But he thought these things (tariffs) really should be as temporary as possible. He thought the more trade you have, the better everybody is."
One of the most ÔÇîfamous metaphors Ôüáfrom the "Wealth of Nations" is that of the "invisible hand", most often interpreted as meaning how free markets channel the self-interest of different participants to the best outcome for all.
"It is not from the benevolence of the butcher, the brewer, or the baker, that we expect our dinner, but from their regard to their own interest," Smith writes.
But other students of the work note the invisible hand metaphor was used just once in the book and must be taken alongside his wider arguments rather than being used to justify "laissez-faire" policies.
"This book ... is actually a critique of the way in which special interests, monopolists, powerful people, lobbies capture the state," said Pratap Bhanu Mehta, a leading Indian academic and public intellectual.
"He says: You fix that, ÔÇőthen free markets come."
U.S. economist Joseph Stiglitz, a professor at ÔÇőColumbia University and Nobel Prize laureate, agreed.
"It was much Ôüámore of an enlightened self-interest looking at society more broadly," he said. "Modern economics is based on infinitely selfish people. And clearly, Adam Smith didn't believe that."
Indeed, Smith - who taught moral philosophy at the University of Glasgow - is explicit about his views on selfishness at the expense of others.
"All for ourselves, and nothing for other people, seems, in every age of the ÔÇőworld, to have been the vile maxim of the masters of mankind," he writes.
Events to mark the 250th anniversary of "The Wealth of Nations" are ÔÇőtaking place throughout the year in ÔüáGlasgow, Edinburgh, London and Smith's birthplace of Kirkcaldy on the Scottish coast.
In a sign of his lasting imprint on the popular imagination, the ghost of Smith made an appearance last year as a character in a satirical musical staged during the Edinburgh festival about the 2008 collapse of Royal Bank of Scotland.
There are, however, limits to how much Smith can be re-branded a progressive or anything else in today's terms.
While he criticised the rich and argued the accumulation of wealth by the few led to the Ôüápoverty of many, ÔÇőMehta argued that Smith, like many of his contemporary thinkers, would have been comfortable with levels of inequality not acceptable today.
Still others - including Karl ÔÇőMarx decades later - have criticised Smith's production-enhancing ideas on the division of labour into small tasks as being deployed in factories to leave workers with morale-sapping, mind-numbing jobs.
Nonetheless, economic historian Richard van den Berg, professor at Goldsmiths, University of London, said the many questions and differing interpretations surrounding the ÔÇőbook had clearly not diminished its appeal to subsequent generations.
"It is a tool," he said. "A tool for producing ideas."

item.title: Two Israeli soldiers killed in southern Lebanon, Israel military says
item.description:
March 8 (Reuters) - The Israeli military said on Sunday that two ÔÇőof its soldiers were ÔÇőkilled in southern Lebanon, marking Ôüáthe first fatalities ÔÇőamong its troops ÔÇősince Israel-Hezbollah hostilities resumed last week as Israeli intensified air ÔÇőstrikes on ÔÇőLebanon.
The military did not immediately ÔÇîprovide Ôüáfurther details on the circumstances of the incident.
Clashes have continued ÔÇőalong ÔÇőthe ÔüáIsrael-Lebanon frontier amid heightened tensions ÔÇőbetween Israel and ÔÇőHezbollah Ôüáfollowing the U.S.-Israeli military campaign on Iran .

item.title: Swiss voters set to reject plan to cut funding for state broadcaster
item.description:
ZURICH, March 8 (Reuters) - Swiss voters looked set on Sunday to reject a referendum to slash public broadcaster SRGÔÇÖs funding, with early ÔÇőprojections showing 62% opposing a plan to cut the annual ÔÇőlicence fee, a move critics warned would weaken media Ôüáand fuel disinformation.
The campaign wanted to reduce the annual licence fee ÔÇőthat all Swiss households must pay from 335 Swiss francs ($431.87) to ÔÇő200 francs.
Supporters, mainly from right-wing groups including the Swiss People's Party (SVP), had argued that the charge ÔÇô the highest in the world ÔÇô was too expensive, and that ÔÇőSRG, which runs 17 radio stations and seven TV channels ÔÇőin four languages had become too bloated.
They also said the SRG was not politically ÔÇîindependent, Ôüáand had a left-wing bias in its coverage.
Opponents had said the move reflected pressure on public media organisations from the political right, which has accused national broadcasters ÔÇőglobally of being ÔÇőpolitically biased Ôüáagainst them.
News, sports and cultural coverage would suffer, opponents said, while an SRG weakened by lower funding ÔÇőcould mean that disinformation would be easier to spread.
"A ÔÇőmajor Ôüádismantling of Switzerland's media infrastructure has been prevented," said Laura Zimmermann, leader of the campaign against the cuts. "Our access to reliable information remains Ôüáprotected."
"We remain ÔÇőfully committed to accompanying the public ÔÇőin their everyday lives with a diverse and high-quality programme," said Susanne Wille, SRG ÔÇődirector general.
($1 = 0.7757 Swiss francs)

item.title: Greece sees deal to buy Italian-made frigates by April, minister says
item.description:
March 8 (Reuters) - Greece hopes to wrap up talks and sign a deal ÔÇőfor the purchase of Bergamini class ÔÇîfrigates from Italy by April, Defence Minister Nikos Dendias said in an interview ÔÇőreleased on Sunday.
High-ranking officials from ÔÇőGreece and Italy last year signed memorandums for Ôüánaval cooperation, opening the way ÔÇőfor the acquisition of two FREMM frigates ÔÇőby Athens with an option for two more.
"We hope that negotiations with Italy will ÔÇőbe concluded and that we ÔÇőwill have signed (a deal) for the acquisition of ÔÇî2+2 Ôüáfrigates by April," Dendias told a Greek newspaper.
Dendias said Athens was working to ensure the frigates could also ÔÇőcarry ÔÇőthe new-generation ÔüáELSA missile.
Greece plans to spend about 28 billion euros ÔÇőas part of a multi-year ÔÇődefence Ôüáplan that includes the purchase from France and Ôüánew ÔÇősubmarines as it tries ÔÇőto keep up with historic rival Turkey.

item.title: Colombians vote in legislative contest, presidential primaries
item.description:
BOGOTA, March 8 (Reuters) - Colombians were heading to the polls on Sunday to elect a new Congress and choose three of the presidential candidates who ÔÇőwill contest elections this May, a vote that will shape the next ÔÇîpresident's ability to push through legislation and fulfill their agenda.
Voters will choose from over 3,000 candidates to fill 102 Senate seats and 182 House seats, in an election analysts predict will ÔÇőbe divided among some two dozen parties, likely forcing the next president ÔÇőto form a coalition government.
Some 41.2 million eligible voters will also Ôüábe able to participate in consultations through which some right-wing, centrist and left-wing ÔÇőparties will select presidential candidates. Several leading candidates - including leftist Ivan Cepeda, right-winger Abelardo ÔÇőDe La Espriella and centrist Sergio Fajardo - will not participate in Sunday's primaries.
"It is very important to come and exercise the right to vote. The most important thing is for Colombia to ÔÇődecide its future and for the results to be respected," said Federico Rodriguez, ÔÇőa 32-year-old business administrator, after voting in Bogota's north.
"It is a source of pride that we ÔÇîcan Ôüágo out and exercise our right to vote and to democracy, but I also have uncertainty about the results, about knowing what Colombia's future will be in the next four years," said university student Isabella Suarez, 21.
The polls are open from 8 ÔÇőa.m. local time (1300 ÔÇőGMT) until 4 Ôüáp.m. (2100 GMT).
President Gustavo Petro, whose term ends in August, has repeatedly questioned the software that will be used for the vote ÔÇőcount in the elections, alleging possible irregularities, while National Registrar ÔÇőHernan Penagos Ôüáhas guaranteed the transparency of the vote and said party observers can verify the software's results.
Some 246,000 members of the military forces and the national police have been placed Ôüáon ÔÇőhigh alert to prevent attacks by illegal armed ÔÇőgroups seeking to disrupt the election or pressure voters to cast their ballots for certain candidates, Defense Minister ÔÇőPedro Sanchez said this week.

item.title: Zelenskiy says Ukraine has unique drone experience, discusses joint production with Dutch PM
item.description:
KYIV, March 8 (Reuters) - Ukraine's President Volodymyr Zelenskiy said he and Dutch Prime Minister Rob Jetten ÔÇődiscussed joint arms production during his visit to Kyiv on ÔÇîSunday, and he stressed Ukraine's unique experience in defending against Iranian-made drones used by Russia.
"It is important that we are producing weapons together with ÔÇőthe Netherlands ÔÇô and we will certainly continue and expand this ÔÇőjoint work," Zelenskiy said on X, adding they had Ôüádiscussed investments and possible production volumes in detail.
In recent months, Ukraine ÔÇőhas stepped up efforts to establish joint arms production with European ÔÇőcountries, opening several factories to manufacture drones.
As Iran launches drones at U.S. allies across the Gulf and beyond in response to intense U.S. and Israeli strikes, Ukraine ÔÇőis hoping the Middle East crisis will increase its leverage ÔÇőwith allies by showing them that the expertise it has built during four ÔÇîyears Ôüáof Russia's full-scale invasion could be invaluable to partners' own long-term security.
Zelenskiy said that Kyiv has unique experience in combating drones, which Iran actively uses in its attacks on Middle Eastern countries.
He said ÔÇőthat Ukraine is ready ÔÇőto sell Ôüáthe interceptor drones that are not used by the Ukrainian military, and the first group of Ukrainian ÔÇőmilitary instructors will head to the Middle East ÔÇőon Monday.
"They Ôüáare coming with the means to help," Zelenskiy noted, adding that Ukraine would provide the expertise and experience in defending against "Shahed" model drones Ôüáand cruise ÔÇőmissiles.
Ukrainian manufacturers of cheap interceptor drones ÔÇődesigned to knock out enemy unmanned aerial vehicles have said they have the capacity to export ÔÇőin large volumes.

item.title: Two killed, 12 injured after projectile fell on residential location in Saudi Arabia
item.description:
CAIRO, March 8 (Reuters) - Two people were ÔÇőkilled and 12 ÔÇîinjured after a projectile fell on ÔÇőa residential ÔÇőlocation in Saudi Arabia's ÔüáAl-Kharj city ÔÇőon Sunday, the ÔÇőSaudi Civil Defense said.
The two people killed ÔÇőwere of ÔÇőIndian and Bangladeshi nationalities, ÔÇîit Ôüáadded in a post on X.
Iran's Revolutionary Guards ÔÇősaid ÔÇőearlier Ôüáon Sunday that they ÔÇőtargeted radar ÔÇősystems Ôüáin locations including Saudi's Al-Kharj.

item.title: Israeli strike kills two Palestinians in a car in Gaza, medics say
item.description:
CAIRO, March 8 (Reuters) - An Israeli air strike killed at least two Palestinians in Gaza City on Sunday, local health officials ÔÇősaid, the deadliest incident in Gaza since Israel and ÔÇîthe United States launched their war against Iran a week ago.
Medics said the two people were travelling in a car near Al-Azhar University ÔÇőin western Gaza City. Their identities were not immediately ÔÇőclear. Several other people in the area were Ôüáwounded in the strike, the health officials added.
Such attacks have ÔÇődeclined since the start of the U.S.-Israeli campaign against Iran, ÔÇőalthough Israeli forces have killed several Palestinians over the past week.
The Israeli military did not immediately respond to a request for comment about ÔÇőSunday's strike.
Israel and Hamas agreed to a U.S.-brokered Gaza ÔÇőceasefire deal that kicked off last October, but violence has continued on ÔÇîa Ôüánear-daily basis. Both sides have blamed the other for the violation of the truce agreement.
The Gaza health ministry said at least 640 Palestinians have been killed by Israeli fire ÔÇősince October. Israel ÔÇősays four Ôüásoldiers have been killed by militants in Gaza over the same period.
Gaza has been devastated ÔÇőby more than two years of an ÔÇőIsraeli onslaught Ôüáthat killed over 72,000 Palestinians, according to local health authorities, and left much of the enclave in ruins.
The war was Ôüásparked ÔÇőby Hamas' October 7, 2023 attack ÔÇőon Israel, where the militants killed 1,200 people and took over 250 hostages, ÔÇőaccording to Israeli tallies.

item.title: Iran's hardline succession challenged by shrinking loyalist base
item.description:
DUBAI, March 8 (Reuters) - Iran's next leader faces a massive external assault and growing internal anger, and while previous leaders relied on a hardcore of diehard ideologues in the population, it is far from clear how far his ÔÇősuccessor can do the same.
Ayatollah Ali Khamenei was killed by U.S. and Israeli strikes a week ago and his hardline son Mojtaba Khamenei is seen as a frontrunner ÔÇîto succeed him.
Yet whether loyalists remain as numerous or as committed as in earlier decades - and whether they would rally behind a figure like Mojtaba - is increasingly uncertain.
Reuters interviews with three Basij members, as well as ordinary Iranians, officials, insiders and political analysts, point to a much narrower support base than the Islamic Republic once enjoyed.
"The strategy in choosing a hardliner as the new leader would be to consolidate the base, but they're ending up with an ÔÇőincreasingly small circle of supporters," said Ali Ansari, a modern history professor at the University of St Andrews in the UK.
"And the longer this goes on, the more it will ÔÇőall fray at the edges," he said.
The Islamic Republic emerged from a 1979 revolution backed by millions of Iranians. But decades of rule marked by corruption, Ôüárepression and mismanagement have thinned that support, alienating many ordinary people.
Still, a core of loyalists remains ÔÇö people who repeatedly show up at the ballot box to back the Islamic system and who ÔÇőturn out on the streets to crush opposition protests.
Highly organised and able to mobilise quickly, they still pose a major obstacle to any U.S. or Israeli hopes of effecting regime change.
"We have given many ÔÇőmartyrs. They have sacrificed themselves for our leader. Now we must show that the path of the leader Khamenei continues. We will solve any problems and support whoever is chosen as leader. We will even give our lives for him," said Mahdi Rastegari, 32, a religion teacher and member of the Basij, an official volunteer militia.
In the last presidential election, the most hardline candidate, Saeed Jalili, won around 9 million votes in the first round ÔÇőand 13 million in the second, according to official results. Over 61 million of Iran's more than 85 million people were eligible to vote that year.
However, the hardliners' apparent status as a small ÔÇőminority in Iran does not give much hope to all those who hope for change, and the continued bombardment has given rise to fears of chaos.
"The Guards and the system are still powerful. They have tens ÔÇîof thousands of Ôüáforces ready to fight to keep this regime in place. We, the people, have nothing," said Babak, 34, a businessman in Arak who asked to keep his family name secret.
With their leader killed on the first day of the war and cracks emerging within the country's hierarchy, the extent of continued hardline support for the Islamic Republic will now be tested like never before.
Men like the Basij member Rastegari represent a network of power extending from the supreme leader's now bombed-out office in central Tehran to every village and city neighbourhood that stands in the way of any internal ÔÇőopposition.
Every night since Khamenei's death, hardliners have held ÔÇőstate-backed mourning ceremonies for him despite the bombs Ôüáraining down across the country.
Among their number are true believers ready to die as martyrs for their fervent belief in rule by a divinely guided cleric, and those with more mercenary motives who have benefited from their status as public supporters of the system.
Another Basij member, Ali Mohammad Hosseini, goes ÔÇőfrom work at his father's grocery shop in the Shi'ite Muslim seminary city of Qom to spend his evenings manning checkpoints to deter ÔÇőany flashes of public dissent.
"The Ôüámost important issue is preserving the regime, which is what the Americans are targeting," the 29-year-old said, saying he would support whichever cleric replaced Khamenei as a "religious duty" that he was prepared to die for.
That degree of commitment is not universal, however. Another Basij member, who asked to give only his first name Hassan, and his location in the Shi'ite shrine city of Mashhad, said he had doubts the Islamic Republic Ôüáwould survive.
"We ÔÇőneed to be realistic," he said, pointing to continued U.S. pressure and the ruinous aftermath of pulverising airstrikes if a ÔÇőhardliner like Mojtaba Khamenei is named as the new leader.
Members of the Basij and others who demonstrate loyalty to the system have for decades enjoyed privileges including preferential places at university, job offers and subsidised bank loans - but a collapsing economy ÔÇőcould end such perks.
"We do not even have airports any more. No ports. How are they going to rebuild this economy?" Hassan, 29, said.

item.title: Syria's Kurds caution Iran's Kurds against aligning with US against Tehran
item.description:
QAMISHLI, Syria March 8 (Reuters) - Kurdish residents of northeast ÔÇőSyria warned Iran's Kurds against aligning with the U.S. to fight the Iranian government, citing their own experience in Syria in recent ÔÇîmonths as evidence their Iranian counterparts would be "abandoned."
Iranian Kurdish militias based in northern Iraq have consulted with the United States in recent days about whether and how to attack security forces in western Iran, as the United States and Israel pound Iran with air strikes , Reuters has reported .
But Syria's Kurds warned their Iranian counterparts against partnering with Washington.
"I hope that the Kurds of ÔÇőIran will not ally themselves with America, because they will abandon them," said Saad Ali, a 45-year-old resident of the northeastern Syrian Kurdish ÔÇőtown of Qamishli.
"Tomorrow, if an agreement is made between them (the U.S.) and the Iranians, they will eliminate you. Do not Ôüámake our mistakes," he told Reuters.
Syrian Kurdish fighters aligned with the U.S. more than a decade ago to fight the Islamic State group, setting up ÔÇőtheir own semi-autonomous zone in the territory they had seized from the ultraconservative Islamist fighters.
But in January, Syria's new army under President Ahmed al-Sharaa captured most of ÔÇőthe Kurdish-held areas in a sweeping offensive. Syria's Kurds called on the U.S. to intervene on their behalf, and felt betrayed when Washington instead urged them to merge with Sharaa's forces.
'A NEGATIVE EXPERIENCE' WITH THE U.S.
It remains a bitter experience for Syria's Kurds and a lesson they say should be heeded by Iranian Kurds.
"In my opinion, the Kurds in Iran should ÔÇőmaintain a firm stance: they will not engage in any wars within Iranian territory without firm, signed guarantees from the United States regarding the future of ÔÇőthese Kurdish regions in Iran," said Amjad Kardo, a 26-year-old Syrian Kurd in Qamishli.
"We Kurds here in Syria, in particular, have had a negative experience with the Americans in ÔÇîSyria, and Ôüátheir abandonment of Kurdish resistance movements."
An Iranian Kurdish source said Kurdish leaders did have concerns about being "betrayed" like the Kurdish groups in northern Syria.
The source said Iranian Kurdish leaders had requested guarantees from the U.S., without saying what they were.
U.S. President Donald Trump told Reuters on Thursday it would be "wonderful" if Kurdish forces crossed the border from northern Iraq into Iran, but declined to answer a question on whether the U.S. would offer them air support if they did so.
On ÔÇőSaturday, he appeared to switch positions, ÔÇőtelling reporters he doesn't want Kurdish Ôüáfighters going into Iran.
Ahmed Barakat, head of the Kurdish Progressive Democratic Party in Syria, told Reuters that Iranian Kurdish forces should exercise "extreme caution".
Barakat said the decision was ultimately up to them, but he believed that "accepting the ÔÇőinvitation of the United States and being considered the spearhead in confronting or weakening the Iranian regime is ÔÇőnot, at present, in Ôüáthe best interest of the Kurds of Iran."
Israel has been holding its own talks with Iranian Kurdish insurgent groups based in the semi-autonomous region of Iraqi Kurdistan for around a year, Reuters reported last week .
The Kurds are an ethnic group which was left stateless a century ago when the borders of the modern Middle East emerged from the Ôüácollapsing Ottoman ÔÇőEmpire.
Mostly Sunni Muslims, they speak a language related to Farsi and are concentrated in a mountainous ÔÇőregion straddling the borders of Armenia, Iraq, Iran, Syria and Turkey.
In Iraq, they inhabit three northern provinces led by their own regional government. But in other countries - Iran, Turkey and now Syria - ÔÇőtheir dreams of an autonomous region or state have remained out of reach.

item.title: Brazil President Lula's annual medical checkup test results are normal
item.description:
SAO PAULO, March 8 (Reuters) - Brazilian President Luiz Inacio Lula da Silva, ÔÇőknown as Lula, underwent ÔÇîhis annual medical checkup on Saturday morning at Sao PauloÔÇÖs Sirio-Libanes ÔÇőHospital and all test ÔÇőresults were normal, the hospital Ôüásaid on Sunday.
Lula will receive ÔÇőroutine followup care with his ÔÇőmedical team, led by cardiologist Roberto Kalil Filho and physician Ana Helena ÔÇőGermoglio, the hospital said ÔÇőin a statement, adding there are currently ÔÇîno Ôüáplans for additional exams.
Lula, 80, said in 2025 that he plans to seek reelection later ÔÇőthis year. ÔÇőIn Ôüá2024, he underwent two operations to relieve and ÔÇőprevent bleeding in his ÔÇőskull. ÔüáHe has been Brazil's president two times - from 2003 to Ôüá2010, ÔÇőand again since ÔÇőJanuary 2023.

item.title: Russia says units intercept 170 Ukrainian drones over four hours
item.description:
March 8 (Reuters) - Russia's Defence Ministry said on Sunday ÔÇőits air defence units ÔÇîhad intercepted 170 drones over various parts ÔÇőof central and ÔÇősouthern Russia over a Ôüáfour-hour period, including two ÔÇődrones headed for ÔÇőMoscow.
No damage or casualties were reported.
The ministry figures, higher ÔÇőthan average, covered ÔÇőthe period of 2 p.m. ÔÇîto Ôüá6 p.m. (1100-1500 GMT). The highest number - 73 - was recorded in Bryansk ÔÇőregion ÔÇőbordering ÔüáUkraine.
Moscow Mayor Sergei Sobyanin, writing on ÔÇőTelegram, also reported ÔÇőtwo Ôüádrones downed while heading for the capital, Ôüáwith ÔÇőemergency crews ÔÇőexamining fragments at the point ÔÇőof impact.

item.title: US considers idea of special operation to seize IranÔÇÖs uranium, Bloomberg News reports
item.description:
March 8 (Reuters) - U.S. President ÔÇőDonald Trump ÔÇîis weighing the ÔÇőoption of ÔÇődeploying special forces Ôüáon ÔÇőthe ground ÔÇőto seize IranÔÇÖs near-bomb-grade ÔÇőuranium, ÔÇőBloomberg News reported ÔÇîon ÔüáSunday, citing three diplomatic officials ÔÇőbriefed ÔÇőon Ôüáthe matter.
Reuters ÔÇőcould ÔÇőnot Ôüáimmediately verify the Ôüáreport.

item.title: German Greens lead Chancellor Merz's conservatives in state election
item.description:
BERLIN, March 8 (Reuters) - Germany's environmental Greens are set to win a state election in the southern region of Baden-Wuerttemberg, leaving them poised to continue a ÔÇőcoalition with Chancellor Friedrich Merz's conservatives, initial forecasts showed after polls ÔÇîclosed on Sunday.
The far-right Alternative for Germany (AfD) party, which has made strong gains in recent years, came third, confirming its position as Germany's main opposition party, even outside its heartland in ÔÇőthe former communist eastern states.
The first forecasts on public broadcaster ARD showed ÔÇőthe Greens in first place with 32% of the vote, followed Ôüáby Merz's Christian Democrats (CDU) on 29% and the AfD on 17.5%.
Merz's coalition partners ÔÇőin Berlin, the centre-left Social Democrats were on course for just 5.5% of ÔÇőthe vote, confirming the precipitous drop in support seen in recent years but just scraping past the minimum threshold to enter state parliament.
Baden-Wuerttemberg, home to Mercedes-Benz (MBGn.DE) , opens new tab , Porsche (P911_p.DE) , opens new tab , Bosch (ROBG.UL) and one of the ÔÇőtraditional strongholds of the German car industry, is one of Germany's most prosperous ÔÇőregions.
But its auto sector and wider economy have been hit hard by increasing competition from ÔÇîChina's surging Ôüáelectric vehicles makers.
After a decade of Green-CDU coalition government in Baden-Wurttemberg, the moderate Green candidate for state premier, Cem Ozdemir is unlikely to make life difficult for Merz in the Bundesrat, the upper house of parliament that represents the states ÔÇőin Germany's federal system.
But ÔÇőthe result underlines ÔüáMerz's struggles to cut through with voters, as promised reforms have stalled and a sluggish economy comes back slowly from ÔÇőtwo years in recession.
Initially trailing in the race, the Greens ÔÇőovertook the ÔüáChristian Democrats as the ballot approached and the result is likely to fuel discontent among conservatives already alarmed at the record low approval levels for Merz's government in ÔüáBerlin.
The Baden-Wuerttemberg ÔÇőelection was the first of five state elections ÔÇőthis year, with voters in neighbouring Rhineland-Palatinate due to go to the polls on March 22, followed ÔÇőby Berlin, Saxony-Anhalt and Mecklenberg-Vorpommern in September.

item.title: Iraqi oil production collapses with Strait of Hormuz blocked by conflict, sources say
item.description:
BAGHDAD, March 8 (Reuters) - Iraqi oil ÔÇîproduction from its main southern oilfields has fallen by 70% to just 1.3 million barrels per day as the country is unable to export oil via the Strait of Hormuz due to ÔÇőthe Iran war, three industry sources said on Sunday.
Production from the fields ÔÇőstood at around 4.3 million bpd before the war.
"Crude storage has Ôüáreached maximum capacity and the remaining output after the major cut will be ÔÇőused to supply the countryÔÇÖs refineries," said an official with the stateÔÇĹrun Basra Oil ÔÇőCompany (BOC), which manages production and export operations from the southern fields.
The Strait of Hormuz, a narrow, strategically vital waterway between the Persian Gulf and the Gulf of Oman, is one of the ÔÇőworld's key oil transit chokepoints, carrying roughly a fifth of global oil and ÔÇőliquefied natural gas flows.
The OPEC member's exports also fell sharply to an average of around 800,000 ÔÇîbarrels Ôüáper day on Sunday, with only two tankers loading because vessels cannot move freely through the strait to Iraq's southern terminals, the source said.
With no new tankers able to reach IraqÔÇÖs southern terminals, exports from there are expected to halt completely ÔÇőat around 8 p.m. ÔÇőlocal time (1700 GMT), Ôüásaid two oil officials with direct knowledge of terminal operations.
IraqÔÇÖs oil exports from the southern oilfields stood at 3.334 million ÔÇőbarrels per day in February, an oil ministry document showed.
A ÔÇődrop in ÔüáIraq's oil production and exports is set to strain the countryÔÇÖs already fragile finances, as the state relies on crude sales for nearly all public spending and more than Ôüá90% ÔÇőof its income.
"This is the most serious operational threat ÔÇőIraq has faced in more than 20 years," a senior Iraqi oil ministry official said.

item.title: Iranian army says at least 104 killed in US attack on Iranian warship last week
item.description:
March 8 (Reuters) - The Iranian army said on ÔÇőSunday that at ÔÇîleast 104 people were killed and 32 ÔÇőwere wounded ÔÇőin an attack by Ôüáthe U.S. on ÔÇőan Iranian warship ÔÇőoff Sri Lanka's coast last week.
A U.S. submarine sank ÔÇőthe frigate Dena ÔÇőin the Indian Ocean ÔÇîabout Ôüá19 nautical miles off Sri Lanka's southern port city ÔÇőof Galle ÔÇőon ÔüáWednesday, killing dozens of ÔÇősailors and dramatically ÔÇőwidening ÔüáWashington's pursuit of the Iranian navy.

item.title: New York City police identify device outside Mamdani's home as explosive
item.description:
March 8 (Reuters) - New York City Police said on Sunday a device that was ignited and thrown during protests outside Mayor Zohran MamdaniÔÇÖs home over the weekend was an explosive that ÔÇőcould have caused serious injury or death.
The device, a jar filled with nuts, ÔÇîbolts and screws and wrapped in black tape with a fuse, was thrown by a counterprotester on Saturday outside Gracie Mansion, but it extinguished itself before any explosion, according to a statement from New York ÔÇőCity Police Commissioner Jessica Tisch. Two people were in custody, Tisch said.
The device was ÔÇőone of two that were thrown during the protests, which were led Ôüáby two opposing groups, according to police. The second device was still being examined, Tisch ÔÇősaid.
Far-right activist Jake Lang led a protest on Saturday outside Gracie Mansion - where Mamdani lives ÔÇőwith his wife - against a purported Islamic ÔÇťtakeoverÔÇŁ of New York City and against public prayer by Muslims. Tisch said at a press conference on Saturday that she did not believe Mamdani and his wife were home ÔÇőat the time.
In a statement on Sunday, Mamdani condemned LangÔÇÖs protest but said the violence ÔÇőthat followed it was more disturbing.
ÔÇťViolence at a protest is never acceptable,ÔÇŁ Mamdani said. ÔÇťThe attempt to use ÔÇîan explosive Ôüádevice and hurt others is not only criminal, it is reprehensible and the antithesis of who we are.ÔÇŁ
LangÔÇÖs protest, which consisted of about 20 people, was opposed by a far larger counterprotest of 125 people aimed at running ÔÇťNazisÔÇŁ out of New York, Tisch said at a ÔÇőpress conference on Saturday. ÔÇőPolice said Emir ÔüáBalat, 18, was among the counterprotesters before he lit and threw the device.
The device rolled near police before it extinguished itself, Tisch said ÔÇőon Saturday. Balat ran after throwing it and eventually lit and dropped ÔÇőa second Ôüádevice in the street, according to Tisch.
Balat and another man, whom police on Sunday identified as Ibrahim Kayumi, were arrested at the scene, Tisch said. New York police are working with the ÔüáFBI and ÔÇőthe U.S. Department of Justice on the investigation.
Tisch said ÔÇőon Saturday there was no immediate indication the incident was related to the U.S. attack on Iran , but that authorities ÔÇőwere still investigating.

item.title: Iran names Khamenei's son, Mojtaba, as new supreme leader, state media report
item.description:
DUBAI, March 8 (Reuters) - Iran's Assembly ÔÇőof Experts has named ÔÇîMojtaba Khamenei to replace his father, Ayatollah Ali Khamenei, as ÔÇőthe country's new ÔÇősupreme leader, state media reported Ôüáon Sunday.
Mojtaba , a mid-ranking ÔÇőcleric with close ties ÔÇőto the powerful Revolutionary Guards, had long been viewed by elements ÔÇőof Iran's ruling ÔÇőestablishment as a potential successor to ÔÇîhis Ôüáfather, who was killed after the United States and Israel launched strikes on ÔÇőIran.
Although ÔÇőIran's Ôüáruling ideology frowns on the principle of hereditary ÔÇősuccession, he has ÔÇőa Ôüápowerful following within the Guards and his dead father's Ôüástill-influential ÔÇőoffice.

item.title: Iran defies Trump, elevates Khamenei's son Mojtaba as successor
item.description:
DUBAI, March 9 (Reuters) - Iran's clerical leadership chose confrontation over compromise in appointing Mojtaba Khamenei to succeed his father, Ali Khamenei, a move regional officials say is a direct rebuke to U.S. President Donald Trump, who had declared the son "unacceptable".
Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei was ÔÇőkilled in a U.S.-Israeli strike at the start of the conflict, now in its second week .
The appointment of Mojtaba as his successor by the Assembly of Experts locks hardliners firmly ÔÇîin control in Tehran - a gamble that could reshape Iran's war with the U.S. and Israel and reverberate far beyond the Middle East.
"Having Mojtaba take over is the same playbook," said Alex Vatanka, a senior fellow at the Middle East Institute.
"It's a big humiliation for the United States to carry out an operation of this scale, risk so much, and end up killing an 86-year-old man, only to have him replaced by his hardline son.ÔÇŁ
Under Iran's complex, theocratic system, the supreme ÔÇőleader is the ultimate authority, including over foreign policy and IranÔÇÖs nuclear programme, as well as guiding the elected president and parliament.
Analysts say the choice ÔÇőof Mojtaba, a deeply hardline cleric whose wife, mother and other family members were also killed in U.S.ÔÇôIsraeli strikes - sends an unequivocal message: IranÔÇÖs leadership Ôüáhas rejected any prospect of compromise to preserve the system and sees no path forward except confrontation, revenge and endurance.
According to insiders, Mojtaba will face immense internal and external strain from a disaffected population and ÔÇőan escalating conflict, but is expected to move swiftly to consolidate power.
That will likely mean expanded authority for the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps, harsher domestic controls and sweeping repression to crush dissent.
"The world will miss ÔÇőthe era of his father," a regional official close to Tehran told Reuters. "Mojtaba will have no choice but to show an iron fist... even if the war ends, there will be severe internal repression."
That stance comes after months of deepening domestic unrest - the bloodiest since the 1979 Islamic Revolution - that had already weakened the Islamic Republic before the war began.
Iran was grappling with a battered economy, soaring inflation, currency collapse and widening poverty, alongside tightening repression that had fuelled public anger and ÔÇőprotests - pressures now likely to intensify under wartime rule.
Difficult days lie ahead under Mojtaba, with far tighter internal controls, intensified pressure at home and an even more aggressive, hostile posture abroad, said ÔÇőanother Iranian insider familiar with the situation on the ground.
Paul Salem, a senior fellow at the Middle East Institute, said Mojtaba was not a figure positioned to strike a deal with the United States or pivot diplomatically.
ÔÇťNobody emerging now ÔÇîis going Ôüáto be able to compromise,ÔÇŁ Salem said. ÔÇťThis is a hardline choice, made in a hardline moment.ÔÇŁ
In the eyes of IranÔÇÖs clerics, many of whom famously label America the "Great Satan", the assassination of Khamenei, the Islamic Republic's highest religious authority, has elevated him to "martyrdom".
Clerics have cast the slain leader as a heroic figure, likening him to Imam Hussein - the ShiÔÇÖite symbol of sacrifice and resistance against oppression.
"Mojtaba is even worse and more hardline than his father," said Alan Eyre, former U.S. diplomat and Iran specialist, adding that he was the preferred candidate of the Guards. "He's going to have a lot of revenge to exact."
That calculus carries risks. Israel ÔÇőhas warned that any successor to Khamenei would also ÔÇőbe a target, while Trump has said Ôüáthe war may only end once IranÔÇÖs military leadership and ruling elite are eliminated .
A powerful midÔÇĹranking cleric, Mojtaba, 56, has long opposed reformist groups advocating engagement with the West. His close ties to senior clerics and the IRGC - which dominates IranÔÇÖs security forces and its economy - give him leverage ÔÇőacross the stateÔÇÖs political and coercive security institutions.
He amassed influence under his father as a key figure within the security apparatus and the vast business ÔÇőempire it controls, operating for Ôüáyears as Ali KhameneiÔÇÖs gatekeeper and, in practice, a ÔÇťmini-supreme leaderÔÇŁ, analysts say.
His elevation comes as the U.S.-Israeli campaign against Iran intensifies, with joint strikes hitting fuel depots and other targets inside Iran, while Iranian missiles and drones have struck Gulf states, widening the conflict.
Mojtaba studied under conservative clerics in the seminaries of Qom, the heart of ShiÔÇÖite theological learning, and holds the clerical rank of Hojjatoleslam.
The U.S. Treasury sanctioned Mojtaba in 2019, saying he represented the Ôüásupreme leader ÔÇőin an official capacity despite never holding elected or formal government office.
A Gulf source familiar with regional government thinking said of ÔÇőMojtabaÔÇÖs appointment: ÔÇťThis tells Trump and Washington that Iran will not back down, they will fight on until the finish."
Salem, of the Middle East Institute, likened IranÔÇÖs trajectory to Iraq under Saddam Hussein after 1991 or Syria under Bashar alÔÇĹAssad after 2012 ÔÇö governments that ÔÇősurvived years of war and isolation but steadily lost control.
ÔÇťTheyÔÇÖre doubling down on the hard line,ÔÇŁ Salem said. ÔÇťInternally, itÔÇÖs terrible ÔÇö and deeply destabilising.ÔÇŁ

item.title: Jailed Istanbul mayor faces trial amid crackdown on Turkey's opposition
item.description:
ISTANBUL, March 9 (Reuters) - Istanbul's jailed mayor Ekrem Imamoglu goes on ÔÇîtrial on Monday in a sweeping corruption case that could extinguish the ambition of Tayyip Erdogan's main rival to become president at Turkey's next election.
Imamoglu, 55, is the chief suspect among more than 400 defendants linked to the Istanbul ÔÇőmunicipality accused of corruption and bribery. He and his main opposition Republican People's Party (CHP) deny ÔÇőthe charges, which carry prison sentences adding up to hundreds of years.
The case Ôüáis being held at a courthouse in the Silivri prison complex west of Istanbul, where Imamoglu ÔÇőhas been held for almost a year, and is the centrepiece of a judicial crackdown on the CHP ÔÇőthat is overshadowing politics ahead of elections that are expected to be held next year.
In comments to Reuters , Imamoglu struck a defiant tone, saying Erdogan should call elections right away, but his prospects of challenging the veteran leader look ÔÇőbleak amid a crackdown that rights groups and foreign observers say has undermined the democratic credentials of ÔÇőNATO member Turkey.
"(I)t's hard to avoid the conclusion that prosecutors are trying to remove Imamoglu from politics and discredit ÔÇîhis Ôüáparty in ways that undermine democracy," said Benjamin Ward, the deputy Europe and Central Asia director at Human Rights Watch, describing the trial as coming after more than a year of "weaponising" the criminal justice system against the CHP.
The government denies exerting influence over the judiciary, which it says is independent.
Newly appointed ÔÇőJustice Minister Akin Gurlek , who ÔÇőas chief Istanbul Ôüáprosecutor led investigations into Imamoglu, said he carried out his duties in the case without bias or consideration for the mayor's position.
"I simply did my duty ÔÇőas a public prosecutor. My conscience is clear," Gurlek told reporters in ÔÇőcomments published Ôüáon Friday.
Imamoglu has already suffered a severe blow to his presidential ambitions, with a court in January rejecting his lawsuit challenging the cancellation of his university degree ÔÇô a qualification required of any presidential candidate.
Erdogan, 72, has led ÔüáTurkey ÔÇőas prime minister or president since 2003. Presidential and parliamentary elections ÔÇőare not scheduled until 2028 but if he wishes to seek a third term as president he is obliged to hold ÔÇőthe vote early, unless the constitutional term limits are changed.

item.title: US energy chief defends waiver on Russian oil sanctions, blames fear for higher gas prices
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 8 (Reuters) - Trump administration officials on Sunday defended a decision to temporarily lift some sanctions on Russian oil ÔÇîand predicted that a sharp increase in gasoline prices resulting from the Iran war would last only weeks.
Appearing on multiple TV talk shows, Energy Secretary Chris Wright and U.S. Ambassador to the United Nations Mike Waltz said a waiver issued last week to allow Indian ÔÇőpurchases of Russian oil would alleviate pressure on the global market.
"It's a 30-day pause to allow, which ÔÇőis just kind of common sense, to allow the millions and millions of barrels of Ôüáoil that are sitting out on ships to go to Indian refineries," Waltz said on NBC's "Meet the Press."
Wright ÔÇőtold CNN's "State of the Union" that the waiver can help "tamp this fear of shortage of oil, tamp the price ÔÇőspikes and the concerns we see in the marketplace."
With the war now in its second week and no end in sight, Americans are grappling with higher prices at the pump, a new complicating factor for the U.S. economy, which unexpectedly lost 92,000 jobs in February.
As ÔÇőof Friday, the national average price for regular gasoline stood at $3.32 a gallon, up 11% from the previous ÔÇőweek and the highest since September 2024, according to data from the motorists group AAA. Diesel was at $4.33, up 15% from a ÔÇîweek Ôüáago, surging to the highest level since November 2023.
"We believe this is a small price to pay to get to a world where energy prices are returned back to where they were," Wright said on the "Fox News Sunday" program.
There is no shortage of oil or natural gas, said Wright, who asserted that the price increases are based on "fear ÔÇőand perception" that the Iran ÔÇőoperation will be a Ôüádrawn-out affair.
"But it won't be," Wright said, echoing President Donald Trump 's prediction that the war will last weeks rather than months.
Trump, in a Reuters interview on Thursday, predicted that gasoline prices ÔÇőwill "drop very rapidly" when the war is over.
Senator John Kennedy, a Louisiana Republican, ÔÇőcriticized energy speculators.
"The Ôüáoil prices have gone up because you've got a bunch of oil traders out there in their Gucci loafers, with their caramel Frappuccinos who are bidding up the price," Kennedy said on "Fox News Sunday."
Political analysts say a persistent rise in Ôüágasoline prices ÔÇőcould hurt Republicans in the November midterm elections when control of ÔÇőthe U.S. Congress will be at stake. A Reuters/Ipsos poll last month found that most respondents rejected Trump's characterization of the economy as "booming."

item.title: Europe now world's biggest arms importer, think tank says
item.description:
STOCKHOLM, March 9 (Reuters) - Europe became the world's biggest arms importer over the last five years as governments reacted to Russia's threat and waning confidence in ÔÇőU.S. security commitments, data from the Stockholm International Peace Research Institute ÔÇîshowed on Monday.
European countries more than trebled their arms imports in 2021-25 versus 2016-2020 to supply Ukraine in its defence against Russian invasion and to rebuild their own militaries ÔÇőafter decades of under-investment.
"The sharp increase in arms flows to European ÔÇőstates pushed global arms transfers up almost 10 per cent," Mathew ÔüáGeorge, Director of the SIPRI Arms Transfers Programme, said.
Europe accounted for 33% ÔÇőof global arms imports, up from 12% in the previous five-year period, the ÔÇőreport showed.
Despite boosting domestic production, European countries continued to increase purchases of U.S. weapons, especially combat aircraft and long-range air-defence systems.
Arms imports by Middle Eastern states fell 13%, though ÔÇőSaudi Arabia and Qatar were among the top four individual buyers.
The decline ÔÇőmainly reflected large Saudi orders during the previous period that still needed to be integrated, ÔÇîSIPRI Ôüásenior researcher Pieter Wezeman said.
Even before the latest U.S. and Israeli attacks on Iran , states in the region had placed significant new orders that have yet to show up in the figures.
The current conflict is likely to lift procurement ÔÇőfurther, particularly of anti-missile ÔÇőand air-defence Ôüásystems.
"They will first replace what they have used, but also that they are going to look at buying more equipment ÔÇőto protect themselves even better than they can do ÔÇőnow," Wezeman Ôüásaid.
In terms of suppliers, the U.S. increased its global share of the arms export market to 42% from 36%, cementing its dominance. France was the second-largest supplier Ôüáwith ÔÇő9.8% of global exports, while Russia's share dropped ÔÇőto 6.8% from 21% after its 2022 invasion of Ukraine.
Europe's combined export share was 28% - four ÔÇőtimes Russia's and five times China's.

item.title: Oman's Muscat airport limits private jet flights, email says
item.description:
March 8 (Reuters) - Oman's Muscat International Airport has asked private jet operators to avoid using the site for "additional flights," giving priority to government ÔÇőand commercial flights as fresh airspace closures hit the regionÔÇÖs ÔÇîattempts to increase travel, according to an email seen by Reuters.
"Due to the current crisis management measures at Muscat International Airport, flight movements are restricted ÔÇőto approved seasonal scheduled services only," the airport authorities ÔÇőtold charter plane operators on Friday.
The airport said this measure Ôüáwas necessary to manage congestion and ensure that airport capacity ÔÇőremains within acceptable limits.
The email, first reported by the Financial Times, ÔÇőasked that all airlines and operators cancel any slots falling outside the approved seasonal schedule and to refrain from submitting non-approved flight requests until further notice.
"The ÔÇőonly additional flights that may be considered during this period are ÔÇőembassy-sponsored repatriation flights, subject to prior approval through the applicable diplomatic channels and ÔÇîon Ôüáthe condition that no commercial sale of seats is undertaken in connection with such operations," the email added.
The outbreak of the U.S.-Israel war against Iran has led to flight cancellations across the Middle East , leaving airlines and ÔÇőgovernments scrambling to ÔÇősupport thousands of Ôüástranded passengers.
With most airspace in the region still closed over missile and drone concerns, some people ÔÇőhave turned to private jets while charter flights and ÔÇőlimited Ôüácommercial services struggle to evacuate tens of thousands of travelers.
Travelers have shelled out high prices to get out of the Middle East, rushing Ôüáto airports ÔÇőor heading over land to quieter ÔÇőhubs, with fighter jets occasionally escorting passenger jets.

item.title: Australia tells families of diplomats to leave UAE as conflict escalates
item.description:
SYDNEY, March 9 (Reuters) - Australia has asked the family members and dependants of diplomatic officials in the ÔÇőUnited Arab Emirates to leave the country, ÔÇîfollowing an escalation in the conflict in the Middle East as several Gulf cities came under Iranian bombardment.
U.S. ÔÇőPresident Donald Trump told reporters on Air Force ÔÇőOne that he was not seeking negotiations Ôüáto end the U.S.-Israeli military strikes against Iran, ÔÇőwhich have driven up global energy prices, disrupted ÔÇőbusiness and snarled air travel.

item.title: As Fukushima memories fade, Japan embraces a nuclear-powered future
item.description:
IWAKI, Japan, March 9 (Reuters) - Takuma Hashimoto was three years old when a massive earthquake and tsunami struck on March 11, 2011, triggering nuclear meltdowns at the Fukushima Daiichi plant just an hour's drive from his home.
As the worst nuclear disaster since Chornobyl unfolded, ÔÇőhis terrified family was trapped, unable to flee like their neighbours because they couldn't find gas for their car.
Now 18, Hashimoto wants to become part of Japan's next generation of nuclear talent.
"I don't think nuclear power ÔÇîshould be treated as something that's automatically dangerous," said the engineering student at a technical college in Iwaki, where a monitoring station still keeps check on local radiation levels.
Reuters spoke with Hashimoto, as well as a former crisis management official and a nuclear industry veteran, ahead of the 15th anniversary of the disaster. Their stories illustrate how Japan is pivoting back to a power source it had all but shunned.
Resource-poor Japan was once one of the world's biggest proponents of nuclear power, which provided roughly 30% of the country's electricity from 54 reactors.
The Fukushima disaster saw public opinion swing dramatically against nuclear, ÔÇőand all reactors were ordered to be shut down for safety inspections and upgrades. In 2012, the government even decided to phase out nuclear energy. That decision was reversed two years later, but reactor restarts have been slow and ÔÇőmany have been shut permanently.
Now Tokyo's staunchly pro-nuclear leader Sanae Takaichi, buoyed by a thumping election win , is pushing to accelerate restarts and advance new nuclear technologies Ôüáto wean the country off costly imported fossil fuels.
The restart of one of the seven reactors at the world's biggest nuclear plant, Kashiwazaki-Kariwa , in January was a milestone. That said, only 15 of 33 reactors in Japan that remain operable are back online.
Middle East ÔÇőchaos unleashed by U.S. President Donald Trump's attack on Iran - a region that accounts for 95% of Japan's oil supplies - and an anticipated surge in energy needs of power-hungry AI data centres promise to bolster a shift in public opinion.
A slim majority of people - 51% - ÔÇőare now in favour of the restarts, an Asahi newspaper survey last month found. That's up from 28% when it began polling on the issue in 2013. The most supportive are young people aged 18 to 29 - at 66%.
Hashimoto's school, which has sent him on visits to nuclear power plants, receives government funding to nurture workers in nuclear power, regulation and decommissioning.
But securing enough talent may be a bottleneck in Japan's nuclear renaissance.
There were just 177 students admitted to nuclear-related courses across Japan in 2024, versus 317 just before the Fukushima disaster and a peak of 673 in the early ÔÇő1990s.
Hashimoto knows not everyone agrees with him. Almost every week, he encounters a vocal band of anti-nuclear protesters at the train station on his commute to school.
But he says: "What matters is using nuclear power properly, having measures in place in case something happens, and ÔÇődeveloping technology to make sure accidents don't occur."
Seiji Inada, 49, was part of the government's crisis response team in 2011. Tasked with tallying the dead, he hunkered down for days with hundreds of officials in an underground bunker beneath the prime minister's office in ÔÇîTokyo.
Inada remembers being Ôüáin the crisis room, watching aghast footage of a building that housed a reactor exploding.
Around 150,000 people living around the plant were evacuated, many of whom never returned, while the government assessed the risks that Tokyo, one of the world's biggest cities, might be blanketed in a radioactive plume.
"I remember during my short lunch break, I called my dad and I told him: 'Well, I can't tell you any details, but just prepare for the worst case scenario," recalled Inada, who now works for private consultancy FGS Global.
An inquiry published in 2012 blamed plant operator TEPCO (9501.T) , opens new tab , regulators and the government for failing to develop safety protocols that could have contained the damage, calling it a "man-made" disaster.
The prime minister at the time, Naoto Kan, quit after he was criticised for bungling the response.
"The lesson of 3/11 is ÔÇőhumility: low-probability shocks happen. What matters is governance," said Inada.
Back then, ÔÇőInada, like most other Japanese, found it hard to Ôüáimagine that the country would ever return to nuclear power, but "time heals trauma", he added.
"The shock of 2011 was profound and it shaped attitudes for years. Over time, the debate shifted from emotion to trade-offs."
Nuclear industry veteran Keiji Matsunaga, 59, is putting the lessons learned from Fukushima into practice by developing safer reactors at his company Toshiba.
Joining the firm straight from ÔÇőuniversity in 1991, five years after Chornobyl, he's always been conscious of the stigma attached to the industry.
Fukushima made that worse and some years after the disaster, his teenage daughter ended ÔÇőup in an argument with a Ôüáteacher at her school who said nuclear power was dangerous and Japan shouldn't be using it.
But even as Toshiba's nuclear business faltered in the wake of Fukushima and the bankruptcy of its U.S. unit Westinghouse, Matsunaga says he has never wavered from the view that Japan needs nuclear for energy security.
And the gradual increase in restarts is helping business.
Though Toshiba, now a private company, does not release its nuclear power division earnings targets, another big player, Mitsubishi Heavy Industries (7011.T) , opens new tab , told Reuters last week that sales for its nuclear power unit will hit a record 400 Ôüábillion yen ($2.5 billion) ÔÇőnext year, a target it didn't expect to reach until 2030.
Any new plants that might be built have been designed to be much safer, says Matsunaga. ÔÇőFor example, buildings housing reactors would have roofs fortified with steel plate and concrete that can resist the impact of falling aircraft.
The plants would also have more failproof natural circulation systems, in addition to other systems, to keep the reactor cool. That would help avoid a situation like Fukushima when the loss of air-conditioner ÔÇőcooling functionality contributed to the meltdowns.
"By adopting these measures, we believe events like Fukushima can be prevented," he said.

item.title: Trump says ending Iran war will be 'mutual' decision with Netanyahu
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 8 (Reuters) - U.S. President Donald Trump ÔÇősaid on Sunday ÔÇîthat when to end the war ÔÇőwith Iran will ÔÇőbe a "mutual" decision made Ôüáwith Israeli ÔÇőPrime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu.
Speaking ÔÇőby phone with The Times of Israel, ÔÇőTrump said Netanyahu ÔÇőwill have input on ÔÇîresolving Ôüáthe conflict.
"I think itÔÇÖs mutual ... a little bit. WeÔÇÖve ÔÇőbeen ÔÇőtalking. ÔüáIÔÇÖll make a decision at ÔÇőthe right ÔÇőtime, Ôüábut everythingÔÇÖs going to be taken Ôüáinto ÔÇőaccount," said ÔÇőTrump.

item.title: South Korea parliament due to finalise draft US investment bill under trade deal
item.description:
SEOUL, March 9 (Reuters) - A South Korean parliamentary committee is expected to finalise the wording of a special bill on Monday, ÔÇőpaving the way for a plenary vote this ÔÇîweek to enable $350 billion of U.S. investments under a bilateral trade deal between the countries.
The bill is expected to be put to a ÔÇőfull National Assembly vote on March 12, as ÔÇőthe U.S. ally responds to pressure from Washington over Ôüáperceived delays in enacting the deal.
The "Special Act on Investment ÔÇőin the U.S." will set up an investment vehicle, as ÔÇőwell as a risk management committee to implement last year's agreement for South Korea to invest in sectors such as shipbuilding and chips in ÔÇőreturn for lower U.S. tariffs.
U.S. President Donald Trump threatened in January ÔÇőto hike tariffs on imports from South Korea, accusing the country's parliament ÔÇîof Ôüádelays in enacting the trade deal.
Top South Korean officials have said the U.S.-South Korea trade deal remains valid despite a U.S. Supreme Court decision in February that struck down a large ÔÇőswath of Trump's ÔÇőtariffs.
Officials in ÔüáSeoul have, however, voiced concerns about the impact of U.S. investments on an already weak ÔÇőwon currency and said that projects would be ÔÇőbased Ôüáon consideration of commercial feasibility and foreign exchange market conditions.
The special committee, which includes lawmakers from the ruling Democratic Party and Ôüáthe ÔÇőopposition People Power Party, is expected to ÔÇőreview the final bill at a subcommittee on Monday and approve it at a general ÔÇőmeeting in the afternoon.

item.title: Brothers of Epstein accuser Virginia Giuffre visit New Mexico ranch, demand unredacted documents
item.description:
STANLEY, N.M., March 8 (Reuters) - Two brothers of one of Jeffrey Epstein's most prominent accusers visited the sex offender's former New Mexico ranch on Sunday for ÔÇőthe first time to demand the Trump administration release unredacted documents to reveal ÔÇîthe identities of men their late sister alleged sexually abused her at the property.
With Epstein's hacienda-style mansion in the background, the brothers of Virginia Giuffre , who took her own life in April, ÔÇőjoined hundreds of protesters at a roadside rally to mark international ÔÇőwomen's day near the gate of the ranch located 30 miles (48 Ôüákm) south of state capital Santa Fe.
Giuffre's brother Sky Roberts, 37, called on ÔÇőthe U.S. Department of Justice to release documents showing, among other things, names of ÔÇővisitors to Epstein's Zorro Ranch where he and his acquaintances are accused of sexually abusing women and girls.
"All those names are in the files and right now the government is covering ÔÇőthose up," said Roberts, flanked by Giuffre's older brother, Daniel Wilson, 47, and ÔÇőtheir families.
The release of millions of records on Epstein has exposed the financier's social connections with ÔÇîpoliticians, business Ôüápeople and scientists who he invited to the ranch.
The files have become a persistent political problem for U.S. President Donald Trump, who was named in FBI records released on Thursday in which an unidentified woman made accusations against him related to an ÔÇőalleged sexual encounter.
New Mexico ÔÇőin February became Ôüáthe first U.S. state to launch a legislative "truth commission" into how Epstein was able to operate in secrecy at Zorro ÔÇőRanch for 26 years.
ÔÇťNew Mexico is setting the example and we ÔÇőexpect other Ôüástates to follow behind,ÔÇŁ said Amanda Roberts, 37, Sky Roberts' wife, citing New York and Florida where Epstein had residences where similar probes needed to occur.
Americans generally view Ôüáthe ÔÇőEpstein case as an example of wealthy and ÔÇőpowerful people rarely being held accountable and believe the U.S. government is still hiding information about Epstein's ÔÇőclients, according to Reuters/Ipsos polling .

item.title: Compounding errors and narrow self-interest threaten global fuel crisis
item.description:
LAUNCESTON, Australia, March 9 (Reuters) - Mounting miscalculations and a retreat to narrow self-interest by major countries, including the United States and China, are threatening to turn the current conflict in Iran into a global crisis for the supply of refined oil products.
Much of the media focus tends to be on the price of crude oil, with benchmark Brent crude futures jumping as much as 20% to $111.04 a barrel, the highest since July 2022, in early Asian trade on Monday.
But while the ÔÇőleap in crude is dramatic, of more concern is the even bigger surge in the cost of refined fuels such as gasoline, diesel and jet fuel, which are the products that consumers actually buy.
The explosive climb in refined ÔÇîproduct prices last week was led by jet fuel, with Singapore spot prices hitting a record high of $225.44 a barrel on March 4, before easing to end the week at $155.82.
However, that price is still 66.7% higher than the $93.45 a barrel that prevailed on February 27, the day prior to the United States and Israel launched an aerial campaign against Iran.
Singapore gasoil , the building block for diesel and jet fuel, reached $123.39 a barrel on March 4, the highest since September 2023 and up 33.5% from the close on February 27.
In effect, product markets in Asia are starting to price in a shortage of supply of the key fuels that keep economies running.
The effective ÔÇőclosure of the Strait of Hormuz is cutting some 18 million barrels per day (bpd) of crude and products, roughly split into 14 million bpd of crude and 3 million bpd of products, according to three-month moving average data compiled by commodity analysts Kpler.
While ÔÇőthe administration of U.S. President Donald Trump claims the Strait remains open and has offered to insure vessels transiting the narrow waterway between the gulfs of Persia and Oman, the market has yet to Ôüábe convinced that Iran won't attack any ship attempting passage.
It is the closure of the Strait of Hormuz that is the most important strategic error made by the Trump administration and Israel in their attack on Iran.
In common with the majority of analysts and presumably Gulf governments ÔÇőthe assumption was that this conflict would follow the same template as previous flare-ups in the region.
The assumption was that everybody would behave rationally and not target oil production and transportation infrastructure, as it is in nobody's interest to halt the flow of crude and products.
But it turns out ÔÇőif you tell a dictatorial religious government that your aim is regime change, then that government feels little compunction to play by the prior rules.
Iran's decision to target its Gulf neighbours that host U.S. military bases has upended all previous calculations about the conflict.
Gulf countries Saudi Arabia, the United Arab Emirates, Qatar, Kuwait and Iraq are highly dependent on their oil, fuel and liquefied natural gas exports, and right now their revenue is severely curtailed.
Dubai, one of the Emirates, is also increasingly dependent on its status as the region's financial and tourism hub, with both sectors now taking a massive hit from the ongoing conflict.
The compounding errors of ÔÇőthe Trump administration are now becoming more apparent.
It's clear that they did not expect the Strait of Hormuz to be effectively closed, and their stop-gap solution hasn't won market backing.
It's one thing to offer insurance and perhaps naval escorts, it's entirely another to ensure safe passage can happen ÔÇőfor hundreds of vessels each week.
All Iran has to do is hit one fully laden crude tanker with a ballistic missile and all bets will be off, with the situation then likely to be worse than it is currently.
The next U.S. step of offering a waiver to India to resume purchases ÔÇîof Russian crude is Ôüáa bit like feeding cookies to an elephant, it's nice but largely ineffective.
What is already happening is that Asia's refiners are scrambling to source crude from other suppliers outside the Strait of Hormuz choke point.
What is the likely result of this?
Crude oil prices will rise, and the longer the close of the Strait continues the more short of crude, and thus refined products, the region will become.
The response to this looming shortage is that countries with refining capacity will focus on their domestic needs and cut exports of fuels, thereby exacerbating the shortage of refined products.
This can be seen by reports that China has ordered its major state-owned refiners to stop exports of refined products.
Some Indian refiners are also reported to be ending exports of fuels, while plants in some countries, such as South Korea, are cutting refinery processing.
The obvious question for the authorities in Beijing is ÔÇőhow long before the decision not to supply refined fuels to ÔÇőimporting nations blows back on the Chinese economy.
It is another example Ôüáof a miscalculation that will prove more costly than the alternative of actually ramping up fuel exports to help meet demand.
China isn't a major exporter of refined products, with about 600,000 barrels per day.
But it is the only major producer with significant spare capacity and a massive crude oil stockpile, estimated at over 1 billion barrels.
This level of inventories means China could continue to refine oil at its current rate for about ÔÇőthree years, even if its imports dropped to zero.
In effect, China could use its huge stockpile to boost refinery runs, lift exports and ease the looming supply crisis.
This would not only be highly ÔÇőprofitable but would also win favour with Ôüáimporting nations, who may otherwise run short of fuels.
Australia is Asia's biggest fuel importer, taking about 900,000 bpd, according to Kpler.
Imagine a situation when the country could no longer meet this demand, resulting in shortages.
The government would have to prioritise food production and delivery, and keeping as much of the economy going as possible.
But a brave Australian government could say to China that no more iron ore would be shipped because of diesel shortages unless Beijing supplied refined fuels.
China gets about two-thirds of its iron ore imports from Australia and without these flows its steel industry would be severely curtailed, which in Ôüáturn would devastate manufacturing ÔÇőand construction.
Getting to this point would only happen if the Strait of Hormuz remains largely closed and countries follow narrow and short-term self-interest.
The problem is that political leaders ÔÇőso far seem unable to grasp the extent of the stress they are putting on the energy supply system, and their actions suggest they see only their own country's needs without realising that it is a global system that needs global solutions.
Enjoying this column? Check out Reuters Open Interest (ROI) , your essential new source for global financial commentary. ROI delivers thought-provoking, ÔÇődata-driven analysis of everything from swap rates to soybeans. Markets are moving faster than ever. ROI can help you keep up. Follow ROI on LinkedIn , opens new tab and X , opens new tab .
The views expressed here are those of the author, a columnist for Reuters.

item.title: The Week in Breakingviews: WhatÔÇÖs next for Iran?
item.description:
LONDON, March 8 (Reuters Breakingviews) - Welcome back! A week ago I woke up to find out that the United States and Israel were attacking Iran . ItÔÇÖs the second time this year that President Donald Trump has targeted another countryÔÇÖs leader ÔÇô on a Saturday. If this newsletter was forwarded to you, sign up here to get it in your inbox every weekend.
ÔÇťIn SienaÔÇÖs brutal Palio horse race, a steed can ÔÇőstill win after losing its rider. Banca Monte dei Paschi di Siena (BMPS.MI) , opens new tab , headquartered in the same picturesque Tuscan town, is applying that tradition to hostile bank M&A.ÔÇŁ
Read more here: ItalyÔÇÖs MPS adds governance risk to dicey ÔÇîM&A .
ÔÇťNo operational plan can extend with any degree of certainty beyond the initial encounter with the main enemy force.ÔÇŁ Field Marshal Helmuth von MoltkeÔÇÖs advice , opens new tab to military ÔÇőstrategists is also a warning for anyone drawing lasting conclusions from the first days of a war. A week after the United States and Israel attacked Iran and killed its Supreme Leader, Ayatollah Ali Khamenei, it would be ÔÇőfoolish to predict the outcome of the conflict, let alone its long-term repercussions. Instead, here are the five most pressing questions:
This key question is the hardest Ôüáto answer. Many strategists expect it to be short, in part because the U.S. bombing campaign means Iran will eventually run out of missiles and launching sites it needs to retaliate. With tepid public support at home and the price of petrol rising, ÔÇőU.S. President Donald Trump may also want to avoid a drawn-out battle. But on Friday he demanded ÔÇť unconditional surrender ÔÇŁ. Financial markets are beginning to price in a longer conflict: the price of a barrel of oil ended the week above $90. Yet while the spot ÔÇőprice of gas in Europe has jumped, contracts for delivery in a few years have barely budged.
Trump could declare ÔÇťmission accomplishedÔÇŁ and attempt to move on, as his predecessor George W. Bush did six weeks after the invasion of Iraq in 2003. But that historical precedent reminds us that an end to fighting depends on what happens on the ground. As George Hay argues , there are three broad scenarios for Iran: the current government clings on to power under a new leader; the country splits along factional or ethnic lines; or the population rises up ÔÇőand takes power. Only the third scenario is consistent with more enduring calm. DonÔÇÖt forget that itÔÇÖs less than nine months since Trump claimed to have destroyed IranÔÇÖs nuclear program.
Blocking the Strait of Hormuz was long seen ÔÇőas a move Iran would only make in desperation. About a fifth of the worldÔÇÖs oil and gas passes through the narrow waterway. Yet the outbreak of hostilities has produced two extraordinary outcomes. First, tanker traffic has stopped largely on the strength of Iranian threats : anxious shipping ÔÇîcompanies and jittery Ôüáinsurers did the rest. Second, the blockage has not yet led to the $200-a-barrel oil price traders once predicted. Markets may expect a swift reopening, but as Yawen Chen points out , this is not entirely in TrumpÔÇÖs gift.
The United Arab Emirates, Qatar, and their neighbours have presented themselves as hyper-modern and impartial finance and technology hubs. They have attracted banks, tax exiles and tourists, while making splashy investments around the world. Iranian missiles and drones flying over gleaming towers shattered that glitzy image. Even though most of the projectiles have been shot down , George Hay and Afiq Fitri Alias make the case that the blow to the GulfÔÇÖs regional reputation is likely to last ÔÇô and may benefit Saudi Arabia. Meanwhile, extra spending on domestic defence ÔÇőmeans less cash for overseas investments.
The president revels in his unpredictability. After abducting Venezuelan ÔüáPresident Nicolas Maduro and killing Khamenei he may feel emboldened to settle other scores. On Thursday, he hinted at intervention in Cuba as ÔÇťjust a question of timeÔÇŁ. Yet overseas military adventures clash with his domestic agenda. Meanwhile, increased military spending, higher interest rates and missing tariff revenue are increasing the risks of a sovereign debt spiral, Gabriel Rubin warns . Success in Iran could backfire ÔÇőat home.
Every financial meltdown brings obscure acronyms to the fore. The Enron scandal taught us about special purpose entities (SPEs). In 2008 collateralised debt obligations (CDOs) were on everybodyÔÇÖs ÔÇőlips. Today, BDCs are finding a Ôüáwider audience. Business development companies are vehicles that allow asset managers to accumulate funds without a set expiration date. Now theyÔÇÖre under stress. Jonathan Guilford explains whatÔÇÖs going on .
If youÔÇÖre looking for something a bit more optimistic, how about a fresh perspective on Africa? ItÔÇÖs easy to be gloomy about its prospects. But Joe Studwell, author of ÔÇťHow Africa WorksÔÇŁ, offers a glass-half-full perspective about the vast continent. He joined me on The Big View , opens new tab to discuss his research and conclusions.
Over on the Viewsroom , opens new tab , George Hay and Yawen Chen joined Aimee Donnellan Ôüáand Jonathan Guilford ÔÇőto provide a download on the fast-moving Iran conflict, and the fallout for energy markets.
For years, Goldman Sachs (GS.N) , opens new tab hid its inner workings behind a ÔÇőthick veil of discretion. But the firm has changed, and this week Lloyd Blankfein published ÔÇťStreetwise: Getting to and Through Goldman SachsÔÇŁ, an amusing and at times brutally frank account of his career at the bank he led between 2006 and 2018. It makes for a highly readable book ÔÇô and also reminds us ÔÇőthat Goldman is now a bit less special .
Want to receive The Week in Breakingviews in your inbox every Saturday? Sign up for the newsletter here .
Follow Peter Thal Larsen on Bluesky , opens new tab and LinkedIn , opens new tab .

item.title: Japan should use nuclear plants to offset Iran crisis, opposition party head says
item.description:
TOKYO, March 9 (Reuters) - Japan should operate all of its nuclear power plants available to offset the impact of the ÔÇőIran war on electricity bills, Yuichiro Tamaki, the ÔÇîleader of a Japanese opposition party, said on Monday.
Japan relies on the Middle East for around 95% of its oil supplies ÔÇőand 11% of its liquefied natural gas imports ÔÇőwith around 70% and 6% respectively coming via Ôüáthe Strait of Hormuz, which is effectively closed due ÔÇőto the war.
"Unless we make full use of nuclear power ÔÇőas a carbon-free power source with less dependence on overseas sources, electricity bills will inevitably rise," Tamaki, who heads the Democratic ÔÇőParty for the People, said in a statement on ÔÇőX.
Oil prices surged about 20% on Monday, hitting their highest since ÔÇîJuly Ôüá2022, as the expanding U.S.-Israeli war with Iran fuelled fears of tighter supply.
Japan shut down all of its 54 nuclear power reactors - which provided roughly 30% of the ÔÇőcountry's electricity - in ÔÇőthe wake Ôüáof the 2011 Fukushima Daiichi plant nuclear meltdowns. Thirty-three remain operable but only 15 ÔÇőhave been restarted.
"A policy shift is needed ÔÇőto protect Ôüáthe lives of the people," said Tamaki, who has consistently supported restarting and expanding nuclear power in Japan.
New Prime ÔüáMinister ÔÇőSanae Takaichi is also staunchly ÔÇőpro-nuclear and Japan's public is also gradually becoming more supportive of nuclear power.

item.title: VIEW Oil surges 20% as Iran war fuels supply fears
item.description:
SINGAPORE, March 9 (Reuters) - Oil prices surged about 20% in early trade on Monday, hitting their highest since July 2022, as the expanding U.S.-Israeli war with Iran led some major Middle East oil producers to cut supplies and on concerns of ÔÇőprolonged disruption to shipping through the Strait of Hormuz.
COMMENTARY:
"I think prices have ÔÇîrallied this morning on the reports that Middle East producers are now reducing output due to storage facilities filling up fast."
"I certainly think the spectre now of Middle Eastern producers curtailing output is going to keep those prices elevated. The next flag will be whether it eventually reaches a ÔÇőpoint where they have to start shutting in oil wells, which not only further impacts output but also delays a ÔÇőresponse once the conflict eases. That would potentially sustain those prices for much longer."
"A sudden supply shock reverberates well beyond just who is a net energy exporter and ÔÇőimporter. There are acute supply chain effects beyond how just price eats into margins. Even for a place like Indonesia (where) it is ÔÇőnot unusual to see street protests if pump prices go up."
"Asia takes the brunt of the sharp escalation in oil prices and there are few places to run and hide. The dollar has to be the one outperforming, given Japan and Korea's exposures here and the sharp pain that ÔÇőcan be expected from Brent at $107."
"The market had been complacent about the scope ÔÇőand duration of the war and associated supply disruptions until last Friday.
"Its a case of the oil market who cried wolf. After three years ÔÇîof geopolitical Ôüárisk premia rising only to fail to translate to supply disruptions, the market became complacent about the current events.
"But this existential Iran war is the energy crisis scenario that has been wargamed for 50 years, finally coming to fore."
"Our baseline scenario is the conflict in Iran will be large but short-lived, though there is a clear risk of a prolonged ÔÇőwar. Among emerging markets, the ÔÇőeconomic impact will be felt Ôüámost clearly in the Gulf Cooperation Council, reflecting the shock's adverse impact on trade, logistics, tourism and investment."
"First, Pakistan and India are most vulnerable as they are energy importers with relatively high exposure ÔÇőto the Strait of Hormuz. Second, outside of physical trade disruption, Egypt and Turkiye are ÔÇőmost exposed. This Ôüáis due to their high energy import bill, fragile external positions, large energy subsidies and unanchored inflation. Third, commodity producing economies in Sub-Saharan Africa and Latin America are least at risk, including Nigeria, Ghana and Peru."
"The threats to attack refineries Ôüáare ÔÇővery concerning. Because it threatens the worst of all economic worlds. Cutting off ÔÇő15%-20% of the world's oil supply not only slows down every economy globally, but it introduces an inflation impulse. And inflation plus slowing growth is stagflation, and ÔÇőthat's an economic disaster."

item.title: Australians reach for VPNs, find porn sites blocked as online age-restrictions take effect
item.description:
SYDNEY, March 9 (Reuters) - Australians ÔÇőwere downloading virtual private networks (VPNs) in droves, while one of the world's largest pornography distributors said ÔÇîit was blocking users as the country rolled out sweeping online age restrictions on Monday.
Last December, Australia became the first country to impose a nationwide ban on teenagers using social media. A separate law now requires AI-powered chatbot services to keep certain content - including ÔÇőpornography, extreme violence and self-harm and eating disorder material - from minors or face fines of up to ÔÇőA$49.5 million ($34.5 million).
The country also joined Britain, France and dozens of U.S. states requiring Ôüáwebsites which disseminate pornography to verify users are over 18. App stores must also run age checks before ÔÇőallowing downloads of software labelled 18+.
The country's eSafety Commissioner Julie Inman Grant said the measures aimed to afford children ÔÇőthe same protection online as the world expected offline.
"A child today can't walk into a bar and order a drink, they can't stroll into a strip club or browse an adult shop or sit down at a blackjack table in a casino," she told ÔÇőthe Australian Broadcasting Corp.
"This just really brings ... those protections that we put for kids in place to the ÔÇődigital realm."
Three of the 15 most downloaded free smartphone apps on Monday were VPNs, a chart published by iPhone maker Apple (AAPL.O) , opens new tab ÔÇîshowed. The Ôüámost downloaded VPN, called VPN - Super Unlimited Proxy, ranked ahead of any social media platform, the chart showed.
VPN - Super Unlimited Proxy did not immediately respond to a Reuters request for comment. All internet-connected devices carry an individual code which discloses their location, and VPNs hide the user's location by assigning a new code to the device.
Canada-based Aylo, ÔÇőowner of a large network ÔÇőof pornography websites, meanwhile Ôüáblocked Australians from accessing the platforms RedTube and YouPorn, while presenting a version of Pornhub without explicit content.
All the websites carried a banner saying it was "not currently accepting ÔÇőnew account registrations in your region".
Aylo did not respond to a Reuters request for ÔÇőcomment. The Sydney ÔüáMorning Herald quoted an Aylo spokesperson saying the company's Australian users would be "presented with a safe-for-work experience when they view our platforms".
Tom Sulston, deputy chair of advocacy group Digital Rights Watch, said it was unsurprising that people were reaching Ôüáfor VPNs.
"My ÔÇőhope is that, not only will they discover that this works ÔÇőfor looking at spicier internet sites, but that it's just generally a good idea to use VPNs when you're traversing the internet, because they ÔÇődo offer you some privacy protections."
($1 = 1.4351 Australian dollars)

item.title: Shots fired at Rihanna's house in Beverly Hills, no injuries reported, media say
item.description:
March 8 (Reuters) - A woman fired numerous shots into the Beverly ÔÇőHills home of pop music ÔÇîstar Rihanna on Sunday, and a round went through a wall ÔÇőof the house, local news ÔÇőoutlets reported Sunday.
