The Los Angeles Times Ôüáand NBC4, citing a ÔÇőLos Angeles Police spokesperson, reported ÔÇőthat authorities responded to the shooting at 1:21 p.m. (2021 GMT) Sunday and detained ÔÇőa 30-year-old female suspect.
Los ÔÇőAngeles police and a representative for Rihanna did ÔÇînot Ôüáimmediately respond to Reuters requests for comment.
Rihanna was at home but no injuries were ÔÇőreported, the ÔÇőLos ÔüáAngeles Times said, citing a source. The ÔÇőnewspaper cited police radio ÔÇőtraffic Ôüáthat "approximately 10 shotsÔÇŁ had been fired from a vehicle across Ôüáthe ÔÇőstreet from the ÔÇőpropertyÔÇÖs gate.

item.title: Bangladesh shuts universities early to save power amid energy crisis
item.description:
DHAKA, March 9 (Reuters) - Bangladesh will close all universities from Monday, bringing forward the Eid al-Fitr holidays as part of emergency measures to conserve ÔÇőelectricity and fuel amid a worsening energy crisis linked to ÔÇîthe conflict in the Middle East.
Authorities said the decision applies to all public and private universities across the country, a move that officials said will not only ÔÇőreduce electricity consumption but also ease traffic congestion, which leads ÔÇőto fuel wastage.
Officials said university campuses consume large amounts of Ôüáelectricity for residential halls, classrooms, laboratories and air conditioning, and the ÔÇőearly closure would help ease pressure on the countryÔÇÖs strained power ÔÇősystem.
Government and private schools in Bangladesh are already closed for the Islamic holy month of Ramadan, meaning most educational institutions across the country will now remain shut ÔÇőduring the period.
The move comes as Bangladesh faces mounting uncertainty over ÔÇőfuel and gas supplies following disruptions to global energy markets caused by the ongoing ÔÇîconflict Ôüáin the Middle East.
The country, which relies on imports for 95% of its energy needs, imposed daily limits on Friday on fuel sales after panic buying and stockpiling.
As part of broader austerity measures, the government has also ÔÇőasked all foreignÔÇĹcurriculum schools and ÔÇőprivate coaching Ôüácentres to suspend operations during this period to limit electricity use.
Severe gas shortages have already forced Bangladesh to halt ÔÇőoperations at four of its five stateÔÇĹrun fertiliser ÔÇőfactories, redirecting Ôüáavailable gas to power plants to avoid widespread outages.
The country has also bought LNG from the spot market at sharply higher prices while seeking additional Ôüácargoes ÔÇőto bridge supply gaps.
ÔÇťWe are doing everything ÔÇőwe can to reduce consumption and ensure stability in power, fuel and import supplies,ÔÇŁ a ÔÇősenior energy ministry official said.

item.title: Ukraine sent drone experts to protect US bases in Jordan, Zelenskiy tells NYT
item.description:
March 9 (Reuters) - Ukraine sent interceptor drones and ÔÇőa team of ÔÇîdrone experts to protect U.S. military ÔÇőbases in ÔÇőJordan, President Volodymyr Zelenskiy Ôüásaid in ÔÇőan interview with The ÔÇőNew York Times published on Monday.
The U.S. ÔÇőmade the ÔÇőrequest for help on Thursday, ÔÇîand Ôüáthe Ukrainian team left the next day, Zelenskiy ÔÇősaid ÔÇőadding Ôüáthat it was expected to ÔÇőarrive in ÔÇőthe ÔüáMiddle East soon, according to Ôüáthe ÔÇőreport.

item.title: Indonesia searches for missing after landfill collapse kills four
item.description:
JAKARTA, March 9 (Reuters) - Rescuers are still searching for five missing people after a large stack of ÔÇőgarbage collapsed at Indonesia's biggest landfill ÔÇîsite over the weekend, killing at least four people, an official said on Monday.
The collapse took place ÔÇőat the Bantargebang landfill on Sunday, located ÔÇőin the region of Bekasi on the Ôüáoutskirts of the capital Jakarta, said Desiana ÔÇőKartika Bahari, head of the local rescue agency.
It ÔÇőwas likely triggered by the heavy rain that hit the area from Saturday evening, she told Reuters.
"It was ÔÇőraining all day even from (Saturday) evening and ÔÇőthe mountain of garbage was unstable," Bahari said.
Some trucks were ÔÇîunloading Ôüágarbage when the collapse took place, and there was also a food stall near the site.
"The missing people are the truck drivers and ÔÇőscavengers," she ÔÇősaid, adding Ôüáthat there there could be more people buried in the landfill.
Four ÔÇőpeople survived the accident.
More than 200 rescuers, ÔÇőincluding Ôüápolice and military personnel and 17 excavators, have been deployed since Monday morning to search Ôüáfor ÔÇőthe missing.
Bantargebang covers around 110 ÔÇőhectares and receives around 6,500 tons-7,000 tons of garbage per ÔÇőday.

item.title: Lawyers for Bondi Beach shooting suspect seek gag order to protect family
item.description:
SYDNEY, March 9 (Reuters) - Lawyers for a man accused of carrying out a deadly shooting on Sydney's Bondi Beach sought a court order ÔÇőon Monday to stop media naming his family, saying their ÔÇîsafety was at risk.
Police allege 24-year-old Naveed Akram opened fire on a Jewish Hanukkah celebration on December 14, killing 15, in Australia's worst mass shooting in decades.
His ÔÇőfather, Sajid Akram, who police also accuse of carrying out ÔÇőthe shooting, was shot dead by police at the scene.
Police Ôüásay the men were inspired by the Islamic State militant group ÔÇőto carry out the attack using several high-powered weapons the elder man had legally ÔÇőacquired.
Lawyers for Naveed Akram on Monday requested a court in Sydney prohibit the publication of the names of his mother, brother and sister, as well as their ÔÇőhome address and places of work and schooling. Magistrate Greg Grogan ÔÇőextended an interim suppression order until next month, citing the global media interest in ÔÇîthe Ôüácase.
Akram faces a total of 59 charges over the attack, including 15 counts of murder, 40 counts of wounding with intent to murder and a terror offence. He is yet to enter a plea.
The case ÔÇőis expected to ÔÇőbriefly return Ôüáto court next week, before a longer hearing on April 8, where the prosecution will outline its brief ÔÇőof evidence.
The Bondi attack shocked a country with strict ÔÇőgun laws Ôüáand fuelled calls for tougher controls and stronger action against antisemitism.
Australia last month launched a government-backed inquiry into antisemitism and social cohesion in the country Ôüáwhich ÔÇőis expected to report its findings by ÔÇőDecember this year.
The government has already responded by tightening gun laws and introducing new legislation ÔÇőagainst hate speech.

item.title: Governments' actions in response to oil price surge and the escalating Middle East conflict
item.description:
March 9 - Oil prices have soared while share markets have skidded on fears that the escalating U.S.-Israeli war on ÔÇőIran will squeeze energy supplies and hamstring industries ÔÇîaround the world.
Following are actions that governments are taking or plan to take to reduce the impact of the war on ÔÇőtheir economies.
South Korean President ÔÇőLee Jae Myung said on Monday that authorities Ôüáwould cap domestic fuel prices for the first time ÔÇőin nearly 30 years. The country will also look ÔÇőfor sources of energy beyond supplies shipped via the Strait of Hormuz, and a 100 trillion won ($67 billion) market-stabilisation programme should be ÔÇőexpanded if needed, he added.
The Japanese government instructed a national oil ÔÇîreserve Ôüástorage site to prepare for a possible release of crude, Akira Nagatsuma, a member of the Centrist Reform Alliance opposition party, told Reuters on Sunday.
Details such as ÔÇőthe timing of ÔÇőthe release Ôüáremain unclear, Nagatsuma said.
Vietnam is planning to remove import tariffs ÔÇőon fuels to ensure supplies amid disruptions, ÔÇőthe Ôüágovernment said, adding that the measure is expected to last until the end of April.
Bangladesh Ôüáwill close ÔÇőall universities from Monday, bringing forward ÔÇőthe Eid al-Fitr holidays as part of emergency measures to conserve ÔÇőelectricity and fuel.

item.title: How Europe can play a bad hand over Iran
item.description:
LONDON, March 9 (Reuters Breakingviews) - How many wake-up calls does Europe need? Donald TrumpÔÇÖs first administration did not do the trick, despite the U.S. presidentÔÇÖs anti-European rhetoric. Nor did RussiaÔÇÖs full-scale invasion of Ukraine four years ago. Nor did TrumpÔÇÖs re-election in 2024. Now in quick succession this year, Washington has pummelled Venezuela, threatened Greenland and attacked Iran.
It is hard to see anything good for Europe from the ÔÇőUnited States-Israeli attack on the Islamic Republic. It strengthens Russia and weakens Ukraine, threatening EuropeÔÇÖs own security. It undermines the global rule of law, which is valuable for the region given that it is ÔÇînot a geopolitical power.
The latest Middle Eastern conflict is also threatening EuropeÔÇÖs economy. Much, of course, will depend on how long the conflict goes on ÔÇö in particular whether Iran can keep up a blockade of oil and gas through the Strait of Hormuz. As a hydrocarbon importer, Europe is vulnerable to high energy prices. Its mostly highly indebted governments are not well placed to cushion a blow to either businesses or consumers. Meanwhile, a descent into civil war in Iran, or a further crackdown on internal dissent, could unleash a wave of immigration, with some of its 93 million ÔÇőcitizens heading towards Europe.
In the short run, there is little the region can do but suffer and squirm. A principled response would have been to condemn TrumpÔÇÖs attack as contrary to international law while at the same time ÔÇődenouncing IranÔÇÖs nuclear weapons programme. But Europe is too dependent on U.S. military support to do that, especially given the Russian threat. It is also increasingly dependent on American energy Ôüásince its gas purchases from Moscow are down dramatically and now Qatar is offline .
Spain has criticised the attack on Iran. But EuropeÔÇÖs big three ÔÇö Germany, France and Britain ÔÇö have provided half-supportive , opens new tab statements , opens new tab that sit uneasily with their values. Not that that has earned them U.S. ÔÇőgratitude: Trump lambasted British Prime Minister Keir Starmer as ÔÇť not Winston Churchill ÔÇŁ.
The lesson is clear. Europe will be helpless in a world of sharks until it gets stronger and more united. The EU itself will need greater integration. Meanwhile, the United Kingdom requires ÔÇőa bolder partnership with the club it left as a result of Brexit, covering economic and energy security as well as defence.
EuropeÔÇÖs core vulnerability is that it needs U.S. protection because it cannot yet defend itself against Russia on its own. Its biggest worry therefore is that the Iran war is strengthening Moscow by relaxing the grip on the KremlinÔÇÖs financial windpipe.
True, Vladimir Putin has had to sit by idly as the United States has bombed an ally and killed its leader. This is bad for the Russian presidentÔÇÖs prestige. What is more, Tehran will ÔÇőstruggle to supply Moscow with more attack drones, as it will need any it can produce for itself.
But what Russia gains from higher oil and gas prices probably more than compensates for this loss. At the start of the year Moscow ÔÇőwas feeling the squeeze from tighter sanctions and lower crude prices. Trump was strong-arming India, previously RussiaÔÇÖs largest customer after China, to stop buying its oil. Moscow had to offer purchasers deep discounts on the global price while some cargoes were piling up on the high ÔÇîseas unsold. ÔüáThe governmentÔÇÖs oil and gas revenue halved to $5.1 billion in January compared to the same month last year.
These dynamics are going into reverse. Brent crude prices are up 50% to around $90 a barrel since the start of the year, before the drumbeats of the Middle East war started in earnest. European gas prices have roughly doubled. With rival Gulf suppliers unable to export, Russia has bargaining power. Indian refiners have started buying its crude again, paying a $4-$5 a barrel premium over Brent on a delivered basis compared to a $13 discount for cargoes traded last month. The KremlinÔÇÖs war chest could soon fill up.
What is more, Ukraine will struggle to get more of the anti-missile defences it desperately needs from the United States, as Washington will keep these for itself, Israel ÔÇőand its Gulf allies. This will make UkraineÔÇÖs infrastructure vulnerable ÔÇőto Russian attack. Not that Kyiv is totally without Ôüácards. It has developed cheap anti-drone defences that could be of use to Washington and Gulf states ÔÇö and may be able to trade some of these for sophisticated weapons it does not produce itself.
Even so, the overall balance has swung in MoscowÔÇÖs favour. And that is bad for Europe.
To be fair, Europe increasingly realises that it will only be able to advance its ÔÇővalues and interests if it increases its autonomy. For example, France last week set out a plan to boost its nuclear arsenal and involve European allies in war games with ÔÇőits atomic weapons. Meanwhile, the EU Ôüáunveiled proposals to work with trusted partners to boost investment in strategic economic sectors to cut dependence on the likes of China.
The EU is also making a renewed attempt to boost its competitiveness by breaking down barriers in services industries and unifying its capital markets. It seems increasingly determined to move ahead even if some of its 27 members disagree. Great Britain is also getting closer to the bloc, albeit with insufficient ambition or urgency.
But it will take a long time for these initiatives to bear fruit. In the meantime, Ôüámany Europeans will be ÔÇőin two minds about how they want TrumpÔÇÖs new war to pan out. If the U.S president triumphs he may feel emboldened to bully ÔÇőEurope ÔÇö perhaps turning his attention back to Greenland, which is part of Denmark, an EU member. On the other hand, if the United States gets embroiled in a quagmire in Iran, the big winner may be China ÔÇö and Europe will not be happy if the PeopleÔÇÖs Republic throws its weight around more.
All ÔÇőthe more reason to heed the wake-up call and make itself stronger and more unified.
Follow @Hugodixon , opens new tab on X

item.title: New Delhi has weak hand in bank deal frenzy
item.description:
MUMBAI, March 9 (Reuters Breakingviews) - A dealmaking boom in India's banking sector has an unlikely loser: the government. Canadian insurance holding firm Fairfax Financial (FFH.TO) , opens new tab leads , opens new tab the race to buy a ÔÇő61% stake from Indian state entities in $13 billion IDBI Bank (IDBI.NS) , opens new tab , Bloomberg reported in February, citing sources. An $8 ÔÇîbillion transaction would be the largest-ever foreign direct investment in a local bank. But crystallising a premium valuation looks challenging.
A deal would complete a full circle for the lender hardest hit by an asset quality crisis: in 2018, bad loans comprised nearly one-third of its portfolio. Provisions for that ÔÇősour pool eroded its capital base and prompted New Delhi, which then owned 86% of IDBI, to press state-backed ÔÇőLife Insurance Corporation (LIFI.NS) , opens new tab to pump in 216 billion , opens new tab rupees, or $2.4 billion at current rates, to raise its 8% Ôüástake to 51% in 2019.
LIC now holds 49% of IDBI's shares and the government owns 45%. Selling a 30% stake ÔÇőto Fairfax at the latest market price would fetch the insurer a 136% return on its 2019 investment. New Delhi would be ÔÇőworse off, though: the lender's shares trade lower than they did 13 years ago.
Yet even current multiples may be difficult to fetch. IDBI's shares are trading at about 2 times forward book value, almost twice that of similar-sized rivals Yes Bank (YESB.NS) , opens new tab and IDFC First Bank (IDFB.NS) , opens new tab . Throwing in employee liabilities, restructuring ÔÇőcosts and the likely absence of indemnity clauses gives the buyer a strong case for a discount.
An abundance of takeover targets ÔÇőhas hurt New Delhi, too. Launched in 2022, the slow-moving sale process of IDBI prompted early potential bidders to look elsewhere: last year ÔÇîSumitomo Mitsui ÔüáBanking Corporation (8316.T) , opens new tab bought a 24% stake in Yes Bank.
With Emirates NBD (ENBD.DU) , opens new tab still in the reckoning with Fairfax, it's a two-horse race to own IDBI. Both bidders already have a foothold in India's credit market: the Dubai-headquartered lender is set to take control of the $2 billion RBL Bank (RATB.NS) , opens new tab and Fairfax owns $675 million CSB Bank (CSBB.NS) , opens new tab .
That chips away at any shred of bargaining power left with the sellers, ÔÇőwho can hardly demand a ÔÇőcontrol premium. Regulations cap voting Ôüárights of private bank shareholders at 26%. That puts the new owner effectively at par on voting decisions with LIC and the government, which will hold a combined 34% after the sale. ÔÇőTo maximise takings, officials could ask the central bank to relax the voting rule. The ÔÇőother option is Ôüáto reduce their total stake to well below 26%.
Otherwise, New Delhi risks catching the weak end of India's banking M&A wave.
Follow Shritama Bose on LinkedIn , opens new tab and X , opens new tab .

item.title: G7 to discuss joint release of emergency oil reserves, FT reports
item.description:
March 9 (Reuters) - The ÔÇőGroup of Seven (G7) finance ministers will ÔÇîdiscuss on Monday a joint release of oil from emergency reserves coordinated by ÔÇőthe International Energy Agency, the ÔÇőFinancial Times reported.
Three G7 countries, including Ôüáthe U.S., have so far ÔÇőexpressed support for the idea, the ÔÇőFT said citing sources, and added that the ministers and the IEA Executive Director ÔÇőFatih Birol will hold a call ÔÇőto discuss the impact of the Iran ÔÇîwar.
The Ôüáreport comes as oil prices surged more than 25% on Monday to their highest levels since mid-2022 as some ÔÇőmajor producers ÔÇőcut Ôüásupplies and fears of prolonged shipping disruptions gripped the ÔÇőmarket due to the expanding ÔÇőU.S.-Israeli Ôüáwar with Iran.
The IEA and the G7 presidency did not respond to Ôüárequests ÔÇőfor comment outside regular ÔÇőbusiness hours.

item.title: Indian refiners tumble as crude surges on widening Iran war
item.description:
March 9 (Reuters) - Shares of Indian refiners slumped on Monday, as the widening U.S.-Israeli war with Iran propelled ÔÇőBrent crude prices toward $120 per barrel - a near four-year high - threatening their ÔÇînear-term earnings.
State-run Indian Oil (IOC.NS) , opens new tab dipped 6.6%, Hindustan Petroleum (HPCL.NS) , opens new tab slid 7.5% and Bharat Petroleum (BPCL.NS) , opens new tab dropped 7.1%, and were headed for their steepest falls in more than ÔÇőa year.
Reliance Industries (RELI.NS) , opens new tab lost 2%.
Brent crude soared as ÔÇőmuch as 26.4% to $117.16 a barrel and was Ôüálast up 23% at $114.08 by 9:15 a.m. IST.
UBS said ÔÇőoil marketing companies are "negatively leveraged" to the crude spike as ÔÇőthey sell more diesel and gasoline than they produce, estimating sales-to-production ratios at 1:2 for IOC and BPCL and 2:2 for ÔÇőHPCL.
The brokerage cut IOC and BPCL to "neutral" and BPCL to "sell" from "buy".
The ÔÇőrout dragged the Nifty oil and gas index (.NIFOILGAS) , opens new tab down 2.7% and the ÔÇîenergy Ôüáindex (.NIFTYENR) , opens new tab 2.1% lower, while the benchmark Nifty 50 (.NSEI) , opens new tab slid 2.8%. The oil and gas index has fallen 6.6% since the U.S.-Israeli strike on Iran on February 27.
Citi warned that refiners' ÔÇőearnings will hinge ÔÇőon how long the Ôüágeopolitical shock lasts, flagging risks from a potential closure of the Strait of Hormuz and ÔÇőany shutdown of Qatar's LNG output - each supplying ÔÇőabout Ôüáhalf of India's crude and LNG imports.
Any disruption beyond the one month currently priced in could sharply tighten LNG Ôüámarkets, ÔÇőwith low European storage for October ÔÇő2026 raising the risk of "non-linear" price spikes, the brokerage added.

item.title: Azerbaijan reopens Iran border to cargo traffic after drone incident, TASS reports
item.description:
BAKU, March 9 (Reuters) - Azerbaijan said on Monday it ÔÇőhad reopened its border ÔÇîcrossings with Iran for all cargo traffic, the ÔÇőRussian state-run TASS ÔÇőnews agency reported.
The border Ôüácrossings, one of the ÔÇőshortest land routes ÔÇőconnecting Iran to its ally Russia, were closed last week ÔÇőafter what Baku ÔÇődescribed as an Iranian drone attack ÔÇîin Ôüáthe Nakhchivan exclave.
Late on Sunday, Iranian President Masoud Pezeshkian phoned his ÔÇőAzerbaijani ÔÇőcounterpart ÔüáIlham Aliyev, telling him Iran had ÔÇőnot been involved ÔÇőin Ôüáthe Nakhchivan incident and that Tehran was Ôüáinvestigating, Aliyev's ÔÇőoffice said.

item.title: Khamenei's hardline son Mojtaba appointed as Iran's new leader
item.description:
DUBAI/JERUSALEM, March 9 (Reuters) - Iran on Monday named Mojtaba Khamenei to succeed his father Ali Khamenei as supreme leader , signalling that hardliners remain firmly in charge, while the week-old ÔÇőU.S.-Israeli war with Iran sent oil prices surging and Asian stock markets into a nosedive.
Iranian institutions and politicians, from the foreign ministry to lawmakers, issued statements expressing their allegiance to the country's new supreme leader ÔÇîas the war entered its 10th day and fresh missile and drone strikes reverberated across the Middle East.
"We will obey the commander-in-chief until the last drop of our blood," a statement from the defence council said.
Senior cleric Ayatollah Sadeq Amoli-Larijani said Mojtaba Khamenei's appointment was "a balm for the spiritual suffering of our people and an emphasis on the need to continue the luminous path of the late Imam (Khamenei senior)."
Mojtaba's father, Supreme Leader Ali Khamenei, was killed in one of the first strikes launched against Iran more than a week ago.
The show of solidarity for Mojtaba comes ÔÇőafter U.S. President Donald Trump earlier rejected him as a candidate to be Iran's new supreme leader, and Israel saying it would target whoever leads Iran.
Mojtaba, a cleric with influence inside Iran's security forces and vast ÔÇőbusiness networks under his father, had been viewed as a frontrunner in the lead-up to Sunday's vote by the Assembly of Experts, a body of 88 clerics tasked with choosing ÔüáAli Khamenei's successor.
The position gives Mojtaba the final say in all matters of state in the Islamic Republic.
Trump said on Sunday that Washington should have a say in the selection. "If he doesn't get approval from us, he's not going to ÔÇőlast long," he told ABC News, adding that ending the war would be a "mutual" decision with Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu.
In an interview with the Times of Israel after the new supreme leader was named, Trump declined to respond, saying only "We'll see ÔÇőwhat happens," according to the newspaper.
Pope Leo on Sunday warned of "a tragedy of enormous proportions" due to the "widespread climate of hatred and fearÔÇŁ in Iran and across the region.
"Let us raise our humble prayer to the Lord that the roar of bombs may cease, that weapons may fall silent, and that space may be opened for dialogue in which the voices of peoples can be heard", Leo said at the Angelus prayer in St. PeterÔÇÖs Square.
The Israeli military said on Monday it had begun a wave of attacks in central Iran ÔÇőand had struck infrastructure belonging to Hezbollah militants in Beirut.
Iran and its proxies appeared to have launched attacks too, with rocket and drone strikes targeting a U.S. diplomatic facility near Baghdad's international airport that were intercepted by C-RAM defence system, ÔÇősaid police sources.
A drone strike targeted a U.S. military base near Erbil airport in Iraqi Kurdistan, security sources said, while Saudi authorities reported intercepting a drone east of its northern Jawf region.
Thick smoke was seen rising from the direction of the Bapco oil refinery in ÔÇîBahrain, according to Ôüáa Reuters witness, after the government said an Iranian drone strike hit the area causing injuries and damage. Bapco Energies later declared force majeure on its group operations following the refinery attack, adding to fears about energy supplies from the region.
The U.S. military reported a seventh American has died from wounds sustained during Iran's initial counter-attack a week ago, a day after Trump presided over the return to the United States of the remains of the six others killed.
The Israeli military said on Sunday that two of its soldiers were killed in southern Lebanon, marking the first fatalities among its troops since Israel-Hezbollah hostilities resumed last week as Israel intensified air strikes on Lebanon.
The military did not immediately provide further details on the circumstances of the incident.
At ÔÇőleast four people were killed when an Israeli strike ÔÇőhit an apartment in the Ramada hotel building in Ôüácentral Beirut early on Sunday, with Israel saying it targeted Iranian commanders operating in the Lebanese capital.
The U.S.-Israeli attacks have killed at least 1,332 Iranian civilians and wounded thousands, according to Iran's U.N. ambassador.
Oil prices surged more than 25% on Monday to their highest levels since mid-2022, as major Middle Eastern oil producers cut supply because they cannot safely send ÔÇőshipments through the Strait of Hormuz to refiners worldwide.
Traffic through the Strait was largely after Iran attacked at least five ships, with a limited number of tankers transiting, choking off ÔÇőa key artery accounting for about Ôüá20% of global oil and LNG supply.
Brent crude futures were up $24.96 or 27% at $117.65 per barrel at 0451 GMT - on track for the biggest-ever jump in a single day, while U.S. West Texas Intermediate crude futures were up $25.72, or 28.3%, to $116.62.
The war has forced production stoppages by oil and gas exporters from Qatar to Iraq, with Kuwait announcing cuts over the weekend. Analysts predict the United Arab Emirates and Saudi Arabia will soon cut output as they run out of oil storage.
The G7 finance ministers will discuss Ôüáon Monday a ÔÇőjoint release of oil from emergency reserves coordinated by the International Energy Agency, the Financial Times reported.
Three G7 countries, including the U.S., have so ÔÇőfar expressed support for the idea, the FT added.
A senior Japanese parliament member said on Sunday that the government instructed a national oil reserve storage site to prepare for a possible release of crude. Japan relies on the Middle East for around 95% of its crude, with roughly 70% shipped ÔÇőthrough the Strait of Hormuz.
The spike in oil prices weighed on stock markets in Asia, with Japan's Nikkei (.N225) , opens new tab down 5.8% and import-reliant South Korea falling 6.5%.

item.title: Kenya says death toll from floods nearly doubles to 42
item.description:
NAIROBI, March 9 (Reuters) - The death toll from heavy rains and flooding in Kenya's capital Nairobi and elsewhere has nearly ÔÇődoubled to 42, the government said in a ÔÇîstatement issued late on Sunday.
Intense rains on Friday unleashed heavy and widespread flooding, causing some people to drown, washing away vehicles ÔÇőand disrupting traffic at the country's largest airport.
The ÔÇőprevious death toll was 23.
Emergency workers from various Ôüáagencies including the military were still conducting search ÔÇőand rescue operations across the country, Geoffrey Kiringa Ruku, ÔÇőminister for public service and human capital development said in the statement.
"Search mission is still in progress by the multi agency emergency ÔÇőresponse teams with the aim of ensuring that ÔÇőbodies of all the flood victims are found and retrieved," he ÔÇîsaid.
The Ôüáfloods had also done extensive damage to infrastructure and livelihoods. But 172 vehicles that had been swept away by flood waters have been recovered.
On Saturday, President William ÔÇőRuto said he ÔÇőhad ordered relief Ôüáfood from the country's national strategic reserves be immediately released for distribution to families ÔÇőaffected by the floods.
Scientists say global warming ÔÇőis Ôüáworsening floods and droughts across East Africa by concentrating rainfall into shorter, more intense bursts. A 2024 World Weather ÔüáAttribution ÔÇőstudy found climate change had made ÔÇődevastating rains in the region twice as likely as before.

item.title: Turkey to deploy six F-16 fighter jets to northern Cyprus, broadcaster NTV says
item.description:
ANKARA, March 9 (Reuters) - Turkey plans to deploy ÔÇősix F-16 fighter jets ÔÇîto northern Cyprus on Monday to ÔÇőbolster the defences ÔÇőof the Turkish community Ôüáthere, broadcaster NTV ÔÇősaid, citing a ÔÇőcivil aviation official from the breakaway Turkish Republic ÔÇőof Northern Cyprus.
On ÔÇőSaturday, a Turkish defence ministry ÔÇîsource Ôüásaid Turkey was considering the deployment of F-16 aircraft to ÔÇőCyprus, ÔÇőamong Ôüáother steps being taken to ÔÇőensure the ÔÇősecurity Ôüáof the Turkish Cypriot state as conflict Ôüáspreads ÔÇőin the ÔÇőregion.

item.title: Allowing Iranian ship to dock was right thing to do, Indian foreign minister says
item.description:
March 9 (Reuters) - Iran had sought India's permission ÔÇőfor three of ÔÇîits ships to dock at its ÔÇőports, which ÔÇőwas granted on March Ôüá1, India's ÔÇőforeign minister said ÔÇőon Monday, adding that the government believed that ÔÇőit was ÔÇőthe "right thing to do".
One ÔÇîof Ôüáthe three ships docked at India's southern port ÔÇőof Kochi ÔÇőon ÔüáMarch 4 and its ÔÇőcrew was ÔÇőat ÔüáIndian naval facilities, Subrahmanyam Jaishankar told Ôüáparliament ÔÇőon ÔÇőMonday.

item.title: Tracking LNG flows as key global gas prices go haywire
item.description:
LITTLETON, Colorado, March 9 (Reuters) - The sudden stoppage of LNG exports from Qatar - the world's second-largest exporter in 2025 - has sent natural gas prices soaring by 50% from year-ago levels across Europe and Asia and has prompted panicked buyers to seek out replacement cargoes.
However, a shortage of available vessels and limited spare liquefaction capacity is restricting ÔÇőthe number of liquefied natural gas cargoes available for immediate delivery to either region, and is likely to keep gas prices elevated for some time.
That said, major LNG ÔÇîexporters outside the Middle East are re-routing whatever shipments they can to needy buyers, spurred on by bumper profit potential created by the widening premium of gas prices in Asia and Europe over local prices.
Here's a breakdown of the latest natural gas price action in key markets that is likely to drive further cargo diversions, as well as the current LNG export flows from key suppliers.
Exporters in the U.S. are likely best placed to capitalize on the redirection opportunities, as ÔÇőthe top global exporter likely has the greatest volumes of undeclared capacity available for spot shipment.
However, exporters in Australia, Russia, Malaysia and Nigeria - the next largest LNG shippers, according to Kpler - ÔÇőwill likely also adjust delivery schedules and locations after the steep run-up in natural gas prices in Asia and Europe.
LNG forward contracts for delivery to key Ôüámarkets in Asia currently average around $12.95 per million British thermal units (MMBtu) for 2026 as a whole, data from LSEG shows.
That marks a 53% rise compared to 2025's average levels, and would mark the highest ÔÇőannual average since 2022.
In Europe, benchmark TTF futures for 2026 currently average around $12.41 per MMBtu, which marks a 49% increase from 2025's average.
While those forward prices are bound to shift dramatically over the coming weeks in ÔÇőresponse to the ongoing market gyrations, it is instructive to take a snapshot of the current picture in order to assess potential arbitrage opportunities.
U.S. exporters face projected average natural gas costs of around $3.63 per MMBtu in 2026 - per current LSEG data - and so could score profits of more than 200% even after accounting for liquefaction and shipping costs to either Europe or Asia.
The profit potential is less clear for other exporters such as Australia and Russia, due to more ÔÇőopaque gas and liquefaction cost levels.
Nonetheless, as long as Europe's and Asia's gas costs remain close to current levels, it can be safely assumed that all LNG exporters face historic profit potential if they could ÔÇőeffectively divert cargoes to those markets.
LNG analysts are already closely scrutinizing shipment patterns from major exporters for signs of sudden diversions.
The U.S. shipped 68% of its LNG to Europe in 2025, and is expected to continue ÔÇîprioritising Europe Ôüáover other regions due to the comparably low cost of shipping to Europe.
That said, loading schedules for the first three months of the year indicate that U.S. exporters raised shipments to Asian buyers by nearly 20% compared to the same period in 2025.
Asia-bound tonnage from the U.S. for the first quarter of 2026 is estimated at 3.75 million metric tons, compared to 3.16 million tons for the same period in 2025, data from Kpler shows.
U.S. shipments to Europe from January to March are estimated at 22.7 million tons, compared to 19.8 million tons for the same months last year.
With buyers in both Asia and Europe set to ÔÇőremain strong bidders for LNG over the near ÔÇőto medium term, further increases in U.S. sales Ôüáto both regions can be expected.
In contrast to U.S. trade flows, an overwhelming majority of shipments from Australian LNG exporters have been bound for customers in Asia, with Asia accounting for over 95% of Australian exports last year.
Relatively short transit times to the likes of China and Japan compared to cargoes shipped ÔÇőfrom the United States means that Australia retains a cost advantage to Asia buyers, as well as more extensive relations with regional buyers.
However, Australian LNG ÔÇősellers have already committed close Ôüáto all their projected supplies to contracted buyers, and so may have limited scope to redirect any shipments to the spot market where potential buyers are prepared to pay up for deliveries.
That may leave openings for other exporters including Russia, Malaysia and Nigeria, which have already established global LNG trade routes and look well placed to offer further competition to their larger exporting peers going forward.
The opinions expressed here are those of the author , a Ôüácolumnist for Reuters.

item.title: Governments scramble to limit fallout of Iran war as oil prices surge
item.description:
SINGAPORE, March 9 (Reuters) - Governments in Asia are scrambling to limit the impact on ÔÇőeconomies and consumers from the widening Iran war, which fuelled a record surge in oil prices on Monday after key ÔÇîproducers cut output and Tehran signalled that hardliners will remain in charge.
In South Korea , which buys 70% of its oil from the Middle East, President Lee Jae Myung said Seoul would cap fuel prices for the first time in nearly 30 years and warned against panic buying.
Speaking at an emergency meeting, Lee called the crisis "a significant burden ÔÇőon our economy, which is highly dependent on global trade and energy imports from the Middle East."
A senior Japanese parliament member ÔÇősaid on Sunday that the government had instructed a national oil reserve storage site to prepare for a possible Ôüácrude release, although the country's chief cabinet secretary said on Monday that no decision had been made to release stockpiles.
Japan imports around 95% of ÔÇőits oil from the Middle East. It has reserves to cover 354 days of consumption.
Elsewhere, Vietnam removed import tariffs on fuels and Bangladesh shut universities to ÔÇőconserve electricity and fuel, while China last week asked refiners to halt fuel exports and try to cancel shipments already committed.
U.S. President Donald Trump tried to tamp down concern about rising U.S. gasoline prices, which were up 11% for the week on Friday, while Senate Minority Leader Chuck Schumer called on him to ÔÇősell oil from the Strategic Petroleum Reserve .
"Short term oil prices, which will drop rapidly when the destruction of the Iran nuclear threat is over, ÔÇőis a very small price to pay for U.S.A., and World, Safety and Peace," Trump posted on Truth Social on Sunday night. "ONLY FOOLS WOULD THINK DIFFERENTLY!"
Oil jumped 25% , with ÔÇîBrent Ôüáon track for a record one-day gain, after Iran on Monday named Mojtaba Khamenei to succeed his father Ali Khamenei as supreme leader, while OPEC producers Kuwait and Iraq cut oil output during the weekend as the crucial Strait of Hormuz remained effectively shut.
Across Asia, which sources 60% of its oil from the Middle East, stock prices slid and the dollar rose, with officials looking to contain the impact ÔÇőof oil prices, including weighing the ÔÇőrelease of strategic reserves, as Ôüáworries mounted that disruption in energy supplies could be prolonged.
Iran on Monday named Mojtaba Khamenei to succeed his father Ali Khamenei as supreme leader, a move expected to draw the ire of Trump. Weekend attacks on Iranian ÔÇőoil storage facilities fueled fears of retaliatory strikes on energy facilities.
"Oil prices have now gathered all the ÔÇőingredients for a Ôüáperfect storm - Middle East Gulf producers cutting output, the prolonged closure of the Strait of Hormuz ... all compounded by a growing pessimism about a quick turnaround in the current situation," said Kpler senior oil analyst Muyu Xu.
Iraq cut oil production from its main southern oilfields by 70% to 1.3 million barrels per Ôüáday, three ÔÇőindustry sources said on Sunday, while Kuwait Petroleum Corp began cutting oil output on Saturday ÔÇőand declared force majeure.
No.2 LNG exporter Qatar has already halted exports of the superchilled fuel, and analysts predict that the United Arab Emirates and Saudi Arabia will also have to ÔÇőcut output soon as they run out of oil storage due to the Strait of Hormuz closure.

item.title: Saudi Aramco offers crude in rare tenders as Iran conflict disrupts exports, traders say
item.description:
SINGAPORE, March 9 (Reuters) - Saudi Aramco has offered ÔÇőmore than 2 ÔÇîmillion barrels of Saudi crude in ÔÇőrare tenders ÔÇőas the U.S.-Iran conflict Ôüádisrupted exports from ÔÇőthe Middle East, ÔÇőtrade sources said.
The state company offered 2 ÔÇőmillion barrels of ÔÇőArab Heavy crude for ÔÇîMarch Ôüáloading in one of the tenders it issued last ÔÇőweek. ÔÇőIt Ôüáalso offered 650,000 barrels ÔÇőof Arab Light ÔÇőcrude Ôüáin a separate tender, the Ôüápeople ÔÇősaid.

item.title: Bahrain's Bapco announces force majeure on operations
item.description:
March 9 (Reuters) - Bapco Energies declared ÔÇőforce majeure ÔÇîon its group operations ÔÇőon Monday ÔÇőfollowing a recent Ôüáattack on ÔÇőits refinery ÔÇőcomplex, the company said.
Bapco said ÔÇőthat all ÔÇődomestic market needs ÔÇîremain Ôüáfully secured and supplies will continue ÔÇőwithout ÔÇődisruption, Ôüásupported by proactive ÔÇőplans ÔÇőin Ôüáplace.

item.title: Oil pares gains but still on track for record surge
item.description:
SINGAPORE, March 9 (Reuters) - Oil prices came off earlier highs on Monday but were still up more than 15% at levels not seen since ÔÇőmid-2022 as some major producers cut supplies and fears of prolonged shipping disruptions gripped the market due to the expanding U.S.-Israeli war with Iran .
Brent crude futures were up $15.51, ÔÇîor 16.7%, at $108.20 per barrel at 0642 GMT - on track for the biggest-ever jump in a single day, while U.S. West Texas Intermediate (WTI) crude futures were up $14.23, or 15.7%, at $105.13.
Disruptions in tanker movements and rising security risks have already slowed shipping activity, and left Asian buyers reliant on Middle Eastern crude especially vulnerable because the crisis is unfolding around the Strait of Hormuz, through which roughly one-fifth of the world's oil supply passes.
WTI surged 31.4% ÔÇőto a session high of $119.48 a barrel earlier on Monday, while Brent rose as much as 29% to $119.50 a barrel. Before the surge on Monday, Brent had already climbed ÔÇő27% and WTI by 35.6% last week.
Prices pared gains after the Financial Times reported that the Group of Seven (G7) finance ministers and the International ÔüáEnergy Agency will discuss on Monday a joint emergency oil reserves release , and Saudi Aramco offered prompt crude supply through a series of rare tenders .
""Unless oil flows through the Strait of Hormuz resume ÔÇősoon and regional tensions ease, upward pressure on prices is likely to persist," said Vasu Menon, managing director for investment strategy at OCBC in Singapore.
Iraq and Kuwait have begun cutting oil output, adding ÔÇőto earlier liquefied natural gas reductions from Qatar, as the war blocked shipments from the Middle East. Analysts expect the United Arab Emirates and Saudi Arabia will have to also cut output soon as they run out of oil storage.
Refinery disruptions continued due to escalating tensions in the region, with Bahrain's BAPCO announcing a force majeure following a recent attack on its refinery complex.
Fujairah Media Office said a fire broke out in the UAE's Fujairah oil ÔÇőindustry zone resulting from debris falling, with no injuries reported. Saudi Arabia's Defence Ministry said on X it intercepted a drone heading to the Shaybah oilfield.
Also boosting prices is the appointment of Mojtaba Khamenei ÔÇőto succeed his father Ali Khamenei as Iran's supreme leader, signalling that hardliners remain firmly in charge in Tehran a week into its conflict with the United States and Israel.
"With the appointment of the late leader's ÔÇîson as ÔüáIran's new leader, U.S. President Donald Trump's goal of regime change in Iran has become more difficult," said Satoru Yoshida, a commodity analyst with Rakuten Securities.
"That view accelerated buying, as Iran is expected to continue its closure of the Strait of Hormuz and attacks on other oil-producing nations' facilities, as seen last week," he said, predicting WTI could rise to $120 and then $130 a barrel in a relatively short period.
The war could leave consumers and businesses worldwide facing weeks or months of higher fuel prices even if the week-old conflict ends quickly, as suppliers ÔÇőgrapple with damaged facilities, disrupted logistics and elevated ÔÇőrisks to shipping.
"The next flag will be Ôüáwhether it eventually gets to a point where they have to start shutting in oil wells, which not only impacts output even further, it delays a response once the conflict eases as well. That would potentially sustain those prices for much longer," said Daniel Hynes, senior commodity strategist at ÔÇőANZ.
Iraqi oil production from its main southern oilfields has fallen by 70% to just 1.3 million barrels per day as the country is unable ÔÇőto export oil via Ôüáthe Strait of Hormuz due to the Iran war, three industry sources said on Sunday. Crude storage has reached maximum capacity, said an official with the state-run Basra Oil Company.
Kuwait Petroleum Corporation began cutting oil output on Saturday and declared force majeure on shipments, though it did not say how much production it would shut.
Israel's military has threatened to kill any replacement for the deceased Ali Khamenei, while Trump said the Ôüáwar might only ÔÇőend once Iran's military and rulers had been wiped out.
Meanwhile, as oil prices surged, U.S. Senate Democratic Leader Chuck ÔÇőSchumer called on Trump to release oil from the Strategic Petroleum Reserve.
"President Trump should release oil from the SPR now to stabilize markets, bring prices down, and stop the price shock that American families are already feeling thanks to his ÔÇőreckless war," Schumer said in a statement.

item.title: Players' union raises alarm over safety of Iran women's soccer team after Asian Cup exit
item.description:
SYDNEY, March 9 (Reuters) - Global players' union FIFPRO said on Monday there were serious concerns for the welfare of the ÔÇőIranian women's soccer team, as they prepared to return home after being labelled "wartime traitors" for refusing to ÔÇîsing their national anthem before an Asian Cup match.
The Iranians' campaign in the Australian-hosted tournament started last weekend just as the U.S. and Israel launched air strikes on Iran, killing the Islamic Republic's Supreme Leader Ali Khamenei.
The team was eliminated on Sunday after losing 2-0 to the Philippines. Fans waved the pre-1979 Iranian ÔÇőflag, booed the national anthem and tried to prevent the team coach from leaving afterwards, chanting "Save our girls!" ÔÇőamid concerns for their safety after their anthem silence.
More than 66,000 people have also signed a petition calling Ôüáon the Australian government to ensure the players, who are on the Gold Coast in Queensland, do not leave "while credible fears for ÔÇőtheir safety remain".
Beau Busch, FIFPRO president for Asia and Oceania, said the union had been unable to contact the players to discuss ÔÇőwhether they would like to seek asylum in Australia.
"The reality at the moment is that we're unable to get in touch with the players. That's incredibly concerning. That's not a new thing. That's really been since the repression really dialled up in February, January," Busch told reporters.
"So ÔÇőwe're really concerned about the players, but our responsibility right now is to do everything within our power to try and make ÔÇősure that they're safe."
Busch said the organisation was working with FIFA, the Asian Football Confederation and the Australian government to ensure that "every bit ÔÇîof pressure Ôüáis applied" to protect the players and give them "agency around what happens next".
"It's a really challenging situation," he said.
"There may be players that want to return. There may be some players within the group that would like to seek asylum and would like to stay in Australia for longer."
The players' decision to stand in silence during Iran's anthem before their first ÔÇőmatch against South Korea was ÔÇőlabelled by a commentator Ôüáon Islamic Republic of Iran Broadcasting as the "pinnacle of dishonour".
"Traitors during wartime must be dealt with more severely," presenter Mohammad Reza Shahbazi said.
When they sang the anthem and saluted before their second ÔÇőmatch against Australia, it sparked fears among human rights campaigners the team had been coerced ÔÇőby government minders.
Iran Ôüácoach Marziyeh Jafari has said they were keen to return home. "We are very impatiently waiting to return," she said during a post-match press conference.
Most of the airspace in the Middle East remains closed as a result of the war.
When asked about whether Australia would grant the Ôüáplayers ÔÇőasylum, Matt Thistlethwaite, the assistant minister for foreign affairs and trade, said the ÔÇőgovernment could not "go into individual circumstances for privacy reasons".
"Anyone that seeks to come to Australia or applies for a visa obviously must meet the conditions, but ÔÇőI can't go into the specifics of details," he told Sky News.

item.title: Synagogue in Belgian city of Liege damaged in explosion, media reports
item.description:
March 9 (Reuters) - A synagogue in ÔÇőthe Belgian city of Liege ÔÇîwas damaged by an explosion early on Monday, Belgian broadcasters VRT ÔÇőand RTBF reported, citing ÔÇőlocal police.
No injuries were reported. ÔüáThe cause of the ÔÇőexplosion, which happened around 4 ÔÇőa.m. local time (0300 GMT), was unknown, VRT said.
Liege's mayor Willy Demeyer called ÔÇőthe incident an "extremely violent ÔÇőact of antisemitism" in comments made to ÔÇîFrench Ôüálanguage public broadcaster RTBF.
The explosion blew out the windows of the synagogue, as well ÔÇőas those ÔÇőof Ôüáa building on the opposite side of ÔÇőthe road, the broadcaster ÔÇősaid.
Police Ôüácordoned off the street while a specialised counter-terrorism team investigated Ôüáthe ÔÇőincident.
Local police were ÔÇőnot immediately available for comment.

item.title: Wall Street's immunity to Mideast oil shock will now be tested
item.description:
ORLANDO, Florida, March 9 (Reuters) - Oil prices surged 30% last week and swathes of global stocks sold off as war in the Middle East escalated, yet an air of complacency hung over Wall Street.
Investors have likely been betting that the turmoil will have a short-lived, mild economic impact, much like most crises in the recent past. Is this ÔÇőan efficient market at work, or wishful thinking? We'll soon find out.
West Texas Intermediate crude rose 35% last week, the biggest weekly rise since the U.S. oil benchmark futures ÔÇîwere launched in 1983, yet the S&P 500 (.SPX) , opens new tab fell only 2%. The Nasdaq (.IXIC) , opens new tab dipped just over 1%.
What gives?
Some investors may believe the U.S. is better positioned to handle an energy shock than many of its peers because it is a net energy exporter. A lingering belief that "U.S. exceptionalism" is alive and well could also be at play.
It's also notoriously difficult to price geopolitical risk, so many asset managers may simply be sitting on their hands and waiting for more clarity, particularly those reluctant ÔÇőto let go of their "fundamentally" bullish outlook on corporate America.
Whatever the root, there is a danger that this is misplaced optimism at best, or reckless complacency at worst.
Consider this. In the first ÔÇő41 trading days of 2026, the S&P 500's trading range, as measured by the difference between the highest and lowest daily closing prices, was just 2.7%, Ôüáaccording to The Kobbeissi Letter, a global markets newsletter. That's the narrowest range for that period on record, going back to 1928.
But this will now be tested. Oil is on track for one of its biggest ÔÇődaily gains in decades , with both Brent and WTI soaring above $100 per barrel on Monday. Prices were up more than 25% at one point during Asian trading hours, though they have retreated slightly.
Other countries' stock markets have ÔÇőbeen rocked. Benchmark European, Asian and emerging market indices, which fell 5-7% last week, are putting up more big losses. Japan's Nikkei is down another 5% today, with Korea's benchmark index falling even further.
Granted, many of these countries are net energy importers, meaning they are more exposed to the historic spikes in oil and natural gas prices than the Unite States. Japan, for example, imports 90% of its energy, with 95% of its oil imports coming from the Middle East.
But can Wall ÔÇőStreet remain an outlier?
From a U.S. macroeconomic perspective, the Middle East conflict couldn't be more ill-timed. Inflation was already running at 3%, well above the Federal Reserve's 2% target and creeping higher, and payrolls ÔÇődata on Friday showed that the U.S. economy lost 92,000 jobs in February.
The whiff of stagflation is growing stronger, and Fed officials can only hold their noses for so long. Chair Jerome Powell, and his likely successor Kevin Warsh, ÔÇîmay be Ôüádamned by Wall Street ÔÇô or, more likely, bond investors ÔÇô if they risk letting inflation get out of control by taking a dovish stance moving forward. But they also risk angering markets if they take a hawkish, anti-growth position that prioritizes price stability.
Yet policy paralysis can't be good either.
Investors appear to be banking that history repeats itself. In recent decades, most bouts of global geopolitical turmoil have sparked a few weeks of mild volatility, then a quick recovery, leaving everyone wondering what all the fuss was about.
Research by Parag Thatte and Binky Chadha at Deutsche Bank shows that geopolitical shocks have had an average negative impact of around 6-8% on U.S. ÔÇőequities over the subsequent three-week period, with markets ÔÇőrecovering those losses within another three weeks.
Larry Adam, Ôüáchief investment officer at Raymond James, notes that since the 1990s, the S&P 500 has been higher on average one, three, six, and 12 months after geopolitical shocks.
JPMorgan analysts say the typical scenario around a major geopolitical event is a 5-6% drawdown in stocks, which is then clawed back within a few weeks.
"There is ÔÇőa tendency among macro strategists to dismiss geopolitics and oversimplify the response: just buy the dip," they wrote on Friday, adding that this rule of ÔÇőthumb has been true approximately Ôüá80% of the time over the past 60 years.
"We think the current episode with the Iran invasion is indeed a buy-the-dip scenario," they added.
Yet the drawdowns appear to be getting shallower, and as of Friday, there was barely a dip to buy.
Are markets getting smarter and better able to look through headline noise now that computers are doing the trading? Maybe the playbook of past crises still applies. Or, has complacency set in, and it really is Ôüádifferent this ÔÇőtime?
The jury is still out, but Wall Street futures are down early on Monday. So a verdict may be imminent.
(The opinions expressed ÔÇőhere are those of Jamie McGeever , a columnist for Reuters)

item.title: South Korea to impose fuel price cap to shield economy from energy shock
item.description:
SEOUL, March 9 (Reuters) - South Korean President Lee Jae Myung said on Monday that authorities would cap ÔÇîdomestic fuel prices for the first time in nearly 30 years to contain a spike in prices after the conflict in the Middle East sent global crude prices sharply higher .
Speaking at an emergency meeting on the impact of the Middle East crisis, Lee said the ÔÇőgovernment would "swiftly introduce and boldly implement" a maximum price system on petroleum products "that have recently seen excessive price ÔÇőincreases".
The current crisis "is a significant burden on our economy, which is highly dependent on global Ôüátrade and energy imports from the Middle East," Lee said in opening remarks.
He added that South Korea will ÔÇőalso look for sources of energy beyond supplies shipped via the Strait of Hormuz.
Presidential policy adviser Kim Yong-beom said after ÔÇőthe meeting that the industry ministry would proceed quickly so that the price capping system could be implemented as soon as this week.
The maximum price could be changed every two weeks, he said, noting South Korea had sufficient oil reserves to meet 208 days ÔÇőof consumption.
The amount of crude oil shipped to South Korea affected by the Strait of Hormuz blockade ÔÇőis around 1.7 million barrels per day, Kim said.
South Korea could secure 20 million barrels of crude stock it jointly stores with ÔÇîoil-producing Ôüácountries if it exercises the right to buy, Kim said, as well as divert overseas production by the state-run Korea National Oil Corp for domestic use.
Kim said 14% of natural gas due to be imported this year is from the Middle East, with about 5 million tons of Qatari gas imports expected to be disrupted. Domestic supplies are unlikely ÔÇőto be hit, however, since ÔÇőKorea Gas Corp and Ôüáother players could import from alternative sources, he said.
When asked if a supplementary budget might be drawn up in case the Middle East crisis is prolonged, Kim said: "If any ÔÇőadditional financial resources are needed... we have to seriously think about it."
Earlier on Monday, ÔÇőPresident Lee said a Ôüá100 trillion won ($66.94 billion) market stabilisation programme should be expanded if needed, and called on the government and the central bank to prepare additional measures to respond to the volatility of the financial and foreign exchange markets.
The benchmark KOSPI (.KS11) , opens new tab closed 6% Ôüálower on ÔÇőMonday, after falling as much as 9% to trigger circuit breakers ÔÇőfor a second time this month. The won weakened nearly 1% to trade near a psychological barrier of 1,500 per dollar, while the benchmark ÔÇőbond yield hit more than two-year highs.
($1 = 1,493.9000 won)

item.title: NATO drills for war in Arctic with focus on civilians being ready
item.description:
OSLO, March 9 (Reuters) - NATO began its biennial drills in the Arctic on Monday, this ÔÇőtime placing more emphasis on the role of civilians in supporting the military, at ÔÇîa time of high tension over U.S. President Trump's push to take over Greenland from fellow NATO member Denmark.
The drills, named Cold Response, focus on defending the alliance in the European Arctic, where NATO-members Norway and Finland share a border with ÔÇőRussia, and will run March 9-19 this time.
The exercise has now become part of Arctic Sentry , ÔÇőthe NATO mission to strengthen its presence in the polar region that was launched Ôüáto defuse tensions with Trump over Greenland.
Trump insists the U.S. needs Greenland to fend off threats from ÔÇőRussian and Chinese interests in the Arctic, and that Denmark cannot ensure its security. The governments of ÔÇőboth Denmark and Greenland say the island is not for sale.
This year, some 25,000 troops from some 14 nations - including the U.S. and Denmark - will take part in Cold Response, mostly across northern Norway and Finland.
The U.S. is expected to ÔÇőhave some 4,000 troops present.
Ahead of the exercise, the U.S. military withdrew one squadron of F-35 fighter ÔÇőjets from the exercise. It declined to say whether the war in the Middle East played a role in ÔÇîthe decision.
"The ÔüáU.S. military is a globally deployed force and it is not abnormal for forces to be dynamically re-tasked or reallocated for a host of reasons," a spokesperson for the U.S. Marine Corps Forces Europe told Reuters.
Norway has declared 2026 the year of "total defence", which puts emphasis on boosting the ÔÇőreadiness of civilians, businesses ÔÇőand public institutions to Ôüácope with war and other catastrophes, the latest move by a Nordic nation to boost civilian preparedness.
"We want our military to do its job of defending ÔÇőthe country. To do that, we are completely reliant on most aspects of ÔÇősociety functioning Ôüáas normal," Major-General Lars Lervik, head of the Norwegian Army, told Reuters.
"This is also an opportunity to rehearse specifically where civilians can give direct support to the military effort, for instance with the health service treating a Ôüáhigher ÔÇőnumber of injured soldiers, Norwegian or from allied forces, than usual."
On ÔÇőThursday, the military will run a scenario testing the ability of hospitals in northern Norway to treat a high number of ÔÇőcasualties being transported from an imaginary frontline in Finland.

item.title: Tehran threatens to confiscate property of Iranians abroad who back attacks on Iran
item.description:
DUBAI, March 9 (Reuters) - Iranians living abroad could have property confiscated and face other legal penalties if ÔÇőthey express support for the United States and ÔÇîIsrael, the Iranian prosecutor general's office said on Monday.
Some members of the Iranian diaspora who want political change ÔÇőin Tehran took to the streets of ÔÇőEuropean and American cities to celebrate the Ôüákilling of Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei ÔÇőin the U.S.-Israeli war against Iran. Iran on Monday named ÔÇőMojtaba Khamenei to succeed his father.
"A warning has been issued to those Iranians living abroad who in different ways ÔÇősympathise, support or cooperate with the American-Zionist (Israeli) ÔÇőenemy," the prosecutor general's office was quoted as saying by ÔÇîstate Ôüámedia.
"They will be met with the confiscation of all their properties and other legal penalties in accordance with the law."
Newly established channels on Telegram ÔÇőhave shared details ÔÇőof prominent ÔüáIranians living abroad who have posted comments critical of Iran's clerical authorities ÔÇőand supportive of the U.S.-Israeli military campaign ÔÇőthat Ôüábegan on February 28.
Up to 5 million Iranians live abroad, the majority of them in the United ÔüáStates ÔÇőand Western Europe, according to ÔÇőIranian government data. Iranian media put their numbers closer to ÔÇő10 million.

item.title: Guinea dissolves 40 political parties, opposition leader cries foul
item.description:
DAKAR, March 9 (Reuters) - Guinea's main opposition leader said "direct resistance" to the country's coup leader-turned-president was now the only way to bring about change ÔÇőafter the government consolidated its position by dissolving 40 political ÔÇîparties.
President Mamady Doumbouya, a former special forces commander, seized power in 2021 and won a seven-year term as president in December in a result contested by his opponents. Legislative elections ÔÇőare expected in May in the West African nation, which is ÔÇőrich in bauxite and iron ore.
Guinea's Ministry of Territorial Administration Ôüáand Decentralization said in a decree late on Friday that 40 parties' ÔÇőheadquarters and local offices would be closed and use of their logos, ÔÇőacronyms "and other distinctive signs" would be prohibited.
The decree said the parties had failed to meet legal obligations such as filing financial statements; several of the dissolved political parties have ÔÇőprotested, maintaining they had fulfilled all legal requirements.
In a video statement published ÔÇőon social media on Sunday, Guinea's main opposition leader Cellou Dalein Diallo said "war has been ÔÇîopenly Ôüádeclared" on Doumbouya's challengers.
He called on Guineans to engage in "direct resistance", without specifying what exactly that should look like. And he said political change would not happen through dialogue or democratic processes.
"The head of the junta and ÔÇőhis malevolent clique want ÔÇőto rewrite the Ôüácountry's history by erasing from the political landscape all forces likely to overshadow his nascent one-party state," he ÔÇősaid.
A government spokesperson did not respond to a request for ÔÇőcomment.
Diallo is Ôüáin exile outside the country, as is former President Alpha Conde, who Doumbouya toppled and whose party was also dissolved on Friday.
Guinea has a history Ôüáof political ÔÇőviolence, including during the 2020 election which ÔÇőDiallo said he had won. The December 2025 election was tightly controlled and unfolded without major ÔÇősecurity incidents.

item.title: At least 14 dead after migrant boat crashes into coast guard vessel off southern Turkey
item.description:
ANKARA, March 9 (Reuters) - At least 14 people were killed when ÔÇőan inflatable boat carrying migrants ÔÇîcrashed into a Turkish coast guard vessel off Turkey's southern ÔÇőprovince of Antalya on ÔÇőMonday, the coast guard said Ôüáin a statement.
The coast guard ÔÇősaid the high-speed boat was ÔÇődetected early on Monday off the coast of the Finike district ÔÇőof Antalya and that ÔÇőit tried to flee from the coast ÔÇîguard Ôüádespite repeated warnings to stop. It later collided with a coast guard vessel, the ÔÇőstatement ÔÇőadded.
It said Ôüásix migrants and one Turkish national were ÔÇőrescued, but that 14 ÔÇőwere Ôüáfound dead and 15 others were caught after continuing Ôüáin ÔÇőtheir boat until ÔÇőthey reached land.

item.title: EU can no longer rely on 'rules-based' system against threats, von der Leyen says
item.description:
BRUSSELS, March 9 (Reuters) - The EU must be prepared to project its power more assertively as ÔÇőit can no longer rely on a "rules-based" ÔÇîsystem against threats and must determine if its institutions and systems help or hinder its credibility, ÔÇőEuropean Commission President Ursula von der ÔÇőLeyen said on Monday.
"We will always defend Ôüáand uphold the rules-based system that we ÔÇőhelped to build with our allies, but ÔÇőwe can no longer rely on it as the only way to defend our interests or assume its ÔÇőrules will shelter us from the ÔÇőcomplex threats that we face," von der Leyen said ÔÇîat Ôüáa conference for EU ambassadors.
"We urgently need to reflect on whether our doctrine, our institutions and our decision making ÔÇô all designed in ÔÇőa postwar ÔÇőworld of Ôüástability and multilateralism ÔÇô have kept pace with the speed of change ÔÇőaround us. Whether the system that ÔÇőwe Ôüábuilt ÔÇô with all of its well-intentioned attempts at consensus and compromise ÔÇô is more a help Ôüáor ÔÇőa hindrance to our credibility ÔÇőas a geopolitical actor," she added.

item.title: Middle East conflict could spur palm oil demand from biodiesel sector
item.description:
MUMBAI/KUALA LUMPUR, March 9 (Reuters) - Rising crude oil prices and higher freight rates driven by the Middle East conflict could boost demand for palm oil ÔÇőfrom the biodiesel sector and for food use, as Asian buyers seek prompt ÔÇîshipments, industry officials told Reuters.
Indonesia and Malaysia's output rose to a record high in 2025, swelling stocks and weighing on prices. But the conflict has suddenly made palm oil attractive to the ÔÇőbiodiesel industry, pushing prices to their highest level in more than a year.
"Palm oil ÔÇőis now trading at a steep discount to gasoil, with the Ôüácurrent spread lucrative enough to boost demand from the biodiesel industry," said Anilkumar Bagani, ÔÇőresearch head of Mumbai-based vegetable oil broker Sunvin Group.
Oil prices surged more than 25% on Monday ÔÇőto their highest levels since mid-2022 as some major producers cut supplies and fears of prolonged shipping disruptions gripped the market.
Indonesia, the world's largest user of palm oil-based biodiesel, said it may revive plans ÔÇőto roll out a B50 grade of palm oil biodiesel mid-year to counter surging ÔÇőcrude oil prices.
In January, Jakarta shelved the plan to produce B50, an equal blend of palm ÔÇîoil biodiesel Ôüáand conventional diesel, citing technical and funding challenges and continued with the B40 mandate instead.
A long-term policy shift from countries such as Indonesia is likely only if palm oil trades at a consistent discount to gasoil over an extended period, said Bagani.
Used in ÔÇőeverything from cakes and ÔÇőfrying fats to Ôüácosmetics and cleaning products, palm oil makes up more than half of global vegetable oil shipments and is especially popular among ÔÇőconsumers in emerging markets, led by India.
Southeast Asia is well positioned ÔÇőto consistently Ôüásupply palm oil to buyers in Asia, the Middle East, and Europe, said Carl Bek-Nielsen, vice chairman and chief executive director at United Plantations Berhad.
Palm oil supplies remain ample and Ôüácan ÔÇőbe shipped quickly to Asian buyers, but it has ÔÇőnow become more expensive than rival soyoil, which could limit gains in demand, said a New Delhi-based dealer ÔÇőwith a global trade house.

item.title: Indonesia may revive B50 biodiesel mix plan as oil prices soar
item.description:
JAKARTA, March 9 (Reuters) - Indonesia may revive a plan to launch a mandatory B50 grade of palm oil-based biodiesel in the middle of this year because of ÔÇősurging crude oil prices due to the conflict in the ÔÇîMiddle East, deputy energy minister Yuliot Tanjung said.
No decision has been made yet by Indonesia, the world's largest palm oil producer, Yuliot added in comments sent to Reuters over the ÔÇőweekend.
In January, authorities scrapped a plan to launch B50 - a blend of ÔÇő50% palm oil-based biodiesel and 50% conventional diesel - this year Ôüádue to technical and funding concerns, instead sticking with a B40 blend.
In light ÔÇőof the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran, however, the government is now looking at ÔÇőtwo scenarios, Yuliot said.
"B50 might be implemented in the second semester or even earlier...But for now the steering committee's decision for B40 until the end of 2026 still stands," ÔÇőhe said, adding that authorities were monitoring price movements in real time.
The steering ÔÇőcommittee consists of several ministries which determine biodiesel policy, led by chief economic minister, Airlangga Hartarto.
Indonesia's ÔÇîbiodiesel Ôüámandate often affects global palm oil prices as increased domestic use reduces available exports.
Oil prices surged on Monday to above $100 per barrel . Palm oil prices also rallied on expectations that rising crude prices will boost demand for biodiesel feedstocks.
Yuliot ÔÇődid not respond to ÔÇőa request for Ôüáfurther comment on Monday. Energy minister Bahlil Lahadalia said the government may accelerate its biofuel programmes, which include B50 ÔÇőand a plan to require a certain blend of bioethanol ÔÇőwith gasoline, Ôüáaccording to local media reports.
Asked about potential implementation of B50 this year, Airlangga told Reuters that authorities were monitoring the impact of the conflict on biodiesel Ôüápolicy.
Finance minister ÔÇőPurbaya Yudhi Sadewa said Indonesia is prepared ÔÇőto raise the allocation for fuel subsidies to absorb the shock from rising global oil prices.

item.title: Norway police search for suspect after U.S. embassy blast
item.description:
OSLO, March 9 (Reuters) - Norwegian ÔÇőpolice are searching for ÔÇîa suspect seen on surveillance footage following an explosion ÔÇőduring the weekend ÔÇőat the U.S. embassy in ÔüáOslo, investigators said ÔÇőon Monday.
The early Sunday morning ÔÇőblast damaged one of the entrances to the embassy, sending ÔÇőthick smoke into ÔÇőthe street but causing no injuries, ÔÇîin Ôüáwhat police said may have been linked to the crisis in the Middle East.
"The ÔÇőinvestigation ÔÇőso Ôüáfar has shown that this was an ÔÇőimprovised explosive device (IED)," ÔÇőthe ÔüáOslo police said in a statement.
Norway's government has Ôüápromised ÔÇőa thorough investigation ÔÇőof the incident.

item.title: King Charles' trip to US should be cancelled over Trump comments, Lib Dem leader says
item.description:
LONDON, March 9 (Reuters) - A planned visit by King Charles to the United States should be cancelled after President Donald Trump repeatedly insulted Britain, the leader of the country's ÔÇőthird-biggest party in parliament said on Monday.
Trump has repeatedly criticised Prime Minister Keir ÔÇîStarmer after London initially blocked the U.S. from using British bases to attack Iran , saying he was "not Winston Churchill" and that Starmer had helped to "ruin" the so-called Special Relationship between the two countries.
That followed ÔÇőTrump's comments in January that European troops had stayed off the front lines ÔÇőin Afghanistan, drawing a rare rebuke from Starmer who described them Ôüáas " frankly appalling " and insulting to the families of the 457 British service personnel who ÔÇődied in that war.
Ed Davey, the leader of the Liberal Democrats, said Starmer should avoid ÔÇőputting the king, Britain's ceremonial head of state, in the centre of a "highly political situation" with an April visit.
"A state visit from our king would be seen as yet another huge diplomatic coup ÔÇőfor President Trump, so it should not be given to someone who repeatedly insults ÔÇőand damages our country," Davey told LBC Radio, citing Trump's recent comments and his "reckless" war in Iran.
In ÔÇîBritain, Ôüáthe government advises the monarch on his relations with foreign leaders. The Starmer government has not been slow to harness the soft power of the royal family to help smooth relations with Trump over the last year.
Charles hosted Trump for an unprecedented second state visit to ÔÇőBritain last September, treating ÔÇőhim to a Ôüácarriage ride and state banquet at Windsor Castle.
Heir-to-the-throne Prince William is also due to visit the U.S. in the summer during the ÔÇősoccer World Cup.
Davey said that while it was clear Trump ÔÇőliked the Ôüároyal family, he did not appear to treat Britain any better as a result, referring to his tariff policy.
Asked about Davey's remarks, senior minister Steve Reed said the king did a Ôüáremarkable ÔÇőjob of representing Britain abroad and it was not ÔÇőfor him or Davey to comment on the arrangements.
Buckingham Palace does not comment on official trips before they ÔÇőhave been confirmed.

item.title: Hezbollah reports Israeli raid in east Lebanon as war enters second week
item.description:
BEIRUT/JERUSALEM, March 9 (Reuters) - Hezbollah said on Monday its fighters clashed with Israeli troops in eastern Lebanon during an Israeli airborne raid overnight, ÔÇőin what would be the second such operation in the area in recent days, as the war between ÔÇîIsrael and the Iran-backed group entered a second week.
A spokesperson for the Israeli military had no immediate comment when asked about the Hezbollah statement.
A Hezbollah statement said its fighters "confronted the helicopters and the infiltrating forces with appropriate weapons".
Lebanon was pulled into the regional war when Hezbollah, founded by Iran's ÔÇőRevolutionary Guards in 1982, opened fire to avenge the killing of Iran's former supreme leader, igniting a new Israeli ÔÇőoffensive against the group.
The Israeli military kept up its airstrikes in the Hezbollah-controlled southern suburbs of ÔüáBeirut on Monday, sending plumes of smoke across the city. It reiterated warnings to its residents to leave, and announced ÔÇőit would act against a Hezbollah financial institution, Al-Qard Al-Hassan.
Nearly 400 people have been killed in Lebanon by Israeli strikes since ÔÇőMarch 2, Lebanon's health ministry reported on Sunday, including at least 83 children and 42 women. The toll does not otherwise distinguish between combatants and civilians.
Israel's military announced on Sunday that two Israeli soldiers had been killed in southern Lebanon, the first casualties in the Israeli army since ÔÇőthe war began.
In its statement, Hezbollah said its fighters had observed 15 helicopters flying over eastern Lebanon just after midnight, ÔÇődropping Israeli troops who were observed approaching Lebanese territory from an area on the Syrian side of the border.
Lebanon's eastern Bekaa Valley region ÔÇîis one Ôüáof the Shi'ite Muslim group's political and security strongholds.
The Israeli military carried out an airborne raid in the same area, near the village of Nabi Chit, overnight Friday-Saturday. Lebanon's health ministry said 41 people were killed in Israeli attacks in the Nabi Chit area on Saturday.
The Israeli military said that raid was an operation to seek the remains of Ron Arad, an Israeli ÔÇőair force navigator missing in ÔÇőLebanon since 1986 but no Ôüáfindings related to him were recovered.
Hundreds of thousands of people in Lebanon have fled their homes since the start of the war, pouring out of the predominantly Shi'ite Muslim southern Lebanon, ÔÇőand the southern suburbs known as Dahiyeh.
Israel has also struck outside those areas.
On Sunday, an ÔÇőIsraeli drone strike hit Ôüáa hotel in Beirut's seafront Rouche district. The Israeli military said the strike killed five senior commanders of what it described as the Lebanon Corps of Iran's Revolutionary Guards Quds Force.
The Israeli military has sent more troops into southern Lebanon since the start of Ôüáthe war, ÔÇőestablishing what it described as forward defensive positions to guard against Hezbollah attacks ÔÇőinto Israel.
Israel had kept soldiers at five positions in southern Lebanon since a war with Hezbollah in 2024.

item.title: Prosecutors expand Swiss ski bar fire probe to include mayor, document shows
item.description:
GENEVA, March 9 (Reuters) - Swiss prosecutors have extended their investigation into a ÔÇîfatal ski bar fire that killed 41 people, mostly teenagers, and added the mayor of the ski town of Crans-Montana to the list of suspects, a document showed.
Mayor Nicolas ÔÇőFeraud is named as a defendant in one of the prosecutors' ÔÇődocuments seen by Reuters summoning him for questioning on April Ôüá13. He did not immediately respond to a request for comment.
Feraud, in ÔÇőhis 50s, has previously admitted that the municipality had missed multiple annual safety checks. "We ÔÇőare deeply sorry. We had no indication that the checks had not been done as requested," Feraud told reporters in January.
The Swiss prosecutor's office in the Valais canton confirmed ÔÇőto Reuters that new individuals had been placed under investigation in the ÔÇőcase involving suspected crimes including negligent homicide, without naming them.
The fire that tore through "Le Constellation" ÔÇîbar Ôüáon January 1 was one of the worst disasters in modern Swiss history and has tested relations with neighbouring Italy, which lost six of its nationals in the blaze.
Many young people remain hospitalised with burn injuries.
The tragedy has also sent ÔÇőchills through the lucrative ÔÇőtourism sector. ÔüáThe affluent town of Crans-Montana is popular among French, Italian and American tourists and well known both for its ÔÇősunny, south-facing ski slopes and its golf courses.
Prosecutors' inquiries initially ÔÇőfocused on Ôüáthe French bar owners who remain under investigation. The probe was expanded in late January to also include a current and a former local official.
Bar owners ÔüáJacques ÔÇőand Jessica Moretti have both voiced grief over ÔÇőthe fire and said they would cooperate with the probe.
If convicted, defendants could face a maximum ÔÇőpenalty of 4-1/2 years in prison.

item.title: Amid wave of kidsÔÇÖ online safety laws, age-checking tech comes of age
item.description:
NEW YORK/STOCKHOLM/SYDNEY, March 9 (Reuters) - For years, tech companies successfully resisted pressure from child safety advocates to do more to keep kids off their services, claiming technical limitations would make any attempt to restrict access for teens impractical, overly broad or a security risk.
Now, a growing list of governments is concluding those hurdles are not insurmountable, and pushing ahead with aggressive new age-checking requirements for social networks, AI chatbots ÔÇőand porn purveyors alike.
Three months after Australia launched a landmark ban on teen social media accounts, regulators across Europe , in Brazil and in a handful of U.S. states are moving to emulate it . California Governor Gavin Newsom - seen as a likely Democratic candidate for president in 2028 - joined the ÔÇîcall , opens new tab last month, while Republican President Donald Trump is also reportedly ÔÇťtaking an interestÔÇŁ , opens new tab in age limits, according to his daughter-in-law.
Spurring them along are escalating concerns over online abuse and teen mental health , and a recent outcry over the spread of AI-generated child sexual images , as well as increased confidence in the capabilities of "age assurance" software that backers say can suss out a person's approximate age using facial analysis, parental approval, ID checks and other digital clues.
Recent advancements in artificial intelligence have boosted the effectiveness and slashed the cost of those age-gating tools, according to Reuters interviews with more than a dozen regulators, child safety advocates, independent researchers and vendors who perform the age checks for big tech companies, including TikTok, Facebook owner Meta (META.O) , opens new tab and OpenAI.
ÔÇťThe age-assurance market ÔÇőhas matured a lot in the last couple of years,ÔÇŁ said Ariel Fox Johnson, a senior adviser to San Francisco-based Common Sense Media, a childrenÔÇÖs online advocacy group. She pointed to improving technology, as well as the establishment of trade groups, technical protocols and certification schemes standardizing evaluation ÔÇőof the various toolsÔÇÖ effectiveness.
Social media companies now can often confidently guess a personÔÇÖs age group using digital breadcrumbs like the year an account was established or the type of content it views, they said, while Ôüáa burgeoning industry of age assurance vendors like Yoti, k-ID and Persona offer additional layers of checks via automated tools like face scans and machine-based analysis of government IDs.
At the app-store level, too, Apple and AlphabetÔÇÖs (GOOGL.O) , opens new tab Google have rolled out tools that allow parents to indicate their childÔÇÖs age range to app developers.
ÔÇťThe tech ÔÇődefinitely has gotten better, not just for age verification specifically but for overall identity verification,ÔÇŁ said Merritt Maxim, a vice president at Massachusetts-based research firm Forrester.
