ÔÇťThat, in turn, has driven down the average cost of verification, so that where you were using it five years ago only for higher-value types of transactions, now you ÔÇőcan use it for pretty much anything without a significant financial impact.ÔÇŁ
Vendors generally charge well under $1 per check for basic machine-only age assurance tools, though for large volumes the price is often as low as single-digit cents, said industry executives. More costly traditional processes like human confirmation and triangulation of personal data that were standard a decade ago are still available at a premium, but are needed less frequently, the executives said.
Independent evaluations back up executives' descriptions of rapid progress. According to an ongoing study run by the U.S. National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST), face-scanning software from firms including Yoti ÔÇô which performs checks for TikTok and MetaÔÇÖs (META.O) , opens new tab Facebook, Instagram and Threads ÔÇô were off in their age estimations by an average of ÔÇő4.1 years as of initial testing in 2014, while by 2024 that average had dropped to 3.1 years, and is currently 2.5 years.
UK-based Yoti said the performance of its latest face analysis model due out in April surpasses that of models it submitted for the NIST and Australian studies, with an ÔÇőaverage error of only 1.04 years for kids in regulatorsÔÇÖ target age range of 14 to 18. Persona, a San Francisco-based identity verification firm used by OpenAI and Reddit (RDDT.N) , opens new tab , touts a similar average error of 1.77 years for the 13-to-17-year-old age range.
A report commissioned by the Australian government likewise determined last year that photo-based age estimation products were ÔÇîbroadly accurate, although it Ôüáacknowledged that users within three years of the lawÔÇÖs age cutoff of 16 were in a ÔÇťgrey zone where system uncertainty is higherÔÇŁ and recommended they be diverted to ÔÇťsupplementary assurance methods, such as ID-based verification or parental consent."
The systems also struggle more with certain skin types, with grainier imagery captured by older phones and when using privacy-protective ÔÇťon-deviceÔÇŁ data processing, which entails performing a check entirely on the person's phone without sending their data out to a cloud server, executives said.
For instance, systems using on-device processing are less likely to catch attempts by enterprising youngsters to appear older than they are, said Rick Song, CEO of San Francisco-based Persona. Common tricks used by teens include donning masks, applying heavy makeup or fake facial hair, or scanning the plastic faces of action figures instead of their own, he said.
Still, said executives, facial age estimation can provide a digital version of the kind of screening performed daily at bars and liquor stores in the offline world.
"If you look ÔÇőyoung, you can be challenged, and you may have to provide your ÔÇőID," said Robin Tombs, CEO of London-based Yoti.
He added that social media Ôüáservices generally require fewer face scans and ID checks than porn or gambling sites because they already have reams of personal information on their users. This means they can lean more on an age assurance method called ÔÇťinferenceÔÇŁ ÔÇö involving analysis of online activities, connected financial information and other signals ÔÇö to satisfy regulatorsÔÇÖ requirements.
The 10 social media companies included in AustraliaÔÇÖs teen ban all declined Reuters requests for data on the effectiveness of their age assurance tools.
AustraliaÔÇÖs internet regulator, the ÔÇőeSafety commissioner, has said it will collect population data for two years to assess the ban's impact and publish first results later this year. Already, companies have locked 4.7 million suspected underage accounts since the law came ÔÇőinto effect in December, it said, although industry Ôüáparticipants have told Reuters that some of the accounts were likely underage Google accounts that were prevented from logging in to YouTube, regardless of whether they were active.
Meta said it took down about 550,000 Instagram, Facebook and Threads accounts suspected to be underage in the first weeks of the Australian ban. Snapchat (SNAP.N) , opens new tab said it took down about 415,000.
Regulators elsewhere are watching carefully. European Commission President Ursula von der Leyen is set to discuss age verification during an upcoming visit to Canberra, according to a European lawmaker briefed on her agenda. The United Kingdom, which requires age verification for porn websites and is considering tightening child safety rules for social media and AI chatbots as Ôüáwell, is likewise swapping ÔÇőnotes with Australian counterparts.
Early results from the Australian experiment should be taken with a grain of salt, as companies affected by the ban generally were doing the bare minimum to comply with ÔÇőlegal requirements, said Iain Corby, the executive director of the Age Verification Providers Association, a trade association that represents about three dozen vendors including Yoti and Persona.
In some cases, he added, the social media companies asked AVPA member firms to turn off controls that make the age checks more robust.
ÔÇťThey are extremely worried this is going to be contagious and be a policy that is ÔÇőadopted around the world, so they are not really motivated for it to be a glowing success,ÔÇŁ he said.
ÔÇťThey are testing the regulatorÔÇÖs patience to see what they can get away with.ÔÇŁ

item.title: Republicans confront cooling support from young men over Trump policies
item.description:
MANCHESTER, New Hampshire, March 9 (Reuters) - In a college library lined with volumes on America's greatest presidents, six young men who voted for Donald Trump gathered to size up his second term. Their verdicts ranged from guarded approval to disappointment, reflecting growing tension in a demographic that helped propel Trump to victory in 2024.
While the students form a very small sample ÔÇősize, their mixed reviews - including criticism of what several called overly harsh immigration enforcement and frustration over rising prices - mirror a broader shift in national polling that shows the Republican president is losing ground with young men.
Public opinion polling suggests that this ÔÇîsoftening of support, part of a broader unhappiness among Americans over Trump's policies, threatens Republicans' hopes of retaining their slim majorities in the Senate and House of Representatives.
Analysts who track the youth vote told Reuters many young men report feeling little tangible improvement in their economic prospects since Trump returned to office in January 2025.
Sitting with five classmates in the political library at Saint Anselm College in New Hampshire, 20-year-old sophomore Tyler Witzgall captured a sentiment common among his peers: The president, for all his accomplishments, has fallen short on issues shaping their daily lives, like high prices.
"I'm still going to graduate and be in an enormous amount of debt. I wonÔÇÖt be able to buy a home for a ÔÇőwhile," Witzgall said, grading Trump a C or C-plus, describing his disappointment in the president's efforts to tackle domestic economic issues.
None of the students said they regretted their vote, viewing the 2024 Democratic presidential nominee, then Vice President Kamala Harris, as a continuation of a ÔÇőpresidency they associated with unchecked immigration and spiraling costs.
Yet even solid Trump backers like Ian Pomfret, a 20-year-old sophomore, joined others in the student panel criticizing what he describes as overly aggressive tactics used by Immigration and ÔüáCustoms Enforcement agents implementing Trump's crackdown.
"The ICE thing is a huge problem," Pomfret said, pointing to the two U.S. citizens killed by federal agents in Minneapolis. "I feel like there is a better way of going about it than raiding and killing and instant deportation."
Pomfret still broadly supports Trump, grading him a ÔÇőB-plus.
Last month, some 33% of men aged 18-29 approved of Trump's performance in the White House, down from 43% in February 2025, according to Reuters/Ipsos polling. Trump garnered the support of 46% of young males in the 2024 presidential election, up 7 points from 2020, according to exit polling analyzed by ÔÇőPew Research.
A party strategist who works on House races pointed to a CBS News survey in February showing that 43% of young voters view Republican positions as "mainstream", and said the party could still secure midterm wins if it captures at least 40% of the youth vote.
Turnout among young voters is historically low in midterm elections, with only a quarter casting a ballot in 2022, half the overall rate.
Still, young voters can tip close races, as in 2018 when a wave of mass shootings spurred youth turnout and helped flip more than a dozen congressional seats to the Democrats, according to John Della Volpe, a pollster who specializes in the Gen Z vote.
"All evidence in the last 15 ÔÇőor 16 months is that this cohort cannot be counted on at this stage to be reliable Republican voters," Della Volpe said. "Mostly because they haven't felt tangible improvements in their day-to-day lives."
TrumpÔÇÖs pledges in his 2024 campaign to curb inflation, boost economic growth, and toughen immigration ÔÇőenforcement helped attract young males to his MAGA movement. A string of attention-grabbing moves, like attending a sneaker event, sitting down with popular podcasters like Joe Rogan and Theo Von and posting content on TikTok, also enhanced his standing with younger men, political analysts said.
Last month, the Republican National Committee, the party's governing body, launched ÔÇîits own account on ÔüáTikTok, which is used by six in 10 Americans under 30, according to Pew Research.
White House spokesman Davis Ingle said no other president has done more for young men than Trump "who is working tirelessly to create jobs, cool inflation, increase housing affordability, and more."
John Brabender, a media consultant who helped the Trump campaign target young men in 2024, said without Trump on the ballot in November, Republican candidates will need to do a better job of talking about tax cuts and other policies that directly benefit someone in their 20s, rather than broad indicators like how the stock market is performing.
"I will be the first to say that collectively we've done a poor job of ... communicating directly to the hearts and minds of people at a much younger age," Brabender said.
Brabender's comments echo concerns being expressed by Republican lawmakers, party strategists and White House aides who say the president needs to give more priority to ÔÇőhis economic messaging and less to foreign policy. The economy is the top ÔÇőissue for voters, polls show.
The battle Ôüáfor the attention of young voters can be seen in the race for an open Senate seat in New Hampshire, one of four competitive seats in the chamber that Democrats must defend in November.
U.S. Representative Chris Pappas, the Democratic frontrunner, launched a TikTok account in October and has been working to build a following, with several posts addressing the high cost of housing, a key concern for young voters.
"If you ask someone in their 20s about owning ÔÇőa home, that seems like a far-off notion," Pappas said in an interview. "We've got to make that dream within reach of more people."
The Pappas campaign plans to host a series of events with online influencers to ÔÇőhelp engage with younger voters.
Former Senator Scott ÔüáBrown, who is seeking the Republican nomination, told Reuters that his own hobbies - including playing guitar in a rock band and coaching high school basketball - help him connect with younger voters.
"ItÔÇÖs not a stretch for me to walk in and start playing hoops with somebody or get up on stage and jam with some teenagers," said Brown, adding that two staffers, ages 17 and 26, were "social media whizzes" and helping build his presence online.
Brown will speak this month at the New Hampshire Institute of Politics at Saint Anselm, a hub for political discussion in the early presidential primary state.
Students pass through the institute each day for classes, walking hallways Ôüádecorated with photographs of ÔÇőpresidents who have visited and past rooms filled with more than half a century of political memorabilia, much of it from an era of more convivial bipartisanship.
Several of the students ÔÇőinterviewed by Reuters said they longed for a return to more civil discourse. While backing many of Trump's policies, they disliked his caustic style, in which he casts Democrats or other critics as the enemy. All said they were still likely to vote Republican in November.
Tyler Delaney, a 19-year-old freshman, said the divisiveness of Trump's approach will come back to bite him ÔÇőif the Democrats, as expected, take control of the House.
"It gets to a point where you need to have some bipartisanship, and I think that's gonna hit Trump hard after the midterms."

item.title: EU to sign defence partnerships with Australia, Iceland and Ghana, Kallas says
item.description:
BRUSSELS, March 9 (Reuters) - The EU will ÔÇősign defence partnerships ÔÇîwith Australia, Iceland and Ghana in ÔÇőthe coming ÔÇődays, EU foreign Ôüápolicy chief Kaja ÔÇőKallas said ÔÇőon Monday.
"There are many other interested countries ÔÇőknocking at ÔÇőour door," Kallas added ÔÇîin Ôüáa speech in Brussels.
"A growing number of countries ÔÇőaround ÔÇőthe Ôüáglobe are seeking to ÔÇődiversify their ÔÇőpartnerships Ôüáto manage the heightened risk."

item.title: UK talking to partners about limiting economic impact of Iran crisis, says PM
item.description:
LONDON, March 9 (Reuters) - The British government is talking to international partners, and its central bank, to assess ÔÇőways to limit the economic damage from the escalating Iranian ÔÇîcrisis, warning that the longer it goes on, the worse it could get.
British borrowing costs have soared since the conflict erupted more than a week ago - by more than those of ÔÇőother European countries and the United States - as investors fear that ÔÇősurging oil and gas prices will stoke already stubborn inflation Ôüáhigher.
That also risks forcing the government to intervene again to cushion the economic ÔÇőblow, a potential huge challenge to an already unpopular government which has limited ÔÇőroom to increase spending.
Prime Minister Keir Starmer told an event in London on Monday that he wanted people to know that the government was working to deal with the ÔÇőfallout from the Iranian war.
"What we're doing is monitoring the risk, working with ÔÇőothers to mitigate the risk," he said.
"The chancellor is talking with the Bank of England ÔÇîevery Ôüáday to make sure that we're ahead of that, on energy prices for households."
He also said the government was talking to international partners about how to reduce the likely impact on people in Britain, and said Britain wanted ÔÇőto seek a way ÔÇőto de-escalate the Ôüáconflict.
Starmer said that he was acutely aware that people would be worried about their bills given the rising oil ÔÇőand gas price, but he noted that for consumers, an ÔÇőexisting energy Ôüáprice cap was in place until June.
He also said the economy was more resilient than the last energy shock, when Russia launched its full scale invasion Ôüáof Ukraine.
"People ÔÇőwill sense, you will sense, the longer ÔÇőthis goes on, the more likely the potential for an impact on our economy," he said.

item.title: Saudi Aramco reducing output at two oilfields, two sources say
item.description:
March 9 (Reuters) - Saudi oil giant Aramco has begun cutting ÔÇőoutput at two of ÔÇîits oilfields, two sources said on Monday, after ÔÇőthe vital Strait of ÔÇőHormuz was choked by Ôüáthe U.S.-Israeli war on Iran ÔÇőand subsequent attacks on ÔÇőthe waterway.
It was not immediately clear at which fields and ÔÇőby how much ÔÇőproduction was being curtailed. Aramco, which ÔÇîhas Ôüábeen rerouting some of its crude cargoes to the Red Sea port ÔÇőof ÔÇőYanbu, Ôüádid not immediately respond to an ÔÇőemailed request for ÔÇőcomment.
Several Ôüáof Saudi Arabia's neighbours, which have also been Ôüásubject ÔÇőto attacks, have ÔÇőalso cut production.

item.title: China makes biggest retail fuel price cap increase in four years amid Iran war
item.description:
BEIJING, March 9 (Reuters) - China raised regulated ceiling prices for retail gasoline and diesel in the sharpest ÔÇőincrease since March 2022 on Monday, following a surge in ÔÇîinternational oil prices due to the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran .
Gasoline and diesel retail price caps in the world's second-largest oil consumer will increase by 695 yuan ($100.46) and ÔÇő670 yuan ($96.84) per metric ton, respectively, from Tuesday, according to ÔÇőa notice from the National Development and Reform Commission. ÔüáThe previous adjustment was made on February 24.
International benchmark Brent crude ÔÇőfutures had climbed 27%, and West Texas Intermediate crude (WTI) futures were up ÔÇőby 35.6% last week.
China last week asked refiners to halt fuel exports and try to cancel shipments already committed as the Iran war curbed refinery output, several ÔÇőpeople with knowledge of the matter said on Thursday.
China's state planner ÔÇőreviews retail gasoline and diesel prices every 10 working days and applies uniform adjustments ÔÇînationwide, Ôüáthough benchmark prices vary by region.
The adjustment rate reflects changes in international crude oil prices while also taking into account average processing costs, taxes, distribution expenses and appropriate profit margins.
China allows retail gasoline and ÔÇődiesel prices to ÔÇőfloat freely between Ôüáfloors and ceilings. However, when international crude prices reach $130 per barrel, retail fuel prices are generally not ÔÇőraised or are raised only minimally. When crude prices ÔÇőfall to $40 Ôüáper barrel or below, retail fuel prices are calculated as if crude were priced at $40, with normal processing margins, according to the price Ôüáadjustment mechanism.
($1 = ÔÇő6.9185 Chinese yuan renminbi)

item.title: Lebanon parliament extends its own mandate for two years as war intensifies
item.description:
DUBAI, March 9 (Reuters) - LebanonÔÇÖs parliament extended its own mandate for two years on ÔÇőMonday, a statement from the speaker's ÔÇîoffice said, pushing back elections that were meant to take place in May of ÔÇőthis year.
The office of parliamentary speaker ÔÇőNabih Berri said 76 lawmakers of Ôüáthe 128-seat body had voted in ÔÇőfavour of the extension. The vote ÔÇőcame a week after Lebanon was dragged into the regional war by militant group Hezbollah's ÔÇőlaunch of rockets and drones into ÔÇőIsrael, which has responded with heavy bombardment across ÔÇîthe Ôüácountry.
Lebanon last held parliamentary elections in 2022 and had been considering an extension of the current body's mandate before ÔÇőthe latest ÔÇőround of Ôüáfighting erupted between Hezbollah and Israel.
Lawmakers have taken similar measures ÔÇőin the past. The parliament ÔÇőelected Ôüáin 2009 voted to extend its own mandate until 2017 , citing security concerns linked Ôüáto ÔÇőthe war that was ÔÇőthen raging in neighbouring Syria.

item.title: Morning Bid: Oil's triple-digit trouble
item.description:
March 9 - What matters in U.S. and global markets today
By Mike Dolan , opens new tab , Editor-At-Large, Finance and Markets
ÔÇśOil shockÔÇÖ and ÔÇśstagflationÔÇÖ are overused phrases in financial markets, often pulled out as a scare tactic when mapping out worst-case scenarios. But both are now very ÔÇőmuch in play with energy markets in turmoil as the Middle East war enters its second week with no end in sight.
Crude oil ÔÇîprices soared above $100 per barrel on Monday to their highest since just after the Ukraine invasion four years ago. If triple-digit oil prices are more than a short-term phenomenon, the U.S. and global economy could be in for some real pain - and central bankers may find themselves facing their worst nightmare: stagflation.
IÔÇÖll get into that and more below.
But first, for more on the market impact ÔÇőof the eye-popping oil surge, listen to today's episode of Morning Bid .
The energy crisis set off by the war in Iran is escalating. U.S. ÔÇőfuel prices have surged well above $3 per gallon, and theyÔÇÖre set to rise further. President Donald Trump wrote on social media overnight Ôüáthat rising oil prices were ÔÇťa very small price to payÔÇŁ for winning the war, though many voters may question that.
The worrying thing for central banks and investors ÔÇőis that this latest inflationary spur comes after FridayÔÇÖs surprisingly weak U.S. employment report. The data may have been skewed by bad weather in February, but there were few ÔÇőpositive points and many signs that the labor market has stalled.
This raises the dark prospect of ÔÇśstagflationÔÇÖ - slow or no growth coupled with mounting price rises. How the Fed and other central banks react to that is very much an open question. Most likely, they will just stand pat for longer, which is apt to please almost no one.
The renewed inflation concerns have rattled global bond markets , ÔÇőintensifying last week's selloff and sending yields higher - especially in Britain, where two-year gilt yields are on track for their biggest one-day rise since 2022.
Meantime, stock markets tumbled around ÔÇőthe world , with JapanÔÇÖs Nikkei losing over 5% and South KoreaÔÇÖs high-flying KOSPI shedding nearly 6%. That comes on top of losses last week of 5.5% and more than 10% for ÔÇîthose indexes, Ôüárespectively.
U.S. stock futures are down more than 1% before the bell and - much like last week - the greenback climbed as investors sought safety in the liquidity of dollar cash. Gold, usually the most reliable safe haven, once again failed to rise to the occasion , weighed by the stronger dollar and climbing Treasury yields.
Markets will now look to see if countries begin tapping their national oil reserves to stem the bleeding, with reports on Monday suggesting G7 finance ministers would discuss a joint release from emergency reserves.
Meantime, U.S. Senate ÔÇőDemocratic Leader Chuck Schumer called on President Trump ÔÇőto release oil from the U.S. ÔüáStrategic Petroleum Reserve, a move the president has so far not supported.
How far or for how long any of that can alleviate the situation remains to be seen as the crisis itself deepens, with Gulf states continuing to cut output amid persistent threats ÔÇőto shipping through the vital Strait of Hormuz.
Meantime, Tehran appears to be doubling down as it appoints hardliner Mojtaba Khamenei, ÔÇőson of Ali Khamenei, Ôüáas IranÔÇÖs new supreme leader.
Chart of the day
The jump in fuel prices since the start of the U.S.-Israel war with Iran could prove a political headache for the Republican Party ahead of November's midterm elections.
Some of the steepest rises have been in parts of the Midwest and the South, including swing states that supported Donald Trump in the 2024 Ôüápresidential election.
Today's events ÔÇőto watch
* U.S. 3-month and 6-month bill auctions
Want to receive the Morning Bid in your inbox ÔÇőevery weekday morning? Sign up for the newsletter here . You can find ROI on the Reuters website , opens new tab , and you can follow us on LinkedIn , opens new tab and X , opens new tab .
Opinions expressed are those of the author. They do not reflect the views of ÔÇőReuters News, which, under the Trust Principles , opens new tab , is committed to integrity, independence, and freedom from bias.

item.title: Hungary asked EU to suspend sanctions on Russian energy, PM says
item.description:
BUDAPEST, March 9 (Reuters) - Hungary's Prime Minister Viktor ÔÇőOrban said on ÔÇîMonday that he asked European Commission President ÔÇőUrsula von ÔÇőder Leyen in a Ôüáletter to suspend ÔÇőall sanctions on ÔÇőRussian energy, as that was needed to stop ÔÇőa rise ÔÇőin crude oil prices in ÔÇîHungary.
Orban Ôüáalso said in a video posted on his Facebook ÔÇőpage ÔÇőthat Ôüáhe called a government meeting ÔÇőfor later ÔÇőin Ôüáthe day to "prevent the price of Ôüádiesel ÔÇőand gasoline ÔÇőfrom rising to unbearable ÔÇőlevels."

item.title: France will send two warships to Red Sea, Macron says
item.description:
PAPHOS, Cyprus, March 9 (Reuters) - France will send ÔÇőtwo frigates as ÔÇîpart of the European Union's naval mission ÔÇőAspides in ÔÇőthe Red Sea, French ÔüáPresident Emmanuel Macron ÔÇősaid on Monday.
"We ÔÇőare in the process of setting up a ÔÇőpurely defensive, ÔÇőpurely escort mission, which must ÔÇîbe Ôüáprepared together with both European and non-European states," Macron ÔÇősaid ÔÇőafter Ôüámeeting with Cypriot President Nikos ÔÇőChristodoulides and ÔÇőGreek ÔüáPrime Minister Kyriakos Mitsotakis in Cyprus.

item.title: Kuwait mourns two officials killed as Iran war rages
item.description:
March 9 (Reuters) - Kuwait held a military funeral on Monday for two interior ministry officials ÔÇőit said were killed "while performing their ÔÇîduty".
The state - which hosts U.S. military installations and has come under Iranian drone and missile fire ÔÇőduring Tehran's war with Israel and ÔÇőthe U.S. - said the two men died Ôüáearly on Sunday, but did not ÔÇőgo into further details.
Kuwait's army separately said ÔÇőareas near the international airport were hit on Sunday and its forces had intercepted missiles and ÔÇődrones.
The Interior Ministry said on Sunday ÔÇőit mourned "the martyrs of duty, Lieutenant Colonel Staff Abdullah ÔÇîEmad ÔüáAl-Sharrah and Major Fahad Abdulaziz Al-Mejmed, from the General Administration of Land Border Security, who were martyred at dawn".
The funeral ÔÇőin Sulaibikhat ÔÇőCemetery was Ôüáled by First Deputy Prime Minister and Interior Minister Sheikh ÔÇőFahad Al-Yousef Al-Sabah, along with other ÔÇősenior Ôüáfigures.
Authorities there say five people have been killed since the start of the ÔüáIran ÔÇőcrisis, including four military personnel ÔÇőand a young girl, and dozens have been wounded.

item.title: Germany's Merz rules out cooperation with far-right AfD after state election
item.description:
BERLIN, March 9 (Reuters) - German Chancellor Friedrich ÔÇőMerz repeated on ÔÇîMonday his conservatives would not ÔÇőcooperate with ÔÇőthe far-right Alternative for ÔüáGermany party ÔÇőfollowing the close ÔÇőresult in the state election in ÔÇőthe southern ÔÇőregion of Baden Wuerttemberg.
He ÔÇîsaid Ôüáthe election, in which the environmental Greens narrowly ÔÇőbeat ÔÇőMerz's Ôüáconservative Christian Democrats, would ÔÇőnot affect ÔÇőthe Ôüáwork of the coalition Ôüágovernment ÔÇőin Berlin.

item.title: Ukraine received 11 requests on countering Iran's Shahed drones, Zelenskiy says
item.description:
KYIV, March 9 (Reuters) - President Volodymyr Zelenskiy said on ÔÇőMonday that Ukraine ÔÇîhad received 11 requests from countries neighboring Iran ÔÇőas well as ÔÇőEuropean states and the ÔüáUnited States for ÔÇődrone interceptors, electronic warfare ÔÇősystems and training.
"We also reviewed in detail requests ÔÇőfrom the states ÔÇőfor security support from our ÔÇîside Ôüáin countering 'Shahed' drones and other similar challenges," Zelenskiy said on ÔÇőthe Telegram ÔÇőapp Ôüáafter meeting his top military ÔÇőand government officials.
"Some ÔÇőrequests Ôüáhave already been met with concrete decisions Ôüáand ÔÇőspecific support."

item.title: Turkey says second Iranian ballistic missile shot down by NATO defences in airspace
item.description:
ANKARA, March 9 (Reuters) - Turkey said on Monday that NATO ÔÇîair defences shot down a second ballistic missile that was fired from Iran and had entered Turkish airspace, warning it will take any necessary steps against threats.
This marks the second Iranian ballistic missile in the last week that has targeted the south of Turkey, ÔÇőwhich is a NATO member and Iran's neighbour. Ankara had warned Iran against attacking again on Saturday, but has ÔÇőnot suggested it wants to formally call on alliance members for further protection.
"We once Ôüáagain emphasise that all necessary measures will be taken decisively and without hesitation against any threat directed at ÔÇőour country's territory and airspace," the Turkish Defence Ministry said, adding some ammunition parts had fallen in the ÔÇősoutheastern province of Gaziantep but there had been no casualties.
"We also reiterate that it is in everyone's interest to heed Turkey's warnings in this regard," it said.
It was unclear where the missile was headed before it was intercepted by NATO defences stationed in ÔÇőthe eastern Mediterranean Sea.
U.S. air forces are stationed at the Incirlik base in southern Turkey, and there is ÔÇőa NATO radar base in Malatya province to the northeast that provides vital protection for the alliance. Ankara said fragments ÔÇîfrom the Ôüámissile fell in empty fields in Gaziantep, which sits roughly between the two.
Burhanettin Duran, President Tayyip Erdogan's communications director, said Ankara was strongly reiterating its warning to all parties, namely Iran, to avoid steps that endanger regional stability and civilians.
Ankara says that Washington has not used Incirlik in its air assault, alongside Israel, on Iran, ÔÇőwhich triggered Tehran's missile and ÔÇődrone attacks.
Iran did not Ôüáimmediately comment on the incident, but it has said repeatedly that it is not at war with regional countries and is not explicitly targeting Turkey.
Turkey, which had sought to ÔÇőmediate U.S.-Iran talks before the air war that began last week, has previously ÔÇősaid it had Ôüáno intention currently of invoking NATO's Article 4 that would call allies to consult if a member is threatened.
That could lead to invoking the alliance's Article 5, which would call NATO to defend its attacked ally.
Turkey has the trans-Atlantic alliances' second-largest Ôüáarmy ÔÇőand has become a leading global actor in the defence industry ÔÇőin recent years. However, it still lacks fulsome air defence of its own despite efforts to develop them. It has relied on NATO's ÔÇőair defences in both incidents in the last week.

item.title: Serbia suspends energy exports to shield market as crude prices surge on Iran war
item.description:
BELGRADE, March 9 (Reuters) - Serbia has suspended crude oil and fuel ÔÇîproduct exports to safeguard its market from shortages and price spikes, the energy minister said on Monday, as global crude prices jump above $119 a barrel amid supply ÔÇőuncertainty due to the war in Iran .
The suspension, effective until March 19, applies ÔÇőto the export of diesel, gasoline and crude oil by Ôüáall modes of transport, Energy Minister Dubravka Djedovic Handanovic said in ÔÇőa statement.
Crude oil price on Monday reached its highest since mid-2022 of above $119 ÔÇőa barrel, due to uncertainty over global supplies amid the war in Iran.
"The essence of this (temporary) ban is to protect the domestic market from shortages and price spikes ÔÇődue to global disruptions in the world market,ÔÇŁ she said.
The government ÔÇőin neighboring Croatia on Monday introduced fuel price caps until March 23 to counter effects ÔÇîof Ôüásurging crude oil prices.
Croatian Prime Minister Andrej Plenkovic said that without caps, the price of diesel could rise by 16 percentage points to 1.72 euros per litre. The government cap limited it at 1.55 euros/litre.
Croatia last July ÔÇőabolished fuel price ÔÇőcaps introduced in Ôüá2022 when the war in Ukraine threatened global energy supplies.
Serbia has kept a price cap on fuel since ÔÇőFebruary 2022. The government controls the maximum retail prices ÔÇőfor Euro Ôüádiesel and Euro Premium BMB 95 gasoline to mitigate the impact of global price surges on the local economy.
Serbia's fuel exports in 2024, mainly to Ôüáneighboring countries, ÔÇőaccounted for around 3.6% of all exports, ÔÇőor $1.26 billion. In 2025, they sharply fell due to U.S. sanctions against the country's only ÔÇőoil refiner, Russian-owned NIS .

item.title: India not planning to release oil in coordination with IEA, source says
item.description:
March 9 (Reuters) - India is not ÔÇőplanning to ÔÇőrelease oil reserves Ôüáin coordination ÔÇőwith the ÔÇőInternational Energy Agency and has ÔÇőno immediate ÔÇőplans to raise ÔÇîretail Ôüáprices for gasoline and diesel as ÔÇőof ÔÇőnow, Ôüáa government source ÔÇőtold ÔÇőReuters Ôüáon Monday.
(This story has been refiled to correct the author's name in the byline)

item.title: Helsinki energy group plans large investment in small nuclear reactors
item.description:
HELSINKI, March 9 (Reuters) - The city of Helsinki's energy utility Helen plans to invest between 1 billion and 5 billion euros ($1.16 billion-$5.78 billion) in Small Modular Reactor ÔÇőcapacity in the coming years, the head of its newly-established nuclear subsidiary ÔÇîtold Reuters.
Helsinki is not alone in eyeing new nuclear generation, as countries ranging from the United States to South Korea , or companies such as Meta (META.O) , opens new tab turn to SMRs to cater for the rapid rise in ÔÇőpower demand from artificial intelligence and electrification.
SMRs are advanced reactors that can ÔÇőbe factory-assembled and transported for installation elsewhere. They have a power Ôüágenerating capacity of up to 300 megawatts, about a third that of traditional reactors.
Finland, ÔÇőtoo, expects its power demand to increase. But it also needs more steady base ÔÇőload following a rapid rise in wind power production, which has rewarded Finns with some of Europe's lowest power prices on average, but also resulted in high price volatility during consumption peaks.
For ÔÇőHelen, which shut down Finland's last coal-fired power plant last year , nuclear is a way ÔÇőto add capacity while further reducing CO2 emissions, Helen Nuclear Ltd's CEO Pekka Tolonen said.
"Nuclear power ÔÇîis Ôüánow the most promising and, among current options, the best way for us to achieve a future without combustion," he told Reuters. Helen is reviewing half a dozen potential Western SMR suppliers, he added.
"The value of the planned investment would be in ÔÇőthe low single billions, ÔÇőmeaning 1 to Ôüá5 billion euros," he said. The amount would be in the lower end if Helen went for nuclear heat production only, ÔÇőand in the upper end of the range if it opted ÔÇőfor combined Ôüáheat and power production.
Helen, which has to meet heat consumption peaks during cold winter days, believes power consumption in the capital region will double in a few years' time, Ôüádue ÔÇőto electrification, data centres and expansion of clean tech, ÔÇőincluding green hydrogen.
Last month, the company initiated a land use planning process in Helsinki and expects to pick ÔÇőa site by late 2027, Tolonen said.
($1 = 0.8649 euros)

item.title: Exclusive: Trump's Gaza plan on hold as Iran war pauses disarmament talks
item.description:
JERUSALEM/CAIRO, March 9 (Reuters) - Talks to advance President Donald Trump's plan to end the Gaza war have been on hold since last week when the U.S. and ÔÇőIsrael jointly attacked Iran, sparking a broader Middle East war , three sources with direct knowledge of the negotiations said.
The pause threatens to stall implementation of Trump's flagship Middle ÔÇîEast peace initiative, which he has cast as a major foreign policy objective. It comes less than a month after he secured billions of dollars in pledges for Gaza from Gulf Arab states - countries that are now facing Iranian attacks as the conflict widens.
Trump's Gaza plan has hinged in part on whether Hamas militants would lay down their arms in exchange for amnesty, a step intended to pave the way for reconstruction and further Israeli military withdrawals. ÔÇőWhite House mediators have been backchanneling between Israel and Hamas on the disarmament question.
Negotiations on this and other issues were paused when the Iran war began on February 28, ÔÇőthe three sources said, speaking on condition of anonymity to discuss sensitive talks. The pause in disarmament talks has not been previously reported.
Zaha Hassan, Ôüáof the Washington-based Carnegie Endowment for International Peace, said countries including the United Arab Emirates and Qatar which pledged funds for Trump's Board of Peace mission may be questioning if this is "really ÔÇőmoney well spent now that they are dodging rocket fire."
One of the sources, who has direct knowledge of work by Trump's Board of Peace mission, described ÔÇőthe pause as a brief, minor delay caused by flight disruptions preventing mediators and representatives from travelling around the region. Talks have frequently been held in Cairo.
Over the longer term, the Board of Peace believes the war could accelerate a resolution of the disarmament issue by removing Iranian influence, which has long supported Hamas financially, the source said.
Another source ÔÇö a Palestinian official close to the mediation effort ÔÇö said Hamas had been expected to hold talks ÔÇőwith Egyptian, Qatari and Turkish mediators on the day the war erupted, but the meeting was scrapped and no new date has been set.
A Hamas official confirmed that talks on Trump's Gaza ÔÇőplan had been frozen for now, but declined to elaborate. Israel's government did not immediately respond to a request for comment. The White House did not immediately provide comment.
Israel's military has eased off strikes in Gaza ÔÇîsince the Ôüástart of the war but, citing Hamas threats, has not ceased attacks, as Israeli jets carry out bombing campaigns in Iran and Lebanon. At least 16 Palestinians have been killed in Gaza since February 28, health officials say.
"The moment the war on Iran is over, (Israel) will come back at us with the same frequency, with the same violence," said Talal Hamouda, 46, who lives in Jabalia in the northern Gaza Strip, with his wife and five children.
Hamas, for its part, has continued to reassert its grip in areas under its control in Gaza since the war began. Sources close to ÔÇőthe group say Hamas fighters in recent days ÔÇőambushed several Israeli-backed militia members in Gaza's north Ôüáand south, killing at least two people.
Trump's plan for Gaza began with an October ceasefire that left Israel controlling more than half the territory, with Hamas controlling the rest. The initiative appeared to gain momentum in the month leading up to ÔÇőthe war, including the reopening of Gaza's border crossing with Egypt and new pledges for reconstruction.
Much of Washington's multinational coordination on Gaza policy ÔÇőhas been run out Ôüáof a U.S.-led military compound in southern Israel. Foreign diplomats posted there said momentum behind the plan appeared to stall as the Iran war escalated.
Three diplomats said the Civil Military Coordination Centre scaled back to minimal operations when the war started, and that there were concerns it could be targeted by Iranian missiles.
The diplomats said senior U.S. officials now appear focused on the Iran war, leaving Gaza with limited top-level attention. ÔüáStill, working-level ÔÇődiscussions among countries have continued in the hope that the plan could move forward once the war ends.
Natan Sachs, a ÔÇősenior fellow at the Washington-based Middle East Institute, said that "only sustained attention from the Trump administration can keep the plan on track ÔÇö and the war with Iran has the potential to undermine exactly that."
"Without it, the divergent aims of the ÔÇőtwo warring parties could easily lead to very different outcomes, and potentially to a resumption of fighting."

item.title: Turkey's Halkbank, U.S. Justice Department in deferred prosecution agreement, judge says
item.description:
NEW YORK, March 9 (Reuters) - Turkish state-run lender Halkbank (HALKB.IS) , opens new tab and the U.S. Justice Department have reached a deferred prosecution agreement, the judge overseeing the long-running U.S. criminal case against the bank said in a ÔÇőcourt filing on Monday, without providing further details of the deal.
In the case, prosecutors had accused ÔÇîof fraud, money laundering and conspiracy for allegedly helping Iran evade American economic sanctions.
Halkbank's case has long been a thorn in U.S.-Turkey relations, with Turkish President Tayyip Erdogan calling it an "unlawful, ugly" step.
The agreement was announced after the U.S. Supreme Court in October 2025 let ÔÇőstand a federal appeals court decision that let the prosecution proceed.
U.S. prosecutors originally charged Halkbank in ÔÇő2019, during President Donald Trump's first White House term.
Erdogan met with Trump in September, and expressed Ôüáhope the following month that the meeting would resolve various issues between the countries.
The Turkish president said Trump ÔÇőtold him at the White House and in a subsequent phone call that "the Halkbank problem is finished for us."
Halkbank was accused of using money servicers and front companies in Iran, Turkey and the United Arab Emirates in its sanctions-evasion scheme .
U.S. prosecutors said the bank secretly transferred $20 billion of restricted funds, converted oil revenue into gold and cash to benefit Iranian ÔÇőinterests and documented fake food shipments to justify transfers of oil proceeds.
Halkbank pleaded not guilty to bank fraud, money ÔÇőlaundering and conspiracy charges.
The case was an outgrowth of several related criminal cases, including against Turkish-Iranian gold trader Reza Zarrab and ÔÇîformer ÔüáHalkbank executive Mehmet Hakan Atilla.
Zarrab pleaded guilty to bank fraud, money laundering and conspiracy charges in 2017, but has not been sentenced.
He testified against Atilla, who was convicted in 2018 of bank fraud and conspiracy charges. Atilla returned to Turkey in 2019 after serving most of a 32-month prison term. He has denied wrongdoing.
Halkbank's case ÔÇőhas taken a circuitous path ÔÇőthrough the U.S. courts.
Berman originally Ôüáruled in 2020 that Halkbank did not deserve immunity from prosecution.
The following year, the 2nd U.S. Circuit Court of Appeals in Manhattan upheld that ruling but on different grounds. ÔÇőIt said Halkbank could be prosecuted under the federal Foreign Sovereign Immunities Act of ÔÇő1976, because its Ôüáalleged misconduct involved commercial activity not covered by that law.
In 2023, the U.S. Supreme Court temporarily voided the prosecution , despite agreeing that Congress' desire to shield foreign countries and their instrumentalities from civil liability did not cover criminal cases. Instead, Ôüáit ordered ÔÇőthe 2nd Circuit to more thoroughly review whether immunity under centuries-old ÔÇőcommon law shielded Halkbank.
The appeals court found no such shield in October 2024, prompting Halkbank's second Supreme Court appeal. The Trump administration argued that the common ÔÇőlaw does not shield foreign state-owned companies from criminal prosecution.

item.title: Israel military says it is unaware of clash with Hezbollah on Syria border
item.description:
TEL AVIV, March 9 (Reuters) - The Israeli military on Monday said that it ÔÇőwas unaware of any clash with ÔÇîHezbollah fighters in eastern Lebanon, after the Iran-backed Lebanese group said it had fought Israeli soldiers ÔÇőapproaching from Syria.
Hezbollah said in a ÔÇőstatement that its fighters had observed 15 Ôüáhelicopters flying over eastern Lebanon just ÔÇőafter midnight, dropping Israeli troops who were observed ÔÇőapproaching Lebanese territory from an area on the Syrian side of the border.
It said that its fighters "confronted ÔÇőthe helicopters and the infiltrating forces ÔÇőwith appropriate weapons". The Israeli military, however, said that ÔÇîit Ôüáwas unaware of any such incident.
Reuters was unable to immediately verify Hezbollah's account.
The Israeli military carried out an airborne raid in ÔÇőthe same area, ÔÇőnear Ôüáthe village of Nabi Chit, overnight Friday-Saturday in what it said ÔÇőwas a mission to seek the ÔÇőremains Ôüáof a soldier missing since 1986. Lebanon's Health Ministry said 41 people were killed Ôüáin the ÔÇőIsraeli raid.

item.title: US to designate Sudanese Muslim Brotherhood a foreign terrorist organization, State Department says
item.description: WASHINGTON, March 9 (Reuters) - The ÔÇőTrump administration ÔÇîis designating ÔÇőthe ÔÇőSudanese Muslim Brotherhood Ôüáas ÔÇőa ÔÇőspecially designated global terrorist ÔÇőand intends ÔÇőto designate it ÔÇîas Ôüáa foreign terrorist ÔÇőorganization ÔÇőeffective ÔüáMarch 16, ÔÇőthe ÔÇőU.S. ÔüáState Department Ôüásaid ÔÇőon ÔÇőMonday.

item.title: IEA called for joint release of emergency oil stocks at G7 online meeting, Japan says
item.description:
TOKYO, March 9 (Reuters) - The International Energy Agency called ÔÇőfor a coordinated ÔÇîrelease of emergency oil reserves during online meeting ÔÇőwith the ÔÇőfinance ministers from the Group Ôüáof Seven nations ÔÇőon Monday, Japanese ÔÇőFinance Minister Satsuki Katayama told a briefing.
"IEA called for ÔÇőeach country to ÔÇődo coordinated release of oil ÔÇîreserves," ÔüáKatayama said, as the ministers gathered online to discuss the ÔÇőimpact ÔÇőof Ôüáwar in Iran on the ÔÇőmarkets which saw ÔÇőoil Ôüáprices surging to more than $119 a barrel Ôüáon ÔÇőMonday.

item.title: Standard Lithium-Equinor JV inks lithium offtake deal with Trafigura
item.description:
March 9 (Reuters) - Standard Lithium (SLI.V) , opens new tab said on Monday its joint venture with Norway's Equinor (EQNR.OL) , opens new tab ÔÇőhad signed its first binding offtake ÔÇîagreement to supply lithium carbonate from its s outhwest Arkansas project to commodities trader ÔÇőTrafigura.
The joint venture, Smackover Lithium, will ÔÇősupply Trafigura with 8,000 metric tons Ôüáper year of battery-grade lithium ÔÇőcarbonate over a 10-year period starting when ÔÇőcommercial production begins.
The agreement marks a key milestone for the southwest Arkansas project as the ÔÇőpartners seek to secure long-term ÔÇőcustomers and financing ahead of a final investment ÔÇîdecision Ôüáand construction.
Smackover Lithium is targeting about 22,500 metric tons of annual lithium carbonate production capacity in the project's first ÔÇőphase and ÔÇőplans Ôüáto secure offtake agreements for around 80% of that output.
The ÔÇőTrafigura agreement covers more than ÔÇő40% Ôüáof that targeted volume.
Smackover Lithium expects to reach a final investment decision Ôüáon ÔÇőthe project in 2026, ÔÇőwith first production anticipated in 2028.

item.title: Oil shock to worsen should US-Israel seize Iran's Kharg Island, JP Morgan says
item.description:
LONDON, March 9 - Iran's oil exports would stall and output halve if the U.S. and Israel were to seize its port on Kharg Island, triggering further attacks from ÔÇőTehran on regional oil infrastructure, JP Morgan said in a note.
Axios reported ÔÇîon March 7 that the U.S. administration had discussed seizing the island, which sits some 30 km off Iran's coast in the Gulf and processes 90% of its crude exports.
"A direct strike ÔÇőwould immediately halt the bulk of IranÔÇÖs crude exports, likely triggering severe ÔÇőretaliation in the Strait of Hormuz or against regional energy infrastructure," ÔüáJP Morgan said.
Iran , the third largest producer in the Organization of the Petroleum Exporting ÔÇőCountries, pumps about 4.5% of global oil supplies, with output of about 3.3 million ÔÇőbarrels per day of crude, plus 1.3 million bpd of condensate and other liquids.
During the 1979 Iran hostage crisis, U.S. President Jimmy Carter imposed sanctions on Iran but refrained from ordering strikes ÔÇőon the island. His successor, Ronald Reagan, during the 1980s Iran-Iraq Tanker War, prioritised ÔÇőprotecting shipping and targeting Iranian vessels and missile batteries, leaving Kharg untouched.
"Although Iraqi forces struck some ÔÇîterminals Ôüáand tankers during the eight-year war, Kharg remained largely operational and damage was typically repaired quickly, demonstrating that disabling it would require sustained, large-scale attacks," JP Morgan said.
The island collects oil coming by pipeline from IranÔÇÖs largest producing fields, including Ahvaz, Marun ÔÇőand Gachsaran.
In the days ÔÇőleading up to Ôüáthe U.S.-Israeli attack, Iran ramped up exports from Kharg to near record levels, loading over 3 million bpd over February 15-20, ÔÇőnearly triple its normal export pace of around 1.3 million to ÔÇő1.6 million Ôüábpd, according to JP Morgan.
Storage capacity on Kharg is estimated at roughly 30 million barrels and, according to Kpler, approximately 18 million barrels of crude are currently stored on Ôüáthe ÔÇőisland, equivalent to roughly 10-12 days of exports under ÔÇőnormal conditions.
Oil prices jumped to $119 per barrel on Monday as production cuts in the Middle East spread with Iraq , Kuwait , Saudi Arabia and ÔÇőthe United Arab Emirates all affected.

item.title: Shipper MSC introduces emergency fuel surcharges
item.description:
March 9 (Reuters) - Shipping company MSC on Monday announced emergency fuel ÔÇősurcharges on all cargo ÔÇîfrom the Mediterranean and Black Sea to the Indian sub-continent, ÔÇőRed Sea and ÔÇőEast Africa.
The move includes the ÔüáWest Mediterranean, Adriatic, the ÔÇőEast Mediterranean, Greece and Turkey ÔÇőand applies from March 16 until March 31, it said.
Details (per twenty-foot ÔÇőequivalent unit, or TEU):
Western ÔÇőMediterranean and Adriatic
Eastern Mediterranean and Black Sea

item.title: Netherlands sends frigate to Mediterranean on protective mission
item.description:
AMSTERDAM, March 9 (Reuters) - The Dutch government on ÔÇőMonday said it ÔÇîwould send a frigate to the Mediterranean ÔÇőat France's ÔÇőrequest to help protect ÔüáCyprus and other ÔÇőallies and secure maritime ÔÇőtraffic imperilled by the escalating crisis in the ÔÇőMiddle East.
The government ÔÇősaid last week that ÔÇîFrance Ôüáhad asked it to provide an air defence and ÔÇőcommand frigate ÔÇőto Ôüásupport the Charles de ÔÇőGaulle aircraft carrier ÔÇőthat ÔüáPresident Emmanuel Macron said would be Ôüásent ÔÇőto the ÔÇőMediterranean.

item.title: Exclusive: US nears deal to resume intelligence operations in Mali
item.description:
DAKAR, March 9 (Reuters) - The U.S. is nearing a deal with Mali that will allow Washington to resume flying aircraft and drones over the West African country's airspace to gather intelligence on jihadist groups linked to al Qaeda, according to one current ÔÇőU.S. official and a former U.S. official.
Last month, Washington made the first move to implement the agreement by lifting sanctions targeting the defense minister and other senior officials who ÔÇîit said had ties with Russian mercenaries, a key request by Mali's government, the sources said. The U.S. hopes this will lead Mali to grant the U.S. permission to fly intelligence-gathering missions over the country's vast terrain where jihadists have been gaining ground, according to the current and former U.S. officials.
The Trump administration has sought to rebuild ties with Mali after they came under strain during the previous administration.
In Washington, the goal to resume intelligence gathering is partly driven by a ÔÇődesire to find an American pilot who was kidnapped by armed men while working for Christian missionaries in neighboring Niger. The pilot is currently believed to be held in Mali by ÔÇőthe local al Qaeda affiliate, Jama'at Nusrat al-Islam wal-Muslimin (JNIM), the officials said.
The State Department declined to comment.
A Malian government spokesperson did not respond to a request Ôüáfor comment.
But after the sanctions were lifted on February 27, Mali's government praised the move, describing it as a step that would help "improve relations between our two countries, while reiterating respect for national sovereignty."
Mali, ÔÇőa gold-producing, landlocked nation in the Sahel region of Africa, is roughly twice the size of France. Like several of its neighbors in the Sahel, it is battling an increasingly violent insurgency led by JNIM.
JNIM ÔÇőhas pursued widespread kidnapping campaigns targeting foreign nationals to finance its operations in West Africa, according to U.S. crisis-monitoring group Armed Conflict Location & Event Data.
The top U.S. envoy to Africa, Nick Checker, visited Mali last month to meet with Malian Foreign Minister Abdoulaye Diop. The goal was to convey Washington's "desire to chart a new course in the bilateral relationship and move past policy missteps," according to the State Department, an apparent reference to Biden ÔÇőadministration policies.
Diop praised American efforts to relaunch the relationship with a new approach based on mutual respect for its sovereignty and non-interference.
The Trump administration has largely dropped efforts to promote elections in the Sahel ÔÇőregion, where military officials have overthrown elected but unpopular, Western-backed governments in recent years and turned to Russia for security support.
That has been a welcome move in Bamako and other Sahelian capitals. In the Sahel, perceived lecturing by ÔÇîWashington is often Ôüáviewed as paternalistic and even racist, according to Catherine Nzuki of the Center for Strategic and International Studies, a U.S.-based think tank, which published a note about the U.S. loss of a base in Niger.
The Biden administrationÔÇÖs efforts to promote democracy after the coups helped trigger a break with Niger in 2024, with Niamey ordering all U.S. troops to leave a newly built, sprawling drone base in Agadez after a meeting with Biden officials went poorly. The multimillion-dollar base was intended to underpin intelligence-gathering operations across the region.
But the Trump administration's effort to improve relations has also encountered setbacks. Mali, Niger and Burkina Faso in December took ÔÇősteps to implement a reciprocal travel ban on Americans after ÔÇőthe three nations were added to the ÔüáWhite House's travel ban list.
In Mali, militants allied with al Qaeda and other groups have gained influence, spreading across the region despite a nearly decade-long intervention backed by France and the West, and later the efforts of Russian mercenaries. Over the past year, suspected jihadists have attacked gold mining assets ÔÇőand key highways , starving the capital of fuel for businesses and schools.
Reuters was unable to determine whether Mali was taking steps to give Washington permission ÔÇőto conduct missions in its Ôüáairspace or when it was expected to take action.
The former U.S. official said cooperation was likely to come eventually because it was in Mali's interest to allow the U.S. to resume intelligence-gathering operations. ÔÇťThey will derive direct benefit from U.S. ISR activities where their national capabilities are insufficient,ÔÇŁ the former official said, referring to intelligence, surveillance and reconnaissance.
"This overflight would build on likely ongoing cooperation with other USG entities,ÔÇŁ the former official added, referring to ÔüáU.S. government agencies.
The U.S. ÔÇőhas shared intelligence with Mali that helped lead to a strike on senior JNIM leadership last year.
It wasnÔÇÖt immediately clear where ÔÇőthe U.S. might base aircraft if Mali proceeds with granting permission to resume overflight operations. Ivory Coast has previously been discussed as an option, as it shares its northern border with Mali. Ghana hosted U.S. contractors late last year when the Trump administration began surveillance ÔÇőflights over Nigeria, ahead of its Christmas Day bombing of what the U.S. said were Islamic State positions.

item.title: Trump says Australia has taken care of some Iranian women soccer players
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 9 (Reuters) - President Donald Trump on Monday said Australian Prime ÔÇőMinister Anthony Albanese told him ÔÇîfive members of the Iranian national women's soccer team had been "taken ÔÇőcare of" amid fears ÔÇőthey could be punished.
Australia's SBS ÔüáNews earlier said five players ÔÇőhad "broken free" after the team ÔÇőwas eliminated from the Asian Cup tournament and were now under the ÔÇőprotection of the federal police, ÔÇőseeking assistance from the government.
"Five have already ÔÇîbeen Ôüátaken care of, and the rest are on their way," Trump said in a ÔÇőTruth Social ÔÇőPost Ôüáafter talking to Albanese. "Some, however, feel they must ÔÇőgo back because they ÔÇőare Ôüáworried about the safety of their families, including threats to Ôüáthose ÔÇőfamily members if ÔÇőthey don't return."

item.title: Israel will target anyone promoting radical ideas against it, says UN envoy
item.description:
March 9 (Reuters) - Israel's U.N. ambassador Danny Danon said on Monday that the new supreme leader of Iran , Mojtaba Khamenei, ÔÇőhas the same radical ideas as in the past ÔÇîand Israel will target anyone who promotes radical ideas against it.
"Changing the man at the top does not change the regime," Danon told reporters at the United ÔÇőNations when asked about Iran's naming of Khamenei to replace ÔÇőhis father Ali Khamenei, who was killed on the Ôüáfirst day of the war the U.S. and Israel launched against Iran at ÔÇőthe end of last month.
"The new leader, unfortunately, is more of the ÔÇősame ideology, the same radical ideas, and ... anyone who will promote those radical ideas against us, we will target them, we will find them," Danon said.
Danon said ÔÇőthe people of Iran should rise up to choose their next ÔÇőleader and added: "We will have to create the conditions for them, and that ÔÇîis Ôüáwhat we are doing now."
Asked about the threat to crucial energy traffic in the Strait of Hormuz and Iran's attacks against neighboring countries, Danon said Israel and the U.S. were hunting Iran's missile launchers and ÔÇődegrading its capabilities.
"So ÔÇőit's going to Ôüábe harder for them to attack civilians in Dubai and to attack vessels in the Strait of ÔÇőHormuz. It doesn't mean it will be 100% guaranteed, ÔÇőbut Ôüáit will be harder for them to do that.
"So I'm optimistic about that. Every day we see the numbers of attacks, going down," Danon Ôüásaid.
The ÔÇőwar has effectively shut the Strait of ÔÇőHormuz, where a fifth of global oil and liquefied natural gas is shipped along ÔÇőIran's coast.

item.title: Anthropic sues to block Pentagon blacklisting over AI use restrictions
item.description:
NEW YORK, March 9 (Reuters) - Anthropic on Monday filed a lawsuit to block ÔÇőthe Pentagon from placing it on a national security blacklist , escalating the artificial intelligence labÔÇÖs high-stakes battle with the U.S. military over usage ÔÇîrestrictions on its technology.
Anthropic said in its lawsuit that the designation was unlawful and violated its free speech and due process rights. The filing in federal court in California asked a judge to undo the designation and block federal agencies from enforcing it.
ÔÇťThese actions are unprecedented and unlawful. The Constitution does not allow the government to wield its enormous power to punish a ÔÇőcompany for its protected speech," Anthropic said.
The Pentagon on Thursday slapped a formal supply-chain risk designation on Anthropic, limiting use of a technology that a ÔÇősource said was being used for military operations in Iran . Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth designated Anthropic after the startup refused to remove Ôüáguardrails against using its AI for autonomous weapons or domestic surveillance. The two sides had been in increasingly contentious talks over those limitations for months, Reuters first reported.
Anthropic ÔÇőofficials said the lawsuit doesn't preclude re-opening negotiations with the U.S. government and reaching a settlement. The company has said it does not want to be fighting with ÔÇőthe U.S. government. The Pentagon said it wouldn't comment on litigation. Last week, a Pentagon official said the two sides were no longer active talks.
The designation poses a big threat to AnthropicÔÇÖs business with the government, and the outcome could shape how other AI companies negotiate restrictions on military use of their technology, though the company's CEO Dario Amodei clarified on Thursday that the ÔÇődesignation had "a narrow scope" and businesses could still use its tools in projects unrelated to the Pentagon.
President Donald Trump has also directed the government to stop working ÔÇőwith Anthropic, whose financial backers include Alphabet's Google (GOOGL.O) , opens new tab and Amazon.com (AMZN.O) , opens new tab . Trump and Hegseth said there would be a six-month phase-out.
Reuters has reported that Anthropic's investors were racing to contain the damage ÔÇîcaused by Ôüáthe fallout with the Pentagon.
Trump and HegsethÔÇÖs actions on February 27 came after months of talks with Anthropic over whether the companyÔÇÖs policies could constrain military action and shortly after Amodei met with Hegseth in hopes of reaching a deal.
The Pentagon said U.S. law, not a private company, would determine how to defend the country and insisted on having full flexibility in using AI for ÔÇťany lawful use,ÔÇŁ asserting that AnthropicÔÇÖs restrictions could endanger American lives.
Anthropic said even the best AI models were not ÔÇőreliable enough for fully autonomous weapons ÔÇőand that using them for that Ôüápurpose would be dangerous. The company also drew a red line on domestic surveillance of Americans, calling that a violation of fundamental rights.
After HegsethÔÇÖs announcement, Anthropic said in a statement that the designation would be legally unsound and set a dangerous ÔÇőprecedent for companies that negotiate with the government. The company said it would not be swayed by ÔÇťintimidation or punishment,ÔÇŁ and ÔÇőon Thursday Amodei reiterated Ôüáthat Anthropic would challenge the designation in court.
He also apologized for an internal memo published on Wednesday by tech news site The Information. In the memo, which was written last Friday, Amodei said Pentagon officials did not like the company in part because ÔÇťwe havenÔÇÖt given dictator-style praise to Trump."
The Defense Department signed agreements worth up to $200 million Ôüáeach with major ÔÇőAI labs in the past year, including Anthropic, OpenAI and Google.
Microsoft-backed (MSFT.O) , opens new tab OpenAI announced a deal to ÔÇőuse its technology in the Defense Department network shortly after Hegseth moved to blacklist Anthropic. CEO Sam Altman said the Pentagon shared OpenAI's principles of ensuring human oversight of weapon systems and opposing ÔÇőmass U.S. surveillance.

item.title: Brazil's finance minister expected to step down next week, sources say
item.description:
BRASILIA, March 9 (Reuters) - Brazilian Finance Minister Fernando Haddad is expected to step down by the end of next week ÔÇőto run for governor of Sao Paulo state as pushed ÔÇîfor by President Luiz Inacio Lula da Silva, four sources told Reuters on Monday.
Haddad has not yet had a final conversation with Lula about running ÔÇőfor governor, one of the sources said, but the two ÔÇőhave been speaking frequently about the matter.
The move was Ôüáreported by newspaper O Globo earlier in the day. The finance ÔÇőministry declined to comment.
Lula's push for Haddad to run in Sao ÔÇőPaulo reflects the president's need for a strong presence in a state that often votes heavily for the right-wing opposition. A weak performance for the leftist ÔÇőleader in Sao Paulo could undermine his presidential campaign, according ÔÇőto his party.
Haddad had not planned to run for office this year and previously ÔÇîvoiced Ôüáhis intention to serve as one of the coordinators of Lula's re-election campaign .
Sources told Reuters, however, that expectations of a tough election for the president have put pressure on Haddad to become a candidate ÔÇőin Sao Paulo.
A Datafolha ÔÇőpoll released Ôüáover the weekend showed that a potential second-round runoff between Lula and Senator Flavio Bolsonaro, the eldest ÔÇőson of former President Jair Bolsonaro, would result in ÔÇőa Ôüátechnical tie.
Another Datafolha poll focused on Sao Paulo state showed Haddad would take 31% of the vote in a first round against 44% Ôüáfor Governor ÔÇőTarcisio de Freitas, backed by the ÔÇőBolsonaro family, who defeated Haddad in 2022.

item.title: US indicts two alleged 'terrorists' accused of trying to bomb NYC protest
item.description: WASHINGTON, March 9 (Reuters) - The U.S. ÔÇőDepartment of ÔÇîJustice has indicted ÔÇőtwo ÔÇőpeople accused of trying Ôüáto ÔÇőbomb a ÔÇőprotest in New York City, ÔÇőAttorney ÔÇőGeneral Pam Bondi ÔÇîsaid Ôüáin a post on X ÔÇőon ÔÇőMonday, Ôüácalling them "ISIS-inspired ÔÇőterrorists."

item.title: Ukraine ready for new peace talks 'at any moment', Zelenskiy says
item.description:
KYIV, March 9 (Reuters) - Ukraine is ready for ÔÇőnew U.S.-backed peace talks "at ÔÇîany moment", but its partners' attention is ÔÇőcurrently focused ÔÇőon the Iran conflict, President ÔüáVolodymyr Zelenskiy ÔÇősaid on Monday, adding ÔÇőthat the U.S. had asked to postpone an ÔÇőupcoming meeting.
Writing on ÔÇőX, Zelenskiy said Moscow was ÔÇîtrying Ôüáto manipulate the conflict in its favour and aiming ÔÇőto ÔÇőturn ÔüáIran's strikes on its neighbours ÔÇőand U.S. bases ÔÇőinto "a Ôüásecond front of Russia's war against ÔüáUkraine".

item.title: Two right-wing politicians lead in Peru's presidential race, but most are undecided, poll says
item.description:
LIMA, March 9 (Reuters) - Two right-wing candidates - the daughter ÔÇîof a former jailed president and the former mayor of Lima - are out in front of other contenders for Peru's April 12 presidential election, according to the latest poll, though many voters are either undecided or do not plan ÔÇőon voting.
Keiko Fujimori and Rafael Lopez Aliaga are the only candidates out of a record ÔÇőroster of 36 presidential hopefuls polling in the double digits. Fujimori polled at Ôüá10.7% versus her rival's 10%, according to a Datum Internacional poll released Sunday night on ÔÇőlocal TV, though roughly two-fifths of those polled either declined to choose a candidate or do not ÔÇőplan on voting in the first place.
The scant support for the leaders suggests that a June 7 runoff is likely, which would be typical for the Andean nation, as no candidate has won in the first round since ÔÇőthe end of the last century.
The job of Peru's head of state has become a revolving ÔÇődoor, with eight presidents since 2018. Four former leaders are currently behind bars. Its unpopular right-wing-controlled Congress removed its ÔÇîlatest Ôüápresident last month after he held secret meetings with a Chinese businessman.
Over 38% of voters polled between February 27 and March 4 were undecided or had decided not to vote, Datum found. Many of those who do vote will not decide until a week before the election, CEO Urpi Torrado ÔÇősaid.
Fujimori, daughter of late ÔÇőformer president Alberto Fujimori, Ôüáwho was imprisoned for 16 years for crimes against humanity - including the use of death squads during his decade-long tenure - is on her fourth run ÔÇőfor the presidency.
She has been trading places in polling with Lopez Aliaga, ÔÇőan ultra-conservative Ôüáformer mayor who has embraced his nickname "Porky" for his resemblance to a U.S. cartoon pig, as part of his campaign. A fortnight ago, another Datum poll showed Lopez Aliaga in the lead with 13.4% and ÔüáFujimori at ÔÇő9.7%.
Other candidates polling around 5% include leftist Alfonso Lopez-Chau, ÔÇőa former central bank director; millionaire businessman Cesar Acuna; Carlos Alvarez, a popular comedian and impersonator of former presidents; and retired ÔÇőgeneral and former intelligence chief Wolfgang Grozo.

item.title: Trump tells NY Post he is 'nowhere near' deciding to send troops to Iran to secure nuclear stockpile
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 9 (Reuters) - President Donald Trump said on Monday that he ÔÇőwas "nowhere near" deciding whether to send ÔÇîU.S. troops into Iran to secure the stockpile of highly enriched uranium there.
"We havenÔÇÖt made any ÔÇődecision on that. WeÔÇÖre nowhere near ÔÇőit,ÔÇŁ Trump told the New York Post Ôüáwhen asked about reported discussions between ÔÇőIsrael and the United States on ÔÇőpossibly deploying special forces to Iran to seize and secure the material.
A longstanding dispute over Iran's ÔÇőnuclear program escalated into military conflict ten ÔÇődays ago, with the U.S. and Israel launching strikes ÔÇîagainst ÔüáIran and Iran responding with attacks across the Middle East.
Without providing evidence, Trump said last month that Tehran was ÔÇőbeginning to ÔÇőrebuild the Ôüánuclear program that he claimed had been ÔÇťobliteratedÔÇŁ by U.S. strikes ÔÇőin June last year.
Iran denies ÔÇőseeking Ôüáa nuclear arsenal, saying its enrichment of uranium ÔÇô a process that produces fuel for Ôüápower ÔÇőplants and nuclear warheads ÔÇődepending on its duration ÔÇô is strictly for civilian use.

item.title: Rubio could designate Afghanistan as sponsor of wrongful detention, source says
item.description: WASHINGTON, March 9 (Reuters) - U.S. ÔÇőSecretary of ÔÇîState Marco Rubio ÔÇőis ÔÇőconsidering whether to Ôüádesignate ÔÇőAfghanistan as ÔÇőa state sponsor of ÔÇőwrongful ÔÇődetention and could ÔÇîact Ôüáas soon as Monday, ÔÇőaccording ÔÇőto Ôüáa source ÔÇőfamiliar ÔÇőwith Ôüáthe matter.

item.title: Islamist militants kill 15 in coordinated raids across northeast Nigeria
item.description:
MAIDUGURI, Nigeria, March 9 (Reuters) - Islamist militants killed at least 12 soldiers and three civilians in coordinated overnight attacks in northeast Nigeria, military ÔÇősources and residents said on Monday, as attacks on armed forces intensify.
A ÔÇî17-year Islamist insurgency in the region has killed thousands of people and displaced 2 million, according to aid groups, despite major military campaigns.
The assaults in Kukawa, Dalwa and Goniri came days after a similar ÔÇőattack on an army post in Ngoshe and showed the militant groups' ÔÇőability to hit multiple fronts at once.
Boko Haram and Islamic State West ÔüáAfrica Province (ISWAP) fighters swept into Kukawa district in Borno state before dawn on Monday, ÔÇőpushing towards the nearby military camp in a three-hour battle, soldiers involved told Reuters.
Troops ÔÇőlater retook the camp but not before the commanding officer and five other soldiers were killed, a military source said. Karta Maina Ma'aji Lawan, the lawmaker representing Kukawa, confirmed the attack and the officer's ÔÇődeath.
In Dalwa, militants killed two soldiers and three residents before torching more than 250 ÔÇőhomes, said resident and traditional leader Shetima Isa.
In neighbouring Yobe state, insurgents overran the Goniri military ÔÇîbase, killing Ôüáfour soldiers and setting vehicles and buildings ablaze, another soldier said.
Military spokesperson Sani Uba said troops had repelled several coordinated attacks on military positions in the northeast, and that all locations remained firmly under control.
"Regrettably, the engagements claimed the lives of some brave ÔÇőand gallant soldiers, including ÔÇőan outstanding officer Ôüáin Kukawa, who paid the ultimate price," Uba said, without providing further casualty details.
Ground forces, backed by air support, were conducting follow-up ÔÇőoperations in the affected areas, while cordon-and-search missions continued in nearby ÔÇővillages where Ôüáwounded militants were reported to be hiding, he said.
Nigeria's military has ramped up raids on insurgent hideouts this year as part of a renewed offensive, but ISWAP and Boko Haram continue Ôüáto ÔÇőexploit the tough terrain, porous borders and a thin ÔÇőstate presence across the northeast's arid belt.
The latest attacks came just hours after military intelligence had warned of ÔÇőa likely ISWAP offensive.

item.title: China's foreign minister calls for ceasefire in Gulf region
item.description:
BEIJING, March 9 (Reuters) - Chinese Foreign Minister Wang Yi on Monday ÔÇőcalled for a ceasefire in ÔÇîthe Gulf region as soon as possible, stressing that the ÔÇősovereignty, security and territorial ÔÇőintegrity of Gulf countries should be Ôüáfully respected, according to ÔÇőstatements from his ministry.
Wang made ÔÇőthe remarks in a phone call with his Kuwaiti counterpart Jarrah Jaber ÔÇőAl-Ahmad Al-Sabah on Monday ÔÇőas Iran answered a U.S.-Israeli bombing ÔÇîcampaign Ôüáwith strikes around the Gulf region.
In a separate call, Wang told Bahraini Foreign Minister ÔÇőAbdullatif bin ÔÇőRashid ÔüáAl Zayani that China was deeply concerned about ÔÇőthe rapidly escalating situation ÔÇőand Ôüáwould play a constructive role in maintaining peace and Ôüástability ÔÇőin the region.

item.title: Exclusive: Trump reviews options to curb energy prices as Iran strikes roil markets
item.description:
March 9 (Reuters) - U.S. President Donald Trump is expected to review as early as Monday a set of options to tame oil prices, ÔÇőwhich have spiked to more than $100 a barrel due to the Iran war, according to two people familiar ÔÇîwith the matter.
The effort reflects White House worries that the surge in oil prices will hurt U.S. businesses and consumers ahead of the November midterm elections when Trump's fellow Republicans are hoping to retain control of Congress.
U.S. officials in Washington have been discussing with counterparts from the Group of Seven major ÔÇőeconomies a possible joint release of crude oil from strategic reserves as one of several measures currently under discussion, the ÔÇősources said.
Other options include restricting U.S. exports, intervening in oil futures markets, waiving some federal taxes and Ôüálifting requirements under a U.S. law called the Jones Act that domestic fuel move only on U.S.-flagged ships, among others, the ÔÇősources said, speaking on condition of anonymity.
Analysts have said that U.S. policy options will have little sway over global oil markets as ÔÇőlong as the fighting blocks Middle East oil exports accounting for a fifth of global supply through the Strait of Hormuz.
"The White House is in constant coordination with the relevant agencies on this important issue, as it is a top priority to the president. President Trump and his entire energy ÔÇőteam have had a strong game plan to keep the energy markets stable well before Operation Epic Fury began, and they will ÔÇőcontinue to review all credible options," White House spokesperson Taylor Rogers said in a statement, using the Trump administration's name for the U.S.-Israel military ÔÇîoperations targeting ÔüáIran.
Global crude oil prices have hit levels not seen since mid-2022, briefly touching $119 a barrel, with gasoline and other fuel costs surging as a result since the U.S. and Israeli strikes began on February 28.
The White House last week asked federal agencies to assemble proposals that could help ease pressure on crude and gasoline prices, Reuters previously reported. The deliberations involve top White House officials, including White ÔÇőHouse Chief of Staff Susie Wiles ÔÇőand top adviser Stephen Miller, Ôüáthe sources said.
Analysts and industry officials have said the White House has few meaningful tools to quickly curb rising oil prices unless authorities can restore the flow of tankers through the Strait ÔÇőof Hormuz, the narrow waterway between Iran and Oman that carries roughly a fifth of the ÔÇőworld's oil supply.
"The Ôüáproblem is options range from marginal through symbolic to deeply unwise," said one of the sources, who is engaged with the White House on the effort.
The turmoil in energy markets comes at a sensitive moment for the president, who has sought to keep fuel prices low Ôüáas a ÔÇőcornerstone of his economic message to voters. A prolonged spike in oil and ÔÇőgasoline costs could ripple through the broader economy, raising transportation and consumer prices.
A White House plan to provide naval escorts and backstop insurance for tankers traveling the Strait ÔÇőof Hormuz so far has failed to significantly boost shipping traffic through the vital waterway.

item.title: Back to the 1970s? Investors brace for a return of stagflation
item.description:
LONDON, March 9 (Reuters) - Investors are now seriously considering the possibility that war in the Middle East could create a stagflationary shock, just as it did 50 years ago, when disruption to global ÔÇőenergy supplies sent inflation surging, and battered growth
ÔÇťThe risk of a 1970s scenario is rising,ÔÇŁ said Kaspar Hense, portfolio manager at RBC BlueBay Asset Management.
ÔÇťIf there is another extended war, ÔÇîwith oil prices going up significantly further, then the safe-haven status of government bonds are at risk, and with that, all assets.ÔÇŁ
Here's how stagflation is a risk and could play out in markets in five charts.
The epicentre of stagflation fears is the surge in oil prices, and the biggest question for world markets now is how long will those prices remain elevated.
Brent crude vaulted above $100 a barrel on Monday and was on track for its biggest daily jump since the 2020 COVID ÔÇőcrisis .
It is up 70% since the start of the year, while European wholesale gas prices are at their highest level in over three years .
That's bad news for inflation.
"A useful rule of thumb is ÔÇőthat a 5% rise in oil prices adds around 0.1 percentage points to developed market inflation," Capital Economics said.
And high oil prices also could dampen economic growth.
The ÔüáInternational Monetary Fund estimates for every persistent 10% rise in oil prices, a 0.1ÔÇô0.2 percent fall in global economic output follows.
Oil price surges contributed to U.S. recessions in 1973, 1980, 1990 and 2008.
This ÔÇőputs central banks in a bind, as rate hikes to restrain inflation could further undermine economic growth.
Chicago Fed President Austan Goolsbee told the Wall Street Journal on Friday a "stagflationary environment that's as uncomfortable as any" could be looming.
Markets now ÔÇősee at least one European Central Bank hike this year, compared to a 40% chance of a cut before the war.
They also see a chance of a rate hike this year from the Bank of England, having earlier priced at least two cuts.
ÔÇťIt seems only retreating oil prices could reverse rate hike fears, even with dovish minds at the ECB also stressing downside growth risks,ÔÇŁ said Commerzbank rates strategist Rainer Guntermann.
That has battered global bond markets as investors ditch fixed-income assets, ÔÇőwhere inflation erodes future returns.
Short-dated bonds are the most sensitive. In Britain, two-year gilt yields have shot up by nearly 50 basis points in the last week, their worst sell-off since the budget crisis of 2022, in ÔÇőlight of the UK's sticky inflation and flat-lining growth .
German and Australian two-year yields have risen more than 30 bps in that time, while U.S. two-year yields are up a comparatively modest 13 bps. ,
That's got investors looking at inflation-linked ÔÇîdebt, where both Ôüáthe principal and the interest are linked to the rate of inflation.
This has pushed British five-year breakeven inflation rates - the difference between inflation-linked and nominal yields - up 28 basis points since the end of February. On Monday, they hit almost 3.5%, their highest since last April .
Meanwhile, five-year inflation-linked Treasury yields have risen by 4.2 bps in the last week, compared with a 15-bp rise in nominal yields .
Those wondering whether the market and economic hit will cause U.S. President Donald Trump to change course have to remember the stagflationary hit from the war will likely hurt the U.S. less than Europe or Asia.
"The U.S., with the Americas, ÔÇőis self-sufficient in many (of the) commodities being choked off ÔÇődirectly or indirectly via (Strait of) Hormuz," said Rabobank Ôüásenior global strategist Michael Every in a note.
As well as oil he flags fertiliser and helium, important for semiconductor manufacturing.
