Not surprisingly, U.S. markets have held up better in relative terms. The S&P 500 dropped 2% last week compared to a 5.5% fall in Europe and 6.3% drop for MSCI's Asia Pacific ex-Japan index. (.SPX) , opens new tab , (.STOXX) , opens new tab , (.MIAPJ0000PUS) , opens new tab
U.S. bonds also ÔÇőoutperformed German last week. ,
However, the U.S. is by no means immune to stagflation and was looking a bit vulnerable even before the energy price spike. ÔÇőThe economy unexpectedly shed jobs in ÔüáFebruary, and data this week is expected to show a tick higher in U.S. inflation.
Investors don't like stagflation because it hurts stocks, bonds - that aren't inflation linked - and even potentially gold, given it does not have a yield.
The precious metal dropped 2% last week, and was down again on Monday, though analysts said this was in part selling to make up for losses elsewhere.
The only safe haven that has really held up since the Ôüáwar begun has ÔÇőbeen the dollar, which has gained on nearly every other developed market currency.
"The U.S. is a major oil producer and ÔÇőcan withstand an oil shock - though there will be political fallout," said Kit Juckes, head of FX strategy at Societe Generale.
"The same simply isnÔÇÖt true of Europe, and the UK in particular."

item.title: Swiss parliament approves compensation for Crans-Montana bar fire victims
item.description:
GENEVA, March 9 (Reuters) - The Swiss lower house of parliament on Monday approved plans to make a ÔÇőone-off payment of 50,000 Swiss francs ($64,000) ÔÇîto survivors and bereaved families of a New Year bar fire at a ski resort that killed 41 ÔÇőpeople and injured 115.
The so-called solidarity contribution, ÔÇőwhich aims to provide swift financial support Ôüáto victims of the blaze at the ÔÇőLe Constellation bar in the town of Crans-Montana, ÔÇőhad already been voted through by the upper house of parliament last week.
The payment is meant to apply ÔÇőto each individual who lost their life ÔÇőin the fire and everyone who was hospitalised. Most ÔÇîof Ôüáthose who died were teenagers and many of the victims were foreigners, including several from France and Italy.
According to witnesses and prosecutors, the ÔÇőfire appeared ÔÇőto have Ôüábeen started by the use of sparkling candles that ignited foam ÔÇősoundproofing on the bar's basement ceiling.
Earlier, a document ÔÇőshowed Ôüáthat Swiss prosecutors have widened their investigation into the fire, adding the mayor of Crans-Montana Ôüáand ÔÇőfour other current and former ÔÇőlocal officials to the list of suspects.
($1 = 0.7785 Swiss francs)

item.title: Venture Global pledges on-time LNG deliveries amid Middle East conflict
item.description:
LONDON/HOUSTON, March 9 (Reuters) - U.S. liquefied natural gas developer Venture ÔÇîGlobal LNG (VG.N) , opens new tab has told customers of its Plaquemines export plant under development in Louisiana that it will start delivering contracted cargoes on schedule and at the already-agreed-upon prices, according to a letter seen by Reuters.
The Virginia-based company wrote on Friday to buyers of Plaquemines Phase 1 - ÔÇőwhich include British major Shell (SHEL.L) , opens new tab and PolandÔÇÖs Orlen (PKN.WA) , opens new tab - and said it remains committed to beginning longÔÇĹterm deliveries from ÔÇőOctober 31 despite a surge in global gas prices because of the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran .
ÔÇťAs global Ôüáenergy markets react to the critical developments surrounding Iran and the Middle East, we wanted to assure you that, ÔÇőas of today, Phase 1 remains on schedule,ÔÇŁ the letter said.
Companies including BP (BP.L) , opens new tab , Shell, Unipec, Edison (EDNn.MI) , opens new tab , Galp (GALP.LS) , opens new tab , Repsol (REP.MC) , opens new tab and Orlen took ÔÇőVenture Global to arbitration in one of the biggest disputes in the history of the LNG industry. The companies accused Venture Global of withholding contracted cargoes during commissioning of its nearby Calcasieu Pass plant to take advantage of higher prices on the spot market during the ÔÇő2021ÔÇô2023 energy crisis.
While Venture Global - which blamed technical issues for the delays in formal commissioning at Calcasieu - prevailed over Shell, ÔÇőBP is seeking at least $3.7 billion after winning its case, a Venture Global filing showed. OrlenÔÇÖs case remains pending.
ÔÇťTo be clear, fluctuating market ÔÇîconditions have Ôüáno impact on our previously communicated schedule,ÔÇŁ a Venture Global spokesperson said. ÔÇťAs we reaffirmed on our earnings call last week, nearly 70% of the 2026 cargoes at Plaquemines are already contracted for and we reaffirm that Phase One remains on target to declare COD in Q4 2026.ÔÇŁ
The U.S.-Israeli war on Iran and Tehran's attacks on Gulf neighbors have disrupted oil and ÔÇőnatural gas exports from the ÔÇőMiddle East and forced production Ôüástoppages, including in Qatar, which stopped operations at its LNG facilities, affecting some of the world's largest plants and a source that supplies about 20% of global LNG.
Benchmark gas prices ÔÇőhave jumped, with Dutch Title Transfer Facility futures trading at a three-year high of ÔÇőaround $21 per million ÔüáBritish thermal units, and the Japan-Korea Marker near a two-year high around $16.
Venture Global is the second-largest U.S. LNG exporter. Plaquemines is its second export terminal and it shipped 2 million metric tons of LNG last month from Plaquemines, according to LSEG data. ÔüáThe plant ÔÇőis sending out early commission cargoes even though it is still under ÔÇődevelopment.
The company told customers it will begin developing its annual delivery program for the first contract year in early May, followed by the second contract ÔÇőyear in July.

item.title: Oil and gas industry urge EU to pause methane emissions law
item.description:
BRUSSELS, March 9 (Reuters) - Oil and gas companies urged the European Union on Monday to pause its methane emissions law, warning it could disrupt Europe's fuel ÔÇőimports when tougher provisions kick in next year.
The call from industry ÔÇîcomes after the U.S. government demanded the EU exempt U.S. oil and gas from the rules. Brussels has since offered companies more flexible compliance options but declined to roll back the policy, a central ÔÇőpillar of its climate strategy.
An industry-backed study by Wood Mackenzie, published on ÔÇőMonday, said up to 43% of EU gas imports and 87% Ôüáof EU crude oil imports could struggle to meet the EU rules ÔÇőfrom 2027, exposing importers to heavy fines and risking supply gaps if producers are ÔÇődeterred from shipping fuel to Europe.
IOGP and fuel manufacturing group FuelsEurope called on Brussels to pause and amend the law.
"The EU cannot afford a self-made regulatory supply shock, even more so ÔÇőin the current geopolitical context," said Francois-Regis Mouton de Lostalot, managing director of ÔÇőIOGP Europe, whose members include ExxonMobil, Chevron, BP and TotalEnergies.
Oil and gas prices have surged ÔÇîthis Ôüámonth in response to the U.S. and Israeli war on Iran , adding short-term pressure on governments already navigating Europe's phase-out of Russian energy imports. Most EU oil and gas now comes from Norway and the U.S.
The EU methane law requires that, from January ÔÇő2027, imported gas ÔÇőmust comply with Ôüámonitoring and verification rules equivalent to Europe's, or meet a voluntary industry standard known as "Oil and Gas Methane Partnership 2.0 ÔÇőlevel 5". The aforementioned companies are all part of the ÔÇőOGMP initiative.
Climate Ôüágroups disputed the study's findings. The Environmental Defense Fund said its own analysis indicated that, following methane-reduction commitments by many oil and gas majors, the global volume of ÔüáOGMP ÔÇő2.0 level 5-compliant gas in 2027 would be ÔÇőmore than double EU demand, including supplies from Norway and the U.S.
A European Commission spokesperson did not ÔÇőimmediately respond to a request for comment.

item.title: Much of Iran's near-bomb-grade uranium likely to be in Isfahan, IAEA's Grossi says
item.description:
PARIS, March 9 (Reuters) - Almost half of ÔÇîIran's uranium enriched to up to 60% purity, a short step from weapons-grade, was stored in a tunnel complex at Isfahan and is probably still there, U.N. nuclear watchdog chief Rafael Grossi said on Monday.
The tunnel complex is the only ÔÇőtarget that appears not to have been badly damaged in attacks last June by Israel ÔÇőand the U.S. on Iran's nuclear facilities.
Diplomats have long said Isfahan has been Ôüáused to store 60% uranium, which the International Atomic Energy Agency confirmed in a report to member ÔÇőstates last month, without saying how much was there.
The IAEA estimates ÔÇőthat when Israel launched its first attacks in June, Iran had 440.9 kg of 60% uranium. If enriched further, that would provide the explosive needed for 10 nuclear weapons, according to an IAEA yardstick.
"What we believe is that Isfahan had ÔÇőuntil our last inspection a bit more than 200 kg, maybe a little bit more than that, ÔÇőof 60% uranium," IAEA chief Rafael Grossi told reporters in Paris.
He said the stock was "mainly" at Isfahan, and some held elsewhere ÔÇîmay Ôüáhave been destroyed.
"The widespread assumption is that the material is still there. So we haven't seen - and not only us, I think in general all those observing the facility through satellite imagery and other means to see what's going on there - movement indicating that the material could have been transferred," Grossi ÔÇősaid.
Iran has not informed the ÔÇőIAEA of the Ôüástatus or whereabouts of its highly enriched uranium since the June attacks, nor has it let IAEA inspectors return to its bombed facilities.
Iran's nuclear programme is ÔÇőone reason Israel and the U.S. have given for their current attacks on ÔÇőIran , arguing Ôüáthat it was getting too close to being able to produce a bomb, despite Trump saying in June that U.S. strikes had obliterated the programme. The IAEA has said it has no credible indication of a coordinated nuclear Ôüáweapons ÔÇőprogramme.
All three Iranian uranium-enrichment plants known to have been operating - two at ÔÇőNatanz and one at Fordow - were destroyed or badly damaged in June.
"There is an amount (of 60% uranium) in Natanz also, which ÔÇőwe believe is still there," Grossi said.

item.title: Airline shares battered, airfares surge as Iran war pushes oil above $100
item.description:
HONG KONG/FRANKFURT, March 9 (Reuters) - Airline stocks were hammered on ÔÇőMonday, while airfares soared as the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran sent oil prices surging, sparking fears of a deep travel slump and the potential for the widespread grounding ÔÇîof planes.
Oil prices jumped 15% to above $105 a barrel, hitting levels not seen since 2022 as some major producers cut supplies and fears of prolonged shipping disruptions gripped the market. At one point, Brent crude futures jumped as much as 29%.
Some jet fuel prices have doubled since the start of the conflict, piling pressure on carriers already navigating tight airspace as pilots reroute to avoid the Middle East conflict and thousands of stranded passengers try to leave the ÔÇőregion.
"Absent near-term relief, airlines around the world could be forced to ground thousands of aircraft while some of the industry's financially weakest carriers could halt operations," Deutsche analysts said ÔÇőin a note to clients.
In Asia, airline shares tumbled, with the worst-hit, including Korean Air Lines (003490.KS) , opens new tab , which slid 8.6%, Air ÔüáNew Zealand (AIR.NZ) , opens new tab down 7.8% and Hong Kong's Cathay Pacific (0293.HK) , opens new tab which dropped 5%.
In Europe, Air France KLM (AIRF.PA) , opens new tab , British Airways-owner IAG (ICAG.L) , opens new tab , Wizz Air (WIZZ.L) , opens new tab and Lufthansa (LHAG.DE) , opens new tab fell between 2.5% and 6% in morning trade.
Major U.S. ÔÇőairlines shares were down about 1% to 5% in afternoon trading with JetBlue Airways (JBLU.O) , opens new tab down 5.35% followed by American Airlines (AAL.O) , opens new tab down 3.44%.
Underscoring that pain on the consumer side were jumps in ticket prices. Direct ÔÇőflights from Seoul to London on March 11 with Korean Air Lines, for example, leapt to $4,359, from $564 seven days earlier, according to Google Flights data. Flights from Los Angeles to Lima on LATAM Airlines (LTM.SN) , opens new tab rose to $2,125 compared to $499 in the same period.
"The issue for the airlines now is that travel demand may be curtailed as costs become prohibitive for leisure travellers and as some companies start to limit business travel due to the uncertain outlook," said ÔÇőLorraine Tan, director of equity research, Asia at Morningstar.
The impact of high airfares could limit travel demand for much of 2026, Tan added.
Fuel is the second-largest expense for air carriers after labour, typically ÔÇőaccounting for a fifth to a quarter of operating expenses. Some major Asian and European airlines have oil hedging in place, but U.S. airlines largely stopped the practice over the last two decades.
"We assume the airlines are ÔÇîable to recapture Ôüáa portion of the spike in fuel prices, but it's hard to envision margin expansion this year barring a rapid decline in energy prices," said Tom Fitzgerald, TD Cowen vice president of equity research on six major U.S. airlines and Air Canada.
A one-way trip to Quebec City from Newark on March 11 aboard Air Canada nearly tripled to $1,499 compared to a week ago, according to data from Google Flights.
High prices could have severe implications for the industry.
"If crude is rising 20%, jet fuel is rising several times more as it is even more scarce, adding significant cost ÔÇőto operations together with crew resources, which are ÔÇőstretched due to longer flying times when Ôüáairspace is closed," said Subhas Menon, head of the Association of Asia Pacific Airlines.
The Deutsche analysts noted that a sharp spike in jet fuel costs in 2005 in the aftermath of hurricanes Katrina and Rita resulted in widespread and significant damage to the industry, including major airlines Delta and Northwest ÔÇőfiling for Chapter 11 bankruptcy that year.
Since February 28, when the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran started, through March 9, more than 40,000 flights to ÔÇőand from the Middle ÔüáEast have been cancelled, according to data from Cirium.
With airspace severely constrained, airlines have been forced to reroute flights, carry extra fuel or make additional refuelling stops to guard against sudden diversions or longer flight paths through safer corridors.
Combined, Emirates, Qatar Airways and Etihad normally fly about one-third of the passengers from Europe to Asia and more than half of all passengers from Europe to Australia, New Zealand and nearby Pacific ÔüáIslands, according to ÔÇőCirium.
Flights to Iraq, Syria, Lebanon and Jordan by Turkish Airlines (THYAO.IS) , opens new tab , AJet, Pegasus (PGSUS.IS) , opens new tab and SunExpress have been cancelled until March ÔÇő13, Turkish Transport Minister Abdulkadir Uraloglu said on Sunday.

item.title: Exclusive: Canadian oil tycoon backs South Bow pipeline to boost US exports
item.description:
CALGARY, March 9 (Reuters) - The Canadian tycoon who heads one of North America's fastest growing oil companies told Reuters he supports ÔÇőa proposal to potentially revive parts of the former Keystone XL pipeline and wants to see Canada use the project as ÔÇîleverage in upcoming trade negotiations with the United States.
Still, Adam Waterous, CEO of Strathcona Resources (SCR.TO) , opens new tab , said he would prefer a new West Coast pipeline to increase access to Asian markets.
The comments by Waterous, one of Canada's most aggressive oil industry dealmakers, are the first public statements by a Canadian oil shipper in favour of a new pipeline proposal led by Canadian company ÔÇőSouth Bow (SOBO.TO) , opens new tab that could increase the country's crude exports to the U.S. by more than 12%.
They are an early indication that the project ÔÇö which would require both ÔÇőa green light from U.S. President Donald Trump and the construction of additional links to U.S. refining hubs ÔÇö has at Ôüáleast some level of commercial support from Canadian oil sands producers at a time when the U.S.-Israel war on Iran has disrupted international supply and sent global ÔÇőcrude prices soaring.
The plan involves a different route through the U.S. than the previous Keystone XL pipeline, which was canceled by former U.S. President Joe Biden ÔÇőin 2021 after years of Indigenous and environmental opposition, but would use some of that project's already built pipe in Alberta.
South Bow launched a competitive bidding process last week offering 450,000 barrels per day of contracted pipeline capacity from Hardisty, Alberta to multiple U.S. delivery points including Cushing, Oklahoma, and destinations on the U.S. Gulf Coast, to gauge potential commercial use.
Waterous ÔÇődeclined to say if Strathcona will commit barrels to the South Bow project, citing ongoing commercial negotiations. But he pointed to his company's stated goal to expand ÔÇőits own oil production from 125,000 barrels per day in 2026 to up to 300,000 bpd by 2035.
"I've been a very early advocate of this project," Waterous said.
"And we ÔÇîare looking Ôüáfor egress now," he said, referring to oil shippers' ability to transport their product to market.
But Waterous said the South Bow proposal is not the Canadian oil industry's first choice for bolstering crude exports. Currently more than 90% of Canada's oil exports go to the United States, and Waterous said Canadian oil shippers have long wanted greater market diversification.
Canadian oil companies would prefer another pipeline to the Pacific, to supplement the existing Trans Mountain pipeline which runs from Alberta to the British Columbia coast ÔÇőand offers an outlet to Asian ÔÇőmarkets, Waterous said.
Four other large Canadian Ôüáoil sands producers did not respond to a Reuters request for comment on whether they support the South Bow proposal.
The Alberta government has said it will submit an application for a new crude oil pipeline to the west ÔÇőcoast for fast-track approval by the federal government, but no private company has said it will build the project.
The ÔÇőmain benefit to Ôüáthe South Bow proposal, Waterous said, is that it can likely be built faster and more cheaply than a new West Coast pipeline, while also carrying potential political benefits for Canada.
He said Canada should try to leverage Trump's previously stated comments that he would like to see the Keystone XL project built.
Prime Minister Mark Carney has also raised with ÔüáTrump the prospect of ÔÇőreviving the Keystone XL oil pipeline from Alberta to the United States as part of ÔÇőhis efforts to ease trade tensions between the two countries.
"The utility of a pipeline to the south is that it is a tool to achieve a tariff agreement to protect principally Canada's steel, ÔÇőauto and aluminum sectors, while also allowing the Canadian oil sector to grow," Waterous said.

item.title: Peru's pipeline disruption tests industrial resilience; businesses blanch at remote work order
item.description:
LIMA, March 9 (Reuters) - Peru's business groups are clashing with the new interim government over sweeping emergency measures imposed after a rupture in the country's ÔÇőmain naturalÔÇĹgas pipeline triggered the worst energy crisis in two decades, an early test of President Jose BalcazarÔÇÖs ÔÇîinterim mandate weeks before national elections.
The breakdown of Transportadora de Gas del Peru's pipeline, the backbone of the system that supplies nearly half of PeruÔÇÖs electricity and most of its LPG, has forced widespread gas rationing, pushing up energy prices and exposing longÔÇĹstanding vulnerabilities in the Andean nation's energy ÔÇősystem.
Balcazar, appointed on February 18 after Congress ousted his predecessor, now faces a highÔÇĹstakes leadership test. More than ÔÇőa thousand firms have struggled with halted gas production, and business associations have called for the rollback Ôüáof emergency directives.
Authorities say gas flows may not resume until midÔÇĹMarch.
The outage could stoke inflation, which has remained among the ÔÇőlowest in the world despite years of political turmoil.
"It can hit inflation, but in a heterogeneous way depending on how energy ÔÇőaffects each activity,ÔÇŁ said former Economy Minister Alonso Segura.
Inflation reached 0.69% in February and could exceed the 0.81% recorded in March last year, Segura said.
Natural gas is used to produce 70% of the LPG consumed domestically. Some 2.1 million households rely on piped gas and about one million vehicles ÔÇőrun on natural gas or LPG.
Peru is the worldÔÇÖs third-largest copper producer.
Former Energy and Mines Minister Carlos Herrera said the rupture ÔÇőunderscored PeruÔÇÖs vulnerability after an alternative pipeline project was canceled in 2017 when the Brazilian construction company Odebrecht was unable to secure financing ÔÇîwhile being Ôüáinvestigated in a case involving bribes paid to secure public works contracts in Peru.
"This crisis highlights the need to diversify the countryÔÇÖs energy matrix," Herrera said. "The serious problem we have is that we havenÔÇÖt made exploratory efforts for natural gas over time either."
Industry groups said more than 1,000 companies have been affected, with some halting production. Felipe James, head of the National Society of Industry (SNI), ÔÇősaid emergency rules allowing firms ÔÇőto switch temporarily to alternative Ôüáfuels will be costly and not all companies are equipped to make the change.
"Not all can switch... they are not prepared," James said.
On Friday, the government ordered public employees in Lima to ÔÇőwork remotely for a week and encouraged private firms to do the same. Schools and ÔÇőuniversities will shift Ôüáto virtual classes during the contingency.
Business associations criticized the measures, saying they amount to a partial shutdown of the economy.
"Stopping the country can never be the solution. We demand this decision be reversed," said COMEX, the foreign trade guild.
Peru's mining and energy chamber said it Ôüáwas working "intensely" ÔÇőto mitigate the crisis and seeking to import LPG in "record time."
Spokespeople for ÔÇőthe presidency and the Ministry of Energy and Mines did not respond to a request for comment.
The TGP gas pipeline began operating in 2004 and also ÔÇőenables the export of natural gas to Japan, South Korea and China.

item.title: Two men charged with terrorism after homemade bomb thrown at anti-Islam protesters in New York
item.description:
NEW YORK, March 9 (Reuters) - Two men accused of igniting homemade bombs at a chaotic anti-Islam protest outside the New York City mayor's mansion told police they were inspired by Islamic State, the Islamist militant group, and were charged on Monday with ÔÇőfederal terrorism-related crimes, according to court records.
Emir Balat, 18, and Ibrahim Kayumi, 19, are accused of traveling from Pennsylvania with ÔÇîdangerous improvised explosive devices to Manhattan on Saturday, where a small group of far-right activists had planned the anti-Muslim rally outside Gracie Mansion against Zohran Mamdani, New York City's first Muslim mayor.
Balat was captured in photographs and video lighting a small IED and throwing it towards the anti-Muslim protesters before lighting a second device ÔÇőhanded to him by Kayumi and dropping it, according to the criminal complaint. Police were able to intercept the smoking devices, ÔÇőduct-taped jars containing screws and bolts and the explosive substance TATP, and no one was hurt.
After police arrested Ôüáthem, they said they were inspired by Islamic State, and Balat told police that he wanted to carry out an attack "even bigger" than ÔÇőthe Boston Marathon bombing in 2013 that killed three people, according to the criminal complaint filed in the U.S. district court in Manhattan on ÔÇőMonday.
Mehdi Essmidi, who represents Balat, did not immediately reply to a request for comment. Kayumi's lawyer Michael Arthus declined to comment.
Speaking alongside police outside Gracie Mansion on Monday, Mamdani condemned the violence. He and his wife were not home at the time, because they had known about the "Stop the Muslim takeover" rally organized by ÔÇőfar-right activist Jake Lang more than a week in advance. More than 100 counter-protesters gathered nearby, the vast majority peaceful, saying they were there ÔÇőto "run Nazis out" of New York.
"New York City will never tolerate violence whether from protests or counter-protests," Mamdani told reporters. He said while he thought the ÔÇîfar-right anti-Muslim Ôüárally was "vile", he defended the "sacred right" of protest, even for objectionable speech.
Police said it was the first time an improvised bomb was deployed in New York City in nearly a decade. In 2017, Akayed Ullah, who also cited Islamic State as inspiration, ignited a homemade bomb strapped to his body in a passage near the Times Square subway station, wounding only himself.
Balat and Kayumi are each charged with five federal crimes, ÔÇőaccording to a complaint filed by ÔÇőthe U.S. attorney's office in Manhattan, Ôüáincluding use of a weapon of mass destruction, attempting to provide material support to a designated foreign terrorist organization, and three charges related to the illegal possession or transportation of explosives.
During their separate police interviews, ÔÇőBalat asked for a piece of paper and wrote on it he had pledged allegiance to Islamic ÔÇőState, while Kayumi Ôüátold police he watched Islamic State videos on his phone, according to the criminal complaint.
A third man, Ian McGinnis, was arrested and appeared in a New York state court on Sunday accused of squirting pepper spray or a similar chemical irritant into the faces of two of the counter-protesters opposing Ôüáthe anti-Islam ÔÇőrally, according to the Manhattan district attorney's office and police. McGinnis faces six charges, including ÔÇőthird-degree assault.
Police in New York have been on alert since the U.S. and Israel attacked Iran, but did not say there was a sign of Saturday's attack being linked ÔÇőto the war.

item.title: Trump says war against Iran is 'very complete,' CBS News reports
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 9 (Reuters) - U.S. President Donald Trump ÔÇőthinks the war against Iran "is ÔÇîvery complete" and that Washington was "very far ahead" of his initial four ÔÇőto five week estimated ÔÇőtime frame, according to a CBS News Ôüáreporter on X, who cited an ÔÇőinterview with him.
"I think the ÔÇőwar is very complete, pretty much. They have no navy, no communications, theyÔÇÖve ÔÇőgot no Air Force," Trump ÔÇőwas quoted as saying by CBS News' ÔÇîWhite ÔüáHouse correspondent Weijia Jiang.
Trump also said the U.S. is "very far" ahead of his initial 4-5 ÔÇőweek estimated ÔÇőtime frame Ôüáfor the war.
On Iran's new Supreme leader Mojtaba ÔÇőKhamenei, Trump told CBS ÔÇőNews: "I Ôüáhave no message for him." Trump said he has someone in Ôüámind ÔÇőto replace Khamenei, ÔÇőbut did not elaborate.

item.title: First Brands row hints at banksÔÇÖ shadow exposure
item.description:
NEW YORK, March 9 (Reuters Breakingviews) - Cockroach troubles tend to extend further than they first appear. JPMorgan boss Jamie Dimon likened high-profile credit ÔÇőblowups to the sneaky insects, implying that deeper problems lurk. And now, six months after heavily indebted auto-parts retailer ÔÇîFirst Brands toppled, regional lender Western Alliance (WAL.N) , opens new tab has disclosed , opens new tab a $126 million hit from the mess. It never lent directly to First Brands, but instead to a fund run by Wall Street financiers at Jefferies (JEF.N) , opens new tab . ItÔÇÖs just one of many smaller banks piling into the murky world of shadow finance.
First BrandsÔÇÖ surprise bankruptcy last ÔÇőyear sent a chill through credit markets. Federal prosecutors alleged , opens new tab that it pledged assets multiple times over to secure billions of ÔÇőborrowings, largely from nonÔÇĹbank lenders. It was not an isolated case, with the government also indicting , opens new tab the Ôüáfounder of sub-prime auto lender Tricolor on similar claims. Jefferies, meanwhile, also lent to now-teetering real-estate lender Market Financial Solutions.
Western Alliance claims a ÔÇődeliberate breach of contract, saying that Jefferies has ceased to pay $126 million outstanding on loans to special-purpose vehicles that held First BrandsÔÇÖ ÔÇőaccounts receivable. Jefferies said , opens new tab on Monday that these are non-recourse loans, with no guarantee of payment from the SPVsÔÇÖ parents, and that the suit lacks merit.
Whatever the case, shares of Western Alliance and Jefferies have tanked 11% and 14%, respectively, since Thursday. Morgan Stanley analysts downgraded their recommendation on JefferiesÔÇÖ shares, citing ÔÇőthe litigation.
Western Alliance is a relative pipsqueak, at roughly $8 billion in market value. Yet many similarly sized institutions have been swept up ÔÇőin the flood of lending to lightly regulated firms that the Federal Reserve labels ÔÇťnon-depository financial institutions.ÔÇŁ Lending to these shadow banks has climbed to $1.9 trillion, ÔÇîup 180% Ôüáfrom $680 billion in 2021, according to Fed data. This growth tracks the vertiginous rise of private credit and asset-backed lending , which have increasingly displaced traditional banks since the 2008 financial crisis. Providing leverage is a way to get a cut of the action back.
The question is how much risk this poses. BanksÔÇÖ equity is a loss-absorbing buffer, taking the hit if loans go awry en masse. Yet 40 ÔÇőU.S. lenders with a combined $4.7 ÔÇőtrillion in assets now have Ôüálent the equivalent of more than 100% of their total equity capital to shadow banks, up from 29 banks holding $1.1 trillion two years ago, according to data from KBRA Financial Intelligence. Nearly all borrowers ÔÇőare current on their payments, and much of the activity relates to the mortgage market, which ÔÇőenjoys effective government Ôüásupport. Yet the territory in which cockroaches can breed is growing.
Follow Stephen Gandel on LinkedIn , opens new tab and X , opens new tab .

item.title: Iran war boosts oil price, but oil major shares are stuck on the sidelines
item.description:
LONDON/HOUSTON, March 9 (Reuters) - Oil prices on Monday climbed to the highest level since 2022, but shares of major producers like Shell (SHEL.L) , opens new tab ÔÇőand Exxon Mobil (XOM.N) , opens new tab have so far seen limited gains since the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran broke out late last month, ÔÇîsuggesting that traders think the pain in the market may be short term.
While crude oil futures have risen more than 40% since Israel and the U.S. launched joint airstrikes on February 28, the iShares Global Energy ETF - which tracks global energy companies - is up around 2%. That suggests that any immediate gains from increased prices will be temporary, ÔÇőor offset by reduced production.
"The market is anticipating a swift end to the closure of the Strait of Hormuz and a subsequent ÔÇőcollapse in oil prices back to normalized levels," said James West, head of energy and power research at Melius ÔüáResearch. "The rally in oil prices is primarily contained to near-term spot prices rather than longer-dated crude oil futures."
Brent crude futures and U.S. West Texas Intermediate ÔÇőcrude futures rose by around 30% to start the week, as the war on Iran has disrupted the passage of traffic in the Strait of Hormuz, ÔÇőoil and natural gas exports from the Middle East and has forced production stoppages.
At the same time, Brent's front-month contract hit an all-time high at around $36 per barrel over its 6-month delivery contract, indicating near-term supply tightness.
The rally conjured oil futures' 2008 all-time highs at around $147 per barrel, when tension between the West and Iran over its nuclear program, ÔÇőa weak U.S. dollar and inflationary factors pushed prices higher. Continued growth in China and other emerging economies was also expected to offset the ÔÇőimpact of any fall in consumption in developed countries.
However, just months after the all-time high, the 2008 financial crisis and concerns around an overbought market sunk oil to ÔÇîbelow $40 per Ôüábarrel, at the time the lowest since 2004.
"Go back to 2008," said David Hewitt, senior consultant at Hewitt Energy Perspectives. "Oil stocks followed crude's rally until about $100 then almost completely disconnected as it went to $147."
"The market was right then - $147 per barrel rapidly became $30," he added. "It's a bit of the same now."
Shell shares rose as much as 4.9% on Monday from their close on Feb. 27, while Chevron (CVX.N) , opens new tab gained 2.6% and Exxon gained 0.9% over the same period. ÔÇőBP (BP.L) , opens new tab gained 7.8%.
Deferred oil contracts are ÔÇőnot as high as the Ôüáspot price, signaling that investors do not expect the supply disruption to last, said Simon Lack, portfolio manager of the Catalyst Energy Infrastructure Fund.
U.S. shale producers, which have seen a bigger boost in share prices than the global ÔÇőoil majors, may be benefitting because U.S. energy supply and infrastructure are safe and insulated from the risk, ÔÇőprompting investors to place Ôüáa higher value on those companies, he added.
Diamondback (FANG.O) , opens new tab , the largest independent producer in the Permian basin, has seen its shares gain as much as 7% on Monday from their close on February 27.
Simon Wong, a portfolio manager at Gabelli Funds, said share prices of Diamondback, APA Corp (APA.O) , opens new tab and Occidental (OXY.N) , opens new tab had risen less Ôüáyear-to-date compared with ÔÇőExxon and Chevron and are now beginning to catch up.
He also believes that the ÔÇőhigher crude prices are temporary and will likely moderate when the disruptions are over.

item.title: Surging oil drives worries for US stock investors
item.description:
NEW YORK, March 9 (Reuters) - A stunning surge in oil prices above $100 a barrel is rattling U.S. stock investors, as they brace for an even sharper rise in energy prices that could stagger the economy and further damage equities.
The 50% jump in U.S. crude to its highest ÔÇőlevel in more than three years is among the most significant financial consequences of the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran, as investors continue to calibrate economic and market fallout following the ÔÇîattacks launched just over a week ago.
"It's a shock," said Michael Reynolds, vice president of investment strategy at Glenmede. "It's out of left field, and people and investors are having to figure out what it means in real time as it's unfolding."
Higher oil and gas prices stand to drive up costs for energy-intensive companies, erode the discretionary spending budgets of consumers and lead to worries about inflation that could stop the Federal Reserve from lowering interest rates.
Reynolds and other investors were scrambling to ÔÇőgame out scenarios should oil reach heights that were not on the horizon just days earlier.
U.S. and Brent crude on Monday both broke above $100 a barrel, which investors have pointed to as a ÔÇőlevel that could create more stock turbulence. The commodities at one point on Monday neared $120. U.S. crude settled at $67.02 on February 27, the last session before the ÔüáU.S.-Israeli strikes.
Meanwhile, stock volatility has spiked. The Cboe Volatility Index (.VIX) , opens new tab on Monday topped 30 for the first time in nearly a year, up from below 20 in late February.
While the declines for U.S. stocks have been moderate ÔÇőin comparison to other global regions, the benchmark S&P 500 (.SPX) , opens new tab was last down nearly 4% from its late January all-time closing high, after paring steeper declines earlier in Monday's session.
Strategists at Yardeni Research said last Tuesday they ÔÇőexpected a 10% stock market correction.
"Now we can't rule out a bear market and even a recession," the firm said in a note on Sunday.
OIL AND STOCKS: MORE TIGHTLY LINKED
As oil prices have surged, the moves have become more tightly linked to the stock market.
The 20-day correlation between the S&P 500 and U.S. crude stood at -0.813 as of Monday morning, according to LSEG data, a strong inverse relationship that shows they have been tending to move in opposite directions.
Deutsche Bank strategists, watching whether the ÔÇőIran situation could prompt a larger risk-off move, said in a note on Monday that the oil price shock ranks "among the more serious of history," but that investors are pricing in "a short rather than protracted conflict."
While stocks ÔÇőand oil are typically thought of as separate markets, stock investors have watched oil's trajectory quite closely at times historically, especially after extreme price moves.
In early 2022, a jump in oil over $120 a barrel following the start of the conflict ÔÇîin Ukraine coincided Ôüáwith declines in stock prices. In 2015-2016, stock investors worried that low oil prices, with U.S. crude dropping below $30 a barrel, represented a sign of broad economic weakness.
Focus is again on the economic ramifications of rising oil prices.
Each 10% increase in the oil price stands to translate to roughly a 15 to 20 basis-point drag on GDP growth, according to JPMorgan economists.
"Effects also could be non-linear, with larger oil price spikes producing an even larger hit to growth," the JPMorgan economists said in a note.
The JPMorgan economists and other analysts cautioned the economic fallout likely hinges on how long crude prices stay elevated.
For now, the jump in ÔÇőcrude is driving up prices at the gas pump. ÔÇőThe national average for gasoline rose to $3.478 a Ôüágallon on Monday, up from $2.902 a month ago, according to data from the motorists' group AAA. That is the highest level since the summer of 2024, the group said.
"From an average consumer perspective, oil prices are about ... as visceral as it gets just because of filling up your gas tank," said Kevin Gordon, head of macro ÔÇőresearch and strategy at Charles Schwab.
Indeed, shares of companies most reliant on discretionary spending are among the potentially most vulnerable to higher oil prices.
Airlines, for which ÔÇőfuel accounts for 20% to Ôüá25% of unit costs, according to Morningstar, have seen their shares hammered with the S&P 1500 passenger airlines index (.SPCOMAIR) , opens new tab down 15% since the conflict began.
Of course, higher oil prices could benefit some corners of the market. The S&P 500 energy sector (.SPNY) , opens new tab has gained 1% since late February, while the broader S&P 500 has fallen over 2% in that time.
And some investors are wary that the situation could change at any moment.
In a note Ôüáon Monday, Raymond ÔÇőJames Chief Investment Officer Larry Adam said the wealth management firm expects the conflict to be "relatively short-lived." The firm maintained a year-end ÔÇőprice target for U.S. crude at $55-$60 a barrel.
Investors are also mindful of how U.S. President Donald Trump has changed course on market-sensitive policies during his term. In particular, there was his softening of his blanket "Liberation Day" tariff policies last April that caused a sharp rebound in ÔÇőinitially battered asset prices.
"We're one ceasefire agreement headline away from all of this just reversing in a very aggressive fashion," Gordon said.

item.title: Australia to grant visa to five Iranian women soccer players after Trump appeal
item.description:
SYDNEY, March 10 (Reuters) - Australia on Tuesday agreed to provide ÔÇővisas to five Iranian ÔÇîwomen's soccer players to stay in the country, after ÔÇőU.S. President Donald Trump ÔÇőurged Canberra not to Ôüásend them back to ÔÇőTehran due to concerns ÔÇőover their safety.
Home Affairs Minister Tony Burke told reporters the other ÔÇőmembers of the ÔÇőteam were welcome to stay back ÔÇîin ÔüáAustralia. The team is in Australia for the Asian Cup tournament.
Trump said ÔÇőon Monday ÔÇőhe Ôüáhad spoken to Australian Prime Minister ÔÇőAnthony Albanese about the ÔÇőIran Ôüáwomen's soccer team after reports that five players Ôüáhad ÔÇősought asylum ÔÇőin Australia.

item.title: US weighs easing Russian oil sanctions to cool global price surge, sources say
item.description:
March 9 (Reuters) - President Donald Trump 's administration is considering reducing oil sanctions on Russia to help cool ÔÇőa surge in global energy prices triggered by ÔÇîthe U.S. and Israeli war on Iran , with an announcement possible as soon as Monday, according to three sources familiar with the planning.
The ÔÇőmove would be intended to boost world supplies ÔÇőof oil amid massive disruptions to Middle East shipments Ôüáfrom the expanding conflict, but could also complicate U.S. ÔÇőefforts to deprive Russia of revenue for its war in ÔÇőUkraine.
The discussions could include broad sanctions relief as well as more targeted options that would allow certain countries, such as India, to ÔÇőbuy Russian oil without fear of U.S. penalties, including ÔÇőtariffs, the sources told Reuters, speaking on condition of anonymity.
The United States ÔÇîlast Ôüáweek allowed India to temporarily purchase Russian crude oil already on tankers at sea to help it cope with the cuts to Middle East supply.
The new moves could be ÔÇőannounced as soon ÔÇőas Monday, Ôüáthe sources said.
"President Trump and his entire energy team have had a strong game ÔÇőplan to keep the energy markets stable well ÔÇőbefore ÔüáOperation Epic Fury began, and they will continue to review all credible options," said Taylor Rogers, a White House spokesperson, Ôüáusing ÔÇőthe administration's term for the war.
"Any ÔÇőpolicy announcement will come directly from the President or his team," Rogers said.

item.title: Kremlin: Putin, in a phone call with Trump, shares┬áproposals to end Iran war quickly
item.description:
MOSCOW, March 9 (Reuters) - Russian President Vladimir Putin, in a telephone conversation on Monday with ÔÇőU.S. President Donald Trump , put forward proposals for a quick ÔÇîsettlement of the conflict around Iran , Kremlin foreign policy aide Yuri Ushakov said.
Ushakov, giving an account of the conversation to reporters, said the two ÔÇőleaders also discussed the conflict in Ukraine and noted that ÔÇőRussian gains should prompt Kyiv's negotiators to move Ôüátowards a settlement.
He described the entire discussion as "very substantial" and ÔÇősaid it would "likely have practical significance for further work between ÔÇőthe two countries".
Ushakov said Putin "expressed several thoughts aimed at a quick political and diplomatic end to the Iranian conflict, including contacts that have taken ÔÇőplace with leaders of Gulf states, the president of ÔÇőIran and leaders of other countries".
He said Trump "offered his assessment of developments ÔÇîin Ôüáthe U.S.-Israeli operation. Let me say that a very substantial and, without doubt, useful exchange of ideas took place."
Ushakov said Trump believed it was in the U.S. interest to see a "rapid end ÔÇőto the ÔÇőconflict in Ukraine Ôüáwith a ceasefire and a long-term settlement".
Developments in Ukraine, he said, were characterised by "Russian troops ÔÇőadvancing quite successfully. This, as was noted, is ÔÇőa Ôüáfactor that should prompt the regime in Kyiv to finally go down the path of negotiating a settlement to the conflict".
Ushakov Ôüáalso ÔÇősaid the two presidents also discussed ÔÇőthe situation in Venezuela in the context of global oil markets.

item.title: Iranian women's soccer players given asylum after Trump speaks with Australian PM
item.description:
WASHINGTON/SYDNEY, March 10 (Reuters) - Australia on Tuesday agreed to provide visas to five of Iran's women's soccer team players to ÔÇőstay in the country after they sought asylum fearing persecution in their home nation.
The announcement came after U.S. President Donald Trump said he had ÔÇîspoken to Australian Prime Minister Anthony Albanese about the Iran women's soccer team in Australia following reports that the players had requested asylum.
The remaining players are in a hotel on the Gold Coast, Home Affairs Minister Tony Burke told a news conference, adding that he had also offered the other team members the chance to stay in Australia.
"I was able to tell ÔÇőfive members of the Iranian women's soccer team that they are welcome to stay in Australia, that they are safe here, and they should ÔÇőfeel at home here," Burke told reporters.
The Iranian women's soccer team are in Australia for the Asian Cup tournament.
Iran's semi-official ÔüáFars news agency said five players in Australia for the Asian Cup secretly left the team hotel with Australian police, and media reports said they were ÔÇőseeking Australian government assistance after "breaking free".
Having initially posted on social media that Australia was "making a terrible humanitarian mistake" by allowing the team to be sent back home, Trump said in a ÔÇőlater post that he had spoken to Albanese and that the Australian leader was "doing a very good job having to do with this rather delicate situation".
Trump said five members of the Iran squad "have already been taken care of, and the rest are on their way.
"Some, however, feel they must go back because they are worried about the safety of their families, ÔÇőincluding threats to those family members if they donÔÇÖt return," he said.
The Iranian team's campaign in the Australian-hosted Asian Cup tournament started just as the U.S. and ÔÇőIsrael launched air strikes on Iran , killing the Islamic Republic's Supreme Leader Ali Khamenei. They were eliminated from the tournament on Sunday after losing 2-0 to the Philippines .
In his earlier post Trump said members of ÔÇîthe team Ôüáwould "likely be killed" if forced to return to Iran. "The U.S. will take them if you wonÔÇÖt, " he added.
Soccer's global players' union FIFPRO said on Monday there were serious concerns for the welfare of the Iran team after they were labelled for refusing to sing their national anthem before a game.
The players' decision to stand in silence during Iran's anthem before their first match against South Korea was labelled by a commentator on Islamic Republic of Iran Broadcasting as the "pinnacle of dishonour".
Iranian ÔÇőmedia quoted Farideh Shojaei, vice president for ÔÇőwomen's affairs at the Iranian Football ÔüáFederation, as saying the team had left the hotel through the back door with the police.
"We have contacted the embassy, the football federation, the foreign ministry and anywhere possible to see what will happen," she said. "We have even spoken with ÔÇőthe families of these five players.ÔÇŁ
Iranian media named the players as Zahra Sarbali, Mona Hamoudi, Zahra Ghanbari, Fatemeh Pasandideh ÔÇőand Atefeh Ramazani-Zadeh.
It said Ôüáother team members were still in Australia and quoted Shojaei as saying the squad had planned to return to Iran via Dubai, but the United Arab Emirates had not allowed them to do so.
It said efforts were now expected to be made for the team to return via Malaysia and Turkey.
The Iranian team sang their national anthem and saluted Ôüábefore their second ÔÇőmatch against Australia , sparking fears among human rights campaigners that the women had been coerced by ÔÇőgovernment minders.
Australia granted emergency humanitarian visas to over 20 members of the Afghanistan women's cricket team after the Taliban returned to power in 2021.

item.title: Cuban university students stage rare protest amid energy, internet shortages
item.description:
HAVANA, March 9 (Reuters) - A group of just over 20 Cuban university students staged a rare, hours-long protest at ÔÇőthe University of Havana on Monday, angry over class disruptions amid a U.S. oil blockade that has contributed to ÔÇîa near collapse of Cuba's electrical grid.
Students sat under a hot mid-morning sun, shading themselves with umbrellas while trading barbs with several of the university's professors and administration staff, who arrived shortly after the "sit-in" began.
"None of us wanted to get up early today in the dark, make a thermos of coffee, and sit ÔÇőhere in the sun," said Anabel Oliva, a 20-year-old university student who spoke before the group. "But there was no other ÔÇőway."
Students said officials had failed to take adequate measures to prevent major disruptions to classes as the ÔüáUnited States has ratcheted up sanctions and pressure on the Caribbean island nation.
The United States in January cut off oil shipments from ÔÇőVenezuela, which further decimated an already failing electrical grid, starving it of fuel and forcing ripple effects on transportation, food supply and internet ÔÇőcommunications.
Cuba announced a series of measures in January, including distance learning as during the COVID-19 pandemic, that officials said would assure continuity for students of the island's once-robust higher-education system.
Students said those measures were not enough, and opted to protest to ensure their voices were heard.
Jose Julian Diaz, a university professor and ÔÇőformer student representative, told Reuters the extraordinary circumstances required dialogue.
"These kinds of scenarios are really rare," he said in an interview. "I think we ÔÇőhave to learn to have these discussions with students."
University security initially prohibited the protesting students from sitting on the stone steps at the main entrance ÔÇîof the ÔüáUniversity of Havana, blocking the entrance with a long rope and a sign that read: "Do not pass, do not sit."
The students eventually walked up the steps and past the rope, however, prompting a rush of security guards who were eventually called off by Higher Education Vice Minister Modesto Ricardo Gomez.
The vice minister told reporters the students had raised legitimate concerns, and blamed the ÔÇőTrump administration's tightening sanctions for ÔÇőthe problems. He said there Ôüáwould be no repercussions for those who protested.
"There are no consequences whatsoever. They have a concern, and we are addressing it. It will always be like this," Gomez said.
While the minister spoke to reporters, ÔÇőthe students left the steps and entered the university with administration officials and teachers, declining requests for ÔÇőinterviews.
The minister said Ôüáthe dialogue would continue inside the university.
One student, who did not give his name to a reporter but spoke before cameras, said there would be more protests if the issues were not resolved.
"We are willing to engage in dialogue if it is to represent our demands, but if Ôüádialogue, as ÔÇőhas already happened, does not work, we will sit down here again.ÔÇŁ
Protests of any ÔÇőkind in Communist-run Cuba are exceedingly rare. Cuba's 2019 constitution grants citizens the right to protest, but a law more specifically defining that right is stalled in the ÔÇőlegislature, leaving those who take to the street in legal limbo.

item.title: Trading Day: Wall Street roars back on war resolution hopes
item.description:
ORLANDO, Florida, March 9 (Reuters) - Stocks in Asia and Europe tumbled on Monday as the Iran war entered its second week and oil prices surged as much as 30% above $100 a barrel. But Wall Street rallied and oil later sank after President Donald Trump indicated the war may soon be over.
In my column today I put ÔÇőWall Street's resilience under the microscope. As selling snowballs across other equity markets, why has the global avalanche not yet engulfed U.S. ÔÇîstocks? Are there reasonable explanations, or is complacency setting in?
If you have more time to read, here are a few articles I recommend to help you make sense of what happened in markets today.
Today's Key Market Moves
Today's Talking Points
* Central bank paralysis
There can be no doubt now - the eye-watering surge in oil prices since the U.S. and Israel launched their joint attack on Iran on February 28 has put central banks in an unenviable bind. Price pressures are clearly intensifying, but the impact of $100 a barrel oil on economic activity will be ÔÇőharsh.
Do they hike rates to nip inflation in the bud, or adopt a more dovish stance in the face of rising unemployment and potential recession? In ÔÇőthe U.S., the labor market was already creaking, household savings are run down, and now gas prices are soaring. Higher borrowing costs will hurt the average consumer. But so will ÔÇîhigher inflation.
* ÔüáPre-war price pressures
Even before the Middle East crisis erupted, price pressures around the world were bubbling up. Figures on Monday showed that consumer inflation in China jumped in February to the highest in three years, Mexico 's inflation rose above the central bank's target, and real wages in Japan rose for the first time in 13 months.
With oil smashing through $100 a barrel - and now up considerably on the same period last year - inflation signals are only pointing one way. U.S. PCE inflation figures for February due ÔÇőout later this week are expected to ÔÇőfollow a similar path, rising further Ôüáabove 3%.
* To tap or not to tap?
Countries around the world are suddenly scrambling for ways to cushion the economic blow of $100 oil. Releasing emergency oil reserves is an obvious option, but it seems like it's not going to be exercised ÔÇőjust yet. G7 countries discussed it on Monday but say there's no immediate supply shortfall. Not yet, anyway.
Elsewhere, China has capped ÔÇőfuel prices, South Korea is Ôüáconsidering a similar move for the first time in 30 years, and Japan is preparing for a possible release of crude and could draw down cash reserves set aside for emergency spending. Authorities will be reluctant to raise interest rates, so they may need to get inventive.
What could move markets tomorrow?
Want to receive Trading Day in your inbox every weekday morning? Sign up for my newsletter here .
Opinions expressed are those of the author. They do not reflect the views of Reuters News, which, ÔÇőunder the Trust Principles , opens new tab , is committed to integrity, independence, and freedom from bias.

item.title: US considering selling oil from strategic reserve, US energy chief says
item.description:
PLATTEVILLE, Colorado, March 9 (Reuters) - The U.S. is considering coordinating ÔÇősales of oil from the U.S. Strategic Petroleum Reserve with ÔÇîreleases from other countries as prices soar during the war on Iran , Energy Secretary Chris Wright said on Monday.
Wright also said the U.S. has "some other options" on allowing ÔÇőmore sales of Russian oil held in tankers on the ÔÇőwater in Asia. Late last week, for instance, Washington issued Ôüáa 30-day waiver allowing the sale of Russian crude currently ÔÇőstranded at sea to continue to India.
"We are talking about coordinated releases ÔÇőfrom the SPR," Wright told reporters gathered at a natural gas plant in Colorado. The U.S. SPR , located on the coasts of Texas and Louisiana, holds 415 million ÔÇőbarrels of oil, more than what the entire world uses in ÔÇőfour days.
Global and domestic oil futures settled on Monday at the highest level ÔÇîsince ÔüáAugust 2022 as the war led by the U.S. and Israel stranded tankers and shut in oil production in the Middle East.
The International Energy Agency, the West's energy watchdog, on Monday called for a coordinated ÔÇőrelease of oil, ÔÇőand G7 countries Ôüáagreed to closely monitor developments in the energy markets. No release has yet been announced.
Fatih Birol, the ÔÇőIEA head, told G7 finance ministers on Monday that ÔÇőhis Ôüágroup's members hold more than 1.2 billion barrels of public emergency oil stocks and a further 600 million barrels of industry stocks are under Ôüágovernment ÔÇőobligation.
The U.S. was not considering imposing restrictions ÔÇőon exports of U.S. energy as a way to control prices, Wright said.

item.title: Canadian PM, Qatari emir discuss diplomacy to prevent wider war in Middle East
item.description:
March 9 (Reuters) - Canadian Prime Minister Mark Carney spoke with Qatar's Emir Sheikh ÔÇőTamim bin Hamad al-Thani on Monday about avoiding a wider ÔÇîconflict in the Middle East , Carney's office said.
Here are some details:
ÔÇó They "underscored the importance of intensifying diplomatic engagement to avoid a wider conflict with ÔÇőglobal consequences," Carney's office said in a statement.
ÔÇó The statement ÔÇősaid the leaders condemned Iranian missile and drone attacks Ôüáon Qatar and Carney expressed Canada's solidarity with Qatar.
ÔÇó The U.S. ÔÇőand Israel began attacking Iran on February 28. Iran responded with its ÔÇőown strikes against Israel and Gulf states with U.S. bases. U.S. President Donald Trump has thus far offered shifting objectives and timelines for the war that ÔÇőhas killed scores in Iran , including Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei .
ÔÇó "In accordance with ÔÇőinternational law, the leaders called for an immediate end to attacks on civilians ÔÇîand Ôüácivilian infrastructure that only serve to destabilise the region and worsen the global economic and energy situation," Carney's office said, adding he and the Qatari emir discussed efforts to de-escalate the Iran war .
ÔÇó ÔÇőAt least 1,230 ÔÇőpeople have been Ôüákilled in Iran, including 175 schoolgirls and staff killed in a missile strike on a primary ÔÇőschool in southern Iran, according to non-profit humanitarian ÔÇőgroup Iranian ÔüáRed Crescent Society.
ÔÇó The Iranian army additionally says over 100 were killed after a U.S. submarine sank an Iranian warship off Sri ÔüáLanka's ÔÇőcoast last week.
ÔÇó Eleven civilians have been ÔÇőkilled in Israel, according to Israeli tallies. The U.S. military has said seven of ÔÇőits service members have been killed.

item.title: Alexander brothers, top NY real estate brokers, convicted at sex trafficking trial
item.description:
NEW YORK, March 9 (Reuters) - Three ÔÇőwealthy brothers, including two of New York's top real estate brokers, were found ÔÇîguilty on Monday at a closely watched sex trafficking trial over allegations they lured women and girls to exclusive parties where they drugged and raped them.
Oren Alexander, Tal Alexander and Alon Alexander had each pleaded not ÔÇőguilty to all charges they respectively faced in an indictment alleging trafficking and other criminal ÔÇőconduct related to seven victims.
Oren and Tal Alexander co-founded the luxury real Ôüáestate brokerage Official, while Alon Alexander was an executive at a private security company.
Defense lawyers ÔÇőhad contended that the women were willing participants who were not paid to have sex.
Tal Alexander ÔÇőfaced seven charges in total, and his twin brothers Oren and Alon faced six.
Over the course of a six-week trial in Manhattan federal court, eleven women testified that one or more of the brothers sexually ÔÇőabused them.
In his closing argument on March 3, prosecutor Andrew Jones said the brothers masqueraded ÔÇőas party boys but really were predators.
"They used a consistent playbook to lure, isolate and rape their victims," ÔÇîJones Ôüásaid. "They did it with callousness and a perverse sense of pride."
Defense lawyers acknowledged that the brothers had discussed sex amongst themselves in crass terms and behaved in ways that angered women with whom they had relationships. But they argued the sexual encounters at issue were consensual.
"He should ÔÇőbe and is embarrassed ÔÇőby how he behaved Ôüáover these years," Howard Srebnick, a lawyer for Alon Alexander, said in his closing argument on March 3. "That doesn't make the conduct a ÔÇőcrime."
The three brothers were arrested in December 2024 and have been ÔÇőjailed since Ôüáthen at Brooklyn's Metropolitan Detention Center.
Each brother had his own legal defense team. Oren Alexander's lawyers include Marc Agnifilo and Teny Geragos, who last year defended rapper and music mogul Sean "Diddy" Combs at his Ôüásex ÔÇőtrafficking trial.
Combs is serving a four-year prison sentence after being ÔÇőconvicted on prostitution charges, but acquitted of the more serious sex trafficking charges he faced that could have landed him ÔÇőin prison for life.

item.title: FBI examines Arizona election records in widening probe of Trump vote fraud claims
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 9 (Reuters) - The FBI has obtained records related ÔÇőto the 2020 election in Arizona , a top state lawmaker said on Monday, as federal ÔÇîlaw enforcement continues to pursue President Donald Trump's false claims that his defeat in that campaign was the result of voting fraud.
The FBI subpoenaed the Arizona Senate last week for material connected to a Republican-led audit of the 2020 election ÔÇőresults in Maricopa County, the most populous county in the state, Arizona Senate President ÔÇőWarren Petersen said in a social media post.
"The FBI has the records," Petersen, Ôüáa Republican, wrote in the post.
The demand for information from Arizona is the latest instance of ÔÇőthe Trump administration deploying U.S. law enforcement and intelligence agencies to gather evidence of alleged irregularities in the 2020 ÔÇőelection, despite courts and audits repeatedly rejecting claims of widespread voter fraud.
It was not immediately clear what crimes the FBI may be investigating. An FBI spokesperson did not immediately respond to a request for comment. A spokesperson for ÔÇőPetersen declined to elaborate on his social media post.
The FBI in January seized 2020 ballots from a Georgia ÔÇőcounty elections center as part of a court-approved search of the facility. Fulton County has sued for the ÔÇîreturn of Ôüáthe material, alleging the search violated the U.S. Constitution and relied on discredited allegations.
The 2021 Maricopa County audit, which was run by Trump's Republican allies in Arizona, largely affirmed the initial 2020 election results, finding slightly more votes for Trump's Democratic opponent Joe Biden . Trump's loss in Maricopa County was key ÔÇőto his narrow defeat to ÔÇőBiden statewide.
The audit Ôüámade several claims of election irregularities, which county officials vigorously disputed .
"What the Trump administration appears to be pursuing now is not a legitimate law enforcement inquiry," ÔÇőArizona State Attorney General Kris Mayes, a Democrat, said in a statement. "It ÔÇőis the weaponization Ôüáof federal law enforcement in service of crackpots and lies."
The investigations into Georgia and Arizona, two states that have been hotly contested in recent presidential elections, come as Trump has spoken openly about the Ôüáfederal government taking ÔÇőcontrol of elections in certain unspecified areas of the ÔÇőcountry.
Trump has put a lawyer, Kurt Olsen, who backed the "Stop the Steal" movement to overturn the 2020 election results, i n charge ÔÇőof an effort to reexamine the 2020 vote.

item.title: Colombia's ruling party to be top force in Senate, but without majority
item.description:
BOGOTA, March 9 (Reuters) - Colombia's ruling party, the left-leaning Historic Pact, emerged from Sunday's elections as the strongest political force in the incoming Senate, ÔÇőanalysts said on Monday, although its failure to secure an absolute ÔÇîmajority means it will need to form coalitions and may struggle if Colombia's divided right wing wins the presidency.
The party, which brought President Gustavo Petro to power in 2022, ÔÇőwon 25 out of 102 Senate seats in Sunday's legislative elections, according ÔÇőto official results. Finishing second was the right-wing Democratic Center, led by Ôüáformer President Alvaro Uribe, with 17 seats.
The two forces will likely jostle ÔÇőto make deals with other parties in the fragmented Senate, which include Colombia's ÔÇőLiberal Party with 13 seats, the Green Alliance with 10, the Conservative Party with 10, La U with nine, and Radical Change with seven. Eleven other seats were distributed among ÔÇővarious smaller parties.
In the House of Representatives, results from the electoral authority ÔÇőshow the opposition Democratic Center leading with 32 out of 182 seats, followed by the ÔÇîLiberal ÔüáParty with 31, and the Historic Pact with 29. The Conservative Party obtained 18 seats, Radical Change 13, and the Green Alliance 7.
Ivan Cepeda, Historic Pact's presidential candidate in May's presidential election, described the legislative election results as a "categorical ÔÇővictory," saying his ÔÇőparty had become Ôüáthe primary political force in Congress.
However, analysts suggest that if Cepeda loses the presidential election to right-wing candidates Paloma ÔÇőValencia or Abelardo De La Espriella, his party will become ÔÇőthe main Ôüáopposition force.
Ariel Avila, a re-elected senator from the Green Alliance, believes that Colombia's next president, whether from the right or the left, will have a tough Ôüátime getting ÔÇőthings done, predicting a "vetocracy" where lawmakers block parties ÔÇősimply because they come from the opposing side.
"It is a very fragmented Congress and it is ÔÇődifficult to mend," Avila said.

item.title: Alexander Butterfield, White House aide who exposed Nixon's taping system, dead at 99
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 9 (Reuters) - Alexander Butterfield, the White House aide who disclosed Richard Nixon's secret audio taping system, providing the "smoking gun" of the Watergate scandal that brought down the president, has died at age 99.
The death of Butterfield, whose revelation about the listening devices and recording system set off a ÔÇőroaring legal battle over the president's right of executive privilege, was confirmed by his wife Kim to The Washington Post and the New York Times.
Both newspapers said he died a ÔÇîmonth shy of his 100th birthday at his home in the seaside La Jolla area of San Diego, but no cause of death was cited.
Butterfield once told journalist Alicia Shepard he disliked being known as the man who exposed the existence of the tapes because it made it seem as if he had "eagerly and breathlessly" told the Watergate congressional committee about them.
A native of Pensacola, Florida, who grew up in California, Butterfield attended UCLA before joining the U.S. Air Force in 1948 and went on ÔÇőto serve as a combat pilot during the Vietnam War, commanding a squadron of tactical reconnaissance aircraft. He later served as a military assistant to a high-ranking Pentagon aide, gaining White House exposure ÔÇőin that job.
Butterfield finally left the Air Force to join the White House staff as a deputy to Nixon's chief of staff, H.R. Haldeman, an old friend Ôüáfrom UCLA. Among Butterfield's White House duties was keeping a historical record of the presidency, which included overseeing installation of the voice-activated taping system.
Butterfield had left the White House for the top job at the Federal Aviation ÔÇőAdministration by the time the investigation into the June 17, 1972, break-in at Democratic headquarters in the Watergate building was heating up.
He was one of the few in the White House who knew about the recording system, ÔÇőand upon learning that he would be questioned by the Senate Watergate Committee, known officially as the Select Committee on Presidential Campaign Activities, Butterfield resolved neither to lie nor volunteer information.
A Republican staff attorney for the committee was questioning him in a private preliminary session when he asked if the White House had a recording system. Butterfield reluctantly acknowledged there was.
On July 16, 1973, three days after his initial closed-door disclosure, Butterfield appeared before the Senate committee at a televised meeting, and Fred ÔÇőThompson, then counsel for the Republicans on the committee and a future actor and senator, asked the same question.
After a long pause, Butterfield said, "I was aware of listening devices, yes, sir."
It was stunning news for the nation ÔÇőbecause it meant there was an actual record of what Nixon said, when he said it and to whom he said it.
Butterfield said the recording system had been secretly taping conversations and meetings in the Oval Office, Nixon's office in ÔÇîthe Executive Office ÔüáBuilding and the Cabinet room, as well as four White House telephones. The purpose of the recordings, he said, was historical.
In a 1975 interview with People magazine, Butterfield said Nixon often forgot about the recorders and had disregarded advice to destroy the tapes because he never thought the Watergate affair would reach a point where he would have to surrender them.
"I'm sure that he hates me as much as anyone can," Butterfield said of his former boss, who died in 1994.
He said he thought Nixon should have resigned earlier.
"I don't feel awful about the president's resignation," he said. "Not at all."
A recording made six days after the Watergate break-in proved to ÔÇőbe Nixon's undoing - the "smoking gun" that showed he knew ÔÇőof the cover-up. He was heard agreeing to Ôüáa plan to have the break-in investigation halted for national security reasons.
The U.S. Supreme Court ultimately rejected White House claims of executive privilege and ordered Nixon to turn over the subpoenaed tapes as his public and political support withered. Rather than face impeachment and a Senate trial, he resigned on August 9, 1974.
Since he was not ÔÇőinvolved with the break-in or cover-up, Butterfield was never indicted, but his old friend Haldeman would be among several Nixon insiders who went to prison in ÔÇőthe scandal.
Butterfield was the focus Ôüáof the 2015 book "The Last of the President's Men" by Bob Woodward, the Washington Post reporter who helped break the Watergate story, and he provided Woodward with thousands of documents he had secretly removed from Nixon administration offices.
The documents and interviews with Butterfield portrayed what the former White House aide described as a "cesspool" within the administration and characterized Nixon as odd, isolated and resentful.
Butterfield said he frequently was the target of hostility from Nixon loyalists and told Time Ôüámagazine that long-time ÔÇőNixon secretary Rose Mary Woods, who said she accidentally erased 18 1/2 minutes of those White House tapes, had disparaged him as ÔÇőa "son of a bitch" who had "destroyed the greatest leader this country ever had."
Butterfield worked as an adviser to Oliver Stone on his 1995 film "Nixon" and had a cameo role in it as a White House staffer.
Butterfield's first marriage, to Charlotte Maguire, ended in divorce in 1985. ÔÇőHe also had previously dated Audrey Geisel, widow of the children's author-illustrator Theodor Seuss Geisel, better known as Dr. Seuss.

item.title: Trump on Cuba: May or may not be a "friendly takeover"
item.description:
DORAL, Fla., March 9 (Reuters) - U.S. President Donald Trump said on Monday Cuba is in "deep ÔÇőtrouble" on a humanitarian basis and ÔÇîthat U.S. Secretary of State Marco Rubio was dealing with the issue which may or may ÔÇőnot be a "friendly takeover."
"He's dealing (with it) ÔÇőand it may be a friendly takover, Ôüáit may not be a friendly takeover. ÔÇőWouldn't really matter because they're really down ÔÇőto...as they say, fumes. They have no energy, they have no money," Trump, speaking at a news ÔÇőconference in Doral, Florida told reporters.
The Cuban ÔÇőgovernment has said it is not in any high-level ÔÇîtalks Ôüáwith the United States but has not outright denied press reports that U.S. officials may be in informal talks with Raul ÔÇőGuillermo Rodriguez ÔÇőCastro, the Ôüágrandson of former Cuban President Raul Castro.
Cuban exiles who are largely ÔÇőconcentrated in Miami have long dreamed ÔÇőof Ôüáoverthrowing the Cuban government or seeing it fall and have in the past plotted Ôüáagainst ÔÇőthe government that was established ÔÇőby the late revolutionary leader Fidel Castro.

item.title: Trump says he discussed Ukraine and Iran conflicts with Putin
item.description:
DORAL, Florida, March 9 (Reuters) - U.S. President Donald Trump on Monday told reporters he had a "very good call" with ÔÇőRussian President Vladimir Putin about Ukraine ÔÇőand the conflict in the Middle East.
"There's Ôüátremendous hatred between President Putin and (Ukraine's) ÔÇőPresident Zelenskiy. They can't seem to ÔÇőget it together, but I think it was a positive call on that subject," Trump said.
Trump ÔÇőadded that Putin "wants to be helpful" ÔÇőwith the Iran conflict.
"I said, 'You could be more helpful ÔÇîby Ôüágetting the Ukraine-Russia war over with. That will be more helpful,'" Trump told reporters in a press conference at ÔÇőhis Florida ÔÇőgolf club.
A Ôüáforeign policy aide for Putin said on Monday that Putin proposed ÔÇőan idea for a "quick political ÔÇőand Ôüádiplomatic end to the Iranian conflict."
Trump recently has expressed dismay about the ongoing conflict Ôüábetween ÔÇőRussia and Ukraine, which ÔÇőstarted more than four years ago.

item.title: Russian drone injures six by apartment block in Ukraine's Kharkiv
item.description:
March 9 (Reuters) - A Russian drone ÔÇőstruck an area near a ÔÇîhigh-rise apartment building on Monday in Ukraine's second-largest city, Kharkiv, ÔÇőinjuring six people, smashing ÔÇőwindows and setting cars ablaze, Ôüáthe city's mayor said.
Ihor ÔÇőTerekhov said a small child ÔÇőwas among the injured.
Kharkiv, located 30 km (18 miles) from the Russian ÔÇőborder, withstood early advances ÔÇőby Russian forces after their full-scale invasion ÔÇîin ÔüáFebruary 2022 and has since been a frequent target of Russian air attacks.
Separately, ÔÇőRussian shelling ÔÇőof Ôüáthe southeastern city of Dnipro injured seven ÔÇőpeople, regional Governor Oleksandr ÔÇőGanzha Ôüásaid on Telegram. Ganzha posted photos online showing rubble Ôüáin ÔÇőstreets and damaged ÔÇőbuilding facades.

item.title: Americans expect gasoline prices to keep rising after Iran strikes, Reuters/Ipsos poll finds
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 9 (Reuters) - Most Americans think gasoline prices are ÔÇőgoing to rise in coming months following President Donald Trump 's decision to launch military strikes on Iran , and ÔÇîmany expect a protracted conflict, according to a Reuters/Ipsos poll that closed on Monday.
Some 67% of respondents in the four-day poll - including 44% of Republicans and 85% of Democrats - said they expect gas prices in the U.S. to get worse over the next year. ÔÇőSixty percent of Americans expect U.S. military involvement in Iran will "go on for an extended period of ÔÇőtime," according to the poll.
U.S. and Israeli forces launched coordinated strikes on Iran on February Ôüá28, killing the nation's leader in an initial surprise attack.
The latest Reuters/Ipsos poll found just 29% of Americans ÔÇőapprove of the strikes, little changed from a 27% approval rate in a Reuters/Ipsos poll conducted in the hours immediately ÔÇőfollowing the start of the military campaign. Both polls had margins of error of about 3 percentage points.
The latest poll underscores the political risks Trump brings to his Republican Party ahead of the November midterm elections when Democrats hope to seize control from Trump's party ÔÇőof at least one chamber of Congress.
Some 64% of poll respondents - including one in four Republicans and nine ÔÇőin 10 Democrats - said Trump has not clearly explained the goals of U.S. military involvement.
Trump returned to the White House ÔÇîlast Ôüáyear after promising to tame inflation and prevent the military from getting stuck in a foreign conflict. Since he launched strikes on Iran, U.S. gasoline prices have surged by roughly 50 cents a gallon and at least seven U.S. soldiers have been killed.
Gasoline prices are highly sensitive in the U.S., as one of the most immediate and visceral ÔÇőindications Americans have of increases ÔÇőand decreases in their Ôüácost of living.
Energy prices were rising daily last week across the country and internationally, including during the weekend as the Reuters/Ipsos poll was conducted. Analysts expect weeks or months of ÔÇőhigher fuel prices worldwide even if the week-old conflict ends quickly.
Trump is considering ways to ÔÇőcombat surging Ôüáprices, but U.S. policy options could have limited sway over global oil markets. On Monday, he told reporters the military operation in Iran is ÔÇťahead of our initial timeline by a lot.ÔÇŁ
Forty-nine percent of Americans - including a third of Republicans and two-thirds Ôüáof Democrats - ÔÇőthink the war in Iran will have a mostly negative impact on ÔÇőtheir personal finances. About one in three Republicans said they were not sure how the war would affect their finances.
The Reuters/Ipsos poll, which ÔÇőwas conducted online, surveyed 1,021 U.S. adults nationwide.

item.title: Trump says he is 'disappointed' that Mojtaba Khamenei became Iran's supreme leader
item.description:
DORAL, Florida, March 9 (Reuters) - U.S. ÔÇîPresident Donald Trump said on Monday he was "disappointed" that ÔÇőIran named Mojtaba ÔÇőKhamenei to succeed his Ôüáslain father Ayatollah ÔÇőAli Khamenei as the ÔÇősupreme leader of the country.
"We think it's going to ÔÇőlead to just ÔÇőmore of the same problem ÔÇîfor Ôüáthe country," Trump told reporters at a press conference in ÔÇőFlorida.
When ÔÇőasked Ôüáwhether the new leader had a ÔÇőtarget on his ÔÇőback, ÔüáTrump said it would be "inappropriate" to say Ôüáwhether ÔÇőor not ÔÇőhe does.

item.title: National Bureau of Economic Research cuts ties with Larry Summers, WSJ reports
item.description:
March 9 (Reuters) - Former U.S. Treasury Secretary Larry Summers has lost his affiliation with The ÔÇőNational Bureau of Economic Research, the ÔÇîWall Street Journal reported on Monday, citing an email from the research organization's president.
Losing the ÔÇőNBER affiliation would be the latest ÔÇőfallout for Summers from his ties with Ôüálate convicted sex offender Jeffrey Epstein. ÔÇőSummers said last month he would resign from ÔÇőteaching at Harvard University at the end of the academic year, months after receiving a lifetime ban ÔÇőfrom The American Economic Association.
NBER said ÔÇőan ad hoc committee reviewed Summers' conduct and recommended ÔÇîthe Ôüátermination of his appointment as a research associate with the agency, the report said. "He is no longer an NBER affiliate," ÔÇőwrote NBER ÔÇőpresident James ÔüáPoterba in an email to the Journal.
Documents released by the ÔÇőU.S. House Oversight Committee showed a ÔÇőclose Ôüárelationship between Summers and Epstein that continued well after the disgraced financier's 2008 Ôüáconviction. ÔÇőThere is no evidence Summers ÔÇőwas involved in Epstein's sex trafficking activities.

item.title: Australia to send missiles to UAE, deploy military surveillance aircraft
item.description:
SYDNEY, March 10 (Reuters) - Australia will deploy a military surveillance ÔÇîaircraft to the Middle East and send missiles to the United Arab Emirates but will not put troops on the ground in ÔÇőIran, Prime Minister Anthony Albanese said on Tuesday.
Australia's ÔÇőmilitary support would help the Gulf countries defend Ôüáthemselves against unprovoked attacks from Iran, Albanese said, stressing Australia ÔÇőwas "not a protagonist".
"Our involvement is purely defensive," Albanese told ÔÇőreporters. "And it's in defence of Australians who are in the region as well as in defence of our friends in the United ÔÇőArab Emirates."
Albanese said Australia would deploy one of its ÔÇőBoeing-manufactured E-7A Wedgetail airborne early warning and control system aircraft for ÔÇîan Ôüáinitial four weeks to protect the airspace above the Gulf countries.
Advanced medium-range air-to-air missiles would be provided to the United Arab Emirates, following a phone call with President ÔÇőMohammed bin ÔÇőZayed Al Nahyan, Ôüáhe added.
The war has effectively shut the Strait of Hormuz, a chokepoint for a ÔÇőfifth of global oil and liquefied natural ÔÇőgas, Ôüáas the conflict escalated.
