"Our No.2 and No.3 crackers are still operating ÔÇőat around 70% for now," Lin said, adding Ôüáthat the refiner is considering shutting one cracker if "naphtha ÔÇőfeedstock does not come at all".
Steam crackers break down feedstocks ÔÇősuch as naphtha into smaller molecules like ethylene and propylene, used to make plastics.
FPCC's No.2 and No.3 crackers at its Mailiao site ÔÇőcan produce up to 1.335 million metric tons per ÔÇőyear of ethylene, while its No.1 cracker is currently not in operation .
For refined ÔÇîfuel Ôüáproducts such as diesel, jet fuel and gasoline, the company is still proceeding with its export deliveries for March and April, although there is likely to be no spot ÔÇőavailability for ÔÇőApril, Lin said.
As Ôüáof today, the refinery is processing around 490,000 barrels per day (bpd) of crude, although ÔÇőthe situation could change as some crude ÔÇődeliveries "will be Ôüáaffected" after March 20, he added.
FPCC is slated to shut its No.2 crude unit for a scheduled maintenance in the Ôüánext ÔÇőfew days, Reuters had earlier reported.
The ÔÇőprivately owned refiner has a total crude processing capacity of 540,000 bpd.

item.title: Quality is key as some coal rallies amid LNG's spike on Iran war
item.description:
LAUNCESTON, Australia, March 10 (Reuters) - The surge in the spot price of liquefied natural gas (LNG) has dragged seaborne thermal coal prices higher, but only for the higher quality grades that can substitute for natural gas in power generation.
The spot price of LNG in Asia more than doubled last week as ÔÇőthe market digested the loss of nearly 20% of the global supply of the super-chilled fuel after the U.S. and Israeli strikes on Iran effectively closed the Strait of ÔÇîHormuz , shutting off Qatar's LNG.
LNG for delivery to North Asia jumped 116% to a two-year high of $22.50 per million British thermal units (mmBtu) in the week ended March 6.
The surge in LNG prices has opened the window for gas-to-coal switching in Japan and South Korea, the two Asian countries with the biggest ability to arbitrage between the fuels used to generate electricity.
Japan and South Korea predominantly buy Australian thermal coal with an energy content of 6,000 ÔÇőkilocalories per kilogram (kcal/kg), and the index of this fuel at Newcastle Port , as assessed by commodity price reporting agency Argus, rose to $129.62 a metric ton in the week to ÔÇőMarch 6.
This was a 14-month high and up 11.6% from the week to February 27, which was the day before the U.S. and Israel Ôüálaunched an aerial campaign against Iran, prompting Tehran to fire missiles and drones at targets across the Persian Gulf region.
EUROPE GAINS
The price of thermal coal heading to Europe, the other region where ÔÇőgas-to-coal switching can happen, also increased, with McCloskey assessing 6,000 kcal/kg coal from South Africa's Richards Bay port at $113.00 a ton on Monday, up 14.3% from $98.90 on February 27.
It's worth noting that the ÔÇőgains in seaborne thermal coal are considerably smaller in percentage terms than the rise in spot LNG prices.
This is likely because there is no threat to the supply of thermal coal from major exporters such as Australia, South Africa, Indonesia and Colombia.
But it's also the case that while the economics of gas-to-coal switching are currently attractive, the ability to do so is constrained by a lack of available coal-fired capacity, especially in Europe.
For example, ÔÇőSpain retired 13.18 gigawatts (GW) of coal-fired capacity from 2000 to 2025 without adding any new plants, while Germany shut down 33.57 GW of capacity while adding just 13.69 GW, according to data ÔÇőfrom the Global Energy Monitor.
Japan retired almost 1,200 megawatts (MW) of coal-fired capacity in the past three years, without adding any new units.
While South Korea has boosted its coal-fired capacity in recent years the government has formally ÔÇîcommitted to Ôüáa long-term phase-out of the fuel, planning to close 40 of 61 units by 2040.
The longer-term policy doesn't preclude a switch to more coal in the short term if LNG prices remain elevated.
It's still too early to ascertain if countries that can burn more coal for electricity are lifting imports, although analysts DBX Commodities are estimating that the European Union's seaborne thermal coal imports will rise to 2.17 million tons in March from 2.01 million in February.
Japan's March imports of seaborne thermal coal are estimated by DBX at 9.52 million tons, down from 10.18 million in February but up ÔÇőfrom 9.22 million in March last year, while ÔÇőSouth Korea's arrivals are forecast at 6.12 Ôüámillion in March, up from 5.05 million for the same month in 2025.
CHINA, INDIA
It's also worth noting that the world's two largest coal importers, China and India, don't usually engage in gas-to-coal switching for power generation.
China's gas-fired generation is a tiny 3% of total electricity production, while coal is ÔÇőjust under 60%. For India, natural gas accounts for about 2.8% of electricity generation and coal is around 70%.
While high LNG prices will ÔÇőlikely curb imports in both ÔüáChina and India, it's unlikely to result in higher coal imports as rising domestic production will be able to compensate.
This dynamic can be seen in the prices of seaborne coal grades favoured by China and India.
Australian coal with an energy content of 5,500 kcal/kg, which is popular with Chinese utilities and Indian industrial users such as sponge iron producers, rose a modest 1.4% in the week to March 6 Ôüáto $87.87 a ton, ÔÇőaccording to Argus.
Indonesian coal with an energy content of 4,200 kcal/kg performed better, rising 5.1% to $58.21 a ton in ÔÇőthe week to March 6, but this was still well below the gain for higher-quality grades.
Enjoying this column? Check out Reuters Open Interest (ROI) , your essential new source for global financial commentary. ROI delivers thought-provoking, data-driven analysis of everything from swap rates ÔÇőto soybeans. Markets are moving faster than ever. ROI can help you keep up. item.description:

The year since the US presidentÔÇÖs ÔÇśLiberation DayÔÇÖ tariff war has produced many surprises. In this episode of The Big View, Peter Thal Larsen and IMD Business SchoolÔÇÖs Simon Evenett discuss why dire forecasts fizzled, how China levies fell to 26%, and why a larger jolt looms.

item.title: Aramco posts drop in annual profit, announces its first buyback
item.description:
March 10 (Reuters) - Saudi Arabia's Aramco (2222.SE) , opens new tab , the world's top oil exporter, reported a 12% drop in annual ÔÇőprofit mainly due to lower crude prices, but announced it ÔÇîwould repurchase up to $3 billion worth of shares in its first-ever buyback.
The buyback program will be conducted over the next 18 months. Until now, the company has relied ÔÇőon its massive dividend payouts to reward shareholders.
The results come ÔÇőat a highly volatile time for global oil markets as Ôüáthe U.S.-Israeli war on Iran has led to a near-closure of the ÔÇőStrait of Hormuz and has forced several regional producers to curb output.
Brent ÔÇőcrude, which surged to near $120 on Monday, is trading around $93 on Tuesday.
Aramco reported $93.4 billion in net income for 2025, below an LSEG consensus estimate of $95.6 billion.
For the fourth ÔÇőquarter, net profit tumbled 20.5% to nearly $17.8 billion on higher operating ÔÇőcosts, marking its 12th consecutive quarter of year-on-year profit decline.
Aramco confirmed paying a base ÔÇîdividend of $21.1 Ôüábillion for the fourth quarter and $219 million in performance-linked dividends, a mechanism calculated based on free cash flow introduced following bumper profits in 2022 following the Ukraine war.
Total dividends paid for the year were $85.5 billion, down ÔÇőfrom $124 billion in ÔÇő2024.
Aramco has long Ôüábeen a cash cow for the Saudi state, which relies on fossil fuels for more than half of government ÔÇőrevenues. The kingdom directly holds nearly 81.5% of the ÔÇőcompany and Ôüáits sovereign investor, the Public Investment Fund, holds another 16%.
Total revenue for the year fell 7.2% to $415.8 billion, on weaker prices for crude oil, as Ôüáwell ÔÇőas refined and chemical products.
The company's gearing ÔÇőratio - a measure of indebtedness ÔÇö dropped to 3.8% at the end of 2025, down from 4.5% ÔÇőat the end of 2024.

item.title: Thailand orders bureaucrats to use stairs and work from home in energy saving drive
item.description:
BANGKOK, March 10 (Reuters) - Thai Prime Minister Anutin Charnvirakul has ordered civil servants to conserve energy amid an energy squeeze brought on by the conflict ÔÇőin the Middle East, a government spokesperson said on Tuesday, ÔÇîwith measures including suspending overseas trips and using stairs instead of elevators.
"The prime minister ordered that starting from today civil servants will work from home," spokesperson Lalida ÔÇőPeriswiwatana told reporters, adding that exceptions would be made for officials ÔÇőwho must serve the public.
Southeast Asia's second-largest economy has around 95 Ôüádays of energy reserves left, and it has been seeking additional sources ÔÇőof liquefied natural gas from the United States, Australia and South Africa, ÔÇőaccording to Energy Minister Auttapol Rerkpiboon.
But it is urgently trying to reduce demand. Lalida said other government measures to conserve energy include setting air conditioning temperatures at 26 ÔÇőto 27 degrees Celsius (78.8 to 80.6 Fahrenheit) and wearing short-sleeved shirts instead ÔÇőof formal attire like suits and ties.
Nearly 68% of Thailand's energy needs are met ÔÇîfrom Ôüánatural gas. Over half of its liquefied natural gas is sourced from domestic production in the Gulf of Thailand, while 35% comes from imports, including 13% from neighbouring Myanmar, according to the country's Energy Regulatory Commission.
Earlier ÔÇőthis month, Thailand ÔÇőalso halted energy exports Ôüáto all countries other than Laos and Myanmar.
Government officials have been ordered to reduce electricity use at office ÔÇőbuildings by switching off lights and electrical equipment when ÔÇőnot needed, ÔüáLalida said. The government will also ask the public to cooperate and encourage energy saving measures such as carpooling.
"If the situation escalates, the government may Ôüáconsider ÔÇőmandatory measures including dimming advertising signs at ÔÇőshops, cinemas and businesses and closing gas stations at 10 p.m.," the government also said in ÔÇőa statement.

item.title: Disturbance hits submarine power link between Finland and Sweden
item.description:
COPENHAGEN, March 10 (Reuters) - A ÔÇîdisturbance has occurred on the Fenno-Skan 2 ÔÇőpower connection between ÔÇőFinland and Sweden, Finnish grid Ôüáoperator Fingrid said ÔÇőon Tuesday.
"The cause of ÔÇőthe failure is being investigated," Fingrid said in a ÔÇőstatement.
Fenno-Skan 2 is ÔÇőa 800 megawatt (MW) high-voltage direct ÔÇîcurrent Ôüáelectricity transmission link between Finland and Sweden.
The outage was expected ÔÇőto last ÔÇőfor Ôüáaround 18 hours, according to ÔÇőa separate regulatory ÔÇőstatement Ôüápublished by Swedish grid operator Svenska kraftnaet Ôüáon ÔÇőthe Nord Pool ÔÇőmessaging platform.

item.title: How oil shock and financial stress can feed each other
item.description:
LONDON, March 10 (Reuters) - Central banks are clearly watching the Iran war oil shock like hawks. But even if inflation is their main concern, it's not the only one - and a worst case for some top policymakers is that the crude price surge proves a breaking point for multiple financial stress fractures.
The surge in oil prices, driven by supply disruption from more than a week of war in the Middle East, is already challenging central banks' stretched mandates .
An age-old question is ÔÇőwhether oil spikes that lift inflation and inflation expectations ultimately crimp household and business finances so much that they depress demand and prices. And then there's the toxic scenario in which they do both, leaving policymakers with ÔÇîa conundrum of whether to prioritize taming inflation or supporting consumers and jobs.
Hawks say act quickly on prices and the hit to demand will lessen, particularly at central banks where price stability is the main or only goal. Others suggest "looking through" volatile inflation, much like central banks did post-pandemic - mistakenly, in hindsight.
A blizzard of "ifs" and "buts" informs all that - from how policy was positioned before the shock, to the scope for government subsidies or energy price caps, to the duration of the conflict and supply outage.
The weight of those uncertainties will likely argue for sitting tight ÔÇőand watching events and markets unfold for a bit before jumping to conclusions.
But there's another consideration under a third hat most major central banks now wear - financial stability.
BOND MARKET TERMITES
Senior officials fear that some ÔÇőof the excesses and behavioural trends they have been monitoring in financial markets for several years could be exposed by a mega-macro disturbance in energy, inflation, interest rates, currencies and generalised Ôüávolatility.
The risk of a perfect storm is keeping some awake at night.
Of the many issues watchdogs have put on their radars in recent years, four stand out - and they typically involve "shadow banks" outside the traditional banking system and their growing role in lending to ÔÇőfirms and governments.
This includes the sharp rise in private credit funds - which have topped $3 trillion globally - where asset managers effectively lend directly to businesses. Without the public glare of bond market pricing or traditional bank lending norms, what happens ÔÇőunder the bonnet of these vehicles during a shock still unnerves many.
Regulators fear that this lack of transparency could trigger sudden investor rushes to exit these funds, with ripple effects for borrowers and, ultimately, for the banks that still help finance or manage many of these vehicles.
Arguably a bigger source of anxiety is the rising share of government debt now financed by highly leveraged hedge funds . Unease has been building for years about the scale of their activity in vital securities repurchase, or repo, markets and in today's gigantic U.S. government bond arbitrage trades, which exploit small ÔÇőgaps between cash and futures pricing with huge leveraged bets.
While these players may help smooth government financing, they also create significant vulnerabilities to shocks - and, once again, the stress ultimately feeds back to the real economy via the sizeable exposure of traditional banks that lend ÔÇőto these funds.
In January, for example, the G20's Financial Stability Board , opens new tab homed in on repos and flagged the potential hit to sovereign bonds from a sudden deleveraging by cash borrowers. It also warned of inadequately priced counterparty risk, often with zero haircuts on ÔÇîsovereign bonds for Ôüárepos, and highlighted cross-border spillover risks. More than $16 trillion in repo backed by government bonds was outstanding last year, with some 60% of that in America.
Two other areas of concern have been the build-up of so-called stablecoins - crypto tokens pegged to the dollar or other currencies using assets in reserve - and their emergence as major holders of sovereign debt .
With the $300 billion market set to rise further, any disturbance to that ecosystem - or run on these tokens - could force an unwind of the bonds and assets backing them. In the meantime, there's a risk they rob banks of deposits .
And that's not to mention a long-standing concern among savers, investors and lenders about the overvalued and hugely concentrated artificial intelligence universe.
How might all those fragilities be affected by the war in the Middle East? One ÔÇőobvious area is the behaviour of giant regional oil-wealth and ÔÇősovereign funds; another is a more traditional dash for the liquidity of Ôüádollar cash rather than paper or physical assets.
But the two main routes would be a surge in volatility in equity and debt and a dramatic change to the interest rate outlook from an inflationary energy price surge.
We have already seen rumblings of the latter over the past week with rising government borrowing rates and jolts to central ÔÇőbanks' interest rate horizons. But the disturbance doesn't yet appear to be at destabilising levels.
Yet since this sort of financial stability is now part of all central banks' remit, ÔÇőit raises a question over how well they Ôüácould cope with a shocking, even viral, disturbance from a geopolitical event like this.
A further question is whether consideration of these financial risks might work against a firm and decisive central bank response to any inflation threat.
While the Ukraine invasion and the related inflation and interest rate jolts back then did pass without too much incident - at least beyond a dire year or two for asset price returns - there was also the tremor of the U.S. regional bank shakeout in 2023.
No two shocks are identical, Ôüáhowever, and triple-digit ÔÇőoil and the pressure for a central bank response are unlikely to pass without impact.
(The opinions expressed here are those of the author, ÔÇőa columnist for Reuters.)

item.title: Aramco sees 'catastrophic consequences' for oil if shipping doesn't resume in Strait of Hormuz
item.description:
DUBAI, March 10 (Reuters) - Saudi Arabia's Aramco (2222.SE) , opens new tab , the world's ÔÇîtop oil exporter, said on Tuesday that there would be "catastrophic consequences" for the world's oil markets if the Iran war continues to disrupt shipping in the Strait ÔÇőof Hormuz.
The disruption has not only upended the shipping and ÔÇőinsurance sectors but also promises to have drastic domino effects Ôüáon aviation, agriculture, automotive and other industries, Aramco CEO Amin Nasser told ÔÇőreporters on an earnings call.
Nasser noted global inventories of oil were at ÔÇőa five-year low and said the crisis will lead to drawdowns at a faster rate, adding that it was critical that shipping in the strait resumed.
"There would ÔÇőbe catastrophic consequences for the worldÔÇÖs oil markets and the longer the ÔÇődisruption goes on, and the more drastic the consequences for the global economy," he ÔÇîsaid.
Nasser Ôüáalso said a small fire from an attack last week on Aramco's Ras Tanura refinery, its largest domestically, was quickly extinguished and brought under control, adding that the refinery was in the process of being restarted.
Iran's ÔÇőRevolutionary Guards said on ÔÇőTuesday they would Ôüánot allow "one litre of oil" to be shipped from the Middle East if U.S. and Israeli attacks continue, ÔÇőprompting a warning from President Donald Trump that the ÔÇőU.S. Ôüáwould hit Iran much harder if it blocked exports from the vital energy-producing region.
His comments come after Aramco reported a 12% drop in annual profit mainly Ôüádue ÔÇőto lower crude prices. It also announced ÔÇőit would repurchase up to $3 billion worth of shares in its first-ever buyback.

item.title: Spain's Repsol hikes payout targets, reduces investments
item.description:
MADRID, March 10 (Reuters) - Spanish energy ÔÇőgroup Repsol (REP.MC) , opens new tab pledged on Tuesday ÔÇîto give shareholders between 30% and 40% of the cash ÔÇőflow it generates from ÔÇőits operations through 2028 via dividends Ôüáand buybacks while slowing down ÔÇőinvestments.
The payout move its in ÔÇőline with the company's strategic plans in the past years, which had ÔÇőshareholder returns as a ÔÇőkey priority.
In its previous plan, the ÔÇîcompany Ôüáhad set a 25%-35% target for remuneration.
In coming years, Repsol will slow down investments after ÔÇőyears of ÔÇősignificant Ôüácapital expenditures.
The company sees net investments through 2028 ÔÇőat between 7.5 and ÔÇő9 Ôüábillion euros. This is down from a 16 to Ôüá19 ÔÇőbillion euros range ÔÇőin its previous multi-year plan.

item.title: 'Maybe we die together': Voices at the Iran-Turkey mountain crossing
item.description:
KAPIKOY, Turkey, March 10 (Reuters) - At a remote mountain pass in eastern Turkey, travellers from Iran step across the threshold with a mix of fear, exhaustion and relief - arriving after a week marked by war, long journeys by train or car, communications blackouts and borrowed phones.
Snow-covered hills surround the Iranian side of the frontier at the Kapikoy border gate in Turkey's Van province, where families and lone travellers emerge, many after days on the road. Hundreds have ÔÇőcrossed in recent days and there is now a steady flow in both directions as the U.S.-Israeli war with Iran expands across the region.
Some say they fled because bombs were falling in their cities. Others decided ÔÇîto leave after losing contact with loved ones, travelling by land when flights were cancelled. Most carry only small suitcases, a phone without a local SIM card, and a determination to finish an already punishing journey.
New arrivals ask how to reach Van, the nearest city two hours away, and how to reconnect to the outside world. Some journalists hand over their phones so travellers can reassure family that they are safe. Each person has a different reason for leaving ÔÇö work drying up, a sick relative to visit, or a life interrupted by conflict.
Ebrahim Eidi, 61, had recently been ÔÇőin Tehran and said many Iranians were waiting to see whether the government was weakened enough for protests to erupt. He said some believed opposition figure Reza Pahlavi ÔÇö the son of Iran's last shah ÔÇö could become a rallying point ÔÇőif large demonstrations begin.
"People are waiting for something to happen, for some change. They want the government to change completely, and many people say they are waiting for Reza Pahlavi."
Eidi left ÔüáIran 34 years ago for the Netherlands, where he works with asylum seekers and refugees in camps. He returns to Iran roughly once a year. This time, he said, the situation felt different.
He said many people chose to stay in Iran because ÔÇőthey still had hope for the country. But they feared their own power was not enough to change the government, and that the situation could spiral.
"People are afraid to go to the streets. They are not afraid of America. Unfortunately, they are afraid of ÔÇőtheir own government."
Leila, 45, was travelling in the opposite direction, heading back into Iran. After losing contact with her family in Shiraz, she decided to return from Istanbul, where she occasionally assists academics working with a German historical research institution.
"How can I be safe when I feel my family maybe they are in danger?"
One of her brothers is seriously ill and in a coma, increasing her worry. For her, being physically with her family - even in danger - felt more bearable than waiting abroad.
She plans to remain in Iran until the war is over.
"I cannot guard them against bombs. ÔÇőBut when I feel I can be with them together, maybe we die together, or I can help them as long as we are alive."
Hamid Shirmohammadzadeh, 35, had travelled back to Iran shortly before the war and fled with his ÔÇőwife and children when Tehran came under attack. He had been building a life in Tokyo, working for an import-export firm, but returned to Iran after his visa lapsed. His wife and two children had remained there.
"The day before the war started I was in TehranÔÇŽ ÔÇîThen the bombs Ôüástarted falling. We saw the war had begun, so we came to Turkey."
His wife, son and daughter ÔÇö aged 9 and 10 ÔÇö are waiting near the border as he seeks help from Japanese authorities to secure visas for them.
ÔÇťI worked in Japan, paid my taxes and followed the rules. I donÔÇÖt understand why they are not helping me now.ÔÇŁ
ÔÇťIn times like this we should help each other. I need help now because my country is at war.ÔÇŁ
Mohammad Soltanzadeh, who lives in Hamburg and is originally from Afghanistan, had been visiting relatives in Iran's Mashhad when flights were cancelled, forcing him onto the road.
"The journey was very exhausting. We were on the train for about 24 hours, then we travelled four hours by car and finally came by taxi."
He described Mashhad as calm despite ÔÇőthe wider turmoil.
"People were a bit saddened and mourning but ÔÇőthey were not stressed. The shops were open, the Ôüámarkets and shopping centres were open and people were continuing their activities. Life was going on."
Egyptian factory worker Mohammad Fauzi, 46, crossed from Iran with no Turkish SIM card, no local currency and no knowledge of the language. He only had the phone numbers of two Egyptian friends in Ankara and Izmir - and a plan to reach Cairo.
He had watched work grind to a halt ÔÇőin Iran during his three months there working in the marble and granite sector, with many factories closing.
"The situation is very difficult and working has stopped. I can't work, I ÔÇőcan't stay because the situation is Ôüádangerous now, so I want to go to my home, my country."
Jalileh Jabari, 63, said she fled Tehran because "bombs are falling" and the situation had become unbearable. The highways to the border were calm, but uncertainty in the capital pushed her to leave. She was travelling to Istanbul, where her daughter studies.
"If things become good there, if Iran becomes good, I will come back. If there is peace, I will return."
Two sisters, Shaylin, 9, and Celine Azizour, 11, crossed into Turkey with their mother, travelling from ÔüáTehran toward Istanbul ÔÇőwith hopes of eventually reaching London.
"We from Tehran," Shaylin said. Asked about conditions there, she replied: "ItÔÇÖs not so good." Despite the arduous journey, she smiled: "IÔÇÖm so ÔÇőhappy."
Yasna, 63, crossed over from Iran with her husband and one of her daughters, travelling to Antalya on TurkeyÔÇÖs southern coast to visit another daughter who lives there with her family.
"I came to see my daughter ÔÇö I haven't seen her for six years. She has children there and I have two grandchildren."
She ÔÇősaid she preferred not to comment on Iran's political situation.
"I donÔÇÖt know what to say about the situation in Iran because we will be back to Iran."

item.title: Congo Republic's Sassou set to extend long rule, focus on succession
item.description:
DAKAR, March 10 (Reuters) - Congo Republic President Denis Sassou Nguesso looks set to extend his decades-long rule in elections on Sunday, even as his advanced age and a term limit fuel speculation about who ÔÇőwill eventually succeed him.
The 82-year-old former paratrooper first took power in the oil-rich Central African nation in a coup in 1979. He lost ÔÇîCongo Republic's first multi-party elections in 1992 but seized power again in 1997 after a civil war.
He has now ruled for a combined total of almost 42 years, making him Africa's third longest-serving leader, after Equatorial Guinea's Teodoro Obiang and Cameroon's Paul Biya.
Sassou will face six candidates in an election with an organising commission dominated by figures appointed by the ruling Congolese Labour Party. Two of ÔÇőthe main opposition parties are boycotting the vote, saying the process lacks transparency, and several potential challengers are in prison or in exile.
"This election is a ÔÇőmere formality. The real stakes lie in what comes next," Remadji Hoinathy of the Pretoria-based Institute for Security Studies think tank Ôüásaid.
A 2015 constitutional reform, adopted despite opposition protests, reset the presidential term limit and allowed Sassou to stay in power. But it also capped presidents at three five-year ÔÇőmandates, meaning that, barring another reform, this is his last election.
The president himself has begun speaking more openly about eventual succession, telling young supporters at his ÔÇőcampaign opening rally on February 28 that his generation was "laying the groundwork" for them to take over.
One potential successor is his son, Denis-Christel Sassou Nguesso, who entered government as Minister of International Cooperation and Public-Private Partnerships in 2021 and has since taken on a higher public profile.
Some analysts say he may not be ready.
"Denis-Christel does not command the same authority within the ruling party as ÔÇőhis father and is widely unpopular. His potential accession to power threatens to unleash a violent succession struggle," Maja Bovcon, an independent consultant focused on West and Central ÔÇőAfrica, said.
Other figures within the ruling system who could take over are Jean-Dominique Okemba, head of CongoÔÇÖs National Security Council and a nephew of the president, and Jean-Jacques Bouya, the Minister of ÔÇîSpatial Planning Ôüáand Major Works and a cousin of Sassou's.
"Sassou wonÔÇÖt leave power unless he can hand it to someone trusted in his close circle, his son or a trusted ally who guarantees stability," Hoinathy said.
A government spokesperson did not respond to a request for comment on the president's succession plans.
Sassou's campaign has focused on continuity. Five years ago, his slogan was "Let us continue the march towards development". This year, it is "Let us accelerate the march".
For all the turbulence of Sassou's early years in power, his ÔÇőcountry is currently one of Central AfricaÔÇÖs ÔÇőmore stable states after a series Ôüáof upheavals, including the 2023 coup in Gabon and last year's post-electoral violence in Cameroon.
The economy returned to modest per-capita growth in 2024 after a decade-long recession triggered by a slump in global oil prices. Oil still accounts for about half of Congo's ÔÇőGDP and 80% of its exports.
The debt-to-GDP ratio, which peaked at 103.6% in 2020, fell to about 93.6% in 2024, and ÔÇőa three-year IMF programme Ôüácompleted last year helped stabilise public finances. Liquidity pressures remain, with debt servicing on the regional market absorbing half of the country's tax revenues.
Sassou has promised new infrastructure upgrades, increased investment in agriculture and economic diversification, though most ordinary Congolese have seen little improvement to date.
More than half - 52% - of Congo's 6.1 million people live in poverty, a rate unchanged Ôüásince 2021, according ÔÇőto the World Bank. Youth unemployment is around 42% in a country where nearly half the population ÔÇőis under 18.
Meanwhile, French and U.S. prosecutors have launched investigations into assets held abroad by Sassou's relatives. The family has regularly denied any wrongdoing.
"We need better health care and education," Fr├ęd├ęric Nkou, a jobless voter ÔÇőin Brazzaville, told Reuters. "But with this new term, we will experience more of the same."
(This story has been refiled to fix the formatting)

item.title: US warns citizens in Nigeria of possible 'terrorist threat'
item.description: LAGOS, March 10 - The U.S. embassy in Nigeria warned American citizens of ÔÇőa possible "terrorist threat" against U.S. ÔÇîfacilities and affiliated schools in the West African country in a security ÔÇőalert late on Monday.

item.title: UAE condemns drone attack on its consulate in Iraq's Erbil
item.description:
March 10 (Reuters) - UAE's foreign ministry condemned ÔÇőa drone attack ÔÇîon its consulate in Iraqi ÔÇőKurdistan's Erbil, ÔÇőit said in a Ôüástatement early ÔÇőon Tuesday.
Iraqi ÔÇőKurdistan's counter-terrorism service said late on Monday ÔÇőthat it ÔÇőhad downed three drones ÔÇîin ÔüáErbil, with debris from one of the ÔÇődrones ÔÇőfalling Ôüánear the UAE consulate. ÔÇőIt said ÔÇőno Ôüácasualties were reported.

item.title: Turkey says U.S. Patriot system deployed to boost air defence amid Iran war
item.description:
ANKARA, March 10 (Reuters) - Turkey said on Tuesday that a U.S. Patriot air defence system was deployed ÔÇőto its southeast, near a NATO radar base, as part ÔÇîof steps by the alliance to boost air defences in the face of missile threats from the Iran war.
It is being deployed to Malatya province, the ÔÇőlocation of the Kurecik NATO radar base, which provides vital ÔÇődata for the alliance and helped identify two Iranian ballistic Ôüámissiles heading toward Turkey over the last week.
Iran has said it ÔÇőis not at war with regional countries and denies explicitly targeting its neighbour ÔÇőTurkey. Ankara has warned Tehran against firing any more missiles towards it and the two countries' presidents discussed the issue on Monday.
"In addition to the measures we ÔÇőtake on a national level, air and missile defence measures by ÔÇőNATO have been increased. In that framework, one Patriot System is being deployed to ÔÇîMalatya Ôüáto contribute to defending our air space," the defence ministry said.
It added that Turkey would continue to evaluate regional developments and cooperate with NATO allies.
The deployment comes amid reports that Washington is looking into redeploying its military assets, ÔÇőincluding Patriot systems, ÔÇőcurrently stationed in ÔüáSouth Korea.
It was not immediately clear where the Patriot system or its batteries were being redeployed from.
Turkey, ÔÇőan emerging leader in the global defence industry that ÔÇőhas the Ôüáalliance's second-largest army, lacks its own fully fledged air defences despite development efforts, and has relied on NATO air defences stationed in the Ôüáeastern Mediterranean ÔÇőSea in both missile incidents in ÔÇőthe last week.
There is currently one Patriot system, from Spain, deployed in Turkey as part ÔÇőof NATO defences.

item.title: Indian restaurants warn of shutdowns as Iran war makes cooking gas scarce
item.description:
BENGALURU/CHENNAI, March 10 (Reuters) - Restaurants and hotels across India warned of disruptions ÔÇőand possible shutdowns on Tuesday, as the Iran war constricts supplies of cooking gas, prompting authorities to set up ÔÇîa panel to review industry requests.
The fuel shortage comes as the U.S.-Israel war on Iran has halted ship traffic in the Gulf and the Strait of Hormuz, driving up energy prices and transport costs, hitting exports and output from Gulf producers, such as Qatar and Saudi Arabia.
India, the ÔÇőworld's second-biggest importer of liquefied petroleum gas (LPG), invoked emergency powers last week to order refiners to crank up production ÔÇőfor domestic use, leaving the hospitality industry struggling to get sufficient supply.
"We have LPG stock for Ôüátwo days. We are working on contingencies," said Bert Mueller, founder of Mexican food chain California Burrito, with more than ÔÇő100 stores spanning Bengaluru and Chennai in India's south to Delhi and Noida in the north.
"We are conserving gas and installing ÔÇőinduction stoves at certain stores."
India's oil ministry said it had set up , opens new tab a panel to review requests for LPG supply to restaurants and other industries, following appeals from two industry bodies.
"The restaurant industry is predominantly dependent on commercial LPG for its operations," the National Restaurant Association of India, which ÔÇőrepresents more than half a million restaurants, told the food processing ministry on Monday.
"Any disruption therein will lead to a ÔÇőcatastrophic closure," the NRAI said in a letter, while another body, the Federation of Hotel & Restaurant Associations of India, also sought government help.
Indian ÔÇîcompanies Ôüáhave raised LPG prices for the first time in about a year, as the war boosts prices of the imports that fill two-thirds of annual LPG consumption.
India's largest supplier of LNG, Qatar, halted production last week after Iran's strikes on Gulf countries in retaliation for Israeli and U.S. strikes against it.
In the southern tech hub of Bengaluru dubbed India's Silicon Valley, several ÔÇőrestaurants said deliveries had dropped ÔÇősharply, fanning that kitchens will Ôüácome to a halt if the situation is not resolved.
"One of our restaurants did not receive gas cylinders today," Manish V Shetty, who runs the Udupi Food Hub chain of restaurants ÔÇőin Bengaluru told Reuters.
"Thankfully, one of our older vendors helped us," he added, because the ÔÇőchain offers immediate Ôüápayment, rather than credit terms of a week or month. "We're also seeing a spike in the price of the sunflower oil that we use for cooking."
Few restaurants stockpile LPG cylinders, because of safety concerns, relying instead on frequent replacement, said Veerendra Kamat, secretary Ôüáof the ÔÇőBengaluru Hotels Association.
"It's a very grave situation. Most of the (gas) companies have ÔÇőstopped supplying," said Ananth Narayan of the NRAI's Bengaluru branch, adding that even those restaurants that do store gas could run out within a week or ÔÇőtwo.

item.title: Middle East countries cut daily oil output, Bloomberg News reports
item.description:
March 10 (Reuters) - Saudi Arabia has lowered oil ÔÇőoutput by between ÔÇî2 million and 2.5 million ÔÇőbarrels a day, ÔÇőand the United ÔüáArab Emirates has ÔÇőcut its output ÔÇőby 500,00-800,000 barrels a day, Bloomberg News ÔÇőreported on ÔÇőTuesday.
Kuwait has also cut output ÔÇîby Ôüáhalf a million barrels a day, and Iraq ÔÇőby ÔÇőabout Ôüá2.9 million, the report ÔÇőadded, citing ÔÇőpeople Ôüáwith knowledge of the matter.
Reuters could Ôüánot ÔÇőimmediately ÔÇőverify the report.

item.title: India invokes force majeure, gives priority to gas sales to key sectors
item.description:
NEW DELHI, March 10 - India has invoked emergency measures to divert gas supplies from non-priority ÔÇősectors to key users after the ÔÇîdisruption of liquefied natural gas shipments through the Strait of Hormuz, a government notification said.
India meets ÔÇőhalf of its 195 million standard ÔÇőcubic metres per day (mmscmd) of gas consumption Ôüáthrough imports. The country was getting ÔÇőabout 60 mmscmd gas from the Middle East ÔÇőprior to the closure of the Strait of Hormuz and force majeure by Qatar .
Depending on the availability of ÔÇőgas, India will be supplying the ÔÇőfuel in proportion to the average use over the ÔÇîlast Ôüásix months by priority sectors, the notification, issued late on Monday, said.

item.title: German start-up plans 30-megawatt AI data centre in boost to sovereign control
item.description:
FRANKFURT, March 10 (Reuters) - German start-up Polarise plans to build a new 30-megawatt artificial intelligence ÔÇődata centre that would double Germany's domestically-run computing capacity as European nations push ÔÇîto gain more control over critical tech infrastructure.
The facility, set to come online in the Bavarian town of Amberg in mid-2027, could eventually expand to 120 MW, the company told Reuters.
AI data centres in ÔÇőGermany boasted total capacity of 530 MW at the end of last year, according ÔÇőto figures from German lobby group Bitkom. Much of that, however, was Ôüáoperated by non-German providers.
European countries are pushing for more sovereign control over data and ÔÇőthe infrastructure required for AI due to global tensions - from tariffs to armed conflict - as well ÔÇőas sharply diverging rules around online content.
The plans for what would be one of Germany's largest data centres have not been previously reported. Large technology companies like Google (GOOGL.O) , opens new tab and Amazon's (AMZN.O) , opens new tab ÔÇőAWS typically run data centres of around 100 MW or more.
Polarise, which operates 13 ÔÇődata centres in Germany and abroad, declined to give details on the size of the required investment.
"The final ÔÇîinvestment Ôüávolume depends heavily on how many customers install their own servers or rent computing power," said Marc Gazivoda, Polarise's marketing director, adding that it did not receive state subsidies and investments could vary.
A source close to the company said the first stage of the project would ÔÇőcost in the "triple-digit million ÔÇőeuro range", which Ôüáincludes the main infrastructure but not the chips themselves. The final cost would depend on the number of chips required and their specific ÔÇőtypes, the source said.
A 12-MW data centre opened by Polarise in ÔÇőMunich last Ôüámonth cost 1 billion euros ($1.16 billion), according to an estimate by Deutsche Telekom. That facility already doubled Germany's existing locally-operated capacity.
With oil prices shooting above $100 per barrel, the cost of energy for Ôüápower-hungry ÔÇődata centres is increasingly in focus.
Polarise said its partner ÔÇőWV Energie will install a wind and solar power plant for the centre as well as batteries for temporary ÔÇőstorage of electricity.
($1 = 0.8640 euros)

item.title: Horse racing-Leading British horses line up for stellar first day as Cheltenham Festival begins
item.description:
LONDON, March 10 (Reuters) - British trainers Dan Skelton and Nicky Henderson will take on the might of the Irish at jump racing's showcase event with the likes of The New Lion ÔÇőand Lulamba vying for a share of the five-million-pound ($6.70 million) prize pot as the Cheltenham Festival opens on Tuesday.
Attracting some 200,000 racegoers across four ÔÇîdays, the festival is worth an estimated 274 million pounds to the local economy boosting local restaurants, hotels and pubs.
It is also horse racing's biggest betting week of the year with bookmaker William Hill predicting 450 million pounds will be wagered across Britain. Guinness, reduced in price by 30 pence, will be drunk by the gallon.
But for those at the heart of the sport, it is all about the horses ÔÇőand whether this year's crop of jumps stars can rival the Irish.
It is almost two decades since the golden years of British jump racing when Somerset-based Paul ÔÇőNicholls and his crop of horses like dual Gold Cup-winner Kauto Star and 18-times consecutive hurdle winner Big Buck's outclassed the rest, before Ôüáthe pendulum swung in favour of the Irish.
Since then, Willie Mullins' sizeable team has continued to dominate the festival, racking up 113 winners to snatch the leading trainer title ÔÇő12 times. Backed by big-spending owners like former Barclays executive Rich Ricci and businessman J.P. McManus, this year's squad looks no less dangerous.
Mullins' pick for jump racing's most prestigious chase is ÔÇőGaelic Warrior who looks set to go off favourite in Friday's feature, the Gold Cup, a race that has so far evaded owner Susannah Ricci. Before that, Majborough is favourite for the Queen Mother Champion Chase on Wednesday.
In with a strong chance of ending Irish dominance is a fresh crop of talented trainers that includes Skelton and Ben Pauling who will take on Mullins in the 625,000-pound Gold Cup ÔÇőwith the fancied Grey Dawning and the Harry Redknapp-owned King George VI Chase winner The Jukebox Man.
Battle lines will first be drawn in Tuesday's opening race, the Grade 1 ÔÇőSupreme Novices' Hurdle, when Mullins' Mighty Park takes on British hope and three-time winning 2/1 favourite Old Park Star, trained by Henderson.
Henderson has made no secret of the fact a win on Tuesday would ÔÇîbe a Ôüábig boost for Seven Barrows, setting a strong tone for the week, after Constitution Hill and Sir Gino were sidelined. He also sends out highly fancied Lulamba in the Arkle Novices' Chase and Jango Baie in the Gold Cup.
Were Britain to also clinch Tuesday's feature race, the 450,000-pound Champion Hurdle in which Mullins' prolific mare Lossiemouth will battle with Skelton's The New Lion, nerves may start jangling in the Irish camp.
Skelton sends his strongest ever team to Cheltenham, lining up some 30-35 horses. But while he's bullish about some big British winners, ÔÇőhe described his chances of snatching the ÔÇőtop trainer prize at the festival as "a Ôüápossibility... not a probability."
"I've thought about it because I'm a competitive bugger but the reality is Willie will be leading trainer," he told the Racing Post on Sunday. "With a bit of luck, we might be pitching behind him along with a few others."
Ireland has won ÔÇőCheltenham's Prestbury Cup title, awarded to the country fielding the most winners, outright in nine of the last 10 years.
Cheltenham's opening ÔÇőday also marks an important Ôüáone for British racing, which is in the spotlight for governance issues at its regulator and recent moves by gaming companies to reduce sponsorship in light of higher taxes .
Cheltenham is also looking to recover from lower ticket sales in recent years, something CEO Guy Lavender is trying to address by lowering the cost of Guinness to 2022 rates, reducing restrictions on drinking areas and reintroducing ÔüáLadies Day ÔÇőon Wednesday.
"There is no doubt that everyone operating in the leisure sector is having to work harder to ÔÇőmake their offerings stand out to the customer," said Lavender.
"However, British racing continues to provide an exceptional day out and we saw total attendance across all 60 racecourses top 5 million last year for the first time ÔÇősince 2019."
Lavender added he was "very positive" about ticket sales with growth from last year. "We are definitely moving in the right direction," he said.
($1 = 0.7460 pounds)

item.title: Russia is the only winner of Middle East war, EU's Costa says
item.description:
BRUSSELS, March 10 (Reuters) - Russia has so far been the only winner from the war in the ÔÇőMiddle East as energy prices soar and ÔÇîattention for its war against Ukraine has faded, EU Council President Antonio Costa said on Tuesday.
"So ÔÇőfar, there is only one winner ÔÇőin this war ÔÇô Russia," Costa said in Ôüáa speech to EU ambassadors in Brussels.
"It ÔÇőgains new resources to finance its war ÔÇőagainst Ukraine as energy prices rise. It profits from the diversion of military capabilities that could otherwise have ÔÇőbeen sent to support Ukraine. And ÔÇőit benefits from reduced attention to the Ukrainian front ÔÇîas Ôüáthe conflict in the Middle East takes centre stage."
Costa stressed the need for the EU to protect the international rules-based order, which ÔÇőhe said ÔÇőwas now Ôüábeing challenged by the United States, and for all parties in ÔÇőthe Middle East to return to ÔÇőthe Ôüánegotiating table.
"Freedom and human rights cannot be achieved through bombs. Only international law upholds them," Ôüáhe ÔÇősaid.
"We must avoid further escalation. ÔÇőSuch a path threatens the Middle East, Europe, and ÔÇőbeyond."

item.title: How many people have been killed in the US-Israel war on Iran?
item.description:
March 10 - Scores of people have been killed across the Middle East since the U.S. and Israel attacked Iran on February 28, with Gulf states that host U.S. military bases and personnel ÔÇőas well as Lebanon quickly drawn into the conflict.
Here are the death tolls from the war ÔÇîas reported by countries as of March 9, more than a week after it began. Reuters has not independently verified these deaths.

item.title: Israel ahead of schedule on war goals in Iran, ambassador to France says
item.description:
PARIS, March 10 (Reuters) - Israel is ahead of schedule in achieving its war goals in Iran, its ambassador to France ÔÇősaid on Tuesday, adding that the operation aims ÔÇîto weaken IranÔÇÖs authorities to curb attacks beyond its borders and allow its people to shape their own future.
"When we were asked at ÔÇőthe start of this war about its duration, ÔÇőwe said it would last a few weeks. That ÔüáhasnÔÇÖt changed," Joshua Zarka told BFM TV. "We are ahead ÔÇőof schedule to achieve our war objectives."
Zarka, formerly IsraelÔÇÖs lead ÔÇődiplomat on Iran, said the goals extend beyond ending IranÔÇÖs nuclear programme. They include weakening the government to the point that its population ÔÇőcan "take its fate into its own hands," and ensuring ÔÇőTehran can no longer mount attacks beyond its borders.
Asked about the appointment ÔÇîof ÔüáMojtaba Khamenei as the new Supreme Leader, Zarka said that if he follows his father Ali Khamenei's positions, he "would also be on a list of those who should be eliminated."
Israel ÔÇőhas simultaneously ÔÇőlaunched major operations Ôüáagainst IranÔÇĹbacked Hezbollah after it struck Israeli territory. The Lebanese government has said it wants ÔÇődirect talks with Israel to halt the ÔÇőfighting, but ÔüáZarka argued that Beirut is not disarming Hezbollah.
"At this stage, IÔÇÖm not aware of any decision to enter negotiations to Ôüáend ÔÇőthis war," he said. "What would end ÔÇőit is the disarmament of Hezbollah ÔÇö and that is a choice for the ÔÇőLebanese government."

item.title: Over 10,000 Chinese citizens returned from Middle East amid war
item.description: BEIJING, March 10 (Reuters) - Chinese authorities and airlines have ÔÇőassisted over 10,000 Chinese citizens in returning from ÔÇîthe Middle East, the foreign ministry said on Tuesday, after the war in the region forced airspace closures and grounded ÔÇőcommercial flights.

item.title: Bangladesh secures diesel supplies amid major energy disruptions, sources say
item.description:
DHAKA, March 10 (Reuters) - Bangladesh has begun receiving diesel from suppliers including China and India, with officials ÔÇősaying the country now has enough fuel to cover about one month of demand, with arrangements under ÔÇîway for another month, after the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran disrupted shipments, including to the massive garments industry.
The South Asian nation of 175 million people, which relies on imports for roughly 95% of its energy needs, has imposed fuel rationing for vehicles, restrictions on diesel sales and closed universities as the war ÔÇőon Iran causes severe disruption to Middle East oil exports.
Some garment makers in Bangladesh, the worldÔÇÖs secondÔÇĹlargest clothing exporter ÔÇőafter China, have already complained they do not have enough diesel to run their back-up generators when Ôüápublic utilities cut power supplies.
Mahmud Hasan Khan, president of the Bangladesh Garment Manufacturers and Exporters Association, said power cuts had ÔÇődoubled to up to five hours a day since the war began on February 28.
"When power goes out, we have to ÔÇőrun our standby generators on diesel, but we canÔÇÖt buy enough diesel anymore, and itÔÇÖs causing problems for us," he said.
State-run Bangladesh Petroleum Corporation (BPC) is receiving around 60,000 metric tons of diesel from three traders, with a further 90,000 metric tons scheduled to arrive later this month, two energy ÔÇőofficials said, requesting anonymity because they were not authorised to speak to the media.
A 27,000-metric-ton diesel cargo from PetroChina arrived ÔÇőat Chittagong port on Monday, while a cargo of 28,000 metric tons from Vitol is waiting at the portÔÇÖs outer anchorage, a BPC official ÔÇîsaid.
In addition, Ôüáaround 5,000 metric tons of diesel is being supplied through a cross-border pipeline from IndiaÔÇÖs Numaligarh Refinery. Officials said talks are ongoing to secure some 30,000 metric tons from Indian Oil (IOC.NS) , opens new tab .
"We have supplies for one month, and another month is being arranged,ÔÇŁ a BPC official said.
The BPC did not immediately respond to requests for comment.
Bangladesh normally needs about 380,000 metric tons of diesel ÔÇőeach month, but officials said ÔÇőthat since rationing began, one Ôüáestimate put current monthly requirements at roughly 270,000 metric tons.
Officials said securing refined fuel cargoes had been relatively easy because most of the traders did not rely on the disrupted Gulf shipping ÔÇőroutes used for crude oil shipments.
However, prices have increased as a result of the intense market ÔÇővolatility triggered by Ôüáthe Middle East crisis, they added.
Bangladesh also imports around 1.4 million metric tons of crude oil a year for its refineries under long-term contracts with Saudi Aramco and Abu Dhabi National Oil Company (ADNOC).
However, supplies from Saudi Aramco and ADNOC could be affected because their Ôüácrude shipments ÔÇőto Bangladesh must pass through the Strait of Hormuz. One Aramco cargo ÔÇőof about 100,000 tons has already been delayed in the Gulf due to the ongoing crisis, officials said.
Severe gas shortages have already forced Bangladesh to halt operations ÔÇőat four of its five stateÔÇĹrun fertiliser factories, redirecting available gas to power plants.

item.title: Greenpeace activists storm stage at France's nuclear summit, confront Macron
item.description:
PARIS, March 10 (Reuters) - Two Greenpeace activists broke onto the stage at the start of a global nuclear summit in ÔÇőFrance on Tuesday, interrupting President Emmanuel Macron and U.N. ÔÇînuclear watchdog chief Rafael Grossi as they were greeting heads of state.
The protesters, dressed sharply in black suits and ties, held banners bearing the Greenpeace logo ÔÇőand reading "Nuclear Power = Energy Insecurity" and "Nuclear power fuels RussiaÔÇÖs war".
One ÔÇőof them shouted at Macron, "Why are we still buying Ôüáuranium from Russia?" to which the president replied, "We produce nuclear power ÔÇőourselves."
France has its own uranium enrichment capacity, but also imports enriched ÔÇőuranium for its power plants, including from Russia, according to the latest customs data published by the French government.
RussiaÔÇÖs state nuclear company Rosatom accounted for about ÔÇő44% of the global uranium enrichment capacity in 2025, according to ÔÇőthe World Nuclear Association, and European nuclear power producers have struggled to wean ÔÇîthemselves Ôüáoff these supplies four years after Russia invaded Ukraine.
Around 15 Greenpeace activists blocked arriving convoys outside the venue in Boulogne-Billancourt on the outskirts of Paris on Tuesday, the environmental campaigning group said in ÔÇőa statement.
France is ÔÇőhosting the Ôüásecond world nuclear energy summit on Tuesday, where world leaders will meet to discuss and promote nuclear ÔÇőpower.
"For Greenpeace France, the holding of such a ÔÇősummit is Ôüáan anachronism, an event completely out of touch with reality and with the lessons to be learned from the tragic situations of the ÔüáRussian ÔÇőaggression in Ukraine, the strikes on ÔÇőIran, and the impacts of the worsening climate disruption," the group said.

item.title: 'My friends are still in jail': Cubans take to the streets, but fear crossing line
item.description:
SAN ANTONIO DE LOS BA├ĹOS, Cuba, March 10 (Reuters) - Without warning, the sleepy Cuban farm town of San Antonio de los Ba├▒os burst into protest one morning nearly five years ago, triggering a series of copycat demonstrations across the country that shook the Communist government unlike any event in decades.
Frustrated by chronic shortages of food and medicine and ÔÇőlengthy power outages as the coronavirus pandemic lingered, protesters marched in the streets chanting ÔÇťPatria y Vida,ÔÇŁ or ÔÇťHomeland and Life,ÔÇŁ the title of a popular song that inverts the Communist slogan "Patria o Muerte," or "Homeland or Death."
The phrase became a rallying cry for a movement that ÔÇîwas quickly crushed by state security forces.
Now, with Cuba under a U.S.-imposed oil blockade and sanctions that have thrown the country into a deeper crisis, Reuters visited the same neighborhoods where police and protesters clashed most fiercely on July 11, 2021: San Antonio de los Ba├▒os and the marginalized Havana neighborhood of La G├╝inera.
In interviews with 12 people on the record, and several others who declined to be identified, Reuters found that people remember the brutality of the last crackdown, a testament to the enduring power of CubaÔÇÖs security apparatus. Some, however, were eager to place blame on Washington and the punishing U.S. economic sanctions against Cuba.
While Cubans have expressed frustration over lengthy power blackouts, banging on kitchen pots in a plea to turn the ÔÇőlights back on, for now, at least, a repeat of July 11 seems unlikely. Dissident leaders have largely been jailed or exiled since then, and the government has shut off the internet at signs of protests, as it did that day. Fear, though, is also a factor.
ÔÇťI assure ÔÇőyou, people won't protest in the streets because they're afraid,ÔÇŁ said Brian Jimenez, 26, a baker from San Antonio de los Ba├▒os, who said he was beaten by police that day and detained for several days afterward.
Police showed restraint Ôüáat first, he said, but then came the feared "black berets," a brigade of the Interior Ministry, and "they roughed up the people," setting an example that was still reverberating.
"A lot of my friends are still in jail," Jimenez said.
Reuters could not verify his account of police beatings of detainees, though other demonstrators and human ÔÇőrights groups reported similar accounts of abuse.
The Cuban government did not reply to a request for comment.
Cubans expressed a range of opinions, from sympathy toward the Cuban government in the face of U.S. antagonism to openly criticizing the Communist government for failing to produce prosperity. Others called for ÔÇődialogue and understanding.
But all agreed there was little prospect of another massive popular uprising any time soon, citing the government's violent repression of 2021, resulting in long prison terms for many protesters, while also observing that demonstrations failed to produce any meaningful change.
Though only 40 km (25 miles) outside Havana, San Antonio de los Ba├▒os, population 50,000, is a world apart, typical of many towns across the eyebrow-shaped island. Tractors and carts drawn by scraggly horses, their ribs showing through weather-beaten hides, are almost as common as cars on the streets, where vendors sell locally grown potatoes, onions and cucumbers.
At the town square where many Cubans gathered in front of the church that day, university student Robert Perez said the government has retaliated against ÔÇőJuly 11 protesters and their families, effectively causing self-censorship.
"When you protest, you can't find work. They take action against you or your family," said Perez, 27, a sociology student at Artemisa University. "In Cuba, everyone knows that one way to make a living is to open a small business and sell goods. Those who ÔÇőrose up on July 11th can't do that."
Rights groups, the European Union and the United States estimate Cuba has jailed between 1,000 and 1,500 people since those widespread protests, many of whom continue to serve extended sentences.
The Cuban government did not respond to a query on how many prisoners it currently holds on convictions related to July 11 ÔÇîprotests.
Cuban authorities say those Ôüájailed were found guilty of crimes including public disorder, resisting arrest, robbery and vandalism. Cuba blames the United States for funding the unrest and encouraging its spread, part of a broader plot to overthrow the island's government.
Frustration over blackouts sometimes spills into the streets. Reuters witnessed several demonstrations in poor neighborhoods of Havana on Saturday night, as people banged on pots. In one instance, the lights came back on mid-protest. The protest promptly ended.
"We're tired of so many days of blackouts. Three days in a row of the same," said a man who identified himself as Edward Rafael, 18.
On Monday, about 20 students sat on the steps of the University of Havana in protest, airing grievances over blackouts and internet disruptions that have made remote learning almost impossible, as classes have been suspended during the crisis.
Long before July 11, the United States imposed the most comprehensive and longestÔÇĹrunning unilateral sanctions regime in the world on Cuba, largely with the intent to pressure and destabilize ÔÇőthe Communist government in the name of promoting democracy and human rights.
The ÔÇőUnited States has further tightened the screws this year, capturing Venezuelan President ÔüáNicolas Maduro, Cuba's most important patron. It then imposed a near-total oil blockade on Cuba and joined Israel in attacking another longtime Washington adversary, Iran. Trump has said his government is negotiating with Cuban leaders to reach some kind of deal, though Cuba has yet to acknowledge official talks.
While tempers may flare should the shortages grow more acute, for now some Cubans are turning their ire toward Washington, not Havana.
Nieves Fuentes Osoria, an 82-year-old woman from San Antonio de los Ba├▒os, ÔÇőcalled the U.S. embargo of Cuba a "genocide." She said Cuba would show the same type of fierce resistance as it did in the Bay of Pigs invasion of 1961, when the armed forces defeated an invasion of ÔÇőexiles financed and trained by the United States.
"When Ôüáwe're in danger, this is what we do," she said, holding up two fists. "Because I saw it on different occasions. When they tried to come through the Bay of Pigs, we stuck together."
Others took a neutral political stand but still concluded the protests went for naught.
"What did they accomplish? Nothing," said Deisy Garcia, 73. "They made a fuss, stirred up all this, and life goes on as usual."
Back in Havana, the streets of La G├╝inera bustled under the midday heat, despite the fuel blockade and lengthy blackouts. Pedestrians and electric motorbikes navigated the potholed streets, while barbers clipped hair with rechargeable cordless razors and food kiosks sold whatever produce was available. ÔüáOn this day, tomatoes ÔÇőwere abundant at several points of sale.
Yuniel Romero, 37, doubted there would be another uprising, as he cut hair from the front porch of a high-street business.
"The people are not going to ÔÇőget involved because in real life nobody wants to be imprisoned again. The people have no way to defend themselves," Romero said.
Ariel Anillo, 52, sold sacks of charcoal, suddenly in demand due to a lack of cooking gas, beneath a poster of Fidel Castro.
He said most Cubans were persevering - "the lights go out for three days and nobody dies" - but the repression was also a factor.
"You know ÔÇőthat if you go to a protest they can put you in prison for 15 to 20 years," Anillo said. "Then the situation is worse, and your family suffers."

item.title: Suspected Hamas member detained in Cyprus over weapons procurement
item.description:
BERLIN, March 10 (Reuters) - Cypriot authorities have detained a suspected member of Palestinian militant group Hamas ÔÇőwanted in Germany for procuring weapons ÔÇîand ammunition for attacks on Israeli or Jewish facilities, German federal prosecutors said on Tuesday.
The Lebanese-born ÔÇősuspect, identified only as Kamel M. ÔÇőin line with German privacy rules, Ôüáwas detained at Cyprus' Larnaca airport on ÔÇőMarch 6, arriving from Lebanon, they added ÔÇőin a statement.
The suspect is wanted in relation to the transport of 300 rounds of live ammunition, ÔÇőaccording to prosecutors. It wasn't clear ÔÇőfrom the statement where the rounds had come from, ÔÇîor Ôüáwhere they were thought to be heading.
"The operation served as preparation for deadly Hamas attacks on Israeli or Jewish institutions in ÔÇőGermany and ÔÇőEurope," they Ôüásaid.
Police also searched the suspect's apartment in Berlin.
Once Kamel M. is ÔÇőextradited to Germany, a judge ÔÇőwill Ôüádecide on pre-trial detention, the statement said.
Attacks against Jews and Jewish targets have risen worldwide Ôüásince ÔÇőIsrael's war against Hamas in ÔÇőGaza, triggered by the militant group's 2023 attacks on ÔÇőIsrael.

item.title: Sri Lanka says second Iranian ship nine nautical miles off its coast
item.description: NEW DELHI, March 10 (Reuters) - A second ÔÇîIranian ship is ÔÇőcurrently ÔÇőnine nautical miles Ôüáfrom ÔÇőSri Lanka's ÔÇőcoast, a spokesperson for ÔÇőSri ÔÇőLanka's cabinet said ÔÇîon ÔüáTuesday, adding that it ÔÇőwould ÔÇőeventually Ôüábe moved.

item.title: Kremlin says Moscow mobile internet outages are done for sake of security
item.description:
MOSCOW, March 10 (Reuters) - The Kremlin on Tuesday said that recent instances of ÔÇőmobile internet being turned off in ÔÇîMoscow and other major cities were aimed at ensuring security and were in accordance ÔÇőwith the law.
In a call with ÔÇőreporters, Kremlin spokesman Dmitry Peskov said: "All Ôüádisconnections and communication restrictions are carried ÔÇőout in strict compliance with current legislation."
"This ÔÇőis all related to the need to ensure security," he added.
Peskov said that the impact ÔÇőof the move on businesses needed ÔÇőadditional analysis, and that solutions to their problems would ÔÇîbe Ôüáproposed.
On a call with reporters on Monday after Russian President Vladimir Putin spoke to U.S. President Donald Trump, Kremlin ÔÇőforeign policy ÔÇőaide Yury ÔüáUshakov's connection cut out at least three times, Reuters reporters ÔÇőon the call said.
In recent ÔÇőweeks, Ôüáthe Kremlin has moved to block messenger apps Telegram and WhatsApp, promoting the Ôüástate-backed ÔÇőservice MAX, which critics ÔÇősay is not secure.

item.title: Exxon looks to ditch┬áNew Jersey incorporation for Texas homecoming
item.description:
HOUSTON/BOSTON, March 10 (Reuters) - Exxon Mobil (XOM.N) , opens new tab is seeking to abandon its corporate registration in New Jersey and reincorporate ÔÇîin Texas where its headquarters is located, the U.S. oil major said on Tuesday, in a move that could help bolster its defenses against activist shareholders and climate advocates.
Exxon announced the proposal in a proxy filing. If the move is approved by ÔÇőshareholders, Exxon would become the latest of several high-profile companies including SpaceX, Tesla (TSLA.O) , opens new tab and Coinbase (COIN.O) , opens new tab to register in ÔÇőthe business-friendly Lone Star State.
Last year, a new Texas law enhanced legal protections for Ôüábusinesses through several mechanisms, including reducing the threat of shareholder litigation by allowing companies to set stock ownership ÔÇőthresholds for lawsuits.
"The Board believes Texas legislators, judges, and juries who might make decisions that impact Exxon Mobil are ÔÇőgenerally more familiar with our business and operations," the company said in the filing.
The top U.S. oil producer, physically headquartered in Spring, Texas, has long been the target of environment-related lawsuits.
New Jersey officials sued Exxon, Chevron (CVX.N) , opens new tab and other fossil-fuel companies in 2022, ÔÇőalleging that they contributed to climate change, forcing the state to spend billions of dollars to clean ÔÇőup after major natural disasters such as Superstorm Sandy and Hurricane Ida. The suit was dismissed last year.
Incorporating in the same ÔÇîstate Ôüáas a company's headquarters can help executives get the attention of state politicians whose help they might need on taxes or other policy questions, said Jill Fisch, a law professor at the University of Pennsylvania.
ÔÇťOne way you might show your loyalty and get the legislators to pay attention to you is to incorporate in your ÔÇőhome state,ÔÇŁ she said.
Exxon's ÔÇőroots in New Jersey Ôüátrace back to its formation after the breakup of Standard Oil in the early 1900s, though its headquarters has been in Texas since 1989.
In September, Exxon took a novel ÔÇőstep to help fend off activist shareholder resolutions during annual meetings, introducing a program ÔÇőto allow Ôüáretail investors to automatically cast their ballots in step with the board's recommendations.
Nearly 40% of the company's shares are held by individuals but just a quarter of them vote during proxy season, though they mostly support the board, Ôüáthe company ÔÇősaid at the time.
Texas has sought to reinforce its reputation as ÔÇőa safe haven for business and has been a major beneficiary as some companies have departed states such as Delaware, a top destination ÔÇőfor incorporation.

item.title: India gains favour with global investors on returns, ESG momentum
item.description:
MUMBAI, March 10 (Reuters) - India is maturing as a market in which foreign investors who take into account ÔÇőenvironmental, social and governance (ESG) factors are confident in dedicating more ÔÇîof their portfolio to, according to fund managers at the Indian Venture and Alternate Capital Association conclave in Mumbai, who cite the economy's scale, improving return outlook and advancing ÔÇőESG standards as key differentiators.
India's market regulator SEBI has been ÔÇőexpanding the scope of ESG-related regulations for over a decade, Ôüámaking detailed Business and Sustainability Report disclosures mandatory for the ÔÇőcountry's top 1000 listed companies since 2023.
"India is clearly earning its space ÔÇőin terms of allocations from global investors," said Ralph Keitel, head of Fund Investments at Zurich-based responsAbility Investment, speaking to Reuters at the conclave.
"Fifteen to 20 years ÔÇőago, India was a great space, but returns took longer," Keitel ÔÇősaid. Now he sees a stronger near-term case as well.
Investors also said the ÔÇîcountry's Ôüáappeal rests on its relative resilience at a time when global allocations are being reassessed across China, the United States and other emerging markets.
India's economy is projected to grow 6.5% in both fiscal years 2024-25 and 2025-26, ÔÇőaccording to the ÔÇőInternational Monetary Fund.
"Besides Ôüárisk mitigation, ESG impact ÔÇö having (a) good impact on communities adds value," Neha Grover, South Asia Lead for ÔÇőPrivate Equity at IFC told Reuters. Grover added that India has become ÔÇőone Ôüáof the most consistent emerging markets for profitable exits, supported by a strengthening initial public offering (IPO) pipeline.
ResponsAbility Investment's Keitel also pointed to climateÔÇĹrelated opportunities as Ôüáa ÔÇőcore area of interest, citing sectors such ÔÇőas mobility and solar panels. As incomes rise, environmental pressures tend to intensify, he added.

item.title: Russian strike on Ukraine's Sloviansk kills four, local official says
item.description:
KYIV, March 10 (Reuters) - A Russian ÔÇőstrike on the ÔÇîeastern Ukrainian frontline city ÔÇőof ÔÇőSloviansk killed four people Ôüáand ÔÇőinjured 16 others, ÔÇőlocal governor Vadym Filashkin said ÔÇőon Tuesday.
Filashkin ÔÇősaid Russia had ÔÇîdropped Ôüáthree guided bombs on the city, ÔÇőand ÔÇőthat Ôüáa 14-year-old girl ÔÇőwas among ÔÇőthose Ôüáwounded.

item.title: China ready to work with countries on inclusive nuclear governance, Vice Premier says
item.description:
PARIS, March 10 (Reuters) - China stands ready ÔÇőto work ÔÇîwith countries on a more ÔÇőinclusive nuclear ÔÇőgovernance and development, ÔüáChinese Vice ÔÇőPremier Zhang Guoqing ÔÇősaid on Tuesday.
"We should open an ÔÇőinternational ÔÇődivision of labour for ÔÇîan Ôüáinclusive workforce across nuclear projects," Zhang Guoqing ÔÇősaid ÔÇőat Ôüáthe world nuclear ÔÇőenergy summit ÔÇőin ÔüáParis.

item.title: Court bars Kosovo president from announcing snap election date
item.description:
PRISTINA, March 10 (Reuters) - Kosovo's highest court told the president late on Monday not to announce the date for a snap election before March 31, ÔÇőa move that further prolongs a political crisis that has ÔÇîengulfed the tiny Balkan nation.
Last Friday, President Vjosa Osmani dissolved the parliament and called a snap election - without setting a date - after lawmakers failed to pick a ÔÇőnew head of state within the constitutional deadline that expired at ÔÇőmidnight on Thursday.
Her move extended a political deadlock in ÔüáKosovo, which is Europe's youngest nation and has aspirations to join the ÔÇőEuropean Union. Kosovo had no functioning government for most of last year ÔÇőas the fractured parliament failed to elect a speaker for months.
In a verdict published late on Monday, the Constitutional Court temporarily barred the president from setting an ÔÇőelection date and also froze the parliament's efforts to elect a ÔÇőnew head of state. The court decision stays in effect until March 31.
Prime Minister ÔÇîAlbin ÔüáKurti had asked the Constitutional Court to rule on whether Osmani had acted in line with the constitution. Kurti is a former ally of Osmani but decided not to back her for a second term in ÔÇőthe ceremonial role ÔÇőof president.
Kurti's government Ôüáwelcomed the court verdict but added: "For three weeks we will be in a state of waiting, while ÔÇőa number of agreements and decisions of importance for ÔÇőcitizens continue Ôüáto remain without approval by the parliament."
The parliamentary election, if it goes ahead, would be the third in just over a year.
Choosing a president - Ôüáthough the ÔÇőrole brings no significant political powers - has ÔÇőalways proven politically challenging because two-thirds of lawmakers must attend the voting session in parliament, ÔÇőmaking it easy to disrupt.

item.title: US designation of Afghanistan 'regrettable,' Afghan Taliban says
item.description:
March 10 (Reuters) - The Afghan Taliban on Tuesday ÔÇőcalled the U.S decision ÔÇîto designate Afghanistan a "state sponsor of ÔÇőwrongful detention" regrettable, ÔÇőand said it wanted Ôüáto resolve the ÔÇőmatter through dialogue ÔÇőafter the U.S. demanded the release of U.S. ÔÇőcitizens held.
U.S. Secretary ÔÇőof State Marco Rubio said on ÔÇîMonday Ôüáhe was designating the Taliban government a "state sponsor of ÔÇőwrongful ÔÇődetention," Ôüáand urged Kabul to free ÔÇőall detained ÔÇőU.S. Ôüácitizens, including Mahmood Habibi and Dennis Coyle.

item.title: Morning Bid: Oil's combustible calm
item.description:
March 10 - What matters in U.S. and global markets today
By Mike Dolan , opens new tab , Editor-At-Large, Finance and Markets
The fog of war remains thick. Crude oil prices have become wildly volatile this week, with intraday swings exceeding $30 per barrel - some of the biggest one-day moves ÔÇőon record.
After soaring as high as $120 early Monday - the highest in four years - oil prices then slumped back below $100 later in the day after ÔÇîPresident Trump once again teased the prospect of a short-lived conflict with Iran, saying the war was ÔÇťvery completeÔÇŁ.
IÔÇÖll get into that and more below.
But first, check out my latest column on why inflation isn't the only risk keeping central bankers up at night as the conflict in Iran continues.
And listen to the latest episode of the Morning Bid daily podcast where I discuss how headlineÔÇĹdriven oil swings are ÔÇőrewiring stocks, bonds and rate expectations.
Subscribe to hear Reuters journalists discuss the biggest news in markets and finance seven days a week.
TrumpÔÇÖs apparent ÔÇőabout-face on Monday not only calmed oil prices but also the complex of world stocks and bonds that had shivered after oil's Ôüátriple-digit price spike. Wall Street stocks closed up on Monday, while on Tuesday South KoreaÔÇÖs KOSPI index regained nearly 6% and JapanÔÇÖs Nikkei climbed nearly 3%.
Meantime, U.S. ÔÇőTreasury yields tumbled and the dollar took a breather on Tuesday, steadying against major currencies, helping gold edge up in turn. U.S. stock futures were up ahead of the bell, having ÔÇőremained remarkably calm amid yesterday's tumult.
Many will say this is the TACO (ÔÇťTrump always chickens outÔÇŁ) trade par excellence, but there are few signs that TrumpÔÇÖs optimistic turn is playing out on the ground, with IranÔÇÖs Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps still asserting that no oil will be exported as long as U.S.-Israeli attacks continued.
Responding to that over social media, Trump threatened further retaliation against Iran if ÔÇőit continued to disrupt oil flows through the Strait of Hormuz. So, for now, the tit-for-tat looks set to continue.
Through all of this, oil prices held above $90 per ÔÇőbarrel - a level that would have seemed frightening only last month. And pass-through to fuel costs is already being felt in the U.S., where a comfortable majority of Americans now believe prices ÔÇîwill worsen Ôüáover the next year.
In the background, G7 finance ministers on Monday mulled a possible joint release of their oil reserves to calm the horses, though they stopped short of doing so now , with a G7 official telling Reuters that the decision was ÔÇťjust about timingÔÇŁ.
Elsewhere, China reported a spectacular surge in its trade surplus for the first two months of the year, with exports up more than 20% year-on-year. While that certainly supports its new growth target of just under 5% and comes despite falling bilateral trade with the U.S., ÔÇőit also predates this monthÔÇÖs spike in ÔÇőoil prices.
Later in the day, after Ôüáthe bell, Oracle is set to report earnings, with traders likely to be on the lookout for signs of a payoff from spiralling capex as it ploughs billions into AI data center expansion.
Chart of the day
China roared into 2026 with exports far outstripping ÔÇőforecasts, fuelled by red-hot electronics demand, putting the economy on track to top last year's record $1.2 trillion trade surplus, despite ÔÇőthe persistent drop in Ôüábilateral trade with the United States.
Although the data preceded this month's energy and shipping shock from the war in Iran, outbound shipments from the world's second-largest economy grew 21.8% in U.S. dollar terms in the January-February period - up sharply from the 6.6% increase recorded in December and blowing past the median forecast of 7.1% in a Reuters poll.
Today's events to Ôüáwatch
* U.S. ÔÇőFebruary existing home sales (10:00 AM EST)
* U.S. 3-year note auction
* U.S. corporate earnings: Oracle, Domino's Pizza
Want to ÔÇőreceive the Morning Bid in your inbox every weekday morning? Sign up for the newsletter here . You can find ROI on the Reuters website , opens new tab , and you can follow us on LinkedIn , opens new tab and X , opens new tab .
Opinions expressed are those of the ÔÇőauthor. They do not reflect the views of Reuters News, which, under the Trust Principles , opens new tab , is committed to integrity, independence, and freedom from bias.

item.title: US ready to work with other countries to develop civil nuclear power, official says
item.description: PARIS, March 10 (Reuters) - The United States ÔÇőis ready to ÔÇîwork with other countries ÔÇőto develop ÔÇőcivil nuclear power to Ôüámeet the ÔÇőglobe's growing energy ÔÇődemand needs, including from data centres, ÔÇőThomas DiNanno, ÔÇőthe U.S. under secretary ÔÇîfor Ôüáarms control and international security, said on ÔÇőTuesday ÔÇőat Ôüáa nuclear energy ÔÇőconference in ÔÇőParis.

item.title: Al Qaeda-linked group killed at least 12 truck drivers in Mali, HRW says
item.description: DAKAR, March 10 (Reuters) - Islamist militants from an al Qaeda-linked group killed 10 long-haul truck drivers and two ÔÇőteenage apprentices who were travelling through Mali's western ÔÇîKayes region in late January, Human Rights Watch (HRW) said on Tuesday.

item.title: Qatar wants to bolster security partnership with US after Iran's strikes
item.description:
DOHA, March 10 (Reuters) - Qatar wants to strengthen its defence partnership with the United States in ÔÇőthe wake of Iranian air strikes on Qatari territory, ÔÇîthe foreign ministry spokesperson said on Tuesday, even as it sees the existing deal as an important deterrent.
Iran launched missile and drone attacks ÔÇőon its oil-producing Gulf neighbours after U.S.-Israeli strikes that ÔÇőkilled its top leaders. The conflict disrupted output in the Ôüáregion and sent oil prices surging.
Qatar hosts the biggest U.S. ÔÇőbase in the Middle East, Al Udeid Air Base, which ÔÇőwas attacked by Iran during the war that erupted on February 28.
The spokesperson, Majed Al Ansari, told a media briefing on Tuesday the security partnership ÔÇőwith the United States was not in question, but added ÔÇőthat its deals with the U.S. and European countries need to be strengthened.
"The ÔÇîpartnerships Ôüáare the main stop-gap and deterrent against any attack on our country," he added.
"When things go out of control and spiral, the result is deterrents do not work... But that doesn't ÔÇőmean you shouldn't ÔÇőhave deterrents. ÔüáYou should strengthen these deterrents and that is exactly what are we trying to do right," ÔÇőAl Ansari said.
U.S. President Donald Trump pledged in ÔÇőOctober Ôüáto treat any armed attack on Qatar as a threat to the United States' own security.
Iran has vowed to retaliate Ôüáagainst U.S. ÔÇőtargets in the region in case ÔÇőof any attack.

item.title: Tuesday will be most intense day of strikes on Iran, Hegseth says
item.description:
