WASHINGTON, March 10 (Reuters) - U.S. Defense ÔÇőSecretary Pete ÔÇîHegseth said Tuesday would ÔÇőbe ÔÇőthe most intense Ôüáday of ÔÇőstrikes against ÔÇőIran in the campaign so ÔÇőfar.
Speaking ÔÇőwith reporters, Hegseth said ÔÇîTuesday Ôüáwould bring the most fighter ÔÇőjets ÔÇőand Ôüábombers against ÔÇőIran.

item.title: Human Rights Watch says that Israel has been illegally using white phosphorus in Lebanon
item.description:
JERUSALEM, March 10 (Reuters) - The Israeli military illegally used white phosphorus munitions over homes in the southern Lebanese town Yohmor on ÔÇőMarch 3, posing a threat to civilians, Human Rights Watch said in a ÔÇîreport on Monday.
"The incendiary effects of white phosphorus can cause death or cruel injuries that result in lifelong suffering," Ramzi Kaiss, Lebanon researcher at Human Rights Watch, said in the statement.
The rights group said it ÔÇőhad verified eight images showing white phosphorus used over a residential part of Yohmor, ÔÇőand civil defense workers responding to fires in the area.
Reuters could not Ôüáindependently verify the group's findings.
The Israeli military told Reuters it was unaware and could not ÔÇőconfirm the use of shells that contain white phosphorus in Lebanon. It added that it ÔÇőhad not reviewed the same videos as HRW and could not comment on the claims.
Lebanese authorities have not commented.
The Israeli military told residents of Yohmor and 50 other villages and towns to evacuate in a ÔÇőstatement early on March 3.
Reuters previously reported on Lebanese farmers testing their soil to determine whether ÔÇőthey could resume planting in the aftermath of Israel's reported use of white phosphorus in 2023.
As of July 2024, the Lebanese ÔÇîNational ÔüáCouncil for Scientific Research said there had been 175 Israeli attacks on south Lebanon using white phosphorus from October 2023, many of them sparking fires that have affected over 600 hectares (1,480 acres) of farmland.
White phosphorus munitions can legally be used on battlefields to make smoke screens, ÔÇőgenerate illumination, mark targets ÔÇőor burn bunkers and Ôüábuildings.
HRW said under international humanitarian law the use of airburst white phosphorus over populated areas is unlawful.
White phosphorus is considered an incendiary weapon ÔÇőunder Protocol III of the Convention on the Prohibition of Use ÔÇőof Certain ÔüáConventional Weapons. The protocol prohibits using incendiary weapons against military targets located among civilians, although Israel has not signed it and is not bound by it.
Nearly 400 people have been killed and hundreds Ôüáof thousands ÔÇőhave been forced to leave their homes as Israeli ÔÇőstrikes have pounded Lebanon for over a week.
HRW previously accused Israel of using white phosphorus during Israel's 2023 operations against ÔÇőLebanon, a charge Israel's military denied at the time.
(This story has been refiled to correct the spelling of 'phosphorus' in the headline, and to add the missing word 'of' in paragraph 8)

item.title: Iran not more formidable than thought, top U.S. general says
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 10 (Reuters) - The top U.S. general said on Tuesday that while Iran was fighting, ÔÇőit was not more formidable than Washington had thought, as ÔÇîthe United States geared up for the most intense day of strikes against Iran in the war so far.
General Dan Caine, chairman of the Joint ÔÇőChiefs of Staff, told reporters that the United States was ÔÇőcarrying out strikes against Iranian mine-laying vessels and the ÔüáPentagon would look at a range of options if it was ÔÇőtasked with escorting ships through the Strait of Hormuz.
The war has effectively ÔÇőshut the Strait of Hormuz, a chokepoint for global oil and liquefied natural gas transport, leaving tankers unable to sail for more than a week and ÔÇőforcing producers to halt pumping as storage fills.
"I think they're fighting, ÔÇőand I respect that, but I don't think they're more formidable than what we ÔÇîthought," ÔüáCaine said.
U.S. President Donald Trump on Monday threatened to escalate the war with Iran if it blocked oil shipments from the Middle East, even as he predicted a quick end to the conflict.
ÔÇőDuring the press ÔÇőconference at the ÔüáPentagon, U.S. Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth said the United States would carry out the most intense day ÔÇőof strikes against Iran on Tuesday.
Hegseth reiterated that ÔÇőthis would Ôüánot be an endless war and said Trump would decide when the U.S. campaign would end.
The United States has carried out strikes Ôüáagainst more ÔÇőthan 5,000 targets in the first ÔÇő10 days of the campaign, including against more than 50 naval ships, Caine said.

item.title: Shots fired at US consulate in Toronto, no injuries reported
item.description:
TORONTO/WASHINGTON, March 10 (Reuters) - Shots were ÔÇőfired at the U.S. consulate in Toronto on Tuesday, ÔÇîpolice said, adding that evidence was found of a discharged firearm and that no injuries were ÔÇőreported.
Toronto police said on X they responded to ÔÇőreports of shots at 5:29 a.m. (0929 GMT).
Representatives for Ôüáthe U.S. consulate in Toronto and ÔÇőthe U.S. Department of State did not ÔÇőimmediately respond to requests for comment.
In another post, police said they had no information on a potential ÔÇősuspect and were on scene investigating the ÔÇőincident. They did not provide details of any motive.
Separately, ÔÇîon ÔüáSunday, an improvised device exploded in Norway at the U.S. embassy in Oslo , and police were still searching for a suspect, with ÔÇőa possible ÔÇőlink to Ôüáthe Iran war among the lines of inquiry.
In New York ÔÇőCity, two men have been charged with ÔÇőterrorism Ôüáafter throwing a homemade bomb at anti-Islam protesters over the weekend.

item.title: Britain working with allies to support shipping through Strait of Hormuz
item.description:
LONDON, March 10 (Reuters) - Britain is working with allies on a range ÔÇőof options to support commercial ÔÇîshipping through the Strait of Hormuz in the face of Iranian ÔÇőthreats, Prime Minister Keir ÔÇőStarmer's spokesperson said on Tuesday Ôüáafter calls with the leaders ÔÇőof Italy and Germany.
After speaking ÔÇőto Germany's Friedrich Merz and Italy's Giorgia Meloni late on Monday, Downing ÔÇőStreet said in a readout ÔÇőthat they agreed on the "vital importance of ÔÇîfreedom Ôüáof navigation" through the Strait and "agreed to work closely together in the coming days ÔÇőin the ÔÇőface Ôüáof Iranian threats."
"We're working with our allies on ÔÇőa range of options ÔÇőto Ôüásupport commercial shipping through the Strait as the threat picture Ôüádevelops," ÔÇőthe spokesperson told ÔÇőreporters when asked about the readout.

item.title: Russia's deportations of Ukrainian children amount to crimes against humanity, UN inquiry finds
item.description:
GENEVA, March 10 (Reuters) - A U.N. investigation found on Tuesday that the deportation and transfer of ÔÇőUkrainian children by Russian authorities since Moscow's full-scale invasion of ÔÇîthe country in 2022 amounted to crimes against humanity.
"In this report, the Commission concluded that crimes against humanity and war crimes ÔÇőby Russian authorities have targeted children, who are ÔÇőamong the most vulnerable victims. These crimes have Ôüáirreversible consequences on their lives and their future," according ÔÇőto the report to be presented to the U.N. Human ÔÇőRights Council in Geneva on Thursday.
The report by the Independent International Commission of Inquiry on Ukraine studied the cases of 1,205 children ÔÇőfrom five oblasts, or regions, in Ukraine and said ÔÇőthat 80% of them have yet to return to Ukraine.
Ukrainian authorities say ÔÇîRussia Ôüáhas illegally deported or forcibly displaced more than 19,500 children to Russia and Belarus in violation of the Geneva Conventions.
U.S.-funded research last year showed Russia expanded its forced ÔÇőre-education programmes of ÔÇődeported children.
"The Ôüádeportations and transfers have originated from various locations across a wide geographic area in ÔÇőRussian-occupied areas in Ukraine, following a well-established ÔÇőpattern Ôüáof conduct, indicating that these acts have been widespread and systematic," the U.N. report said.
Russia denies it is taking Ôüáchildren against ÔÇőtheir will and says it ÔÇőhas been evacuating people voluntarily to remove them from the war zone.

item.title: Pope Leo fires San Diego bishop accused of stealing $250,000
item.description:
VATICAN CITY, March 10 (Reuters) - Pope Leo has accepted the resignation of a Catholic bishop in ÔÇőSan Diego who was arrested by ÔÇîlocal authorities on suspicion of stealing $250,000 from his congregation, the Vatican announced on Tuesday.
Bishop Emanuel Hana Shaleta had ÔÇőled the small Chaldean Catholic community ÔÇőin the California city since 2017. He Ôüápleaded not guilty to 16 counts ÔÇőof embezzlement and money laundering at a hearing ÔÇőon Monday, according to local media reports.
San Diego Deputy District Attorney Joel Madero said the alleged crimes ÔÇőtook place in 2024 and were reported ÔÇőby a church employee who noticed the missing funds, ÔÇîaccording Ôüáto KGTV, a local ABC affiliate.
Hana Shaleta was arrested on March 5 at San Diego's international airport while attempting to leave ÔÇőthe U.S., according ÔÇőto Ôüáa sheriff's office statement.
The bishop's lawyer said at Monday's hearing that ÔÇőHana Shaleta was taking a planned ÔÇőtrip Ôüáto Germany.
Chaldean Catholics recognise the authority of the pope but worship according to an eastern ÔüáChristian ÔÇőliturgical rite. There are about ÔÇő71,000 Chaldean Catholics in San Diego, according to Vatican ÔÇőstatistics.

item.title: Ex-Syrian intelligence officer appears in UK court charged with crimes against humanity
item.description:
LONDON, March 10 (Reuters) - A former member of Syria's Air Force Intelligence attended a British court hearing via videolink on Tuesday charged with crimes against humanity and torture relating to the ÔÇősuppression of pro-democracy demonstrations in Damascus in 2011.
Salem Michel Al-Salem, 58, who now ÔÇîlives in Britain, appeared virtually at the hearing at London's Westminster Magistrates' Court from his home. He was wearing a breathing apparatus mask and the court was told he suffered from degenerative motor neurone disease.
Al-Salem is charged ÔÇőwith three counts of murder as a crime against humanity relating to deaths in ÔÇőApril and July 2011 "as part of a widespread or systematic attack against a Ôüácivilian population with knowledge of the attack".
He is also accused of three charges of torture ÔÇőrelating to incidents in 2011 and 2012, and one of conduct ancillary to murder as a crime ÔÇőagainst humanity. He did not speak during the hearing and there was no indication as to how he would plead.
His lawyer Sean Caulfield told the court that Al-Salem was too unwell to confirm his name.
The seven charges were ÔÇőbrought under a British law that allows the prosecution of serious international crimes committed abroad. ÔÇőThe Crown Prosecution Service said it was the first time it had brought charges of murder as crimes against ÔÇîhumanity.
In Ôüá2005, Afghan warlord Faryadi Zardad was convicted by a British court of torture that had taken place in Afghanistan.
Al-Salem, who has sought indefinite leave to remain in Britain, was a colonel in the Syrian Air Force Intelligence department with oversight of the Information Branch in the district of ÔÇőJobar, to the east of ÔÇőcentral Damascus, British Ôüáprosecutors say.
He is accused of leading a group tasked with quelling the demonstrations, which mostly occurred during Friday afternoon prayers. Prosecutors say he gave ÔÇőhis men orders to open fire on protesters, which led to the ÔÇődeaths of Ôüásome individuals.
Prosecutors say he was also present at, or took part in, the torture of men at the Information Branch building.
Al-Salem was first arrested in central England in December 2021. His lawyer had sought Ôüáan order ÔÇőto withhold his name, arguing it could pose a ÔÇőrisk to his safety. England's Chief Magistrate Paul Goldspring rejected the application but ordered that his address not be made ÔÇőpublic.
He will next appear on Friday at London's Old Bailey court.

item.title: Iran shock creates problematic fiscal loop
item.description:
LONDON, March 10 (Reuters Breakingviews) - ItÔÇÖs never a convenient time for war-induced spikes in oil and gas prices, as RussiaÔÇÖs invasion of Ukraine in 2022 can attest. But todayÔÇÖs flare up in the Middle East hits Western economies at a ÔÇőmore fragile moment. Labour markets are looser, corporate margins thinner, and interest rates much higher than they were four years ago. Whether ÔÇîgovernments shield households and firms from costlier energy or leave them exposed, the result is likely the same: borrowing costs will climb.
Bond markets are already tense. Even after Donald Trump on Tuesday predicted rapid de escalation with Iran , 10 year yields in Germany, France, the United Kingdom and Japan remain well above their pre crisis levels. Unlike the United States, all are large ÔÇőnet importers of energy and particularly natural gas, which plays a key role in setting electricity prices.
Yet, if disruption at the Strait of Hormuz ÔÇőpersists, governments may be forced to brave hostile debt markets and once again fund energy price caps, subsidies and tax Ôüácuts. South Korea has already moved in that direction, and UK Prime Minister Keir Starmer signalled on Monday that Britain may follow. BruegelÔÇÖs data on EuropeÔÇÖs last ÔÇőenergy shock shows how expensive such measures can be: between September 2021 and January 2023, they cost European countries -- including the UK -- 3.7% of GDP. This would ÔÇősit atop the 3.4% deficit that official projections , opens new tab had already pinned on the European Union for this year.
But inaction would lead to a similar place. The post-pandemic experience showed that companies react to energy shocks by coordinating price hikes to protect their margins , opens new tab , which is why even ÔÇťcoreÔÇŁ inflation in the U.S., the eurozone and Britain ÔÇô which excludes the price of fuel and food ÔÇô peaked ÔÇőat range of between 5.7% and 7.1% in 2023. In theory, central banks should look through one off energy shocks; in practice, this so-called pass through ÔÇőeffect forces them to respond. Markets have already shifted from expecting year end rate cuts at the Bank of England and the European Central Bank to pricing in a hike ÔÇîfrom the Ôüáformer and no change from the latter, according to derivative prices collected by LSEG.
The inflation hit should still be smaller this time. European natural gas trades around 50 euros per megawatt hour, far below the 300-euro peak of 2022. Deutsche BankÔÇÖs Sanjay Raja estimates that even a generous UK support package would total only 14 billion pounds, or 0.4% of GDP, compared with 90 billion pounds previously. Still, that extra fiscal hit comes at a time when government tax revenues are already ÔÇőstrained by low growth. The danger is ÔÇőthat more, potentially open-ended financial commitments Ôüápush up bond yields, making the fiscal math even trickier.
Central banks could help break the feedback loop with a clear pledge to backstop sovereign bond markets, as the ECB did during the pandemic. At the margin, energy subsidies might help ease ÔÇőfears of a price spiral. But the current level of inflation, which is at or above target in the ÔÇőEU and UK, means Ôüáratesetters probably wonÔÇÖt act unless bond markets are under extreme duress. If the Iran war continues, government coffers could become a pain point.
Follow Jon Sindreu on X , opens new tab and LinkedIn , opens new tab .

item.title: Critical Metals approves $30 million for Greenland rare earth project development
item.description:
March 10 (Reuters) - Critical Metals (CRML.O) , opens new tab said on Tuesday its board approved a $30 ÔÇőmillion acceleration program for the ÔÇîdevelopment of the Tanbreez heavy rare earth project in Greenland.
The push ÔÇőcomes as the United ÔÇőStates ramps up efforts to build Ôüádomestic rare earth and ÔÇőcritical minerals supply chains amid concerns ÔÇőover reliance on China for these materials used in electronics, defense and ÔÇőclean energy technologies.
The program for ÔÇőone of the world's largest known rare ÔÇîearth Ôüádeposits will advance drilling, infrastructure, engineering design and metallurgical programs.
It expects first ore production in ÔÇőfourth ÔÇőquarter of Ôüá2028 or first quarter of 2029, with concentrate ÔÇőexport beginning by third ÔÇőquarter Ôüáof 2029.
Last year, Reuters reported Trump administration officials had discussed the Ôüápossibility ÔÇőof taking a ÔÇőstake in the company.

item.title: WHO warns of health risks from 'black rain' in Iran
item.description:
GENEVA, March 10 (Reuters) - The World Health Organization warned on Tuesday that the "black rain" falling in Iran ÔÇőafter strikes on oil facilities could cause respiratory ÔÇîproblems, and it backed IranÔÇÖs advisory urging people to remain indoors.
The U.N. health agency, which has an office in ÔÇőIran and works with authorities on health emergencies, ÔÇősaid it has received multiple reports of oil-laden Ôüárain this week. Tehran was choked in black ÔÇősmoke on Monday after an oil refinery was hit, ÔÇőin an escalation in strikes on Iran's domestic energy supplies as part of the U.S.-Israeli campaign.
"The black rain and the acidic rain ÔÇőcoming with it is indeed a danger for ÔÇőthe population, respiratory mainly," WHO spokesperson Christian Lindmeier told a press ÔÇîbriefing Ôüáin Geneva, adding that Iran had advised people to stay indoors.
Asked whether the WHO backed that advice, he said: "Given what is at risk right now, the ÔÇőoil storage facilities, ÔÇőthe refineries Ôüáthat have been struck, triggering fires, bringing serious air quality concerns, that is ÔÇődefinitely a good idea."
One video sent to ÔÇőReuters by Ôüáa WHO staff member showed what they said was a cleaner mopping up black liquid at its office Ôüáentrance ÔÇőin Tehran on March 8. ÔÇőReuters was not able to independently verify the footage.

item.title: Decision to turn back on nuclear was a strategic mistake, EU's Von der Leyen says
item.description:
PARIS, March 10 (Reuters) - The decision to reduce the share of nuclear energy in the electricity production mix in the European Union ÔÇőwas a strategic mistake given that the bloc is ÔÇîneither an oil or gas producer, EU Commission President Ursula Von der Leyen said on Tuesday.
"This reduction in the share of nuclear was ÔÇőa choice, I believe that it was a strategic ÔÇőmistake for Europe to turn its back on a Ôüáreliable, affordable source of low-emissions power," she said in ÔÇőa speech at an event on nuclear energy held in Paris.
The ÔÇőEU relied on nuclear for one third of its electricity in 1990 and now the share is only close to 15%, she said.
The European ÔÇőUnion aims to stimulate the development of small nuclear reactors, ÔÇőto make Europe less dependent on imported fossil fuels, she added.
"We see ÔÇîa Ôüáglobal revival of nuclear energy. And Europe wants to be part of it," von der Leyen said as she announced a 200 million euros ($233 million) guarantee by the EU to stimulate ÔÇőinvestments in the ÔÇődevelopment of Ôüásmall modular reactors.
The money would come from the EU's Emissions Trading System, she said.
"We want this ÔÇőnew technology to be operational by the early ÔÇő2030s," Ôüáshe said.
Von der Leyen said soaring energy prices caused by the war in the Middle east were a "stark reminder" of Europe's Ôüávulnerability as ÔÇőan importer of fossil fuels and ÔÇőunderlined the importance of increasing power production from renewable sources and nuclear reactors.
($1 = ÔÇő0.8584 euros)

item.title: Spanish researchers develop low-cost artificial cornea from fish scales
item.description:
MADRID, March 10 (Reuters) - Researchers at Spain's University of Granada have developed an artificial cornea made from the scales of several fish ÔÇőspecies commonly found in markets, which could become a ÔÇîlower-cost alternative to donor transplants for severe eye diseases.
The cornea - the eye's transparent front layer - is difficult to repair when badly damaged because it ÔÇőhas no blood vessels and limited regenerative capacity. Severe ÔÇőcorneal disease is often treated with donor transplants, which Ôüácan be constrained by organ availability and waiting lists.
Scientists from ÔÇőthe university's Tissue Engineering Group and the ibs.GRANADA biomedical research institute ÔÇősaid in a press release they had created corneal implants with fish scales that were highly biocompatible, transparent and resistant.
"Due to its origin, this product ÔÇőis very accessible, easy to obtain and inexpensive, and could ÔÇőhelp boost the local fishing industry," said Ingrid Garzon, professor of histology ÔÇîat Ôüáthe southern Spanish university, who co-authored the research.
While testing suggested they could be useful for corneal repair and regeneration, the technology remains far from possible clinical use in humans pending human trials.
The ÔÇőteam said the ÔÇőmaterial produced Ôüágood functional results in laboratory studies and in animals that received the implants.
"Although the standard transplant ÔÇőusually offers good results, it's necessary to develop ÔÇőnew effective Ôüáregeneration methods that do not depend on organ donation, which is subject to waiting lists," Miguel Alaminos, also a histology professor at Ôüáthe ÔÇősame university, said in the release.
A ÔÇő2016 peer-reviewed paper by researchers at Antwerp University found that fish scale-derived artificial corneas ÔÇőwere biocompatible with humans.

item.title: Argentina's Milei is pitching to Wall Street as Middle East spooks investors
item.description:
NEW YORK, March 10 (Reuters) - President Javier Milei aims on Tuesday to persuade investors that Argentina's economic turnaround can stay on track even as war ÔÇőin Iran pushes oil prices higher, strengthens the dollar and rattles emerging markets.
Milei is due to speak to investors and ÔÇîexecutives inside JPMorgan's new headquarters in Midtown Manhattan as part of "Argentina Week," a New York roadshow to convince financiers that the South American country's stabilization effort remains an attractive investment story despite the global backdrop.
"We're talking about making connections, about creating the right environment for these conversations to be productive," said Manuel Adorni, chief of Milei's ÔÇőcabinet and his spokesperson, addressing investors at a reception on Monday night in the Argentine consulate in New York. "We want to ÔÇőbuild trust, we want to lay the foundations so that we can establish a long-term relationship."
U.S. government backing has Ôüábecome a key part of that pitch. President Donald Trump 's administration publicly supported Milei ahead of Argentina's October 2025 midterm elections and expanded financial ÔÇőcooperation with Buenos Aires. A liquidity facility championed by the United States helped avoid a run on the peso ahead of that vote.
The two countries ÔÇődeepened economic ties in February, when the United States and Argentina signed a reciprocal trade and investment agreement designed to facilitate U.S. investment, including in the critical minerals sector.
"They're simply reiterating the investment opportunities in Argentina and sending a message of macroeconomic and political stability," Armando Armenta, a senior economist and strategist at AllianceBernstein, said of ÔÇőthe roadshow. "It's good that they're doing this because Argentina has been out of the spotlight for a long time."
Argentina's government says aggressive spending cuts, ÔÇőderegulation and fiscal tightening are beginning to restore macroeconomic stability after years of deficits, currency crises and galloping inflation.
Investors have cautiously welcomed the adjustment and recent reforms, ÔÇîincluding a labor Ôüáoverhaul approved by Congress that marked a major legislative victory for Milei.
Still, Argentina must rebuild foreign-exchange reserves, attract longer-term investment and regain reliable access to international capital markets after years of default and capital controls -some of which remain in place.
Officials hope Argentina Week, which will also host Economy Minister Luis Caputo, central bank governor Santiago Bausili and deregulation minister Federico Sturzenegger, will help demonstrate that reforms are translating into investment opportunities, particularly in ÔÇősectors such as energy, mining, agriculture ÔÇőand technology.
The closer alignment with the ÔüáUnited States marks a shift after years in which China expanded its economic footprint across South America. Yet China remains one of the country's largest trading partners and a major creditor.
Global conditions may complicate the message.
Oil ÔÇőprices are up nearly 30% this month to about $90 per barrel as the United States and Israel ÔÇőattack Iran, while a Ôüáflight to safety has strengthened the dollar and drawn some investors away from emerging markets. The dollar (.DXY) , opens new tab has gained more than 4% against developed currencies since late January, while the EM currency index (.MIEM00000CUS) , opens new tab has surrendered its gains for the year.
Last week, the Argentine local stock benchmark (.MERV) , opens new tab touched its lowest level since ÔüáOctober, while ÔÇőthe dollar bond yield spread to U.S. Treasuries widened in line with the global ÔÇőbenchmark (.JPMEGD) , opens new tab .
For Milei, the challenge is convincing investors that Argentina's reforms deserve attention even amid a flight to safety.

item.title: China's leaders mourn the death of 109-year-old Communist Party elder
item.description:
BEIJING, March 10 - Chinese leaders on Tuesday paid their respects to Song Ping, the longest-lived member of the most powerful decision-making body in the Communist Party, who died last week in Beijing at the age of 109.
President Xi ÔÇőJinping, Premier Li Qiang and other members of the party's current Politburo Standing Committee attended a ceremony ÔÇîthat closed the chapter on a political career that spanned five generations of leaders of the People's Republic.
They stood in silent tribute then bowed three times in front of his remains, which were draped in the red flag of the Communist Party, before he ÔÇőwas cremated. The event was shown on state broadcaster CCTV.
"The life of Comrade Song Ping was a ÔÇőlife of revolution, of struggle and of glory, a life spent in the pursuit Ôüáof truth and progress and in dedication to the communist cause," read an official account of his life released ÔÇőby state-run Xinhua news agency on Tuesday.
Born in 1917 into a family of farmers in the eastern province of ÔÇőShandong, Song became a revolutionary youth when he joined the Communist Party in 1937.
He briefly served as a personal secretary of Zhou Enlai, who became communist China's first premier after 1949.
Song, as an economic planner and the party's personnel chief, was a prominent member ÔÇőof China's second generation of leadership, an era when reform-minded Deng Xiaoping remade China after the 27-year rule ÔÇőof Mao Zedong ended in 1976 with Mao's death.
He rose to the apex of power in China's political system - the Standing Committee ÔÇîof Ôüáthe Politburo, comprised of only a handful of people - in June 1989, following the bloody crackdown on pro-democracy demonstrations in and around Tiananmen Square in Beijing.
Song was elevated by Deng to the top leadership in 1989, as Jiang Zemin emerged as the head of China's third generation of leaders - a move partly made in recognition of Song's commitment to Deng's ÔÇőreform programme.
In the 1970s, ÔÇőwhile serving as the party Ôüáchief of Gansu, a province in the country's barren northwest that housed the young republic's space and atomic programmes, Song sided with Deng in the tumultuous years following ÔÇőMao's death, as party elites debated whether to continue on the path set by ÔÇőMao or to Ôüáfollow Deng's call to modernise, reform and open up.
Song was also widely credited with bringing Hu Jintao, China's leader before Xi, to the attention of Deng.
"Hu Jintao sent a wreath to express his condolences on the passing of Comrade Song ÔüáPing," ÔÇőXinhua reported on Tuesday.
Song retired from the Politburo in 1992, aged 75.
Popular ÔÇőand respected within the party for his seniority, Song remained politically active after his retirement. In 2022, already aged 105, he was seen on ÔÇőstage at a Communist Party meeting flanked by other retired senior officials.

item.title: Germany's Merz sees no plan for bringing Iran war to swift end
item.description:
BERLIN, March 10 (Reuters) - German Chancellor Friedrich Merz underlined growing concern in Europe at the U.S. and Israeli war against Iran on Tuesday ÔÇősaying a "dangerous escalation" was underway with "clearly no joint plan" for bringing ÔÇîit to an end.
Merz's comments, which echoed remarks he made at the end of last week, came after U.S. President Donald Trump said the war could end "soon" ÔÇőbut also said the U.S. could escalate its attacks if Iran ÔÇősought to block tanker traffic through the Strait of Hormuz.
Merz Ôüásaid that Germany shared "many of the goals" of the U.S. and Israeli ÔÇőoperation, which killed Iran's Supreme Leader Ali Khamanei, but he said questions ÔÇőwere arising with each day that passed, amid signs of a dangerous escalation.
"We are particularly concerned that there is clearly no joint plan for bringing this war to ÔÇőa swift and convincing end," Merz said at a joint press conference ÔÇőin Berlin with Czech Prime Minister Andrej Babis.
"We have no interest in a war ÔÇîwithout Ôüáend. We have no interest in the dissolution of Iran's territorial integrity, statehood or economic viability," he added.
The world needed "a stable, viable Iran as part of a regional peace and security order in which neither Israel nor other ÔÇőpartners are threatened," ÔÇőhe said.
The remarks Ôüáreflect the concern in Europe at the economic damage inflicted by any prolonged closure of the Strait of ÔÇőHormuz, through which a fifth of the world's oil ÔÇőpasses, as Ôüáwell as by fears of the disorder that would follow a breakdown in Iran's state institutions.
"A scenario such as we have seen in Libya, Iraq Ôüáor other ÔÇőcountries in the region would also harm ÔÇőus all," he said. "This affects our security, our energy supply and possibly also the situation ÔÇősurrounding migration."

item.title: Brazil's Haddad says Durigan likely to replace him as finance minister
item.description:
SAO PAULO, March 10 (Reuters) - Brazil's Finance ÔÇîMinister Fernando Haddad confirmed on Tuesday that he will step down next week ÔÇőand said that ÔÇőhis deputy, Dario Durigan, is Ôüálikely to succeed him.
A ÔÇőformal announcement on who will take over ÔÇőthe ministry, however, is up to President Luiz Inacio Lula da Silva, Haddad told reporters ÔÇőin Brasilia.
"Dario has a ÔÇővery good relationship with the president, ÔÇîone Ôüáof great trust... He is a great public administrator," said Haddad, who had ÔÇőpreviously announced ÔÇőhe would leave Ôüáhis role ahead of Brazil's general elections ÔÇőin October.
Haddad is ÔÇőexpected Ôüáto run for governor of Sao Paulo state, four sources Ôüátold ÔÇőReuters earlier this ÔÇőweek.

item.title: Netherlands moves Iran embassy staff to Azerbaijan
item.description:
AMSTERDAM, March 10 (Reuters) - The Netherlands will ÔÇőtemporarily move its ÔÇîembassy staff in Iran to ÔÇőAzerbaijan, Foreign ÔÇőMinister Tom Berendsen said Ôüáon Tuesday, ÔÇőciting safety concerns ÔÇőover the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran.
"Due to ÔÇőincreasing risks ÔÇőto the safety of ÔÇîour Ôüástaff, it has been decided to temporarily relocate ÔÇőthe ÔÇőactivities Ôüáof the Dutch embassy ÔÇőin Iran ÔÇőto ÔüáBaku, Azerbaijan," Berendsen wrote Ôüáon ÔÇőX.

item.title: Private credit alarm bells echo 2007 subprime warnings
item.description:
ORLANDO, Florida, March 10 (Reuters) - Every financial market crisis is different, but they do rhyme, and parallels are beginning to emerge between the tremors now rippling through private credit and those in U.S. subprime housing that led to the 2007-09 Global Financial Crisis.
This isn't to say a repeat of that historic crash is in the cards. ÔÇőBut there is a growing risk that the mounting stress in private credit - scarce or nonexistent liquidity, opaque pricing, and spiking redemptions - could spill over into the public ÔÇîsecurities markets.
BlackRock (BLK.N) , opens new tab , the world's biggest asset manager with some $14 trillion under management, said on Friday it had limited withdrawals from a flagship debt fund after a surge in redemption requests. A few days earlier, alternative asset manager Blackstone (BX.N) , opens new tab said it had raised the redemption cap on its BCRED private credit fund to meet record withdrawal requests.
The alarm bells at these two behemoths come after a similar event at smaller alternative asset manager Blue Owl (OWL.N) , opens new tab last month, and the bankruptcies of U.S. auto-parts supplier First Brands and car dealership Tricolor late last year ÔÇőthat prompted JPMorgan Chase (JPM.N) , opens new tab CEO Jamie Dimon to warn: "When you see one cockroach, there are probably more."
Investors with a sense of history, or who were around in the 2000s, may think this ÔÇőall sounds a bit familiar. In 2007, BNP Paribas, Bear Stearns and HSBC blocked redemptions from U.S. subprime funds or warned that they were in trouble ÔÇô Ôüáa seemingly small risk that metastasized into a global financial meltdown.
Of course, the GFC didn't fully explode until September 2008 when U.S. authorities allowed Lehman Brothers to go under. But the crisis had been building ÔÇőup steadily for at least 18 months, with tremors at those subprime funds offering investors early warnings that trouble was brewing.
The rationale for not letting investors access their own money today is likely similar to ÔÇőthe justifications in 2007: assets have probably fallen significantly in value and thus would need to be sold at a heavy loss; the asset manager may fear triggering a fire sale in other assets to raise the cash being demanded; or the fund may be struggling to unload illiquid assets. Or it may be a bit of all three.
Either way, as was the case with subprime mortgages and related derivatives in 2007, it is hard to know what private credit ÔÇőassets today are truly worth because the market is so opaque and illiquid. When price discovery evaporates, the more bearish assumptions often win out.
Another similarity with subprime in 2007 is that private credit and private ÔÇőmarkets more broadly are not believed to pose a systemic financial stability risk. As we all know, that turned out to be wishful thinking back then.
Is it different this time?
Probably, if we're looking at sheer ÔÇîsize. The mortgage-backed Ôüásecurities market, the root of the GFC, was worth around $7.2 trillion in 2007, or 5% of the total value of global securities at the time, according to Investec. The private credit market today is worth around $2 trillion, less than 1% of all global securities.
On the other hand, like subprime in 2007, private credit today is loosely regulated, certainly relative to traditional bank lenders, meaning its true reach isn't easily ascertained.
What's more, ordinary mom-and-pop investors are getting more involved. Retail investors represented 16.6% of holdings in private credit funds at the end of 2024, up from 5.5% in 2020, according to Investec.
Meanwhile, private credit default rates are ÔÇőrising, hitting a record 9.2% in 2025, credit rating ÔÇőagency Fitch Ratings said last week. That's Ôüáup from the previous record of 8.1% in 2024.
Perhaps ominously, these defaults didn't include any software companies, which have become major private borrowers. The software sector has been hammered this year by fears of artificial intelligence-related disruption, which has slammed shares in private credit giants Blackstone, KKR (KKR.N) , opens new tab and Apollo (APO.N) , opens new tab down 30-45% in recent months.
The broader risks ÔÇőfrom private credit seem skewed to the downside. The U.S. economy is at a delicate juncture, facing a shaky labor market and the fallout from ÔÇőthe war in the Middle East , Ôüáincluding wild volatility in oil markets and the specter of modern-day "stagflation."
True, the consensus view is that the economy's underlying fundamentals are solid and that private credit isn't large enough or well-integrated enough to torpedo GDP growth or broader asset markets. As strategists at Barclays note, private credit has problems, but they aren't big enough to push the U.S. into recession.
Of course, this is exactly how subprime was viewed in 2007.
To paraphrase Warren Buffett, when Ôüáthe liquidity tide ÔÇőgoes out, you see who's been swimming naked. Recent events in the private credit market suggest more funds may soon ÔÇőbe exposed.
(The opinions expressed here are those of Jamie McGeever , a columnist for Reuters)
Enjoying this column? Check out Reuters Open Interest (ROI), , opens new tab your essential new source for global financial commentary. Follow ROI on LinkedIn, , opens new tab and X. , opens new tab
And listen to the Morning Bid daily podcast on Apple , opens new tab , Spotify , opens new tab , or the Reuters app , opens new tab . Subscribe to ÔÇőhear Reuters journalists discuss the biggest news in markets and finance seven days a week.

item.title: Exclusive: Exxon CEO says non-essential employees in Middle East have been evacuated
item.description:
HOUSTON, March 10 (Reuters) - Exxon Mobil (XOM.N) , opens new tab has evacuated ÔÇőnon-essential employees from its operations in the ÔÇîMiddle East, CEO Darren Woods said in an interview on Tuesday, as the U.S.-Israel war ÔÇőon Iran continues.
Some operations have been ÔÇőscaled back to manage inventory levels as Ôüátraffic through the Strait of Hormuz ÔÇőhas been challenged, he said. Exxon is a ÔÇőminority partner in oil projects in the UAE, Qatar and Saudi Arabia.
"Our first and highest priority ÔÇőis making sure our people remain safe, ÔÇőand we evacuated folks who weren't critical or essential ÔÇîto Ôüáthe operations that we were providing support for," Woods said.
The Strait of Hormuz, an important waterway that handles one-fifth of ÔÇőthe world's oil ÔÇősupply, Ôüáhas been strained as Iran has threatened to attack tankers that ÔÇőnavigate through it. U.S. President Donald ÔÇőTrump Ôüáon Monday threatened to escalate the war with Iran if it blocked oil shipments from Ôüáthe ÔÇőMiddle East, even as ÔÇőhe predicted a quick end to the conflict.

item.title: Denmark's Vestas receives UK order for 92 wind turbines
item.description:
COPENHAGEN, March 10 (Reuters) - Denmark's Vestas (VWS.CO) , opens new tab said on Tuesday it has received a ÔÇőfirm order for German utility ÔÇîRWE's 1.38 gigawatt (GW) Vanguard East offshore wind project in Britain.
The order supports ÔÇőBritain's advance towards its 2030 ÔÇőclean power targets and the wider Ôüáenergy transition across Europe, Vestas ÔÇősaid in a statement.

item.title: Zambia seeking global investors to help triple copper output by 2031
item.description:
LUSAKA, March 10 (Reuters) - Zambia is courting global investors, including from the ÔÇîUnited States, as it aims to more than triple its copper output to 3 million metric tons by 2031, its mines minister said on Tuesday.
Africa's second-largest copper producer after Democratic Republic ÔÇőof Congo is seen as one of the countries Washington is keen ÔÇőto partner with as the U.S. escalates efforts to loosen China's Ôüágrip on materials crucial to advanced manufacturing.
Zambia produced 890,346 tons of copper ÔÇőlast year, missing an annual target of 1 million tons. The red metal is ÔÇőa key material in electric vehicles and renewable energy infrastructure vital for the transition to a low-carbon economy.
Zambian Mines Minister Paul Kabuswe said investment talks extend to various countries as part ÔÇőof a broader strategy to meet the 2031 target.
"The U.S. is part of ÔÇőit," he said, without providing details of specific discussions.
Negotiations between Lusaka and Washington are also underway ÔÇîfor Ôüáa more than $1 billion health aid deal that health advocates have warned links the money to mining access and has data-sharing risks.
"There is nothing linked to anything," Kabuswe told Reuters when asked whether ÔÇőU.S. health funding ÔÇőwill be linked Ôüáto mining collaboration, adding: "Zambia is very mindful when seated on the table to do negotiations over any matter."
Kabuswe emphasized the ÔÇőimportance of fair deals, adding that "investors must also take their share". "It ÔÇőhas Ôüáto be a win-win situation for Zambia and for the investors," he said.
In addition to copper, Zambia is also rich in cobalt, nickel, manganese, graphite, lithium and rare-earth elements.
Mining Ôüáfirms ÔÇőoperating in Zambia include China's JCHX Mining (603979.SS) , opens new tab , Canada's ÔÇőBarrick Gold (ABX.TO) , opens new tab and First Quantum Minerals (FM.TO) , opens new tab , India's Vedanta Resources (VDAN.NS) , opens new tab , United Arab Emirates' International Resources Holding and KoBold ÔÇőMetals, which is backed by U.S. investors.

item.title: Reducing Europe's nuclear energy sector was 'strategic mistake', EU chief says
item.description:
PARIS, March 10 (Reuters) - Reducing Europe's nuclear energy sector was a "strategic mistake", European Commission chief Ursula von der Leyen said on Tuesday, as governments grapple ÔÇîwith an energy crunch from the Iran war .
Europe produced around a third of electricity from nuclear power in 1990 but that has fallen to 15%, she told an event in Paris, leaving it reliant on oil and gas imports whose prices have surged in recent days.
Being "completely dependent on expensive and volatile imports" of fossil fuels ÔÇőputs Europe at a disadvantage to other regions, von der Leyen said in a speech.
"This reduction in the share of nuclear ÔÇőwas a choice. I believe that it was a strategic mistake for Europe to turn its back Ôüáon a reliable, affordable source of low-emissions power."
Von der Leyen's native Germany took a political decision under then-Chancellor Angela Merkel to phase out nuclear power ÔÇőplants owing to public opposition and safety concerns after the 2011 Fukushima disaster. Von der Leyen was a minister in Merkel's government when that decision ÔÇőwas made.
Germany's Environment Minister Carsten Schneider on Tuesday criticised von der Leyen's "backward-looking strategy" on nuclear power.
"Cleaner, safer electricity from wind and sun is cheaper, has long been driving the energy transition, and produces no radioactive waste," Schneider said in a statement.
The EU has rapidly expanded renewable energy, but gas power plants still form an ÔÇőimportant part of its power mix, and fossil fuels still dominate energy consumption in sectors such as transport and heating.
The continued heavy reliance on imported oil ÔÇőand gas exposed European countries to soaring energy prices in 2022, when Russia cut gas deliveries after the invasion of Ukraine .
The EU budget does not directly ÔÇîfund nuclear Ôüáenergy projects because they are not unanimously supported by its 27 member governments.
In a sign of the EU's increasing acceptance of the technology, von der Leyen said the executive Commission would offer a 200-million-euro guarantee for private investments in innovative nuclear technologies.
She said the money would come from the EU's carbon market.
Some EU countries which previously opposed nuclear, such as Denmark and the Netherlands, have recently softened their stance, as they ÔÇőhunt for ways to secure large ÔÇőamounts of stable, low-carbon electricity Ôüáfor heavy industry.
Others, including Austria and Luxembourg, remain opposed.
France, Europe's biggest nuclear energy producer, argues that stable, low-carbon power from nuclear plants is key to industrial competitiveness.
French President Emmanuel Macron said the EU - where nuclear power producers still imported ÔÇő15% of their uranium from Russia in 2024 - needed to move towards other suppliers.
"We need to ÔÇőcooperate internationally to make Ôüáprogress on this issue, to diversify our supply sources," he told the Paris event, adding that France planned to increase its own enrichment capacity.
France imported 39% of its enriched uranium from Russia in 2025, customs data showed.
Macron also proposed standardising reactor designs across Europe. That could benefit France's state-owned Ôüánuclear giant ÔÇőEDF, which has struggled to win recent tenders for new projects.
In 2024 South ÔÇőKorea's KHNP won a tender worth at least $18 billion to build a new nuclear power plant in the Czech Republic, a decision which losing bidder EDF sought to block in the ÔÇőcourts.
($1 = 0.8584 euros)

item.title: Western nations bolster military presence in Eastern Mediterranean during Iran conflict
item.description:
ATHENS, March 10 - Western nations have bolstered their military presence in the eastern Mediterranean during the conflict in Iran , focusing on the security of Cyprus after an Iranian-made drone hit a British military ÔÇőbase on the island on March 2.
Here is a snapshot of military assets ÔÇîthat have been sent to the region in addition to those that had been there long-term before the conflict began on February 28.
USS Gerald R. Ford, the biggest U.S. aircraft carrier, docked at the ÔÇőSouda base on the Greek island of Crete two weeks ago, before sailing ÔÇőfurther east with escort ships carrying missiles.
Britain dispatched Wildcat helicopters on March Ôüá6, equipped with counter-drone technology. As speculation mounted in February on U.S.-led strikes on Iran, ÔÇőBritain stationed additional F-35B jets at its base in Cyprus, joining the Typhoon FGR4 aircraft ÔÇőalready there. Britain has said it will also send its destroyer, the HMS Dragon, to Cyprus.
France sent its flagship aircraft carrier Charles de Gaulle to the East Mediterranean last week, deploying a dozen naval vessels around ÔÇőit. It was off the Greek island of Crete on Tuesday and was heading ÔÇőto Cyprus. Another French frigate was docked in Souda base on Tuesday.
German navy frigate FGS Nordrhein-Westfalen arrived in ÔÇîthe ÔüáCyprus region on March 8.
Greece has sent its new Belharra frigate, Kimon, and MEKO 200 frigate called Psara, equipped with the Centauros anti-drone system, to protect the island's airspace. It also sent 4 F-16 Viper fighter jets to be stationed in western Cyprus and deployed a ÔÇőPatriot anti-aircraft missile system ÔÇőon the southeastern ÔüáGreek island of Karpathos to protect eastern Crete.
Italy deployed the Martinengo frigate to the region as part of a coordinated mission with EU ÔÇőpartners. It was docked in Souda base on Tuesday and was ÔÇőexpected to sail Ôüáto Cyprus in the coming days.
Spain sent its most advanced warship, the ├ülvaro de Baz├ín-class frigate Cristobal Colon, to the eastern Mediterranean. It was docked in Souda Bay on Tuesday.
The Netherlands Ôüáis preparing ÔÇőto send the air defence frigate HNLMS Evertsen to ÔÇőthe region.
Turkey has sent six F-16 aircraft and air-defence systems to northern Cyprus, a breakaway Turkish Cypriot state recognized ÔÇőonly by Ankara.

item.title: Governments plan repatriations as Middle East flights stop
item.description:
March 10 - Commercial flights in parts of the Middle East have been grounded due to the escalating conflict following U.S. and Israeli strikes on Iran , stranding foreign nationals and prompting governments to plan repatriations.
Below is what governments have said about repatriation plans:
Austria's foreign ministry said it had helped 117 vulnerable citizens leave the UAE and Israel via neighbouring states and had planned a first charter flight from Muscat on March 4 for 170 people, while warning that land departures would be ÔÇőundertaken at travellers' own risk.
Three flights by GullivAir, Bulgaria Air and the State Aviation Operator planned to return Bulgarian citizens from Dubai, Abu Dhabi and Oman on March 4 and March 5, including a 326ÔÇĹseat GullivAir ÔÇîflight from Dubai, a Bulgaria Air Boeing 737 rotation via Oman, and a 90ÔÇĹseat government aircraft from Abu Dhabi.
The Czech government has arranged 10 repatriation flights to return more than 1,500 stranded citizens, the Foreign Ministry said on March 9.
Estonia's foreign ministry on March 4 said it had organised a 180-seat flight from Muscat the following day, available to its citizens in Oman and the United Arab Emirates.
The European Commission has said it is coordinating repatriation flights under the EU Civil Protection Mechanism as more member states request help.
The EU has supported so far more than 42 flights, bringing ÔÇőover 4,100 Europeans safely back to Belgium, Bulgaria, Czechia, Italy, Cyprus, Latvia, Lithuania, Luxembourg, the Netherlands, Austria, Portugal, Romania, Slovakia, and Sweden, a statement from the European Commission said on March 9.
Finland planned a single repatriation flight with about ÔÇő160 seats on March 8 for the roughly 3,000 Finnish citizens stranded in the United Arab Emirates, the foreign ministry said.
Finnair said on March 6 that it was preparing to operate Ôüáflights from Muscat, Oman to Helsinki to bring home customers who have been stranded in Dubai. The first flight is scheduled for March 10, with more flights planned later in the week.
The French foreign minister said several repatriation flights for French nationals, around ÔÇő400,000 of whom are in the region, were planned from March 4.
France said it had deployed consular teams at Israel's borders with Egypt and Jordan to facilitate land exits so people can fly onward, and has put in place a similar mechanism in the ÔÇőUAE at borders with Oman and Saudi Arabia, where airspace has remained open.
Two flights with about 250 people were scheduled for March 5 and March 6 from the Gulf, after the first landed March 5 in Frankfurt, said German Foreign Minister Johann Wadephul.
Greek foreign ministry said it has repatriated hundreds of citizens from Abu Dhabi, Dubai, Jerusalem, Oman and the UAE during last week.
87 people were repatriated on March 4 from Amman, Jordan, with another flight carrying 88 people planned for March 5, Hungary's foreign ministry said in a statement.
Another flight was scheduled for March 6 from Sharm-el Sheikh, Egypt. ÔÇőHungary rented a Flydubai plane for March 5 and March 6 to repatriate Hungarians from Dubai. Hungary had two flights each on March 7 and March 8 leaving from Riyadh, Saudi Arabia.
Italy's foreign ministry said about 25,000 Italians have returned from the Middle ÔÇőEast on commercial flights facilitated by the ministry.
It has expanded consular staffing in Oman and the UAE and is coordinating returns across multiple countries, with large groups moving from the UAE and others travelling by land through Qatar, Kuwait, Bahrain and Saudi Arabia. Italians in Oman, Israel, ÔÇîthe Maldives and ÔüáThailand are also getting help.
The Dutch government on Friday said it was preparing to use its military to repatriate its citizens from countries they cannot leave on their own. No details on specific countries and timing were given.
The first group of Polish citizens evacuated by military airlift from the Middle East arrived in Poland on March 6, the Operational Command of the Polish Armed Forces said.
A charter TAP flight carrying 139 Portuguese citizens and eight foreigners landed in Lisbon on March 6, with Portugal's foreign ministry planning further repatriations.
Romania's foreign ministry said on March 4 airline FlyDubai has scheduled two flights to Bucharest on March 4 and March 5. It had received more than 3,000 repatriation requests and had around 16,000 nationals registered in the region.
An Air Serbia flight from Sharm el-Sheikh landed ÔÇőin Belgrade early on March 4, carrying 67 passengers, all ÔÇőevacuated from Israel.
The government will deploy an air force A330 Ôüámulti-role aircraft to support the assisted departure of Singaporeans from Riyadh on March 10, with a second flight from Saudi Arabia planned for March 12, the foreign affairs ministry said on Facebook.
Slovakia's government has carried out 15 repatriation flights since evacuations started, transporting 641 people, most of whom were Slovak nationals, the Foreign Ministry said on March 9. It is planning more flights.
Slovenia organised four ÔÇőbuses on March 3, escorted by police, to take Slovenian citizens and families with children from Dubai to Muscat airport in Oman, the prime minister's office said in a statement.
The first ÔÇőflight for Slovenia was organised on ÔüáMarch 3, with two more flights scheduled for March 4.
Spain has begun evacuating its citizens from the Middle East on March 3, with more than 175 Spaniards arriving on a flight from Abu Dhabi and further flights expected from the UAE via Istanbul, according to Foreign Minister Jose Manuel Albares.
Spain was also reinforcing its embassies in the UAE, Saudi Arabia, Oman and Bahrain to provide support and facilitate further repatriations, Albares said.
Sweden chartered a plane to repatriate on Saturday 180 stranded Swedes identified as vulnerable.
Thailand plans to evacuate its nationals from Iran by land Ôüáto Turkey on ÔÇőMarch 7 and March 10, while others stranded in Iraq, Qatar, Bahrain, the UAE and Jordan are returning or will return once airspace reopens.
The UAE civil aviation authority plans to begin operating "special flights" across the country's airports to help some of the tens of thousands of passengers stranded in the region leave, state news agency WAM reported.
British chartered flights left Oman on March 5 after technical delays on March 4, prioritising vulnerable UK nationals wanting to ÔÇőleave the region, the British Foreign Office said, after some 130,000 citizens registered their presence in the region.
($1 = 0.8615 euros)

item.title: Iran's intelligence ministry arrests foreign agents, state media reports
item.description:
DUBAI, March 10 (Reuters) - Iran's intelligence ministry ÔÇőhas arrested a ÔÇîforeign national it said was conducting ÔÇőespionage on ÔÇőbehalf of the ÔüáUnited States and ÔÇőIsrael, and ÔÇőacting as a proxy for two Gulf ÔÇőcountries, state ÔÇőmedia cited the ministry ÔÇîas Ôüásaying on Tuesday.
The ministry also said it had ÔÇőarrested ÔÇő30 Ôüáspies, internal mercenaries, and ÔÇőoperational agents ÔÇőof ÔüáIsrael and the U.S. over Ôüáthe ÔÇőpast few ÔÇődays.

item.title: EU leaders to demand carbon market reform by July, draft shows
item.description:
BRUSSELS, March 10 (Reuters) - European Union governments are set to ask the European Commission to ÔÇőpropose reforms to the bloc's carbon ÔÇîmarket by July, draft conclusions for a summit of EU leaders next week showed.
At ÔÇőtheir summit on 19 March, EU ÔÇőleaders will ask Brussels "to present a Ôüáreview of the emissions trading system (ETS) ÔÇőat the latest by July 2026, to ÔÇőboth reduce the volatility of the carbon price and mitigate its impact on electricity prices," ÔÇőaccording to the draft conclusions, seen ÔÇőby Reuters.
The draft conclusions said the reform should preserve ÔÇîthe Ôüácentral role of the ETS in the EU's energy transition. That's despite calls from some governments, including Slovakia and ÔÇőthe Czech ÔÇőRepublic, for Ôüáthe EU to suspend or weaken the system, as a ÔÇőway to cut energy bills.
The ÔÇőEuropean ÔüáCommission has said it plans to propose the ETS reform in the third Ôüáquarter ÔÇőof the year, but ÔÇőhas not given a specific date.

item.title: Spain's Repsol may bring new investors into US renewables unit
item.description:
MADRID, March 10 (Reuters) - Spanish energy group Repsol (REP.MC) , opens new tab is considering welcoming new investors in its renewables business in ÔÇőthe United States as it reviews its approach ÔÇîto renewable power generation, CEO Josu Jon Imaz said on Tuesday.
Since 2018, Repsol has built up a portfolio of wind, ÔÇősolar and hydroelectric projects as part of its ÔÇőbroader strategy of transitioning from a traditional oil Ôüáand gas company to a multi-energy player. It has ÔÇőalso been investing in the production of green hydrogen and ÔÇőlow-carbon fuels.
The United States, alongside Spain, is a key market for its lowÔÇĹcarbon division. In the U.S., the company has about ÔÇő2.8 gigawatts (GW) of capacity operating or under construction, plus ÔÇőa development pipeline of more than 15 GW.
"We are evaluating the ÔÇîpotential Ôüáincorporation of additional investors at the platform level in the U.S.," Imaz told analysts and investors at an event presenting the company's strategic update .
"The key message for this ÔÇődivision is clear - ÔÇőthis business Ôüáwill be self-financed over the next three years," Imaz said.
"This represents a critical inflection ÔÇőpoint as the business transitions from cash ÔÇőconsumer to Ôüácash generator, moving from a build-out phase to disciplined growth," he said.
Repsol, which also cut its renewable power generation target, Ôüáwill ÔÇőfocus on green energy projects ÔÇőoffering the best returns while limiting exposure thanks to asset sales and ÔÇőpartnerships, he said.

item.title: Exclusive: Hezbollah returns to guerrilla roots, awaits Israeli invasion
item.description:
BEIRUT, March 10 (Reuters) - Lebanon's Hezbollah is applying lessons from its last war with Israel as it braces ÔÇőfor a possible full-scale Israeli invasion and protracted conflict, returning to its roots in guerrilla warfare in south Lebanon, four Lebanese sources said.
Operating in ÔÇîsmall units, fighters from the Iran-backed group are avoiding the use of communication devices that could be at risk of Israeli tapping, and are rationing the use of key anti-tank rockets as they engage Israeli troops, said the sources, who are familiar with Hezbollah military activities.
Some 15 months since Israel pounded Hezbollah in their last war, the group sparked a new Israeli offensive last week by ÔÇőopening fire to avenge the killing of Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei at the start of the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran .
Heavily criticised in Lebanon for dragging ÔÇőthe country into a war that has displaced 700,000 people, Hezbollah has described its actions as "existential defence", framing it as a response to Israeli Ôüáattacks that have continued since a 2024 ceasefire.
While Israel plans for the likely continuation of its Lebanon offensive after the Iran war, the four sources said Hezbollah's calculations ÔÇőare based on Iran's clerical leadership surviving the war, leading to a regional ceasefire of which it would be part.
The sources who ÔÇőare familiar with Hezbollah thinking declined to be named due to the sensitivity of the matter. The details of how Hezbollah is operating in the field have not previously been reported.
Hezbollah's media office didn't immediately respond to a request for comment.
Hezbollah, a Shi'ite Muslim group founded by Iran's Revolutionary Guards in 1982, was the only Lebanese group to keep its weapons at the end of ÔÇőthe 1975-90 civil war to fight Israeli troops who occupied the south until withdrawing in 2000.
Hezbollah's role in driving them out has underpinned its popularity among many Shi'ites, ÔÇőthough its decision to enter the Iran war has drawn criticism from within the Shi'ite community.
This war has come at a critical juncture for Hezbollah.
Greatly weakened during the 2024 war, Hezbollah has ÔÇîfaced pressure Ôüáfrom the Lebanese state to disarm. The Beirut government last week banned Hezbollah's military activities.
Adding to the pressures on Hezbollah since the 2024 war, its Syrian ally President Bashar al-Assad was toppled in December of that year, severing the main supply route from Iran.
The sources said much of Hezbollah's fighting on the ground had been focused so far near the town of Khiyam, near the intersection of Lebanon's border with Israel and Syria.
This is one area where Hezbollah believes any Israeli land invasion could begin.
Reuters reported last week ÔÇőthat Hezbollah's elite Radwan fighters, who withdrew from ÔÇőthe south following the 2024 ceasefire, Ôüáhad returned to the area.
ISRAELI SOURCE: HEZBOLLAH STABILIZING RANKS DESPITE BLOWS
An Israeli security source said there was no sign that Hezbollah was looking to de-escalate - quite the opposite. While Israel had eliminated a few of Hezbollah's very senior commanders, it seemed that the ÔÇőgroup was managing to stabilize its ranks and make and execute decisions.
Two of the Lebanese sources said four deputies had ÔÇőbeen appointed for every ÔüáHezbollah commander, to ensure continued operations.
The Israeli military says it has struck hundreds of Hezbollah targets since March 2, launching airstrikes in the south, Beirut's Hezbollah-controlled southern suburbs, and the eastern Bekaa Valley.
The Israeli military has also sent more soldiers into south Lebanon, where some of its troops had remained since 2024, establishing what it has called forward defensive positions Ôüáto guard ÔÇőagainst the risk of Hezbollah attacks on northern Israel.
Two Israeli soldiers have been killed in Lebanon.
Hezbollah has ÔÇőlaunched daily drone and rocket attacks at Israel.
In 2024, not only did Israel booby-trap hundreds of pagers used by Hezbollah, but it also penetrated the group's private phone network, according to Lebanese officials familiar with ÔÇőHezbollah's post-war investigation into breaches.
The sources said Hezbollah was avoiding any devices that could be susceptible to eavesdropping.

item.title: Israeli strikes hit near Beirut as envoy says disarming Hezbollah could end war
item.description:
BEIRUT, March 10 (Reuters) - Israel's military pounded the Lebanese capital's southern suburbs with air strikes on Tuesday and its troops ÔÇőpushed deeper into the country's south, as an Israeli envoy said the key to ending the war ÔÇîwas disarming Lebanese militant group Hezbollah.
Lebanon was pulled deep into the war in the Middle East last week, when Iran-backed Hezbollah opened fire on Israel to avenge the killing of Iran's supreme leader.
Israel has since launched air strikes across Lebanon's south, east and Beirut's suburbs, killing nearly 500 people including more ÔÇőthan 80 children, according to Lebanon's health ministry.
Strikes on Beirut's southern suburbs on Tuesday afternoon sent thick columns of ÔÇősmoke over the city. Two hours before they began, an Israeli military spokesperson ordered residents to Ôüáleave immediately, specifying three new districts that should be evacuated.
A member of the municipal council for the area told Reuters ÔÇőfamilies there were fleeing, adding to the nearly 700,000 that Lebanese authorities say have already been displaced by the war.
Lebanon's Minister of ÔÇőSocial Affairs Haneen Sayed said on Tuesday that the state was bracing itself for higher displacement figures than in 2024, when the last war between Israel and Hezbollah pushed more than a million people out of their homes.
"So we expect that the needs, the numbers of displacement, will ÔÇőbe higher than in 2024. Now on the other side in terms of resources, there's far less resources this year ÔÇőgiven the global situation, the regional war that's happening," she said.
Sayed spoke to Reuters at Beirut's airport, ÔÇîwhere Ôüáthe European Union was delivering 45 tons of emergency supplies including medical kits and blankets.
"Our traditional partners and friends in the Gulf are of course under stress themselves. So we're appealing to the international community to be with us at this moment to help stabilize the situation in terms of humanitarian needs," Sayed said.
Israeli troops made advances on Tuesday in additional towns in ÔÇősoutheastern Lebanon, including with armoured columns, ÔÇőLebanese security sources told ÔüáReuters.
Lebanese President Joseph Aoun on Monday had signaled his openness to enter direct negotiations with Israel to end the war.
But Israel's ambassador to France Joshua Zarka said on Tuesday that words ÔÇőwere not enough.
"At this stage, IÔÇÖm not aware of any decision to enter negotiations to end ÔÇőthis war," Zarka Ôüásaid. "What would end it is the disarmament of Hezbollah ÔÇö and that is a choice for the Lebanese government," he said.
Zarka said Lebanon's government was "making very good statements, but to these comments they need to add actions."
Lebanon's government last year vowed to establish a Ôüástate monopoly ÔÇőon arms and confiscated part of Hezbollah's arsenal in the country's south, without ÔÇőobjections from the group.
But Hezbollah has refused to disarm in full, and Lebanese authorities were fearful that taking its arms by force could ignite a civil ÔÇőconflict.

item.title: British warship HMS Dragon departs for Eastern Mediterranean
item.description:
LONDON, March 10 (Reuters) - British warship HMS Dragon departed for the Eastern Mediterranean on Tuesday, more than a week after Britain's air base in ÔÇőCyprus was attacked by a drone in the aftermath of ÔÇîthe United States and Israel launching attacks against Iran.
Britain's government has been criticised for not being able to send a warship to the region sooner, ÔÇőwith military vessels from allies Greece and France arriving within days ÔÇőof Iran launching retaliatory attacks across the Gulf.
On March Ôüá1 RAF Akrotiri in Cyprus was hit by an Iranian-made drone, ÔÇőthought to have been launched from Lebanon or Iraq.
The decision to ÔÇődeploy the destroyer was made a week ago but it has taken days to prepare it for the journey, adding to scrutiny of Britain's military readiness .
The government ÔÇőhas defended its response, saying it had pre-deployed other assets to ÔÇőthe region including radar systems, air defence, and F-35 jets.
HMS Dragon is a Type ÔÇî45 Ôüáair-defence destroyer equipped with the Sea Viper missile system and advanced radar designed to track and neutralise airborne threats, according to the Royal Navy's website.
The government has also cited its close cooperation with ÔÇőthe United States in ÔÇőpreparation and Ôüáin response to the conflict, despite criticism from Trump over Britain's support.
Trump has lambasted Prime Minister Keir ÔÇőStarmer for limiting Britain's military support for the ÔÇőstrikes on ÔüáIran, plunging the relationship between the historically close military allies into crisis.
Starmer has allowed the U.S. to use British bases for defensive action Ôüábut ÔÇőhas said that he would not approve ÔÇőUK involvement in offensive actions unless he was sure they were legal and were ÔÇőpart of a clear plan.

item.title: US State Department OKs potential sale of rocket systems to Sweden
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 10 (Reuters) - The ÔÇőU.S. State ÔÇîDepartment said on ÔÇőTuesday ÔÇőthat it had Ôüáapproved ÔÇőthe potential ÔÇősale of M142 High ÔÇőMobility ÔÇőArtillery Rocket Systems ÔÇîand Ôüárelated equipment to Sweden.
The ÔÇőprincipal ÔÇőcontractor Ôüáwill be ÔÇőLockheed ÔÇőMartin (LMT.N) , opens new tab .

item.title: Russia told Trump it isn't sharing US military asset info with Iran, says Witkoff
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 10 (Reuters) - Russia has denied sharing ÔÇőintelligence with Iran on U.S. military ÔÇîassets in the Middle East, U.S. special envoy Steve Witkoff said on Tuesday in a CNBC ÔÇőinterview.
Witkoff said the denial came during ÔÇőa phone call that U.S. President Donald ÔüáTrump had with Russian President Vladimir ÔÇőPutin on Monday.
The Washington Post reported on ÔÇőFriday that Russia was providing Iran with targeting information that included locations of U.S. warships and aircraft ÔÇőin the Middle East.
"Yesterday on the call ÔÇőwith the president, the Russians said that they have ÔÇînot Ôüábeen sharing," Witkoff said when asked if Washington thought Russia had shared with Tehran intelligence about the location of U.S. military ÔÇőassets.
"We can ÔÇőtake them Ôüáat their word. But they did say that. And yesterday ÔÇőmorning, independently, Jared (Kushner) and I had ÔÇőa Ôüácall with (Kremlin foreign policy aide Yuri) Ushakov who reiterated the same," said Witkoff.
He added: "That's a Ôüábetter ÔÇőquestion for the intel ÔÇőpeople, but let's hope that they're not sharing."

item.title: Ukrainian missile strike on Russia's Bryansk causes casualties, local governor says
item.description: MOSCOW, March 10 (Reuters) - Ukraine ÔÇőhas ÔÇîlaunched a ÔÇőmissile ÔÇőstrike on the Ôüácity ÔÇőof ÔÇőBryansk in south-western ÔÇőRussia, ÔÇőcausing casualties and ÔÇîinjuries, Ôüáregional governor Alexander ÔÇőBogomaz ÔÇősaid Ôüáon ÔÇőTuesday.

item.title: Trump administration estimates cost of Iran war's first two days at $5.6 bln, source says
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 10 (Reuters) - U.S. President Donald Trump's administration said it used $5.6 billion in munitions during the ÔÇőfirst two days of strikes against ÔÇîIran in a report provided to U.S. congressional committees, a source familiar with the information said on Tuesday.
Members ÔÇőof Congress, who may soon have ÔÇőto approve additional funding for the war, Ôüáhave expressed concern that the conflict will deplete ÔÇőU.S. military stocks at a time when the ÔÇődefense industry was already struggling to keep up with demand.
Trump met executives from seven defense contractors on Friday as ÔÇőthe Pentagon worked to replenish supplies.
The administration ÔÇőhas not provided a public assessment of the cost of ÔÇîthe Ôüáconflict it launched on Feb. 28 along with ally Israel.
Members of Congress have clamored for more information, including public testimony from officials about ÔÇőissues including ÔÇőhow the Ôüáconflict might affect the U.S. military's readiness to defend the country.
Several ÔÇőcongressional aides have said they expect ÔÇőthe ÔüáWhite House to soon submit a request to Congress for additional funding for the war. Some Ôüáofficials ÔÇőhave said the request could ÔÇőbe for $50 billion, but others have said that estimate seems ÔÇőlow.

item.title: Zelenskiy says Turkey ready to host next trilateral peace talks
item.description:
KYIV, March 10 (Reuters) - Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskiy ÔÇősaid Turkey was ÔÇîready to host the next ÔÇőround of trilateral ÔÇőpeace talks between ÔüáKyiv, Moscow and ÔÇőWashington, after speaking ÔÇőto President Tayyip Erdogan on Tuesday.
"The President ÔÇőnoted that ÔÇőT├╝rkiye is ready to host ÔÇîthe Ôüánext round of talks in a trilateral format. ÔÇőWe ÔÇőappreciate Ôüáthis initiative and hope ÔÇőit can ÔÇőproduce Ôüáresults," he wrote on Facebook.

item.title: Trump envoy Witkoff says he will probably travel to Israel next week-CNBC interview

item.title: Israel not seeking endless war with Iran, foreign minister says
item.description:
JERUSALEM, March 10 (Reuters) - Israel is not seeking an endless war with Iran and will coordinate ÔÇîwith the United States on when to end the fighting, Foreign Minister Gideon Saar said on Tuesday, declining to publicly state a timeline for when the conflict could end.
The U.S.-Israeli war with Iran, now in its ÔÇő11th day, has engulfed the Middle East, with Iranian strikes hitting neighbouring states, including ÔÇőthe United Arab Emirates, and Israel fighting Hezbollah in Lebanon as well as Ôüástriking Iran.
"We will continue until the minute that we and our partners think that is ÔÇőappropriate to stop," Saar told journalists in Jerusalem alongside his German counterpart.
"We are not looking for ÔÇőan endless war," he said.
German Chancellor Friedrich Merz said earlier in Berlin that there was growing concern in Europe over the war and there appeared to be no plan to bring it to an end.
"We want ÔÇőto remove, for the long-term, existential threats from Iran to Israel," Saar said in response to ÔÇőa Reuters question on what victory would look like to the government.
He described Iran's newly appointed supreme leader ÔÇîMojtaba ÔüáKhamanei - son of Ayatollah Ali Khamenei who was killed by the Israeli military on the first day of the war - as an extremist.
Israel has said it aims to eliminate Iran's clerical rule by destroying its nuclear and ballistic missile programs and creating conditions for Iranians to overthrow their ÔÇőrulers.
Saar said there was ÔÇőan opportunity to Ôüácreate conditions for Iranians to "regain their freedom," while acknowledging that it may not happen during the war and could come afterward.
"We must not miss ÔÇőthis opportunity with partial results."
German Foreign Minister Johann Wadephul, the first senior ÔÇőforeign official Ôüáto publicly visit Israel since the war began, said he was confident Israel and Washington were open to a diplomatic solution that could lead to an end to the war.
But any such solution Ôüáwould need ÔÇőto include agreements with Iran on its nuclear and ÔÇőmissile programs, as well as its support for regional militias, terms that Wadephul said Tehran had made clear that it ÔÇőwas not currently prepared to accept.

item.title: Spain's LNG imports rise almost 20% from last year, with US as main supplier
item.description:
March 10 (Reuters) - Liquefied natural gas from the United States made up 33.8% of Spain's ÔÇőtotal gas imports in February, as ÔÇîthe country's gas imports rose almost 20% from the same month last year, data ÔÇőshowed on Tuesday.
The U.S. has become Europe's ÔÇődominant supplier of LNG as the region Ôüápushed to phase out energy imports ÔÇőfrom Russia in the wake of ÔÇőits invasion of Ukraine.
In February, Spain imported the equivalent of 31,422 gigawatt-hours (GWh) of gas, sharply up ÔÇőfrom the 26,318 GWh it imported ÔÇőa year earlier, according to data from Spanish gas ÔÇîgrid Ôüáoperator Enagas.
Spain imported 10,612 GWh of gas from the United States, while Algeria, traditionally Spain's largest gas supplier, which ÔÇőmostly pumps ÔÇőgas directly Ôüáto Spain through pipelines, was the second biggest supplier with ÔÇő9,151 GWh of gas, about ÔÇő29% Ôüáof the total imported.
Natural gas imports from Russia accounted for 14.6% of supply in ÔüáFebruary, ÔÇődeclining roughly 52% in ÔÇőabsolute terms from a year ago.

item.title: Higher gasoline prices, volatile stocks, could hit both low- and high-income US consumers as war persists
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 10 (Reuters) - Rising gasoline prices and a wobbly stock market are increasing the risk that the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran could hit hard among consumers across the economic spectrum in the United States, undercutting a key prop of economic growth that had been expected to surge this year on the basis of beefed-up income tax refunds, ÔÇőrelatively low unemployment, and rising asset values.
Heading into the year, analysts felt there was reason both "spurs" of the U.S.' so-called K-shaped economy could sustain or even grow spending in coming months, with wealth effects ÔÇîleading better-off families to consume more and windfall tax refunds from new exemptions on overtime and tip income helping hourly and some service industry workers.
Instead, both sides could be under different sorts of pressure. Average national gasoline prices topped $3.50 a gallon as of Tuesday morning according to American Automobile Association data, a 17% jump from the roughly $3 average from before the beginning of the conflict. Prices are now above $3 in every state except Kansas, where the average was $2.96, and with oil markets still volatile given shipping disruptions in the Strait of Hormuz analysts see $4 gas as ÔÇőa possibility if the conflict persists.
Stocks have come off their highs and the uncertainty about what's next, a factor in household spending based on rising net worth, was on display when President Donald Trump on Monday hinted at ÔÇőa possible quick end to the conflict and sent stocks higher, only to temper his language overnight and keep open the chance of an extended conflict with all of the attendant Ôüárisks to global supply chains, commodity markets, and corporate earnings.
Major U.S. stock indices were little changed when trading opened on Tuesday morning.
For lower-income households, rising gasoline costs could pull money from other categories of spending, spreading the pain across businesses that ÔÇőcould in turn begin to lower job and investment plans in a suddenly uncertain environment.
"The higher the price and the longer it goes the more you shift from higher prices benefitting some companies who boost oil production and get more revenue...to really ÔÇőpinching consumers and being a drag on the economy. There is some point of prices being at a certain level for a certain amount of time that it flips from being a plus to GDP to a drag on GDP and increasing the likelihood of a downturn," said Luke Tilley, chief economist at Wilmington Trust, with oil in the $85 to $100 per barrel range for several months likely to "materially increase the risk of recession because the labor market is already in such a challenging state."
Benchmark Brent crude topped $116 a barrel on Monday before falling below $90 ÔÇőa barrel, then rising again on Tuesday morning. Far from an offramp, U.S. Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth said Tuesday would see the most intense strikes yet on Iran, even as a top general talked of targeting Iran's capacity to lay mines ÔÇőin the Strait of Hormuz, a possible step towards reopening shipping through a chokepoint where the movement of oil from the Middle East has all but stopped.
The sudden shift in risks to the U.S. and global economy has posed a challenge to central bank policymakers, particularly ÔÇîin the U.S., Ôüáwhere officials saw an essentially strong economy facing a balanced set of risks between inflation that was lodged about a percentage point above their 2% target but expected to fall, and an unemployment rate seemingly stable in a range around 4.3% with no clear consensus that it was about to spike higher.
That view has now been hit from both sides: with the economy already having shed jobs unexpectedly in February, there is now the added risk that uncertainty stemming from the conflict could cause businesses to become more cautious about hiring, while inflation could rise more broadly if higher oil prices drive up costs for other things, like shipping rates or home heating bills.
Even though higher fuel prices may have only a temporary influence on inflation, ÔÇőor could even ease broader price pressures on net if ÔÇőconsumers shift spending away from other areas and overall Ôüágrowth slows, the current context poses other risks.
In analysis conducted after oil costs jumped in 2022 following Russia's invasion of Ukraine, Kansas City Fed researchers concluded that increases in high-profile consumer prices , opens new tab like those for gasoline can have an outsized effect on household inflation expectations if those are already elevated by a previous inflation shock - a dynamic Fed policymakers continue to worry about and ÔÇőhave cited as a reason to keep monetary policy restrictive in order to hold expectations in check.
Investors still expect the Fed to cut interest rates this year, but the ÔÇőtiming shifted back after the Ôüástart of U.S. military action, with a potential standoff developing among policymakers between inflation and growth concerns.
The Fed meets next week and is expected to keep its policy rate steady in the current 3.5% to 3.75% range.
Vincent Reinhart, chief economist at BNY Investments and a former top Fed staffer, said it was far too early for the central bank to draw any conclusions about the likely economic fallout from the conflict.
