Even concerns about the impact on growth have to be tempered by the fact that the U.S.' role Ôüáas a net ÔÇőenergy producer means that while higher global prices may mean higher prices at the pump for U.S. consumers, it also means higher income, and ÔÇőpotentially more jobs and investment, for U.S.-based energy firms.
But the longer prices remain elevated the greater the risks, with per-barrel costs in the upper $90 range for a month or longer the sort of material shock that could start to undercut consumption and growth.
"You have to have prices meaningfully higher than what ÔÇőpeople are used to," for an oil shock to change the course of the U.S. economy, Reinhart said. "It's got to be big enough. We're not at the big enough stage."

item.title: Brent oil prices to remain above $95/bbl over next 2 months on Mideast conflict, EIA says
item.description:
HOUSTON, March 10 (Reuters) - Brent oil prices are set to remain ÔÇőabove $95 a barrel over the ÔÇînext two months as conflict in the Middle East rages ÔÇőon, the Energy Information ÔÇőAdministration said on Tuesday in Ôüáits monthly short term ÔÇőenergy outlook report.
Brent crude futures ÔÇőwill then fall below $80 a barrel in the third quarter of 2026, ÔÇőbefore slipping to around $70 ÔÇőby the end of the year.
The ÔÇîeffective Ôüáclosure of the critical chokepoint, the Strait of Hormuz, through which a fifth of ÔÇőglobal oil ÔÇőflows Ôüáevery day, will cause Mideast oil output ÔÇőto fall further in ÔÇőthe Ôüácoming weeks.
Those production shut-ins will gradually ease as transit Ôüáresumes, ÔÇőthe EIA added.

item.title: Elected in an economic upswing, Chile's Kast takes office as global turmoil rattles markets
item.description:
SANTIAGO, March 10 (Reuters) - Elected on promises of economic growth, deregulation and public spending cuts, local markets rallied after Chile elected far-right Jose Antonio Kast as president in December, but the economic tailwinds have turned turbulent as the Iran war has sent global markets into a tailspin.
Kast, who ÔÇőtakes power on Wednesday, is now in charge of managing the turmoil. A spokesperson for Kast's economic team said that no economic ÔÇîcontingencies were planned at the moment, but did not provide further details of how recent developments would impact their economic agenda.
"(Kast) was elected on a promise of being an 'emergency government' that was going to fix things that mattered to Chileans quickly," said Kenneth Bunker, a political analyst and academic at University of San Sebastian, adding that one of Kast's main promises ÔÇőwas economic growth.
"Kast's priorities will probably remain stable, but his ability to carry them out will probably be affected by the exchange rate, inflation ÔÇőand economic growth," Bunker said, adding that if his government can't achieve the growth it was expecting, several of its Ôüáplans could be delayed.
Chile, the world's largest copper producer and second-largest lithium producer, is sensitive to fluctuations in international markets.
Copper, Chile's main export and the country's ÔÇőmain source of revenue, climbed from less than $10,000 per ton last June to a peak of $13,618 at the end of January and every cent increase represents ÔÇőan additional $27 million to $35 million for the Chilean treasury, according to the outgoing government.
Before the Iran war, experts estimated Chile could receive up to $4 billion in additional revenue from the soaring prices of the red metal but the price has been volatile in recent weeks, dropping up to 8% from recent highs. As of Tuesday, the price has risen back up ÔÇőto $13,098.
Chile is also one of the largest oil importers in Latin America due to its lack of domestic production, increasing the impact of rising oil prices ÔÇőwhich hit nearly $120 a barrel since the start of the war.
"The Iran war has increased inflation risks considerably," Oxford Economics said in a report surveying emerging markets published on Monday.
The ÔÇîreport added ÔüáCentral and Eastern Europe, Chile and India would be the hardest hit in its oil shock simulation, which showed Q2 inflation estimates rising between 0.4ppts to 1.7ppts.
"(Chile) is very sensitive to external shocks, very sensitive, whether they be geopolitical, military, or fluctuations in global markets," said Marcela Vera, an economist at the University of Santiago.
"The economy has very few financial protection barriers and it has a lot of free trade agreements, so its model is based on a primary export system."
After ÔÇőmonths of setting new highs, Chile's ÔÇőIPSA stock market continued to rise Ôüáfollowing the election and peaked in late January, with about a 65% surge from a year earlier.
Chile's peso, boosted by strong copper prices, had been gaining steam since July and reached its strongest point in years in early February.
But since ÔÇőthose recent highs, the stock market has dropped over 10% and the currency has depreciated about 5% as spiking ÔÇőoil prices and global Ôüáeconomic uncertainty rock the nation.
Vera noted that Chile has a fuel stabilization fund, MEPCO, that operates on three-week cycles to help smooth out price spikes.
"If the war continues for months, then we'll have a chronic effect on our economy," Vera said. "It won't just be the price of oil going up, but the cost of logistics and Ôüáthe price ÔÇőof the dollar."
A JPMorgan report released Friday said that while MEPCO dampens the impacts of spiking ÔÇőoil prices, it "does not eliminate pass-through effects" and raised its inflation forecast by 20 bp to 3.6% for December with "risks tilted to the upside."

item.title: Brazil's Bolsonaro seeks court approval for prison visit by Trump adviser
item.description: BRASILIA, March 10 (Reuters) - Lawyers ÔÇőfor former ÔÇîBrazilian President Jair ÔÇőBolsonaro have ÔÇőasked the country's ÔüáSupreme ÔÇőCourt to ÔÇőallow Darren Beattie, who ÔÇőwas ÔÇőrecently tapped by ÔÇîthe ÔüáTrump administration for a senior advisory ÔÇőrole ÔÇőoverseeing ÔüáBrazil , to ÔÇővisit ÔÇőhim Ôüáin prison, a Ôüádocument ÔÇőshowed ÔÇőon Tuesday.

item.title: US urges Israel to stop attacks on Iran energy sites, Axios reports
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 10 (Reuters) - The ÔÇőUnited States has asked Israel ÔÇîto halt strikes on Iran's energy infrastructure, Axios reported on Tuesday, ÔÇőciting three sources familiar ÔÇőwith the matter.
Washington sent the message Ôüáat a senior political ÔÇőlevel and to IDF Chief of ÔÇőStaff Eyal Zamir, Axios reported, citing an Israeli official.
The Trump administration cited ÔÇőthree reasons, including a goal ÔÇőto cooperate with Iran's oil sector after ÔÇîthe Ôüáwar, according to the report.
Other reasons included a fear that such strikes would harm the ÔÇőIranian ÔÇőpublic and Ôüátrigger massive Iranian retaliatory attacks on energy infrastructure ÔÇőacross Gulf states, Axios ÔÇőreported.
The ÔüáWhite House, U.S. State Department and Israeli Embassy in Washington Ôüádid ÔÇőnot immediately respond ÔÇőto requests for comments.

item.title: Ukraine sends drone experts to three countries in Middle East, Zelenskiy says
item.description:
KYIV, March 10 (Reuters) - Ukraine has sent air defence teams to Qatar, the United Arab Emirates and ÔÇîSaudi Arabia to help them in their fight against Iran's aerial attacks, President Volodymyr Zelenskiy said on Tuesday.
Gulf states have expended large quantities of precious air-defence missiles to counter IranÔÇÖs attack drones and have sought Ukraine's expertise in downing ÔÇőthem. Kyiv downs Russian drones every night using an array of weaponry including cheaper, ÔÇősmaller drones or jamming equipment.
"Regarding the situation in the Middle East, we have Ôüásent our teams: three professional, equipped teams," Zelenskiy told reporters. He did not give details ÔÇőon the teams but said they should all be in place this week.
His communications adviser also said ÔÇőUkrainian specialists had already been deployed to a U.S. military base in Jordan, without providing details.
Zelenskiy said the presence of Ukrainian pilots was key to the effectiveness of interceptor drone operations.
"Even those countries that were quietly buying ÔÇőinterceptors have, I think, realised that without our military, our operators, our software... without all of ÔÇőthat, the interceptors simply don't work."
Since the U.S. and Israel began airstrikes on Iran on February 28, ÔÇîUkraine has Ôüásought to leverage its long experience of countering Russia's Iranian-designed Shahed attack drones.
Kyiv says Russia - which now manufactures them itself - has launched more than 57,000 Shahed-type drones at Ukraine so far.
Reuters reported last week that the U.S. and Qatar were looking to buy interceptor drones from Ukraine as a ÔÇőmuch cheaper alternative to missiles ÔÇőfor downing Shaheds.
Asked Ôüáwhat Kyiv would get in return for the deployment of the specialist teams, Zelenskiy said Ukraine was seeking air defence missiles above all else.
Kyiv has ÔÇőcomplained for months of critical shortages of those munitions, particularly for the ÔÇőU.S.-made Patriot Ôüásystems which are the Ukraine's only effective tool to intercept ballistic missiles.
The Iran war has delayed a fresh round of peace talks brokered by Washington between Russia and Ukraine.
Zelenskiy told reporters on Tuesday the Ôüánext ÔÇőtrilateral meeting had been postponed to next week, adding that ÔÇőhe wanted more swaps of prisoners of war and discussion of a possible meeting between himself, U.S. President Donald Trump ÔÇőand Russia's Vladimir Putin.

item.title: Exclusive: As many as 150 US troops wounded so far in Iran war, sources say
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 10 (Reuters) - As many as 150 U.S. troops have been wounded in the 10-day-old ÔÇőwar with Iran, two people familiar with the matter told ÔÇîReuters on Tuesday.
The casualty figure has not been previously reported. Prior to Reuters' publication of the figure, the Pentagon had only disclosed eight U.S. personnel seriously injured.
In a statement after ÔÇőReuters published its report, the Pentagon estimated the figure to be approximately ÔÇő140 wounded and said the vast majority of them were Ôüáminor.
"Since the start of Operation Epic Fury, approximately 140 U.S. service members ÔÇőhave been wounded over 10 days of sustained attacks," said chief Pentagon ÔÇőspokesperson Sean Parnell.
He said 108 of the wounded service members had already returned to duty.
Parnell said the eight seriously wounded service members were receiving the highest level of medical ÔÇőcare.
Reuters could not determine the types of injuries and whether they include traumatic brain ÔÇőinjuries, which are common after exposure to blasts.
Iran has launched retaliatory strikes against U.S. military bases ÔÇîsince Ôüáthe start of the conflict on Feb. 28. It has also struck diplomatic missions in Arab Gulf states as well as hotels and airports and damaged oil infrastructure.
The Pentagon says the number of Iranian strikes has fallen sharply ÔÇősince the start of ÔÇőthe war, as Ôüáthe U.S. military bombs Iran's weapons inventories and targets Iran's more limited number of missile launchers.
Asked if ÔÇőIran was a stronger adversary than he expected when the ÔÇőU.S. military Ôüádrew up its war plans, General Dan Caine, chairman of the Joint Chiefs of Staff, told reporters earlier on Tuesday that the fight was not harder than Ôüáexpected.
"I think ÔÇőthey're fighting, and I respect that, but ÔÇőI don't think they are more formidable than what we thought," Caine told a Pentagon briefing.

item.title: US Senate confirms National Security Agency director, ending long vacancy
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 10 (Reuters) - The U.S. Senate confirmed General Joshua Rudd on Tuesday to lead the National Security Agency and the ÔÇőnation's Cyber Command, filling a position that had been vacant ÔÇîsince President Donald Trump abruptly fired his predecessor in a national security purge.
The Senate voted 71-29 to confirm Rudd, making him a four-star general.
Trump nominated Rudd, then ÔÇőa lieutenant general and No. 2 at the military's ÔÇőIndo-Pacific Command, for the so-called dual-hat position in December.
The NSA Ôüámonitors, collects and processes information and data for global intelligence ÔÇőand counterintelligence purposes. Cyber Command conducts offensive and defensive cyber operations, ÔÇőan increasingly important part of military campaigns including the current Iran war .
The agency was shaken up in April, when Trump fired General Timothy Haugh along with Wendy ÔÇőNoble, his deputy.
No reason was given for the firings, which took ÔÇőplace after the Republican president had an Oval Office meeting with Laura Loomer, ÔÇîa Ôüáfar-right political activist known as a conspiracy theorist, who said on X that she had given Trump a list of officials she considered disloyal to him.
In a statement, Republican U.S. Senator Tom Cotton ÔÇőof Arkansas, chairman ÔÇőof the Senate ÔüáIntelligence Committee, praised the confirmation.
"General Rudd is a war hero with a lifetime of service to ÔÇőour nation. He is the right choice to lead ÔÇőthe Ôüáprotection of our nation from cyberattacks by Iran , Russia, and China," said Cotton, who criticized Democrats for not acting more quickly.
Senator Ron Wyden of ÔüáOregon, a ÔÇősenior member of the intelligence panel, ÔÇősaid in a letter to Trump , opens new tab that he did not feel Rudd was qualified and ÔÇőblocked efforts to fast-track his confirmation.

item.title: U.S. Navy has not yet escorted ships through Strait of Hormuz, White House says
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 10 (Reuters) - The U.S. military has not yet escorted any commercial ships through the Strait of ÔÇőHormuz, the White House said on Tuesday, just after U.S. Secretary of ÔÇîEnergy Chris Wright deleted a post on X in which had said the U.S. Navy successfully escorted an oil tanker through the key water way.
The U.S.-Israel war against Iran has already effectively halted ÔÇőshipments through the Strait along Iran's coast, where a fifth of global ÔÇőoil and liquefied natural gas normally passes, and Middle East oil Ôüáproducers have run out of storage and stopped pumping.
U.S. President Donald Trump said ÔÇőon March 3 that the U.S. would provide protection through the Strait for oil ÔÇőtankers. The Pentagon on Tuesday renewed threats to hit Iran harder unless shipments can flow through and said it was striking Iranian mine-laying vessels and mine storage facilities.
Wright then posted on X ÔÇőthat the U.S. Navy had escorted an oil tanker through the Strait of ÔÇőHormuz "to ensure oil remains flowing to global markets."
Soon after, he deleted the post for reasons that ÔÇîwere Ôüáunclear.
The United States has not yet escorted any oil tankers or vessels through the Strait of Hormuz, White House Press Secretary Karoline Leavitt told reporters when asked about the issue at a press conference later on Tuesday.
Commenting on Wright's remarks, a ÔÇőspokesperson for Iran's Revolutionary ÔÇőGuards denied an Ôüáoil ship had been escorted.
"Any movement of the U.S. fleet and its allies will be stopped by our missiles and ÔÇődrones," Ali Mohammad Naini said in comments carried by Iranian ÔÇőstate media.
The Ôüátop U.S. general earlier on Tuesday said the U.S. military has started looking at ways to potentially escort ships through the Strait of Hormuz, should it be ordered to Ôüádo ÔÇőso.
"We're looking at a range of options there," General ÔÇőDan Caine told reporters at the Pentagon.

item.title: White House to Americans: Rise in energy prices over Iran is temporary
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 10 (Reuters) - The American public will see oil and gas ÔÇőprices drop rapidly, White House press secretary ÔÇîKaroline Leavitt said on Tuesday, once the objectives of the joint Israeli-U.S. air war against Iran are fully achieved.
"Rest ÔÇőassured, to the American people, the recent ÔÇőincrease in oil and gas prices is temporary, and Ôüáthis operation will result in lower gas ÔÇőprices in the long term," Leavitt told reporters.
Oil ÔÇőprices on Monday surged to more than $119 a barrel - their highest since June 2022 - as supply cuts by Saudi Arabia ÔÇőand other producers stoked fears of major ÔÇődisruptions to global supplies.
Leavitt added that Trump and his energy team ÔÇîwere Ôüáclosely watching the markets, speaking with industry leaders, and that the U.S. military was drawing up additional options in line with the president's directive ÔÇőto keep the ÔÇőStrait of ÔüáHormuz open.
The deliberations reflect White House worries that the surge in ÔÇőoil prices - following more than a week ÔÇőof ÔüáU.S. and Israeli strikes on Iran - will hurt U.S. businesses and consumers ahead of the November midterm Ôüáelections, ÔÇőwhen Trump's fellow Republicans hope ÔÇőto retain control of Congress.

item.title: Nigeria prioritises local gasoline supply, suspends import licences
item.description:
LAGOS, March 10 (Reuters) - Nigeria has suspended the issuance of gasoline import licences for a second straight month as regulators begin enforcing provisions of ÔÇőthe Petroleum Industry Act (PIA) that only allow imports when the African ÔÇîcountry's domestic supply falls short.
Data from the Nigerian Midstream and Downstream Petroleum Regulatory Authority (NMDPRA) show no import licences were issued in February, while the Crude Oil Refineries Association of ÔÇőNigeria (CORAN) confirmed that none has been issued so far in March, signalling ÔÇőa shift towards prioritising local output.
This shift highlights a stronger Ôüáintent by Nigerian authorities to protect domestic refining and marks a win ÔÇőfor the Dangote Refinery, which last year sued the regulator and the state ÔÇőoil company to force a halt to imports.
Under the PIA, the regulator may grant import permits only when domestic production is not enough to meet national demand.
The previous regulator, who quit ÔÇőlast year, argued that issuing licences was necessary to maintain competition and ÔÇőprevent market dominance.
Fuel pump prices have surged by more than 54% since the U.S. and ÔÇîIsrael Ôüábegan strikes on Iran last week, pushing global oil markets higher. NMDPRA spokesperson George EneÔÇĹIta blamed the sharp rise in prices on escalating conflict in the Middle East.
Nigeria's average daily petrol consumption fell to 56.9 million litres per day ÔÇőin February 2026, ÔÇődown from 60.2 Ôüámillion litres in January.
In February, the Dangote Refinery supplied 36.5 million litres of petrol and 8 million litres of ÔÇődiesel to the local market. The regulator deemed these volumes ÔÇőwere sufficient, Ôüáleading to its decision to withhold import licences.
Eche Idoko, spokesperson for the Crude Oil Refiners Association of Nigeria (CORAN), which has long urged the government to stop Ôüáissuing import ÔÇőlicences that undermine local refiners' margins, welcomed the ÔÇőregulator's stance.
"For us, anything that protects local production is a good move. The challenge now is ÔÇőto sustain the momentum," Idoko said.

item.title: Rising gas prices from Iran war imperil Trump's Republican majority in Congress
item.description:
March 10 (Reuters) - Minutes into Donald Trump 's State of the Union address laying out his argument for why voters should keep Republicans in power in November's midterm elections, the president boasted that falling gasoline prices had ÔÇősolved a "disaster" left behind by his predecessor.
Two weeks later, gas prices have risen by nearly 60 cents a gallon, part of a burgeoning energy crisis that began when ÔÇîTrump and Israel launched air strikes against Iran on February 28 and set off a regional conflict that has expanded well beyond Iran's borders.
The pain at the pump is quickly turning into a political headache for Trump and his fellow Republicans, whose narrow congressional majority looks increasingly fragile ahead of the midterms.
Crude oil prices on Tuesday were easing off Monday's dramatic gains, and it remains to be seen where gasoline prices will head in the nearly nine months ÔÇőleading up to the November 3 election. But even before the Iran war, U.S. voters were already angry about the high cost of living and frustrated that Trump has not done ÔÇőmore to address it, Reuters/Ipsos polls show .
"You can't hide gas prices," said Jacob Perry, a Republican strategist. "You can lie about all this other stuff and claim Ôüáeverything is fake news. But there's a giant sign on every street corner saying how bad things are. Literally every commute to work is a reminder."
Democrats have promised to put ÔÇőaffordability center stage in their campaigns. The party needs to flip only three Republican-held House of Representatives seats to win a majority, though they face a tougher path in the Senate.
"Trump promised a Golden ÔÇőAge in America," House Democratic leader Hakeem Jeffries said on X. "Meanwhile, Republicans are crashing the economy, gas prices are out of control and the extremists are spending billions dropping bombs in the Middle East."
Some House Republicans, who are huddling in Florida this week at a policy retreat to discuss their legislative agenda, acknowledged that surging gas prices are a worry to voters, but cited Trump's assertion that the increase would be short-lived and said the administration's efforts ÔÇőto increase domestic energy production would lessen the sting.
"Every day that people pull into the gas station and pay what they're paying for fuel, I mean, it hurts, and we know it hurts," ÔÇősaid Representative Austin Scott, of Georgia. "We're going to do what we can to fix it. They understand it's a short-term issue."
The average gas price nationwide on Tuesday was $3.54 a gallon, up about 19% ÔÇîsince the Ôüáwar began, according to AAA.
In a sign of the White HouseÔÇÖs concern, Trump planned to review several options aimed at curbing oil prices, Reuters reported on Monday, including a possible release of crude oil from strategic reserves, restricting U.S. exports and waiving some federal taxes. Trump said on Monday evening that the U.S. would waive some oil-related sanctions, though he did not offer details.
Trump plans to discuss the economy when he travels to Ohio and Kentucky on Wednesday, the White House said.
Higher gas prices may be particularly damaging for a president who won the White House in 2024 largely on the promise that ÔÇőhe would curb inflation, lower energy rates and ÔÇőbring down the cost of living.
Representative Warren Davidson, Ôüáan Ohio Republican, said the rise in gas prices is a "concern" for the midterms.
"The economy is always going to be one of the things on the ballot," he said. "The other things the president's done on the economy have been tremendous, so we'll see how it turns out."
Trump's own response to the spiking prices seems unlikely to assuage Americans' anxiety. In an interview with Reuters last week, Trump said he had no ÔÇőconcern about gas prices and Ôüápredicted they would drop once the Iran conflict ends, adding, ÔÇťIf they rise, they rise.ÔÇŁ
He repeated his contention on social media over the weekend, saying that ÔÇťshort term oil pricesÔÇŁ were a ÔÇťvery small price to payÔÇŁ for global security.
TrumpÔÇÖs shifting rationales for the Iran war, however, have made it more difficult for him to argue that the disruption to the U.S. economy is worthwhile. Administration officials have not presented evidence that Iran posed an Ôüáimmediate threat, and ÔÇőTrump himself had insisted that U.S. strikes last year ÔÇťobliteratedÔÇŁ the countryÔÇÖs nuclear weapons program.
"I donÔÇÖt think that people care that ÔÇőmuch about Iran," said John Feehery, a Republican strategist. "I think they care about their gas prices. Any president that spends the majority of his time talking about international issues and not talking about dinner table issues, they run a risk of ÔÇőalienating voters.
"This better be pretty quick, thatÔÇÖs all I can say," he added of the war.

item.title: US power use to beat record highs in 2026 and 2027 as AI use surges, EIA says
item.description:
NEW YORK, March 10 (Reuters) - U.S. ÔÇîpower consumption, which hit its second straight record high in 2025, will rise further in 2026 and 2027, the Energy Information Administration (EIA) said in its Short-Term Energy Outlook (STEO) on Tuesday.
The EIA projected power demand ÔÇőwill rise from a record 4,195 billion kilowatt-hours (kWh) in 2025 to 4,260 billion ÔÇőkWh in 2026 and 4,388 billion kWh in 2027.
Demand is surging due Ôüáin large part to data centers dedicated to artificial intelligence and cryptocurrency, and as ÔÇőhomes and businesses use more electricity and less fossil fuel for heat and transportation.
The EIA ÔÇőforecast power sales in 2026 will rise to 1,535 billion kWh for residential consumers, 1,525 billion kWh for commercial customers and 1,056 billion kWh for industrial customers.
The forecasts compare with all-time highs of 1,517 ÔÇőbillion kWh for residential consumers in 2025, 1,486 billion kWh in 2025 for commercial ÔÇőcustomers and 1,064 billion kWh for industrial customers in 2000.
As renewable output rises, the EIA said the ÔÇîshare Ôüáof power generation from natural gas would hold at 40% in 2026, the same as 2025, before sliding to 39% in 2027. Coal's share will slide from 17% in 2025 to 16% in 2026 and 15% in 2027.
The percentage of renewable generation will rise ÔÇőfrom around 24% in ÔÇő2025 to 25% Ôüáin 2026 and 27% in 2027, while nuclear power's share will rise from 18% in 2025 to 19% in 2026 before easing ÔÇőback to 18% in 2027, according to the outlook.
The EIA projected ÔÇőgas sales Ôüáin 2026 would slide to 13.0 billion cubic feet per day (bcfd) for residential consumers, 9.6 bcfd for commercial customers and 23.3 bcfd for industrial customers, but rise to 36.2 bcfd for Ôüápower generation.
Those ÔÇőfigures compare with all-time highs of 14.3 bcfd ÔÇőin 1996 for residential consumers, 9.9 bcfd in 2025 for commercial customers, 23.8 bcfd in 1973 for industrial customers, ÔÇőand 36.8 bcfd in 2024 for power generation.

item.title: Russian oil prices soar though tanker costs eat into gains
item.description:
March 10 (Reuters) - The price of Russian Urals oil, shunned by many buyers ÔÇîamid Ukraine-related sanctions, has surged on the war in Iran, though costs are also rising, traders said on Tuesday.
With tankers from top exporter Saudi Arabia effectively blocked by Iranian ÔÇőthreats to Gulf shipping, attention has returned to Russia, the world's ÔÇősecond-largest seller.
Urals crude from Russian ports was being offered at around $76 Ôüáper barrel on Monday on a free on board (FOB) basis, Reuters data ÔÇőshowed, up from $45 just two weeks ago.
The price of a Urals cargo loading ÔÇőfrom the Baltic Sea port of Primorsk has jumped to around $54 million this week from about $35 million in February, according to Reuters calculations.
Russian sellers faced near negative margins on exports earlier this ÔÇőyear, traders said.
Despite G7 and other efforts to cap Russian seaborne oil ÔÇőprices, Urals for delivery to Indian ports is now selling at a premium to benchmark Brent ÔÇîcrude Ôüáfor the first time ever.
The United States last week granted Indian refiners a temporary waiver to resume imports of Russian crude already aboard tankers.
Yet traders said Russian sellers also face sharply higher costs. They said several vessels were fixed ÔÇőat around $22-23 million from ÔÇőRussia's Baltic ÔüáSea ports to India amid a scarcity of the vessels.
That's nearly double rates seen in early February and up about $5-8 ÔÇőmillion from late last week.
From Russia's Black Sea port ÔÇőof Novorossiysk, Ôüáwhich resumed loadings on Friday after recent drone damage, freight costs also rose above $20 million to India, traders said.
Brent oil surged to more than $119 a barrel on ÔüáMonday ÔÇőto its highest since mid-2022 amid supply cuts by ÔÇőSaudi Arabia and other Gulf producers.
Prices pared gains after U.S. President Donald Trump predicted the war in the ÔÇőMiddle East could end soon .

item.title: US Navy tells shipping industry Hormuz escorts not possible for now
item.description:
LONDON, March 10 (Reuters) - The U.S. Navy has refused near-daily requests from the shipping industry for military escorts through the Strait of Hormuz since ÔÇőthe start of the war on Iran, saying the risk of attacks is too high for now, according to sources familiar with the matter.
The ÔÇîNavy's assessments spell continued disruption to Middle East oil exports and reflect a divergence from President Donald TrumpÔÇÖs statements that the U.S. is prepared to provide naval escorts whenever needed to restart regular shipments along the key waterway.
Shipping along the narrow strait has all but halted since the start of the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran more than a week ago, preventing exports of around a fifth of the worldÔÇÖs oil supply ÔÇőand sending global oil prices surging to highs not seen since 2022.
A senior official with Iran's Revolutionary Guards has said the strait is closed and Iran will ÔÇőfire on any ship trying to pass, Iranian media reported last week . Several ships have already been hit.
The U.S. Navy has held regular Ôüábriefings with shipping and oil industry counterparts and has said during those briefings it is unable to provide escorts for the time being, three shipping industry sources familiar with ÔÇőthe matter said.
The sources, who declined to be identified due to the sensitivity of the matter, said the shipping industry has been making requests almost daily during the calls for naval ÔÇőescorts through the strait.
One of the sources said the NavyÔÇÖs assessment during TuesdayÔÇÖs briefing had not changed and that escorts would only be possible once the risk of attack was reduced.
The Pentagon did not immediately respond to a request for comment.
Trump has said repeatedly in recent days that the United States is prepared to escort tankers through the Strait of Hormuz when necessary.
"When the time comes, ÔÇőthe U.S. Navy and its partners will escort tankers through the strait, if needed. I hope it's not going to be needed, but if it's needed, we'll escort them ÔÇőright through," he said on Monday during a press conference at his Mar-a-Lago resort in Florida.
The U.S. military has started looking at options to potentially escort ships through the strait, should it be ÔÇîordered to do Ôüáso, General Dan Caine, chairman of the Joint Chiefs of Staff, said on Tuesday.
"We're looking at a range of options there," Caine told reporters at the Pentagon.
A U.S. official told Reuters the U.S. military has not yet escorted any commercial ships through the strait. Earlier in the day, U.S. Secretary of Energy Chris Wright deleted a post on X in which he said the Navy had successfully escorted one through.
While there have been some voyages through the waterway in recent days , the majority of shipping traffic remains on hold with hundreds of ships ÔÇőanchored.
Saudi Arabia's Aramco, the ÔÇőworld's top oil exporter, said on ÔüáTuesday there would be "catastrophic consequences" for the world's oil markets if the war on Iran continues to disrupt shipping in the Strait of Hormuz.
Maritime security specialists and analysts said securing the strait will be difficult, even if the effort involves an international coalition, because of ÔÇőIranÔÇÖs ability to deploy mines or cheaply made attack drones.
"Neither France, the United States, an international coalition or anybody is in a ÔÇőposition to secure the ÔüáStrait of Hormuz," said Adel Bakawan, director of the European Institute for Studies on the Middle East and North Africa.
Last week, Iran used a remote-controlled boat laden with explosives to damage a crude oil tanker anchored in Iraqi waters, according to initial assessments from two Iraqi port security sources.
A maritime security source said securing the strait could require the U.S. to take control of Iran's Ôüávast coast.
"There ÔÇőare not enough naval vessels to do that and the risks remain high even with an escort. One ÔÇőor two vessels can be overwhelmed by a swarm (of fast boats or drones)," the source said.
The Pentagon renewed threats on Tuesday to hit Iran harder unless shipments can flow through and said it was striking Iranian mine-laying ÔÇővessels and mine-storage facilities.

item.title: Zelenskiy says Ukraine's military hit key Russian missile plant in Bryansk region
item.description:
KYIV, March 10 (Reuters) - President Volodymyr Zelenskiy said Ukraine's military struck a key ÔÇîplant in Russia's Bryansk region bordering Ukraine on Tuesday that produces components for missiles .
Ukraine's military said British Storm Shadow missiles were deployed against the Kremniy bfactory ÔÇőand posted a video which it said showed explosions ÔÇőand a fire at the plant. It said the facility Ôüáproduced critical missile components.
"Our fighters struck one of the important ÔÇőRussian military factories in Bryansk," Zelenskiy said in his nightly video ÔÇőaddress, referring to a report from top Ukrainian commander Oleksandr Syrskyi.
"The plant produced electronics and components for Russian missiles. The very missiles that strike our ÔÇőcities, our villages and civilians," he said.
The governor of Bryansk region, ÔÇőAlexander Bogomaz, said on Telegram that six civilians were killed and 37 injured ÔÇîand Ôüáposted a video showing him at the scene.
Bogomaz made no mention of the plant in what he described as a "terrorist missile attack". The injured were being treated in hospital.
The Ukrainian military's general ÔÇőstaff, posting on ÔÇőTelegram, described the Ôüáplant as "a critically important link in the chain of production of Russian high precision weapons" producing ÔÇősemiconductor devices and integrated micro chips.
"The target was hit ÔÇőand Ôüásignificant damage to production facilities was recorded," the General Staff said. "The extent of the damage is being clarified."
The video posted on Telegram shows Ôüáaerial ÔÇőimages of a series of explosions and ÔÇőlarge fires over a large area near woods.

item.title: Israeli president tells Bild: War with Iran needs 'end result', not exact timetable
item.description:
March 10 (Reuters) - Israel's President Isaac Herzog on Tuesday did not offer a timetable on when the war ÔÇőwith Iran could end, telling Germany's Bild newspaper: "We ÔÇîneed to take a deep breath and get to the end result."
Herzog said the U.S. and Israeli attacks on ÔÇőIran were changing the whole configuration of the ÔÇőMiddle East. He defended strikes on Iranian Ôüáoil sites as a way of taking away ÔÇőmoney from Tehran's "war machine".
The interview was published as the ÔÇőU.S. and Israel pounded Iran with what the Pentagon and Iranians on the ground said were the most intense airstrikes of ÔÇőthe war, despite global markets betting that President ÔÇőDonald Trump will seek to end the conflict soon.
Israel's foreign minister, ÔÇîGideon ÔüáSaar, had earlier said his country was not planning for an endless war and was consulting with Washington about when to stop it.
ÔÇťThe Iranians are ÔÇőthe ones spreading ÔÇőchaos and Ôüáterror throughout the region and the world. So I think if we measure ÔÇőeverything by a speedometer, we wonÔÇÖt get ÔÇőanywhere. ÔüáWe need to take a deep breath and get to the end result,ÔÇŁ Herzog told Bild.
Eliminating the ÔüáIranian threat ÔÇőwould "enable the entire system in ÔÇőthe region to suddenly breathe again and develop further. ThatÔÇÖs fantastic,ÔÇŁ ÔÇőhe added.

item.title: Anthropic gives lesson in AI revenue hallucination
item.description:
LONDON, March 10 (Reuters Breakingviews) - AnthropicÔÇÖs battle ÔÇőwith the Pentagon carries immensely high stakes for the future of artificial intelligence. Along the way, though, it ÔÇîhas revealed something just as interesting about the prosaic realities of accounting.
In a court filing , opens new tab , Anthropic Chief Financial Officer Krishna Rao said revenue has exceeded ÔÇť$5 billion to date.ÔÇŁ This figure sits awkwardly beside the Claude developerÔÇÖs ÔÇťrun-rateÔÇŁ claims: $14 billion , opens new tab as of February 12, rising to $19 billion by monthÔÇÖs end. The gap reflects ÔÇőSilicon ValleyÔÇÖs habit of touting metrics that assume a lot about the future.
The $5 billion figure refers to GAAP revenue generated ÔÇőfrom 2023 through to December 2025, a person familiar with the matter told Breakingviews; the $19 billion Ôüáis an extrapolation. Anthropic defines ÔÇťrun-rate revenueÔÇŁ in two parts. Use the last 28 days of sales from customers charged on a ÔÇőconsumption basis and multiply it by 13. Then, multiply the monthly subscription take by 12, and add the two together.
These snapshots are ÔÇőinexact, capturing or missing sudden consumption spikes or dips. It helps explain how $14 billion can become $19 billion within weeks.
Big businesses account for 80% of AnthropicÔÇÖs revenue, and they tend to be billed for consumption, making the headline run-rate highly sensitive. Pricing changes, promotional credits or attempts to optimize usage ÔÇőall have an impact. This makes calculating trailing revenue from reported numbers error-prone. Still, working backwards from Anthropic's run-rate at various ÔÇőtimes, it's clear most sales have been generated in recent months.
How metrics are defined also complicates comparisons. OpenAIÔÇÖs annual recurring revenue, which it said , opens new tab reached $20 ÔÇîbillion as Ôüáof the end of December, is meant to specifically capture subscriptions rather than metered sales. Further twists include revenue-sharing agreements with partners such as Microsoft (MSFT.O) , opens new tab .
Run-rate figures can be useful when companies start small and are growing quickly. But it also makes it easier to tout brief, rapid momentum in support of projections showing astronomical growth far out into the future.
Because the Pentagon has moved to ÔÇőblacklist Anthropic, these dangers are ÔÇőparamount. Anthropic Chief Commercial Officer ÔüáPaul Smith said , opens new tab one customer paused discussions on a $15 million contract after the company was labeled a supply-chain risk, while two financial-services companies refused to finalize agreements worth a combined $80 million unless they secured ÔÇőbroad cancellation rights. Anthropic has sued to challenge the designation.
No one is being misled. Until ÔÇőAI companies standardize Ôüáhow they report revenue and are upfront about potential volatility, however, their metrics risk looking like a plausible hallucination.
Follow Karen Kwok on LinkedIn , opens new tab and X , opens new tab .

item.title: LNG Canada ramps up output as Iran war threatens global gas supplies
item.description:
HOUSTON, March 10 (Reuters) - LNG Canada, a Shell-led (SHEL.L) , opens new tab venture, has ramped up production and exports to Asia this ÔÇîmonth, LSEG data show, as the Iran war threatens Asian natural gas supplies which are particularly vulnerable to global disruptions.
The LNG project in Kitimat, British Columbia, which began operations in June 2025, has exported five cargoes in the first 11 days of March, already exceeding half its total February ÔÇővolume, the data shows. A sixth shipment is due to depart on Tuesday.
All cargoes have been sent to ÔÇőAsia, with two heading to Japan, two to South Korea and one to the Philippines. ÔüáThe plant appears to be operating close to its full capacity of 14 million metric tons per year, according to ÔÇőthe LSEG data.
An LNG Canada spokesperson did not comment on the facility's current production volumes but said the company continues to ÔÇőadvance early operations at the site safely and responsibly.
"A 58th cargo is scheduled to depart in the coming days," the spokesperson said in an email.
The venture can export just under 1.2 million metric tons per month. In the first one-third of this month it has loaded ÔÇőmore than 400,000 tons, the data showed.
Global markets have rushed to adapt after Qatar, which supplies about 20% of globally traded ÔÇőLNG, was forced to halt production and declare force majeure when the conflict blocked tankers from transiting the Strait of Hormuz.
"They are Ôüáfurther ramping up activity to push toward full capacity, as well as trying to make a quick surge in LNG output to get more LNG on the water to Asia and take advantage of higher prices in the region," said Martin King, an analyst with RBN Energy.
LNG Canada is the first large-scale Canadian LNG facility to start production and the first major ÔÇőNorth American plant with direct ÔÇőaccess to the Pacific, shortening Ôüásailing time to Asian buyers compared with U.S. Gulf Coast exporters.
The plant has faced operational challenges since startup but has gradually increased output since January, LSEG data shows.
Canadian natural gas producers ÔÇőhad stepped up production significantly in anticipation of LNG Canada's startup last summer, but then ÔÇőfaced a slump Ôüáin domestic prices when the project did not draw down supplies as quickly as the markets had expected.
"It seems like they are pretty close, for the last two weeks, to capacity," said Mike Belenkie, CEO of Advantage Energy (AAV.TO) , opens new tab , which was one of several Ôüácompanies that ÔÇőtemporarily curtailed production in September when Western Canadian natural gas prices temporarily ÔÇőwent negative.
Daily spot prices at the Alberta Energy Company (AECO) storage hub hovered close to $2 per million British thermal units on Tuesday, a $1.25 discount to the ÔÇőU.S. Henry Hub benchmark.

item.title: US lawmakers worry Trump may put 'boots on the ground' in Iran
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 10 (Reuters) - Democratic U.S. senators expressed alarm on Tuesday about the Iran war , saying they were worried that President Donald Trump could deploy U.S. ground forces and noting the high risks ÔÇőgiven Russian support for Tehran's military.
"We know that Russia is already helping with ÔÇîintel, providing that to Iran , and that there's an axis with Russia and China , Iran and North Korea that puts at greater risk the United States and our national security," Democratic Senator Jeanne Shaheen of New Hampshire said on ÔÇőCNN.
Speaking after a classified briefing from administration officials to the Senate Armed Services ÔÇőCommittee, Shaheen called on the administration to send officials to testify publicly in ÔüáCongress.
Two sources familiar with U.S. intelligence reports have said that Russia is providing Iran ÔÇőwith targeting intelligence on U.S. bases and ships.
Shaheen and other senators said a series of closed-door ÔÇőbriefings from Trump aides have left them with more questions than answers, especially about the cost and expected duration of the war, and whether U.S. forces would be sent into Tehran.
"We seem to be on a ÔÇőpath toward deploying American troops on the ground in Iran to accomplish any of the ÔÇőpotential objectives here," Senator Richard Blumenthal of Connecticut told reporters after Tuesday's classified briefing.
Trump has not ruled ÔÇîout sending ÔüáU.S. ground troops into Iran. His Republicans, who have slim majorities in both houses of Congress, have almost unanimously backed his strategy on Iran, with only a handful expressing doubt about the war.
Blumenthal also cited the risk of Russian involvement putting American lives at risk. "Literally, Russia ÔÇőseems to be aiding ÔÇőour enemy actively and Ôüáintensively with intelligence and perhaps with other means and China also may be assisting Iran," Blumenthal said.
"So the American people deserve to know ÔÇőmuch more than this administration has told them about the cost ÔÇőof the war, Ôüáthe danger to our sons and daughters in uniform and the potential for further escalation and widening of this war," he said.
The lawmakers' comments came as they awaited a request the White ÔüáHouse ÔÇőis expected to make for more funding for the war. ÔÇőSeveral congressional aides have said they expect Trump to ask for $50 billion, although others have said that estimate seemed ÔÇőlow.

item.title: Boeing signs $289 million Israel contract for 5,000 smart bombs, source says
item.description:
March 10 (Reuters) - Boeing (BA.N) , opens new tab has signed a new $289 million contract ÔÇőwith Israel to deliver ÔÇőas many as 5,000 new Ôüáair-launched smart bombs, a ÔÇősource told Reuters on Tuesday.
The ÔÇőnew contract is not related to the ongoing U.S.-Israeli air strikes on ÔÇőIran, with deliveries not ÔÇőscheduled to start for 36 months, Bloomberg ÔÇîNews Ôüáreported earlier, citing a person familiar with the matter.
Boeing declined to comment when contacted ÔÇőby Reuters.
The ÔÇőcompany's ÔüáSmall Diameter Bomb is a guided munition ÔÇőthat can be launched ÔÇőby ÔüáIsraeli jets at targets more than 40 miles (64 kilometers) away.

item.title: Trading Day: Role reversal, as Wall Street lags
item.description:
ORLANDO, Florida, March 10 (Reuters) - Oil slumped more than 10% on Tuesday, the biggest one-day fall since 2022, on hopes that conflict in the Middle East will de-escalate. Asian and European stocks rose sharply, but in a rare instance since the war broke out, Wall Street lagged and U.S. stocks posted mild losses.
In my ÔÇőcolumn today I look at parallels between the stresses emerging in private credit markets today and U.S. subprime mortgages in 2007. Of course, ÔÇîthat doesn't mean a global crash will follow, but investors should be aware of the potential risks under the hood.
If you have more time to read, here are a few articles I recommend to help you make sense of what happened in markets today.
Today's Key Market Moves
Today's Talking Points
* Timing is everything
Like many things in life, timing is everything in investing. In trading, it's even more crucial. In that light, the ÔÇőlast 24 hours have been particularly challenging for oil traders, to put it mildly, with crude prices notching their biggest intraday swings on record.
With oil trading in ÔÇőa $36 intraday range, as it did on Monday, fortunes and careers can be made - or lost - in minutes. Leveraged positions will be particularly exposed, and it wouldn't be a total ÔÇîshock if Ôüáit soon emerges that some hedge funds suffered some big losses.
* Fake news?
Underlining how sensitive markets are right now to headlines, oil extended heavy losses on Tuesday after U.S. Energy Secretary Chris Wright posted on X that the U.S. navy escorted a tanker through Strait of Hormuz, indicating that supply constraints may be easing.
But the post was deleted minutes later, and oil rebounded around $10, a bounce boosted by a CBS report that U.S. intelligence detected signs Iran may be considering steps to deploy mines ÔÇőin the Strait of Hormuz. Headlines can ÔÇőalways move markets. But these are Ôüáextraordinary times.
* Trading places
China's powerful export machine is racing up through the gears. Exports in the first two months of this year soared 22%, more than three times the pace of growth in December and the Reuters poll forecast. The January-February ÔÇőtrade surplus was $213 billion.
With tariffs crimping shipments to the U.S., trade with the rest of the world is booming. Last ÔÇőyear's record $1.2 trillion trade Ôüásurplus, which revived complaints about China's FX regime, could be broken this year. Meanwhile, figures from Berlin on Tuesday showed that German exports in January shrank at their fastest rate since May 2024.
What could move markets tomorrow?
Want to receive Trading Day in your inbox every weekday morning? Sign up for my newsletter here .
Opinions expressed are those of the author. They do not reflect the views ÔÇőof Reuters News, which, under the Trust Principles , opens new tab , is committed to integrity, independence, and freedom from bias.

item.title: Brazil's Petrobras to auction 20 million liters of diesel amid shortage reports, sources say
item.description:
RIO DE JANEIRO, March 10 (Reuters) - Brazilian state-run oil company ÔÇőPetrobras (PETR3.SA) , opens new tab plans to ÔÇîhold an auction to sell 20 million liters of ÔÇődiesel in the ÔÇősouthern Rio Grande do Sul Ôüástate amid reports ÔÇőof shortages, sources familiar ÔÇőwith the issue told Reuters on Tuesday.
The auction will take ÔÇőplace on Wednesday, ÔÇőthey said.
A spike in diesel prices is ÔÇîemerging Ôüáas the first and most immediate threat to Brazil's farm sector ÔÇőfrom the ÔÇőU.S.-Israeli Ôüáattacks on Iran, raising costs for ÔÇőproducers harvesting a ÔÇőrecord Ôüásoybean crop and planting corn they cannot afford Ôüáto ÔÇődelay.

item.title: Oil dives, settles down 11% after Trump predicts Middle East de-escalation
item.description:
NEW YORK/LONDON, March 10 (Reuters) - Oil prices plunged by more than 11% on Tuesday, the steepest percentage drop of any session since ÔÇő2022, a day after U.S. President Donald Trump predicted a quick end to the war with Iran that has disrupted global crude flows .
Brent futures fell $11.16, or 11%, to settle at $87.80 a barrel. U.S. West Texas ÔÇîIntermediate (WTI) crude settled at $83.45 a barrel, down $11.32, or 11.9%. Both benchmarks logged the biggest single-day percentage loss since March 2022, after rocketing to four-year highs a day earlier.
Prices temporarily sank lower in midday trade, after U.S. Energy Secretary Chris Wright wrote on X that the American military had facilitated a shipment of oil out of the Strait of Hormuz.
"President Trump is maintaining stability of global energy during the military operations against Iran," Wright posted at 1:02 p.m. local time before the post appeared to be removed.
"The ÔÇőU.S. Navy successfully escorted an oil tanker through the Strait of Hormuz to ensure oil remains flowing to global markets," Wright said.
Trump's Republicans will soon be campaigning to retain control of U.S. Congress ÔÇőin November midterm elections, with many voters worried about rising energy prices.
ÔÇťThis is the market reacting to the possibility that the Strait of Hormuz could reopen," said ÔüáAndrew Lipow, founder of Lipow Oil Associates. "From the administrationÔÇÖs perspective, the move also carries clear optics: lower oil and gasoline prices help ease consumer pain."
On Monday, both crude benchmarks surged to a session high above $119 a barrel, ÔÇőtheir highest since June 2022, as supply cuts by Saudi Arabia and other producers stoked fears of major disruptions to global supplies.
Prices settled with more modest gains on Monday, then retreated in late trade and into Tuesday after Trump ÔÇőand Russian President Vladimir Putin had a call and shared proposals aimed at a quick settlement to the war, according to a Kremlin aide.
In addition, Trump said on Monday in a CBS News interview that he thought the war against Iran was "very complete" and Washington was "very far ahead" of his initial four- to five-week estimated time frame.
Israel's foreign minister said Israel is not seeking an endless war with Iran and will coordinate with the U.S. on when to end the fighting.
Brent oil prices are set to trade above $95 a ÔÇőbarrel over the next two months as the Iran war disrupts supplies, before falling to around $70 by the end of the year, the Energy Information Administration said in a monthly report.
Even ÔÇőif the war ends, oil supplies will not immediately rebound, said Simon Flowers, chairman and chief analyst at Wood Mackenzie.
"When the conflict ends, cranking up the supply chain won't be swift," Flowers said. "Product barrels in storage at refineries or in port ÔÇîmight be Ôüámoved on vessels quite quickly.
But if wells are shut-in for a prolonged period, restarting production to full output could take weeks or even longer."
In response to Trump, Iran's Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps said Tehran would not allow "one litre of oil" to be exported from the region if U.S. and Israeli attacks continued, state media reported on Tuesday.
Meanwhile, Trump was considering easing oil sanctions on Russia related to Moscow's war in Ukraine, and releasing emergency crude stockpiles to help curb spiking prices, according to multiple sources.
"Discussions around easing sanctions on Russian oil, comments from Donald Trump hinting that the conflict could eventually de-escalate, and the possibility of G7 (Group of Seven) countries tapping strategic oil reserves all pointed ÔÇőto the same message - that oil barrels will somehow ÔÇőcontinue to reach the market," Priyanka Sachdeva, a ÔüáPhillip Nova analyst, said in a note.
G7 energy ministers stopped short of agreeing on a release of strategic oil reserves on Tuesday and instead asked the International Energy Agency to assess the situation before acting.
The U.S. and Israel pounded Iran on Tuesday with what the Pentagon and Iranians on the ground said were the ÔÇőmost intense airstrikes of the war, even as global markets bet that Trump will seek to end the conflict soon.
Saudi Arabia's Aramco (2222.SE) , opens new tab , the world's top oil ÔÇőexporter, said there would be "catastrophic consequences" Ôüáfor global oil markets if the Iran war continues to disrupt shipping in the Strait of Hormuz.
Nearly 1.9 million barrels per day of crude refining capacity in the Gulf has been shut in due to the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran, consultancy IIR said.
"Policy measures may have limited impact on oil prices unless safe passage through the Strait of Hormuz is assured, given the potential losses of up to 12 million bpd over the next two weeks," JPMorgan said in a note.
In the Ôüálatest disruption to ÔÇőglobal supplies, Abu Dhabi state oil giant ADNOC has shut its Ruwais refinery, a source said on Tuesday, after a fire broke ÔÇőout at a facility within the complex following a drone strike.
Goldman Sachs said that because the situation remains fluid, it was not changing its oil price forecast for Brent at $66 per barrel in the fourth quarter and WTI at $62 per barrel.

item.title: Iran's new leader, still silent, was elevated by the Revolutionary Guards
item.description:
DUBAI, March 10 (Reuters) - Iran's Revolutionary Guards forced through the choice of Mojtaba Khamenei as the new supreme leader, seeing him as a more pliant version of his father who would back their hardline policies, bludgeoning aside the concerns of pragmatists, senior Iranian sources said.
Already very powerful, the Guards have gained ÔÇőyet greater sway since the war began and quickly overcame the misgivings of senior political and clerical figures whose opposition to the choice delayed the announcement by hours, the sources said.
Adding ÔÇîto the concerns of those who opposed Khamenei's installation as supreme leader, he had still issued no statement by Tuesday evening, nearly 48 hours after his selection during a war that has killed more than a thousand Iranians.
Khamenei's selection, engineered by the Guards, may add up to a more aggressive stance abroad and sterner internal repression, said the three senior Iranian sources, a reformist former official and another insider.
Two of them said they feared the Guards' domination of the system would further transform the Islamic Republic into a military state ÔÇőwith only a thin veneer of religious legitimacy,
undermining an already shrinking support base and allowing less room to address complex threats.
Though an influential backroom operator for decades spent ÔÇőrunning his father's office, Mojtaba Khamenei remains an obscure figure to many Iranians and may have been wounded in the U.S.-Israeli strikes that killed his father.
A state television anchor Ôüáappeared to confirm widespread rumours Khamenei was hurt, describing him as a "janbaz", or "wounded veteran" of the Ramadan War, as Iran calls the current conflict. Reuters has not been able to confirm his condition.
That - and security fears after ÔÇőhis father's assassination on February 28 - may explain his silence since the 88-member Assembly of Experts announced late on Sunday that they had elected him as the country's supreme leader.
Authority is most visibly held by the Guards and the ÔÇősupreme leader's office, known as the beyt, which operates a parallel system of influence across the bureaucracy.
Any doubts over who was really in charge evaporated on Saturday when President Masoud Pezeshkian, part of a triumvirate mandated to rule during the gap between leaders, was forced into a climbdown after apologising to Gulf states for attacks. Senior Guards were furious at his apology, sources told Reuters.
One of the three senior sources, who said the Guards were now running Iran, said the late Ayatollah Ali Khamenei had been able to rein in ÔÇőthe corps, balancing its views against those of political and clerical elites in the system.
But even assuming the new leader is well enough to take the helm, the Guards may now get the final say in major decisions ÔÇőin future, the source added.
Alex Vatanka, a senior fellow at the Middle East Institute in Washington, D.C. said: "Mojtaba owes his position to the Revolutionary Guards and as such he is not going to be as supreme as his father was."
The choice of leader constitutionally belongs to the Assembly of Experts, but in both elections of a new leader since the Islamic Revolution of 1979, it has been swayed by the advice of other power brokers.
When Ayatollah Ruhollah Khomeini died in 1989, the kingmaker was influential politician Ali Akbar Hashemi Rafsanjani, who told the assembly that Khomeini had whispered Khamenei's name to him on his deathbed.
This time, the kingmakers were the Guards and they were a lot blunter in their messages, all five of the sources said. The Guards used the argument that the war required a fast process and selecting a candidate who defied the United States.
Because their hall in the seminary city of Qom was bombed, ÔÇőthe Assembly of Experts had to gather in a different - ÔÇőso far undisclosed location - and some of the Ôüámembers could not be present or even informed of the vote, said one member, Ayatollah Mohsen Heydari, on state television.
The body reached its quorum of two-thirds, he said, without specifying how many had in fact taken part, with 85-90% of those present backing Mojtaba Khamenei.
It was not clear how many of those not present might have backed or ÔÇőopposed him but the figures showed less than the unanimous decision the Guards may have hoped for.
A group of ayatollahs had disliked the apparent ÔÇőhereditary succession and feared that the Ôüáchoice would alienate even many supporters of the ruling system, said two of the sources.
Behind the scenes, some clerics and members of the political establishment were trying to push for an alternative in numerous discussions over the past week, one of the sources said.
However, the reformist former official said the Guards had threatened critics of Khamenei's accession. The Islamic Republic insider said the Guards had contacted members of the assembly, prompting objections, but in the end they felt compelled to support him.
Khamenei's Ôüáappointment was originally ÔÇőintended to be announced on Sunday morning, but only came late in the evening as a result of the lingering opposition to ÔÇőhis choice, all five sources said.
As head of the beyt for many years under his father, Mojtaba Khamenei had built very close ties with the Guards, particularly the second-tier commanders who have replaced the top generals killed in the war, one of the officials said.
The upshot, said the ÔÇőreformist former official, will be a foreign and domestic policy moving in a more radical direction with the Guards finally having what they sought for years: full control.

item.title: North Korea supports Iran's choice of new supreme leader, KCNA says
item.description:
SEOUL, March 11 (Reuters) - North Korea ÔÇîsaid on Wednesday it supports the Iranian people's ÔÇőchoice of new ÔÇőSupreme Leader Mojtaba Khamenei , who Ôüáwas named on ÔÇőMonday to succeed his ÔÇőfather killed in the initial strikes against the ÔÇőcountry by the ÔÇőU.S. and Israel, state media ÔÇîsaid.
North ÔüáKorea's foreign ministry also said it strongly denounces acts of ÔÇőaggression by ÔÇőthe ÔüáU.S. and Israel of "illegal" attacks ÔÇőagainst Iran, which ÔÇőare Ôüádestroying peace and escalating instability worldwide, ÔüáKCNA ÔÇőstate news ÔÇőagency said.

item.title: Two more Iranian women's soccer players seek asylum in Australia, media reports
item.description:
SYDNEY, March 11 (Reuters) - Two more players from the Iranian women's soccer team sought asylum in Australia, local media reported, after five players were granted humanitarian ÔÇővisas on Tuesday over safety concerns on their return home for ÔÇînot singing the national anthem.
One player may have refused to board a flight at Sydney airport at the last minute on Tuesday night, national broadcaster ABC News said ÔÇőin a report, citing unidentified sources. The remaining players have ÔÇőleft Australia for Iran.
Home Affairs Minister Tony Burke did not immediately Ôüárespond to a request for comment.
Soccer fans and governing agencies started ÔÇőexpressing concerns about the welfare of the team after they were labelled "wartime traitors" ÔÇőon Iranian state television for refusing to sing their national anthem at an Asia Cup match.
The Iranian team's campaign in the Australia-hosted tournament started just as the U.S. ÔÇőand Israel launched air strikes on Iran , killing the Islamic Republic's Supreme Leader ÔÇőAyatollah Ali Khamenei. They were eliminated from the tournament on Sunday.
The Iranian players boarded ÔÇîa Ôüáflight to Sydney from the northeastern city of Gold Coast on Tuesday afternoon before flying back home.
A group of Iranians living in Australia gathered to protest against the Iranian government and surrounded the players' bus in Gold ÔÇőCoast when they ÔÇőleft the hotel Ôüáfor the airport. Many also turned up at the Sydney airport on Tuesday evening while they were being transferred ÔÇőto the international terminal, television footage showed.
The office of ÔÇőIran's general Ôüáprosecutor said on Tuesday the remaining members of the team were invited back to the country "with peace and confidence," Iranian media reported.
U.S. President Donald Trump praised ÔüáAustralian ÔÇőPrime Minister Anthony Albanese for allowing the women ÔÇőto stay, saying on social media the United States was ready to take the players ÔÇőif Australia did not.

item.title: At least six people die in coach fire in Switzerland, local police say
item.description:
GENEVA, March 10 (Reuters) - At least ÔÇősix people died in ÔÇîa coach fire on Tuesday in a small ÔÇőtown in western ÔÇőSwitzerland, prompting the police Ôüáto launch a ÔÇőcriminal investigation.
Fribourg city police ÔÇősaid the bus became engulfed in flames on a ÔÇőroad in Kerzers, ÔÇőa town in the canton of ÔÇîFribourg.
"At Ôüáthis stage, we have elements suggesting a deliberate act by ÔÇőa person ÔÇőwho Ôüáwas inside the bus," said ÔÇőFrederic Papaux, a ÔÇőspokesperson Ôüáfor Fribourg police.
Three injured people were taken Ôüáto ÔÇőhospital, the police ÔÇőadded.

item.title: Macron to host G7 leaders call on Iran crisis, energy prices
item.description:
PARIS, March 10 (Reuters) - French President Emmanuel Macron will convene a ÔÇőcall with leaders of the Group of ÔÇîSeven on Wednesday to discuss the Iran crisis and rising energy prices , the French president's office said.
The talks come ÔÇőas G7 governments weigh how to respond ÔÇőto a sharp rise in oil prices triggered Ôüáby the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran .
G7 energy ministers stopped ÔÇőshort of agreeing on a release of strategic ÔÇőoil reserves on Tuesday and instead asked the International Energy Agency to assess the situation before acting.
Benchmark oil prices ÔÇősurged to almost four-year highs on Monday but ÔÇőprices plummeted 11% on Tuesday after U.S. President Donald Trump ÔÇîpredicted Ôüáthe war in the Middle East could end soon.
U.S. officials are also weighing steps to keep oil flowing through the Strait of Hormuz , including providing ÔÇőnaval escorts ÔÇőfor commercial Ôüávessels and backstopping war risk insurance for tankers, as Washington seeks to ÔÇőreassure shippers and prevent further disruption ÔÇőto global Ôüáenergy supplies.
The G7 comprises the United States, Canada, Japan, Italy, Britain, Germany and France.
France is the Ôüácurrent ÔÇőG7 chair.

item.title: El Salvador likely committed crimes against humanity during state of emergency, jurists say
item.description:
March 10 (Reuters) - A group of international jurists on Tuesday accused Salvadoran authorities of committing crimes against ÔÇőhumanity in a report filed with the ÔÇîInter-American Commission on Human Rights.
The complaint alleges systematic acts of torture, murder, and forced disappearances under the country's ÔÇőcontroversial state of exception, which the ÔÇőgovernment of President Nayib Bukele imposed four years Ôüáago on March.

item.title: Russian consulate in Iran's Isfahan damaged in strikes, spokeswoman says
item.description:
March 10 (Reuters) - Russia's consulate in the Iranian city of Isfahan was damaged ÔÇőin shelling earlier this week, Russian ÔÇîForeign Ministry spokeswoman Maria Zakharova said on Tuesday.
An attack on a diplomatic representation ÔÇőwas a "blatant violation" of international ÔÇőconventions and all sides should observe Ôüáthe "inviolability of diplomatic sites", she ÔÇősaid.
"On March 8, in the Iranian ÔÇőcity of Isfahan, as a result of an attack on the governor's administration of ÔÇőthe province of the same ÔÇőname located nearby, the Russian consulate was damaged," ÔÇîZakharova Ôüásaid in a statement on the ministry's website.
"Windows were shattered in the office building and residential apartments, ÔÇőand several ÔÇőemployees were Ôüáthrown by the blast wave. Fortunately, there were ÔÇőno casualties or serious injuries."
President ÔÇőVladimir ÔüáPutin discussed the conflict with Iranian President Masoud Pezeshkian on Tuesday, the ÔüáKremlin ÔÇősaid. The Kremlin leader ÔÇőhas called for a halt to all hostilities.

item.title: Satellite firm extends Middle East image delay to prevent use by US adversaries
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 10 (Reuters) - California-based Planet Labs has expanded restrictions on accessing ÔÇîits imagery of the Middle East to prevent adversaries from using it to attack the U.S. and its allies, a sign of how the expansion of commercial space business can impact conflicts.
Planet, which operates a large fleet of Earth-imaging satellites and ÔÇősells frequently updated images to governments, companies and media, told customers on Monday that it ÔÇőwas extending restrictions to a period of 14 days from a delay of Ôüáfour days imposed last week.
The move was temporary and in ÔÇťan effort to limit any uncontrolled distribution ÔÇőof the images that might result in their unintentional access and use as tactical leverage by adversarial ÔÇőactors,ÔÇŁ a Planet spokesperson told Reuters in a statement.
ÔÇťThis conflict is dynamic and in many ways unique to others, and thus Planet is taking robust steps to help ensure our images do not contribute in any way to attacks ÔÇőon allied and NATO personnel and civilians," the spokesperson said.
Some space specialists say that Iran could ÔÇőbe accessing commercial imagery, including via other U.S. adversaries.
Militaries rely on space for everything from identifying targets, ÔÇîguiding weapons Ôüáand tracking missiles to communications. In a sign of spaceÔÇÖs central role in modern warfare, U.S. officials last week said their space forces were among ÔÇťthe first moversÔÇŁ in the operation against Iran.
A U.S. Space Command spokesperson declined to detail the capabilities it used. Space Command is responsible for helping with missile ÔÇőtracking, secure communications and ÔÇőusing Pentagon satellites as Ôüáoverwatch for U.S. and joint forces on the ground.
While high-quality satellite imagery used to be the preserve of advanced space powers, access to commercial satellite ÔÇőimagery has leveled the playing field, as Ukraine has experienced during its war ÔÇőwith Russia . Now, satellite Ôüáoperators are deploying AI to help speed up the ability to analyze imagery and identify areas of interest.
ÔÇťThis expert analysis used to be the preserve of high-end military analysts, not anymore,ÔÇŁ said Chris Moore, a Ôüádefense industry ÔÇőconsultant and retired air vice-marshal in the British military.
ÔÇťUltimately it ÔÇőwill create an all-seeing eye from space that will make the concealment of military forces and deception operations difficult to achieve.ÔÇŁ

item.title: US oil prices up nearly $3 as Middle East crisis constrains supply
item.description:
TOKYO, March 11 (Reuters) - U.S. West Texas Intermediate crude oil prices rose by $2.90 per barrel, or 3.5%, to $86.33 in early trade on Wednesday as supplies from the Gulf remain constrained amid the U.S. and Israeli war on Iran.
Brent futures start trading ÔÇőat 0100 GMT. WTI rebounded after both contracts plunged by more than 11% on ÔÇőTuesday, the steepest percentage drop since 2022, a day after U.S. President ÔüáDonald Trump predicted a quick end to the war.
The U.S. and Israel pounded Iran on Tuesday ÔÇőwith what the Pentagon and Iranians on the ground called the most intense airstrikes of the ÔÇőwar.
The U.S. military also "eliminated" 16 Iranian mine-laying vessels near the Strait of Hormuz on Tuesday, the U.S. Central Command said , as Trump warned any mines laid in the Strait by Iran must be removed immediately.
Trump has repeatedly said ÔÇőthe U.S. is prepared to escort tankers through the Strait of Hormuz when necessary. However, ÔÇősources told Reuters the U.S. Navy has refused requests from the shipping industry for military escorts as the risk of ÔÇîattacks is Ôüátoo high for now.
"We continue to expect crude oil to remain highly volatile, driven by headlines while trading within a wide range between $75ish and $105ish in the sessions ahead," Tony Sycamore, market analyst with IG in Sydney, said in a note.
On Monday, crude oil prices surged to a ÔÇősession high above $119 a ÔÇőbarrel, their highest since ÔüáJune 2022, and G7 officials have since gathered online to discuss a potential release of emergency oil stockpiles to soften the market blow.
French President Emmanuel ÔÇőMacron will host a video call with other G7 country leaders on ÔÇőWednesday to discuss Ôüáthe impact of the conflict in the Middle East on energy and measures to address the situation.
War in Iran is currently cutting Gulf oil and oil products supply to the market by some Ôüá15 ÔÇőmillion barrels per day which could raise crude prices to $150 per ÔÇőbarrel, according to research and consultancy firm Wood Mackenzie.
Reflecting higher demand, U.S. crude, gasoline and distillate stocks fell last week, ÔÇőmarket sources said, citing American Petroleum Institute figures on Tuesday.

item.title: Drone strike hits US diplomatic facility in Iraq, reports Washington Post
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 10 (Reuters) - A drone struck a major U.S. diplomatic facility in Iraq on Tuesday in suspected retaliation by pro-Tehran militias over the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran, ÔÇőthe Washington Post reported, citing an unidentified security official and an ÔÇîinternal U.S. State Department alert.
The strike hit the diplomatic support center, a logistical hub for U.S. diplomats near Baghdad airport and Iraqi military bases, the Post reported.
It was not clear from ÔÇőthe report whether there were any injuries. The White House, U.S. State ÔÇőDepartment and Pentagon did not immediately respond to requests for comment.
The ÔüáPost reported that six drones were launched toward the compound in Baghdad, one ÔÇőof which hit the U.S. facility while five were shot down. The security ÔÇőofficial, who the Post said spoke on the condition of anonymity to discuss a sensitive security situation, was not aware of any casualties.
The State Department issued an internal alert saying a drone ÔÇőhit near a guard tower and that individuals at the facility were ordered to "duck ÔÇőand cover," according to the Post.
The attack was likely carried out by the Islamic Resistance ÔÇîin Iraq, Ôüáan umbrella group of Iran-backed armed factions, the Post reported, citing the security official.
The U.S. and Israel began attacks on Iran on February 28. Iran has responded with its own strikes on Israel and on U.S. bases in Gulf countries. Iran's ÔÇőU.N. ambassador said the ÔÇőU.S.-Israeli strikes had Ôüákilled more than 1,300 civilians.
Israel says 11 civilians have been killed in Iranian attacks while the U.S. military says seven ÔÇőservice members have been killed. Earlier on Tuesday, Reuters reported that ÔÇőas many Ôüáas 150 U.S. troops have been wounded in the war.
President Donald Trump has said the strikes were aimed to eliminate what he called imminent threats from Iran, citing Ôüáits nuclear ÔÇőand ballistic missile programs, its support for Hamas ÔÇőand Hezbollah and its "menacing activities." Iran, which denies seeking a nuclear weapon, has called the attacks a ÔÇőviolation of its sovereignty.

item.title: Australia grants asylum to two more from Iranian women's soccer delegation
item.description:
SYDNEY, March 11 (Reuters) - Australia on Wednesday granted humanitarian visas ÔÇîto two more members of the Iranian women's soccer team who decided to stay in the country due to fears for their safety if they returned home.
Home Affairs Minister Tony Burke said a player and ÔÇőa support staff member accepted the government's offer, after five players from the team ÔÇőwere granted asylum a day earlier.
"I made them the same offer that I Ôüámade the five players the night before," Burke told reporters.
"If they wanted to receive a ÔÇőhumanitarian visa for Australia, which would have a pathway to a permanent visa, I had the ÔÇőpaperwork ready to execute that immediately."
Australian officials talked to most members of the team separately at Sydney airport and were informed of their options before they flew out of Australia.
"What we made sure of was that ÔÇőthere was no rushing, there was no pressure. Everything was about ensuring the dignity for ÔÇőthose individuals to make a choice," Burke said during a media briefing in Canberra.
Some discussed their options with ÔÇîfamily Ôüábut they declined the offer to remain in Australia. The team has reached Kuala Lumpur on their way to Iran.
Concerns about the players' safety grew after Iranian state television labelled the team "wartime traitors" for refusing to sing the national anthem during an Asia Cup match in Australia.
The ÔÇőIranian team's campaign in ÔÇőthe tournament started Ôüájust as the U.S. and Israel launched air strikes on Iran , killing the Islamic Republic's Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei. They were eliminated from the ÔÇőtournament on Sunday.
A group of Iranians living in Australia gathered to ÔÇőprotest against Ôüáthe Iranian government and surrounded the players' bus in Gold Coast when they left the hotel for the airport.
Many also turned up at the Sydney airport on Tuesday evening while they were being Ôüátransferred ÔÇőto the international terminal, television footage showed.
The office of ÔÇőIran's general prosecutor said on Tuesday the remaining members of the team were invited back to the country "with peace and ÔÇőconfidence," Iranian media reported.
(This story has been corrected to say 'soccer delegation,' not 'soccer players,' in the headline)

item.title: Trump nominee for State Department role drops out after his race comments jeopardized confirmation
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 10 (Reuters) - President Donald Trump's nominee for a senior ÔÇőState Department position withdrew from consideration on Tuesday after his controversial comments about Jewish ÔÇîpeople and diminishing white power stirred rare Republican opposition to the president's choice.
In a statement on X, Jeremy Carl, Trump's nominee for assistant secretary of state for international organizations, thanked Trump and Secretary of State Marco Rubio for their ÔÇősupport, but said their backing was not sufficient.
"We also needed the unanimous support of ÔÇőevery GOP Senator on the Committee on Foreign Relations, given the unanimous opposition Ôüáof Senate Democrats to my candidacy, and unfortunately, at this time this unanimous support was not ÔÇőforthcoming," Carl said, using the acronym for Grand Old Party, a nickname for the Republican Party.
The influential ÔÇőSenate committee typically votes on a nomination before sending it to the full Senate for a confirmation vote.
Carl's nomination was in doubt since Republican Senator John Curtis of Utah, a member of the committee, said after Carl's nomination hearing in ÔÇőFebruary he did not believe Carl was the right person to represent the country's best interests ÔÇőat international organizations.
Curtis cited Carl's "anti-Israel views" and "insensitive remarks" about Jewish people as disqualifying factors.
Failing to support a Trump nominee ÔÇîis Ôüáa rare rebuke by the Republican-majority Senate, which to date has backed the vast majority of the president's nominations and policies.
The White House and State Department did not immediately respond to requests for comment.
Lawmakers questioned Carl at the hearing about his prior comments about Jewish people and his belief in the "great ÔÇőreplacement theory," a discredited ÔÇőconspiracy theory associated with Ôüáwhite supremacy that leftist and Jewish elites are engineering the ethnic and cultural replacement of white people with non-white immigrants.
Carl said at the hearing that ÔÇőhe did not remember making some of the comments read aloud by ÔÇősenators and he Ôüáregretted some others. "I made some comments in interviews about minimizing the effects of the Holocaust that were absolutely wrong," he said.
When asked at the hearing whether there was an effort to replace white Americans under Ôüáway, ÔÇőCarl said he believed Democratic immigration policies have "certainly sent signs ÔÇőof that."
Carl is currently a senior fellow at the conservative Claremont Institute think tank. He was a deputy assistant secretary of ÔÇőthe interior during Trump's first term.

item.title: Exclusive: Chevron, Shell closing in on first big oil production deals in Venezuela since US captured Maduro,┬ásources say
item.description:
HOUSTON/CARACAS, March 10 (Reuters) - International oil majors Chevron (CVX.N) , opens new tab and Shell (SHEL.L) , opens new tab are closing in on the first big oil production deals with Venezuela since the U.S. capture of President Nicolas Maduro in January, five sources close to the negotiations told Reuters.
The deals would allow both companies ÔÇőto boost production in coveted oil regions in the South American country, the biggest steps to date toward what U.S. President Donald Trump has said would be a $100 billion effort to rebuild Venezuela's oil industry ÔÇîafter decades of mismanagement and underinvestment under Maduro and his predecessor Hugo Chavez.
Venezuela's National Assembly in late January approved a sweeping reform of the country's main oil law. It now grants foreign companies autonomy to operate, export and sell Venezuelan oil even when they are minority partners of state-owned oil company PDVSA.
Chevron and Venezuela's energy authorities have agreed on preliminary terms to expand Chevron's largest oil project, Petropiar, in the vast Orinoco Belt, two of the sources said.
Venezuela's oil ministry, PDVSA and Chevron did not reply to requests for comment.
The deal would give Chevron the rights to produce from the Ayacucho 8 area ÔÇőlocated south of the Petropiar project area, the two sources added, a large block with proven oil resources. This would allow Chevron to make a substantial increase in the extra-heavy oil it produces and exports.
Chevron aims to secure a reduced ÔÇőroyalty rate for the new area and other tax and trade incentives offered to companies under the new legislation to develop greenfield oil and gas areas, according to the two sources.
