The outbreak of the U.S.-Israel war against ÔÇőIran has led to flight cancellations, rescheduling and re-routing for airlines ÔÇîacross Ôüáthe globe, as most airspaces in the Middle East, including Qatar, remain shut over missile and drone concerns. It has also led to an energy crisis that has ÔÇőled to ÔÇőa spike in fuel Ôüáprices.
UAE airlines like Dubai's Emirates and Abu Dhabi's Etihad have resumed some flights since ÔÇőthe beginning of the crisis on February ÔÇő28 Ôüábut they are still operating below their capacity, with the attack on Wednesday marking a renewed hit to DXB, Ôüáthe world's ÔÇőbusiest airport for international passengers, ÔÇőwhich last year handled nearly 100 million passengers.

item.title: Opposition candidates in Congo Republic's presidential election
item.description:
DAKAR, March 11 (Reuters) - Denis Sassou Nguesso , the 82-year-old president of Republic of Congo, is seeking another five-year term in an election on March 15, aiming to extend his more than four decades in office in ÔÇőthe oil-rich Central African nation.
A former paratrooper, Sassou seized power in 1979 and has ÔÇîgoverned almost continuously since, except for a five-year hiatus in the 1990s.
He faces six opposition candidates in a race he is widely expected to win. The opposition remains fragmented, with established parties boycotting the vote and some prominent potential ÔÇőcandidates either in prison or in exile.
Following are details on his challengers: JOSEPH KIGNOUMBI KIA MBOUNGOU
Joseph ÔÇőKignoumbi Kia Mboungou, 73, a veteran lawmaker from the southwestern Lekoumou department and leader of the political Ôüáparty La Cha├«ne, is running for president for the fifth time since 2002. He earned 0.62% ÔÇőin the 2021 election.
His campaign has focused on what he describes as the need for political change ÔÇőand economic diversification, highlighting his goals of reducing Congo's heavy reliance on oil and tackling poverty.
Uphrem Dave Mafoula, 43, an economist, is making his second consecutive presidential bid after running in 2021, when he was the youngest candidate and ÔÇősecured 0.52% of the vote.
His platform centres on governance reforms, job creation and reducing territorial inequalities.
Melaine Destin Gavet Elengo, 35, is the youngest contender in the race and a first-time presidential candidate.
An engineer ÔÇîin Ôüáthe oil sector, he presents himself as part of a new generation seeking political renewal. His campaign emphasizes transparency, an independent justice system and inclusive development.
Mabio Mavoungou Zinga, 69, a retired customs inspector and former member of parliament, is running under the banner of the Alliance party, a coalition of opposition ÔÇőgroups.
He has pledged to ÔÇőtackle corruption and free Ôüájailed opposition leaders Jean-Marie Michel Mokoko and Andr├ę Okombi Salissa, who were arrested after the disputed 2016 election and later sentenced to 20 years in ÔÇőprison on charges of undermining state security. This is his first presidential race.
Vivien ÔüáRomain Manangou, 43, an independent candidate and public law university lecturer, is also making his first presidential bid.
His campaign has centered on institutional reforms, improving public finances and promoting national unity.
Anguios Nganguia Engambe, around 60, president Ôüáof the ÔÇőParty for Action of the Republic, is running for president ÔÇőfor the fourth consecutive time, having stood in 2009, 2016 and 2021, when he won just 0.18% of the vote.
His platform ÔÇőfocuses on bridging political divisions and increasing political participation.

item.title: South Korea Presidential aide says AI cooperation with UAE could accelerate after conflict
item.description: SEOUL, March 11 (Reuters) - South Korea's senior presidential secretary for AI, Ha Jung-woo, said on Wednesday that artificial intelligence cooperation with the ÔÇőUnited Arab Emirates could accelerate after the Middle East ÔÇîconflict ends, given the UAE's need to strengthen its defence capabilities.

item.title: Israel believes Iran's new leader was lightly wounded in attacks, senior official says
item.description:
JERUSALEM, March 11 (Reuters) - Israel's ÔÇőintelligence assessment is that Iran's ÔÇînew supreme leader, Mojtaba Khamenei , was lightly wounded in the Israeli-U.S. joint air war ÔÇőagainst Iran and that is ÔÇőwhy he has not been Ôüáseen in public, a senior ÔÇőIsraeli official told Reuters.
Iran's Revolutionary ÔÇőGuards forced through the choice of Mojtaba Khamenei as the new supreme leader, ÔÇőseeing him as a more ÔÇőpliant version of his father who would back ÔÇîtheir Ôüáhardline policies.
Khamenei's selection may add up to a more aggressive stance abroad and sterner internal ÔÇőrepression, said ÔÇőthree Ôüásenior Iranian sources, a reformist former official and ÔÇőanother insider.
Israeli Defense Minister ÔÇőIsrael ÔüáKatz said last week that any leader appointed by the current Iranian leadership Ôüáwould "be ÔÇőan unequivocal target ÔÇőfor elimination."

item.title: Romanian to review US request to use local air base for Iran operations
item.description:
BUCHAREST, March 11 (Reuters) - Romanian President Nicusor Dan has convened the EU ÔÇîand NATO nation's top defence council on Wednesday to discuss whether to allow U.S. aircraft access to its military bases for support linked ÔÇőto its Tehran operations , political sources said.
The council will meet ÔÇőfor the first time this year to discuss the Ôüásecurity fallout from the conflict in the Middle East, ÔÇőits impact on Romania's energy market and "the temporary deployment of military ÔÇőcapability on Romanian territory."
That deployment, political sources said without elaborating, referred to a U.S. request to use the Mihail Kogalniceanu air base.
While some EU ÔÇőcountries--such as France, Greece and Italy-- have sent warships to Cyprus ÔÇőafter Iranian-made drones struck a British base on the island, others allow use ÔÇîof Ôüátheir military bases.
Most EU top officials have condemned Iranian strikes in the region and urged an end and diplomatic solution to the conflict.
The U.S. withdrew about ÔÇő1,000 troops from ÔÇőRomania's Mihail ÔüáKogalniceanu air base last year, as the U.S. focused on its own borders and the Indo-Pacific ÔÇőregion. Another 1,000 U.S. troops remain in ÔÇőRomania.
The permanent Ôüáallied presence in Romania stands at around 3,500 NATO troops, including U.S. soldiers.
Romania shares a 650 km (400 mile) land border with ÔüáUkraine, ÔÇőover which Russian drones have flown ÔÇőtowards Kyiv, while mines in the Black Sea from the conflict impact key ÔÇőtrade and energy routes.

item.title: Russia says it shot down two British-supplied Storm Shadow missiles
item.description:
MOSCOW, March 11 (Reuters) - Russian Defence Ministry said on ÔÇőWednesday that its forces ÔÇîshot down two British-supplied Storm Shadow missiles over ÔÇőthe past 24 ÔÇőhours, according to the ÔüáRIA news agency.
Earlier ÔÇőUkraine said it had ÔÇőused Storm Shadow missiles to strike a key plant ÔÇőproducing missile components ÔÇőin the Russian city of Bryansk ÔÇîon ÔüáTuesday.
The governor of Bryansk region, Alexander Bogomaz, said that six ÔÇőcivilians were ÔÇőkilled Ôüáand 42 injured in the ÔÇőattack.
Separately, the Russian ÔÇődefence Ôüáministry said its forces had captured the Ôüásettlement ÔÇőof Chervona Zoria ÔÇőin Ukraine's Sumy region.

item.title: Gasunie urges Dutch government to build strategic gas reserve
item.description:
AMSTERDAM, March 11 (Reuters) - Dutch natural gas grid operator Gasunie on Wednesday urged ÔÇőthe government to build a strategic gas ÔÇîreserve to ensure the Netherlands can withstand a prolonged disruption of supplies.
Gasunie said the Netherlands was protected against short-term ÔÇődisruptions due to cold weather, but not for ÔÇőlong-term problems caused by, for instance, geopolitical tensions Ôüáor sabotage.
"The current geopolitical climate demands realism and ÔÇőpreparations for the unexpected to happen on the ÔÇőlonger term," Gasunie director Hans Coenen said.
The gas company called for research on the use of the so-called cushion gas that ÔÇőis available in the country's large gas storages ÔÇőas a reserve.
Cushion gas is permanently stored in natural gas ÔÇîstorages Ôüáto maintain adequate pressure.
The Netherlands was once one of Europe's major gas suppliers, but has turned into an importer after it shut down the large Groningen ÔÇőfield due to ÔÇőthe earthquakes Ôüáthat gas extraction had caused there.
Most of the gas used in the Netherlands ÔÇőnow comes from Norway, but an ÔÇőincreasing part Ôüáis supplied by the U.S. in the form of liquefied natural gas.
The country still has large underground Ôüágas ÔÇőstorages, of which about 85% of capacity ÔÇőwould be needed to meet yearly gas demand under regular circumstances, ÔÇőGasunie said.

item.title: Dutch court scraps cap on number of flights at Amsterdam airport
item.description:
AMSTERDAM, March 11 (Reuters) - The Dutch Supreme ÔÇőCourt on ÔÇîWednesday scrapped a government decision ÔÇőto limit ÔÇőthe number of flights Ôüáat Amsterdam's ÔÇőSchiphol airport, ÔÇőone of Europe busiest hubs.
The court ÔÇősaid the ÔÇő2024 decision to limit ÔÇîthe Ôüánumber of flights to 478,000 per ÔÇőyear, ÔÇőmade Ôüáin an effort ÔÇőto cut ÔÇőnoise Ôüápollution, had not been Ôüáproperly ÔÇőmotivated.

item.title: South Korea and Ghana to expand cooperation on climate, tech and maritime security
item.description: SEOUL, March 11 (Reuters) - South Korean President Lee Jae Myung held summit talks on ÔÇőWednesday with Ghanaian President John ÔÇîMahama, agreeing to deepen cooperation in climate, digital innovation and maritime security.

item.title: Exclusive: Foreign hacker in 2023 compromised Epstein files held by FBI
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 11 (Reuters) - A foreign hacker compromised files relating to the FBIÔÇÖs investigation of the late sex offender Jeffrey Epstein during a break-in at the bureauÔÇÖs New York Field Office three years ago, according to ÔÇőa source familiar with the matter and recently published Justice Department documents reviewed by Reuters.
The details of who accessed a server at the FBIÔÇÖs New York Field Office, ÔÇîincluding the allegation that a foreign hacker was involved, are being reported here for the first time.
In a statement, the FBI said what it described as a "cyber incident" was "an isolated one."
"The FBI restricted access to the malicious actor and rectified the network. The investigation remains ongoing, so we do not have further comments to provide at this time."
Although the source said the intrusion appeared to have been carried out by a cybercriminal rather than a foreign government, ÔÇőthe incident underscores the files' potential intelligence value, one academic said. The legally mandated publication of U.S. Justice Department documents has exposed the dead financier's ties to prominent people in politics, finance, academia and business, ÔÇőtriggering investigations in numerous countries around the world.
ÔÇťWho wouldnÔÇÖt be going after the Epstein files if youÔÇÖre the Russians or somebody interested in kompromat?ÔÇŁ said ÔüáJon Lindsay, who researches the role of emerging technology in global security at the Georgia Institute of Technology. ÔÇťIf foreign intelligence agencies are not thinking seriously about the Epstein files as a target, then ÔÇőI would be shocked.ÔÇŁ
The breach was reported contemporaneously by CNN and Reuters on February 17; the connection to Epstein materials was made by the French magazine Marianne.
Epstein, a longtime associate of President Donald Trump, ÔÇőpleaded guilty in 2008 to prostitution charges, including soliciting an underage girl. He was found hanged in his jail cell in 2019, in what was ruled a suicide, after being arrested again on federal charges of sex trafficking of minors.
The hack occurred after a server at the Child Exploitation Forensic Lab in the FBIÔÇÖs New York Field Office was inadvertently left vulnerable by Special Agent Aaron Spivack, who was trying to navigate the bureauÔÇÖs complex ÔÇőprocedures for handling digital evidence, according to the source and the documents.
A timeline written by Spivack and included in the large cache of Epstein documents released earlier this year said the break-in happened ÔÇőon February 12, 2023. It was discovered the following day when Spivack turned on his computer and discovered a text file warning him that his network had been compromised, according to that document.
Further investigation turned up traces ÔÇîof unusual activity Ôüáon the server, the document said, adding that the activity "included combing through certain files pertaining to the Epstein investigation.ÔÇŁ
The timeline does not say which specific files were accessed, whether the hacker downloaded the data, or who the hacker was. Reuters could not establish what, if any, overlap the affected data had with the Epstein documents published earlier this year or the files that remain under wraps.
Spivack, whose name appears elsewhere , opens new tab in the documents , opens new tab in connection with the Epstein investigation, did not return repeated messages seeking comment. Reuters was unable to reach the man identified in the documents as SpivackÔÇÖs lawyer, Richard J. Roberson, Jr. Seven FBI ÔÇőagents identified in the documents as being involved ÔÇőin the investigation into the incident did Ôüánot return messages.
In his statement to FBI investigators examining whether he was responsible for the breach, Spivack said he was being made "a scapegoat for the intrusion" and that conflicting bureau policies and faulty guidance around information technology were to blame. Reuters could not establish the result ÔÇőof the bureau's internal investigation.
The person familiar with the breach said the intrusion was carried out by a foreign hacker who did not appear ÔÇőto realize they had penetrated Ôüáa law enforcement server. The hacker expressed disgust at the presence of child abuse images on the device and left a message threatening to turn its owner over to the FBI, the person said.
The source said bureau officials defused the situation by convincing the hacker that they actually were the FBI, in part by having the hacker join a video chat where they flashed their law enforcement credentials in front Ôüáof a web ÔÇőcamera.
Reuters could not determine - and the source said they did not know - who the hacker was, what country they ÔÇőwere operating from, what they did with the material accessed, or whether any effort was made to identify or punish them for breaking into the FBIÔÇÖs server.
Many of the Justice Department's documents have been heavily redacted and others have been kept ÔÇősecret altogether despite a law mandating their full release last year . The Trump administration says it is withholding material that could compromise victimsÔÇÖ identities or jeopardize ongoing investigations.

item.title: As Iran war rages, Trump pivots to cost of living in Kentucky trip
item.description:
HEBRON, Kentucky, March 11 (Reuters) - Twelve days into the Iran war, U.S. President Donald Trump is refocusing on domestic issues, traveling ÔÇőto Kentucky on Wednesday to promote his economic plans as Republicans urge him to address rising concern over the ÔÇîcost of living.
The president's campaign trip is his first since the start of the U.S.-Israeli military operation in Iran . It offers Trump a chance to sharpen his economic message ahead of the November midterm elections, when Republicans will be defending narrow majorities in both chambers of the U.S. Congress.
Trump is due to ÔÇődeliver remarks at two events in northern Kentucky and nearby Cincinnati, Ohio. White House press secretary Karoline Leavitt could not ÔÇősay why Trump had chosen to go to those places in particular, but the Kentucky district Ôüáis also home to Republican U.S. Representative Thomas Massie, a Trump critic who has frequently broken with his party.
Massie led the high-profile ÔÇőpush for more transparency from Trump's Justice Department's handling of files connected to the late convicted sex offender Jeffrey Epstein.
Trump has endorsed the ÔÇőRepublican candidate who will face off against Massie in the party's primary in May.
In his public remarks, Trump is expected to focus on his efforts to lower some prescription drug costs, a key selling point for Republicans trying to address voters' economic concerns.
It is the latest in a series of speeches Trump ÔÇőhas delivered around the country to sell the American public on his economic policies, but they have often been overshadowed by his ÔÇőpolicies abroad, most recently the war in Iran, which has caused gas prices to soar.
Gas prices are up 61 cents on average in both Kentucky ÔÇîand nationwide Ôüácompared to last month, according to travel organization AAA.
"Short term oil prices, which will drop rapidly when the destruction of the Iran nuclear threat is over, is a very small price to pay," Trump posted earlier this week, "ONLY FOOLS WOULD THINK DIFFERENTLY!"
Bill Kunkel, a 67-year-old retired welder in Union, Kentucky, told Reuters he is concerned about how the Iran war and Trump's tariff program will continue to affect an ÔÇőarea known for logistics and ÔÇőmanufacturing.
"I voted for Trump because Ôüáhe was going to get us out of all of these wars. That is one of the reasons why I agree with Massie - it can't just be a forever war," Kunkel added, referring to ÔÇőMassie's criticism of Trump's decision to go to war against Iran.
Massie said in an interview that ÔÇőhe welcomes Trump Ôüáto his district as they share a lot of the same supporters.
ÔÇťIÔÇÖve taken care to never insult the president, to never call him names. If I have a policy disagreement with him, I articulate it and keep it to that," he said.
Trump has endorsed Ed Gallrein, a Ôüáformer Navy ÔÇőSEAL officer and farmer Trump calls "high-quality," to take on Massie, a man Trump ÔÇőcalls "unreliable." GallreinÔÇÖs campaign said he will attend the presidentÔÇÖs event.
"There is growing dissatisfaction with where Massie is, and part of it is not supporting the president," said ÔÇőLarry Mazzuckelli, a retired federal government employee and political commentator from Union, Kentucky.

item.title: Kremlin says Britain was involved in Ukraine's missile strike on Russia
item.description:
MOSCOW, March 11 (Reuters) - The Kremlin on Wednesday accused British specialists of involvement in a deadly ÔÇőUkrainian strike on the Russian city of Bryansk that ÔÇîused British Storm Shadow missiles and said it would take Britain's role "into account".
The Bryansk regional governor said Tuesday's strike killed at least six civilians and injured ÔÇő37 in what he called a "terrorist missile attack", without saying ÔÇőwhat was hit. Ukraine said it had struck a Ôüákey plant producing missile components. Moscow accused it of deliberately targeting ÔÇőcivilians.
Asked by Reuters if there would be a military response to ÔÇőthe use of British missiles against sovereign Russian territory, Kremlin spokesman Dmitry Peskov said Russia would take into account the British involvement.
"It is obvious that the launch ÔÇőof these missiles was impossible without British specialists," Peskov told ÔÇőreporters. "We are aware of this, we know it well, and we naturally take it ÔÇîinto Ôüáaccount."
"In order to prevent such barbaric actions by the Kyiv regime from continuing, the special military operation is being conducted," he said, adding that one of the goals of the operation was to demilitarise ÔÇőUkraine.
There was no ÔÇőimmediate comment Ôüáfrom London, though Russia has repeatedly said that Ukraine needs Western expertise and Western-supplied targeting data and ÔÇősatellite imagery to fire advanced missiles deep into Russia.
Ukrainian ÔÇőPresident ÔüáVolodymyr Zelenskiy said that Kyiv had struck one of the most important military factories in Bryansk that produced electronics for Russian missiles.
Russian Foreign Ministry Ôüáspokeswoman ÔÇőMaria Zakharova said the strike was premeditated ÔÇőand directed against civilians, and challenged the U.N. to assess what had occurred.

item.title: China 'does not agree' with attacks on Gulf countries, says foreign ministry
item.description: BEIJING, March 11 (Reuters) - China "does ÔÇőnot agree" ÔÇîwith attacks on ÔÇőGulf states ÔÇőand condemns all Ôüáindiscriminate ÔÇőattacks on ÔÇőcivilians and non-military targets, ÔÇősaid ÔÇőGuo Jiakun, spokesperson ÔÇîat Ôüáthe Chinese foreign ministry, at ÔÇőa ÔÇőregular Ôüápress conference ÔÇőon ÔÇőWednesday.

item.title: Thailand says 3 crew missing after ship hit by projectiles in Strait of Hormuz
item.description:
BANGKOK, March 11 (Reuters) - Twenty crew have been rescued ÔÇőfrom a Thai ÔÇîship hit by unknown projectiles in the ÔÇőStrait of Hormuz ÔÇőbut three crew remain Ôüámissing, Thailand's Transport ÔÇőMinistry said on Wednesday.
The ÔÇőministry said the crew had abandoned the vessel ÔÇőin a lifeboat ÔÇőand were rescued by the ÔÇîOmani Ôüánavy. It said an explosion occurred in the stern of ÔÇőthe ÔÇőship Ôüáand caused a fire in the ÔÇőengine compartment, ÔÇőwhere Ôüáthe remaining three crew members had been Ôüáworking.

item.title: Pope Leo laments death of civilians, children in Iran war
item.description:
VATICAN CITY, March 11 (Reuters) - Pope Leo on Wednesday ÔÇőlamented the death ÔÇîof numerous civilians in the ongoing Iran war ÔÇőand also expressed ÔÇőcloseness to people in ÔüáLebanon, saying the ÔÇőcountry, targeted by Israeli ÔÇőstrikes, was going through a "great trial."
Leo, the first ÔÇőU.S. pope, called ÔÇőon pilgrims in his weekly ÔÇîaudience Ôüáin St. Peter's Square to pray for peace.
"Let us continue ÔÇőto pray ÔÇőfor Ôüápeace in Iran, and throughout ÔÇőthe Middle East, ÔÇőespecially Ôüáfor the many civilian victims, including many Ôüáinnocent ÔÇőchildren," said ÔÇőthe pontiff.

item.title: Thailand's Phuket airport closes runway after Air India Express plane malfunction
item.description:
BANGKOK, March 11 (Reuters) - Thailand's Phuket airport said on Wednesday it had temporarily closed its runway to remove a disabled Air India Express plane ÔÇőafter it experienced a malfunction upon landing.
Air India Express Flight AXB938 ÔÇîarriving from Hyderabad suffered damage to its landing gear as it hit the runway, Phuket airport said in a statement.
The plane "experienced an issue with the nose wheel at ÔÇőPhuket Airport," a spokesperson for Air India Express said, adding: "The ÔÇőcrew followed all standard protocols, and guests were deplaned.
There were no Ôüácasualties and the runway was expected to reopen at 6 p.m. ÔÇőlocal time (1100 GMT), the airport said.
An image posted on X by Thai state ÔÇőbroadcaster MCOT showed part of the aircraft landing gear, with a wheel missing, in direct contact with the runway, with a long, swerving skid mark on the tarmac.
Air India Express, the ÔÇőbudget arm of Air India, has a fleet of over 100 Boeing ÔÇőand Airbus aircraft.
India's aviation watchdog reprimanded Air India Express in March last year for ÔÇînot Ôüáchanging the engine parts of an Airbus A320, as directed by the European Union's aviation safety agency, Reuters has reported . It was also criticised for falsifying records to show compliance.
Air India Express reported 95 technical defects from January to November last ÔÇőyear, India's civil ÔÇőaviation ministry said Ôüáin December, the second highest among India's airlines over the period. The ministry did not provide details on the ÔÇőnature of the faults.
Parent Air India, a full-service carrier, ÔÇőhas come Ôüáunder intense scrutiny in the last few months after a series of safety lapses and a deadly crash in in Ahmedabad last June that killed Ôüá260 ÔÇőpeople.
Phuket is one of Thailand's busiest gateways, with ÔÇőhundreds of flights daily, including from the Middle East, Europe and Asia, as well as ÔÇődomestic routes.

item.title: Kremlin says it is in constant touch with Iranian leaders
item.description:
MOSCOW, March 11 (Reuters) - Russia is constantly in touch ÔÇőwith the Iranian leadership and ÔÇîwilling to contribute to efforts to stabilise the region, ÔÇőthe Kremlin said on ÔÇőWednesday.
"Here I can only say Ôüáthat we are in ÔÇőconstant contact with the Iranian ÔÇőside and with the Iranian leadership," Kremlin spokesman Dmitry Peskov ÔÇőtold reporters.
"As President (Vladimir) Putin ÔÇőhas said, Russia is always ready ÔÇîto Ôüádo what it can to restore peace and stability in the region."
Putin and ÔÇőForeign Minister ÔÇőSergei ÔüáLavrov have spoken with their Iranian counterparts ÔÇőin recent days, ÔÇőwhile ÔüáPutin also held a phone call with U.S. President ÔüáDonald ÔÇőTrump on Monday.

item.title: Hungary's opposition Tisza party maintains lead as election nears, poll shows
item.description:
BUDAPEST, March 11 (Reuters) - The main Hungarian opposition party Tisza's lead over long-time Prime Minister Viktor Orban's Fidesz shrank slightly in a poll published on ÔÇőWednesday, as campaigning enters its final month before an April ÔÇî12 election.
Nationalist Orban faces the biggest challenge to his rule in 16 years, although the outcome of the vote - coming against the backdrop of the Ukraine-Russia war ÔÇőand building economic challenges - remains highly uncertain, with opinion polls showing ÔÇőthat many voters are still undecided.
A poll by 21 Research ÔüáCentre (21 Kutatokozpont), conducted between March 2 and 6 and released on ÔÇőWednesday, showed Tisza leads by 14 percentage points ahead of Fidesz among ÔÇődecided voters, down from a 16-point lead in the agency's last poll in January.
Centre-right Tisza, led by former government insider Peter Magyar , had the support of 53% of decided ÔÇővoters, unchanged from January, according to 21 Research Centre, while 39% backed ÔÇőFidesz, up from 37% in the previous poll.
The poll, published by news site 24.hu, ÔÇîshowed Ôüá38% support for Tisza among all voters, with Fidesz backed by 30%.
Based on the poll, Tisza could win 115 seats in Hungary's 199-seat parliament, and Fidesz could expect 78 seats. The far-right Our Homeland (Mi Hazank) party ÔÇőwould be the ÔÇőonly other party Ôüácrossing the 5% threshold to win seats.
Magyar has said his party would curb corruption, unlock billions of euros ÔÇőin frozen European Union funds to boost the economy, ÔÇőand firmly Ôüáanchor Hungary in the EU and NATO.
Fidesz has pointed to other surveys that still show it on course to victory, though opponents - who criticise Orban Ôüáfor ÔÇőharming the rule of law and keeping ÔÇőwarm ties with Russia - say these have mainly been conducted by institutes with financial or personal ÔÇőties to the ruling party.

item.title: Sweden's Stegra seeks to raise over $2.3 billion, daily DI reports
item.description:
STOCKHOLM, March 11 (Reuters) - Swedish startup Stegra must raise more than 2 billion euros ($2.3 billion) to ÔÇőcomplete construction of its green steel plant, ÔÇîmore than double its previous estimate, business daily Dagens Industri reported on Wednesday, citing unnamed sources.
The ÔÇőcompany has appointed a new CFO and ÔÇőshaken up its board , as it scrambles to Ôüáraise more cash to finish its hydrogen-based ÔÇősteel plant in northern Sweden.
($1 = 0.8602 euros)

item.title: US ignites Iran war, but Gulf Arab states pay the price, Gulf sources say
item.description:
DUBAI, March 11 (Reuters) - The U.S. may have pulled the trigger on the Iran war , but it is the oil-producing Gulf that will pay the price, Gulf sources and analysts say, signalling unease in ties between a region under Iranian attack and the superpower it relies on for protection.
Behind the scenes, resentment is mounting ÔÇőin Gulf Arab capitals at being drawn into a war they neither initiated nor endorsed but are now paying for economically and militarily, with airports, hotels, ports and military and oil installations hit by Iranian strikes, said three ÔÇîregional sources, who declined to be identified as they were not authorised to speak publicly.
"It is not our war. We did not want this conflict, yet we are paying the price in our security and our economy," Ebtesam Al-Ketbi, President of the Emirates Policy Center, told Reuters.
That doesn't mean Iran is "innocent", she said. Gulf governments had assured Tehran they would not allow their territories or airspace to be used by Washington in the war. Yet Iran has unleashed waves of drone and missile strikes across the region despite those assurances, denting business confidence in the process.
While ÔÇődisquiet about U.S. President Donald Trump is growing over a conflict many believe he launched without consultation, some regional sources argue that having started the war, Washington should now see it through to eliminate what they see as a persistent ÔÇőIranian threat on their doorstep.
ÔÇťIf America leaves the war now without achieving victory, it will be like abandoning an injured lion,ÔÇŁ Ketbi said. ÔÇťIran will remain a threat to the region, capable of Ôüástriking again. And if the regime collapses, leaving a power vacuum, neighbouring states will suffer the consequences.ÔÇŁ
Asked for comment, the White House said U.S. and Israeli strikes had reduced IranÔÇÖs retaliatory missile attacks by 90 percent, "crushing their ability to shoot these weapons or ÔÇőproduce more".
White House spokeswoman Anna Kelly added that Trump was in close contact with Middle East partners and that IranÔÇÖs attacks on its neighbors underscore why the threat had to be eliminated.
There was no immediate response from Gulf states to requests for comment.
Iran's Supreme ÔÇőLeader, Ayatollah Ali Khamenei, was killed on the first day of the war. Tehran responded by hitting Israel and Gulf states hosting U.S. military installations, effectively halting oil and gas shipments through the Strait of Hormuz -- a conduit for roughly a fifth of the worldÔÇÖs petroleum and LNG.
Across the region, airspace closures have resulted in airlines cancelling some 40,000 flights, the largest disruption to global air travel since the COVID-19 pandemic. Gulf tourism meanwhile is also taking a hit , putting at risk the region's carefully crafted image as a safe and high-end vacation hotspot.
Amid the turmoil, Gulf states have ÔÇősought to project calm and resolve. The president of the United Arab Emirates, Sheikh Mohammed bin Zayed Al Nahyan, said on Friday his nation was in a time of war but was well and told his enemies it was no easy prey , in his ÔÇőfirst public comments since Iran fired missiles at the UAE.
At the same time, analysts say the war has left Gulf states reassessing both their security dependence on Washington and the prospect of eventually engaging Tehran on new regional security arrangements -- even as trust in Iran has collapsed.
For decades, ÔÇîrelations between Washington Ôüáand the Gulf states rested on an implicit trade-off: Gulf energy and capital -- including hundreds of billions of dollars spent on U.S. arms, advanced technology and goods and services -- in return for U.S. protection, said Fawaz Gerges of the London School of Economics.
Gerges said the war had shaken those assumptions. Now, he said, Gulf states would accelerate efforts to diversify their foreign and security partnerships, realising "they cannot really rely on the United States to protect their energy, oil, gas, their people and their sovereignty.ÔÇŁ
The Gulf's latest misgivings about Washington echo disquiet felt in Saudi Arabia at the U.S. failure to retaliate against Iran after drone and missile attack in 2019 on the kingdom's Abqaiq and Khurais oil facilities. Washington and Riyadh blamed the attacks on Tehran, which denied responsibility.
Abdulaziz Sager, Chairman of the Saudi-based ÔÇőGulf Research Center, said Washington had failed to prepare safeguards ÔÇőfor its regional allies or ensure oil and gas flows Ôüáduring wartime, saying the cost on the Gulf states' economies is "horrendous".
Sager said the war had demonstrated the limits of relying solely on external security guarantees, particularly from the United States, adding that Gulf countries need to strengthen their own defense capabilities and prepare for future crises.
"External powers also tend to make decisions based on their own strategic interests rather than ÔÇőthose of the Gulf. As a result, Gulf states will likely pursue a more cautious and balanced approach in their relations with both Iran and major international partners," added Sager.
In a ÔÇőrare public rebuke, prominent Emirati businessman ÔüáKhalaf Al Habtoor questioned the objectives of the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran.
ÔÇťIf the strikes were aimed at containing Iran, did they take into account the regional consequences -- or was the cost of dragging the Gulf into a conflict it was not party to simply ignored?ÔÇŁ said Al Habtoor, founder of the Al Habtoor Group.
Saudi Aramco CEO Amin Nasser warned on Tuesday that continued disruption to shipping through Hormuz would have ÔÇťcatastrophic consequencesÔÇŁ for oil markets .
IranÔÇÖs Revolutionary Guards, however, said they would not allow ÔÇťone litre of oilÔÇŁ to be shipped from the Middle East if U.S. Ôüáand Israeli attacks ÔÇőcontinued. Trump has warned Washington would strike Iran harder if it blocked oil exports.
Sources close to Gulf Arab government circles say frustration with Trump privately runs ÔÇődeep across the region. Many believe he dragged the Gulf into a war shaped heavily by Israel, without sharing a plan and acting hastily and without fully weighing the political and economic fallout for allies.
One Gulf source familiar with U.S. policymaking said key decisions were crafted by a small inner circle around Trump operating ÔÇőlargely outside traditional U.S. policy channels.
ÔÇťThey are businessmen and dealmakers, not career policymakers,ÔÇŁ the source said, adding this approach left Gulf partners exposed to the consequences of this circle's decisions.

item.title: Meloni bets on a lottery to curb Italy's judicial clans
item.description:
ROME, March 11 (Reuters) - Italy has tried random selection before.
Ancient Romans used to cast lots to decide who governed in distant provinces while Renaissance Florence pulled names from bags to avoid powerful families gaining a stranglehold on power.
Now Prime Minister Giorgia Meloni is looking to revive the centuries-old ÔÇőtradition of "sortition" as a way to stop what she says are political factions lording it over Italy's long-troubled judiciary.
The plan to select magistrate members of judicial governing councils by lottery ÔÇîlies at the heart of a reform being put to referendum on March 22-23, pitting Meloni's conservative government against most of the nation's judges and prosecutors.
Recent opinion polls show the "no" camp, backed by the main opposition centre-left parties, has nudged ahead, threatening one of Meloni's signature initiatives which was championed by her predecessor Silvio Berlusconi before his death in 2023.
Berlusconi, who faced dozens of trials largely tied to his media empire after entering politics, accused the judiciary of being in the ÔÇőhands of the left. Magistrates denied this, but Meloni vowed to take up his crusade after she won power in 2022.
"I think change is needed because justice is one of the ÔÇőthree fundamental powers that keep Italy afloat, but it's also the one that we haven't managed to substantially reform since the 1980s," Meloni told RTL Ôüáradio last week.
"If we don't succeed this time, we won't have another chance," she added, saying future governments would be unlikely to take on this sort of constitutional reform that almost always requires ÔÇőa referendum that is hard to win in a divided country.
The referendum campaign is unfolding amid growing distrust between the judiciary and the ruling coalition, with "no" supporters arguing that Meloni's plan would undermine the justice ÔÇősystem and boost political interference.
They seized on remarks by Giusi Bartolozzi, the justice ministry's chief of staff, who urged voters in a TV interview this week to back the reform, saying it would allow Italians to "get rid of a judiciary that acts like a firing squad."
The government's reform has three main pillars.
First, it would separate the career paths of judges and public prosecutors, who currently enter through the same exam, can swap ÔÇőjob roles and are governed by a single council (the CSM), which oversees appointments and promotions.
Second, it would split the CSM into two councils - one for judges and one for prosecutors. Third, it would create ÔÇőa new disciplinary court to handle misconduct cases.
Under the proposal for the new-look CSMs, two thirds of seats would be reserved for working judges or prosecutors, and one third for lay members, such as law professors or ÔÇîlawyers, whose Ôüánames would be put forward by parliament.
All CSM members will ultimately be drawn by lot.
Meloni says this will prevent the sort of wheeler-dealing between various internal factions that recent scandals have revealed, and create a system in which council members do not have to "say thank you to anyone" after an election.
Critics counter that in institutions designed to safeguard independence, legitimacy comes from having leaders chosen on purpose, not picked at random.
"It is one thing to punish individuals who make mistakes; it is another to humiliate the entire judiciary by saying that you are not capable of electing your own representatives," said David Ermini, a ÔÇőformer vice president of the CSM and an ÔÇőex-lawmaker with the centre-left Democratic Party.
No one looms over this debate quite like Luca Palamara, a former CSM member whose spectacular downfall spurred the reform.
Wiretaps in 2019 exposed late-night meetings involving Palamara, senior judicial figures and politicians, with discussions focused on steering appointments ÔÇö especially to coveted and sensitive prosecutor posts.
Careers were clearly being fixed by cliques ÔÇőand Palamara was expelled from the magistracy.
Now, in a twist that infuriates many of his former colleagues, Palamara is campaigning for a "Yes" vote.
"The introduction ÔÇőof random selection would dismantle Ôüáthe possibility of pre-selecting who sits on the CSM, thereby eliminating factional ties," he said, calling the reform "the one most feared" by those within the factions.
Some opponents say the proposed new selection rules risk promoting people who are ill-prepared or under-qualified, sweeping away any notion of merit and leaving everything up to chance. But Palamara said these concerns were overblown.
"We are talking about people who have passed a highly competitive public examination Ôüáto enter the ÔÇőprofession and who make extremely serious decisions every day - life sentences, delicate family situations," he said.
But even if the reform ÔÇőpasses the referendum hurdle, history suggests that sortition may not achieve the desired result.
In both ancient Rome and Renaissance Florence, lotteries promised fair rotation in theory, but in practice elites learned to game the system, steering outcomes so that the biggest ÔÇőprizes still tended to land in familiar hands.
"The mechanism of factionism would not end with drawing lots. Everyone selected might all come from the same faction," said Ermini.

item.title: Ukrainian trainers will help German army get ready to defend against Russia by 2029, chief says
item.description:
BERLIN, March 11 (Reuters) - Ukrainian military trainers will help ÔÇőGermany's military meet a target of being ready to defend against any Russian attack on ÔÇîNATO by 2029, the German army chief said, in a role reversal after years of Western troops training Ukrainian forces.
Berlin and Kyiv agreed last month that Ukraine would send military instructors to German army schools to teach lessons they ÔÇőhave learned from fighting against Russia's invasion.
"We have high expectations," Lieutenant General Christian Freuding, the head ÔÇőof the German army, told Reuters in an interview. "The Ukrainian military is currently Ôüáthe only one in the world with frontline experience against Russia."
Germany is not the only Western country ÔÇődrawing on Kyiv's experience: Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskiy on Thursday pledged to provide assistance to the U.S. in ÔÇődealing with Iranian drones in the Middle East.
Freuding said Germany was the first country to reach an arrangement with Ukraine to provide military instructors but predicted others would follow suit.
Asked what the Ukrainian trainers would contribute, he referred to assessments ÔÇőby German and other Western intelligence agencies that Russia could be ready for a large-scale attack on ÔÇőthe NATO military alliance by 2029.
"That's almost the day after tomorrow. We have no time - the enemy doesn't wait for ÔÇîus Ôüáto declare we're ready. So we have to use every possibility to prepare," Freuding said.
Russia has insisted it does not intend to attack NATO, but Western officials say such assurances cannot be taken at face value as Moscow made similar statements before invading Ukraine.
Freuding, who became army chief ÔÇőlast October after previously coordinating ÔÇőGermany's arms supplies to ÔüáKyiv , said the initial contingent of Ukrainian trainers would likely number in the "middle double-digits" and stay for a few weeks at a time.
The Ukrainian instructors are ÔÇőexpected to contribute expertise in artillery, engineering, armoured operations, drone usage and command ÔÇőand control.
Since ÔüáRussia's full-scale invasion of Ukraine in 2022, Germany has trained Ukrainian forces on weapons such as Marder infantry fighting vehicles, Leopard tanks, howitzers and air defence systems.
Freuding said Ukraine had built on these skills in combat, developing Ôüátactics ÔÇőand capabilities in data-centric warfare - the military term for highly ÔÇődigitalised and interconnected operations - that cannot be found in textbooks.
"The fact that they are now coming to us as instructors reflects a ÔÇősecurity partnership on an equal footing," Freuding said.

item.title: ItalyÔÇÖs Meloni criticises US war on Iran as part of dangerous trend
item.description:
ROME, March 11 (Reuters) - Italy's Prime Minister ÔÇîGiorgia Meloni on Wednesday delivered her strongest criticism yet of the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran, describing it as part of a growing and dangerous trend of interventions "outside the scope of international law".
Her ÔÇőremarks to parliament came after repeated accusations from the opposition that her ÔÇőright-wing government had been too soft toward its allies. Most other ÔüáEuropean nations, with the notable exception of Spain , have withheld direct critique of the U.S. ÔÇőand Israeli attacks, largely calling for restraint.
Meloni - who has close ties with U.S. President ÔÇőDonald Trump - also said Iran must not be allowed to develop nuclear weapons, as that would end the international non-proliferation framework with "dramatic repercussions for global security", leaving Italy and Europe exposed to a ÔÇőpotential nuclear threat from Tehran.
The U.S. and Israel traded air strikes with Iran ÔÇőacross the Middle East as the war stretched into its 12th day, a conflict that has halted ÔÇîthe Ôüáflow of one-fifth of the world's oil and gas supplies.
Addressing parliament on the crisis, Meloni drew parallels between the Middle East conflict and Russia's 2022 invasion of Ukraine, which she said triggered a broader global destabilisation.
"It is in this context of ÔÇőstructural crisis in the ÔÇőinternational system, in Ôüáwhich threats are becoming increasingly frightening and unilateral interventions outside the scope of international law are multiplying, that we must also ÔÇőplace the American and Israeli intervention against the Iranian regime," she ÔÇőtold the ÔüáSenate.
Meloni said Rome was providing air-defense assets to Gulf countries hit by strikes from Tehran.
"This is not only because these are friendly nations and strategic partners of Italy, but also Ôüábecause ÔÇőthere are tens of thousands of Italian citizens in ÔÇőthat area whom we must protect, not to mention the fact that there are about 2,000 Italian ÔÇősoldiers stationed in the Gulf."

item.title: US navy aircraft transits Taiwan Strait ahead of Trump's planned China visit
item.description:
TAIPEI, March 11 (Reuters) - A U.S. Navy P-8A Poseidon aircraft transited the Taiwan Strait in international ÔÇőairspace on Wednesday, in a move the ÔÇîU.S. 7th Fleet said "demonstrates the United States' commitment to a free and open Indo-Pacific."
The transit comes ahead of ÔÇőU.S. President Donald Trump's planned visit to China ÔÇőthis month as Washington and Beijing look Ôüáto stabilise ties marred by issues including Taiwan, ÔÇőtrade disputes and technology curbs.
"By operating within the ÔÇőTaiwan Strait in accordance with international law, the United States upholds the navigational rights and freedoms of all nations," the ÔÇő7th Fleet said in a statement.
U.S. military ÔÇőships or aircraft routinely pass through or above the waterway ÔÇîthat Ôüáseparates democratically governed Taiwan from China - missions that always anger Beijing.
China claims democratically governed Taiwan as its own territory and says it has jurisdiction over ÔÇőthe strait. ÔÇőTaiwan and Ôüáthe U.S. dispute that, saying the strait is an international waterway.
China has repeatedly ÔÇősaid the Taiwan issue is its ÔÇőinternal affair Ôüáand at the core of Beijing's interests - a red line that must not be crossed or Ôüátrampled ÔÇőon.
Taiwan's government rejects China's sovereignty claims, ÔÇősaying that only Taiwan's people can decide their future.

item.title: A 'corporate welfare' claim vs Walmart and Amazon over worker benefits
item.description:
March 11 (Reuters) - (The opinions expressed here are those of the author, a correspondent for Reuters. This column is part of the Reuters Sustainable Finance Newsletter, which you can sign up for here )
Providing low-paid workers with government benefits like health insurance and food assistance is usually considered to be socially minded policy. But another way to look at the payments is as subsidies giving big businesses access to a large pool of labor able to work for less-than-living wages.
That's the critique of Sarah Anderson, Global Economy ÔÇőProject Director at the Institute for Policy Studies, a progressive-leaning think tank in Washington, D.C. In a new report , opens new tab she reviewed pay practices of companies she dubbed "The Low-Wage 20," or the 20 largest employers of low-wage U.S. ÔÇîworkers.
Among her top findings: more than half of these firms reported 2024 median pay that would make a family of three eligible for food benefits under the federal Supplemental Nutrition Assistance Program, also known as SNAP, and for government-subsidized Medicaid health insurance benefits in most states. All the while, most of their CEOs earned hundreds of times as much per year.
The following transcript of our conversation has been edited for length and clarity. At the end I've published responses from the two top U.S. employers, Walmart and Amazon. (AMZN.O) , opens new tab
Question: Sarah, could you talk about how you were able to develop the calculations?
Answer: I have worked on executive compensation issues for a long, ÔÇőlong time. And this is a report that really connects those high CEO pay levels to low-wage worker issues. These are all companies that are in the S&P 500 and they're the ones that have the lowest median pay levels ÔÇőthat they've reported to the SEC (U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission) and that also have a majority of their workers in the United States.
Q: You found a lot of these employees were eligible for things Ôüálike Medicaid and SNAP food assistance, which are social benefits to lower-wage Americans.
A: We think that median pay number that the companies report themselves is a really good indicator of how many of their workers might be on Medicaid and SNAP. We don't have ÔÇőthe exact figures because they're not required to report that, but there were a handful of state governments that have revealed specific data company by company, and Walmart and Amazon were at the top of all of those lists with really clear numbers in terms ÔÇőof how many of their employees are.
These benefits are at risk right now. So it's a good time to be having this conversation because these workers who are already having to stretch to pay their bills are going to be facing even more hardship.
Q: If I were an executive for one of these companies, I'm sure I would respond by saying, well, a lot of these are part-time workers who don't want to be working full time. And, the calculations for public assistance are based on household size. Maybe it's not realistic to expect a head of household, a three-person household, to survive ÔÇőon just one income in this day and age.
A: I would say that having a business model that is heavily reliant on part-time workers is a choice. It's a way to get around having to pay full benefits at a lot of these companies under ÔÇőthe Affordable Care Act. Large companies have to provide affordable health insurance, or at least offer it to people working at least 30 hours a week. So if you can keep your workers, you know, at a lower hourly level, then you can get around that. I (also) question ÔÇîwhether people are Ôüáworking part time because they want to work part time. A lot of people are having to work several part-time jobs to try to make ends meet.
You know, a huge share of the low-wage workforce are single parents. And for these companies to just abdicate their obligation to provide a living wage says a lot about their priorities.
Question: Surely mom-and-pop businesses face the same challenge. A lot of them can't pay their workers enough to get them above the thresholds for public assistance as well. What's the difference?
A: These should be the leading among the leading corporations of America. They're in the S&P 500. They set the standards for wages and other things. The other thing that just makes it so outrageous to so many people is to see the CEO pay levels at these companies. There's ÔÇőno owner of a small mom and pop that is making ÔÇőthe $18 million a year, which is the average (for CEOs) Ôüáin our low-wage 20. It's a business model that is based on really extracting from the people often who are the frontline workers.
Q: Something that jumped out at me, among the large employers in Nevada (a state cited in the study) is that a couple of public sector organizations, a school district and a county that have a lot of their employees eligible for public assistance.
A: That's true. I have to ÔÇősay I was dismayed to see the U.S. Postal Service was on some of these lists as well. It just gets to my broader view on this that having these extreme ÔÇőgaps between CEO pay and worker Ôüápay, where people don't feel like they're part of a team, it just doesn't bring out the best in people and that does have real costs in terms of lower employee morale and higher turnover rates.
Here is what a Walmart spokesman said about the study - "We offer a ladder of opportunity so people can build a career at Walmart, which includes a small percentage of our workforce that comes to us on public assistance. We hire them, train them and give them the chance to build a better life."
Here is what Amazon spokesperson Eileen Hards told me: ÔÇťAmazon pay Ôüáis among the ÔÇőbest in the industry ÔÇô well over double the federal minimum wage and significantly more than other retailers. Pointing fingers at Amazon over SNAP and/or Medicaid is a ÔÇőred herring when eligibility is based on total household income and size ÔÇô and not individual wages."
"For example, two employees who work at the same site and earn identical pay in the same state can have completely different SNAP or Medicaid eligibility depending on whether they support children, elderly parents, or are the sole earner in ÔÇőtheir household. As weÔÇÖve said for years, what really needs to happen is a significant and large increase in the federal minimum wageÔÇöthat would be a big boost for American families.ÔÇŁ

item.title: Morning Bid: From panic to patience
item.description:
March 11 - What matters in U.S. and global markets today
By Mike Dolan , opens new tab , Editor-At-Large, Finance and Markets
Both energy and stock markets appear to be in a holding pattern after the wild swings earlier this week as they look for any evidence that oil and gas supplies might ÔÇőbe freed up in the Middle East.
For now, there are few signs on the ground to back up President TrumpÔÇÖs view that the war will ÔÇîbe over very soon, with Tuesday seeing intense bombardments , shipping still blocked in the Strait of Hormuz, and Saudi AramcoÔÇÖs CEO warning of ÔÇťcatastrophic consequencesÔÇŁ for the oil market if conflict drags on.
IÔÇÖll get into that and more below.
But first, check out my latest column on why an Iran oil shock could prove politically toxic in the U.S. even if GDP escapes largely unscathed.
And listen to today's episode of the Morning Bid podcast , where I discuss ÔÇőoil-shock inflation fears and look ahead to today's big macro release - inflation data for the leadup to the crisis.
Subscribe to hear Reuters journalists discuss the biggest news ÔÇőin markets and finance seven days a week.
Oil prices initially dropped below $90 per barrel on Wednesday on reports that the ÔüáInternational Energy Agency had proposed the largest release of oil reserves in its history - more than double the size of the 182 million barrels released after the Ukraine invasion in 2022.
Oil then staged ÔÇőa rebound , however, as traders digested the proposal. Goldman Sachs estimated a release the size of that in 2022 would offset 12 days of an estimated 15.4 million barrel per day ÔÇőexport disruption in the Gulf.
As prices seesaw, though, it will be hoped that Monday's panic surge in crude markets to nearly $120 per barrel has set the parameters for prices - at least until the duration of the Iran conflict becomes clearer.
Wall Street was mixed on Tuesday as the intense bombardments in the Middle East - and further Iranian threats of a comprehensive oil blockade - dampened earlier hopes for a short-lived conflict. All major ÔÇőindexes closed largely flat.
Global shares steadied somewhat on Wednesday as JapanÔÇÖs Nikkei gained 1.7% and South KoreaÔÇÖs KOSPI rose by 1.75%. European stocks opened lower, however, while U.S. stock futures held ÔÇősteady ahead of the bell.
Meantime, the dollar rebounded after slipping early on Wednesday, holding onto some of the early-week gains it recently shed as traders looked for signs of a quick conclusion to the war.
Later ÔÇîtoday, traders Ôüáwill get a read on where U.S. CPI was in February, just before the Iran attacks started late in the month. More important, though, will be the release on Friday of February PCE data, the FedÔÇÖs favoured measure of inflation. Core inflation measured by the latter was likely already running above 3% before the oil spike.
Elsewhere, OracleÔÇÖs shares surged 8% in extended trading after its latest results, which saw it predict boosted revenue estimates well into 2027 on the AI data center boom, helping to reassure investors about the massive scale of its capex. ÔÇőThe results also serve as a reminder of ÔÇőthe enduring AI theme in equities Ôüábeyond the day-to-day energy market ructions.
Ongoing angst about private credit funds and the quality of the loans contained within them is also rising, however. JPMorgan Chase has marked down the value of certain loans held by private credit groups and is tightening its lending to the sector, ÔÇőthe FT reported on Wednesday
Chart of the day
U.S. consumer prices likely picked up in February as the cost of gasoline increased in ÔÇőanticipation of an escalating Ôüáwar in the Middle East, and with the conflict driving up oil prices, a further rise in inflation is expected in March.
The anticipated increase in the Consumer Price Index last month would also reflect the continued but staggered pass-through from President Donald Trump's sweeping tariffs, which he pursued under a law meant for use in national emergencies, that have since been struck down by the ÔüáU.S. Supreme ÔÇőCourt.
Today's events to watch
* U.S. February CPI (8:30 AM EDT)
* U.S. 10-year note auction
* Fed's Michelle Bowman speaks
* OPEC ÔÇőMonthly Oil Market Report
Want to receive the Morning Bid in your inbox every weekday morning? Sign up for the newsletter here . You can find ROI on the Reuters website , opens new tab , and you can follow us on LinkedIn , opens new tab and X , opens new tab .
Opinions expressed are those of ÔÇőthe author. They do not reflect the views of Reuters News, which, under the Trust Principles , opens new tab , is committed to integrity, independence, and freedom from bias.

item.title: Exclusive: Shell, TotalEnergies and others declare FM to their clients who take LNG from Qatar - sources say
item.description:
LONDON, March 11 - Several companies that buy liquefied natural ÔÇîgas (LNG) from QatarEnergy as portfolio players or offtakers ÔÇö including Shell (SHEL.L) , opens new tab , TotalEnergies (TTEF.PA) , opens new tab and some firms in Asia ÔÇö have declared force majeure ÔÇőto customers they supply, three sources told Reuters ÔÇőon Wednesday.
Qatar, the world's second-largest LNG exporter, announced Ôüáa production halt at its 77 million tons ÔÇőper annum (mtpa) facility last week and declared force majeure ÔÇőon LNG shipments.
Shell , the world's largest LNG trader, declined to comment. TotalEnergies did not immediately respond to Reuters request for comment.
Both ÔÇőcompanies have long-term partnerships with QatarEnergy and are partners ÔÇőin the company's massive North Field expansion project which aims to ÔÇîboost Ôüácapacity by 2027.
Analysts estimate Shell takes 6.8 million tons per annum (mtpa) of Qatari LNG, while TotalEnergies takes 5.2 mtpa and sells them to their clients worldwide.
Qatari ÔÇőEnergy Minister Saad ÔÇőal-Kaabi told Ôüáthe Financial Times last week that it would take "weeks to months" to return to ÔÇőnormal deliveries, even if the war ended ÔÇőtoday. ÔüáThe company declared force majeure on LNG shipments on Wednesday.
Sources told Reuters last week that the FM notices Ôüásent ÔÇőto clients stated that LNG deliveries ÔÇőfor March will not be affected, with the impact being felt as ÔÇőof April.

item.title: Return to Russian fuels would be a strategic blunder, von der Leyen says
item.description:
BRUSSELS, March 11 (Reuters) - Europe would be making a strategic ÔÇőblunder if it sought to ÔÇîblunt soaring energy prices with a return to Russian ÔÇőfossil fuels, European ÔÇőCommission president Ursula von der ÔüáLeyen said on Wednesday.
"In ÔÇőthe current crisis, some argue ÔÇőthat we should abandon our long-term strategy and even go ÔÇőback to Russian fossil ÔÇőfuels. This would be a strategic ÔÇîblunder," Ôüávon der Leyen said in a speech in European Parliament.
Von der Leyen ÔÇősaid the ÔÇőEU Ôüáwas preparing options to lower energy prices, ÔÇőincluding the better ÔÇőuse Ôüáof power purchasing agreements, state aid measures and Ôüágas ÔÇőprice subsidies or ÔÇőcaps.

item.title: Israel sees no certainty IranÔÇÖs government will fall despite war
item.description:
JERUSALEM/DUBAI, March 11 (Reuters) - Israeli officials in closed discussions have acknowledged there is no certainty the war against Iran will lead to a collapse of its clerical government, a senior Israeli official told Reuters, with no sign ÔÇőof an Iranian uprising amid the bombardment.
However, despite commentary by U.S. President Donald Trump that the war may finish soon , Israel's assessment is that Washington is not close to instructing an end ÔÇîto the conflict , two Israeli officials said.
The intense U.S. and Israeli bombing campaign killed the supreme leader, Ayatollah Ali Khamenei, as well as a host of senior military commanders, but it has also killed civilians and smashed homes and public buildings, angering many Iranians.
With missiles striking across Tehran and other cities, and with the Iranian authorities threatening deadly force against any who dares protest, Iranians who might otherwise come to the streets may also fear to do so until the war ends.
Still, Iran's long-term challenges ÔÇőlook worse than ever, with ever harsher sanctions strangling the economy and little prospect of better times ahead for a population whose protests in January were crushed with thousands dead.
The senior Israeli official did not say what had led the country to assess that a collapse of Iran's ruling system was not Ôüáa certainty.
On the day Israel launched its joint air war with the U.S., its Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu said: "Our joint action will create the conditions for the brave Iranian people to take their destiny ÔÇőinto their own hands."
He particularly spoke of Iran's main ethnic and linguistic minorities, the Kurds , Baluchis and Arabs, adding to reports that the U.S. or Israel may back uprisings by those groups.
However, in a statement on Tuesday, ÔÇőNetanyahu repeated that though Israel's aspiration was to help Iranians "cast off the yoke of tyranny," that ultimately "it is up to them" - an apparent acknowledgement that a rising does not look imminent.
Israel and the U.S. have not issued a joint public statement setting out clear unified war aims, or articulating the conditions under which they might decide to end the campaign.
On Monday, Trump described the war as "very complete, pretty much", but on Tuesday the White House said it would only end when Trump determined its ÔÇőobjectives were met and that Iran was in a state of unconditional surrender.
In a closedÔÇĹdoor briefing with foreign diplomats on Tuesday, Israeli Foreign Minister Gideon Saar declined to set a timeframe for the military campaign - ÔÇőchiming with the government's assessment that Trump was not close to ending it.
Saar acknowledged in the briefing that IranÔÇÖs government could survive the war but expressed confidence it would collapse later, sources said.
Speaking to reporters on Tuesday, Saar said ÔÇîthe war Ôüáwould proceed until Israel and the U.S. determined the time had come to end hostilities, but that Israel did not seek "endless war".
Assaf Orion, a former head of strategy with the Israeli military and a fellow at the Washington Institute for Near East Policy, said weakening Iranian military capabilities appeared a more direct, tangible and measurable war aim.
"Creating conditions for regime change is indirect and hence more difficult to fathom," he said, adding that while the military campaign appeared to be planned for weeks, any uprising against Iran's ruling system might take months or years.
On Wednesday Iran's police chief Ahmadreza Radan warned ÔÇőthat "anyone taking to the streets at the enemy's ÔÇőrequest will be confronted as an enemy, not Ôüáa protester. All our security forces have their fingers on the trigger".
While many Iranians want change and some openly celebrated the death of Khamenei, whose security forces killed thousands of anti-government protesters just weeks ago, there has been no sign of protest since the war began.
"I hate this regime. I want it to ÔÇőgo, but under bombardment there are no streets left to protest in," said Ali, 26, from Tehran.
Although Tehran's streets are far quieter than usual, banks, petrol ÔÇőstations and shops are all Ôüáoperating as usual, though with reduced hours and tighter rationing of fuel. All government offices are open.
"Every day and every night there is bombing. My apartment building and its windows shake," said a Tehran resident who asked to retain anonymity for fear of reprisals.
"Despite discontent with the regime, the sense of Iranian pride and patriotism is growing and people are expressing hatred for Reza Pahlavi, Trump and Netanyahu," the resident added, naming the son of ÔüáIran's former shah, ÔÇőwho had urged military intervention.
However, the bombing has destroyed airports and ports as well as other civilian infrastructure, adding to the ÔÇőproblems Iran's government may face in reviving a stagnant economy that has angered much of the population.
"Iran prefers the war to continue because if it ends, protests will start due to economic problems and shortcomings that the war has also worsened," said a university ÔÇőteacher in Tehran who also requested anonymity.

item.title: Spain permanently withdraws ambassador as rift with Israel deepens
item.description:
MADRID, March 11 (Reuters) - Spain permanently withdrew its ambassador to Israel on Tuesday as a ÔÇődiplomatic standoff worsened between the two countries ÔÇîover Spain's opposition to the U.S.-Israeli attacks on Iran .
The ambassador was summoned back to Spain last September amid a diplomatic ÔÇőrow over Spanish measures banning aircraft and ships ÔÇőcarrying weapons to Israel from its ports Ôüáor airspace due to Israel's military offensive in ÔÇőGaza, which Israeli Foreign Minister Gideon Sa'ar denounced ÔÇőas antisemitic.
On Tuesday, Spain published an announcement in its official gazette that the ambassador's position had been terminated. Spain's Foreign ÔÇőMinistry said its embassy in Tel Aviv ÔÇőwill be led by a charge d'affaires for the foreseeable ÔÇîfuture.
The Ôüámove marks the latest escalation in diplomatic relations between the two countries, which have been heavily strained since Israel launched its assault on the Gaza ÔÇőStrip in ÔÇőOctober of Ôüá2023.
Israel's embassy in Spain is also run by a charge d'affaires after the ÔÇőcountry summoned its ambassador last May ÔÇőin protest Ôüáat Spain's decision to recognise a Palestinian state.
Tensions have heightened since the U.S.-Israeli strikes on Iran, Ôüáwith ÔÇőSa'ar accusing Spain in early March ÔÇőof "standing with tyrants" for opposing the war.

item.title: Iran's new supreme leader 'lightly injured' but still active, Iranian official tells Reuters
item.description:
DUBAI, March 11 (Reuters) - Newly-appointed Supreme ÔÇőLeader Mojtaba Khamenei ÔÇîwas lightly injured but ÔÇőis continuing ÔÇőto operate, an Iranian Ôüáofficial ÔÇőtold Reuters on ÔÇőWednesday.
The official did not give details ÔÇőabout ÔÇőwhen he was injured ÔÇîor Ôüáwhy he had not made any ÔÇőstatement ÔÇőto Ôüáthe public since ÔÇőbeing appointed ÔÇőthe Ôüánew leader.

item.title: Drones hit east Congo's biggest city, killing French aid worker, rebels and UN say
item.description:
March 11 (Reuters) - Drone strikes hit Goma in east Congo ÔÇőon Wednesday, killing at least three people including a French aid worker, the ÔÇîfirst such attack in the city since AFC/M23 rebels seized it last year, according to the group and the U.N.
The AFC/M23 blamed the attack on the Congolese army, saying in a post on X that Kinshasa ÔÇőhad launched drones against a densely populated urban area in the lakeside city.
A spokesperson ÔÇőfor the rebels said in a separate post that three people had died, Ôüáincluding a foreign humanitarian worker.
A senior official for U.N. children's agency UNICEF told Reuters ÔÇőthat one of its employees, a French national, was killed.
French President Emmanuel Macron and Hadja Lahbib, ÔÇőEuropean Commissioner for equality, preparedness and crisis management, condemned the attack in posts on X, calling for respect for international humanitarian law and saying aid workers must never be targeted.
A spokesperson for Congo's army declined to ÔÇőcomment.
A spokesperson for Congo's government did not immediately respond to a request for comment.
A Reuters ÔÇőjournalist in Goma reported hearing two loud explosions at around 4 a.m. (0200 GMT). The blasts shook windows and ÔÇîdoors Ôüáand were followed by the sound of ambulance sirens.
A senior AFC/M23 official told Reuters the house struck by one of the drones had been rented by UNICEF employees and is close to a residence used by former Congolese President Joseph Kabila in a neighbourhood that houses several ÔÇőprominent political and business ÔÇőfigures.
The official said a Ôüásecond drone had targeted the residence of AFC/M23 political coordinator Corneille Nangaa, but fell into Lake Kivu.
The strikes come after weeks of intensifying ÔÇődrone operations on both sides of the conflict.
AFC/M23 military spokesperson Willy Ngoma ÔÇőwas killed in Ôüáa drone attack by the Congolese army near the strategic mining town of Rubaya, some 60 km from Goma, on February 24.
The rebels have also claimed a series of drone attacks on the airport Ôüáserving ÔÇőthe northeastern city of Kisangani in recent weeks.
Fighting has ÔÇőcontinued in eastern Congo on several fronts, despite mediation efforts by parties including Qatar and the U.S.

item.title: Israeli settler violence rises in West Bank under Iran war curbs
item.description:
ABU FALAH, West Bank, March 11 (Reuters) - Israeli settlers in the occupied West Bank are taking advantage of curbs on movement imposed during the war on Iran to attack Palestinians, with military roadblocks ÔÇőpreventing ambulances reaching victims quickly, rights groups and medics say.
Settlers have killed at least five Palestinians in the West Bank since the United States and Israel began airstrikes against Iran ÔÇîon February 28, according to the Palestinian health ministry. A sixth man died after inhaling teargas fired during an attack, according to Israeli rights group B'Tselem.
Israel's military blocked many West Bank roads with iron gates and mounds of earth on the first day of the war, and has largely shut crossings with Israel.
The Israeli military says the curbs are preemptive measures while it is carrying out airstrikes on Iran and against Lebanese group Hezbollah, which has fired missiles at Israel in solidarity with ÔÇőTehran.
Palestinians in remote West Bank villages say the roadblocks have left them increasingly exposed to settler violence.
The Israeli military has also continued to carry out the raids it frequently conducts in Palestinian ÔÇőcities and towns during peacetime to arrest Palestinians, often without charge, they say.
A spokesperson for the Yesha Council, which represents Jewish settlements, did not immediately respond Ôüáto a Reuters request for comment on the attacks.
Malak Beirat's husband, Thaer, was one of two Palestinians who residents and the Palestinian health ministry say were shot dead by settlers before dawn on Sunday ÔÇőin Abu Falah, north of the city of Ramallah.
"Thaer loved life. I never expected he would die," said Beirat, fighting back tears as she sat with her two children.
Witnesses told Reuters that when over 100 settlers ÔÇőgathered on the outskirts of Abu Falah, a local WhatsApp group rallied men to protect the small village. The initial confrontation involved stone throwing, but armed settlers arrived later and began shooting, they said.
Beirat's husband was shot dead while trying to protect a house from attack, a man who helped defend the village said.
Blood could still be seen on Monday in olive groves at the scene of the attack, where villagers have erected two Palestinian flags at the spots where the ÔÇőtwo men were killed - one for each victim.
A third Palestinian died after the attack. B'Tselem said his death was probably caused by the effect of teargas fired by Israeli troops deployed to the village during the ÔÇőattack.
The Israeli military says an investigation has been launched into the incident and that it condemns "violence of any kind".
Medics say the new roadblocks have led to delays in reaching injured Palestinians.
"There are obstacles - and even ÔÇîattacks by settlers Ôüáand the military on the (medical) crews," said Ahmed Jibril, spokesman for the Palestinian Red Crescent ambulance service.
There have been over 109 reports of settler violence against Palestinians in the West Bank since the start of the war with Iran including shootings, physical assaults, property damage, and threats, said Israeli monitoring group Yesh Din.
All the reported killings of Palestinians by settlers this year were in the last week, B'Tselem said.
Settlers shot dead Amir Muhammad Shanaran in a village near the city of Hebron on Saturday, and brothers Muhammad and Fahim ÔÇśAzem were shot dead in Qaryut southeast of the city of Nablus last Monday, the Palestinian health ministry said.
"Taking advantage ÔÇőof the war, armed settler militias, often operating with ÔÇősupport from the army, continue to attack and Ôüáharass Palestinian communities across the West Bank in an effort to force them out," B'Tselem said.
In three of the settler shootings, the settlers were wearing Israeli army uniform, Yesh Din said. The Israeli military did not immediately respond to a request for comment.
Palestinians accuse the military of protecting settlers rather than villagers. Israel's ÔÇőmilitary denies this.
Israeli indictments of settler violence are rare. At the end of 2025, Yesh Din said that of the hundreds of cases of settler ÔÇőviolence it had documented since Ôüáthe Hamas-led attacks on Israel in October 2023 that led to the Gaza war , only 2% resulted in indictments.
The United Nations says nearly 700 Palestinians were displaced by settler violence from the start of 2025 through early February 2026.
Israel's government has expanded settlements in a construction push that Finance Minister Bezalel Smotrich says is aimed at burying the idea of a Palestinian state.
Right-wing Israeli minister Yossi Dagan announced on Wednesday the establishment of a new settlement Ôüáin a strategic ÔÇőposition in the mountain overlooking Nablus, one of 22 new settlements announced by the Israeli government last May.
Palestinians have long sought an ÔÇőindependent state in the West Bank, Gaza and East Jerusalem, territories Israel captured and occupied in the 1967 Middle East war.
Over 700,000 settlers live in East Jerusalem and the West Bank among more than 3 million Palestinians, according to a European Union report in 2024.
Most ÔÇőof the world considers Israel's settlement activity in the West Bank illegal under international law relating to military occupations. Israel disputes this view.

item.title: EU envoys approve sanctions on 19 Iranian officials, entities over rights violations
item.description:
BRUSSELS, March 11 (Reuters) - European Union envoys have approved new ÔÇősanctions targeting 19 ÔÇîIranian officials and entities responsible for serious human rights ÔÇőviolations, the bloc's ÔÇőforeign policy chief, Kaja ÔüáKallas, said on Wednesday.
"As ÔÇőthe Iran war continues, ÔÇőthe EU will protect its interests and pursue those ÔÇőresponsible for domestic repression," ÔÇőKallas said on X, adding ÔÇîthat "it Ôüáalso sends a message to Tehran that IranÔÇÖs future cannot be built ÔÇőon ÔÇőrepression".
The Ôüásanctions, which have been in the ÔÇőmaking since before ÔÇőthe Ôüábeginning of the war, will require final approval Ôüáfrom ÔÇőthe Council ÔÇőof the EU.

item.title: Israeli dentist returns to identifying war victims in Iran conflict
item.description:
JERUSALEM, March 11 (Reuters) - After helping to identify the remains of the last Israeli hostage in Gaza, dentist Esi Sharon-Sagie hoped her work naming the dead killed ÔÇőin more than two years of war was finally over. Weeks later she was called back to duty.
On February 28, a ÔÇîU.S.-Israel air assault in Iran ignited a war now engulfing the Middle East and Sharon-Sagie is again seeking to give a name to those whose bodies are too badly mutilated for visual identification techniques.
On March 1, she was called to the site of an Iranian missile attack on the town of Beit Shemesh in central Israel.
"We were informed that a missile ÔÇőkilled nine people that weren't able to get into a shelter in time. And the minute we heard that the disaster happened, ÔÇőwe had to go to do the identification," said Sharon-Sagie.
Among the dead were a brother and two sisters, 13, 15 Ôüáand 16 years old.
Although she has been volunteering with police since 2010, Sharon-Sagie says that everything changed on ÔÇőOctober 7, 2023, when Palestinian militant group Hamas launched a surprise attack on Israel.
Health authorities struggled to cope with the hundreds killed in Israel's single deadliest ÔÇőday. Identification of about 1,200 fatalities, some badly burned, was not completed for many months.
Forensic odontology is used to identify the remains of people whose bodies are in a condition that makes other methods, like visual recognition or fingerprinting, impossible. For instance, a charred or decomposed body can be identified by matching X-rays with past dental records.
Sharon-Sagie, who ÔÇőis on duty day and night, often had to summon the strength to overcome exhaustion. On one occasion, at the end of an hours-long shift, ÔÇőshe was asked to help with one more victim. She looked at the woman's slim hand, and rings.
"I spoke with her in my heart with no sound. I ÔÇîlooked at Ôüáher and I said: 'now you're going to help me because I have no strength anymore. You'll help me and I'll help you. I'll bring you back to your family'," said Sharon-Sagie.
A year later she helped to identify one of the masterminds of the October 7 attack.
Yahya Sinwar , then the leader of Hamas, was killed by Israeli fighters in southern Gaza in October 2024.
Sinwar's body was transferred to Israel, which still held his ÔÇőmedical records from his time in ÔÇőan Israeli prison, where he was Ôüájailed for killing Israeli soldiers and Palestinians whom Hamas suspected were spying for Israel.
"He had brain surgery in Israel many, many years ago. So we had all his information," said Sharon-Sagie.
For many in Israel, the pain of the ÔÇőOctober 7 attack could only be alleviated with the release of the last remaining hostages from Hamas captivity ÔÇőin Gaza.
That relief finally Ôüácame on January 26 when the remains of the last of the 251 hostages were found in the Palestinian enclave.
Sharon-Sagie was on the team that checked scores of bodies buried in a cemetery in northern Gaza in the search for police officer, Ran Gvili, who fought Palestinian militants at an Israeli kibbutz.
As a volunteer with the police Ôüáforce, it ÔÇőwas a complex moment for Sharon-Sagie.
"I was surprised it was actually happening. I felt my ÔÇőhands trembling, I was very moved. When we did the identification and finished signing the report that this is him, at first I was happy but seconds after that I understood ÔÇőthat it is over, he is not coming back to Israel alive," she said.

item.title: OPEC confirms big Saudi oil production hike ahead of Iran war, holds forecasts steady
item.description:
LONDON, March 11 (Reuters) - OPEC said on ÔÇőWednesday that Saudi Arabia sharply increased ÔÇîoil production in February ahead of U.S. and Israeli strikes on Iran and ÔÇőkept its forecasts for relatively ÔÇőstrong global oil demand growth this Ôüáyear.
Saudi Arabia increased its oil ÔÇőoutput and exports as part of ÔÇőa contingency plan in case any U.S. strike on Iran disrupts supplies from the Middle ÔÇőEast, sources familiar with the ÔÇőplan had said in February.
The attack came on ÔÇîFebruary Ôüá28 and the resulting conflict has disrupted oil exports, forced production stoppages and sent prices soaring.
OPEC, in a ÔÇőmonthly report ÔÇőon its Ôüáwebsite, said that Saudi Arabia told OPEC its ÔÇőoil supply to the market ÔÇőin ÔüáFebruary was 10.111 million barrels per day while production was 10.882 million Ôüábpd. ÔÇőSaudi Arabia reported its ÔÇőproduction at 10.10 million bpd for January.

item.title: EU countries give final approval to 2040 target
item.description:
BRUSSELS, March 5 (Reuters) - European ÔÇîUnion countries gave the final approval on Thursday to a new climate target to slash greenhouse gas emissions 90% by 2040, pressing ahead with the bloc's ambitious climate agenda despite political resistance.
