The new climate target ÔÇőis a hard-fought political compromise, struck by governments and EU lawmakers last year. ÔÇőIt is more ambitious than most major economies' emissions-cutting commitments, including China's.
In Ôüápractice, the target will require an 85% emissions reduction from European industries against 1990 ÔÇőlevels. The EU will pay developing countries via carbon credits, so they cut emissions on ÔÇőEurope's behalf to make up the rest, to reach 90%.
The EU agreed the target last year following months of wrangling between countries, such as Spain, which say worsening droughts and wildfires justified more ambitious ÔÇőgoals, and those like Poland and Italy, which sought to soften the emissions cuts, ÔÇőarguing that struggling industries cannot afford the upfront investments.
A reinforced majority of EU countries' ministers gave the ÔÇîfinal Ôüáformal sign-off to the legally binding goal at a meeting in Brussels. The Czech Republic, Slovakia, Poland and Hungary opposed it. The climate target will now pass into EU law.
As part of the agreement, the EU will also consider the option in future to ÔÇőuse international carbon credits ÔÇőto meet a Ôüáfurther 5% of its 2040 emissions reductions - potentially further softening the domestic efforts required.
The deal also delays the launch of a politically ÔÇősensitive new EU carbon market by one year, to 2028 - a ÔÇőmove designed Ôüáto win over countries sceptical of the climate goal.
The target, which is designed to keep Europe on track to meet its pledge to reach net-zero emissions by 2050, falls short of the Ôüá90% domestic ÔÇőemissions cut recommended by the EU's climate science ÔÇőadvisers.
It is also weaker than Brussels' original plan for the goal, reflecting disagreement between EU governments over the speed and ÔÇőcost of their green agenda.

item.title: U.S. military tells civilians to avoid port facilities where Iranian navy operating
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 11 (Reuters) - The U.S. military on ÔÇőWednesday warned civilians ÔÇîto leave port facilities where Iranian ÔÇőnaval forces are ÔÇőoperating, adding that U.S. Ôüáforces could ÔÇőnot guarantee their ÔÇősafety.
"Iranian dockworkers, administrative personnel, and commercial vessel crews ÔÇőshould avoid ÔÇőIranian naval vessels and military ÔÇîequipment," ÔüáU.S. Central Command said in a statement. The ÔÇőUnited States ÔÇőhas Ôüábeen carrying out strikes ÔÇőagainst Iranian naval ÔÇővessels-- Ôüásinking or destroying 60 so Ôüáfar.

item.title: Turkey opposition leader says Imamoglu trial is purely political
item.description:
ISTANBUL, March 11 (Reuters) - The trial of Istanbul's jailed mayor, Ekrem Imamoglu, is a purely political affair ÔÇőaimed at preventing him from challenging President Tayyip Erdogan in the next election, Turkey's main opposition leader told ÔÇîReuters in an interview on Wednesday.
Imamoglu, 55, appeared in court on Monday in the opening hearing of a sweeping corruption case involving more than 400 defendants linked to the Istanbul municipality.
Prosecutors accuse him of leading a criminal organisation for profit through tender-rigging and bribery, charges he and his Republican People's Party (CHP) deny.
"Let ÔÇőme put it plainly. In Turkey, what is happening is not a trial in the normal sense of the word. ÔÇőIt is not a legal process but a political one," CHP leader Ozgur Ozel said.
The judges handling Ôüáthe case have been tasked "not with conducting a fair trial but with delivering the decision that Erdogan wants", he said.
Erdogan's government denies ÔÇőinfluencing the judiciary and says Turkey's courts are independent. The investigation into the Istanbul municipality was led by Akin Gurlek, who last month was ÔÇőappointed justice minister.
Imamoglu has been held in pre-trial detention for nearly a year amid an unprecedented judicial crackdown on the CHP that rights groups and opposition figures say has damaged the democratic credentials of NATO member Turkey.
Ozel said he did not expect Imamoglu to be released from detention during ÔÇőhis trial, adding that such an outcome would require greater social pressure within Turkey, international pressure, or a further drop in Erdogan's ÔÇőpoll ratings.
Ozel said Erdogan wanted to keep Imamoglu in prison because, if released, he could campaign across the country ahead of elections that the opposition ÔÇîleader Ôüábelieves will take place next year, ahead of schedule.
"If Imamoglu gets out, he could travel all over Turkey and launch a major campaign. That is why Erdogan wants to keep him inside," he said.
Opinion polls show Imamoglu performing strongly against Erdogan in any presidential race, while party polling suggests a tight race between the secularist CHP and ErdoganÔÇÖs Islamist-rooted AKP, with the CHP holding a narrow lead.
Ozel said ÔÇőTurkish democracy had sharply deteriorated ÔÇőunder Erdogan, who has led ÔüáTurkey as prime minister or president since 2003.
"Turkey's democracy has probably gone back 50 or 60 years," he said. "We have fallen into what I would call the third division in the world."
Ozel also said ÔÇőhe expected pressure on the opposition to intensify as elections approach.
"Things will get worse, and [Erdogan] will ÔÇőbecome more aggressive. ÔüáWe are open to every kind of attack, both through legal channels and through other means," he said, adding that the opposition was not afraid and believed it would defeat Erdogan and his ruling AK Party at the ballot box.
Ozel said he believed Erdogan was preparing for Ôüáan early ÔÇőelection in September or October 2027 rather than waiting until the scheduled 2028 ÔÇővote because under the constitution an earlier ballot could allow the president to run again.
"We want an election as soon as possible," Ozel added. "If the election is held ÔÇőin June 2028, Erdogan cannot run. If it is held early, he can."

item.title: Iran cannot participate in World Cup, sports minister says
item.description:
March 11 (Reuters) - Iran cannot participate in the 2026 World Cup after co-host ÔÇőthe U.S. launched airstrikes alongside Israel, Sports Minister Ahmad Donyamali said on Wednesday.
The attacks killed the Islamic Republic's Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali ÔÇîKhamenei and triggered a region-wide conflict .
"Considering that this corrupt regime has assassinated our leader, under no circumstances can we participate in the World Cup," the minister told state television.
The 48-team World Cup will be held in the U.S., Mexico and Canada from June 11 to July 19.
"Our children are not safe and, fundamentally, such conditions for participation do not exist," Donyamali said.
"Given ÔÇőthe malicious actions they have carried out against Iran, they have forced two wars on us over eight or nine months and have ÔÇőkilled and martyred thousands of our people. Therefore, we certainly cannot have such a presence."
More than 1,300 Iranian civilians have Ôüábeen killed since the U.S. and Israeli airstrikes began on February 28, according to Iran's U.N. ambassador Amir Saeid Iravani.
Iran are grouped with Belgium, Egypt and New Zealand.
All three of their Group G matches are scheduled to take place in the U.S., two in Los ÔÇőAngeles and one in Seattle.
Iran, who dominated the Asian qualifying rounds to qualify for the tournament in March last year, was the only nation missing from a FIFA planning summit for World Cup participants held last week in Atlanta.
There was no immediate comment from the Iranian Football Federation or world soccer's governing body FIFA.
FIFA regulations state that any team that ÔÇőwithdraws from the tournament "no later than 30 days before the first match" will be fined at least 250,000 Swiss francs ($320,800).
"Disciplinary sanctions may include the expulsion ÔÇőof the participating member association concerned from subsequent FIFA competitions and/or the replacement of the participating member association with another member association," FIFA's regulations say.
"The FIFA Council or the ÔÇîrelevant committee Ôüámay decide, in particular, to replace the member association in question with another association."
Iran had selected Tucson's sprawling Kino Sports Complex as the team's base camp and 18 months of preparation hang in the balance with a potential economic hit in Arizona.
There is also the question of tickets to World Cup games involving Iran. Should Iran boycott the tournament, Iranian fans who bought tickets for eye-watering prices may be tempted to sell them on the huge resale market .
Earlier, FIFA President Gianni Infantino said ÔÇőhe had met U.S. President Donald ÔüáTrump, who told him he welcomed Iran's participation in the World Cup.
Trump had previously said " I really don't care " if Iran participated or not, but Infantino said he had a productive discussion with the president.
"During the discussions, President Trump reiterated that ÔÇőthe Iranian team is, of course, welcome to compete in the tournament in the United States," Infantino said.
A ÔÇősource in Tehran familiar Ôüáwith the matter said that as well as Iran's decision not to attend the World Cup, warm-up games were not possible because of the war.
Earlier this week, Australia granted humanitarian visas to five Iranian women soccer players after they sought asylum, fearing persecution on their return home for their refusal to sing the national anthem at Ôüáa Women's ÔÇőAsian Cup match.
Trump had called on Australia to give asylum to members of the Iran ÔÇőwomen's soccer team.
On Wednesday, Australian police helped two more members of the Iranian women's soccer delegation slip their minders to claim asylum, but one has changed her mind and decided to go back to ÔÇőIran, the country's interior minister said.
($1 = 0.7793 Swiss francs)

item.title: Turkey's Erdogan says Iran war must stop before whole region dragged in
item.description:
ANKARA, March 11 (Reuters) - Turkish President Tayyip Erdogan ÔÇîsaid on Wednesday that the war in Iran must be stopped before the whole Middle East region is "thrown into the fire", adding that diplomacy must be given a chance.
NATO air defences have in the last week intercepted two ÔÇőIranian missiles headed toward southern Turkey, where there are air and radar bases used ÔÇőby NATO and the United States, and the alliance has since beefed up Ôüádefences there.
"This war must be stopped before it grows and the region as a whole ÔÇőis thrown into the fire. If diplomacy is given a chance, it is very possible to ÔÇőachieve this," Erdogan told lawmakers in parliament.
"In light of the sensitivity of the period we are in, we are speaking very carefully. We are acting with caution to protect Turkey from the fire that is surrounding ÔÇőit," he said, adding that Ankara was engaged with all sides in seeking an end to ÔÇőthe war.
Turkey had offered to mediate between the sides before the U.S.-Israeli strikes on Iran began 12 days ÔÇîago. ÔüáSince then, Erdogan has repeatedly called for diplomacy to end the conflict, while also warning that Turkey would respond to any threats to its national security.
Ozgur Ozel, chairman of Turkey's main opposition Republican People's Party (CHP) told Reuters in an interview on Wednesday that Erdogan had avoided criticising U.S. President ÔÇőDonald Trump by name ÔÇőin order to Ôüámaintain their good personal rapport, adding that this had only emboldened the U.S. leader.
Ankara's "shy silence regarding Trump's actions, and its failure to take a ÔÇőstance against that disproportionate force, is not neutrality," Ozel said, adding that ÔÇőthe U.S. Ôüáwas not taking Turkish sensitivities into account during the conflict.
Erdogan has criticised U.S., Israeli and Iranian actions and has called the U.S.-Israeli air campaign a "clear violation" of international law.
But in his comments, like many other world Ôüáleaders, he ÔÇőhas refrained from mentioning Trump by name. Erdogan's good ÔÇőpersonal ties with Trump have helped ease bilateral U.S.-Turkish tensions over the last year.

item.title: More than 330 arrested in Europol-led operation against illicit waste trade
item.description:
MADRID, March 11 (Reuters) - More than 330 people have been arrested across 70 countries in a ÔÇőcrackdown on organised crime networks accused ÔÇîof trafficking illicit waste within Europe and exporting it to Africa, Asia and Latin America, Europol said ÔÇőon Wednesday.
Authorities seized 127,149 metric tons ÔÇőof waste, 602 tons of polluting agents Ôüáalong with 75 tons of plant ÔÇőprotection chemicals, 2.3 tons of mercury and nearly ÔÇő10 million euros ($11.6 million) in cash and bank accounts.
The seized waste could have generated illicit profits of ÔÇőat least 31 million euros, officials said.
Illegal ÔÇőwaste trafficking has become a global problem, operating through ÔÇîparallel Ôüácriminal circuits, Spain's Guardia Civil, which coÔÇĹled the operation, said.
"These criminal networks not only fraudulently manage urban and industrial waste, but ÔÇőalso systematically resort ÔÇőto Ôüádocument forgery and fraud to move hazardous materials, causing negative environmental ÔÇőimpacts and risks to public health," ÔÇőit Ôüásaid.
Last year, Spanish police said they had arrested a gang suspected of smuggling more than 40,000 Ôüátons ÔÇőa year of untreated urban ÔÇőwaste from Italy to Spain.
($1 = 0.8627 euros)

item.title: How many ships have been attacked in the Gulf since start of Iran war?
item.description:
DUBAI, March 11 (Reuters) - The U.S.-Israeli war on Iran has threatened Gulf ports and disrupted global trade through the Strait of Hormuz through which roughly a fifth of the world's oil and liquefied natural gas normally passes.
Iran's Revolutionary Guards have warned that ÔÇőany ship passing through the narrow Strait will be targeted.
Below is a list of reported attacks since the ÔÇîwar started on February 28:
A crew member was killed on board Marshall IslandsÔÇôflagged crude tanker MKD VYOM after it was hit by a projectile as the vessel sailed off the Omani coast, 50 nautical miles north of the capital Muscat, manager V.Ships said.
Gibraltar-flagged oil bunkering tanker ÔÇőHercules Star, which supplies fuel to ships, was struck by a projectile 17 nautical miles northwest of UAE's ÔÇőMina Saqr in Ras Al Khaimah. A fire caused by the attack was extinguished, UKMTO said.
A ÔüáPalau-flagged tanker was attacked in the Strait of Hormuz, two nautical miles north of Oman's Kumzar. The crew of ÔÇőthe U.S.-sanctioned Skylight was evacuated, Oman's maritime security centre said.
U.S.-flagged products tanker Stena Imperative was struck by two projectiles in ÔÇőthe port of Bahrain, which resulted in a fire and the crew evacuating, UKMTO said.
Marshall Islands crude oil tanker Libra Trader and Panama-flagged bulker Gold Oak also sustained minor damage about 7-10 nautical miles off the UAE's port of Fujairah, UKMTO said.
Maltese-flagged container ship Safeen ÔÇőPrestige was damaged by a projectile as it sailed towards the top of the strait, two nautical miles north of ÔÇőOman. The attack caused a fire in the engine room and prompted its crew to abandon ship, according to shipping sources.
Crude oil ÔÇîtanker ÔüáSonangol Namibe was hit by a blast while anchored near Iraq's Khor al Zubair port, U.S. representative company Sonangol Marine Services said.
An Iranian remote-controlled boat laden with explosives was used to target and damage the Bahamas-flagged ship, according to initial assessments from two Iraqi port security sources.
A tugboat was hit by projectiles in the Strait of Hormuz, six nautical miles north ÔÇőof Oman, while conducting operations ÔÇőwith vessel Safeen Prestige, which Ôüáwas struck on March 4, UKMTO said.
UKMTO reported - citing a third party it did not name - a possible drone attack 10 nautical miles north of Saudi Arabia's Jubail. It ÔÇősaid the majority of the crew was evacuated.
Thailand-flagged bulk carrier Mayuree Naree was attacked ÔÇőby a projectile Ôüáin the Strait, 11 nautical miles north of Oman. A fire broke out on board and prompted the crew to evacuate, the vessel's owner Bangkok-headquartered Precious Shipping (PSL.BK) , opens new tab said in a statement.
Japan-flagged container ship One Majesty sustained minor damage after it was hit by Ôüáa projectile ÔÇő25 nautical miles northwest of UAE's Ras Al Khaimah.
The hull of Marshall ÔÇőIslands-flagged bulk carrier Star Gwyneth, was damaged after being hit by a projectile 50 nautical miles northwest of Dubai, maritime risk management company Vanguard said. ÔÇőThere was no environmental impact reported and the crew was reported safe, UKMTO said.

item.title: Trump tells Axios war in Iran will end soon, 'practically nothing left' to target
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 11 (Reuters) - U.S. President Donald Trump told Axios on Wednesday that ÔÇőthere's "practically nothing ÔÇőleft" to target in Iran Ôüáand that the war ÔÇőthere will end "soon."
"Little this ÔÇőand that... Any time I want it to ÔÇőend, it will ÔÇőend," Trump said during a ÔÇîbrief Ôüáphone interview.
The conflict began nearly two weeks ago with ÔÇőstrikes ÔÇőon ÔüáIran by the U.S. and ÔÇőIsrael aimed at ÔÇődismantling ÔüáIran's military capabilities and nuclear program.

item.title: UK releases first files on Mandelson's appointment as US ambassador
item.description:
LONDON, March 11 (Reuters) - The British government published the first documents covering the appointment of ÔÇőPeter Mandelson as ambassador to Washington on Wednesday, reviving questions about Prime Minister Keir Starmer's judgment in employing a man ÔÇîclose to Jeffrey Epstein.
The publication of the first tranche of documents covering the vetting of Mandelson, under police investigation for allegedly leaking government documents to the late sex offender, does little to reduce the pressure on Starmer, who is under fire over the appointment and a series of policy U-turns.
The more than 100 pages of documents ÔÇősuggested concerns were raised about the "reputational risks" in appointing Mandelson, particularly over his friendship with Epstein, but also his previous resignations ÔÇőfrom government and his support for closer ties with China.
Starmer sacked Mandelson from what is considered the most prestigious Ôüáposting in Britain's diplomatic service in September, when the depth of his friendship with the convicted sex offender started to become clear.
A document labelled "Advice to the prime minister, checks conducted on 4 December, 2024", said: "After Epstein was first convicted of procuring an underage girl in ÔÇő2008, their relationship continued across 2009-2011, beginning when Lord Mandelson was business minister and continuing after the end of the Labour government. Mandelson reportedly stayed in Epstein's House while he was in jail in June 2009."
In a summary of a fact-finding call between the prime minister's general counsel and the national security adviser, Jonathan Powell, in ÔÇőSeptember, a document said that Powell found the appointment process "unusual" and "weirdly rushed".
Powell said he had concerns about Mandelson's "reputationÔÇŁ in conversations with Morgan McSweeney, ÔÇőthen the prime minister's chief of staff. According to the note, Philip Barton, then the most senior government official at the foreign ministry, "also had reservations around the ÔÇîappointment".
Starmer's allies have sought to play down the importance of the first set of documents, saying an ongoing police investigation meant some of the more revealing exchanges were being withheld.
Further documents will be released at a later date, papers which the British prime minister's team say will prove Mandelson lied to Starmer about the extent of his relationship with Epstein before his appointment as ambassador in December 2024.
Darren Jones, Starmer's chief secretary, ÔÇőtold lawmakers last month the first ÔÇőtranche of documents would not Ôüáinclude any correspondence between Starmer's Downing Street office and Mandelson, when follow-up questions were asked, due to the probe.
Mandelson, a government minister when Labour was previously in power more than 15 years ago, quit the House ÔÇőof Lords, the upper house of parliament, in February over his links to Epstein. He was arrested ÔÇőlast month on suspicion of Ôüámisconduct in a public office, and later released on bail.
Files released by the U.S. Justice Department in January included emails suggesting Mandelson had leaked government documents to Epstein, and that the convicted offender had recorded payments to Mandelson or his then-partner, now husband.
Mandelson has said he does not recall having Ôüáreceived payments. ÔÇőHe has not commented publicly on allegations he leaked documents, and did not respond to ÔÇőmessages seeking comment.
Starmer has expressed regret over the appointment, saying the Labour veteran had created a "litany of deceit" about his ties to Epstein, an apology that did little to quieten ÔÇőopposition voices saying the prime minister should step down.

item.title: UNESCO fears for fate of historical sites during Iran war
item.description:
PARIS, March 11 (Reuters) - UNESCO said it is deeply concerned about the fate of world heritage sites in Iran and across the region, after Tehran's Golestan palace, often compared to ÔÇőVersailles, and a historic mosque and palace in Isfahan were damaged in the ÔÇîwar.
The United Nations' cultural agency on Wednesday urged all parties to protect the region's outstanding cultural sites, saying four of Iran's 29 world heritage sites had been damaged since the start of the U.S. and ÔÇőIsraeli war with Iran.
"UNESCO is deeply concerned by the first impact that the hostilities ÔÇőare already having on many world heritage sites," Lazare Eloundou Assomo, director Ôüáof the World Heritage Centre, told Reuters, adding he was also concerned for sites in ÔÇőIsrael, Lebanon and across the Middle East.
Tehran's Golestan palace, damaged in U.S.ÔÇôIsraeli strikes, is testimony ÔÇőto the grandeur of Iran's civilization in the 19th century, he said.
The palace was chosen as the Persian royal residence and seat of power by the Qajar family and shows the introduction of European styles in ÔÇőPersian arts, according to the UNESCO website. The last Shah of Iran, Mohammad Reza Pahlavi, ÔÇőheld a coronation ceremony there in 1969.
"We sometimes even compare it with the Versailles Palace in France, ÔÇîfor instance, Ôüáand it has suffered, unfortunately, some damage. We don't know the extent for the moment. But clearly, with the images that we have been able to receive, we can confirm ... it has been affected," Eloundou Assomo said.
Photos of the interior of the palace have shown piles ÔÇőof smashed glass and ÔÇőshards of wood Ôüáon the floor, and shattered woodwork.
Isfahan was one of Central Asia's most important cities and a key point on the Silk Road trading ÔÇőroute. Its Masjed-e Jame (Jameh Mosque) is more than 1,000 years old and ÔÇőshows the Ôüádevelopment of Islamic art through 12 centuries.
Buildings close to the buffer zone of the prehistoric sites of the Khorramabad Valley have also been damaged, UNESCO said.
UNESCO has shared coordinates of key cultural Ôüásites to ÔÇőall parties, Eloundou Assomo said, and was monitoring damage.
"We ÔÇőare calling for the protection of all sites of cultural significance ... everything that tells the history of all the ÔÇőcivilisations of the 18 countries in the region," he said.

item.title: Carney on verge of majority in Canada parliament after opposition MP defects
item.description:
OTTAWA, March 11 (Reuters) - Canadian Prime Minister Mark Carney on Wednesday moved closer to a parliamentary majority that could help ÔÇőhim counter U.S. tariffs when an opposition legislator joined the ÔÇîruling Liberals.
The centrist Liberals, governing with a minority after an election last April, need opposition support to pass key legislation such as budgets. That can be a ÔÇőslow process, and Carney has said he needs a majority ÔÇőto react more effectively to U.S. President Donald Trump's trade Ôüámeasures.
Carney said in a statement that Lori Idlout of the ÔÇősmall left-leaning New Democratic Party, who represents the Arctic territory of Nunavut, ÔÇőwould sit with the Liberals in the House of Commons, the elected chamber.
Idlout is the fourth opposition legislator to defect to the Liberals since last November and ÔÇőher decision means the party now has 170 seats in the ÔÇő343-seat House, two short of a majority.
Special elections will be held on April ÔÇî13 Ôüáin three vacant constituencies previously held by Liberals, two of which are safe seats. If the Liberals win two of the three, Carney would have control of the chamber.
A majority would open the way to ÔÇőCarney serving until ÔÇőOctober 2029. Canadian Ôüáelections are held no later than every four years.
"At this important moment in our history, Canadians are ÔÇőcoming together to build a strong future," said Carney, ÔÇőwho Ôüáhas promised to increase defense spending to better protect the vast and almost totally uninhabited Canadian Arctic.
ÔÇťWith new threats against our sovereignty and pressures Ôüáon ÔÇőthe wellbeing of people throughout the North, ÔÇőwe need a strong and ambitious government that makes decisions with Nunavut ÔÇö not only about ÔÇőNunavut," Idlout said in a statement.
(This story has been refiled to fix the hyperlink in paragraph 2)

item.title: Poland to seek help from two other countries in Epstein investigation
item.description:
WARSAW, March 11 (Reuters) - Poland will ask two other European countries for information and evidence needed for its investigation into human trafficking related to late U.S. sex offender Jeffrey Epstein, ÔÇőprosecutors said on Wednesday.
In a statement, they said documents from the Epstein ÔÇîfiles indicated a reasonable suspicion that human trafficking had taken place in Poland. They did not name the European countries they would contact but a source familiar with the matter told Reuters ÔÇőthe prosecutors would ask France and Sweden for help.
The U.S. Justice Department's release of ÔÇőmillions of internal documents related to Epstein has revealed the late financier Ôüáand sex offender's ties to many prominent people in politics, finance, academia and ÔÇőbusiness - both before and after he pleaded guilty in 2008 to prostitution charges.
In February, Polish ÔÇőPrime Minister Donald Tusk announced that Poland would launch an investigation into possible links between Epstein and Russian intelligence, as well as any offences affecting Polish citizens.
The Polish National Prosecutor's Office confirmed in its ÔÇőstatement that it had initiated an investigation into human trafficking committed in the period ÔÇőfrom 2009 to August 2019 on the territory of Poland and other countries.
Prosecutors suspect the trafficking consisted ÔÇîof Ôüárecruiting women and girls for work abroad under false pretences and of then transporting them outside Poland and handing them over to other people for sexual exploitation, the statement said.
Files reviewed by Reuters show that a man called Daniel Siad had informed Epstein ÔÇőabout his travels through ÔÇőPoland, Slovakia, the Czech ÔüáRepublic, among other countries, scouting for models.
He also mentioned his cooperation with Jean-Luc Brunel, a key suspect and longtime Epstein associate, ÔÇőwho died in a French prison in 2022.
According to Polish media ÔÇőreports, Siad Ôüáwas born in Algeria and moved to Sweden at the age of 23.
Reuters reached out to him on two phone numbers and an e-mail address found in the files, but Ôüáhas ÔÇőnot yet received answers to questions sent.
In February, Swedish ÔÇőnewspaper Expressen quoted Siad as saying he had never committed a crime and was open to talking to ÔÇőinvestigators in any interested country.

item.title: Higher oil clouds Wall Street's sunny earnings outlook
item.description:
ORLANDO, Florida, March 11 (Reuters) - U.S. companies face structurally higher oil prices this year even if the Iran war ends soon, meaning investors may need to rethink those sunny 2026 corporate earnings forecasts.
Heading into the year, the consensus 2026 outlook for oil was fairly bearish, while the earnings forecasts for Wall Street were pretty optimistic.
The latter ÔÇőhasn't changed. As of Friday, full-year 2026 earnings growth estimates, according to LSEG data, were nearly 16%, up from 14% last year and 12% the year before.
However, those ÔÇîrosy outlooks assume an average oil price this year close to $60 per barrel ÔÇô an expectation that disappeared into thin air with the U.S.-Israeli strikes on Iran on February 28 and subsequent supply disruption.
Oil market volatility has been wild. Crude recorded its biggest weekly rise on record last week, and rose further to almost $120 a barrel this week before plunging on hopes for a swift end to the conflict.
While oil will likely drift lower when the war eventually subsides, the damage is done. The finely tuned ÔÇőglobal energy system has been upended, infrastructure has been hit and the expected supply glut has evaporated.
Average oil prices this year will almost certainly be much higher than businesses were budgeting ÔÇőfor on January 1. Companies will absorb part of that increase, and consumers will certainly feel the pinch. Either way, corporate earnings will be squeezed.
In a Reuters poll in December, the consensus average 2026 forecast for Brent crude was $61.27 a barrel, with oversupply expected to offset possible supply disruption from the brewing U.S.-Iran tensions. That would have ÔÇőrepresented a 7% decline from the 2025 average of $68.20.
These forecasts have since been torn up.
Analysts at HSBC on Tuesday raised their average 2026 Brent forecast to $80 from $65 and their U.S. West Texas Intermediate crude outlook to $76 from $61. These ÔÇőare increases of 23% and 25%, respectively.
Also on Tuesday, the U.S. Energy Information Administration upped its average 2026 Brent crude forecast to $79 from $58 last month. That's a 36% increase.
Few sectors of the economy will escape the impact of energy price hikes like that. Gasoline, jet fuel, fertilizer, petrochemicals and plastics will all be more expensive, with pain felt in industries as diverse as transportation, manufacturing, metals, retail and food.
"As prices rise, consumption is affected, and, ultimately, corporate earnings erode," ÔÇősays Joe Brusuelas, chief economist at RSM US LLP.
Equity strategists at Goldman Sachs reckon the direct impact of "modestly" higher oil prices on S&P 500 earnings should be fairly muted, ÔÇőbut an extended period of supply disruption or uncertainty poses much greater risk to economic activity.
For every one percentage point decline in real U.S. GDP growth, S&P 500 earnings per share could fall 3-4%, they posit.
Other estimates suggest ÔÇîa 30% Ôüárise in oil prices could knock as much as 4% off S&P 500 earnings, with the pain felt most acutely in transportation, industrial, and consumer discretionary.
Consumer spending accounts for around 70% of U.S. economic activity. Sharply higher energy costs will squeeze household budgets, and spending elsewhere will suffer.
Alarm bells are already ringing. Average U.S. gasoline prices are now over $3.50 a gallon, according to American Automobile Association data. That's up 17% from before the war started.
There's a flip side, of course. Sustained higher oil prices boost energy sector profits, and double-digit earnings growth for the sector is not out of the question. But energy only accounts ÔÇőfor 4ÔÇô5% of total S&P 500 earnings and ÔÇőthus is unlikely to offset the hit to Ôüámargins elsewhere.
Within current 2026 earnings growth forecasts, there is incredibly wide dispersion among the 11 sectors.
As of Friday, the energy sector EPS had sunk to minus 1.2%, by far the gloomiest forecast and the only one pointing to declining profits.
At the other end of the scale, tech's ÔÇő2026 EPS growth estimate was 35.9%, comfortably the highest among all sectors and up from 30.8% on January 1. Tech has contributed the lion's ÔÇőshare of the S&P 500's Ôüáearnings growth in recent years.
But higher energy prices are a blow for the mega-cap firms leading the artificial intelligence arms race. UBS analysts estimate capex spending by the "hyperscalers" this year will hit $770 billion. The construction and running of those data centers could now get a lot more expensive.
Investors were already nervous about the future returns on these outlays. Significantly more expensive energy will only deepen their fears at a time when risks to Ôüámany other sectors ÔÇőare growing too.
(The opinions expressed here are those of Jamie McGeever , a columnist for Reuters)

item.title: UN warns global aid at risk as Middle East war spreads
item.description:
GENEVA, March 11 (Reuters) - The United Nations aid chief warned on Wednesday that the conflict in the Middle East is straining humanitarian operations worldwide, ÔÇődisrupting supply chains and slowing the delivery of lifeÔÇĹsaving assistance ÔÇîto numerous crisis zones.
ÔÇťWe are in a moment of grave peril for the Middle East and, actually I believe, for the wider world,ÔÇŁ Tom Fletcher, the U.N. aid ÔÇőchief, told Reuters.
The U.S.-Israeli war with Iran, which has expanded to ÔÇőLebanon and dragged in Gulf countries, has convulsed global markets Ôüáand disrupted supply chains , with airspace closures and the halt of shipping ÔÇőthrough the critical Strait of Hormuz .
Fletcher said aid supplies to Gaza and subÔÇĹSaharan Africa are ÔÇőbeing affected by the conflict, as humanitarian relief that needs to travel through the Strait of Hormuz or through airspace in the Gulf has been largely blocked or constrained.
Conflict-ridden ÔÇőSomalia, which is facing a major drought, and Sudan are among ÔÇőa number of countries facing dire humanitarian crises.
"These (constraints) will damage our humanitarian supply chains, reduce ÔÇîthe Ôüáhumanitarian supplies we can get to people who need them, but they'll also drive up energy costs and food costs across the region," Fletcher said.
"This really is a perfect storm of factors right now, and I'm seriously ÔÇőworried," he stated.
There ÔÇőis particular concern Ôüáfor aid supplies to subÔÇĹSaharan Africa, Flether said, which are being impacted by restricted movement through the Strait ÔÇőof Hormuz, with alternative routes slapped with rising freight costs.
Higher ÔÇőoil Ôüáprices are making the U.N.'s aid deliveries by air more expensive, at a time when UN agencies and NGOs budgets are already facing major donor cuts , Fletcher Ôüáexplained.
"We've ÔÇőjust about put some money together to keep ÔÇőmore of our UN humanitarian flights in the air, but they've suddenly, almost overnight, become ÔÇőmuch more expensive," Fletcher said.

item.title: Iran has laid about a dozen mines in Strait of Hormuz, sources say
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 11 (Reuters) - Iran has deployed about a dozen mines in the Strait of Hormuz, two sources familiar with the matter ÔÇősaid, in a move likely to complicate the reopening ÔÇîof the narrow waterway, an important route for shipping oil and liquefied natural gas.
Exports of oil and LNG through the strategic chokepoint along ÔÇőIran's coast have effectively been halted by the war ÔÇőlaunched 12 days ago by the United States and ÔüáIsrael, helping to drive a surge in world energy ÔÇőprices.
IranÔÇÖs military command on Wednesday said the world should be prepared ÔÇőfor oil to hit $200 a barrel.
One source said the locations of most of the mines are known but declined to say how the ÔÇőU.S. planned to deal with them.
CNN first reported the mining ÔÇőof the strait on Tuesday.
Iran has long threatened to retaliate against any ÔÇîmilitary Ôüáattack by mining the strait. About a fifth of global oil and LNG normally passes through the strait , and Tehran's ability to stop shipping through the channel gives it enormous leverage ÔÇőover the U.S. ÔÇőand its allies.
The ÔüáU.S. military says it has targeted Iranian mine-laying vessels, eliminating 16 of them on Tuesday. But ÔÇőthe U.S. Navy has so far declined to provide ÔÇőprotective Ôüáescorts to commercial ships through the strait.
U.S. President Donald Trump on Tuesday demanded that Iran immediately remove any mines deployed in Ôüáthe ÔÇőstrait and he said that it ÔÇőwould face unspecified military consequences if it failed to do so.

item.title: Trump administration considering looser protections for North Atlantic right whale
item.description:
March 3 (Reuters) - The Trump administration on Tuesday took the first ÔÇőstep toward loosening regulations ÔÇîthat protect the endangered North Atlantic right whale from ÔÇővessel strikes.

item.title: Spain's wild horses in peril, despite key role in wildfire prevention
item.description:
By Nacho Doce
Filed March 10, 2025, 10:41 a.m. GMT
Photography and reporting by Nacho Doce Reporting by Charlie Devereux
Filed: March 25, 2025, 8 a.m.┬áGMT
The wildfires that used to burn each summer in the woods above the Spanish village of Barro have diminished to almost none since Lucia Perez started grazing wild horses there.
ÔÇťThere used to be fires every year but since 2019 when we started coming here weÔÇÖve had one small fire in the first year and nothing since,ÔÇŁ said Perez, 37, explaining how by clearing the undergrowth between the trees the horses help stop fires igniting and spreading.
Fire prevention is one of several roles wild horses play in preserving the northwestern region of GaliciaÔÇÖs delicate ecosystems, scientists say. But EuropeÔÇÖs largest herd of wild horses has dwindled to less than half the 22,000 that roamed its mountains, forests and heathlands in the 1970s.
On the Serra da Groba heath 80 km (50 miles) southwest of Barro, a herd of wild horses feasts on the yellow flowers of gorse, ÔÇťselectively clearingÔÇŁ a plant that is especially combustible, said Laura Lagos, a researcher at the University of A Coruna.
This allows other plants such as purple-flowering heather and white asphodels to thrive, she said. It also helps preserve the heather around peat bogs, abundant in sphagnum mosses, one of the most effective ecosystems for capturing carbon.
A 2021 study by the University of A Coruna, in which Lagos took part, found that grazing heathlands by wild horses was best for preventing wildfires while promoting plant biodiversity and capturing carbon, when compared to other potential uses of the land such as planting long-term pine forests, short-term Eucalyptus forests or grazing by domestic animals.
ÔÇťStudies show that where wild horses graze the heathÔÇÖs biodiversity increases,ÔÇŁ Lagos said. ÔÇťThey are a fundamental part of the ecosystem. They benefit both the flora and the fauna associated with these open spaces.ÔÇŁ
Grazing by sheep and cows can also prevent fires, but the horses have particular physiological characteristics that allow them to thrive in GaliciaÔÇÖs terrain, including moustaches that appear designed to protect their lips from the prickly gorse.
ÔÇťThey seem specially adapted to eat gorse and are hardy - a cow canÔÇÖt live year round on the heath,ÔÇŁ Lagos said.
Neolithic rock carvings
Drier, hotter weather due to climate change is increasing the frequency of wildfires in Galicia. The region lost 96,900 hectares through fires between 2001 and 2023, according to Global Forest Watch.
This has coincided with the proliferation in Galicia of the Eucalyptus tree, a pyrophyte species that depends on fires to help it release and spread seeds. Eucalyptus trees also reduce pasture for the horses as only ferns grow under them.
Brought from Australia to Galicia by a missionary in the 19th century, they have spread thanks to demand for their wood from local pulp mills and now account for 28% of all trees in Galicia, according to a local government study.
In September, the village of San Colmede was spared from a fire that ripped through 270 hectares of Eucalyptus and scrub in the surrounding hills thanks to grazing by animals and by the native oaks, sweet chestnuts and birches that grow near the village and burn more slowly, acting as a shield, said firefighter Cesar Leiros.
ÔÇťWe saw the village surrounded by fire but thanks to native trees, the clearing we do, and the clearing our animals do, the village was saved,ÔÇŁ Leiros said.
Rock carvings depicting wild horses being hunted by men are evidence of their existence in Galicia dating back to the Neolithic period.
In time, humans and horses developed a relationship. Known as ÔÇťbesteirosÔÇŁ in the Galician dialect, people would look out for the health of the free-roaming horses in exchange for occasionally domesticating one or selling it for meat.
They would round up the horses once a year in events known as the ÔÇťrapa das bestasÔÇŁ or ÔÇťshearing of the beastsÔÇŁ to be deloused, vaccinated, and have their manes cut to make it harder for wolves to catch them.
These evolved into festivals, with the most famous in Sabucedo drawing thousands of tourists who come to see the horses wrestled to the ground to be treated.
ÔÇśEcological jewelÔÇÖ
Perez, who works in a school, runs a smaller rapa with her stonemason husband Niko Souto, 41, in the village of Amil. They donÔÇÖt make a profit, she said. The proceeds help pay for fencing to stop the horses wandering onto roads or farmland.
Costs, including vet fees, insurance, fencing, microchipping and GPS equipment for tracking, are multiplying, Souto said.
The horses are classified as livestock and Javier Alvarez-Blazquez, a retired lawyer who owns ten horses in the Serra da Groba, wants new legislation that recognises their unique circumstances.
The Galician regional government should help besteiros cover some of the costs of looking after them, he said. The sale of a foal can generate 60 euros ($65.08) but it doesnÔÇÖt compensate for expenses - excluding insurance and erecting fencing - of about 100 euros a year per horse, he said.
Increasing the number of horses could even save the government money, said Melina Barrio, an economist at the University of Alcala. She estimates that each horse carries out clearing to prevent fires that would cost between 8,000 and 10,000 euros per year if humans did it.
GaliciaÔÇÖs regional government acknowledged the role wild horses play in preventing forest fires and preserving ecosystems, but said that the current legislation was adequate for their conservation because the horses can generate an income by being sold or through tourism from the rapa festivals.
ÔÇťAlthough these animals are bred in the wild, in reality they have an owner and there is an economic benefit,ÔÇŁ the government said in an emailed statement.
ÔÇťIn this sense, it is logical to consider horses as livestock, in accordance with European and Spanish legislation on the matter, and therefore they are not exempt from complying with obligations relating to health, animal welfare or herd control,ÔÇŁ it said.
Besteiros receive some help on costs, such as with the implanting of microchips for identification, the government added.
Carlos Souto, NikoÔÇÖs father, said the horses were descending from the hills more frequently in search of pasture as Eucalyptus plantations spread, meaning they needed more minding.
From visiting his horses once a week he is now going every morning, while Niko and Lucia go in the evening after work.
Niko said the horses were becoming used to their presence and their behaviour was changing - they were becoming less wild.
Alvarez-Blazquez said without the besteiros, the horses would die out, killed by cars or local farmers angry at the horses transgressing onto their crops and tree plantations.
ÔÇťWe are guardians doing it as a hobby. Our only aim is to preserve this animal that we believe is an ecological jewel,ÔÇŁ he said.
The Wider Image
Photography and reporting: Nacho Doce
Reporting and writing: Charlie Devereux
Photo editing and design: Marta Montana and Maye-E Wong
Text editing: Janet Lawrence and Sharon Singleton
LICENSE THIS STORY
Reuters, the news and media division of Thomson Reuters, is the worldÔÇÖs largest multimedia news provider, reaching billions of people worldwide every day. Reuters provides business, financial, national and international news to professionals via desktop terminals, the world's media organizations, industry events and directly to consumers.

item.title: South Africa says it has asked US ambassador to explain 'undiplomatic remarks'
item.description:
JOHANNESBURG, March 11 (Reuters) - South Africa has summoned the U.S. ambassador ÔÇőto the country Leo Brent Bozell III ÔÇîto explain recent "undiplomatic remarks", Foreign Affairs Minister Ronald Lamola said on Wednesday.
On Tuesday, Bozell caused ÔÇőa public backlash by criticising "Kill the ÔÇőBoer", a South African liberation chant Ôüáthat a court had previously ruled was ÔÇőnot hate speech.
At a conference on Tuesday, ÔÇőBozell said: "IÔÇÖm sorry, I donÔÇÖt care what your courts say. ItÔÇÖs hate speech." Later, in a ÔÇőpost on social media platform X, he ÔÇősaid that the U.S. government respected the independence and ÔÇîfindings Ôüáof South Africa's judiciary.
Lamola said in a briefing on Wednesday: "While South Africa welcomes active public diplomacy and the strengthening of ÔÇőbilateral ties, ÔÇőwe emphasize Ôüáthat such engagements must remain consistent with established diplomatic etiquette ÔÇőand international protocols."
Bozell arrived in Pretoria as ÔÇőU.S. ÔüáPresident Donald Trump's envoy last month. He has said he intended to communicate Washington's Ôüádispleasure ÔÇőwith what he called ÔÇőSouth Africa's "geostrategic drift... toward our competitors", including Iran.

item.title: Albania's highest court says one-year ban of TikTok was unconstitutional
item.description:
TIRANA, March 11 (Reuters) - Albania's Constitutional Court ruled on Wednesday that the government's one-year ban of TikTok, which ÔÇőexpired last month, was unconstitutional and violated ÔÇîfreedom of speech.
The ban, imposed last year and initially linked to the death of a teenage boy after ÔÇőbullying online, saw Chinese-owned short video platform ÔÇőTikTok banned for all users in the country. ÔüáAt the time TikTok said the clash ÔÇődid not originate on their platform.
The ban expired on ÔÇőFebruary 5, 2026, and the government cited new "safety mechanisms" implemented by TikTok.
In its ruling on Wednesday, the Constitutional Court said ÔÇőthe government had not sufficiently examined the ÔÇőrepercussions of implementing a ban and had not "convincingly proven that ÔÇîa Ôüágeneral blocking of the platform constitutes the only effective and necessary means to achieve the intended objective."
It also said that the ban violated freedom of ÔÇőthe press ÔÇőand freedom Ôüáof expression.
The ruling was issued in response to a motion filed by ÔÇőthe Association of Journalists of Albania (AGSH) and ÔÇőBalkan ÔüáInvestigative Reporting Network (BIRN) last March.
Opposition parties accused the government of trying to silence them by banning the Ôüáplatform ÔÇőweeks before parliamentary elections last ÔÇőyear, which the government denies.

item.title: What are key findings from UK's release of Mandelson documents?
item.description:
LONDON, March 11 - The British government disclosed on Wednesday the first tranche of documents relating to Peter Mandelson's time as ambassador to the United States, including what was known at the time about his friendship ÔÇőwith the late sex offender Jeffrey Epstein.
More documents are expected in the coming weeks, with ÔÇîPrime Minister Keir Starmer's government not wishing to prejudice an ongoing police investigation.
Here are some details from the first release of documents.
In a note labelled "Advice to the prime minister", a due diligence checklist said Mandelson's relationship with ÔÇőEpstein posed a "reputational risk".
The document, which said it was based on checks conducted on December 4, ÔÇő2024, said: "After Epstein was first convicted of procuring an underage girl in 2008, Ôüátheir relationship continued across 2009-2011, beginning when Lord Mandelson was Business Minister and continuing after the end ÔÇőof the Labour government".
"Mandelson reportedly stayed in Epstein's house while he was in jail in June 2009," ÔÇőthe note said.
Alongside the due diligence checklist, a briefing note on the decision added that Starmer's then-Chief of Staff Morgan McSweeney had discussed Mandelson's relationship with Epstein but Director of Communications Matthew Doyle had been satisfied with Mandelson's responses to ÔÇőquestions about contact.
Further correspondence and follow-up questions to Mandelson about the relationship were not part of this ÔÇődisclosure.
Government officials told Starmer that he would be "exposed" if anything went wrong if he chose to make a political ÔÇîappointment Ôüásuch as Mandelson.
"If anything goes wrong, you could be more exposed as the individual is more connected to you personally," the officials said prior to the appointment.
Following the allegations about Mandelson's ties to Epstein which led to his dismissal last September, Downing Street began a "fact-finding" mission to establish what happened around his appointment.
In ÔÇőa summary of a ÔÇőfact-finding call between Starmer's Ôüágeneral counsel and the government's National Security Adviser Jonathan Powell in September, it says that Powell found the appointment process "unusual" and "weirdly rushed".
Powell said he had concerns ÔÇőabout Mandelson's "reputation" in conversations with McSweeney.
According to the note Philip Barton, then the ÔÇőmost senior Ôüágovernment official at the Foreign Office, "also had reservations around the appointment".
Documents were published on discussions over Mandelson's severance pay after he was fired. Officials said these began with a request from Mandelson to be paid the Ôüáremainder ÔÇőof "the 4-year salary costs of the fixed term appointment", amounting ÔÇőto 547,201 pounds ($733,523), the document said.
The government eventually approved 34,670 pounds in discretionary payments plus 40,330 pounds he was legally entitled to, ÔÇőtotalling 75,000 pounds.
($1 = 0.7460 pounds)

item.title: Norway police apprehend three suspects in U.S. embassy bombing
item.description:
OSLO, March 11 (Reuters) - Norwegian ÔÇőpolice said on ÔÇîWednesday they had apprehended three suspects following Sunday's bombing ÔÇőat the U.S. ÔÇőembassy in Oslo.
The powerful Ôüáearly morning blast from ÔÇőan improvised explosive device (IED) ÔÇődamaged the entrance to the embassy's consular section but ÔÇőcaused no injuries, Norwegian ÔÇőauthorities have said.
"They are suspected ÔÇîof Ôüáa terror bombing," Police Attorney Christian Hatlo told reporters.
Images of one ÔÇőof ÔÇőthe Ôüásuspects released by police on Monday ÔÇőshowed a hooded ÔÇőperson, Ôüáwhose face was not visible, wearing dark clothes Ôüáand ÔÇőcarrying a ÔÇőbag or rucksack.

item.title: Historic oil reserve release is only a band-aid on a gaping supply shock
item.description:
LONDON, March 11 (Reuters) - The International Energy AgencyÔÇÖs plan to release 400 million barrels of oil reserves is unprecedented in scale and desperately needed to blunt the devastating supply shock triggered by the Iran war . But it will offer only limited relief as long as energy exports from the Middle East remain blocked.
The IEA said ÔÇőon Wednesday that its 32 member countries unanimously agreed to move forward with the biggest collective drawdown ever from their strategic petroleum reserves (SPR).
The release is more than double ÔÇîthe scale of the previous ÔÇô and until now, largest-ever ÔÇô coordinated drawdown in March 2022 following RussiaÔÇÖs invasion of Ukraine , when the U.S. released 180 million barrels .
Yet the enormity of the crisis confronting the global oil market today certainly warrants these record-breaking volumes ÔÇô if not more. Nearly 20 million barrels per day (bpd) of supply ÔÇô roughly a fifth of global output ÔÇô have been trapped inside the Gulf since the effective closure of the Strait of Hormuz shortly after the launch of the joint IsraelÔÇĹU.S. war against Iran ÔÇőon February 28.
Measured against that disruption, the announced release looks a lot less impressive. After only 11 days of conflict, the current market deficit has already reached roughly 220 million barrels. Saudi Arabia ÔÇőand the United Arab Emirates are working to divert some exports to ports outside the Gulf, which should provide additional relief, but those flows remain limited Ôüáand vulnerable.
The duration of the Iran conflict and the continued closure of the Strait of Hormuz are therefore critical in determining whether the release can meaningfully stabilise markets or merely slow the pace of ÔÇődamage.
The IEA has coordinated emergency stock releases only four times since it was created by OECD members in the midÔÇĹ1970s in response to the 1973 OPEC oil embargo. That crisis caused severe fuel ÔÇőshortages and a quadrupling of oil prices.
The infrequency underscores the gravity of the current situation ÔÇô but also potentially the limits of the tool being deployed.
Several key details of the release have yet to be announced, and they are crucial to assessing its likely impact.
First, the pace of the drawdown is unknown. The IEA said that members will determine the timing of their release and, more importantly, how quickly oil will actually reach the market.
JPMorgan estimates that a coordinated ÔÇőSPR release could flow at a maximum rate of about 1.2 million bpd, based on past precedents. The 2022 drawdown equated to roughly 1 million bpd. At those rates, the release would offset only a ÔÇősmall fraction of the current supply disruption, leaving the market heavily undersupplied.
Where the oil is released matters almost as much as the quantity. The region most heavily hit by the supply shock so far is Asia , ÔÇîwhich relies on Ôüáthe Gulf for about 60% of its oil imports. Refiners there have already started cutting operating rates, while several countries have begun rationing fuel to conserve dwindling stocks.
Japan, which holds the secondÔÇĹlargest strategic reserves in the OECD, said it will release around 80 million barrels from both state and private inventories, equivalent to 45 days of supply, starting as early as March 16. That should provide Japanese refiners with rapid, tangible relief.
The U.S., which has yet to spell out its contribution, will almost certainly account for the largest share of the release. Its SPR stands at about 415 million barrels stored in dozens of salt caverns along the ÔÇőGulf Coast. ThatÔÇÖs less than 60% of total ÔÇőcapacity of roughly 713 million barrels, according Ôüáto Energy Information Administration data.
For refiners in Asia, however, the release of U.S. barrels might not be that helpful. A tanker journey from the U.S. Gulf Coast to Asia takes 40 to 60 days depending on the route, more than twice as long as shipments from the Middle East.
Those delays will be ÔÇőcompounded by sharply higher freight costs. The recent surge in tanker rates due to the conflict adds an extra $10 to $12 per barrel for a ÔÇővoyage from the U.S. Gulf ÔüáCoast to Singapore ÔÇô around 15% of the cargoÔÇÖs value ÔÇô compared with just 2% to 3% before the war, according to Reuters calculations.
There is also a longerÔÇĹterm cost. A release of this magnitude will drain a substantial share of the worldÔÇÖs emergency reserves, leaving consuming nations with a far thinner cushion should the conflict drag on or escalate further.
The SPR release will unquestionably offer the global oil market vital breathing room as governments Ôüágrapple with the ÔÇőthreat of surging inflation, economic slowdown and social strain.
But without a rapid resumption of energy flows from the Middle East, ÔÇőeven this historic intervention could quickly become a drop in the ocean.
(The opinions expressed here are those of Ron Bousso , a columnist for Reuters.)

item.title: "We are having a tremendous impact," Trump says in G7 call after IEA decision on oil stocks
item.description:
PARIS, March 11 (Reuters) - U.S. President Donald Trump praised the "tremendous impact" of decisions ÔÇőbeing made on Wednesday as ÔÇîleaders of the Group of Seven Nations met to discuss war in Iran ÔÇőand its economic consequences, ÔÇőaccording to a short videoclip shared Ôüáby the French presidency.
"I think ÔÇőwe are having a tremendous impact, ÔÇőunbelievable actually, on the world," Trump said, after being given the floor by French ÔÇőPresident Emmanuel Macron, who chaired ÔÇőthe G7 meeting. It was not immediately ÔÇîclear Ôüáwhether Trump was talking about the release of oil stocks decided minutes earlier.
Macron and Trump were speaking ÔÇőafter a ÔÇőrecommendation Ôüáby the International Energy Agency (IEA) to release 400 million ÔÇőbarrels of oil, the ÔÇőlargest Ôüásuch move in the IEA history, to try to restrain soaring crude Ôüáprices ÔÇőamid the U.S.-Israeli ÔÇőwar with Iran.

item.title: China scientists rush to climate-proof potatoes
item.description:
By Florence Lo
Filed Nov. 18, 2024, 5:06 p.m. GMT
Photography and reporting by┬áFlorence Lo
Reporting by Mei Mei Chu and ┬áXiaoyu Yin
Filed November 27, 2024, 11:00 p.m.┬áGMT
In a research facility in the northwest of Beijing, molecular biologist Li Jieping and his team harvest a cluster of seven unusually small potatoes, one as tiny as a quailÔÇÖs egg, from a potted plant.
Grown under conditions that simulate predictions of higher temperatures at the end of the century, the potatoes provide an ominous sign of future food security.
At just 136 grams (4.8 oz), the tubers weigh less than half that of a typical potato in China, where the most popular varieties are often twice the size of a baseball.
China is the worldÔÇÖs biggest producer of potatoes, which are crucial to global food security because of their high yield relative to other staple crops.
But they are particularly vulnerable to heat, and climate change, driven by fossil fuel emissions, is pushing temperatures to dangerous new heights while also worsening drought and flooding.
With an urgent need to protect food supplies, Li, a researcher at the International Potato Center (CIP) in Beijing, is leading a three-year study into the effects of higher temperatures on the vegetable. His team is focusing on ChinaÔÇÖs two most common varieties.
ÔÇťI worry about what will happen in the future,ÔÇŁ Li said. ÔÇťFarmers will harvest fewer potato tubers, it will influence food security.ÔÇŁ
LiÔÇÖs team grew their crop over three months in a walk-in chamber set at 3 degrees Celsius above the current average temperature in northern Hebei and Inner Mongolia, the higher altitude provinces where potatoes are usually grown in China.
Their research, published in the journal Climate Smart Agriculture this month, found the higher temperatures accelerated tuber growth by 10 days, but cut potato yields by more than half.
Under current climate policies, the world is facing as much as 3.1 C of warming above pre-industrial levels by 2100, according to a United Nations report released in October.
In Inner Mongolia, dozens of workers clutching white sacks rush to gather potatoes dug up from the soil before the next downpour.
ÔÇťThe biggest challenge for potatoes this year is the heavy rain,ÔÇŁ said manager Wang Shiyi. ÔÇťIt has caused various diseases... and greatly slowed down the harvest progress.ÔÇŁ
Meanwhile, seed potato producer Yakeshi Senfeng Potato Industry Company has invested in aeroponic systems where plants are grown in the air under controlled conditions.
Farmers are increasingly demanding potato varieties that are higher-yielding and less susceptible to disease, particularly late blight, which caused the Irish Potato Famine of the mid-19th century and thrives in warm and humid conditions.
ÔÇťSome new and more aggressive (late blight) strains have begun to appear, and they are more resistant to traditional prevention and control methods,ÔÇŁ said general manager Li Xuemin, explaining the Inner Mongolia-based companyÔÇÖs strategy.
The research by CIP, which is headquartered in Lima, is part of a collaborative effort with the Chinese government to help farmers adapt to the warmer, wetter conditions.
In the greenhouse outside Li's lab, workers swab pollen on white potato flowers to develop heat-tolerant varieties.
Li says Chinese farmers will need to make changes within the next decade, planting during spring instead of the start of summer, or moving to even higher altitudes to escape the heat.
ÔÇťFarmers have to start preparing for climate change,ÔÇŁ Li said. ÔÇťIf we donÔÇÖt find a solution, they will make less money from lower yields and the price of potatoes may rise.ÔÇŁ
A Chinese flag flutters next to sacks of newly harvested potatoes, at a farm under Hebei Jiuen Agricultural Development Company, in Xilingol League, Inner Mongolia, China, September 24, 2024. REUTERS/Florence Lo
Workers load a lorry with sacks of newly harvested potatoes at a farm under Hebei Jiuen Agricultural Development Company, in Xilingol League, Inner Mongolia, China, September 24, 2024. REUTERS/Florence Lo
Sprinklers on a centre pivot irrigation system water growing potato plants, at a farm under Yakeshi Senfeng Potato Industry Company, in Old Barag Banner, Inner Mongolia, China, June 17, 2024. REUTERS/Florence Lo
Potato tubers dug up by harvesters lie on the ground, at a farm under Hebei Jiuen Agricultural Development Company, in Xilingol League, Inner Mongolia, China, September 24, 2024. REUTERS/Florence Lo
Researcher Li Yafei rinses a potato plant, before separating the tubers and weighing them, at a research facility under the CIP, in the Yanqing district, Beijing, China, May 14, 2024. REUTERS/Florence Lo
Lab workers sort vessels with potato plantlets as they prepare them for propagation, in a tissue culture laboratory at a research facility under the CIP, in the Yanqing district, Beijing, China, April 17, 2024. REUTERS/Florence Lo
Potato plantlets propagated in a laboratory lie on the potting mix as workers transplant them in a greenhouse, at a research facility under the CIP, in the Yanqing district, Beijing, China, May 20, 2024. REUTERS/Florence Lo
A lab worker holds potato plantlets propagated in a laboratory to transplant them in a greenhouse, at a research facility under the CIP, in the Yanqing district, Beijing, China, May 20, 2024. REUTERS/Florence Lo
Researcher Li Yafei sets up a device to measure the photosynthesis rates of potato plants grown inside a heat chamber, at a research facility under the CIP, in the Yanqing district, Beijing, China, April 2, 2024. REUTERS/Florence Lo
Potato plants grow in a pot at a research facility under the CIP, in the Yanqing district, Beijing, China, May 14, 2024. REUTERS/Florence Lo
A worker adjusts the hose as she waters potato plantlets transplanted from a laboratory, in a greenhouse at a research facility under the CIP, in the Yanqing district, Beijing, China, May 20, 2024. REUTERS/Florence Lo
Researchers transport a cart of potato plantlets to a greenhouse, at a research facility under the CIP, in the Yanqing district, Beijing, China, May 20, 2024. REUTERS/Florence Lo
A worker places clasps on potato plants to help them grow vertically, at a greenhouse under Yakeshi Senfeng Potato Industry Company, in Yakeshi, Inner Mongolia, China, June 15, 2024. REUTERS/Florence Lo
Potato flowers grow in a greenhouse at a research facility under the CIP, in the Yanqing district, Beijing, China, May 20, 2024. REUTERS/Florence Lo
Workers pack potato plants into containers to transplant them to the field, in a greenhouse at a farm under Yakeshi Senfeng Potato Industry Company, in Yakeshi, Inner Mongolia, China, June 17, 2024. REUTERS/Florence Lo
Workers sort sacks of seed potatoes to be distributed to other facilities, in a warehouse at a research facility under the CIP, in the Yanqing district, Beijing, China, April 2, 2024. REUTERS/Florence Lo
Workers rest on mats placed outside a greenhouse during their lunch break, at a research facility under the CIP, in the Yanqing district, Beijing, China, May 20, 2024. REUTERS/Florence Lo
Workers sit in a bus for a lunch break Workers sit in a bus for a lunch break, after their work to transplant greenhouse-grown potato plants to the field was disrupted by the rain, at a farm under Yakeshi Senfeng Potato Industry Company in Yakeshi, Inner Mongolia, China, June 16, 2024. REUTERS/Florence Lo
The Wider Image
Photography: Florence Lo
Reporting: Mei Mei Chu, Florence Lo, Xiaoyu Yin
Writing: Mei Mei Chu

Text editing: Kate Mayberry
Design: Marta Montana Gomez
LICENSE THIS STORY
Amid IsraelÔÇÖs military assault, the worldÔÇÖs hunger monitor has struggled to access key data to determine whether conditions in Gaza constitute a famine.
OnlyFans is among the world's most successful social media platforms ÔÇô but a secretive one. Reuters traces its journey from an obscure, porn-free site to an adults-only phenomenon turbocharged by erotic performers and influencers.
ItÔÇÖs a simple but brutal equation: The number of people going hungry or otherwise struggling around the world is rising, while the amount of money the worldÔÇÖs wealthiest nations are contributing toward helping them is dropping.
Multiple OnlyFans accounts featured suspected child sex abuse, investigator reports.
Reuters, the news and media division of Thomson Reuters, is the worldÔÇÖs largest multimedia news provider, reaching billions of people worldwide every day. Reuters provides business, financial, national and international news to professionals via desktop terminals, the world's media organizations, industry events and directly to consumers.

item.title: BuffettÔÇÖs Berkshire Hathaway operates the dirtiest set of coal-fired power plants in the US
item.description:
In his letter to investors last year, Berkshire Hathaway Chairman Warren Buffett urged readers to come ÔÇťinhale the air, drink the waterÔÇŁ and attend the companyÔÇÖs annual shareholder meeting in Omaha, his hometown.
The letter, a document pored over by shareholders, analysts and press every year for insights into the legendary investorÔÇÖs thinking, playfully sought to link the success of his conglomerate with the setting of the heartland city, in eastern Nebraska along the Iowa border.
One detail omitted: Federal emissions data show that OmahaÔÇÖs air quality ranks in the bottom third of U.S. cities, fouled in part by coal-fired power plants owned by Berkshire in neighboring Iowa. A modeling tool of the Environmental Protection Agency, or EPA, shows that pollution from the plants each year causes up to seven premature deaths, $104 million in healthcare expenses and 1,800 lost school days in the metropolitan area of Omaha, with nearly 1 million people.
The calculations, performed by Reuters using the EPAÔÇÖs modeling data for 2023, were reviewed and confirmed by three outside emissions scientists consulted for this report. The scientists are all independent research experts with no affiliation with the news agency or the electricity industry.
The air over Omaha, and other areas near BerkshireÔÇÖs dozen coal-fired power plants nationwide, casts a pall over the environmental record of a company that touts itself at the vanguard of clean energy. Despite investments to date of $41 billion in renewable energies, mostly wind and solar power, Berkshire operates the dirtiest corporate coal fleet in the U.S. by at least one important measure, a Reuters investigation shows.
BerkshireÔÇÖs coal plant fleet, operated by three utility companies it has acquired in recent decades, emits more nitrogen oxides than any other coal fleet in the country, federal emissions data show. Reuters analyzed the most recent full calendar year of EPA emissions data available for coal plants, from 2023.
Known as NOx, nitrogen oxides are poisonous gases and key components of smog and haze. They help form lung-damaging ozone and tiny soot particles that can cause respiratory illness and premature death in communities up to hundreds of miles away.
Berkshire has lobbied regulators and filed lawsuits to avoid installing expensive pollution controls, known as SCR scrubbers, that can reduce NOx and that are more commonly employed at coal plants owned by rivals. And the company has said it plans to operate some of its coal-fired plants ÔÇô aging, decades-old facilities in Iowa, Nevada, Utah and Wyoming ÔÇô for years after many of its corporate competitors plan to shut theirs, company disclosures show.
In an email response to detailed questions from Reuters, Berkshire said its electric utilities operate their coal plants ÔÇťin full compliance with state and federal environmental laws, regulations and requirements.ÔÇŁ The company said it hasnÔÇÖt employed more SCR scrubbers because it is ÔÇťan expensive technology for our customers,ÔÇŁ but that it has reduced emissions through other methods, retired some coal plants early and converted others to gas. Berkshire didnÔÇÖt address the toll its coal plants have exacted on the health of residents affected by their emissions.
Its efforts to reduce pollution, the company added, have sharply cut NOx emissions from its electricity portfolio over the last two decades. Coal power now accounts for only 22% of BerkshireÔÇÖs power generation, it said, down from 71% in 2005. Berkshire said it plans to exit coal power entirely by 2049.
The Reuters analysis used federal emissions data ÔÇô submitted to the EPA by plant operators themselves ÔÇô to determine the NOx pollution produced by BerkshireÔÇÖs coal fleet and compare it against industry peers. Reporters also used an EPA tool known as Co-Benefits Risk Assessment, or COBRA, to see how BerkshireÔÇÖs coal plant pollution affects the health of communities.
Widely used by scientists and regulators, COBRA assigns an economic value to pollution variables, calculating the economic and public health costs of contaminated air. Reuters also reviewed thousands of pages of regulatory and corporate documents and interviewed more than three dozen energy and medical experts as well as people living near BerkshireÔÇÖs plants.
The examination found:
Abel declined to comment for this story.
The EPA didnÔÇÖt respond to requests from Reuters for comment on the health tolls of emissions from BerkshireÔÇÖs plants.
Despite advances in renewable energy across the U.S., electricity generation still creates significant costs for the environment and public health. At a time of surging electricity demand because of intense heat waves, power-hungry data centers for artificial intelligence and the ever-growing needs of the U.S. economy, BerkshireÔÇÖs resistance to clean up its coal-fired plants worries doctors and environmental scientists.
ÔÇťWarren Buffett is making a multi-layered environmental mess heÔÇÖs not being required to clean up,ÔÇŁ said Brian Moench, a doctor whose group, Utah Physicians for a Healthy Environment, has studied the impact of BerkshireÔÇÖs coal plants.
Known for his folksy demeanor, frugal lifestyle and generous philanthropy, Buffet over more than half a century built Berkshire into a champion of American industry. Its trillion-dollar portfolio, forged through acquisitions such as the power plants, ranges from railways to insurance, fast food to real estate. Buffett, 94, is known for his hands-off approach to Berkshire companies, allowing managers to navigate the disparate industry and regulatory environments each confronts.
BerkshireÔÇÖs late vice chairman, Charlie Munger, told shareholders at the 2019 annual meeting that ÔÇťthe environmental stuff is done one level down from us. I think Greg Abel is just terrific at it.ÔÇŁ Pushing back at the time against shareholder proposals for Berkshire to share more information with the public about its emissions, Munger added: ÔÇťWe are not going to do reports like everyone else.ÔÇŁ
The energy policies are likely to outlast BuffetÔÇÖs tenure. Abel, who has led energy operations since the early 2000s, is widely expected to succeed the billionaire at BerkshireÔÇÖs helm.
Electricity has been fundamental to modern life and economic growth for more than a century. The risks and costs associated with its production are an afterthought for some users. As with cars and industrialized foods, convenience often outweighs concerns about downsides, particularly because much of the toll on health happens slowly and not always in plain sight.
ÔÇťWe all know coal plants have this continuous impact that is going to elevate the risk of asthma, heart attacks and death,ÔÇŁ said Elena Krieger, who oversees scientific research at PSE Healthy Energy, a California-based policy institute. ÔÇťBut it is very, very difficult to pinpoint a single death. And that makes it very hard to point the finger at any given facility.ÔÇŁ
The harm is considerable.
According to ReutersÔÇÖ COBRA analysis, the roughly 200 coal plants still operating in the U.S. each year cause up to 8,400 premature deaths and $130 billion in excess healthcare costs. BerkshireÔÇÖs coal plants are estimated to cause up to 260 deaths and $3.9 billion in health expenditures, the model shows. ReutersÔÇÖ analysis, Krieger said, offers ÔÇťa reasonable portrait of the magnitude of the health impacts from these coal plants.ÔÇŁ
The news agencyÔÇÖs COBRA calculations were also reviewed by Nicholas Mailloux, an expert in the health effects of energy systems at the University of Wisconsin. Tim Canty, a University of Maryland professor who is an expert on pollution at coal plants, reviewed ReutersÔÇÖ calculations of NOx emissions.
NOx, among the worst air pollutants, is a precursor for hazardous ground-level ozone and fine particulate matter. Wildfires and vehicle tailpipes are also large sources of NOx, which can travel over 1,000 miles to cause faraway smog and soot. The particulate pollution NOx helps form can penetrate the bloodstream and brain, contributing to respiratory issues, cancers and other illnesses. Children are particularly vulnerable.
Joel Schwartz, a Harvard University epidemiologist who studies pollution and human health, said there is no known safe threshold for exposure to soot particles formed from NOx. ÔÇťItÔÇÖs affecting systems that do bad things all over the human body,ÔÇŁ he told Reuters.
Coal plants are also big emitters of climate-warming carbon dioxide, or CO2, a gas not removed by SCR scrubbers. A research report from the University of Massachusetts-Amherst last year found that Berkshire ranked fourth among the top CO2 emitting companies ÔÇô from any industry ÔÇô in the U.S. The report relied on data from 2022.
Unlike many big industrial firms, Berkshire doesnÔÇÖt issue annual reports breaking out its company-wide CO2 emissions. The company says it is aiming for net zero greenhouse gas emissions by 2050.
Coal-fired electricity is largely in its twilight in the U.S.
The countryÔÇÖs remaining coal plants generate about 16% of its electricity. ThatÔÇÖs almost twice the level of the European Union, which has more aggressively pursued renewable energy. But itÔÇÖs a fraction of the 52% reliance on coal in 1990, when the U.S. had nearly 500 coal plants, according to data from the U.S. Energy Information Administration and from Eurelectric, a Brussels-based industry group.
Wind and natural gas, now the leading fuel for U.S. power generation, account for much of BerkshireÔÇÖs electrical output. But the company still relies on coal for over a fifth of its generation, according to company data.
Berkshire recently slowed some initiatives to wean itself further.
In Utah, its PacifiCorp unit, after announcing plans to invest heavily in renewable energy, last year changed course and said it would prolong the life of its two coal plants there. In an April statement, PacifiCorp said slower-than-expected changes to regulation made it attractive to do so.
ÔÇťThe twilight of coal seems to be turning into an odd sunrise,ÔÇŁ said Ulla Reeves, director of the clean air program at the National Parks Conservation Association, one of several environmental groups that in federal court have sought stricter regulations against the Utah plants.
Berkshire began adding electric utilities to its portfolio in 2000, when it purchased Iowa-based MidAmerican Energy. Among that companyÔÇÖs five coal-fired plants is Walter Scott Jr Energy Center, a 50-year-old facility in Council Bluffs, Iowa, 10 miles down the Missouri River from BerkshireÔÇÖs Omaha headquarters. MidAmericanÔÇÖs four other Iowa coal plants all add to BerkshireÔÇÖs NOx tally, but Walter Scott, EPA data show, is the largest industrial source of NOx pollution in the Omaha area, emitting 4,000 tons in 2023 alone.
The facility is named in honor of one of BuffettÔÇÖs childhood friends, who was also a big investor in MidAmerican. It operates with two boilers, one of which doesnÔÇÖt have an SCR scrubber. Pollution from the plant settles over Omaha on days when winds blow from the south and southeast, a typical weather pattern, according to Dan Goldberg, a George Washington University professor who uses satellite data to examine real-time changes in air quality.
In 2023, metro Omaha residents had ÔÇťgoodÔÇŁ air to breathe during only half the days of the year, according to standards used by the EPAÔÇÖs Air Quality Index. It ranked 299 in air quality out of 416 small and large U.S. metro areas, according to a Reuters analysis of EPA data. Metro Omaha had 25 days in 2023 when the air quality was deemed unhealthy for sensitive groups, ranking in the bottom 10% among U.S. metro areas by this metric. That was largely caused by lung-damaging ozone topping EPA limits on 23 days, the most so far for Omaha this century.
ÔÇťIf I had an asthmatic child, I would definitely not want to live in Omaha,ÔÇŁ said Wilma Subra, a Louisiana-based scientist and environmental consultant who formerly served on the EPAÔÇÖs National Environmental Justice Advisory Council.
Omaha Mayor Jean Stothert didnÔÇÖt respond to messages seeking comment on the cityÔÇÖs air quality.
In its email to Reuters, Berkshire said MidAmerican is investing billions in renewable energy. Its Iowa coal plants operate under the ÔÇťdetailed oversight of local regulatory agenciesÔÇŁ and as part of MidAmericanÔÇÖs ÔÇťpromise to customers to deliver safe, reliable and affordable energy.ÔÇŁ
One day last year, a massive coal heap sat outside the Walter Scott plant. When wind kicks up over the Missouri River, black dust flies from the stockpile into nearby neighborhoods. ÔÇťItÔÇÖs like a black cloud rolling through,ÔÇŁ said Treg Hecker, who lives two miles away and says he sometimes has to hose the powder off the sides of his house. Medical research shows that communities near coal stockpiles can have increased risk of lung, kidney and other ailments because of such dust.
ÔÇťOur facilities employ methods to eliminate coal dust from leaving the facility,ÔÇŁ Berkshire said in its email, ÔÇťand our current leadership team is not aware of any formal complaints that have been filed with regulators.ÔÇŁ
About 11 miles northwest of Walter Scott lies North Omaha, a predominantly Black area with the highest prevalence of asthma in Nebraska. The asthma rate in North Omaha is nearly 13% of the population, according to 2021 data from the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention, outpacing the state average of 8.8% and the national average of 7.7%.
For years, members of OmahaÔÇÖs Black community have complained about pollution from another nearby coal plant, North Omaha Station. The plant, which operates without an SCR scrubber, is just two miles from the neighborhood. Operated by Omaha Public Power District, a state-run utility, it has been targeted at public hearings by neighborhood activists pressing for stricter pollution curbs there.
OPPD declined to comment.
