Over the past 20 years, EPA data show, Walter Scott has produced 403,000 tons of NOx and soot-producing sulfur dioxide pollution, 22% more than North Omaha Station. Preston Love Jr, a longtime advocate for North Omaha residents, said the risks from BerkshireÔÇÖs plant have largely escaped community notice.
ÔÇťPeople are not aware of what is happening to them,ÔÇŁ Love told Reuters. ÔÇťThey live and die without ever knowing.ÔÇŁ
In September 2023, Memphis Zabawa, a 12-year-old boy from West Omaha, another of the cityÔÇÖs neighborhoods, competed in a local cross-country race. Despite an asthma diagnosis, Memphis was running at a time of high pollution in Omaha. The cityÔÇÖs Air Quality Index, a metric public officials use to warn the public about unhealthy air, that week topped 154 for tiny soot pollution, a measure of fine particle emissions, the highest score of the year. Soot pollution on race day registered a score of 75, a ÔÇťmoderateÔÇŁ level that can still pose risks for some asthmatics.
During the race, Memphis carried his asthma inhaler and used it at least once, an Omaha Police Department report shows. But while running down a hill, he collapsed. His heart stopped beating. A nurse raced to MemphisÔÇÖ side and started CPR, police said. Paramedics rushed him to a local hospital, where he recovered.
Medical research has shown that coal plant pollution can exacerbate asthma and that tiny soot particles can trigger heart attacks. ThereÔÇÖs no way to know, though, whether that weekÔÇÖs air quality, let alone emissions from local power plants, caused MemphisÔÇÖ cardiac arrest. Linking a precise cause to any individualÔÇÖs illness is often difficult, even impossible. MemphisÔÇÖ parents, Erin and Justin Zabawa, told Reuters they canÔÇÖt assign blame for their sonÔÇÖs collapse.
About 1,000 miles to the west, Utah is known for Rocky Mountain slopes, red rock arches and national parks with panoramic vistas. Less advertised are the stateÔÇÖs air quality problems, which experts say in part stem from heavy reliance on coal power ÔÇô source of nearly half of UtahÔÇÖs electricity, federal energy data show, around three times the national average.
Ringed by mountains, Salt Lake City, the stateÔÇÖs fast-growing capital, often ranks among the worst cities on the American Lung AssociationÔÇÖs list for ozone pollution and dangerous particulate matter. The valleyÔÇÖs topography traps smog from industrial emissions, vehicles and other sources, sometimes creating a bowl of hazy pollution.
Hunter and Huntington, the two coal plants Berkshire owns more than 100 miles to the south of Salt Lake City, generate enough electricity to power about 800,000 homes. Built in the 1970s, during a coal mining boom in central Utah, the plants became part of BerkshireÔÇÖs portfolio in 2006, when it acquired PacifiCorp, owner of utility companies in several western states. Neither of the plants is equipped with SCR scrubbers.
In 2023, EPA data show, Hunter and Huntington had NOx pollution rates around 60% higher than the national average for coal plants, and more than twice the average rate of plants with SCR scrubbers. The two plants are responsible for some of Salt LakeÔÇÖs smog, causing up to $8.4 million a year in healthcare costs there, according to EPA data.
Much of their emissions also fuel regional haze pollution elsewhere, notably at Arches and Canyonlands national parks. The natural range of visibility at Canyonlands, which attracts hundreds of thousands of visitors to its gorges and buttes each year, on a clear day can span a majestic 200 miles. But in 2023, pollution some days dimmed the range by nearly 40%, according to federal visibility data for national parks.
The pollution is caused by a variety of industrial sources and vehicles. Within five years, however, the Hunter and Huntington coal plants are projected to be the top two emitters of the NOx particle pollution at Canyonlands, according to the EPA. The plantsÔÇÖ NOx output also helps form health-harming ozone pollution. Canyonlands and nearby wilderness areas have exceeded the federal health limit for ozone on 19 different days since 2018, including three in 2024, according to EPA air monitor data.
Hunter and Huntington also embody BerkshireÔÇÖs success opposing stricter NOx curbs, including a well-documented legal battle that enabled it to avoid the installation of SCR scrubbers there. Led by Abel, the Buffett protege and Berkshire energy boss, pushback against regulators has been driven by his focus on the bottom line, according to people familiar with the companyÔÇÖs tactics.
Abel is ÔÇťa consummate numbers guy,ÔÇŁ said Steven Guyer, an attorney and former environmental director at MidAmerican, BerkshireÔÇÖs first utility. ÔÇťEverything is about the earnings.ÔÇŁ Guyer left the company in 2008 and is now an outspoken critic of BerkshireÔÇÖs environmental policies at the Iowa Environmental Council, an organization that advocates for clean energy.
In its email to Reuters, Berkshire said it has made some of PacifiCorpÔÇÖs plants cleaner even without SCR scrubbers. Last year, for instance, PacifiCorp converted two of the four coal boilers at its Jim Bridger plant, in Wyoming, to run on natural gas.
In 2015, during the administration of former President Barack Obama, the EPA was evaluating tougher ozone pollution standards. That year, according to agency memos, Abel met twice with Gina McCarthy, EPA administrator at the time. The memos didnÔÇÖt disclose details about the meetings.
The EPA declined to comment on the meetings.
McCarthy didnÔÇÖt respond to requests for comment.
The next year, the EPA ordered PacifiCorp to install SCR scrubbers at Hunter and Huntington. Within five years, the agency said, the scrubbers would cut NOx pollution from the two plants by around 80%. ÔÇťEPA acts to reduce regional haze in Utah and improve visibility in National Parks,ÔÇŁ read a press release about the decision at the time.
Months later, PacifiCorp filed suit to block the plan in a Denver federal court. During two years of litigation, Berkshire resisted the EPAÔÇÖs orders, arguing that the steep price tag for two SCR scrubbers at each of the plants would outweigh the benefits. The estimated total cost of $711 million, it said, would force PacifiCorp to hike rates for customers.
The EPA hired Andover Technology Partners, a Boston-area pollution control consultant, to audit BerkshireÔÇÖs estimate. Andover said SCR projects at the two plants could be done for $517 million, or about 30% less.
PacifiCorp, the consultancy found, overestimated costs by including items like parking lot signs, crushed rock for roadways and employee eye-wash stations. Those expenses, Andover said, have nothing to do with SCR controls, adding that PacifiCorp also inflated estimates for property taxes and the fuel needed to generate steam. ÔÇťThey make the projects look as expensive as possible,ÔÇŁ said James Staudt, Andover TechnologyÔÇÖs owner.
In 2016 comments published by the EPA, PacifiCorp described AndoverÔÇÖs review as ÔÇťgrossly flawed.ÔÇŁ The EPA, in the same comments, defended the report.
In 2020, under the administration of former President Donald Trump, the EPA reversed the Obama-era order to install SCR scrubbers at Hunter and Huntington. Because PacifiCorp in 2015 had shut another Utah plant, also coal-fired, it had helped the government meet long-term goals, the agency said.
As of 2023, Berkshire had avoided installing SCR scrubbers on all but four of 24 coal-fired boilers at a dozen power plants, EPA data show. In Utah and Wyoming alone, according to a letter PacifiCorp sent the EPA in 2022, Berkshire has saved up to $2 billion by not having to install more advanced pollution controls.
In recent years, air quality experts and clean power advocates had some new reasons to be hopeful about Utah. In a May 2023 regulatory filing, PacifiCorp said it planned to shutter Hunter and Huntington by 2032. In their place, the company wrote, it was considering construction of two small nuclear plants on the sites.
The company was planning other clean energy investments over the next decade, too. In regulatory filings, PacifiCorp said the investments would create 13 gigawatts of renewable wind and solar power infrastructure in various Western states, enough electricity to power roughly 10 million average U.S. homes.
But optimism was short-lived.
In June 2023, PacifiCorp lost a high-profile lawsuit filed by fire survivors in Oregon, where a jury found its power lines responsible for sparking a wildfire. The judgment raised the specter that Berkshire utilities could face billions more in similar liabilities. Forced to set aside cash, regulatory filings show, PacifiCorp pared back many of its planned investments in renewable power.
In April of last year, PacifiCorp said in a regulatory filing that it would keep its Utah coal plants running for years longer, still with no SCR scrubbers. HuntingtonÔÇÖs operations are now expected to last an additional four years, until 2036, and HunterÔÇÖs a full decade longer than previously foreseen, until 2042, the filing said.
Pollution from the plants doesnÔÇÖt just stay in Utah. COBRA modeling shows it also increases health harms hundreds of miles away, in big cities like Denver, Los Angeles and Phoenix.
Separately, the EPA recently suffered a setback to its ÔÇťGood Neighbor Plan,ÔÇŁ part of a longstanding initiative to limit the impact that coal plant emissions have on surrounding states. Under the plan, UtahÔÇÖs coal plants would be subject to new rules that crack down on interstate ozone pollution. In June, however, the U.S. Supreme Court blocked the plan , pending further litigation from opponents including Berkshire. Among utility rivals, the industrial giant has been the most active in its opposition, submitting 125 different written objections to the initiative, according to an EPA summary document.
The decision leaves ÔÇťupwind States free to keep contributing significantly to their downwind neighborsÔÇÖ ozone problems,ÔÇŁ wrote Justice Amy Coney Barrett in a dissenting opinion. ItÔÇÖs unclear what changes may occur to the federal regulatory landscape in the next Trump administration, but the incoming president campaigned on promises to curb environmental rules and encourage energy companies to further tap fossil fuels.
State officials in Utah have been friendly to the industry. As with other supporters of continued coal generation, Utah legislators have cited the fuelÔÇÖs reliability compared with wind and solar power, whose output can vary depending on weather and daylight. ÔÇťMany people sleep better knowing these coal plants can still supply the grid,ÔÇŁ said James Scott Holladay, an energy economist at the University of Tennessee.
In March, the Utah State Legislature passed a law that could preserve coal as a preferential power source there. Utah lawmakers also offered PacifiCorp protection against wildfire liabilities, allocating taxpayer money to a fund that could cover costs from blazes sparked by power lines.
At last yearÔÇÖs shareholder meeting, Buffett and Abel lauded the fund and praised Utah for providing electric utilities with a favorable regulatory environment. Utah, Abel said, is ÔÇťthe best example we have. ItÔÇÖs the gold standard across the country.ÔÇŁ
By Tim McLaughlin and Joshua Schneyer
To determine BerkshireÔÇÖs NOx emissions from its fleet of 12 U.S. coal plants, and to compare their NOx emissions rate against those of other large corporate coal power plant operators, Reuters analyzed public EPA emissions data for 2023, the most recent full calendar year available.
The EPA breaks out the data for the 200 or so coal-powered plants that still supply electricity to the U.S. power grid.
The data show coal boilers at plants Berkshire controls released 35,891 tons of NOx in 2023, more than any other single coal plant fleet operator in the country. Reuters also compared Berkshire plantsÔÇÖ average NOx emissions rate to that of other large U.S. corporate coal plant fleets ÔÇô those owned by the 20 utility operators with coal plants in the Standard and PoorÔÇÖs Utilities Index.
The NOx rate takes into account a coal fleetÔÇÖs NOx emissions by weight relative to the heat energy it produces from burning coal, in British thermal units, or BTUs, a standard methodology used by the EPA.
BerkshireÔÇÖs coal fleet had a higher average NOx emissions rate ÔÇô 0.163 pounds per million BTUs ÔÇô than any of its big corporate utility peers. It did not have advanced pollution controls ÔÇô selective catalytic reduction, or SCR, scrubbers ÔÇô on most of its coal-fired boilers.
To estimate the healthcare costs and negative health outcomes attributable to BerkshireÔÇÖs coal plant emissions in the Omaha metropolitan area and nationwide, Reuters used the EPAÔÇÖs Co-Benefits Risk Assessment Health Impacts Screening and Mapping Tool, or COBRA.
COBRA provides the range of estimated health costs associated with coal plant pollution in a given area. Reuters used the EPAÔÇÖs baseline emissions data estimates for 2023. COBRA results for metro Omaha include the upper range of estimated impacts within the "Omaha, NEÔÇôIA, Metropolitan Statistical Area (MSA)," an eight county, bi-state area including the City of Omaha and surrounding areas.
To explore the air quality metrics in metro Omaha and elsewhere, Reuters analyzed the EPAÔÇÖs Air Quality Index data, which relies on air monitoring equipment nationwide. Reuters analyzed air quality data from 416 U.S. urban areas. The analysis excluded areas where air monitoring readings were incomplete.

item.title: Is climate change lighting a fuse under Iceland's volcanoes?
item.description:
Scientists are racing to find out whether the rapid retreat of glaciers could drive a surge in eruptions as magma builds under the island nation ÔÇö and if so, whether the same might occur at ice-covered volcanoes around the world, putting many lives at risk.
By GLORIA DICKIE Photography by STOYAN NENOV Graphics by SAM HART and ANURAG RAO Video by KUBA ST─ś┼╗YCKI
Filed Oct. 26, 2024, 10 a.m. GMT
Toxic sulphurous┬ágas, carrying┬áthe┬átelltale reek of rotten eggs, wafted through vents in the steep walls of IcelandÔÇÖs Viti crater, while carbon dioxide bubbled to the surface of the milky blue crater lake. Veils of steam wreathed the landscape of loose rock in eerie half-light.
Through this forbidding terrain ÔÇô ÔÇťVitiÔÇŁ is derived from the Icelandic for ÔÇťhellÔÇŁ ÔÇô Michelle Parks, a volcanologist with the Icelandic Meteorological Office, picked her way toward the waterÔÇÖs edge one day last August. With a monitor strapped to her hip to warn her if the gases┬áreached dangerous levels, she stooped to submerge a temperature probe in the lake ÔÇô 26.4 degrees Celsius (79.5 degrees Fahrenheit), consistent with recent readings.
That was reassuring, at least for the time being. The crater was formed when Askja, a volcano in Vatnajokull National Park in IcelandÔÇÖs central highlands, uncorked in an explosive eruption in 1875. AskjaÔÇÖs last eruption, in 1961, was milder, and for decades after, the volcano was quiet. But in 2021, Parks and other scientists keeping tabs on it were shocked to find that in just a few months, the volcano had rapidly expanded, uplifting by 11 centimetres┬á(4.3 inches). This phenomenon, called inflation, occurs when magma or pressurised┬ágases┬áaccumulate under a volcano, pushing the ground upwards and outwards.
In the three years since, AskjaÔÇÖs bloat has reached about 80 cm (32 inches). That uplift, scientists estimate, is the result of 44 million cubic metres┬á(1.6 billion cubic feet) of magma flowing into the existing reservoir around three km (two miles) beneath the surface.
Volcanologists have established a correlation between magma buildup under a volcano and subsequent eruption. But they donÔÇÖt know exactly how much magma is needed to help trip an eruption.┬áThat is why Parks and her colleagues are closely monitoring the temperature and acidity of VitiÔÇÖs crater lake. A jump in either, indicating that more gases┬áare pushing in from below, would suggest the volcano is moving closer to an eruption. So far, those metrics have remained stable, but the scientists watching Askja donÔÇÖt take that for granted.
ÔÇťThereÔÇÖs so much uncertainty on when this will erupt ÔÇö if it will erupt,ÔÇŁ Parks said. It could be years, or decades, she said, or ÔÇťit could be sooner.ÔÇŁ
An eruption could be catastrophic, though smaller ones are much more likely. On the scale volcanologists use to measure the size of explosive eruptions, Askja is capable of one equivalent to that of Mount St. Helens in the U.S. state of Washington in 1980, although its eruptive style would differ. That eruption, which sparked the Australia-born ParksÔÇÖ lifelong passion for volcanoes, unleashed a force equivalent to 25,000 Hiroshima-type atomic bombs.
The aim of the volcano observatory at the Icelandic Meteorological Office is to monitor the countryÔÇÖs volcanoes for changes in activity, like those now observed at Askja, helping to protect the nationÔÇÖs nearly 400,000 citizens and the hordes of foreign tourists who visit every year to enjoy IcelandÔÇÖs world-renowned geothermal attractions.
ÔÇťIf itÔÇÖs a big explosion, then itÔÇÖs really dangerous for the people around here, the tourists.ÔÇŁ
However, the teamÔÇÖs work has taken on broader significance. Last year, Parks and colleagues with the University of Iceland received government funding for a pioneering research project across 12 institutions to test a theory that could have dire implications not just for Iceland, but for every person on the planet: Whether the rapid retreat of glaciers as a result of human-caused climate change will trigger increased volcanic activity.
The basic process underpinning the idea is simple. The tremendous weight of glaciers and ice sheets can tamp down volcanoes. When the ice retreats, the downward pressure on the planetÔÇÖs thin outer crust and much thicker underlying mantle┬áeases, allowing the ground to rebound. This change in pressure spurs dynamic forces beneath volcanoes to produce more magma and alter its movement, influencing eruptions.
ÔÇťIceland is essentially one of the best places in the world to study this ÔÇŽ because we have both volcanism┬áand glaciers,ÔÇŁ Parks said. ÔÇťAt the end of the day, what weÔÇÖre aiming for with this project is a much bigger picture. ItÔÇÖs the future of volcanic eruptions. How large can they be? ÔÇŽ And whatÔÇÖs in store for us in the future, not only in Iceland but for the rest of Europe and potentially farther afield.ÔÇŁ
Ground surface inflation at Olafsgigar monitoring station
Jan 2021
Source: Icelandic Meteorological Office with data processing by Sigr├║n Hreinsd├│ttir
In July 2021, one of the observation stations at the vast Askja volcanic system showed that the ground there was uplifting ÔÇö a process called inflation ÔÇö indicating that magma was accumulating under the volcano after decades of deflation. The sudden reversal coincided with a burst of seismic activity in the area.
The rapid uplift continued through September 2021 and then gradually slowed as earthquakes persisted.
As of September 2024, Askja was still expanding, having inflated by about 80 cm in just three years. Scientists fear an eruption could be increasingly likely, but the timing and intensity remain uncertain.
Uncorking cataclysm
What scientists already know of IcelandÔÇÖs eruptive history supports the theory.
When the thick glaciers and ice caps that had covered the North Atlantic island during the last major Ice Age receded between about 15,000 and 10,000 years ago, underlying volcanoes responded with fury. In 2002, scientists calculated changes in IcelandÔÇÖs volcanic activity over time by analysing┬áthe chemical composition of lava rock samples. They found that eruption rates surged an estimated 30 to 50 times during and shortly after the ice loss compared to the preceding Ice Age and recent times.
It was likely a cataclysmic scenario, with a ÔÇťridiculous amount of eruptions,ÔÇŁ Parks said, as rivers of lava reshaped the island and ash rained into surrounding seas. Askja, too, registered a major explosive eruption during this time.
Once again, scientists say, the elements needed to set off another surge in eruptions are converging. Glaciers now cover just 10% of Iceland, but that ice still weighs on more than half the nationÔÇÖs 34 active volcanic systems, and it is rapidly melting as global temperatures climb. In the past 130 years, IcelandÔÇÖs glaciers have lost about 16% of their volume, with half of that in just the past three decades. Scientists predict roughly half of the remaining volume will be gone by this centuryÔÇÖs end.
Already, the magma chambers beneath Iceland may be responding to the loss of ice, and not just those directly under glaciers. Askja, which has been free of ice for 10,000 years, and much of Iceland are rebounding because pressure changes from glacial retreat affects large parts of the EarthÔÇÖs crust and mantle.
Over the last three decades, magma has been produced beneath Iceland at a rate 2 to 3 times what it would have been without the ice loss, according to preliminary modelling┬áresults from ParksÔÇÖ project shared exclusively with Reuters. ÔÇťMore magma is coming in underneath Iceland, and we just donÔÇÖt need it,ÔÇŁ Parks said. ÔÇťWeÔÇÖve got enough.ÔÇŁ
Scientists first theorised┬áin the 1970s that melting ice might impact volcanic eruptions. But only recently have they begun to understand the scale of the potential threat. Four years ago, volcanologists compiled the first comprehensive global database of volcanoes under ice or within five km (three miles)┬áof it, publishing their findings in the journal Global and Planetary Change. They found that some 245 active or potentially active volcanoes around the world met the criteria, from the Andes to North AmericaÔÇÖs Cascade Mountains and Alaska, to RussiaÔÇÖs Kamchatka Peninsula and Antarctica.
The same study also found that about 160 million people live within 100 km (62 miles) of at least one of those volcanoes and that 20,000 people are within the immediate vicinity. Numbers like that underscore the threat if glacial melt were to push more volcanoes to pop off, unleashing deadly floods and mud flows and spewing vast clouds of ash and deadly gases┬áinto the atmosphere.
A global concern
A 2020 study identified 245 volcanoes within a 5 km radius of a glacier.
Volcanoes near or under ice
Iceland
Fault lines
ICELAND
Atlantic Ocean
0
300 km
CHILE
Pacific Ocean
0
300
Arctic Ocean
Alaska
RUSSIA
Atlantic Ocean
0
1,000
A 2017 study identified 138 suspected volcanoes beneath Antarctica
ANTARCTICA
Sources: ÔÇśGlobal Mapping of Future GlaciovolcanismÔÇÖ Global and Planetary Change, December 2020; 'A new volcanic province: an inventory of subglacial volcanoes in West Antarctica' , Geological Society, London,┬áMay 2017
The effects wouldnÔÇÖt end there. Eruptions rich in carbon dioxide, a greenhouse gas, could exacerbate global warming, outlasting the sulphur┬áaerosols in the atmosphere that can cause initial periods of cooling. And the volcanoes now known to sit under the West Antarctic Ice Sheet could help speed ice melt into the ocean, raising┬ásea levels.
Evidence suggests that what happened in Iceland after the last Ice Age was duplicated to a lesser extent around the world. Eruptions were between 2 and 6 times higher globally between 12,000 and 7,000 years ago owing to more frequent eruptions in areas that were losing their ice cover, according to a 2009 study in the journal Earth and Planetary Science Letters.
A violent history
Iceland is uniquely primed for frequent volcanic eruptions because of its location at the juncture of two tectonic plates that are pulling apart over an upwelling of anomalously hot material in the EarthÔÇÖs mantle. Many smaller eruptions have little impact beyond their immediate vicinity, though they are still able to put on spectacular shows. Others can have lethal effects far beyond Iceland.
When Laki, southwest of Askja, erupted in 1783-84, the fluorine it expelled contaminated the islandÔÇÖs plants and water sources, killing more than half of IcelandÔÇÖs livestock. This agricultural collapse led to a famine that killed around a fifth of the islandÔÇÖs human population, while the resulting haze of sulphurous┬áfog that later reached Europe may have contributed to the deaths of thousands more people. And as far away as Africa and Asia, the eruptionÔÇÖs effects weakened monsoons, initiating more famines.
Near the southern coast under a small ice cap lies Katla, considered one of the worldÔÇÖs most dangerous volcanoes under ice. Katla has racked up more than 20 eruptions since the Middle Ages, averaging one every 60 years, and it is past due for another, having last erupted in 1918. During that eruption, heat pouring from KatlaÔÇÖs caldera rapidly melted the ice atop the volcano, releasing a torrent of water greater than the combined discharges of the Amazon, Mississippi, and Yangtze rivers at its peak. And, like Laki, Katla has explosive potential.
ÔÇťThe time for Katla to erupt is coming close ... It is high time for European governments and airline authorities all over Europe and the world to start planning for the eventual Katla eruption," then-Icelandic President Olafur Ragnar Grimsson told BBC News in 2010, following the eruption of Eyjafjallaj├Âkull. The ash cloud from that eruption caused ÔéČ1.3 billion (US$1.4 billion) in losses from cancelled┬áflights during a six-day European airspace ban, making it the worldÔÇÖs costliest eruption in the last century.
The Icelandic government has emergency plans in place for a Katla-style eruption and works with local police districts to create near-term risk assessments for other volcanoes.
But the government has also tapped its fiery foundation to its benefit, using it to heat homes and businesses and draw big-spending tourists to rejuvenating geothermal baths. After the Eyjafjallaj├Âkull eruption, the Icelandic government made volcanoes a pillar of a now multibillion-dollar tourism industry. Souvenir shops in the capital, Reykjavik, sell lava rocks from a recent eruption on the Reykjanes Peninsula for 2,000 Icelandic krona (US$14) apiece.
ÔÇťWatching your friends disappearÔÇŁ
Like many Icelanders, 29-year-old Iris Ragnarsdottir Pedersen and her father, 62-year-old Ragnar Frank Kristjansson, have an intimate knowledge of the extremes of their land of ice and fire. Along the south coast in Svinafell, Ragnarsdottir Pedersen, a mountain guide, lives with her husband and their Icelandic sheepdog, Blika. Her father, a retired national park manager, has a small turf-roofed summer house next door. Towering over them is a large cliff, beyond which lies Vatnajokull ice cap and, under it, the volcano Oraefajokull.
After Oraefajokull erupted in 1362, sailors reported seaborne pumice floating ÔÇťin such masses that ships could hardly make their way through it.ÔÇŁ
Father and daughter both recall joining fellow Icelanders over the years to marvel at the glowing fountains and rivers of lava produced by eruptions. They also are familiar with the impact of climate change.
For 25 years, Kristjansson has volunteered as a glacier monitor with the Icelandic Glaciological Society, tracking the retreat of two glaciers that flow out of the Vatnajokull ice cap, the nationÔÇÖs largest. Every autumn, he treks for hours to one of the glaciers, Skeidararjokull, to take measurements that he sends to the Icelandic Meteorological Office. ÔÇťIt's a lonely walk toward the glacier ÔÇö 15 km in the black sand,ÔÇŁ he said.
Kristjansson used to be able to stride right up to the edge of Skeidararjokull. But as it has rapidly shrunk over the past decade, a lake has formed at the foot of the glacier, blocking his way. He now has to use special binoculars to measure the distance. This year, he said, one point along the glacierÔÇÖs edge showed a retreat of 300 metres┬á(984 feet), which is the greatest he has recorded.
Ragnarsdottir Pedersen has witnessed the retreat from year to year on treks to the glaciers with her father and by simply looking out her windows. ÔÇťItÔÇÖs just devastating to see,ÔÇŁ she said. ÔÇťIÔÇÖve sometimes said to people, ÔÇśItÔÇÖs like watching your friends disappear.ÔÇÖ ÔÇŁ
As a child, she knew Oraefajokull lurked under the Vatnajokull ice cap. But it only became a concern when the volcano started rumbling in 2017, just as she and her husband began planning to build their home near it. When she felt the earthquakes and caught the smell of sulphur┬áfrom the rivers, she said, ÔÇťYouÔÇÖre like, ÔÇśOh yeah, we actually have this powerful, quite dangerous volcano right above our home.ÔÇÖ ÔÇŁ
Ultimately, she and her husband werenÔÇÖt concerned enough to cancel the move, having faith that scientists would closely monitor the volcano for any danger. ÔÇťPeople have lived in Svinafell since the settlement of Iceland,ÔÇŁ she said.
A scientific mecca
Oraefajokull is one of six active volcanoes covered by the Vatnajokull ice cap. As Vatnajokull has thinned and retreated, some of the volcanoes beneath it, as well as nearby Askja, have become agitated. Oraefajokull has calmed since its 2017 awakening. Grimsvotn and Bardarbunga, two volcanoes under the ice cap that are being assessed in ParksÔÇÖ┬áproject, have together notched five eruptions in the past 30 years, compared with just one under the ice cap in the preceding 40 years.
Parks and her scientific colleagues said itÔÇÖs too early to conclude that the increased activity under Vatnajokull is linked to rapid loss of ice cover. Other scientists have established that clusters of eruptions can occur in natural cycles under the ice cap. But to determine whether climate change may also be playing a part, they will have to collect more data on ice retreat and magma generation and feed it into complex computer models.
Because of its continuing unrest, Askja is the main draw for scientists, who flock to its enormous calderas when IcelandÔÇÖs brief summer allows for field research. Like most of IcelandÔÇÖs volcanoes, Askja does not conform to the stereotypical cone shape of volcanoes such as JapanÔÇÖs iconic Mount Fuji. Instead, fissures snake across a sprawling Mars-like landscape carpeted with lava rocks and pockmarked with craters from past eruptions.
To reach the centrepiece┬áof the Askja system, an 11-square-km (four sq miles) caldera lake called Oskjuvatn, scientists journey hours across the highlands in Super Jeeps and Land Rovers balanced on 35-inch tires. Oskjuvatn was formed in an explosive 1875 eruption that shot out huge volumes of airy pumice, much of it still covering the area around Askja, with fist-sized chunks tossed about by strong winds.
Above the magma reservoir near the western side of the lake is where Askja has been inflating fastest, a lure for researchers. On a visit in August, Reuters encountered a team of three scientists from the University of Geneva at the lakeÔÇÖs edge, loading equipment into an inflatable dinghy. Their aim: to measure carbon dioxide concentrations in the lake and collect water samples from its deepest points.
The data is not easy to get. The numbingly cold and often tempestuous waters are dangerous. Landslides on the calderaÔÇÖs steep scarp can launch tsunamis big enough to swamp neighbouring┬áViti crater. In 1907, two German scientists set off onto the lake and were never seen again. The Geneva teamÔÇÖs boat would be the first on the lake in nearly a decade. The University of Geneva researchers suited up in thick thermal gear, just in case.
Weight of glacier
Increased volatility from below
Magma migration pathways change
More magma is generated, and existing magma bodies may be destabilised
The great weight of glaciers places downward pressure on the EarthÔÇÖs crust and mantle, in effect dampening upward force from magma chambers beneath volcanoes.
As the ice cover melts, this downward pressure eases, enabling more rock in the mantle to melt and form magma.
The rebound of the crust can also affect stress fields in the Earth, altering the movement of magma and creating new fractures underneath a volcano. Magma reservoirs can become unstable and move closer to eruption.
As magma pushes upward, it can cause the ground to expand and trigger swarms of small earthquakes. When the pressure is high enough, an eruption can burst through the surface.
One of them, geologist Nicolas Oestreicher, said the work was important given both AskjaÔÇÖs explosive capabilities and the increased activity around the lake. ÔÇťIf itÔÇÖs a big explosion, then itÔÇÖs really dangerous for the people around here, the tourists,ÔÇŁ Oestreicher said.
His team, which later returned safely from their boat trip, was one of several from Iceland and abroad that day racing to figure out what was going on at Askja. University of Iceland geophysicist Freysteinn Sigmundsson, co-head with Parks of the government-funded project, was there, too. ÔÇťToday may be the richest day in all of AskjaÔÇÖs┬áhistory in terms of the number of scientists here,ÔÇŁ he said.
Sigmundsson has been coming to the volcano almost every year since 1990 and he knows the terrain well. Shouldering a surveying tripod, he clambered with sure-footed expertise across beds of jagged, crunchy lava, searching for round metal markers that scientists anchored in the area in the 1960s and 1970s. These would tell him where to set up his equipment to check how much Askja had grown or shifted over the past year.
The measurements Sigmundsson and his colleagues took in August showed 12 cm (five inches) of uplift at Askja since the previous year, confirming that the volcano was still in a state of unrest and could erupt at any time.
From the Andes to Antarctica
Regardless of what they ultimately find, the interplay between volcanoes and ice will remain a chief worry among volcanologists. The most deadly eruption in the last 100 years was that of Nevado del Ruiz in the Colombian Andes in 1985. Some 23,000 people were killed after a superheated amalgam of ash, lava fragments and gases┬áknown as a pyroclastic flow melted snow and ice near the volcanoÔÇÖs summit, sending massive rivers of mud and debris rushing down the volcanoÔÇÖs flanks.
ÔÇťAny eruption where there is a possibility of (snow or ice) interacting with the hot stuff adds to the elements of danger,ÔÇŁ said Ben Edwards, a volcanologist at Dickinson College in Pennsylvania and lead author of the 2020 study that produced the database of ice-clad volcanoes.
Edwards and his fellow researchers drew up a list of the worldÔÇÖs most dangerous volcanoes that lie under glaciers,┬ábased on the volume of ice on or near each one, the frequency of past eruptions, and the population living within 30 km (19 miles). Seven of the top 10, they found, are in the Andes. The most dangerous is Villarrica┬áin Chile, with more than 35,000 people living in its shadow. Edwards is in the middle of a five-year study seeking to understand how Villarrica responded to glacial retreat at the end of the last Ice Age.
The small number of studies to date of the interaction between retreating ice and volcanoes in other parts of the world, scientists said, mean the research underway in Iceland will help create a template for what could happen elsewhere. ItÔÇÖs not a perfect comparison: The underlying plate tectonics in Iceland differ from those in the Andes. Magma reservoirs also lie much deeper in the crust in the Andes than in Iceland, Edwards said.
ÔÇťWe donÔÇÖt have a very good physical understanding of all the processes that control melting (of rock into magma) deeper in the mantle,ÔÇŁ said David Pyle, a volcanologist at the University of Oxford who studies Chilean volcanoes. ÔÇťWhereas in Iceland, the conceptual model is much better developed because in a way itÔÇÖs geologically simpler.ÔÇŁ
Antarctica, where both ice and volcanoes are abundant, has become an area of concern in recent years. While a handful of volcanoes were known to reside under the vast West Antarctic Ice Sheet, in 2017 scientists reported detecting another 91 possible volcanoes hidden there.
How many of them are active or have the potential to awaken is difficult to ascertain. ÔÇťThere are probably two or three which are certainly active. But there may be as many as 100 or 150,ÔÇŁ said John Smellie, a former senior volcanologist with the British Antarctic Survey who has completed 27 field seasons on the continent and won two Polar Medals from the British royals.
If climate change kicks off a new era of eruptions in the coming decades, Smellie said, it would hasten the already rapid loss of the continentÔÇÖs ice cover due to climate change, adding to rising sea levels and swamping the worldÔÇÖs big coastal cities. Antarctica is shedding more than 150 billion metric tons┬áof ice a year, according to satellite measurements, and scientists believe that as oceans continue to warm, the West Antarctic Ice Sheet is fast approaching a tipping point beyond which the melt cannot be stopped.
ÔÇťIf an eruption adds even a small amount to global sea level, it will exacerbate effects already happening due to climate change,ÔÇŁ Smellie said.
Under the worst-case scenario, rapid melting of the West Antarctic Ice Sheet would ease the pressure on buried volcanoes, triggering eruptions. This in turn would speed up ice loss, awakening even more volcanoes that melt more ice, and so on, leading to global catastrophe. However, Smellie said, that is unlikely to happen because thousands of nearly simultaneous eruptions would be needed to melt even a small proportion of the ice sheet.
In the danger zone
Few people make their home near Askja. The closest village lies about 60 km (37 miles) away. But some 13,000 people visit the volcano during the summer months, when Askja is more accessible, according to data from the national park authority. Often, tourists climb down into Viti crater to swim in the Instagram-worthy lake, despite signs that warn them about acidic water, which can irritate skin, and falling rock.
After a week of field work at and around Askja last August, the volcanologists gathered park rangers and local police and lodging staff in a nearby ranger hut one evening for a safety briefing on the state of the volcano.
Related content
In the Andes, mountain guides bear witness to shrinking glaciers
Climate change thaws worldÔÇÖs northernmost research station
Iceland builds defences for a future of increased volcanic activity
Iceland volcano erupts near town after weeks of quake activity
Parks hovered over her laptop displaying a series of charts and graphs on her most recent findings that showed Askja was still inflating. Sigmundsson, standing in the doorway, explained to the rangers and police that predicting eruptions is an imprecise science; they may not necessarily have weeks of seismic activity as a warning of an imminent eruption. ÔÇťThe time scale can be quite short,ÔÇŁ he said. ÔÇťHours, maybe ÔÇŽ You would want to have a plan if something happens to quickly evacuate the caldera.ÔÇŁ
One of the greatest risks to tourists is a phreatic explosion ÔÇö a blast of hot steam, ash and rocks that comes with little warning. ThatÔÇÖs what formed Viti crater nearly 150 years ago. In 2019, a phreatic explosion at New ZealandÔÇÖs White Island volcano killed 22 people who were visiting the island at the time.
At SigmundssonÔÇÖs words, the room fell quiet. One of the police officers then piped up with a plan: ÔÇťWe just have to hope that it blows up in the middle of the winter.ÔÇŁ
By Gloria Dickie
Graphics: Sam Hart and Anurag Rao
Photography: Stoyan Nenov
Video: Kuba St─Ö┼╝ycki
Video production: Lauren Cook, Holly Murtha and M├şa Womersley
Photo editing: Maye-E Wong and Eve Watling
Art direction: Eve Watling
Visual editing: Feilding Cage
Edited by Katy Daigle and John Blanton
Amid IsraelÔÇÖs military assault, the worldÔÇÖs hunger monitor has struggled to access key data to determine whether conditions in Gaza constitute a famine.
OnlyFans is among the world's most successful social media platforms ÔÇô but a secretive one. Reuters traces its journey from an obscure, porn-free site to an adults-only phenomenon turbocharged by erotic performers and influencers.
ItÔÇÖs a simple but brutal equation: The number of people going hungry or otherwise struggling around the world is rising, while the amount of money the worldÔÇÖs wealthiest nations are contributing toward helping them is dropping.
Multiple OnlyFans accounts featured suspected child sex abuse, investigator reports.
Reuters, the news and media division of Thomson Reuters, is the worldÔÇÖs largest multimedia news provider, reaching billions of people worldwide every day. Reuters provides business, financial, national and international news to professionals via desktop terminals, the world's media organizations, industry events and directly to consumers.

item.title: 'Sustainable' logging operations are clear-cutting CanadaÔÇÖs climate-fighting forests
item.description:
Nonprofit environment watchdogs put their stamps of approval on countless wood products that get touted as responsibly produced. But Reuters found that the timber firms these groups certify are harvesting large swaths of CanadaÔÇÖs older forests, which are critical to containing global warming.
By CHRIS KIRKHAM , GRANT SMITH and JESSICA DINAPOLI Photographs by CHRIS HELGREN and DAVID JACKSON
Filed Sept. 7, 2024, 10 a.m. GMT
With its vast expanses of forest, Canada has the most ÔÇťcertifiedÔÇŁ sustainable timber operations of any nation, according to the nonprofit organizations that attest to the environmental soundness of logging practices.
Such forestry-standards groups were born in the 1990s out of rage┬áover tropical rainforest destruction. Today, they put their leafy seals of approval on toilet paper, two-by-fours and other wood and paper goods to assure eco-conscious consumers and investors they were responsibly produced.
Yet research shows Canadian forests have seen some of the worldÔÇÖs largest declines in ecologically critical primary and old-growth woodlands over the last two decades, even as sustainability-certification programs grew to include nearly all of CanadaÔÇÖs logging.
To track destruction of older woodlands in these certified zones, Reuters analyzed forestry data in Ontario, a major logging province. The analysis found that about 30% of the certified boreal forests harvested from 2016 to 2020 were at least 100 years old. That resulted in the loss of 377 square miles of these older forests, an area the size┬áof New York City and Washington D.C. combined, the analysis found.
CanadaÔÇÖs forests ÔÇô accounting for 9% of the worldÔÇÖs total ÔÇô are considered critical to containing global warming. Environmental advocates have long pushed to end logging in primary or old-growth forests, which soak up far more climate-damaging carbon than logged-and-replanted areas. Primary forests are those that show no sign of previous harvesting. They can include old-growth areas ÔÇô some with trees hundreds or thousands of years old ÔÇô but also relatively newer woodlands that, for instance, might have regrown after wildfires.
Forest-certification nonprofits have chosen to allow logging of older forests through a host of concessions to industry. The harvesting of such areas in Ontario came despite the fact that 94% of the provinceÔÇÖs managed forests are certified by one of the two dominant environmental-certification┬áorganizations in Canada, the analysis found. Reuters analyzed satellite-derived logging data, government forest-age estimates and forest-certification maps to estimate the harvest of forests at least 100 years old in OntarioÔÇÖs certified zones.
ÔÇťWhy the heck are they allowing logging ÔÇô certified logging ÔÇô in primary forests that are over 100 years old?ÔÇŁ asked Dominick DellaSala, a conservation biologist with environmental group Wild Heritage who studies Canadian logging impacts.┬áÔÇťFor Canada to claim that itÔÇÖs doing sustainable management, itÔÇÖs laughable. To put a certification seal of approval on it is more alarming.ÔÇŁ
The rapid loss of older Canadian forests┬áhighlights the flaws of certification programs that have come under heavy influence of the logging and forest-products┬áindustries, a Reuters investigation has found. The damage has come under the watch of the Forest Stewardship Council (FSC), the worldÔÇÖs first such certification organization, founded in 1993 with environmentalist support; and the Sustainable Forestry Initiative (SFI), a rival founded by a timber┬áand forest-products┬átrade group the following year.
This account is based on the Reuters analysis of Ontario forests, a review of hundreds of pages of FSC and SFI audits, along with policy and strategy documents, and interviews with 20 current or former FSC employees or members and more than a half-dozen┬áresearchers who study the environmental effects of Canadian logging.
In a statement, FSC said it has not wavered from its original commitment to responsible forest┬ámanagement and┬áthat its certification standards are ÔÇťrobust and credible.ÔÇŁ SFI said its standards are ÔÇťstrong and continuously improvingÔÇŁ and that its certification has become a ÔÇťhighly trusted solutionÔÇŁ to the growing demand for products from sustainably managed forests.
Neither organization commented on the Reuters analysis or on whether they considered harvesting large sections of century-old┬áforests to be sustainable.
The FSC and SFI certify logging companiesÔÇÖ practices in specific forests and examine consumer-product supply chains. Their seals of approval ÔÇô a leaf insignia┬áfor SFI, and a tree with a checkmark for the┬áFSC ÔÇô have become essential to timber and forest-products┬áfirms amid rising pressure for environmental stewardship.
But these companies hold immense leverage over the big forest-certification nonprofits, which depend heavily on the industry for funding through certification fees, Reuters found. And since its inception, the FSC has watered down its forestry standards in response to the competitive threat posed by SFI and other industry-friendly certifiers, according to environmentalists and more than a dozen current and former FSC staffers and members, who advise the organization on policy and strategy.
Companies are free to choose which certifier to use, allowing them to avoid those with stricter standards and giving them clout to lobby all certifiers for permissive policies, said the FSC staffers and members.
ÔÇťItÔÇÖs easy to pull the wool over peopleÔÇÖs eyes about what is good forestry.ÔÇŁ
Widespread certification of British Columbia timber operations over the past two decades hasnÔÇÖt stopped the disappearance of more than half of the provinceÔÇÖs old-growth woodlands over that period. Logging caused the vast majority of the declines in the biggest old-growth trees storing the most carbon, according to one 2021 study in the Canadian Journal of Forest Research and another last year in the journal Frontiers in Forests and Global Change.┬áStudies in 2009 and 2017 examined areas of Quebec woodlands and found┬ásections┬áof forests dominated by trees more than a century old had dwindled to between 13% and 28% of the forest amid heavy logging. Without logging, these older sections would account for between 40% and 68%┬áof these woodlands, the researchers estimated.
Herb Hammond, a veteran forest ecologist,┬áran a British Columbia nonprofit┬áorganization that conducted some of CanadaÔÇÖs first FSC audits in the late 1990s.┬áHe later left the organization, frustrated with what he described as┬átoo many compromises with industry.
ÔÇťItÔÇÖs easy to pull the wool over peopleÔÇÖs eyes about what is good forestry,ÔÇŁ he said. ÔÇťCertification has turned out to be a bit of a dogÔÇÖs breakfast. It doesnÔÇÖt really mean anything.ÔÇŁ
A ÔÇśchess moveÔÇÖ
Forestry certification has become ubiquitous in the global forest-products┬átrade, helping companies such as Procter & Gamble, Starbucks and Penguin Random House appeal to eco-conscious consumers and investors. Those three companies declined to comment.
The certifying trend started in the 1990s when environmental organizations including Greenpeace, Friends of the Earth and the World Wildlife Fund helped launch the FSC after failing to secure forest-conservation pledges from governments worldwide. They hoped to incentivize companies instead with a market-driven system that branded goods as sustainable, stimulating demand from discerning buyers.┬áThe FSC was founded in 1993 with a membership of business, environmental and community representatives.
Still, many companies were wary of aligning with environmentalists. The following year, the American Forest &┬áPaper Association, a trade-group, started the SFI as an industry-friendly alternative. The trade association said its discussions about sustainable forestry began earlier, in 1990, and included input from academics and conservation groups.
Competition from the industry-backed SFI forced the FSC to reckon with how to maintain rigorous forestry standards while recruiting companies to certify, 10┬ácurrent and former FSC members said. A 2002 FSC management report highlighted the need to ÔÇťrapidly increase the supply of certified timberÔÇŁ or risk losing out to ÔÇťan ever-increasing number of competing certification schemes.ÔÇŁ
The FSC launched an internal push to boost its market share that led to compromises with industry and weaker harvesting restrictions, according to FSC documents and the FSC members.
Weakening FSC standards didnÔÇÖt stop the SFIÔÇÖs growth, however. The FSC certified about 46 million hectares of Canadian forests┬áat the end of 2023, less than half the SFIÔÇÖs 119 million hectares, according to the Forest Products Association of Canada, an industry group. Globally, the FSC certifies 160 million hectares compared to 295 million hectares by the Programme for the Endorsement of Forest Certification┬á(PEFC). The PEFC is a global organization that oversees the SFI, which covers North America, and affiliated certifiers in other regions.
Both the FSC and the SFI largely survive on industry-paid fees. FSC International reported in 2022 that such fees accounted for 86% of its $58 million in annual revenue. The SFI derived 77% of its $12 million in revenue from such fees, according to its 2022 tax return.
Some environmental groups and advocates, while acknowledging the FSCÔÇÖs shortcomings, continue to view the organization as the best option among imperfect alternatives. Jen Skene, a policy director at the Natural Resources Defense Council, said FSC certification represents a minimum standard.
ÔÇťFSC is the most credible certification system out there,ÔÇŁ she said, while adding that it should be viewed as ÔÇťa floor, not a ceilingÔÇŁ for sustainability standards.
FSC told Reuters it had not weakened standards in response to SFI competition. Instead, FSC said, the rivalry has prompted it to ÔÇťimprove and refineÔÇŁ its certification process to ensure it remains ÔÇťthe gold standard for responsible forestry.ÔÇŁ
SFI said competition among certifiers doesnÔÇÖt ÔÇťexert a downward pressure on standardsÔÇŁ but rather promotes continuous improvement. The PEFC said it allows regional groups including the SFI to develop their own standards, which the PEFC said┬áÔÇťcontributes to long-term commitment to sustainable forest management┬ápractices.ÔÇŁ
Though some corporations favor FSC-certified wood, few customers know the difference among certifying groups and their labels.
Peter Wood, a forestry lecturer at the University of British Columbia who has served on FSC-rulemaking committees, called the SFIÔÇÖs creation a ÔÇťchess move.ÔÇŁ
The industry ÔÇťwanted to take the power away from FSC, and it worked,ÔÇŁ he said. ÔÇťNow, everything is certified.ÔÇŁ
Race to the bottom
FSCÔÇÖs early standards emphasized the need to protect primary and old-growth forests. One pivotal provision read: ÔÇťPrimary forests ÔÇŽ shall be conserved. Such areas shall not be replaced by tree plantations or other land uses.ÔÇŁ
But companies complained the policy was too restrictive and difficult to enforce, said Grant Rosoman, a Greenpeace forests advisor and former FSC International board member.
FSC members spent years debating policy changes and in 1999 removed requirements to conserve primary forests. Instead, the FSC adopted a more subjective requirement to protect forests with ÔÇťhigh conservation value,ÔÇŁ based on a complex matrix of environmental, economic┬áand cultural characteristics.
That vague language, still in effect, gives companies broad influence over which forests qualify for protection. It has also spawned an industry of consultants ÔÇô hired and paid┬áby forest-products firms┬áÔÇô to perform studies determining which woodlands┬áhave high conservation value, according to FSC audits and six current and former FSC members.
Rosoman of Greenpeace was among the FSCÔÇÖs┬ámembers┬áwho approved the language at the time. He now regrets it, believing its subjectivity enabled destruction of critical forests. ÔÇťThe continued logging of primary forests and old-growth forests was never resolved,ÔÇŁ he said.
FSC acknowledged that its rules allow certified logging in such areas but said the high conservation value designation aims to ensure such harvesting is ÔÇťconducted with the highest level of scrutiny and responsibility.ÔÇŁ
In another major concession, FSC in 2004 introduced the ÔÇťFSC MixÔÇŁ system, which created a new label for products containing up to 30% wood┬áfrom non-certified sources.
The move came after pressure from pulp-and-paper companies including Klabin of Brazil, SCA of Sweden and Mondi of South Africa, as well as book publishers and furniture manufacturers, said Rosoman, who participated in the negotiations. Mondi did not comment. SCA said it could not address its role at the time because the company has since been split into two firms. Klabin did not answer questions on whether the company influenced the FSC Mix rules. But┬áit said the label eased the logistical burden of separating wood from certified and non-certified sources, a view echoed by SCA.
FSC Mix has since become the certification groupÔÇÖs dominant label, accounting for more than three-fourths of the FSC-product trade, according to a 2017 FSC paper. The paper added that FSC Mix was┬áÔÇťthe main source of income for the operating costs of FSC.ÔÇŁ
ÔÇťI just wanted to turn away from the whole project, and warn people: ÔÇśDonÔÇÖt trust it.ÔÇÖÔÇŁ
The FSC told Reuters it doesnÔÇÖt know what percentage of FSC-certified products use the Mix label today. The label, it said, helps companies ÔÇťtransition to more sustainable practices.ÔÇŁ
FSC Mix rules give companies wide latitude to use the label.┬áSome consumer-products firms┬áare allowed to put the Mix label on products that contain no FSC-certified material at all because the FSC gives them credit for certified content in other products they sell.
The SFI also offers a label ÔÇô ÔÇťSFI Certified SourcingÔÇŁ ÔÇô that makes no guarantees that products contain any wood from certified forests, so┬álong as companies meet certain other conditions.
Phil Guillery, a former FSC United States board member and supply chain integrity director, said allowing uncertified wood into the FSC system brought many more timber and forest-products┬ácompanies into the organization and gave them more clout.
ÔÇťThey understood and learned about the politics of FSC, and they became very powerful,ÔÇŁ he said.
Wood, the University of British Columbia lecturer, served on two FSC groups that starting in 2011 tried to overhaul what internal critics had called a weak system of company self-assessments to ensure their FSC Mix products did not contain wood from ÔÇťunacceptable sources,ÔÇŁ such as illegally harvested forests. The rules changes took eight years in a process that was heavily influenced by industry, he said.
The FSC told Reuters the process resulted in a ÔÇťsubstantial strengtheningÔÇŁ of rules governing non-certified wood.┬áWood had a different take, saying the endless deliberations did little to screen out problematic sources of timber. He called his involvement ÔÇťan awful experience.ÔÇŁ
ÔÇťI just wanted to turn away from the whole project,ÔÇŁ he said, ÔÇťand warn people: ÔÇśDonÔÇÖt trust it.ÔÇÖÔÇŁ
Certified forest devastation
Environmentalists criticize the FSC but generally take a harsher view of the SFI, citing its founding by an industry group and weaker forestry standards.
The SFI disputes that it serves only industry interests, telling Reuters its standards reflect input from a diverse group of collaborators including environmentalists on its board.
Environmental groups including the Sierra Club, Stand.earth and the Natural Resources Defense Council say the impact of the SFIÔÇÖs industry-friendly approach is clear in British Columbia, where the organization has dominated certification.
The province, a showcase of CanadaÔÇÖs raw beauty and diverse ecosystems, has seen old-growth forests decline┬áby more than 50% over the last two decades, according to the 2021 and 2023 studies. A subset of ÔÇťhighly productiveÔÇŁ old-growth woodlands ÔÇô forests with the largest┬átrees storing the most carbon, and also the most attractive to logging companies ÔÇô has declined by an estimated 85%.
The SFI became the certifier of choice in British Columbia largely because industry perceived the FSCÔÇÖs early rules as too onerous, said Karen Tam Wu, an FSC consultant during the 2000s.
The timber industry and CanadaÔÇÖs government share in the logging wealth. CanadaÔÇÖs forests are generally on public land, which means provincial governments get a cut of the revenue from every felled tree. In British Columbia, that totaled more than $7.3 billion┬áover the decade ending in March of this year, according to the provinceÔÇÖs forest ministry.
British Columbia in 2020 announced a plan to protect its dwindling old-growth forests after years of public pressure. A year later, officials released maps showing at-risk areas where it called for a deferral of logging. But the government never barred logging in those zones, instead leaving it to industry discretion.
Some major firms chose instead to continue harvesting, including Vancouver-based Canfor Corp, a global timber-and-pulp producer.
Canfor in 2022 whacked about 3,700┬áacres of old-growth forest the government had recommended┬áfor deferral of logging, according to satellite imagery analysis from Stand.earth. The provincial government said earlier this year that more than 50,000 acres of old-growth forest had been harvested in areas it sought to protect.
BCÔÇÖs Ministry of Forests said it is not seeking to end all old-growth logging and that harvesting in some areas is ÔÇťpossible and necessaryÔÇŁ to support ÔÇťlocal, sustainable jobsÔÇŁ while protecting forests.
SFI certified CanforÔÇÖs vast western Canada operations in 2019, 2021, 2022┬áand again last year. None of the publicly released audit summaries ever mentioned the cutting of old-growth forests. Major auditing firm KPMG, which conducted the reviews, had no comment.
Nothing in SFIÔÇÖs standards would have prevented┬álogging of old-growth forests.
SFI said old-growth-forest harvesting in British Columbia is ÔÇťcontentious,ÔÇŁ involving negotiations among governments, industry and indigenous communities. It said its standards require compliance with ÔÇťall applicable laws.ÔÇŁ
Canfor said it is working with indigenous groups, communities and government to ÔÇťreview old-growth management and seek input into our proposed harvesting.ÔÇŁ
ÔÇśLike printing moneyÔÇÖ
Logging companiesÔÇÖ ability to choose their own watchdogs poses the biggest obstacle to promoting high sustainability standards, environmental advocates said.
The auditing structure all but guarantees logging companies can get certified, said Simon Counsell, who was an FSC founding member while with the nonprofit group Friends of the Earth. HeÔÇÖs now an FSC critic.
ÔÇťThereÔÇÖs a clear, vested financial interestÔÇŁ for the auditor, because granting FSC certifications leads to more auditing opportunities, Counsell said. ÔÇťItÔÇÖs like printing money.ÔÇŁ
The FSC said it avoids conflicts of interest by outsourcing reviews and certification to independent auditors who examine companiesÔÇÖ forestry practices and are paid by the firms being certified. The companies, it said, pay a separate ÔÇťannual administration┬áfeeÔÇŁ based on their forest-products revenue that goes to the FSC after being collected by the auditor.
In one example of industry influence over sustainability audits, a major Canadian timber company, Resolute Forest Products, defeated an effort in 2014 to strip its FSC certification in a western Ontario forest by suing and ultimately firing its auditor.
Resolute for years faced charges from scientists and environmentalists that its clear-cuts in the FSC-certified Black Spruce Forest had decimated habitat for threatened woodland caribou. As early as 2012, auditors at the Rainforest Alliance, a nonprofit hired by Resolute, found the timber company failed to meet FSC habitat-protection requirements. Another 2013 Rainforest Alliance audit examined complaints from environmental groups that ResoluteÔÇÖs logging ÔÇťwill lead to the extirpation of caribou from the Black Spruce Forest.ÔÇŁ
Auditors suspended ResoluteÔÇÖs certification in January 2014, citing a failure to meet FSC forest-protection requirements.┬áIn May 2014, Resolute sued the Rainforest Alliance and its auditors, personally, calling their reviews flawed and biased. The company sought $400,000 in damages.┬áIt also asked for an injunction blocking the auditÔÇÖs public release, which an Ontario court granted.┬áThe lawsuit noted that certification was ÔÇťcritical to ResoluteÔÇÖs business model.ÔÇŁ
The lawsuit was settled in 2015, with the alliance agreeing to assign new auditors to redo ResoluteÔÇÖs negative review. The follow-up audit found Resolute met FSC standards and had resolved the issues from the earlier audit.
Chris Wedeles, one of the original auditors Resolute sued, said he was ÔÇťdisappointedÔÇŁ that the new auditors ÔÇťreviewed the same evidence and came to a different conclusion.ÔÇŁ
The Rainforest Alliance reinstated ResoluteÔÇÖs certification.┬áResolute dumped the alliance anyway, moving its auditing business in 2016 to SAI Global, which has re-certified the company every year since.
After the settlement, ResoluteÔÇÖs then-CEO Richard Garneau told FSCÔÇÖs international director┬ágeneral in a 2015 letter that the firm would pull out of FSC unless the certifier addressed the companyÔÇÖs complaints about burdensome FSC requirements. A top Resolute executive was elected to FSC CanadaÔÇÖs board in 2021 and continues to serve today.
Resolute did not answer questions about its forestry practices or its lawsuit but said it upholds ÔÇťthe highest standardsÔÇŁ in forestry management.
SAI Global, Garneau and the Rainforest Alliance, which no longer conducts FSC forestry audits, declined to comment.
The FSC said it was not involved in the dispute between Resolute and its auditor and that it wasnÔÇÖt influenced to change its standards by GarneauÔÇÖs 2015 letter. FSC pointed to recent suspensions of certifications in Quebec as evidence of its commitment to protect caribou.
Meanwhile, problems with caribou in the Black Spruce Forest persist.
In 2020 and 2021, SAI Global auditors found that Resolute could not substantiate the effectiveness of its caribou-conservation plan. The auditors resolved the matter, however, after a Resolute consultant argued that logging would decline to a level that could sustain caribou populations ÔÇô though not until 2039.
Bulldozing forests for oil
One of┬áthe worldÔÇÖs largest stretches of certified forests is in northern Alberta, where the FSC has signed off on the logging practices of Alberta-Pacific Forest Industries Inc.
Over the last two decades, about 878,000 acres of these woodlands, an area more than twice the size of┬áLos Angeles, have been set aside to make way for oil companies to operate open-pit mines, drilling sites and pipelines in CanadaÔÇÖs oil┬ásands. The oil exploration involves clear-cutting and bulldozing the forest. Some environmentalists consider it one of the world's most destructive industrial projects.
Alberta-Pacific Forest Industries holds logging rights to the forest, which includes old-growth woodlands, according to company disclosures and environmental studies. A clause in the companyÔÇÖs agreement with Alberta allows regulators to allocate chunks of the woods for oil-and-gas development.
When that happens, the FSC allows Alberta-Pacific to do a carve-out: removing the FSC certification from the land marked for oil development, while retaining certification for the surrounding forest. The arrangement has allowed Alberta-Pacific to retain certification in the area since 2005 despite the oil-related destruction.
FSC said it encourages certified firms to ÔÇťengage in dialogueÔÇŁ and use their influence to affect land-use decisions such as oil-and-gas development. But the organization said such decisions are ÔÇťoutside of FSCÔÇÖs direct certification scopeÔÇŁ and are governed by provincial and national laws.
Alberta-Pacific said it is proud to have been FSC-certified since 2005 and that it has a goal of ÔÇťmaintaining biodiversity and other forest values.ÔÇŁ It said FSCÔÇÖs policies allow carve-outs for oil development because the resulting environmental impacts are ÔÇťbeyond the full controlÔÇŁ of Alberta-Pacific.
Alberta-Pacific makes money from the oil┬ádevelopment: Under an agreement with Alberta, it receives compensation from oil-and-gas firms for the destroyed forests. It can also sell wood from forests cleared for oil mining under the ÔÇťFSC MixÔÇŁ label, FSC audits show.
Some of the oil is extracted through surface mining, a process that requires the forest to be bulldozed and stripped of vegetation and soil to make way for pits that can be hundreds of feet deep.
ÔÇťThe mining is totally unsustainable,ÔÇŁ said Barry Robinson, an Alberta environmental attorney who has specialized in oil-and-gas issues. ÔÇťIt will be generations before it ever grows trees again.ÔÇŁ
To estimate the amount of Ontario primary and old-growth forest harvested in areas with sustainable certification,┬áReuters analyzed satellite-derived logging data from the National Terrestrial Ecosystem Monitoring System for Canada , forest-stand attribute data from OntarioÔÇÖs Forest Resources Inventory and forest-certification maps from the Forest Products Association of Canada .
The news organization focused on the provinceÔÇÖs productive boreal forest stands within OntarioÔÇÖs forest management units, as defined in the Forest Resources Inventory, and calculated ages in 2016 through 2020 based on the last-known age of the stand.
Reuters used forest certification maps from 2016 through 2020 to determine how much of the annual harvest was in these areas. Reuters then combined the certified forests harvest data with boreal forest stands to estimate the harvests by age in those areas.
Reuters consulted with nine experts in either forestry or geographic information systems in developing and reviewing its methodology.
Related content
How a US health agency became a shield for polluters
The next pandemic may come from bats. Reuters investigates where.
The Musk industrial complex
reuters investigates
More Reuters investigations and long-form narratives
Got a confidential news tip? Reuters Investigates offers several ways to securely contact our reporters
A Cut of the Action
By Chris Kirkham, Grant Smith and Jessica DiNapoli
Photography: Chris Helgren and David Jackson
Photo editing: Corinne Perkins
Graphics and art direction: John Emerson and Jillian Kumagai
Edited by Vanessa OÔÇÖConnell and Brian Thevenot
Amid IsraelÔÇÖs military assault, the worldÔÇÖs hunger monitor has struggled to access key data to determine whether conditions in Gaza constitute a famine.
OnlyFans is among the world's most successful social media platforms ÔÇô but a secretive one. Reuters traces its journey from an obscure, porn-free site to an adults-only phenomenon turbocharged by erotic performers and influencers.
ItÔÇÖs a simple but brutal equation: The number of people going hungry or otherwise struggling around the world is rising, while the amount of money the worldÔÇÖs wealthiest nations are contributing toward helping them is dropping.
Multiple OnlyFans accounts featured suspected child sex abuse, investigator reports.
Reuters, the news and media division of Thomson Reuters, is the worldÔÇÖs largest multimedia news provider, reaching billions of people worldwide every day. Reuters provides business, financial, national and international news to professionals via desktop terminals, the world's media organizations, industry events and directly to consumers.

item.title: Trump says he is not worried about Iran-backed attacks on US soil
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 11 (Reuters) - President Donald Trump said on Wednesday he was not worried about Iran-backed attacks on U.S. soil, as the ÔÇőFederal Bureau of Investigation warned of Iranian drones potentially striking the U.S. ÔÇîWest Coast, ABC News reported.
The U.S. and Israel carried out strikes on Iran nearly two weeks ago, launching the Gulf region into a war . Tehran has carried out retaliatory strikes in ÔÇőresponse to the U.S.-Israeli strikes that killed top Iranian officials, including ÔÇőthe country's supreme leader .
When asked on Wednesday if he was worried Ôüáthat Iran may increase it retaliation to include strikes on U.S. soil, ÔÇőTrump told reporters, "No, I'm not."
ABC News later reported that the FBI had warned ÔÇőpolice departments in California that Iran could retaliate for U.S. attacks by launching drones at the West Coast.
"We recently acquired information that as of early February 2026, Iran allegedly aspired ÔÇőto conduct a surprise attack using unmanned aerial vehicles from an unidentified ÔÇővessel off the coast of the United State Homeland, specifically against unspecified targets in California, ÔÇîin Ôüáthe event that the US conducted strikes against Iran,ÔÇŁ the FBI wrote in an alert distributed at the end of February, according to ABC News.
"We have no additional information on the timing, method, target, or perpetrators of this ÔÇőalleged attack."
Spokespeople for ÔÇőthe FBI, Los ÔüáAngeles Police Department, California governor and Los Angeles mayor did not immediately respond to requests for comments.
Reuters reported earlier ÔÇőthis month that Iran and its proxies could target the ÔÇőU.S. Ôüáwith attacks in response to U.S. strikes.
A threat assessment produced by the Office of Intelligence and Analysis at the Department of Homeland Security said Iran and its proxies "probably" Ôüápose ÔÇőa threat of targeted attacks on the United ÔÇőStates, although a large-scale physical attack was unlikely.

item.title: US gasoline prices surpass $3.50 a gallon at the pumps as Iran war rages on
item.description:
NEW YORK, March 11 (Reuters) - The national average retail price of gasoline crossed $3.50 a gallon this week to hit its highest since May 2024, data from ÔÇőprice tracking services AAA and GasBuddy showed, as the Israel-U.S. war with ÔÇîIran stokes supply concerns.
Disruptions to Middle Eastern oil exports through the Strait of Hormuz have caused a surge in fuel prices across the globe that is threatening to pinch consumers' ÔÇőwallets and derail global economic activity. That could pose the biggest risk ÔÇőfor U.S. President Donald Trump and his Republican Party in the ÔüáMidterm elections in November, especially as a major driver of Trump's re-election in ÔÇő2024 was his vow to lower energy costs.
"Geopolitical shockwaves don't take months to ÔÇőhit your wallet. They take days," said William Stern, chief executive officer at U.S.-based small business lender Cardiff. "You feel the exact squeeze the second you fill up your car just to ÔÇőtake the kids to practice."
U.S. average retail gasoline prices have jumped nearly 60 ÔÇőcents since Trump's decision to join Israel in attacking Iran on February 28 and stood at $3.58 ÔÇîa Ôüágallon on Wednesday.
Even more increases are likely to follow in the days ahead as more ships were hit in the Strait of Hormuz on Wednesday and as the United States undergoes a transition to summer-grade gasoline sales, which burns cleaner but ÔÇőis costlier to produce.
Spot ÔÇőand wholesale gasoline Ôüáprices were registering double-digit increases on Wednesday, said Denton Cinquegrana, chief oil analyst at Oil Price Information Service.
Wholesale price changes ÔÇőare typically reflected at the pumps the next day.
Crude oil ÔÇőprices , , the Ôüábiggest component in fuel costs, were also on the rise on Wednesday despite a proposal by the Paris-based International Energy Agency to release a record 400 million barrels Ôüáof ÔÇőoil from strategic stockpiles.
The IEA's announcement on the release ÔÇőraised more questions than answers as the group did not announce who would release how much oil ÔÇőand when, Cinquegrana said.

item.title: Canada looks┬áfor ways to increase oil production to curb price spikes from Iran war
item.description:
CALGARY, March 11 (Reuters) - Canada will look at ways to increase its crude production to ÔÇőhelp global efforts to stabilize oil ÔÇîprices in the face of the Iran war, Natural Resources Minister Tim Hodgson said Wednesday.
The ÔÇőCanadian government is talking to the ÔÇőcountry's oil producers about delaying planned Ôüámaintenance projects at oil sands facilities ÔÇőin order to temporarily increase output, Hodgson ÔÇőtold reporters in Ottawa. It is also asking Canadian refineries that are using imported oil to ÔÇőuse more domestic oil, in order ÔÇőto free up supply in other regions
The International Energy ÔÇîAgency Ôüáon Wednesday agreed to release 400 million barrels of oil, the largest such move in its history, to try to ÔÇőrein in ÔÇőcrude Ôüáprices that have soared due to supply shocks from the U.S.-Israeli ÔÇőwar with Iran.
Canada ÔÇö the world's ÔÇőfourth Ôüálargest oil producer ÔÇö is a member of the IEA, but does not have its Ôüáown ÔÇőstrategic petroleum reserve because ÔÇőit is a net exporter of oil.

item.title: India tells consumers not to panic over gas shortages and save energy
item.description:
NEW DELHI, March 11 (Reuters) - India on Wednesday asked consumers not to panic about gas supplies and conserve energy wherever possible, as the world's ÔÇősecond-largest importer of liquefied petroleum gas (LPG) struggles to meet domestic demand.
The federal ÔÇîgovernment has invoked emergency powers ordering refiners to maximise production of LPG and cut sales to industry to avoid a shortage for its some 333 million homes with LPG connection.
The government's measures ÔÇőhave increased local LPG output by 25%, said Sujata Sharma, a joint ÔÇősecretary in the federal oil ministry.
Despite the steps, restaurants, hotels, and Ôüáindustries across sectors are beginning to feel the strain from the tighter supplies of ÔÇőLPG, mainly used as cooking fuel.
"Panic booking (of LPG cylinders) and hoarding behaviour have been ÔÇődriven by misinformation," Sharma said.
India's crude oil, LPG, and Liquefied natural gas (LNG) supplies have been disrupted due to global shipping constraints after the U.S. and Israel's war with Iran halted traffic ÔÇőthrough the Gulf and the Strait of Hormuz .
India consumed 33.15 million metric tons of ÔÇőcooking gas last year, with imports accounting for about 60% of demand. About 90% of those ÔÇîimports Ôüácame from the Middle East.
India also imports about half of its 190 million standard cubic metres per day (mscmd) of gas consumption.
Sharma said LNG imports of 47.4 mmscmd have been hit due to supply constraints and force majeure by its top supplier Qatar .
India ÔÇőhas begun diverting gas supplies ÔÇőfrom non-priority sectors Ôüáto key users.
India is securing crude and LNG from alternative sources with two LNG cargoes already on their way, Sharma said.
Indian ÔÇőrefiners have bought millions of barrels of Russian oil floating on ÔÇőthe high Ôüáseas after Washington granted a 30-day sanctions waiver.
Most Indian refineries are operating at full capacity, she said.
"The Middle East conflict has posed challenges for the world, but India's fuel Ôüádemand ÔÇőis being met. The Government is continuously monitoring the ÔÇőglobal situation and taking necessary steps to ensure uninterrupted fuel supplies and protect households and priority sectors," ÔÇőshe added.

item.title: Iraq asks KRG to help pipe crude oil to Turkey, sources say
item.description:
BAGHDAD, March 11 (Reuters) - Iraq has asked the Kurdistan Regional Government (KRG) if it would pump at least 100,000 barrels of crude per day from ÔÇőits state-managed Kirkuk oilfields to Turkey's Ceyhan port, two oil officials familiar with ÔÇîthe matter told Reuters.
Iraq's oil ministry sent a letter to the KRG with the request early last week, the officials said.
The Kurdistan Pipeline Company operates Iraqi Kurdistan's main oil pipeline, which transports crude from ÔÇőnorthern Iraq to a border connection point with the IraqÔÇôTurkey pipeline.
Shipments could gradually increase ÔÇődepending on availability, and Baghdad would pay the transit fees, the officials Ôüásaid.
The officials said the step was taken to mitigate the huge losses to IraqÔÇÖs ÔÇőoil revenues caused by the halt of southern crude exports.
They added that the KRG had not ÔÇőyet responded to the request. However, speaking to Reuters, a senior KRG official said any decision to grant use of the pipeline would come with conditions.
"No drop of oil will pass through the pipeline unless ÔÇőBaghdad gives the KRG immediate dollar relief for imports and security guarantees for international ÔÇőoil companies to restart production (in Kurdistan)," said the official who asked not to be named due to the ÔÇîsensitivity Ôüáof the matter.
Some energy companies operating in Iraqi Kurdistan shut oil and gas production as a precaution as the U.S. and Israel launched strikes on neighbouring Iran.
Oil production from Iraq's main southern oilfields, where most of its oil is produced and exported, has fallen by 70% to ÔÇőjust 1.3 million from ÔÇő4.3 million bpd, Ôüásources said on March 8, as the country is unable to export via the Gulf due to the Iran war.
Iraq's northern Kirkuk oilfields, ÔÇőmeanwhile, are currently producing around 350,000 bpd, with all of it ÔÇődiverted to Ôüádomestic refineries in the north, including the country's largest, the Baiji refinery.
The oil ministry would send crude from its southern oilfields to Baiji to compensate for the Kirkuk oil being sent to ÔüáCeyhan, ÔÇőone oil ministry source said.
Falling oil production and lower ÔÇőexports are likely to tighten IraqÔÇÖs already fragile fiscal situation, as the government relies on crude income for nearly ÔÇőall of its budget.
